Another typical deficiency is the difference between the Claimant’s damaged vehicle, the vehicle offered as a replacement and the actual vehicle provided. I have seen many occasions where a Claimant has ended their relationship with the credit-hire company reliant on the offer in the Copley letter only to discover that the vehicle offered by the insurer is not available and a far inferior vehicle is supplied. In some instances, the rental company nominated to provide the replacement vehicle concedes to the Claimant that they would never have been able to offer a comparable vehicle because their fleet does not, and never has had, that model of vehicle. Some insurers claim they can provide a comparable car for a specific rate but the customer discovers that not only is a comparable replacement car not available, but that the insurer has an agreement with the hire company to provide a lower group of vehicle to reduce their indemnity spend or that they are required to make a contribution to the hire charges to reduce the excess on the hire vehicle to nil. Any of those circumstances could make the Copley letter ineffective on the basis that the solution available to the Claimant when hiring from a CHO are not matched by the arrangements, costs and obligations proposed by the insurer.	 

The credit-hire company has a choice as to whether they ignore the Copley letter completely knowing that the insurer will probably not settle the credit-hire charges and litigation will ensue, and the issue will be resolved in court in a year’s time. Alternatively, they might pick up the telephone, speak to the insurer and look to negotiate a discounted rate with that insurer in order that their customer might remain in the CHOs hire car. Most of the larger CHOs will have a small and dedicated team dealing explicitly with intervention challenges. Where an insurer does not subscribe to the GTA the threat of intervention is high. However, whilst most insurers, are incapable of reacting with the same level of precision and focus to the challenges posed by those credit-hire companies, as insurers work more collegiately with primary suppliers, the challenge may well increase. The other issue with intervention relates to the choice of the repairer carrying out the repair and the associated cost.  In truth, it is just the cost. Most CHOs will have a financial relationship with the repairer nominated to effect repairs either because they were the source of the referral or because they are part of the CHOs network of approved repairers to whom the CHO will direct their own credit-repair activity. The credit-hire company should have inspected and confirmed the competence of the repairer and the quality of their work where they are part of an approved repairer network. If the repairer is expecting to repair a Claimant’s car on credit-repair terms, they expect to be paid a commercial rate and not sacrifice any discount on parts or materials other than a percentage agreed with the credit-hire company. Because of these two factors, the repairer will be allowed to commit the necessary labour hours and use the right parts to repair a damaged car to its pre-accident condition. Where an insurer offers to intervene, they do so with the intention of reducing the indemnity spend incurred because of the negligence of their policyholder. They do not have the Claimant’s best interest at heart and will be  motivated to direct the repair to one of their own approved repairers where the repairer may be encouraged or compelled to charge  a lower hourly rate, complete the repair using fewer labour hours or use parts that may not be manufactured by the vehicle manufacturer or which are reconditi
articles.source: Stephen Evans, Credit Hire (2020)

articles.title: Assessing Need: A Practical Guide for Claim Handlers
articles.author: Stephen Evans
articles.summary: A practical operational guide to assessing whether a claimant has a genuine need for a replacement hire vehicle, what type of vehicle is appropriate, and how to document the decision. Covers private motorists, multiple-vehicle households, business users, and the interaction between need assessment and vehicle group selection.
key_points: Need assessment is the most important decision a claim handler makes — getting it wrong invalidates the entire hire claim
The fact that a claimant owns an expensive vehicle creates a prima facie inference of need, but this must still be tested
Availability of other vehicles in the household must be investigated — a spare car that meets the claimant's needs may defeat the need argument
The appropriate hire vehicle group should match the claimant's actual use, not their aspiration — a like-for-like vehicle is not always necessary
Business users must demonstrate specific business need — the inference available to a private motorist does not automatically apply
The duration of hire must be tied to the repair or write-off timeline — hire that continues without active repair progress is vulnerable
All need assessment discussions should be documented contemporaneously with date and time stamps — these records protect the claim in litigation
Insurers will ask the claimant's own insurer whether alternative vehicles were available — the CHO should ask the same question first
cases_cited: Giles v Thompson
articles.full_text:
Assessing need

From the narrative thus far, it should be clear that a credit-hire claim handler has a challenging and difficult role to perform requiring a level of technical ability that is often not appreciated by outside observers. Ensuring compliance with the principles set down by the FCA, treating customers fairly, acting as a detective in trying to piece together the circumstances of an accident and assessing against whom an allegation of negligence can be made, understanding the operational requirements of the GTA, authorising the claim as suitable for credit-hire and credit-repair and addressing the intervention risk. These are all issues that influence the success of a credit-hire company and affect the passage of a claim over the brief period to the point where the decision to supply a hire car is made. That decision involves determining whether the client has a need to hire and, if they do, what type of car do they need and what model of hire car should be supplied and at what rate. In addition, there needs to be a determination about when the hire should start, whether the repair should be by credit-repair or not and documenting all of the actions taken, ensuring the client is fully appraised of the arrangements made on their behalf and issuing  the appropriate instructions for the hire to commence. An important consideration is whether the Claimant needs a replacement hire vehicle. Insurers will, when looking at a claim, ask the Claimant’s insurer whether they have any other vehicles which they might utilise instead of incurring the expense of hiring a car. Whilst that is a valid enquiry to make, and one the credit-hire company should also consider before starting a hire, proving need is not usually that difficult to do. The fact that I have spent £100,000 buying an Audi RS4 probably means that I have a need for a car and if that car is damaged because of the fault of another then that other party has an obligation to compensate me for the loss of use of the Audi which is usually referable to the cost of hiring an equivalent vehicle from a conventional hire company. I would still need to demonstrate the need to hire a replacement car whilst mine was being repaired and that raises two distinct issues: Do I really need to hire a car and, if I do, what do I need the hire car for? We have three cars at home. As well as the Audi, my wife and daughter both have their own cars and I am named as an additional driver on the insurance policy for both cars. There is not, however, a spare car that I could use instead of hiring a replacement and I know that my daughter and my wife both have a daily need for their own vehicles which rules them out of the equation.

Most of the issues which surface when negotiating claims relate to the reasonableness of the actions taken by the victim. Because the type of hire car influences the cost, the more pressing question is why the Claimant needs a car. If, for example, they are going on holiday abroad for two weeks then they clearly have no justification in hiring a car which they might just leave in an airport car park for 2 weeks. Equally, if they are incapacitated because of the accident and incapable of driving then proving a need to hire vehicle is difficult unless their car is the only car in the family and is used by their partner as well. If, then, the Claimant can show a need for the vehicle based on predicted usage and did not have a spare and unused alternative in the garage, then they are part way there. The second issue, however, is what car type of car do they need. Does the fact that they own a Range Rover Vogue mean that they are entitled to hire one as a replacement. In simple terms the answer is probably yes, if they are clear why that type of vehicle is needed. It may be because they drive a high number of miles and require a safe and durable car and want the comfort and solidity of a Range Rover. It may also be that they need a car with a large boot because they have a large dog or carry a large amount of equipment in the rear. Or it may even be that they are extremely nervous having been involved in an accident where the air-bags detonated and avoided injury as a consequence of the car they were driving and so wants to ensure that they have the same security and safety and familiarity in the vehicle they intend to hire. Those questions supporting need are important questions to ask. They are better asked, and the answers recorded, before the hire starts rather than when the Claimant is in a witness box in a year’s time and concedes that they would have been happy with a Ford Focus because they only travels 10 miles a week. They are also important questions on determining whether any Copley compliant offer is a reasonable offer in relation to a suitable available vehicle. Occasionally a CHO will offer the Claimant a vehicle from a superior group to the damaged vehicle because they do not have the correct vehicle available. If the credit-hire company only charges rate consistent for the hire vehicle there should be no real issue recovering the hire charges. There is, however, the basis for a complaint from the customer. If a Claimant owns a vehicle where the fuel consumption is 35mpg and they are given a larger vehicle where the fuel consumption averages just 17mpg then they are likely to be unhappy if they cover a high annual mileage. Equally, if a motorist has a company car for which they are taxed based on the CO2 emissions output by that vehicle and is given a less efficient vehicle, the implication for the tax they pay could be material especially if the hire period is longer than average. In neither of these circumstances would the hirer have any simple recourse against the at-fault driver for the increased costs they incurred, and nor would they have any claim against the credit-hire company. However, the credit-hire company needs to be prepared that the inconvenience incurred might affect how co-operative the Claimant will be in supporting the settlement of the hire charges especially if litigation becomes necessary.

Finally, there are occasions when a Claimant will need certain extra features which are present on their own damaged vehicle but are not always standard on a hire car. These could include integral satellite navigation or an isofix child seat if the seat in the client’s car has been damaged, or motorbike leathers and a helmet where the originals have been destroyed or a top box where storage is required on the hired motorcycle because it was present in the damaged vehicle. There are also occasions when the vehicle itself is special. Light commercial vehicles are never as standard as they might appear and sometimes a white van is more than just a white van. The most unusual vehicles I have seen on a credit-hire fleet include a hearse, an ice cream van and a horse box. But commercial vehicles can be equally unusual with a need for specialist racking or an internal refrigeration unit, for example. Because of the general lack of demand there is no GTA tariff for many specialist vehicles, but somebody involved in food distribution might need a chiller or refrigerated unit and such a vehicle will have a much higher rental cost than a standard vehicle. Equally, a pick-up truck may need to be a drop side, have a hydraulic lifting mechanism or have a hi-abs fitted. Licensed hire drivers and taxi or black cab operators will require vehicles that are licensed and plated and compliant with the regulations operable in the area from which the damaged vehicle is registered. The costs of these added services should all be recoverable. Where they are not specified in the GTA tariff then it is essential that the credit-hire company makes contact with the at-fault insurer and, by reference to market data, agrees a hire-rate for the provision of that vehicle before the hire starts. It is always easier to settle the claim  when any basis for disputing the rate charged is removed early on.
articles.source: Stephen Evans, Credit Hire (2020)

articles.title: Repair Monitoring: Protecting the Hire Period
articles.author: Stephen Evans
articles.summary: Explains why repair monitoring is as financially important as rate — a 2-day reduction on a 10-day hire equals a 20% rate reduction. Sets out the GTA's prescriptive monitoring obligations and the practical controls CHOs need to protect the full hire period from insurer challenge.
key_points: Insurers know that challenging the hire period is as effective as challenging the rate — a 20% time reduction equals a 20% rate reduction
The GTA imposes prescriptive monitoring obligations: defined checkpoints, notification of delays, parts supply issues must be reported to the insurer
All repair progress notes must be recorded contemporaneously with date and time stamps — records that can be altered will be challenged
Entries in the repair monitoring log should be factual only — opinions or commentary can be used against the CHO in litigation
Hire must end within 24 hours of the claimant being notified that their vehicle is ready — this is a hard GTA obligation
Where delays arise from the repairer obtaining parts, notify the at-fault insurer promptly so they cannot later allege the CHO failed to manage the repair
Even outside the GTA, the monitoring steps it prescribes represent the standard of reasonableness a court would expect
Start date of hire is a common battleground — hire that begins before the engineer's visit or before repairs start is vulnerable to reduction
articles.full_text:
Repair monitoring

The argument against the hire rate charged to a Claimant is often also accompanied by a challenge to the length of hire.  Accordingly, a CHO must have controls in place to watch and record the progress of repair to their Claimant’s damaged vehicle so that they can justify the entirety of the hire period later. For an insurer, the opportunity to reduce their liability for hire charges by arguing that the Claimant behaved unreasonably can be as efficient as arguing for a reduction of the daily hire rate. Persuading a CHO to accept a reduction of 20% from their daily rate is likely to meet greater resistance than conceding that the Claimant did not behave reasonably. For example, if a hire started a day before the engineer arrived at the repairer to inspect the damaged vehicle and the hire should have ended on the day after the repairer had complete repairers and not the following  day when the CHO collected their hire car, there is the basis  for a dispute. Losing two days on a typical 10-day hire is as effective for an insurer as arguing for a 20% reduction in rate.

The GTA is proscriptive about the steps a CHO should take whilst a Claimant’s car is being repaired. Whilst not necessarily as relevant for claims processed outside of the GTA, both the guidelines and the remedial steps suggested in case of a delay are the kind of reasonable actions that a court might expect a Claimant to follow. Of course, in credit-hire claims, relying on the steps a Claimant might take is an unreliable thing to do especially when most Claimants expect their claim to be managed by the CHO and to react to events when they are asked to do so. Consequently, responsibility for the controls put in place and the checks carried out during a hire are usually carried out by the CHO.  For evidential purposes there is a benefit to ensuring that all notes relating to the progress of the repair are recorded contemporaneously in a data table linked to each claim. Ideally, the entry should be time and date stamped and the entries should be incapable of alteration, modification or deletion so that the accuracy or veracity of the entries cannot be impugned. Entries should also be restricted to factual points associated with the progress of the repair or negotiation of the total loss value in circumstances where the vehicle is declared beyond economic repair. Typically, the first entry in the repair monitoring table should be the plan for the repair and the actions taken thus far with the Claimant. For example, if the Claimant has decided to allow the at-fault insurer to manage repairs on her behalf, then that decision should be recorded in the file together with the instructions from the approved repairer of the date on which the Claimant attend and the time at which the CHO will attend with the hire car. Thereafter, in a case where the third party has taken responsibility for repairs, it would not be unreasonable for the Claimant to wait until she is told that repairs to her car have concluded and she should arrange to collect the car. However, mistakes happen, even with at-fault insurers that take on the responsibility for repairs and so it is prudent to speak with the Claimant after the hire car has been delivered and to confirm and record  any expectation given by the repairer as to the expected time to complete the repair. It is then wise to call the repairer after three days or a few days before the repair is expected to complete to verify the progress. Each entry should record who supplied any information about the repair, the state of repair including whether the car has been stripped down, is awaiting parts, is on the jig or in the spray booth or whatever state the car is in. In addition, at the end of the call the handler should ask when repairs are likely to complete and, again, record it and diary to make a call in the shorter of three days from then or two days before expected completion. That process should continue until the Claimant calls to say that they have been told to collect their car or the repairer calls the CHO with the same information.  At that point, the CHO should clarify with the Claimant that they can collect the car at the allotted time. If they are, for example, out of town on business or the notice they have been given is too short to make arrangements to collect the car then that must be noted in the repair log to head off any later argument about the recoverability of the final day of hire. The same attention to detail should apply irrespective of whether repairs have been arranged through the at-fault insurer, the Claimant’s insurer or by credit-repair. Every event should be recorded to prove positive engagement with hastening the repair and where unexpected delays are reported then they should be investigated and recorded. For example, if repairs are halted because more damage was noted, and the repair needs a further authorisation for the cost of the added work to complete the repair.  In addition, at busy periods, a repairer may report delays to the repair because of the volume of work he has underway and because there is a queue of vehicles waiting for the spray booth, oven or jig. Equally, issues associated with the weather where, for example, snow causes the repairer to stop work because of adverse travelling conditions. Those issues can never be the fault of the Claimant but that will not stop a barrister arguing before a judge that the estimate predicted ten days to complete the repair and the repairs took twenty. Absent persuasive evidence to support the delay, a judge may order a reduction in the number of days hire. There are occasions when, as well as recording the delay to repair, a CHO is obliged to report the delay to the at-fault insurer under their obligations within the GTA. This should be done, and a note kept that the correct notice has been sent. Often a delay occurs because of a difficulty sourcing parts from abroad and the purpose of the insurer  being notified of the associated delay is to afford them the chance of expediting the delivery of the parts where, for example, they might have a  relationship  with the vehicle manufacturer.  Often, the most challenging problem for a CHO is when the Claimant’s vehicle is beyond economic repair. In those circumstances the cause of delay passes between the Claimant, any finance company and whichever insurer is dealing with the valuation of the damaged vehicle. The insurer will often require copies of certain documents prior to making an offer. These could include the registration document, copies of purchase invoices or the MOT certificate.  Claimants are often slow at supplying those documents and to avoid being penalised through a reduction in the hire period, the CHO needs to keep pressure on their hirer and the insurer to follow all reasonable requests. The finance company becomes involved where there is an outstanding liability to be discharged from the insurance proceeds. As long as the hirer behaves reasonably and responds to all reasonable requests then they cannot be held liable  for delays associated with the finance  company or insurer but a detailed record of actions and events needs to be kept to ensure no adverse effect on the recoverability of the hire charges.

In terms of the use of that recorded data, individual CHOs will have their own view. The GTA requires a pro-forma document be sent with every hire invoice to support the key dates in the repair process. Notifying every insurer that the repair period is fully supportable if proceedings follow is a helpful way of forcing them to raise any concerns about the hire period early in negotiations. Where litigation does follow then the CHO should consider providing the Claimant or her solicitor, on a privileged basis, a copy of the itemised repair log  in order that consideration can be given  to serving the chronology with a supporting witness statement to  eliminate any risk that the trial judge will
articles.source: Stephen Evans, Credit Hire (2020)

articles.title: Defendant Insurer Solicitor Strategies
articles.author: Stephen Evans
articles.summary: An insider account of how defendant insurer solicitors — Keoghs, DAC Beachcroft, Horwich Farrelly, BLM, Plexus and others — approach credit hire claims. Covers their stated strategies, the tactics they use within and outside the GTA, and how CHOs and claimant solicitors can best respond.
key_points: Insurers routinely instruct Keoghs, DAC Beachcroft, Horwich Farrelly, BLM and Plexus to manage large volumes of credit hire claims
Defendant firms are financially incentivised to reduce insurer spend — their commercial relationship rewards cost reduction, not just winning cases
Tactics include: deliberately delaying GTA claims where they believe a better outcome is achievable outside the protocol
Defendant solicitors will exploit every procedural opportunity — late or missing notifications, repair monitoring gaps, and unsigned agreements are all targeted
Some firms use a differential strategy: different approaches against different CHOs based on what has historically worked against each one
The firms market themselves on 'commercially focused settlement strategies' — this means systematic cost reduction, not fair assessment of each claim
Knowing which insurer has instructed which firm is tactically important — each firm has characteristic approaches and pressure points
Collaboration with the CHO's own legal team and proactive claim preparation is the best defence against systematic challenge
articles.full_text:
The position of the defendant and their solicitors

Managing every aspect of the challenge that every insurer will bring is critical. However, insurers will have different perspectives on how they deal with claims. Some will manage claims in-house and will adhere to the GTA principles but may not settle every claim because they do not believe that the hire charges are justifiable or that liability is in dispute. The GTA protocol does not permit the  insurer to  take points of principle about contract or enforceability or the overall quantum of the claim but, if an insurer  believes a claim could be  resisted or the cost reduced if it were litigated then they will often make an  unrealistic offer to settle, or make no offer at all. Then, when the CHO shows an intention to issue proceedings, they might re-consider settlement but, more probably will just nominate a solicitor to accept service. Others might have taken out their cost of managing claims and so they will instruct a solicitor to manage the entirety of their claim process from first notification through to settlement. They do this generally because they have been promised a more cost-effective outcome than if the insurer manages the claims internally. Insurers routinely instruct Keoghs, DAC Beachcroft, Horwich Farrelly, BLM and Plexus to manage large volumes of claims and they will bring their own strategic approach to resisting and defending those credit-hire claims. In some instances, a solicitor will have different approaches with different CHOs, determined by the results of different strategies applied against each credit-hire company. Some of the strategies are crude and involve, for example, delaying the settlement of claims within the GTA where the solicitor believes they can deliver a better outcome for their insurer client, and for themselves, outside of the GTA. They are rewarded when they save money for their insurer client and, when litigation is pursued, they will be as aggressive as they can and exploit every opportunity to increase the level of costs awarded against every Claimant. The various marketing claims made by each firm are reproduced below. They are almost interchangeable and show the scale of the challenge faced by the credit-hire industry with an adversary that claims to deliver:

“commercially focused and pragmatic settlement strategies which align our client’s litigation philosophy to their key objective of managing, controlling and reducing indemnity spend…we understand what strong performance looks like…continually improving cost effectiveness and the range of services offered with the aim of minimising our client’s total indemnity spend…dedicated teams focusing on early intervention and early expert involvement…we seek opportunities to work aggressively with clients to pursue the right response to litigation…we litigate to win…we provide regular statistical analysis of claim records, reports on trends including claimant behaviour…we have tailored training programmes…including “Know you Opponent” which… helps to deal with adverse behaviours and deter further claims or litigation.”

Many of the larger defendant law firms publish intelligence relating to their approach. However, they publish more about their respective successes in test case litigation, litigation which they have often encouraged to alter the environment in which claims are compromised. Articles routinely appear on their websites, typically trailed on LinkedIn or Twitter, and eventually feature in one of the insurance or legal titles as a means for reporting the successful outcome of their strategy.  There is a ludicrous irony that became clear during the CMA investigation. Without credit-hire being so heavily contested, many defendant solicitors would not have a business model. Their strategic aim is to win as many insurer relationships as possible and then justify the incremental cost that their involvement brings by highlighting the economic saving that they can deliver for their client. Even where a CHO does  not wish to litigate and seeks to manage its claims in the GTA protocol, where an insurer has outsourced its claim management strategy to a third-party solicitor then there is no guarantee that the credit-hire company will  be rewarded for its adherence to a strategy intended to limit friction. The moment a defendant solicitor highlights any opportunity to reduce the overall sum paid out by their principal, they will do so. It is largely what they are incentivised to do. However, a CHO can be prejudiced beyond the engagement of a defendant solicitor in individual claims. Much of the regulatory change that has affected credit-hire claims over the past 10 years has been a result of the corporate lobbying activities of the ABI in association with a number of those larger solicitor firms. DAC Beachcroft and Keoghs are probably the most prolific. Regrettably, the achievement of the credit-hire industry in resisting the changes sought by the CMA in 2014 is the high watermark of the efforts of the trade body to protect the industry from further change. Since 2014, the credit-hire industry has articulated no meaningful response to the ongoing efforts made by the ABI and its advisers to limit the common law rights of individuals or to advance the economic value the credit-hire industry delivers for society. That is one of the reasons why I believe the future of the credit-hire industry in its current form is limited.

Insurer relationships

With the potential disruption that defendant solicitors can create, building a productive relationship with insurers is paramount. The difficulty, in most cases though, is that those relationships are ordinarily created between junior employees when individual claims are negotiated and compromised. That is not the best environment for a CHO to build a transformational relationship with insurers especially when the discourse between two combatant claim handlers can easily become personal and sometimes acrimonious. And that acrimony spreads amongst employees in both organisations as handlers in one insurer and opposing handlers in a credit-hire company respectively decide that their opponents are idiots. For a finance director, used to a conventional business and where every element of the customer relationship tracks back to the finance function, the idiosyncrasies of a credit-hire business can seem quite destabilising. In a widget manufacturer, for example, the finance director controls the cost of production, the terms on which he allows the sales director to transact business, the credit worthiness of the customer and the collectability of any invoices raised. Any organisational tensions will usually resolve in his favour because he alone can prove the financial outcome from any commercial relationship. A sales director would be unlikely to persuade his colleague in finance that it makes sense to sell widgets at a loss or for a marginal return because the relationship is worth having. Yet, many relationships between credit-hire operators and referrers are rationalised on that basis. In some cases, there may be a solid reason the relationship makes sense. For example, where the volume of guaranteed referrals crystallises the opportunity to buy fleet vehicles at discounts that would not otherwise be available to the CHO. However, that argument rarely makes sense if the business generated for the fleet is loss-making.  More significantly, issues with the settlement of outstanding invoices for widgets ordered and supplied would be the ultimate responsibility of the finance director and might resolve with him speaking to his counterpart to negotiate a satisfactory resolution to any dispute.

Those relationships rarely exist for a finance director in a credit-hire business. If there is interaction between an insurer and a CHO it is typically at the operational level and rarely with senior decision-makers at the insurer. If there is any informal engagement, it ofte
articles.source: Stephen Evans, Credit Hire (2020)

articles.title: Claim Settlement: How Insurers Approach It
articles.author: Stephen Evans
articles.summary: An operational guide to how credit hire claims are actually settled — how insurers evaluate claims, the typical points of attack on every invoice, why claims rarely settle at face value, and the skills required in claim negotiators to achieve good outcomes.
key_points: Credit hire invoices rarely settle at face value — both the rate and the period are routinely challenged on every claim
Before settling, an insurer must be satisfied on: (1) negligence of their policyholder, (2) rate (GTA or court-equivalent), (3) period reasonableness
Common attack points on every invoice: hire starting before repairs began, hire ending after repairs were complete, disputed liability
Insurers will sometimes make a deliberately low or no offer when they believe litigation would yield a better outcome for them — this is a bluff that should be called where the claim is solid
Claim negotiators need to understand both GTA mechanics and tort law to rebut insurer challenges effectively
Some CHOs deliberately over-invoice expecting concessions — this is a risky strategy that can damage the credibility of the claim overall
The longer a claim remains unsettled, the more working capital pressure falls on the CHO — insurers know this and use it
Settlement of high-value claims may require senior insurer engagement — operational-level negotiations can stall on claims where only a commercial relationship at senior level will break the deadlock
articles.full_text:
Claim settlement

Whilst the revenue recognition process relies on assessing the historic level at which claims have settled and the quality of the assessment and monitoring of a hire whilst a claim is in progress, it also relies upon the ability of the people tasked with settling the claim. In most conventional businesses the finance director manages the collection of the debtor book. Typically, a credit control team will handle the financial relationship with customers, and they will be tasked with recovering the value of outstanding invoices. Where necessary, they will resolve any disputes, issue credit notes if appropriate and, if the debtor refuses to pay, will instigate proceedings for the recovery of any outstanding balance. Mechanically, the process of collecting credit-hire debt should involve most of the same processes but there are some fundamental differences. The first is that many credit-hire invoices are unlikely to settle at their face value for a variety of reasons. In some cases, CHOs are happy for the hire invoice to be over-stated hoping that if they concede something in negotiation that they will still settle at a figure higher than their real expectations. Another reason is that every invoice will have an element susceptible to attack by an insurer. It may be that the hire period began before repairs were started or on a date when the Claimant’s own vehicle was still capable of being driven. Equally, it could be that liability for the accident was not as straightforward as it first appeared when the claim was approved. Before they will settle a credit-hire invoice, an insurer has to be convinced that the hire charges have arisen as a result of the negligence of their policyholder and that the amount claimed is either at an agreed protocol rate or, if  there  is no protocol, at a level consistent with an award a court might make if they adjudicated the matter. To defeat those arguments raised by insurers claim negotiators must be cognisant of how the GTA affects the management of a claim and, where the GTA has no application to the claim, must be familiar with the law of tort. They also need to have experience in arguing about need and be able to prove why the at-fault driver was liable for the damages that have befallen the Claimant. More importantly, they need to be focused and tenacious and show a level of attention to detail which will limit the number of claims that any individual claim negotiator can manage at any one time. The attributes and skills of a competent credit controller are wholly different to those necessary to persuade an insurer to settle a claim. On the basis that a team of individuals with the most appropriate skills and experience to settle the claim should be the individuals appointed to do so, a credit control team managed by the finance director is rarely involved in recovering credit-hire debt. Most CHOs will employ a separate settlement team to manage credit-hire recoveries. Others might outsource the opportunity to a solicitor, perhaps an ABS, and pass any outstanding claims to a paralegal capable of either negotiating or litigating them.

However, Utopia for any CHO is for claims to settle with the least human intervention and ideally through a bilateral protocol. Wherever such a protocol exists, the number of individual employees normally engaged to manage the claim volume with that insurer can be reduced, also reducing costs, with any individual disputes managed as an exception. A protocol is also positive news for a finance director. Each negotiated protocol improves how predictable the cash flow is for claims made against each insurer because the basis on which the quantum for each claim determined is usually agreed as part of the negotiation. That helps to reduce the uncertainty in reporting revenue. However, not all CHOs manage to negotiate individual protocols and, even where they do, it would be rare for all their revenue to be covered by protocols.  Because of that, a CHO will face several choices. What involvement should the finance team have in the ongoing relationship with insurers, what are the alternatives, who has absolute responsibility for the receivables book and how should the collection exercise be managed? These are critical issues to consider because most CHOs will report revenue after making an accounting provision, effectively an estimate the amount of invoiced hire charges that they will have to concede to settle a claim with an insurer. That provisioning should reflect the points I mentioned above; concessions made in respect of an unsupportable or excessive hire period, the provision of a vehicle better than the Claimant’s own vehicle, the Claimant not being able to demonstrate a need  for the hire car or a mistake in respect of the apportionment of liability. I will return to that issue.

In the last chapter, I followed the life of the claim to the point that a payment pack was sent to the insurer. If that pack was sent under the terms of the GTA, then most CHOs will probably do nothing for 30 days other than perhaps to call the insurer and confirm the payment pack has been received. It makes no sense to do more than that. Most insurers have a persistent backlog of work and trying to force settlement of a claim when it is in a queue waiting to be processed just leads to unnecessary friction. That said, confirming that the payment pack has been received is a useful check to ensure that 30 days of cash flow are not wasted because, for example, the payment pack was sent to the wrong address. That can happen if the CHO does not follow the default instructions given by individual insurers or does not quote the correct insurer reference number on the claim. It is important, therefore, that the correct contact details are recorded for every insurer on the CHOs back office system. Some insurers will manage different classes of policyholder at different offices, others might instruct solicitors to process their payment packs and most will require references to be applied that help them process the claim internally. Getting this fundamental information right from the outset is critical. The process is sometimes made easier if insurers either allow payment packs to be transmitted electronically or to be supported by an electronic bordereau, in which case calling to confirm receipt is unnecessary. In claims that involve an insurer that does not subscribe to the GTA, some of the above issues might apply but leaving the claim for 30 days without chasing the insurer is not the best way to stimulate a positive cash flow. Claims processed by non GTA subscribers need a more determined process from day one.

In all cases, the insurer will carry out some form of validation. Their investigations into whether they believe the claim is tainted by fraud should have been completed by then. If they are unhappy then they will either reject the claim or notify the CHO that they have continuing enquiries to make. There is extraordinarily little collaboration between an insurer and a CHO where the insurer suspects fraud. That is both an inefficient basis to reduce the risk of dishonest claims and, for the CHO, a truly irritating way to behave. If one of the parties to the accident are fraudsters then that will affect the credit-hire company just as much, if not more than it might affect an insurer. The insurer will simply delay settlement of the claim for as long as their enquiries continue. The CHO, however, will have paid for the referral, incurred the cost of the hire car and without co-operation will be faced with the choice of litigating or waiting until the insurer concludes their enquiries which might take months. Insurers will argue that, if they suspect fraud, the referrer, the CHO, the repairer or anybody else associated with the claim might be complicit and until such time as their investigations conclude there is no safe way to share their concerns.  Other checks they might perform inclu
articles.source: Stephen Evans, Credit Hire (2020)

articles.title: Litigation Strategy in Credit Hire
articles.author: Stephen Evans
articles.summary: Covers when and how to litigate credit hire claims, the changed legal environment since the CMA Report 2014, the financial pressures on solicitors taking credit hire cases, and why thorough preparation and clear strategy are essential before proceedings are issued.
key_points: Since the CMA Report (2014), appellate courts have become more challenging for credit hire claimants — litigation should only be pursued on well-prepared claims
The economics of credit hire litigation for solicitors have changed dramatically — no-win no-fee funding is far less profitable in low-value cases
Defendant firms have developed systematic strategies to maximise costs against claimants, exploiting every procedural gap
Before issuing proceedings, the CHO must ensure: compelling evidence on liability, need, rate, and period — a weak claim will be exploited
The insurer's threat to nominate a solicitor on receipt of intention to proceed is often a bluff — but requires a calculated response
Claims that cannot settle and cannot viably be litigated must be written off — the cost of pursuing a weak case often exceeds the potential recovery
Historically, most appellate credit hire battles have been fought over enforceability (CCA 1974) — the current battleground is rate, need, and period
Collaboration between the CHO's claim team, its solicitors and counsel from an early stage produces better outcomes than crisis management before trial
articles.full_text:
Litigation and disbursements

Litigation was the weapon of choice before the ABI GTA was introduced. It was an attractive method of resolving credit-hire claims in the first 2 decades of the industry because solicitors were able to contract with Claimants on a ‘no-win no-fee’ basis and generate a very profitable revenue stream from business referred by CHOs. For the credit-hire company, the relationship was equally beneficial because it meant they could employ fewer people in the process and the costs of recovery were passed to another entity. Today, the financial environment could not be more different especially for solicitors as they struggle to justify devoting resources to litigating credit-hire or personal injury claims. The exceptions, perhaps, are those solicitors that have evolved ABS arrangements with credit-hire or claim management companies.  The challenge is not just the lack of profit from litigating those claims, it is the reaction to those claims from the judiciary. Certainly since 2014 and the publication of the CMA report, it is a brave credit-hire company that wants to go anywhere near an appellate court but, if the CHO is unable to  reach agreement with the insurer, then they must either be written off or litigated.  Historically, the legal environment seemed to be more supportive of Claimants and the attitude of the judiciary was less discernible. Today, the regulatory and legislative changes introduced over the past 10 years and the success of corporate lobbying has created a more combative environment.  A CHO must ensure that any case they choose to litigate is fully and properly prepared with compelling evidence to defeat the resistance to settlement. Strategy is key but so too are the financial challenges. There is evidence that the CHO trade body has developed a more insurer-friendly approach over the last 5 years and, to the extent they have, collaboration is always better than conflict. However, conflict is still at the heart of the strategy embraced by insurers. They have developed relationships with several defendant law firms that continue to challenge claims both within and outside of the GTA. Appendix  C  has details of certain solicitors that routinely act for insurers and who typically define their goal in terms of reducing insurer spend on vehicle damage and credit-hire claims. Whilst ever those firms are actively engaged in reducing the potency of the credit-hire industry, the industry needs a more considered approach. The evidence from the last 40 years show that where insurers find a means to inflict harm on the credit-hire industry they will not hesitate to do so again. That should not come as a surprise, but it does mean that the credit-hire industry must adopt a strategy which is right for when the financial conditions encourage collaboration and co-operation with insurers and potentially right for when they are not. As the industry volume becomes dominated by primary suppliers and the potential for growth is still controlled by tier three referrers, much of the credit-hire industry faces an impending challenge to the future of their business model which they may not yet have fully realised.

The priority for a litigation strategy should probably start with defining how to avoid having to litigate in the first place. Ensuring the internal processes work so that every day of a hire claim is fully supportable is key. Following best practice should result in more claims settling within the GTA or bilateral protocols. The next stage, however, is harder because, when claims do not settle, there is usually a reason. Before rushing to litigate, a credit-hire company must understand that specific obstacle to settlement to determine how it can be defeated. If dealing with the objection relies on evidence, then the CHO must be satisfied that the evidence is powerful enough to succeed. But it is not just a matter of evidence. The costs and risk of litigation need to be factored into the decision process before proceedings are issued. Litigating a claim carries a cost which will increase as the claim progresses to a trial. However, if the claim is issued in the small claim track then most of those costs will not be recovered, even if the claim is successful. Even if the claim is issued in the fast or multi-track then not all the costs are recoverable although if the claim is lost  at trial, the credit-hire company will be faced with their own costs as well as the cost incurred by the Defendant. Add to the equation the fact that it will take more than a year for a claim to reach a trial and the potential attractiveness of any pre-litigation offer to settle must be weighed in the decision of whether litigation will deliver the best outcome available. Of course, the danger for those CHOs that routinely avoid issuing proceedings because of the potential cost risk is that those solicitors acting for insurers will recognise that their strategy of refusing to pay a claim works. DAC Beachcroft and Horwich Farrelly, for example, both claim to have "Know Your Opponent" strategies which supply market insight and benchmarking for all CHOs, something that most credit-hire operators probably do not appreciate. The difficulty for a CHO is that, when they concede a point and write off the claim on economic grounds, those defendant solicitors will record their approach as a success. They will then communicate with their insurer clients about how the strategy worked and do more of the same but on a larger population of claims. That is why litigation must remain a potential choice but only after both the costs and risks are evaluated against any pre-issue offer, something an accountant might show to be a safer and more financially expedient outcome.

Where litigation is unavoidable, the choice of the solicitor to recover any outstanding hire charges is paramount. The hire agreement will usually stipulate the basis on which a solicitor is nominated but, procedurally,  any appointment of a solicitor  will be by the Claimant and he or she will be expected  to sign a retainer with the  nominated solicitor and incur any liability for costs subject always to any arrangements entered into with the solicitor or any undertaking given by the CHO. There are few solicitors in the UK that properly understand credit-hire litigation. For the most part, those that do, have been active for decades and have built associations within the sector for a similar period. Before a CHO embarks on a spate of litigation it is important that they explore the experience of the solicitor and perhaps take references from other CHOs as to their suitability. Some firms are more suited to managing high volumes of low value claims, others are better suited to limited volumes of high value claims and some are helpful for managing strategic issues which require a more considered approach. All of them will expect to generate revenue from their involvement with a CHO and will have a unique perspective on how that revenue is earned. The high-volume processor will deal with claims in the small-claim track, will expect to charge a fixed fee per claim and have disbursements funded and will expect to keep any costs awarded by the court. The low-volume processor will be attracted by fast-track or multi-track claims and so will usually be happy to manage the work based on the costs awarded when they succeed in making a recovery although they will also expect disbursements to be funded. Where a CHO has an ABS relationship with a solicitor then these financial issues become more nuanced.  Typically, the CHO will regard the cost of litigation incurred by the ABS as part of their cost of recovery. They will also recognise any costs awarded in their favour as balancing those irrecoverable costs and the basis on which the ABS will generate a profit for its shareholders. There are risks for a CHO working with a solicitor. Where the Claimant insists on nominating their own solicitor, for exa
articles.source: Stephen Evans, Credit Hire (2020)

articles.title: Setting the Rental Tariff: GTA Rates vs Court Rates
articles.author: Stephen Evans
articles.summary: Explains why setting a defensible rental tariff matters even for GTA-subscribing CHOs, why courts do not treat the GTA rate as the market rate, and how non-GTA operators must establish their rates for litigation. Also explains why the relationship between credit hire rate and basic hire rate is central to quantum disputes.
key_points: Courts do NOT treat the GTA rate as the market rate — it is a non-contractual industry agreement with no empirical basis for judicial rate assessment
Even GTA-subscribing CHOs should maintain a separate commercial rate for litigated claims — the GTA rate is a settlement floor, not a ceiling on what can be claimed
The basic hire rate (BHR) is what a reasonable person would pay in the spot market for a comparable vehicle without credit — this is the court's benchmark
A credit hire rate above BHR is only recoverable if the claimant was impecunious, i.e. unable to fund hire from their own resources
The rental agreement itself defines the rate the CHO can charge — terms must be clear, compliant, and consistent with the rate being claimed
Non-GTA CHOs serving impecunious clients (e.g. Anexo Group) must establish a defensible rate through evidence of their fleet costs and market comparators
An insurer argument that the GTA rate is the correct measure for a non-GTA claim has no legal foundation
The argument that a claimant should have found a cheaper rate ignores the credit component — credit hire provides something a cash hire does not
cases_cited: Lagden v O'Connor
articles.full_text:
Setting a rental tariff

Irrespective of how a credit-hire company obtains its rental fleet, they will have to address the challenge of setting their rental tariff for each group of vehicle they rent. For most CHOs that subscribe to the GTA, there is a logical expectation that claims will settle at the published GTA rates which begs the question of why any other rate setting exercise is needed. There are two possible explanations. The first is that if the claim does not settle at the GTA rate then having a higher commercial rate which will be the basis on which settlement will be sought is, in some way, an incentive for insurers to settle at the lower of the two rates. The flaw in that argument is that if a claim has not settled within the GTA, there is probably a reason and it has nothing to do with the rate charged. The second reason for a commercial rate to be set is because the Courts will not accept the GTA as having any basis in the settlement of litigated hire claims. They recognise that it is a non-contractual industry agreement in which a rate has been agreed. However, that rate has no empirical value in determining the market rate for hiring a car which is the basis on which hire damages will be awarded in contested litigation. As a result, even if the CHO adopts the GTA rate as its commercial rate to be recovered in litigated cases, insurers will still challenge that rate as being the correct measure for determining quantum. It is probably a situation that could be resolved if the credit-hire industry and insurers signed a memorandum of understanding that the GTA rate was agreed as the basis on which  litigated claims should be assessed but, no doubt, that would raise issues about competition and price fixing and, in any event, would remove an opportunity for insurers to continue to try to reduce the value of claims paid. It is important to note, in addition, that there are also CHOs that do not subscribe to the GTA. Whether that is because, like Anexo Group, they deal predominantly with impecunious clients, or because they run a specialist fleet where the GTA tariff does not reflect their fleet mix, they have to establish a rate that they  can support in order to recover their charges.	

The rental agreement and the terms agreed with the hirer are important in defining the rate that a CHO can charge and later recover. As well as ensuring that the agreement is compliant with current regulations, the rental agreement creates a binding contract and defines the rate that the hirer is agreeing to pay and so defines the maximum rate that can be recovered as a consequence of the claim being litigated.  For that reason, and even though most CHO’s will try to settle claims within the terms of the GTA or other bilateral protocols, the rental agreement should set out the maximum commercial rate that the hire company is expecting to charge the customer ahead of recovering those charges from the at-fault insurer and not the GTA rate. The commercial rate should be clearly set out together with any additional charges the hire company makes in relation to the cost of excess waiver, additional driver charges, the mileage allowed in the agreement and the cost of any mileage surcharges or charges for satellite navigation systems or child seats. All these charges must be set out separately on the face of the rental agreement. If those details show the GTA rate, then that will be the maximum level at which hire charges will be awarded following litigation. So how should CHOs set their commercial rates?	The answer to will be determined by the level of commercial reality shown by whoever is setting the rate. Currently, the relevant test of the recoverable rate was defined by Jackson LJ in Stevens and relies heavily on the word ‘reasonable’ which features 32 times. In the judgement it was determined that:

“a proportionate way to arrive at a reasonable approximation to the BHR … must rather be for the lowest reasonable rate quoted by a mainstream supplier for the basic hire of a vehicle of the kind in issue to a reasonable person in the position of the Claimant.”

What constitutes a reasonable person in the position of the Claimant will be a question for the Court based on an evaluation of the facts in each case. Whilst the law regarding the recovery of hire charges has evolved over the past 40 years, the concept of reasonableness is now well enshrined in the vocabulary of those tribunals tasked with resolving credit-hire litigation and so it should equally be at the forefront of the minds of those responsible for setting commercial rates.  The utopian solution would involve the CHO operating a dynamic pricing strategy in much the same way that airlines, hotels and conventional rental companies do when setting their rates with the aim of maximising the rate  they can charge as a function of the difference between supply and demand. The CHO is, however, not allowed to adopt that approach. Instead it is limited to the lowest reasonable rate from a mainstream supplier. However, it is still possible for a CHO to set its rates dynamically but instead of relying on the competing forces of supply and demand, rely on the dynamic rates offered by the conventional rental industry at the time of hire. That means that where the CHO is engaged in collecting daily hire rates from the websites of multiple car rental companies it would make sense to fix their rate for each vehicle consistent with the evidence that will later support a recovery at court. For example, if I were hiring a BMW 320i to a customer in Birmingham on November 12th then I would search mainstream rental companies a few days ahead of the hire start date and look to see what rates were being charged in Birmingham to a reasonable person in the position of the Claimant, with an excess comparable to that which I intend to charge to my customer. I would keep screen shots of each of the quotations from each supplier and append them to the claim file for later reference purposes. I would also base the pricing for that rental transaction on the lowest reasonable rate quoted by a mainstream supplier for the kind of vehicle hired with specific reference to the position of the Claimant as regards their age, driving experience, occupation and excess. It sounds a daunting process but, by following this approach in every case, the CHO will have secured the best evidence available of the Basic Hire Rate and will also have maximised the potential revenue available in each individual transaction. Because rental rates vary based on supply and demand, the type of vehicle, seasonality and general market conditions, the rate obtained for a BMW 320i in Birmingham on November 12th may be higher or lower for the same vehicle in a week’s time. It is, however, a strategy that comes with downside risks as well. If, for example, the rate offered by the mainstream supplier is lower than the GTA rate then that may persuade the insurer not to settle the claim at the GTA rate knowing that they would gain a financial advantage settling the  claim at the commercial rate as soon as the claim was moved out of the GTA protocol.

Absent that approach to underpinning the commercial hire rate on a per transaction basis, it is inevitable that if a claim is eventually litigated then those representing the insurer of the at-fault driver will present information to persuade the court that the commercial rate claimed is not reasonable. Of course, not every CHO has the time or resource to be able to build a forensic snapshot at the time of rental, but there are reputable organisations that do the necessary screen-scraping of rental company websites and do offer access to the data indicating what the reasonable rate might have been at a particular point in history.  Where the majority of a CHOs claims are settled within the GTA or other protocols and litigation is unlikely, then a less rigorous approach to setting the commercial rate might be justifie
articles.source: Stephen Evans, Credit Hire (2020)

articles.title: Treating Customers Fairly: FCA Requirements and Common Pitfalls
articles.author: Stephen Evans
articles.summary: Covers FCA treating customers fairly (TCF) obligations in credit hire, the critical importance of ensuring claimants genuinely understand they have a liability to pay hire charges, and how failures in customer communication are exploited by defendants in litigation to defeat otherwise sound claims.
key_points: A district judge can find hire charges irrecoverable where a claimant believed they were receiving a free courtesy car — even if the written agreement says otherwise
This 'courtesy car' misunderstanding is a recurring pitfall in litigation — it can defeat the claim entirely and is hard to recover on appeal
Recording key telephone conversations with claimants is the best protection against this challenge — the recording proves informed consent
Where recording is not available, a written confirmation or hyperlink to a plain-English explanation of the credit hire arrangement before hire starts is the next best protection
The FCA's TCF principles require that claimants are given adequate information to make an informed decision — this is a regulatory obligation, not just a litigation precaution
Scripts used by claim handlers must be carefully drafted and regularly reviewed — a poor script that leaves claimants confused is a litigation liability
The claimant's first contact experience shapes whether they remain loyal to the CHO or seek alternative advice — engagement and reassurance at FNOL are commercially vital
Contemporaneous records of all key discussions — accident circumstances, need assessment, tariff explanation — are essential to protect every aspect of the claim
articles.full_text:
Treating customers fairly

There are a number of first-instance decisions arising where the at-fault insurer has failed to settle the hire charges because the Claimant was cross-examined and specifically asked whether they were told that they were liable for and would have to pay the hire charges. Where the Claimant admits that they thought the car was a courtesy car, notwithstanding the fact that the rental agreement provides otherwise, a district judge hearing the case can still misdirect himself to ignore the existence of the written contract and, without hearing from a witness from the credit-hire company, rule that the hire charges are not recoverable because the Claimant had no liability to pay them. Those perverse judgements can be overturned on appeal, but they highlight how important the scripting and contractual documentation are when supplying a hire car under credit terms. Where the credit-hire company records all, or at least the most important telephone conversations with their clients, it reduces the risk of this challenge arising in litigation and maximises the opportunity to reverse any perverse decision on appeal. For smaller operators, or those without recording capabilities there is a clear merit in either emailing the client or sending them a hyperlink to a page on a website, a simple explanation of how the credit-hire process works before the transaction is executed. On the last occasion I hired a car after an accident, at the end of the various formalities I was invited to stay on the line whilst a recorded message was played to me. It outlined and clarified the basis of the agreement for the provision of the hire car. I think I gave it about 20 seconds before I hung up but the CHO will have had a log recording the date and time that I started to listen to the message which they could use to demonstrate that they had explained the service fully and completely to me prior to the hire  starting.

In practical terms, as well as persuading Rosie not to speak with anyone else about her accident, the primary role for the individual who has first contact with a potential client is to note the circumstances of the accident,  record the name, address and contact details of all the parties to the accident including any witnesses or police officers that might have attended the scene. At the same time, they should determine if there was any dashcam footage of the incident or if there might have been a camera near to the locus from which footage of the accident circumstances might be obtained. They should also provide the client with an explanation of the options as regards who could repair the damaged car, whether they will claim under their own policy to commence the damage repair process, or whether they would prefer to invite the insurer of the at-fault driver to deal with repairs or prefer the repairs to be funded by way of a credit-repair transaction. The handler should also record details of all other losses sustained by the Claimant. In the event the Claimant’s car is likely to be beyond economic repair then the issues of whether a car might be hired following an accident are reliant on whether the damaged car is legally drivable or, if it could remain drivable if very minor running repairs were carried out. Assuming that the car is not capable of being used then the Claimant is entitled to hire a car, subject always to establishing a need, for such period until they receives a settlement equivalent to the pre-accident value of their damaged car and for a reasonable period thereafter whilst they locate and purchase a replacement. Rosie may pursue a claim for damages for the loss of her vehicle through her own insurer if she is comprehensively insured or she may pursue a claim against the insurer of the at-fault driver. The choice is hers. The former will affect her no claims bonus until such time as the insurer of the at-fault driver concedes that liability for the accident rests with their policyholder. The latter approach, pursuing the third-party insurer, is likely to involve more disagreement as to the proper basis for valuing the damaged car which will, ironically, increase the length of hire and the hire charges that fall to be settled by the at-fault insurer. There are issues that arise from the determination to pursue any claim through the Claimant’s comprehensive cover or through the third-party and specifically how to address the issue of the relevant damages for the value of a car when it is deemed to be a write off. Either choice poses potential conflicts for the CHO and demonstrates why adherence to the doctrine of Treating Customers Fairly is important even at the early stages of formulating a claim.	

Analysis carried out by the credit-hire industry when the CMA were conducting their market investigation revealed that where a CHO assumed responsibility for arranging and funding repairs to the Claimant’s damaged car that any hire period would be shorter than if the policyholder’s own insurance company arranged and effected repairs.  In most instances a consumer might be agnostic as to which route to follow save that any claim made against their own insurer would require them to pay the amount of any policy excess to the repairer at the completion of the repair and recover that money back as part of the claim for damages. Insurers have increased the level of mandatory policy excesses over the last 10 years and some policyholders are prepared to accept added voluntary excesses to enjoy a reduction in the premium. My wife, for example, insures her car and has a compulsory excess of £250 and a voluntary excess of £750 to reduce the premium. If she were involved in an accident for which she was not at-fault, then it would not be unreasonable for her to want to avoid having to pay out an excess of £1,000 and run the risk that it might take a long time to recover back that money from the insurer of the at-fault driver. There is also the added complication that, if she made a claim on her policy, her renewal premium would increase next year simply because she had made a claim and irrespective of who was at-fault. The CHO might prefer not to fund the repair costs by authorising a credit-repair if, because of their efficiency, they inadvertently helped the at-fault insurer by reducing the potential length of hire and associated costs. The referrer, if they were a body repairer, might have a different view.  In a credit-repair transaction they can schedule and control the speed at which the repair is undertaken, charge a labour rate beyond that which an insurer might look to impose and can avoid offering discount on parts and paint. With these various conflicts, in one single event it is possible to perceive a Claimant, an insurer, a repairer and a CHO all with competing priorities.

Where a Claimant’s vehicle is considered beyond economic repair and is not capable of being driven, the challenge of negotiating a fair value for the loss of the vehicle can be problematic for the CHO because most people have an unrealistic expectation of the value of their own car. They might base their expectation on the price that they paid and how well they have looked after their car but the correct approach references the cost at which an equivalent vehicle, determined in terms of age, mileage and specification, can be bought in the open market. Finding evidence to support that value is hard and made harder because there is rarely an exact match on which to draw a comparison. Insurers will tend to take a proscriptive view advancing a value derived from one of the trade guides such as CAP or Glass’s Guide. Those publications show valuations for vehicles based on the exact make and model of the vehicle adjusted for a series of factors including age, mileage and condition but without any increment for the value of extras fitted to the vehicle. Either guide will show the trade price that a dealer might offer to pay to buy such a vehicle. The guid
articles.source: Stephen Evans, Credit Hire (2020)

articles.title: Managing Regulatory Risk and the Value of Expert Counsel
articles.author: Stephen Evans
articles.summary: Explains why regulatory compliance is an existential risk for credit hire operators — how insurers weaponise statutory challenges (Consumer Credit Act 1974, FSMA 2000, cancellation regulations) to defeat entire books of claims, and why proactive investment in competent counsel and industry engagement is essential.
key_points: Regulatory challenges to hire agreements can affect entire books of business simultaneously — a successful challenge can freeze cash flow industry-wide
Insurers will resurrect historic legal arguments even decades after they were defeated — no regulatory challenge should be dismissed as 'settled'
The Consumer Credit Act 1974 has been the primary battleground historically: regulated vs unregulated agreements, exemptions, signing requirements
The Cancellation of Contracts Made at a Consumer's Home or Place of Work Regulations 2008 is a recurring basis for challenge — particularly for agreements executed at the roadside or via telephone
Civil Liability Act 2018 changed the whiplash claims landscape and indirectly affected credit hire referral economics
Future regulatory risk is likely to focus on pre-contractual representations and the acquisition of customers — particularly online and through lead generation
Investment in specialist credit hire counsel is not discretionary — it is essential to drafting compliant agreements and anticipating the next regulatory challenge
Active participation in the CHO trade body provides advance intelligence on emerging regulatory threats and shared approaches to managing them
articles.full_text:
Civil Liability Act 2018

At the time of writing, the MoJ has yet to reveal its plans for the second part of the proposed overhaul of personal injury claims. Part one has already been enacted through the Civil Liability Act 2018 and will have the effect of reducing the quantum of damages arising from a road accident through a tariff of defined compensation levels. Together with other measures which will see a reduction in the fees chargeable by solicitors acting for accident victims in cases where the level of injury is limited to soft tissue damage, the number of solicitors active in the sector is declining rapidly. The desired aim appears to be both fewer solicitors able to supply help to accident victims because of the unfavourable economics and fewer Claimant’s able to pursue a claim without the support of a solicitor. That will result in lower aggregate compensation being paid out with a lower associated cost of claim and a potential windfall to insurers. The Government has, however, looked to hold insurers to account and have promised that any financial benefit arising from the new measures must flow through to policyholders in the form of reduced motor insurance premiums. Beyond those changes, the MoJ had made it clear during the original consultation period that the reform programme included plans to deal with other costs arising from minor claims. The credit-hire sector is waiting to hear more about part two of the intimated reforms which might have a detrimental effect on their business model.

Since at least 1990, credit-hire has irritated the insurance industry. They have made repeated allegations that the costs associated with credit-hire claims are out of control and that the basis on which claims are pursued advances the commercial interests of the CHO ahead of the true interests of the Claimant. The CHO trade body is focused on potential further developments which may follow from part two of the work being undertaken by the MoJ. Likewise, I am sure that those acting for insurers are equally focused on the opportunity they might have to launch a further attack on the credit-hire sector. If only for this reason, every CHO must ensure that they have a resource dedicated to reviewing and managing a response to any output from the MoJ or from other regulators that affect the sector, specifically the FCA. And whilst referencing acronyms, it is just as important for every CHO to understand the role performed by the ABI GTA and the GTA Technical Committee and the way the Technical Committee has the power to limit the growth of the sector.

The GTA and other issues

The ABI GTA is a voluntary non-binding protocol. Most insurers and a majority of CHOs subscribe to the protocol. It sets out the terms and conditions which subscribers must adhere to and, at any point in time, it specifies the maximum recoverable rate of hire, effectively the GTA rate, for a replacement vehicle provided to a Claimant when the subscribing insurer has insured the party deemed to be at-fault for an accident. 9 of the 10 largest motor insurers subscribe to the GTA. The CHO estimated that approximately 80% of credit-hire and credit-repair claims are settled under the GTA or through bilateral protocols based on it. That must be a major endorsement of its value.	 

The CMA looked at the GTA during its 2-year investigation into the private motor insurance market in 2012. It found that the protocol had 2 main effects. Firstly, it directly reduced both transactional and frictional costs compared with the level that would prevail absent the agreement. It provided a framework for CHOs to provide replacement vehicles charged at a pre-negotiated daily rate agreed by insurers  and then reduced transactional and frictional costs by providing for more streamlined processes related to the acceptance of a claim, notification to the third-party insurer, management of the repair, and submission of a payment pack. They also concluded that it had the tendency to encourage early agreement on liability. That further reduced transactional and frictional costs and reduced the rates claimed by CHOs below the level that would otherwise have been claimed, based on evidence from historic court awards. In summary, the CMA determined that the GTA contributed to a significant reduction in the number of litigated replacement vehicle cases.  Subscribing to the GTA is a positive step for most credit-hire organisations although there are some CHOs who do not recognise any benefit. This is usually because they supply vehicles within a specialist niche or because they work in a limited geographic area where the guidance on rates that the GTA offers is less helpful. Membership of the GTA is not automatic, however, and there are some hurdles to overcome before a CHO can subscribe. The application process has changed over the years. Originally it required a formal application to the ABI with supporting evidence from the CHO to show that they had protocols with at least 1 insurer, and that they had the ability to set up protocols with others, on a national basis. Thereafter an insurer appointed by the ABI would visit the CHO. They would review the mechanisms in place to assess the CHOs ability to work within the terms of the GTA and would review any scripts or text used in standard communications. They would also look at the strength of internal processes for reviewing a claim and for checking the progress of a repair to control the length of any associated hire transaction. In addition, they would assess the quality of management systems and seek the CHOs agreement to work within the terms of the GTA and to allow for their business to be audited for compliance in future. The site work associated with inspecting new GTA applications now falls largely on an independent auditor. They make a recommendation, but the final decision on whether to admit a new subscriber is made by the GTA Technical Committee. That committee includes members of both the insurance industry and the credit-hire industry and has an independent secretary.

I went through the application process in around 2002 and I later sat on the GTA Technical Committee for about 11 years from 2005. I can remember the individual from Liverpool Victoria who carried out the review and I recognised then the value of obtaining GTA membership as, I suspect every other GTA subscriber does. However, in 2011, a few insurers expressed   concern that CHOs were not committed to the spirit and the letter of the GTA. They felt they were paying out more in hire charges than they should pay for settling individual claims. The concern was expressed  anecdotally and generally, rather than against  any specific CHO, and the allegation was eventually limited to concerns that either the wrong vehicle was being routinely supplied and so the associated hire rates were too high, or else the length of individual hire accounts were exaggerated because the associated repair period was not adequately managed.
