articles.filename: Dealing with Basic Hire Rate Evidence.docx
articles.title: Dealing with Basic Hire Rate Evidence: A Practical Guide to Challenging Defendant BHR Reports
articles.author: Stephen Evans
articles.author_role: Credit hire practitioner and author of 'Credit Hire' (96 SAE Ltd)
articles.summary: A comprehensive practical guide by Stephen Evans (May 2025) on challenging Basic Hire Rate (BHR) evidence served by defendant insurers in credit hire claims. The presentation covers the legal framework established by Stevens v Equity and McBride (BHR is the lowest reasonable rate from a mainstream supplier, assessed objectively); the correct approach to nil excess/CDW comparisons; why defendant rate reports are frequently unreliable (wrong dates, wrong locations, wrong vehicles, selective quoting, broker intermediaries); the restrictions imposed by mainstream hire companies that affect comparability (age, penalty points, maximum hire periods, deposit requirements); and the strategic options for challenging rate provider organisations including Arbitrate, BHR Assist, WhichRate, BHR Compare and VeriRate. The document draws extensively on the author's experience with the Autofocus fraud litigation as a model for systemic challenge.
key_points: The legal test for BHR (Stevens v Equity para 36, confirmed in McBride): the court should identify the lowest reasonable rate quoted by a mainstream supplier in the claimant's geographical area for the type of car actually hired. If there is no mainstream supplier, the lowest rate from a local reputable supplier. The exercise is objective — it is not about what the individual claimant knew, could have done, or would have chosen.

Mainstream suppliers are generally accepted to be Avis, Europcar, Enterprise, Hertz and similar national operators. For prestige or EV vehicles, the category of 'mainstream' is less settled. Challenge 'locally reputable' suppliers via Companies House searches (trading history, profitability), Trustpilot ratings, and internet reviews. Rentalcars.com, Expedia, Booking.com, Enjoy Car Rentals, Economy Bookings and Easy Rent are booking brokers with no fleet or UK rental presence — they are not rental companies and cannot be 'mainstream suppliers'. Rentalcars.com has an average Trustpilot score of 2.2/5.

Nil excess / CDW comparison — a common error: if the CHO provides a vehicle with zero excess (after CDW charges), the correct BHR comparator is also a zero-excess rate. Rate providers often present a lower rate with a non-reducible excess and treat a Questor excess reimbursement policy as equivalent. That is wrong: (1) Questor is not a contractual reduction of the excess — the hirer must still pay the excess upfront and then claim back; (2) there is no guarantee of recovery under Questor; (3) the hirer would also need to fund the deposit. Clayton v EUI (para 105) confirms that where a standalone excess elimination product (Questor, Insurance4carhire.com) is available, courts should admit it as evidence of the reasonable cost of a nil excess — but only where the zero-excess rate itself is genuinely unavailable.

Where zero-excess rates are available from mainstream suppliers (Avis, Budget, Europcar, Arnold Clark, Hertz, Sixt all offer zero excess dependent on vehicle), rate providers often omit them to present a lower headline rate. If your CHO offers zero excess and a mainstream supplier also offers zero excess, the comparison is zero-excess vs zero-excess — the Questor argument does not arise. Per Stevens para 79: 'only the lowest reasonable basic hire rate (including the cost of a nil excess) will be recoverable' where the defendant has demonstrated it is less than the CHR.

Rate timing is routinely manipulated: defendant rate providers typically obtain quotes for a hire start date several days or weeks in the future (or in the past), because same-day rates are almost always higher than advance-booking rates. The correct date is the actual hire commencement date, quoted on that day (for undriveable vehicles) or for the confirmed start date (for roadworthy vehicles where date is known). The price difference across a single day between two nearby locations can be over 21%. Always obtain your own contemporaneous screenshot of the rate for the correct vehicle, correct location, correct date — even as a basic sense check.

Pay Now vs Pay Later rates: BHR rates have historically been advertised on one of two bases. The 'Pay Now' rate — typically the cheapest headline figure — requires the hirer to prepay at the time of booking, restricts the right to cancel, and crucially requires a commitment to the full anticipated hire period at the outset. If a bodyshop delays ordering parts and the hire start date shifts, or if repairs finish earlier than anticipated, the hirer loses the unused prepaid element. Equally, if the hire runs longer than expected, the rate for any extension is unknown at the time of booking. There is no equivalence between a 'Pay Now' rate and a credit hire rate. The correct comparator has always been the 'Pay Later' rate, which requires a deposit based on the daily rate at the start of hire but allows flexibility to delay, cancel or extend the hire at the prevailing daily rate booked at the outset. An additional challenge now is that many conventional rental companies advertise rates on a 'From...' basis — showing the lowest possible rate a rental might be charged at. The actual price is only revealed at the point of confirmation, where differentiating prices emerge based on timing of payment or commitment to a fixed term. Those scrutinising BHR evidence should always establish whether the rate cited is a genuine 'Pay Later' rate or an artificially low 'Pay Now' or 'From...' rate that would not have been available to a credit hire claimant on equivalent terms.

Rate providers (Arbitrate, BHR Assist, WhichRate, BHR Compare, VeriRate) market their services as covering 'any vehicle, any postcode, any day' and claim databases of 150-200 million rows. However: (1) their data has unexplained gaps; (2) they are selective about which rates they include/exclude; (3) they are instructed by and paid by defendant insurers; (4) they use queries designed to produce low rates. When WhichRate and BHR Compare produce different results for the same day, both cannot be correct. The strategic approach is to insist the rate witness attends for cross-examination rather than relying on a Civil Evidence Act notice.

Vehicle type and comparability: defendant rate reports often quote rates for a vehicle category without specifying the model. A 'BMW 3 Series' rate could be for a 316 or a 330 — very different vehicles. Ask the rate witness which specific model was quoted. GTA vehicle groups were negotiated between CHOs and insurers for the GTA protocol; they have no role in hostile litigation and do not reflect how the rental industry categorises vehicles. One case involved Arbitrate suggesting a Hyundai Ioniq 5 EV as a replacement for a Toyota Corolla Diesel — manifestly wrong. Always check that the claimant's specific need (estate, automatic, EV, higher seating, tow bar, pet-friendly) is reflected in the BHR comparator — Europcar, for example, prohibits animals.

Age and licence restrictions limit the pool of comparable suppliers significantly. Sixt/Thrifty: minimum age 21 (small/economy only). Europcar: minimum 22. Hertz: minimum 23. Avis/Budget: minimum 23. Arnold Clark: minimum 23 (2-year licence). Enterprise/Alamo/National: minimum 25 for ALL vehicles. Where the claimant is under 25 (and particularly under 23), Enterprise — one of the most commonly cited mainstream suppliers — cannot be used as a comparator at all. Young driver surcharges also apply and must be included in any comparison. Zero excess is unavailable to under-25s at Sixt (minimum 25) and Europcar (minimum 26).

Penalty point restrictions further narrow comparability: Arnold Clark, Avis, Budget, Sixt, Europcar: maximum 6 points. Thrifty: maximum 9 points. Hertz: maximum 11 points (across no more than 2 convictions). Enterprise (only): no points restriction for drivers over 25. Sixt will not hire to under-24s with any convictions. Where a claimant has penalty points, many 'mainstream' suppliers become unavailable and must be removed from the BHR comparison — always check the claimant's licence.

Long hire periods: where hire exceeds 28-30 days, many mainstream suppliers require the vehicle to be returned and a new agreement entered into. Thrifty/Hertz/Avis/Budget: maximum 30 consecutive days. Sixt/Europcar: 84 days. Enterprise: 28 days retail (then 14-day cooling-off period). If the BHR rate comes from a supplier who would have required the claimant to return the vehicle mid-hire, re-enter a new agreement and potentially pay a further deposit, that supplier is not a genuine comparator for the full hire period. The available daily rate may also differ for a new agreement.

Security deposit requirements are often ignored in BHR comparisons. For a mainstream car the deposit can be as low as £20, but for an Audi A4 from Sixt it is £1,600. Where a claimant is impecunious or has limited financial resources, the requirement to pre-pay a substantial deposit (in addition to the hire charges and excess) may make a BHR rate in practice unavailable to them — supporting impecuniosity arguments or at minimum reducing the weight of the rate evidence.

Strategic options for challenging BHR evidence: (1) Obtain your own contemporaneous rate screenshot pre-hire or immediately on hire start — free, and provides a direct comparator. (2) On receipt of defendant's rate report, conduct your own review: are quoted suppliers genuinely mainstream/locally reputable? Check Trustpilot. Are the rates contemporaneous? Is the vehicle truly comparable? (3) Challenge the rate provider's methodology in correspondence and insist the witness attends trial rather than relying on a CEA notice. (4) For a strategic industry-wide approach, identify the weakest rate provider and run a targeted, forensic challenge across selected cases — as was done successfully against Autofocus. 'Shoot one and frighten thousands.'
cases_cited: Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management Ltd [2015] EWCA Civ 93
McBride v UKI [2017] EWCA Civ 144
Clayton v EUI [2017] EWCA Civ 144
Bent v Highways and Utilities Construction [2011] EWCA Civ 1384
Bee v Jenson [2007] EWCA Civ 923
Dickinson v Tesco [2013] EWCA Civ 36
articles.full_text:
Dealing with Basic Hire Rate Evidence – a presentation by Stephen Evans March 2026
I want to set out my understanding of the current legal position, and this is a summary of where we are.

The starting point is found in the two cases of Stevens and McBride and the key passage in Stevens is paragraph 36 which provides a reasonable starting summary:
“It follows that a judge faced with a range of hire rates should try to identify the rate or rates for the hire, in the claimant’s geographical area, of the type of car actually hired by the claimant on credit hire terms. If that exercise yields a single rate, then that rate is likely to be a reasonable approximation for the BHR. If, on the other hand, it yields a range of rates then a reasonable estimate of the BHR may be obtained by identifying the lowest reasonable rate quoted by a mainstream supplier or, if there is no mainstream supplier, by a local reputable supplier.”
If the defendant identifies a range of rates, then the court should adopt the lowest reasonable rate from a mainstream supplier. If there is no mainstream supplier, the court should adopt the lowest reasonable rate from a reputable local supplier.
The most important bit to take on board about the decision in Stevens is that it mandates that the test to be applied in determining the appropriate rate is an objective one. It is not about what the client could have done, what he knew, whether he would have gone to supplier A or B, it is about evidence of the BHR to serve as the basis for stripping out the irrecoverable benefits in the credit hire rate at the time of hire. That point is important because hire rates will vary depending on when a booking is made. The rate evidence providers instructed by insurers will typically obtain hire rates for a hire start several days in the future because they know that rates obtained for the day the hire starts, and obtained on that day, will usually be higher. 

The decision in McBride re-affirmed this position. They also refused permission to appeal against the findings that Cortec, Dream Car Hire, and Premiere Velocity  were mainstream suppliers. They held that this was a finding of fact open to the Judge to make at first instance and not something to be disturbed on appeal.
The court did, however, address the nil excess point which, until that point was an issue of confusion. The Court held that where the evidence demonstrates that the BHR is cheaper than the credit hire rate (without considering excess waivers) then that credit hire rate contains additional benefits which should be stripped out. That ordinary car hire companies are not prepared to offer a nil excess should not “be used as a smokescreen to enable credit hire companies to recover their charges in full.”

The relevant paragraph (paragraph 76) continues:
“…where a nil excess is not available from car hire companies, the correct approach is to treat the nil excess separately from the comparison exercise between the default credit hire rate and the basic hire rate with an excess. It will almost invariably be the case that it was reasonable for the claimant to seek a nil excess for the reasons given in Bee v Jenson and, on that hypothesis, the only question for the Court will be how much should be recoverable as the cost of purchasing a nil excess.”
This is an important issue many CHOs get wrong.
If you offer a car with zero excess (after your CDW charges are applied – and the average cost for that is c £35 per day in BHR world) then the right rate for comparison purposes is a BHR rate with zero excess after the CDW charges have been applied.
Many rate experts will not disclose the zero excess quotes from Europcar and Sixt, for example, because there might be a lower rate with a non-reducible excess. D will then argue that as the correct BHR and argue that the excess is dealt with by the daily cost for Questor. That is a deceit. In addition, at the very minimum, if there genuinely is no zero-excess mainstream provider then it’s the BHR rate and your CDW charge that matters.
The logic to be applied was then clarified in the appeal in Clayton (paragraph 105):
“I consider that where there is evidence of the availability of an excess elimination insurance as a stand-alone product from Questor or other providers such as Insurance4carhire.com, the Courts should admit and accept such evidence as evidence of the reasonable cost of obtaining a nil excess, provided of course that the quote obtained from such a provider is for a car which is comparable with the one hired from the credit hire company and is for the same period as the period of actual hire from the credit hire company. Certainly, the Court should not reject such evidence because the judge or the claimant has not heard of the product, as the district judge did here. The exercise is an objective one and such evidence should be admissible irrespective of the subjective knowledge or lack of it of the Court or the claimant. Information about the availability of such products can, in any event, be readily accessed on the internet. It is apparent that, whilst some judges in the County Courts have not been admitting such evidence, others have: see the ﬁndings of the judge in one of the cases appealed in Dickinson v Tesco [2013] EWCA Civ 36; [2013] RTR 27 referred to at [26] of the judgment of Aikens LJ. The admission and acceptance of evidence of these stand-alone products should be the norm.”

If the BHR relied on have quoted for a nil excess, then there is no further issue because, per paragraph 79 in Stevens, “only the lowest reasonable basic hire rate (including in such cases the cost of a nil excess) will be recoverable, on the assumption that the defendant has demonstrated that this is less than the charges of the credit hire company.”
So where does that leave us?
Simply, the burden is on the defendant to establish the BHR and the task then facing the court is to try and identify the BHR for the type of vehicle that the Claimant hired (or a reasonable equivalent).
The best evidence is evidence of the right vehicle at the right time and at the right location. But defendants will probably argue that the reference in Stevens to a ‘reasonable approximation’ and a ‘degree of imprecision’ when read with the statement in Bent 1 by Jacobs LJ that one should not be ‘hypnotised’ by an exactly comparable vehicle suggest that it is open to the Court to accept rate evidence even if it does not exactly replicate the original hire.
Comments in McBride support this approach when the exercise of determining a rate is referred to as being ‘approximate and artificial’ and refusing to criticise the trial Judge for estimating percentage judgments to the daily hire rates he was presented with.
Importantly, perhaps, although the guidance in Stevens and McBride suggests the Court should adopt the ‘lowest reasonable rate’ there is a base for Claimants to argue that the word ‘reasonable’ qualifies the rates that the court should adopt, discarding any rates that impose a high excess, high deposit or onerous conditions
Beyond that is the issue of whether a company qualifies as a ‘mainstream supplier’ or ‘locally reputable supplier’.
It is probably accepted that names such as Avis, Europcar, Enterprise and Hertz are the ‘mainstream’ suppliers. But who are the mainstream suppliers for prestige or electric vehicles, and what type of business qualifies as a ‘locally reputable supplier’.  

Probably worth looking at how long they have been trading, whether they are profitable (do a search at Companies House), what their Trust Pilot rating is like and whether there are example of how they are not reputable. This is an issue of fact and so it is arguable, but it needs coherent evidence to support the allegation. Those of you working with Steve Perry or Dean Robert should ask them to include this point in any rebuttals.
Rentalcars, for example, have 155,000 review on Trustpilot. Almost 35% of them are just 2 Star or w
articles.source: Stephen Evans, 'Dealing with Basic Hire Rate Evidence' — presentation, May 2025 (© 96 SAE Ltd)

articles.title: What is a BHR Rebuttal? A Practical Guide to Constructing and Deploying Rebuttal Evidence
articles.author: Stephen Evans
articles.author_role: Credit hire practitioner and author of 'Credit Hire' (96 SAE Ltd)
articles.summary: A practical guide by Stephen Evans (seminar notes, March 2015, principles remain current) on what a BHR rebuttal is and how to construct one effectively. A BHR rebuttal is a claim-specific witness statement presented to contradict the defendant's rate evidence; it is confined solely to the subject matter of the evidence rebutted. It must evidence the rate for the hire, in the claimant's geographical area, for the type of car actually hired, from a mainstream supplier (or reputable local supplier where mainstream is unavailable), available to a reasonable person in the claimant's position. The guide draws on the CMA's 2015 investigation into short-term car rental (a separate report from the 2014 PMI investigation) to demonstrate that headline screen-print rates are unreliable and do not reflect true costs — because mandatory extras are added at pick-up, fuel policies are opaque, and damage charges are often added post-rental. Practical guidance covers: template construction, knowing the defendant's rate provider, obtaining telephone call recordings, Pay Now vs Pay Later rate distinctions, and use of the Wayback Machine to retrieve historic terms and conditions.
key_points: A BHR rebuttal is a claim-specific witness statement to contradict the defendant's rate evidence. It is confined to the subject matter of the evidence being rebutted — new evidence on unrelated subjects cannot be introduced in rebuttal. It is the only document through which the claimant can present a counter-rate to the judge.

A rebuttal is essential because: (1) a barrister cannot give evidence on the claimant's behalf — evidence must come from a witness; (2) without a rebuttal, the defendant's rate evidence is the only rate evidence before the court; (3) judges need something to hang their judgment on — absent a rebuttal, they will use the defendant's figures; (4) a well-constructed rebuttal consistently generates enhanced pre-trial settlement offers.

The rebuttal must positively evidence five elements (Stevens v Equity framework): (1) the rate for the hire; (2) in the claimant's geographical area; (3) for the type of car actually hired by the claimant on credit terms; (4) from a mainstream supplier (or reputable local supplier if no mainstream supplier is available); (5) available to a reasonable person in the claimant's position (including their age, licence history, penalty points and any other personal characteristics affecting eligibility).

Use the CMA's 2015 investigation into short-term car rental (EU/UK) to undermine the reliability of defendant screen-print evidence. The CMA found: headline rates are misleading because mandatory extras are added at the pick-up desk; 56% of complaints concerned damage deposits and unexplained post-rental charges; 19% concerned lack of price transparency in terms and conditions. The five major operators (Avis-Budget, Enterprise, Europcar, Hertz, Sixt) were all investigated and all made undertakings to improve transparency. Screen-print rates therefore do not represent the true cost the claimant would have paid.

Hertz's own published terms state: 'Vehicle rental rates vary considerably from place to place and from time to time, and they change very frequently... a rate quote you receive is merely indicative of the rates then being offered and creates neither legal rights nor practical expectations.' This is powerful evidence that any historic screen-print rate cited by the defendant is merely indicative and may not have been available or applicable at the actual time of hire.

Pay Now vs Pay Later rates: 'Pay Now' rates (often the cheapest headline rates cited by defendant experts) require the hirer to prepay at the time of booking, restrict the right to cancel, and crucially require a commitment to the full anticipated hire period at the outset. If the bodyshop delays ordering parts and the hire start date shifts, or if repairs are shorter than anticipated, the hirer loses the unused prepaid charges. If the hire is longer than anticipated, the rate for any extension is unknown at the time of booking. The correct comparator is always the 'Pay Later' rate, which requires a deposit at the start but allows flexibility to delay, cancel or extend at the prevailing daily rate. Watch for this especially where Europcar, Avis, Hertz and Enterprise are cited. A further challenge: many rental companies now advertise rates on a 'From...' basis — showing the lowest possible rate — with the actual price only revealed at confirmation depending on payment timing and commitment to a fixed term. Always verify whether the rate in the defendant's evidence is a genuine 'Pay Later' rate or an artificially low 'Pay Now' or 'From...' headline figure.

Telephone call recordings: where the defendant's rate expert claims to have made telephone calls to verify rates or availability, always request the recordings. They do not always agree to send them (often served a day or two before trial) and they frequently do not match the written evidence. Mention the failure to provide recordings in the rebuttal itself. Consider a supplemental statement after call logs are eventually received.

Rebuttal construction — practical steps: (1) create a standard template capturing the material hire terms (hirer's postcode, exact hire dates, period, actual vehicle hired, excess on hire car, CDW/excess waivers, extras, additional drivers, driver age, convictions, prior accidents); (2) know the defendant's rate provider — research their methodology and known weaknesses; (3) build a resource of terms, conditions and rental agreements for each mainstream supplier; (4) use web.archive.org (Wayback Machine) to retrieve historic terms and conditions that were in force at the time of hire.

Each rebuttal takes approximately two hours to construct properly. The investment is justified because a well-evidenced rebuttal: undermines the credibility of the defendant's rate expert if their evidence is repeatedly challenged and fails; makes the defendant's rate surveyor's job harder across all cases; and in the author's experience makes the whole process 'quite good fun' when the other side's dishonesty is exposed.
cases_cited: Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management Ltd [2015] EWCA Civ 93
articles.full_text:
What is a BHR Rebuttal – Stephen Evans March 2015
A rebuttal is a form of evidence that is presented to contradict or nullify other evidence that has been presented by an adverse party.
In law, special rules apply to rebuttal; rebuttal evidence must be confined solely to the subject matter of the evidence rebutted.
New evidence on other subjects may not be brought in rebuttal.
It is a claim-specific witness statement which is
Essential if your barrister wants to argue against the evidence adduced by the Defendant,
The only document which might persuade a judge that, on the balance of probabilities, D has not discharged their evidential burden
From experience, likely to generate enhanced offers from D before trial, and
It needs to be constructed well to decimate the credibility of their evidence.
It is important if:

You want to recover your hire charges in full;
Your barrister can never give evidence on your behalf;
Absent a rebuttal, D’s evidence will all the judge has to form his view on rate;
Not all judges hate credit hire companies but they have to have something to hang their judgement on;
It makes the job of D’s rate surveyor harder if their evidence is consistently challenged and repeatedly fails;
Because it’s quite good fun making the other side look dishonest.
You need to ensure your barrister understands what you are doing;
You need to identify who will provide your rebuttal evidence;
Expect each rebuttal to take a couple of hours;
Create a template so you know the material terms of hire (hirer’s postcode, dates of hire, period of hire, actual car hired, excess on hire car, excess waivers, other extras, additional drivers, driver experience – age, convictions and other accidents);
Know your enemy (more later);
Build a resource of terms, conditions, rental agreements and other documents and use http://www.waybackmachine.org where necessary.
BHR Rebuttals have to evidence
The rate or rates for the hire,
In the claimant’s geographical area,
Of the type of car actually hired by the claimant on credit terms,
By a mainstream supplier (or a reputable local supplier IF a mainstream supplier is not available),
To a reasonable person in the position of the claimant.
The concept of providing a rental tariff to establish a headline rate was superficially attractive until the Competition and Markets Authority (“CMA”) conducted an investigation and issued their report into short-term car rental in the European Union and the UK.
I have researched the area of hire extensively and am aware of a report issued on 13th July 2015 by the Competition and Markets Authority (“CMA”) (“The CMA Report”), which reports on the conclusions of their investigation into short-term car rental in the European Union.  This is a 69 page report which can be found at https://assets.digital.cabinet-office.gov.uk/media/55a3cdd940f0b62889000001/Short-term_car_rental_in_the_EU.pdf and rather than exhibiting a full copy, I will provide extracts within this statement.
Commenting on their review of the UK Car Rental sector, at paragraph 1.3 of the report the CMA said:
“Our review found that the main areas of concern related to:
A lack of transparency about the total price when making a booking, particularly when additional charges were only revealed to the consumer at the pick-up desk;
A lack of transparency of rental contract terms and conditions;
The way in which additional waiver and insurance products are sold by car  rental companies;
A lack of transparency and potential unfairness of some car rental companies’ fuel policies;
How vehicle damage is assessed and charged for and how disputes are dealt with; and
Additional payments being taken from the consumer’s credit or debit card after the rental period without adequate prior notification, explanation or supporting evidence.”  (emphasis added)
These points have particular relevance in this case where the evidence of Mr. Pollard is entirely reliant on information contained on web screens adduced in evidence without any further exploration or exposition of the detail or other obligations that might sit behind those screens.
The CMA found, inter alia, (paragraph 4.3 of their report) a lack of transparency of pricing: 

“for example, where the headline price may appear low, but extra compulsory charges are added during the booking process or at the pick-up desk meaning the final price can be much higher”.
At paragraph 5.7 they expressed concern that 

“the practice of initially quoting a headline price then subsequently adding additional charges during the booking journey can impair consumers’ ability to compare prices. They may ‘anchor’ to the headline price, whilst taking insufficient account of the later additional costs.”
They also identified concerns about 

“the way in which additional waiver and insurance products are sold by car rental companies, particularly at the pick-up desk… and the lack of transparency and potential unfairness of some car rental companies’ fuel policies and associated charges”.
In addition, they expressed concern about 

“the level of additional payments being taken from the consumer’s credit or debit card at the end of the rental period, for example to cover vehicle damage or traffic fines, without any prior notification, explanation or supporting evidence”.
In the Annex to the report they identified that
19% of the complaints that they investigated related to lack of clarity on the rental terms and conditions and price transparency concerns;
7% related to concerns over fuel and waivers and insurance;
56% related to disputes over vehicle damage, deposits and unexplained payments taken and failure to refund, and
18% relating to the consumer not being supplied the vehicle they had booked/reserved or no vehicle being supplied at all and vehicle safety and mechanical issues.
The investigation involved five car rental companies (Avis-Budget, Enterprise Rent-a-Car, Europcar, Hertz and Sixt), which currently account for over 80% of UK rentals. All of the companies participating in the investigation, together with the industry trade body, the British Vehicle Leasing and Rental Association (“BVRLA”), have put forward voluntary proposals to address the issues identified by the CMA in the investigation.
In light of the purpose to which the Defendant is utilising screen prints to seek to persuade the Court what it would cost to hire a car in 2014 the reliability and completeness of those screen prints is pivotal.
To provide a sense of perspective as to the reliability of using rates from a different period in order to gauge whether the same rates applied in March 2015, I exhibit a screen grab from the Hertz website which provides at page 19

“Vehicle rental rates vary considerably from place to place and from time to time, and they change very frequently. You may contact Hertz to inquire about rates for vehicle rentals through a variety of channels, including reservations telephone lines, websites and computerized reservations systems such as those utilized by travel agents. Unless it is provided in connection with certain types of reservations, a rate quote you receive is merely indicative of the rates then being offered and creates neither legal rights nor practical expectations of vehicle availability or the continued applicability of the quoted rate“ (emphasis added).
From my employment as Group Marketing Director at United Kenning Rental group (where I was Group Marketing Director) I would express a similar view. Supply and demand (often even intra day demand), seasonality, regionality, climate and the economy all had the capacity to influence the movement of rates materially and frequently at Kenning.
Telephone calls - Always ask for the recordings if the bhr expert claims to have made calls to verify details and listen to them. They don’t always agree to send them and often serve them a day or so before trial. They often do not match the written evidence.
Mention the lack of response in the rebuttal.
Consi
articles.source: Stephen Evans, 'What is a BHR Rebuttal' — seminar notes, March 2015 (© 96 SAE Ltd)

articles.title: Credit Hire Counsel and Chambers Directory

articles.content:
This entry provides a directory of barristers (counsel) and chambers who specialise in credit hire litigation and are recommended by the Credit Hire Forum. Users can contact them directly via their profile links below. This list is suitable for claimant solicitors, CHOs and insurers seeking counsel with credit hire expertise.

## BARRISTERS / COUNSEL

### Kings Bench Chambers
- **Allyn Winchester** (Kings Bench Chambers, called 2002, 545+ cases handled, exclusively claimant work). Geographic coverage: Country wide. Profile: https://www.kingsbench.co.uk/members/allyn-winchester-barrister
- **Bobby Shabbir** (Kings Bench Chambers, called 2001, 275+ cases handled, exclusively claimant work). Geographic coverage: Country wide. Profile: https://www.kingsbench.co.uk/members/bobby-shabir
- **Christopher Gair** (Kings Bench Chambers, called 2000, 636+ cases handled, exclusively claimant work). Geographic coverage: Country wide. Profile: https://www.kingsbench.co.uk/members/christopher-gair-barrister
- **Christopher Oseykeyre** (Kings Bench Chambers, called 2017, 266+ cases handled, exclusively claimant work). Geographic coverage: Country wide. Profile: https://www.kingsbench.co.uk/members/christopher-owusu-sekyere
- **Elinor Hoile** (Kings Bench Chambers, called 2008, 1171+ cases handled, exclusively claimant work). Geographic coverage: Country wide. Profile: https://www.kingsbench.co.uk/members/elinor-hoile-barrister
- **Justin Shale** (Kings Bench Chambers, called 1982, exclusively claimant work). Geographic coverage: country wide. Profile: https://www.kingsbench.co.uk/members/justin-shale-barrister
- **Kathy Anderson** (Kings Bench Chambers, called 1997, 551+ cases handled, exclusively claimant work). Geographic coverage: Country wide. Profile: https://www.kingsbench.co.uk/members/kathleen-anderson-barrister
- **Mark Maitland** (Kings Bench Chambers, called 1988, 769+ cases handled, exclusively claimant work). Geographic coverage: country wide. Profile: https://www.kingsbench.co.uk/members/marc-maitland-barrister
- **Michael Cameron Mowat** (Kings Bench Chambers, called 2017, 481+ cases handled, exclusively claimant work). Geographic coverage: Country wide. Profile: https://www.kingsbench.co.uk/members/michael-cameron-mowat
- **Neil Hinton** (Kings Bench Chambers, called 1997, 380+ cases handled, exclusively claimant work). Geographic coverage: Country wide. Profile: https://www.kingsbench.co.uk/members/neil-hinton-barrister
- **Neil Johnstone** (Kings Bench Chambers, called 2015, 447+ cases handled, exclusively claimant work). Geographic coverage: Country wide. Profile: https://www.kingsbench.co.uk/members/neil-johnstone
- **Neil Whittle** (Kings Bench Chambers, called 2018, 495+ cases handled, exclusively claimant work). Geographic coverage: country wide. Profile: https://www.kingsbench.co.uk/members/nick-white
- **Nick White** (Kings Bench Chambers, called 2000, exclusively claimant work). Geographic coverage: Country wide. Profile: https://www.kingsbench.co.uk/members/nick-white
- **Pete Bush** (Kings Bench Chambers, called 2008, exclusively claimant work). Geographic coverage: Country wide. Profile: https://www.kingsbench.co.uk/members/peter-bush-barrister
- **Rod Hine** (Kings Bench Chambers, called 1985, 177+ cases handled, exclusively claimant work). Geographic coverage: country wide. Profile: https://www.kingsbench.co.uk/members/roderick-hine-barrister
- **Stuart MacDonald** (Kings Bench Chambers, called 2009, 420+ cases handled, exclusively claimant work). Geographic coverage: country wide. Profile: https://www.kingsbench.co.uk/members/stuart-macdonald

### Ropewalk Chambers
