articles.title: FCA Regulatory Priorities: Insurance (February 2026) — Implications for Credit Hire
articles.author: Financial Conduct Authority
articles.source: FCA insurance-report.pdf
articles.content:
This entry summarises the FCA's Regulatory Priorities: Insurance report (February 2026) and identifies the threads most relevant to credit hire practitioners, CHOs and claimant solicitors.

## Overview

Published in February 2026, this report replaces the FCA's previous portfolio letters. It sets out the FCA's annual regulatory priorities for the insurance sector, directed at retail insurers, insurance intermediaries, price comparison websites, and life insurers. Credit hire is not mentioned by name, but several priorities intersect directly with credit hire practice.

## What the FCA said about claims handling (most relevant to credit hire)

The FCA's lead priority for 2026 is improving consumer understanding, claims handling and service quality. The FCA's stated expectations of regulated firms are that they must:

- Tell consumers clearly what their insurance cover includes and test that they understand it;
- Respond promptly, fairly and transparently to claims and queries;
- Deliver good outcomes when consumers claim;
- Monitor whether their products and services are actually delivering what was promised.

This is directly relevant to credit hire. The FCA has signalled — following the Which? super-complaint — that it is actively reviewing how insurers manage claims. Any insurer that fails to explain a claimant's entitlement to a replacement vehicle (whether under their own comprehensive policy or as a third-party claimant) risks regulatory scrutiny for breach of Consumer Duty. CHOs that are FCA-authorised and regulated are subject to equivalent obligations.

## Motor Total Loss underpayment — 270,000 drivers

The FCA confirmed it had tackled underpayment of motor total loss claims, with approximately 270,000 drivers to receive compensation as a result of its intervention. This confirms the FCA's willingness to intervene in claims valuation disputes at scale, and is significant background context for credit hire cases involving total loss or write-off vehicles, where PAV disputes frequently arise.

## Motor Insurance Taskforce (December 2025)

The FCA committed to work with the ABI and insurers to improve efficiency and control costs in claims handling to support lower, more affordable motor insurance premiums. The Motor Insurance Taskforce Final Report (December 2025) is the vehicle for this work. This is the area of greatest potential risk for credit hire: if the FCA and ABI succeed in reducing overall claims costs, credit hire rates may come under renewed pressure as part of that process. Practitioners should monitor the Motor Insurance Taskforce work closely.

## Consumer Duty — the key regulatory hook

The Consumer Duty is the thread running through every FCA priority. For credit hire, the key implication is this: an insurer who fails to advise an accident victim of their right to a replacement vehicle — whether on a credit hire basis or otherwise — may be in breach of the Consumer Duty obligation to communicate clearly and deliver good outcomes. This argument is available both in litigation (as background context) and as a regulatory complaint to the FCA or Financial Ombudsman Service.

Similarly, CHOs registered with the FCA are themselves subject to Consumer Duty requirements and should ensure their communications with accident victims are clear, fair and not misleading about the nature of the credit hire arrangement and its cost implications.

## Premium finance

The FCA's premium finance market study found the cost of monthly insurance payments has fallen by approximately £157m annually since 2022. This has no direct credit hire litigation impact but illustrates the FCA's willingness to intervene in insurance pricing structures.

## What was NOT targeted

Credit hire as a business model was not singled out or criticised in this report. The FCA's intervention work has focused on home, travel and motor total loss claims. There is no FCA investigation into credit hire rates, CHO practices or the GTA.

## Practical implications for credit hire practitioners

1. Consumer Duty arguments: Where an insurer has failed to explain a claimant's entitlement to a replacement vehicle, the Consumer Duty framework provides a regulatory basis for complaint alongside any litigation.

2. Claims handling delays: The FCA's focus on prompt and fair claims handling supports arguments in litigation where defendant insurers have caused unnecessary delay — delay that prolonged the hire period or prevented timely off-hire.

3. Motor Insurance Taskforce — watch this space: The Taskforce's remit to reduce claims costs could produce recommendations affecting credit hire in future. Practitioners should monitor FCA publications from this workstream.

4. FCA-regulated CHOs: Any CHO that is FCA-authorised should review its consumer communications in light of the Consumer Duty priorities. The FCA has made clear it will take stronger, faster action against firms that fall short.

## Source

FCA Regulatory Priorities: Insurance, February 2026. Published at www.fca.org.uk/regulatory-priorities. Pub ref: 1-009074.
key_points: FCA's lead 2026 priority is improving consumer understanding, claims handling and service quality — insurers must tell consumers clearly what their cover includes
FCA tackled motor total loss underpayment — approximately 270,000 drivers to receive compensation
Motor Insurance Taskforce (December 2025) tasked with improving claims efficiency and controlling costs — potential future pressure on credit hire rates
Consumer Duty is the central regulatory hook: insurers who fail to explain accident victims' entitlement to replacement vehicles may be in breach
FCA-authorised CHOs are subject to Consumer Duty obligations on consumer communications
Claims handling delays by defendant insurers are directly targeted by FCA — supports litigation arguments on prolonged hire periods
Credit hire as a business model was not targeted or criticised in this report
FCA will continue supervisory and enforcement investigations on home and travel insurance claims handling
FCA expects firms to respond promptly, fairly and transparently to claims
Practitioners should monitor Motor Insurance Taskforce publications for any future recommendations affecting credit hire

articles.title: A Practical Guide to Motor Insurance Law for Learners – 2TG Insurance Group
articles.author: 2TG Insurance Group (James Doyle)
articles.source: 2TG Practical Guide - Motor Insurance Law - JMD.pdf
articles.content:
This is a 2023 guidance note from 2TG Chambers (James Doyle) providing a practical overview of motor insurance law, aimed at practitioners new to the field but also useful as a structural framework for experienced practitioners.

## The Motor Insurance Liability Tree

The English motor insurance system requires that any innocent person injured or suffering damage in a road traffic accident has recourse to a solvent body, not just an impecunious defendant. The system has been described as "a somewhat rickety house onto which various outbuildings and lean-tos have been added over the years." The key principle is that a motor insurer may be required to meet a claim even where there is a contractual exclusion, the driver is not insured, or the vehicle was being used for an unauthorised purpose.

Who pays depends on the insurer's position in the liability "tree" (Advantage v Stoodley [2018] EWHC 2135). The contractual insurer sits at the top; the MIB Central Fund sits at the bottom. If a higher insurer "falls out of the tree", liability passes to the insurer immediately below.

## Key features of the English system

In England and Wales, motor insurers insure the driver, not the vehicle. This means multiple insurers may be involved in a single accident. Under joint and several liability, a claimant can recover all their damages from any one joint tortfeasor — meaning if one is insured, the victim can recover fully from that insurer, leaving the insurer with a potentially worthless remedy against the uninsured co-defendant.

## Section 148 RTA 1988 — banned policy exclusions

Section 148 of the Road Traffic Act 1988 prohibits certain policy terms from being used to defeat third-party claims. Examples of banned exclusions include: no cover if the driver is under the influence of alcohol; no cover if the accident is not reported within a set period. These exclusions may exist in the policy, but cannot be used to defeat a third-party claimant. Insurers often include them to enable internal recovery rights.

## Practical relevance for credit hire

Understanding the liability tree and insurer obligations is important in credit hire because:
1. It clarifies which insurer is obliged to meet the claim, including where the at-fault driver is uninsured (MIB involvement).
2. It establishes the framework within which the defendant insurer operates, helping practitioners identify the correct respondent.
3. Section 148 exclusions are sometimes raised to complicate credit hire claims — understanding their limits is essential.

The guide also flags areas of uncertainty post-Brexit where EU law formerly informed English motor insurance requirements, and notes that litigation in those areas is likely.
key_points: The liability tree: contractual insurer at the top, MIB at the bottom — if a higher insurer falls out, the next one below picks up
English insurers insure the driver, not the vehicle — multiple insurers may be involved in one accident
Section 148 RTA 1988 bans certain policy exclusions from defeating third-party claims (e.g. alcohol, late reporting)
Joint and several liability means a claimant can recover all damages from one insured tortfeasor even if others are uninsured
An insurer may have to meet a claim even where there is a contractual exclusion, the driver was uninsured, or the vehicle was used for an unauthorised purpose
Post-Brexit uncertainty remains in areas where EU insurance law previously applied

articles.title: 2025 Horizon Scanning: Claims Inflation in Motor Claims – Horwich Farrelly
articles.author: Horwich Farrelly
articles.source: 20250324 HF Horizon Scanning.pdf
articles.content:
This is Horwich Farrelly's March 2025 horizon scanning report on claims inflation in UK motor claims. It is a defendant insurer perspective but contains data and analysis directly relevant to credit hire practitioners.

## Fixed Recoverable Costs — the most significant credit hire development

The FRC reforms (October 2023) have had a dramatic effect on credit hire costs from the defendant insurer perspective. Average credit hire costs in 2023 pre-October were £3,660. Post-October 2023, that figure dropped to £1,895 — a 49% reduction. Whilst official 2024 statistics were awaited at time of publication, the direction is clear. The FRC regime has significantly compressed credit hire recoveries in intermediate track and fast track cases.

The intermediate track savings range is described as "vast" (25-70%), including for mixed PI/credit hire claims. For PI only, savings range 50-75%. However, Horwich Farrelly reports that some claimant firms were seeking unrealistic complexity bands to escape lower fixed costs, and courts were in some cases prepared to allocate simple claims to the fast track despite pleaded values being in the intermediate track range.

A fixed costs increase is anticipated in April 2026.

## Credit hire and the Motor Insurance Taskforce

The Motor Insurance Taskforce, established to identify the causes of rising motor insurance costs, is highlighted as a key government initiative. Its focus on claims cost reduction is noted as a significant factor that could further affect credit hire volumes and recoverable rates.

## EV vehicles — repair cost implications

The Labour government confirmed the ban on new petrol and diesel cars from 2030 (brought forward from the previous 2035 target). BEV sales reached 381,970 in 2024 (+21.4%). Unless the repair industry addresses skills shortages, EV repair costs and repair duration will increase — directly extending hire periods and increasing credit hire exposure.

## Vehicle theft

Insurers paid out £669 million in vehicle theft in 2023 (+23% from 2022). The average theft claim was £12,600. Luxury SUVs and high-performance sports coupes dominate stolen vehicles. Vehicle theft directly generates credit hire claims through total loss and write-off scenarios.

## Other claims inflation drivers

Global supply chain disruption (tariffs, Ukraine/Russia conflict, shipping route issues), increased unemployment, higher National Living Wage, climate-related claims, and pothole damage are all identified as upward pressure on claims inflation in 2025.

## Complaint levels

Motor insurance complaints rose significantly from 2023 to 2024, with FOS uphold rates increasing. The FCA Consumer Duty focus is expected to drive further complaints growth, with associated cost implications for insurers.
key_points: FRC reforms: average credit hire cost dropped from £3,660 to £1,895 post-October 2023 — a 49% reduction
Intermediate track FRC savings on credit hire claims range 25-70%; fixed costs increase expected April 2026
Motor Insurance Taskforce identified as key government pressure point on credit hire costs
EV repair skills shortage and longer repair times will extend hire periods and increase credit hire exposure
Vehicle theft cost reached £669 million in 2023 (+23%), generating write-off credit hire claims
Courts prepared to allocate pleaded intermediate track claims to fast track, restricting claimant to lower fixed costs
Claimant firms seeking unrealistic complexity band designations to escape reduced fixed costs regime

articles.title: The Motor Insurance Market in Northern Ireland – ABI Briefing
articles.author: Association of British Insurers (ABI)
articles.source: ABI motor insurance NI.pdf
articles.content:
This is an ABI briefing to the Northern Ireland Committee on the state of the motor insurance market in Northern Ireland, covering NI-specific factors behind premium and claims cost increases.

## Why NI matters for credit hire practitioners

Northern Ireland has its own court system (County Court and QBD), its own procedural rules, and higher baseline claims costs than England and Wales. Several of the factors below directly affect the credit hire landscape in NI.

## Claims costs significantly higher in Northern Ireland

The average motor insurance claim cost in Northern Ireland in 2023 was £5,700 compared to a UK-wide average of £4,300 — 33% higher. The cost of providing replacement vehicles in NI is also higher than in England and Wales or Scotland.

The higher bodily injury compensation cost is the primary driver: compensation for bodily injury in NI was running at more than 40% above the UK market average in 2023 at £20,100, compared to £14,000 in England and Wales.

## New Green Book — Judicial Studies Board NI (April 2024)

On 8 April 2024, the JSBI published updated Guidelines for the Assessment of Damages in Personal Injury Cases in Northern Ireland. Average increases are 25-30%. Key changes:
- Minor neck injuries (under 6 months): £3,000 → £5,000 (+67%)
- Minor neck injuries (6 months to 1 year): up to £7,500 (+50%)
- Minor neck injuries (1-2 year recovery): up to £17,500 (+40%)

These compensation increases will be reflected in overall NI claim values and are relevant background when considering the credit hire exposure attached to NI road traffic accident claims.

## Road safety and young drivers

NI has a higher rate of deaths and serious injuries from road collisions than the rest of the UK. 16-24 year olds represent 20.3% of those killed or seriously injured despite accounting for only 9% of drivers. This younger driver risk profile drives higher NI premiums and is relevant to the impecuniosity assessment in credit hire cases (younger, lower-income claimants more likely to qualify).

## Insurance Premium Tax

IPT at 12% adds approximately £67 to the average motor insurance premium. The ABI called for a reduction ahead of the Spring Budget; this was not taken forward by HM Treasury.

## UK-wide context

The average UK motor premium reached £543, driven by above-inflation increases in repair costs, vehicle theft and replacement car costs. Insurers paid out a record £9.9 billion in claims in the same period (average £1.13 million per hour). For every £1 in premiums, insurers incurred £1.14 in claims and expenses in 2023 (EY data).
key_points: Average motor claim cost in NI in 2023: £5,700 vs UK average £4,300 — 33% higher
Replacement vehicle costs in NI are higher than England/Wales or Scotland
Bodily injury compensation in NI runs 40% above UK average at £20,100 (England/Wales £14,000)
New NI Green Book (April 2024): minor neck injury awards increased 25-67% across categories
16-24 year olds represent 20% of NI KSI casualties despite being only 9% of drivers
UK-wide: average premium reached £543; insurers paid £1.14 in costs per £1 of premium (2023)

articles.title: McKibben v UK Insurance and Clarke v McEvoy — CHO as Agent of Claimant (Northern Ireland 2020)
articles.author: Unknown (credit hire practitioner article)
articles.source: Article on McKibben v UK Insurance and Clarke v McEvoy[1].pdf
articles.content:
This article analyses two Northern Ireland QBD decisions handed down on 8 March 2020: McKibben v UK Insurance and Clarke v McEvoy. Both are credit hire appeals. QBD decisions in Northern Ireland are binding on the County Court, where most credit hire matters are heard in NI.

## The facts

Both cases involved non-fault accident claimants who dealt with a local CHO, Crash Services, for vehicle damage and replacement hire. The main dispute in both cases was hire duration — specifically, which party was responsible for delays that extended the hire period.

## The key finding — CHO as agent of the claimant

The Judge held that the CHO was an agent of the claimant. As a result, delays caused by the CHO could be directly imputed to the claimant. For example, a 2-day delay by the CHO would be treated as the claimant's delay and the hire account reduced accordingly.

The Judge went further: other parties could also be agents or sub-agents of the claimant. The solicitor and the engineer could both be treated as agents, and their delays similarly imputed to the claimant.

## The limit of the agency finding — the repairing garage

Critically, the Judge held that the repairing garage was not an agent or sub-agent of the claimant. This preserves the Mattocks v Mann principle for garage delays: once repairs have started, delays are highly unlikely to be imputed to the claimant.

The Judge also distinguished between: (a) delays in getting the vehicle to the repairing garage; and (b) delays in repairs starting once at the garage. The latter is more likely to be the garage's fault and cannot be imputed to the claimant.

## No "counsel of perfection"

The Judge explicitly stated that courts must not impose a "counsel of perfection" standard on claimants or CHOs. Nit-picking by defendants over individual days should be rejected. The example given was a defendant engineer arguing a 10-day hire should be 5 days based solely on labour hours — this is less likely to succeed following these judgments.

## Impact

These judgments are binding in Northern Ireland. They give defendants in NI a legal basis to challenge duration attributable to CHO or solicitor delays, while preserving the claimant's position on garage delays and preventing excessive micro-management of hire periods.
key_points: CHO is an agent of the claimant for duration purposes — CHO delays can be imputed directly to the claimant
Solicitors and engineers can also be sub-agents of the claimant — their delays similarly imputable
The repairing garage is NOT an agent — garage repair delays cannot be imputed to the claimant (Mattocks v Mann preserved for this purpose)
Distinction drawn between delays in delivering the vehicle to the garage vs delays in starting repairs once there
Courts must not impose a 'counsel of perfection' standard — defendants cannot nit-pick individual days
These are binding NI QBD decisions, binding on the NI County Court

articles.title: Poku v Abedin [2020] — Abuse of Process: Credit Hire Outside MOJ Portal (Article)
articles.author: Amrit Atwal, PSQB Chambers
articles.source: Article on Poku.pdf
articles.content:
This article by Amrit Atwal of PSQB Chambers analyses Poku v Abedin [2020], a significant appeal on whether a separate credit hire claim constitutes an abuse of process where a prior personal injury claim was settled through the MOJ RTA portal without including the hire charges.

## The background

The claimant suffered a road traffic accident on 2 May 2017. Personal injury proceedings were pursued through the MOJ portal; on the CNF and stage 2 pack, the claimant's solicitors stated that no hire was being pursued through the portal. The PI claim was settled for £1,700 PSLA.

Meanwhile, the credit hire had been intimated directly to the defendant's claims handlers (Eldon Insurance and their agents Validus) from the very start: an intimation was sent 10 May 2017, an intervention letter was issued, and Eldon specifically invited the claimant's representatives to negotiate the hire claim with Validus.

Separate proceedings for credit hire were issued in December 2018. The defendant applied to strike out the hire claim as an abuse of process.

## First instance decision

DDJ Omoregie struck out the hire claim. The focus was on the CNF and stage 2 pack, which stated no hire was being claimed via the portal.

## The appeal — HHJ Backhouse at the RCJ

HHJ Backhouse allowed the appeal. The DDJ had failed to conduct the necessary broad merits-based judgment required by Johnson v Gore Wood, taking into account all the circumstances. The CNF was not to be considered in a vacuum.

The critical facts were: (1) the defendant had been aware of the hire claim from the outset; (2) Eldon had itself sent an intervention letter and directed the claimant's representatives to deal with Validus for the hire; (3) the defendant could not exploit the claimant's failure to include hire in the portal when it had actively directed the hire claim to a separate negotiation track.

## The broader principle

The Court could not allow a defendant to benefit from the claimant's procedural error where the defendant had itself invited a separate process for the hire claim. The entire correspondence — including the defendant's own conduct — must be considered in any abuse of process balancing exercise.

## Portal rule 6.4

HHJ Backhouse noted interest in the lack of definition in portal rule 6.4 about what constitutes the "industry agreement" referred to when excluding vehicle-related losses from the portal. This is an area of continuing uncertainty.

## Practical implications

Where a defendant insurer has been separately notified of a credit hire claim and has directed the claimant to a parallel negotiation process, an abuse of process argument based solely on the portal CNF is unlikely to succeed. The full course of dealings must be considered.
key_points: Credit hire claim not included in MOJ portal PI claim is not automatically an abuse of process
HHJ Backhouse: broad merits-based judgment required (Johnson v Gore Wood) — the CNF cannot be read in isolation
Where the defendant insurer actively notified of the hire claim and directed it to a separate agent (Validus), it cannot later rely on the CNF to strike out
A defendant cannot benefit from a claimant's procedural error where the defendant's own conduct invited the separate claim process
Portal rule 6.4 on vehicle-related loss exclusions remains uncertain and is ripe for future litigation
The full course of pre-action correspondence between all parties must be examined before a strike-out can be ordered

articles.title: Assessing Impecuniosity in Credit Hire Claims — Practical Guide
articles.author: Unknown (industry guide, Financial Resilience series)
articles.source: Assessing Impecuniosity in Credit Hire Claims.pdf
keywords: sacrifices claimant cannot reasonably be expected to make
articles.content:
This is a practical guide to objectively assessing impecuniosity in credit hire claims, enabling a claimant to establish that they cannot pay for a replacement vehicle at basic hire rate and are therefore entitled to recover credit hire charges.

## The legal definition — Lagden v O'Connor [2003] UKHL 64

Lord Nicholls provided the primary definition: impecuniosity means inability to pay car hire charges without making sacrifices the claimant could not reasonably be expected to make. This is an open-ended test, but the House of Lords did not expect it to generate excessive litigation.

Lord Hope added a practical guidance: the dividing line should lie between those who have and those who do not have a recognised credit or debit card. In Northern Ireland, credit card facilities are specifically to be discounted from impecuniosity considerations.

## The credit card question

Mere possession of a credit card does not make a claimant pecunious. A credit card is only usable if: (i) it has enough available credit to cover the hire vehicle and any deposit; and (ii) the claimant has the means to pay off the credit card when the bill arrives.

A debit card is no different from cash for these purposes — it merely accesses funds in the account.

## Developing case law

**Irving v Morgan Sindall LLP** — Turner J addressed the issue of whether a claimant should use available credit options. Miss Irving had modest income (£472 basic, up to £700 with overtime). Her vehicle was written off and the defendant took 4 months to send a cheque. The judge found she was entitled to hire for the period needed to secure a replacement, during which time the credit card argument was not dispositive. Turner J noted: "impecuniosity need not amount to penury."

**Putta v RSA** — Stewart J took a more robust approach. Mr Putta had available credit card balance of £5,585. The trial judge found he was not impecunious because of this available balance — he could have paid for repairs to his vehicle with the credit card. On appeal, Mr Putta argued he should not have to use every penny of available credit without knowing how long hire would last or how he would repay the debt. Stewart J rejected this, citing Opoku v Tintas — the available balance on a credit card is a relevant factor that the court is entitled to consider.

## The practical framework for assessment

To objectively assess impecuniosity practitioners should examine:
1. Bank statements showing income, expenditure and balance patterns
2. Credit card statements — available credit limit and available balance
3. Overdraft position and available overdraft
4. The pre-accident value of the vehicle and the cost of a replacement
5. The cost of basic hire for the hire period claimed
6. Whether using available credit would require sacrifices the claimant cannot reasonably be expected to make (e.g. leaving no financial buffer for unexpected expenses, being required to carry card debt indefinitely with no clear repayment mechanism)

Motor insurers and credit hire companies have not agreed on standardised financial enquiries, despite Lord Nicholls' suggestion that they should.
key_points: Lagden v O'Connor: impecuniosity = inability to pay hire charges without making sacrifices the claimant could not reasonably be expected to make
Lord Hope: the dividing line is between those who have and those who do not have a usable credit/debit card
Mere possession of a credit card is not enough — it must have sufficient available credit and the claimant must be able to repay it
Irving v Morgan Sindall: 'impecuniosity need not amount to penury' — Turner J did not require use of credit card where repayment was uncertain
Putta v RSA: available credit card balance of £5,585 meant claimant was pecunious — courts are entitled to look at available credit (Opoku v Tintas)
In Northern Ireland, credit card facilities are specifically discounted from impecuniosity assessment
No agreed standard financial enquiries between insurers and CHOs despite Lord Nicholls' suggestion that there should be

articles.title: Motor Insurance Taskforce: Final Report and Actions (December 2025)
articles.author: HM Government / Department for Transport
articles.source: motor-insurance-taskforce-final-report.pdf
articles.content:
This is the Motor Insurance Taskforce Final Report (December 2025), published by the Department for Transport. It sets out the government's conclusions after a year-long review of the causes of rising motor insurance premiums and the actions to address them. It is directly relevant to the future regulatory landscape for credit hire.

## Why this report matters for credit hire

The report explicitly addresses replacement vehicles and credit hire as a component of rising motor insurance costs. It acknowledges the legitimacy of credit hire but identifies weaknesses in cost control and incentive structures. The key passages are summarised below.

## Replacement vehicles and credit hire — the report's findings

The cost of replacement vehicles rose by almost 50% from £473 million to £699 million between 2019 and 2023, contributing around 10% to the overall increase in motor insurance claim costs.

Critically, the report notes this increase is NOT solely driven by higher day rates. The length of repair times has also increased: lead times before work begins have doubled and repair times have increased by 36%. This means vehicles must be hired for longer, increasing total credit hire exposure.

The report acknowledges that credit hire rates are higher than direct hire rates due to the risks inherent in credit hire: deferred payment, uncertain recovery, and the need to maintain a readily available fleet.

The report identifies a structural problem: since credit hire costs are charged back to the third-party insurer, the incentives to control replacement vehicle costs are weak. Insurers earning referral fees from CHOs further distort the incentives.

## The GTA revision — Action 2.4

The government explicitly welcomed the work by the Credit Hire Organisation (CHO) and the ABI to revise the General Terms of Agreement (GTA) to ensure credit hire costs are fair for consumers. The new GTA (effective March 2026) is the vehicle for this reform.

## The ABI good practice code — Action 2.2/2.3

The FCA will work with the ABI and firms on better claims management to ensure greater efficiency and cost control without adversely affecting consumer outcomes. The ABI and firms are developing a good practice code to reduce referrals to third parties and manage more claims in-house. This is a direct signal that defendant insurers will be under regulatory pressure to reduce credit hire spend by handling more replacement vehicle arrangements themselves.

## Premiums already falling

The report notes that average motor insurance premiums had dropped for three consecutive quarters in 2025, with Q3 2025 premiums £56 lower than Q3 2024. The taskforce sees this as early evidence its work is having effect.

## Broader context: road safety, uninsured driving, vehicle repair

The government plans a new Road Safety Strategy, £24 billion in highway maintenance funding (2026-29), a Crime and Policing Bill introducing new offences for electronic vehicle theft devices, and consultation on electric vehicle battery health measures.
key_points: Replacement vehicle costs rose 50% from £473m to £699m between 2019-2023 — contributing 10% to overall claims cost increase
Increase driven not only by higher day rates but also longer repair times: lead times before work doubled, repair times up 36%
Government explicitly welcomes CHO/ABI GTA revision to ensure credit hire costs are fair — Action 2.4
FCA to work with ABI on good practice code reducing third-party referrals and managing more replacement vehicle claims in-house — Actions 2.2/2.3
Structural concern: costs charged to third-party insurer weaken incentives to control prices; referral fees further distort incentives
Motor insurance premiums fell three consecutive quarters in 2025; Q3 2025 average £56 lower than Q3 2024
Credit hire rates acknowledged to be higher than direct hire due to inherent risks (deferred payment, uncertain recovery, fleet availability)

articles.title: Range Anxiety Makes Electric Vehicles Safer — Daily Telegraph (September 2021)
articles.author: Ewan Somerville, Daily Telegraph
articles.source: EV Accidents.pdf
articles.content:
This is a Daily Telegraph article (September 2021) reporting research findings on electric vehicle accident rates compared to petrol and diesel vehicles. It is relevant background for credit hire practitioners as EVs become an increasingly significant part of the replacement vehicle fleet.

## Key finding: EVs have lower accident rates

Research by Lex Autolease (a fleet with 350,000 vehicles) found that in the year to August 2021, 24% of petrol/diesel vehicles required workshop repairs after an accident, compared to only 14% of electric vehicles and 15% of hybrids — a significant differential.

The explanation is "range anxiety" — EV drivers instinctively drive more slowly and conservatively to extend battery range. This produces safer, more economical driving behaviour.

## Implications for credit hire

If EV drivers have fewer accidents, the volume of EV credit hire claims may grow more slowly than the overall EV fleet would suggest. However, when EV accidents do occur, repair costs are substantially higher and repair times longer (due to specialist skills shortages), meaning EV credit hire claims, when they arise, will tend to be higher value and longer duration.

By 2030, sales of new petrol and diesel cars will be banned (reinstated at 2030 from the previous 2035 date). The credit hire fleet will increasingly need to include EV replacement vehicles.

## Background statistics (as at 2021)

- Fully electric cars: approximately 224,000 (March 2021), up from 108,000 (March 2020)
- EV breakdowns due to charge outages: 3.71% of attended e-car breakdowns
- 51% of drivers polled by AA worried about running out of charge on a motorway
- UK road KSI (killed or seriously injured): approximately 24,470 in the year to June 2020
key_points: EV accident rate (14%) significantly lower than petrol/diesel (24%) — Lex Autolease data
Range anxiety causes EV drivers to drive more slowly and cautiously, reducing accident frequency
EVs involved in fewer accidents, but repair costs and repair times are higher when accidents do occur
Credit hire EV claims will be higher value and longer duration due to specialist repair requirements
2030 ban on new petrol/diesel sales will progressively increase EV proportion of replacement vehicle fleet

articles.title: The Ethical Use of Google Adwords in Credit Hire — Passing Off, FCA Compliance and Best Practice
articles.author: Claimsline
articles.source: The Ethical Use Of Google Adwords.pdf
articles.content:
This document, produced by Claimsline (an FCA-regulated accident management company), provides a guide to the ethical and legally compliant use of Google Adwords in credit hire and accident management marketing, with specific reference to passing off concerns raised by insurers.

## The legal basis for competitor keyword bidding

Google itself has no rules against bidding on competitor brand name keywords, including insurer brand names. This practice is widespread across industries: Mercedes targets Audi searches; Admiral targets Aviva Car Insurance searches. It is a legitimate marketing strategy.

However, the use of competitor trademarks in ad URLs or adverts that mislead consumers into thinking they are dealing with the insurer itself is unlawful — this constitutes passing off under English law.

## The history of abuse and Google's response

From 2018, advertisers began bidding on insurer keywords without adequate verification or compliance. Many operated without relevant FCA licences, used insurer trademarks in URLs, and deliberately misled consumers. Google had no controls in place at this stage.

From 2019 onwards, Google introduced a strict two-stage verification process:
1. Stage 1: review of business model, services provided, customer journey and all firms involved in the client journey
2. Stage 2: ID verification of directors and owners

In September 2021, Google required financial services verification — only FCA-authorised firms may advertise within their relevant financial regulatory remit. Unregulated firms can no longer advertise in this space. Google also began displaying the advertiser's limited company details on the advert itself.

The number of firms advertising in this space dropped from hundreds to fewer than a dozen following these controls. The ability of rogue firms to be banned and re-appear under a different name has been substantially eliminated.

## The passing off risk and insurer attacks

Insurers have attacked CHOs and accident management companies using competitor keyword bidding, alleging they are impersonating insurers and misleading policyholders. The key legal principles on passing off require: (1) a misrepresentation; (2) made in the course of trade; (3) to prospective customers; (4) likely to deceive them into thinking they are dealing with the brand owner.

A properly structured Google Ads campaign, where adverts clearly state the advertiser is not an insurer, is independent, and is FCA-regulated, does not constitute passing off. Claimsline's approach: adverts clearly state they are not an insurer or affiliated with any insurance company; on receiving a call, staff disclose exactly who they are, their FCA regulation, and that they are independent.

## Due diligence checklist for CHOs/solicitors taking Google Adwords referral work

Before accepting referral work generated via Google Ads:
1. Ask for proof of how long the provider has held their Google Ads account
2. Verify the Google Ads account is in the name of the FCA-registered entity (not "rented")
3. Request a sample of call recordings to verify compliance
4. Obtain a copy of the compliance script
5. Perform a site audit to ensure the source matches the Google Ads account and FCA register
6. Check for independent customer reviews (Trustpilot, Feefo, etc.)
key_points: Competitor keyword bidding on Google is legal — Google has no prohibition on bidding for competitors' brand terms
Passing off occurs when adverts actively misrepresent the advertiser as the insurer (trademark in URL, misleading copy) — not from keyword bidding alone
Since September 2021, only FCA-authorised firms can advertise in the financial services / insurance space on Google
Google's two-stage verification process (business model + director ID) has reduced rogue advertisers from hundreds to fewer than a dozen
Proper compliance: adverts must state clearly the advertiser is not an insurer; calls must open with identity, FCA status and independence disclosure
CHOs and solicitors taking Google Ads referral work should verify: account age, FCA registration match, call recordings, compliance script, and customer reviews

articles.title: Arbitrate Limited — BHR Rate Survey Reports: Critique and Methodology Analysis
articles.author: Stephen Evans (Credit Hire Forum)
articles.source: Arbitrate.pdf
articles.content:
This entry analyses the BHR evidence methodology used by Arbitrate Limited, a rate survey report provider used by defendant insurers in credit hire litigation. It is based on a detailed critique of a partially redacted Arbitrate witness statement (Daniela Criscov, dated 15 September 2021) prepared in response to a claim involving a VW Golf R TSI 4Motion hired as an Audi Q5 replacement. The critique was authored by a practitioner with direct experience in BHR evidence methodology, including the exposure of the Autofocus dishonesty and the development of the original digitised rate comparison tool.

## Who is Arbitrate?

Arbitrate Limited is a BHR evidence provider owned by Bridgetech Group Ltd (bridgetechgroup.co.uk), a software house delivering solutions to the insurance, banking and finance sector. Bridgetech Group net assets: £24,000 (March 2024 accounts). Arbitrate Ltd net assets: £243,000 (March 2024 accounts). Arbitrate was first trialled in January 2020. The company claims to hold a database of over 11 million rows of rental rate data, aggregated electronically from mainstream rental company websites on a daily basis.

Report writers at Arbitrate are not the compilers of the database they rely upon. They cannot give evidence of its methodology. Arbitrate is based at 75 Park Lane, Croydon, CR9 1XS. Company number: 11435451.

## The core methodological problem

Arbitrate's reports are produced by employees who access an internal database but who had no part in creating it. This means any witness statement they produce is inherently limited: they can describe what the database shows but cannot give admissible evidence about how it was compiled, whether it is complete, whether it is accurate, or why certain providers are absent.

This is a structural weakness that mirrors the problems identified with Autofocus evidence prior to the contempt proceedings — the report writer is not the person who can speak to the underlying data collection methodology.

## Identified flaws — the Arbitrate critique

**1. Enterprise data absent without explanation**
Enterprise Rent-A-Car is the largest rental company in the UK by network size and fleet. In the sample case (hire start date 4 August 2021, Milton Keynes area), Arbitrate provided no Enterprise data for that date or the day after. No explanation is given for the absence. The question of whether Enterprise would have had a comparable vehicle available — and at what rate — is unanswered and untested.

**2. Undated exhibits — no timestamp or provenance**
None of the screen print exhibits carry hypertext timestamps at the header or footer. There is no evidence of when the enquiries generating the results were made. They may have been sourced weeks or months before the hire start date. The only valid comparison interval is between the date the claimant recognised their need to hire and the actual hire start date. Rates obtained outside this window are of questionable relevance.

**3. Personalised Google search results**
Arbitrate uses a Google search to demonstrate which rental companies a claimant could have found. Google search results are personalised to the searcher's location, prior browsing history and account activity. The results obtained by an Arbitrate report writer in Croydon searching at an unspecified time are not the same as those a claimant in Milton Keynes would have seen. This undermines the inference that the claimant could readily have found those companies.

**4. Inappropriate and non-comparable vehicles selected**
The damaged vehicle was a VW Golf R TSI 4Motion (266bhp, automatic, 4.21m). The Arbitrate report selected as comparators: Skoda Octavia (manual), Kia Ceed Auto and Peugeot 308 (manual). None of these vehicles are comparable in performance, transmission or specification to either the damaged vehicle or the Audi Q5 hired. No explanation is provided for why these vehicles were selected. The Audi Q5 hired is itself less powerful (201bhp) and slower (0-60: 7.4s v 5.3s) than the damaged VW, though the size differential is marginal (4.68m v 4.21m).

**5. Transmission errors — manual rates used for automatic vehicles**
The damaged vehicle and the hired replacement were both automatics. Arbitrate's report quotes rates for manual transmission vehicles. The automatic rate is materially higher: in the Sixt example, the manual Peugeot 308 rate was £45.57/day whereas the automatic rate was £64.42/day. Using the manual rate understates the genuine market BHR for the claimant.

**6. Rate calculation errors**
Arbitrate's calculations in the sample case failed to include mandatory excess reduction (CDW) costs. The 7-day Europcar rate in the report was understated; adding the £15/day excess reduction and pro-rating over 13 days produced a corrected gross rate of £909.87 — materially higher than the figure deployed in the report. The Sixt rate similarly required addition of £19/day excess reduction to £1,000 compulsory excess.

**7. Generic vehicle images**
Exhibit MM2 purported to show the claimant's damaged vehicle (VW Golf R TSI 4Motion). The image used was a standard VW Golf, not the R derivative. The Golf R has distinctive features — a unique grille logo, different alloy wheels, and specific body styling. The generic image meant the court had no proper basis to assess comparability between the damaged vehicle and the proposed comparators.

**8. Database opacity — methodology unexplained**
Arbitrate's explanation of how their database is compiled states only that software performs "an electronic search of several mainstream rental company websites" capturing images of available vehicles over 1, 7, 14 and 28-day periods. This explanation does not address: how far in advance of the intended hire start rates are collected; what criteria determine which vehicles are selected; why specific providers are absent for specific dates; the geographic scope of searches; or how the database handles rate changes between collection and the hire date.

**9. GTA classification misapplication**
The VW Golf R 2.0 TSI 4Motion DSG is not currently grouped in the GTA vehicle groupings. Referencing GTA classification in the context of this vehicle is therefore potentially misleading. The vehicles presented as comparators (Skoda Octavia S4, Peugeot 308 S5/S6 depending on engine) are classified at lower GTA groups than the appropriate classification for the Golf R, which could be as high as S5A or S6A.

**10. Third-party hearsay without identified source**
Certain sections of the Arbitrate report contain assertions attributed to unnamed third parties. This is hearsay without an identified source and is accordingly inadmissible as evidence of fact.

**11. Delivery, collection and transport costs — unsupported figures**
The report suggests a £47 delivery/collection charge for Europcar. No evidential basis is given for this figure, nor is any company policy identified. A further £80 taxi cost at hire start and end is also suggested without evidential support.

**12. Google Maps — undated journey time calculations**
The Google Maps screenshot showing rental depot distances carries no time/date stamp. Journey times calculated using "Leave Now" reflect conditions at the unspecified time the map was produced — potentially a Sunday evening with light traffic. Journey times to a depot stated as 11.6 miles away were actually to a location 24 miles from the claimant's home postcode.

## Parallel with Autofocus

The critique explicitly draws a parallel with the Autofocus evidence methodology which was ultimately found to be dishonest and led to contempt proceedings. Both companies: (a) used generic Google searches to identify rental companies; (b) relied on databases whose compilation methodology the report writer could not speak to; (c) produced undated screen prints without provenance; and (d) failed to include the market's largest provider (Enterprise) in their data.

## Practical use of this entry

When faced with an Arbitrate BHR report in litigation, claimant practitioners should consider challenging: (1) the absence of Enterprise data; (2) the timestamp/provenance of all exhibits; (3) the transmission type of comparator vehicles; (4) whether excess reduction costs have been included; (5) the GTA classification of the damaged and hired vehicle; (6) the database compilation methodology by requiring the report writer to identify who compiled it and on what basis; and (7) any hearsay passages in the witness statement.
key_points: Arbitrate is a BHR evidence provider owned by Bridgetech Group (software house, net assets £24k); report writers cannot give evidence of how the underlying database was compiled
Enterprise — the UK's largest rental company — was absent from the sample case data with no explanation: its absence is itself a ground of challenge
All screen print exhibits in the sample case were undated — no hypertext timestamps, no evidence of when enquiries were made
Google search results used by Arbitrate are personalised to the searcher's location and history — not representative of what the claimant would have found
Arbitrate selected manual transmission comparators for a claimant whose damaged and hired vehicles were both automatics — the automatic rate was materially higher
Rate calculations omitted mandatory excess reduction (CDW) costs of £15-19/day, significantly understating the gross BHR
Vehicle images used were generic, not specific to the claimant's actual vehicle model (standard Golf vs Golf R with distinctive styling)
The VW Golf R TSI 4Motion DSG is not in the GTA vehicle groupings — GTA classification references in the report are therefore potentially misleading
The methodology mirrors Autofocus practices that were found to be dishonest: same reliance on undated screen prints, same Google search approach, same absent providers
When challenging an Arbitrate report: demand Enterprise data, check timestamps, check transmission types, add CDW costs, challenge the database compilation witness

articles.title: Salt v Helley [2009] NIQB 69 — Agent's Conflict of Interest in Northern Ireland Credit Hire
articles.summary:
Salt v Helley [2009] NIQB 69 (Stephens J) is a foundational Northern Ireland High Court judgment on the agent's duty of loyalty in credit hire. It binds Northern Ireland County Courts. English High Court or County Court decisions on agency in credit hire do not apply in Northern Ireland.

THE FACTS: The plaintiff had a free courtesy car available under her own insurance policy. MIS Claims Service (acting as her agent) arranged credit hire through its wholly-owned subsidiary ICH instead, without disclosing its financial interest in doing so. The District Judge awarded the hire charges; the defendant appealed.

THE LAW: Stephens J held that MIS was in breach of its duty as the plaintiff's agent. Applying Imageview Management Ltd v Jack [2009] EWCA Civ 63, an agent must act 100% in the principal's interests; undisclosed conflicts of interest breach that duty. The remedy — per Rhodes v MacAllister (1893) — is forfeiture of ALL remuneration by the agent, regardless of whether the principal benefited. Once MIS forfeited its right to remuneration, ICH was not entitled to hire charges, the plaintiff owed nothing, and had no loss to recover from the defendant. Defendant's appeal ALLOWED.

IMPORTANT LIMITATION: Salt was subsequently distinguished (not overruled) by Smyth v Diamond [2010] NIQB 74. McCloskey J held that the conflict-of-interest finding in Salt depended on its specific facts (proof that MIS was the plaintiff's agent, and proof of deliberate non-disclosure). Where the agency relationship has not been clearly established and there is no proven non-disclosure, Salt does not automatically apply to defeat credit hire claims.

PRACTICAL SIGNIFICANCE: Insurers regularly invoke Salt in NI credit hire claims. The counterargument — following Smyth v Diamond — is that the Salt fact pattern must be specifically proved. Practitioners should check: (1) Is there clear evidence MIS/claims manager was the plaintiff's agent? (2) Is there evidence of non-disclosure of the conflict? (3) Was a courtesy car or insurance-provided vehicle actually available?
articles.source: Salt v Helley [2009] NIQB 69, Stephens J
articles.court: High Court of Justice in Northern Ireland

articles.title: Smyth v Diamond [2010] NIQB 74 — Governing Principles of Credit Hire Law in Northern Ireland
articles.summary:
Smyth v Diamond; Phillips v Ritchie; Torrens v Tally; McCabe v Moffett [2010] NIQB 74 (McCloskey J) is the foundational Northern Ireland High Court authority on the governing principles of credit hire law in Northern Ireland. It is essential reading for any NI credit hire practitioner. It binds Northern Ireland County Courts. English High Court decisions on credit hire do not bind Northern Ireland courts.

BACKGROUND: Four conjoined appeals from District Judges, all involving the standard MIS Claims Service / Independent Car Hire (ICH) arrangement. Three plaintiff appeals against dismissed credit hire claims; one defendant appeal against a successful claim.

THE ELEVEN GOVERNING PRINCIPLES (McCloskey J):
1. Restitutio in integrum: damages place the plaintiff in the pre-accident position.
2. Reasonable necessity: the plaintiff must prove need for a replacement vehicle.
3. Duty to mitigate: plaintiff must take reasonable steps to minimise loss.
4. Expenditure must be reasonable: cannot recover hire that is disproportionate.
5. Prima facie rule: the credit hire invoice is the prima facie measure of damages.
6. Onus on defendant: defendant must prove failure to mitigate.
7. Onus of proof rule: if defendant fails to prove mitigation failure, normal measure applies.
8. Additional benefits: charges for benefits beyond the replacement vehicle must be stripped out.
9. Spot rate measure: the spot (market) hire rate is the normal measure; defendant must prove a lower available rate.
10. Lagden impecuniosity exception: an impecunious plaintiff may recover credit hire rates.
11. Res inter alios acta: a tortfeasor cannot require the plaintiff to invoke insurance entitlements to mitigate loss.

SALT V HELLEY DISTINGUISHED: The standard MIS/ICH agreement is a genuine, enforceable contract. Non est factum not available to literate adults who signed. Salt is fact-sensitive and requires clear proof of agency and non-disclosure — not established on these facts.

OTHER KEY POINTS:
- Phillips: Novus actus rejected — where Wrights diverted plaintiff from insurance provision, res inter alios acta applied.
- McCabe: Past consideration argument rejected — substantial post-execution benefits and a prior oral contract gave good consideration.
- Copley v Lawn (CA, England) followed: insurer must disclose true cost of offered replacement vehicle before plaintiff can be criticised for rejecting it.

OUTCOME: Three plaintiff appeals ALLOWED; defendant appeal (McCabe) DISMISSED.
articles.source: Smyth v Diamond [2010] NIQB 74, McCloskey J
articles.court: High Court of Justice in Northern Ireland

articles.title: Clarke v McCullough [2012] NIQB 104 — Mitigation, Protection Insurance and 2008 Regulations in NI
articles.summary:
Clarke v McCullough [2012] NIQB 104 (McCloskey J, first instance) is an important Northern Ireland High Court decision applying the Smyth v Diamond principles in a complex hire scenario. Binding on NI County Courts.

THE FACTS: 328-day hire period (April 2010 to March 2011). Five credit hire agreements with Accident Exchange. Liability denied throughout. A protection insurance policy was triggered; the underwriter paid Accident Exchange £19,676. Accident Exchange subrogated the claim and pursued the defendant for £32,380.97.

KEY FINDINGS:

1. ENFORCEABILITY / 2008 REGULATIONS: Two of the five agreements (162 days) unenforceable under the Cancellation of Contracts Made in a Consumer's Home Regulations 2008 (Reg 7(4)) — no compliant cancellation notice. BUT this was IRRELEVANT: the charges had been paid through the protection insurance policy, giving the plaintiff an accrued financial loss. W v Veolia ES [2011] EWHC 2020 followed: where hire charges have already been paid, enforceability is immaterial.

2. CANCELLATION NOTICE / INCORPORATION: Agreements and cancellation notices received, considered and signed simultaneously = properly 'incorporated' under Reg 7(4) even without physical attachment. No statutory pro-forma required.

3. MITIGATION — HIRE PERIOD LIMITED: From 23 September 2010, the plaintiff should have repaired his own vehicle at ~£2,500 (within his means via credit card). Defendant discharged mitigation burden from that date. Hire recovery LIMITED to first 164 days (approximately half the claimed period).

4. NON-STANDARD DRIVER CHARGE: £7.17/day irrecoverable — unexplained and unjustified; stripped out as additional benefit or unreasonable mitigation.

5. SUBROGATION: Real claimant was Accident Exchange via protection policy. Claim limited to actual loss incurred by plaintiff.

OUTCOME: Judgment for plaintiff £12,408.37 (reduced from £32,380.97 claimed).
articles.source: Clarke v McCullough [2012] NIQB 104, McCloskey J
articles.court: High Court of Justice in Northern Ireland

articles.title: Northern Ireland Credit Hire Law — Jurisdiction Primer and Key Authorities
articles.summary:
CRITICAL JURISDICTIONAL POINT: Northern Ireland has its own distinct body of credit hire case law. The High Court in Northern Ireland (NIQB) decisions ARE BINDING on Northern Ireland County Courts. English High Court and County Court decisions are NOT binding in Northern Ireland and do not apply in NI proceedings. English Court of Appeal and UK Supreme Court/House of Lords decisions apply as binding authority across all UK jurisdictions including Northern Ireland.

NORTHERN IRELAND DOES NOT APPLY ENGLISH CREDIT HIRE COURT DECISIONS: The legal framework in NI is built on the NI High Court's own case law (the Turley/Smyth/Clarke line), applying House of Lords authority. English 'Hire Court' or County Court decisions on BHR evidence, mitigation, hire period and similar issues are not part of NI law and cannot be cited as binding authority in NI proceedings.

KEY NI CREDIT HIRE AUTHORITIES (binding in NI):

1. McMullan v Gibney [1999] NIJB 17 (NICA): Res inter alios acta — tortfeasor cannot require plaintiff to use insurance entitlements to mitigate.

2. O'Hagan v Wright [2001] NI CA 26 (NICA): Consumer Credit Act 1974 — unenforceable agreement gives rise to no loss recoverable from tortfeasor.

3. Turley v Black [2010] NIQB 1 (McCloskey J): Standard framework and structure of NI credit hire litigation.

4. Salt v Helley [2009] NIQB 69 (Stephens J): Agent with undisclosed conflict of interest (MIS/ICH arrangement) forfeits remuneration; plaintiff has no recoverable loss. Fact-sensitive; distinguished by Smyth v Diamond.

5. Smyth v Diamond [2010] NIQB 74 (McCloskey J): FOUNDATIONAL JUDGMENT — eleven governing principles of NI credit hire law; standard MIS/ICH agreements valid and enforceable; Salt v Helley distinguished; novus actus and past consideration arguments rejected.

6. McAteer v Kirkpatrick [2011] NIQB 52 (McCloskey J): Full restatement of the 11 principles.

7. Stokes v Macaulay [2010] NIQB 131 (McCloskey J): Application of the prima facie invoice rule and evidential burdens.

8. Clarke v McCullough [2012] NIQB 104 (McCloskey J): Mitigation limits hire recovery to reasonable period; once charges paid through protection insurance, enforceability is irrelevant; non-standard driver charge irrecoverable.

ENGLISH HOUSE OF LORDS AUTHORITIES APPLIED IN NI (binding):
- Giles v Thompson [1994] 1 AC 142: Credit hire agreements not champertous; genuine commercial arrangement.
- Dimond v Lovell [2002] 1 AC 384: Unenforceable agreement = no loss; additional benefits and spot rate principles.
- Lagden v O'Connor [2004] 1 AC 1067: Impecunious plaintiff may recover credit hire rate.

ENGLISH COURT OF APPEAL AUTHORITIES APPLIED IN NI (persuasive, not binding):
- Copley v Lawn [2009] EWCA Civ 580: Insurer must disclose true cost of offered vehicle.
- Bee v Jenson [2007] EWCA Civ 927: Spot rate applies even if charges not paid directly by plaintiff.

ENFORCEABILITY IN NI: The Cancellation of Contracts Made in a Consumer's Home Regulations 2008 apply in NI. Consumer Credit Act 1974 applies in NI. W v Veolia ES (English QB) applied persuasively in Clarke v McCullough: once charges paid, enforceability immaterial.
articles.source: Compilation of NI High Court and NI Court of Appeal decisions

articles.title: Credit Hire — Need: Proving and Challenging the Need for a Replacement Vehicle
articles.summary: Comprehensive tactical guide to proving and challenging need for a replacement vehicle in credit hire claims. Covers the 'not self-proving' threshold, type/like-for-like principle, prestige vehicles, business and self-employed claimants, and key defendant practice points.
articles.content:
The claimant's need for a replacement vehicle is the threshold issue in every credit hire claim. It must be proved but the standard is low.

**The 'Not Self-Proving' Rule**
Need is not self-proving (Giles v Thompson [1994] 1 AC 142 per Lord Mustill), but the threshold is very low. A court will readily infer that a motorist who pays to run a private car has a need for it, and thus a need to replace it when it is put out of commission. However, there remains scope for a defendant to displace that inference (for example, if the hirer was hospitalised, abroad, or had other vehicles available). If need fails entirely, the hire claim fails in its entirety.

**Unreasonable Expenditure**
A claimant cannot recover for expenditure that was unreasonable. If the car hired was bigger or better than was reasonable, the claim must be reduced to the cost of hiring an equivalent car (Lagden v O'Connor [2004] 1 AC 1067 per Lord Hope).

**Like for Like — Type of Vehicle**
A hirer is generally entitled to hire a like-for-like vehicle (Lagden v O'Connor). If he hires an inferior vehicle, he recovers only the rate for that vehicle. For BHR purposes, once the court accepts that the vehicle hired was reasonable, the BHR evidence should relate to that model — not to the claimant's own pre-accident vehicle (Darren Bent v Highways and Utilities Construction Ltd). For example, if the claimant's own car was a Ford Fiesta but he reasonably hired a Golf, the BHR evidence should be for a Golf.

**Prestige Vehicles**
A claimant may establish a personal need for a like-for-like prestige replacement (Gow v NFU Mutual [2016] — McLaren: 'he does establish need'). However, defendants should obtain the claimant's insurance certificate to identify other vehicles available, check mileage restrictions (which may show limited use of the damaged vehicle), and scrutinise need where hire charges for a prestige vehicle are very large (Frankland v UK Insurance [2016] — Ferrari California, 219 days at £236,724; claim defeated where claimant had 5 other vehicles and the damaged car was insured for 1,000 miles per year only). The ABI General Terms of Agreement note that where a vehicle is over 6 years old it is 'the exception rather than the rule that a similar prestige vehicle is required', though ABI terms are not always relevant in litigation.

**Business and Fleet Vehicles**
For business claims, need is not inferred — specific evidence is required: actual use of the vehicle for business purposes before the accident, the use to which the hired vehicle was put during the period, and (for fleets) whether other vehicles were available (Singh v Yaqubi [2013] EWCA Civ 23 — Rolls-Royce hire claim dismissed entirely where partnership had 6 other prestige vehicles; 'very large car hire claims should be scrutinised carefully'). Park Lane v Whipp (2009) is sometimes cited but is a County Court decision with no binding authority and is not relied upon in hostile litigation — the binding authority on business need is the Court of Appeal in Singh v Yaqubi. But see Beechwood Birmingham Ltd v Hoyer Group [2010] EWCA Civ 647 — a motor dealer with available courtesy cars receives damages based on interest, capital, and depreciation, not hire cost.

**Self-Employed Drivers and Taxis**
If hire cost exceeds the profit the claimant would have earned, only the lower figure may be recoverable (A Line Taxis v Ward; Clark and Lindsell on Torts). The default measure for a taxi driver is loss of profit (Humayum Hussain v EUI Ltd [2019] EWHC 2647). Exceptions apply where: (i) the business hired to protect future trading; (ii) the claimant also had a genuine private and domestic need; (iii) impecuniosity made credit hire the only option. Evidence of social/domestic use is therefore valuable (Rashid v Naylor [2018] — school run, medical appointments, children's football; court satisfied a domestic need existed alongside the business use).

**Courtesy Vehicle from Own Insurer**
The availability of a courtesy vehicle under the claimant's own policy does not prevent recovery of hire charges — res inter alios acta; a defendant cannot take advantage of the claimant's own insurance (Seddon v Tekin [2001]; McMullen v Gibney and Bibney [1999] NIQB 1; McCauley v Brennan [2017] NIQB 41). A finding that the claimant should have invoked their own insurance would penalise a conscientious person who took out comprehensive cover relative to one who only had third-party insurance.

**Defendant's Offer of a Free Vehicle (Copley)**
A defendant insurer's offer of a free replacement vehicle may reduce credit hire recovery if rejecting it constituted a failure to mitigate — but only where 'other things are equal' (Copley v Lawn [2009] EWCA Civ 580). Defendant offer letters must not be threatening or intimidating, must advise the claimant to show the letter to their solicitor or insurer, and must specify the cost of the replacement (failure to state the cost means rejection is not a failure to mitigate). Cold-calling claimants was described as 'inappropriate' and should be discontinued. Arguments that offer was not truly equivalent (different excess, no delivery/collection, different terms) remain available.

**Defendant Practice Points**
- Check the claimant's insurance certificate for other available vehicles
- Check mileage restrictions on the damaged vehicle's policy
- Obtain Part 18 responses detailing use of the vehicle
- Challenge need where hire commenced before repairs — did the claimant actually need the vehicle during that period?
- Contradict witness statements with defendant's own intelligence on claimant's circumstances
- For large prestige or business claims, require specific evidence of actual pre-accident use and use of the hired vehicle during the period
key_principles: Need is not self-proving but the threshold is low — a motorist is easily inferred to need a replacement; defendants must point to specific facts displacing the inference
If need fails entirely, the hire claim fails in its entirety
Claimant must hire a vehicle equivalent ('like for like') to their own; BHR evidence should match the model actually hired, not the claimant's damaged car (Bent v Highways)
Business/fleet claimants must produce specific evidence of actual use before and during the hire period — need is not inferred from ownership alone (Singh v Yaqubi)

For taxis/self-employed drivers the default measure is loss of profit, not hire charges; hire charges are recoverable only where the claimant also has a domestic need or where hire protected future trading (Hussain, Rashid)
A defendant's offer of a free vehicle must state the cost and must not be threatening; rejection of an offer that does not specify cost is not a failure to mitigate (Copley)
Own insurer's courtesy vehicle does not reduce the defendant's liability — res inter alios acta (McMullen, McCauley v Brennan)
For prestige vehicles: check whether other vehicles were available and whether the damaged car was genuinely used at the rate implied by the hire

Jones v Thompson (Halifax CC, 28 May 2010): need for a replacement Ferrari 360 Spider was established even though the claimant had available a Range Rover, and through his business a Ford Mondeo, Land Rover Discovery and delivery vans — the existence of other vehicles does not automatically defeat a need argument for a like-for-like prestige replacement

Frankland v UK Insurance [2016]: in contrast, need was not established where the claimant hired Ferraris at £236,724 total to replace a Ferrari 360 Modena despite having a BMW 335, Land Rover Defender, MG Midget and Citroen Berlingo available, and where defendant's investigations revealed the claimant's own Ferrari was insured for only 1,000 miles per year — summary judgment entered for defendant; the case underlines the necessity of full early inquiry into circumstances where need may be in dispute
cases_referenced: Giles v Thompson [1994] 1 AC 142
Lagden v O'Connor [2004] 1 AC 1067
Singh v Yaqubi [2013] EWCA Civ 23
Darren Bent v Highways and Utilities Construction Ltd
Gow v NFU Mutual (Central London CC, 2016)
Frankland v UK Insurance Ltd (Darlington CC, March 2016)
Humayum Hussain v EUI Ltd [2019] EWHC 2647
Rashid v Naylor [2018] 8 WLUK 363
Park Lane v Whipp (HHJ Harris, 2009)
Beechwood Birmingham Ltd v Hoyer Group UK Ltd [2010] EWCA Civ 647

McCauley v Brennan [2017] NIQB 41

articles.title: Credit Hire — Impecuniosity: Proof, Financial Disclosure and Key Cases
articles.summary: Detailed tactical guide to impecuniosity in credit hire claims. Covers the Lagden test, burden and standard of proof, financial disclosure requirements, key cases on what constitutes pecuniosity (Putta, Irving, Umerji), mitigation obligations on impecunious claimants, and the risk of fundamental dishonesty findings for non-disclosure.
articles.content:
Impecuniosity is the exception to the general rule that a pecunious claimant recovers only the Basic Hire Rate (BHR). It entitles the impecunious claimant to recover the full credit hire rate.

**The Lagden Test**
The test is whether the claimant had an 'inability to pay car hire charges without making sacrifices the plaintiff could not reasonably be expected to make' (Lagden v O'Connor [2003] UKHL 64). The claimant need not be destitute — 'impecuniosity need not amount to penury' (Irving v Morgan Sindall [2018] EWHC 1147 per Mr Justice Turner). In Irving, the correct analysis required considering the total sum the claimant would need (cost of hire plus cost of replacing the written-off vehicle) rather than simply asking whether they had enough cash to buy a replacement at pre-accident value.

**Burden of Proof**
The burden is on the claimant to prove impecuniosity. It is an exception to the general rule and the party relying on an exception bears the burden; additionally, all the relevant evidence is in the claimant's control. The failure to provide evidence, in compliance with any court order requiring it, will debar the claimant from relying on impecuniosity — for both rate and period (Zurich Insurance v Umerji [2014] EWCA Civ 357).

**Financial Disclosure Requirements**
A claimant asserting impecuniosity must disclose: copies of all bank/current account statements, credit card statements (all cards, including those at their limit), savings account statements, overdraft facility details, and details of earnings — typically covering three months before the period of hire to three months after it ends. PI firms handling the credit hire element often fail to appreciate the breadth of what is required: failing to obtain and disclose all required documents, or providing a statement explaining why they are not within the claimant's possession or control, will result in the impecuniosity argument being struck out.

**Putta v Royal Sun Alliance [2020] EWHC 117**
The court was entitled to consider all available funds from a range of sources — current accounts, savings accounts, credit cards, and overdraft facilities — in assessing whether a claimant was pecunious. Using an overdraft facility for a short period did not make the claimant impecunious. On the facts, the claimant would have needed to fund approximately £5,756 (repairs plus hire); his current account including overdraft covered the shortfall. The argument that significant interest would accrue on credit cards was addressed by noting that the defendant would have been expected to settle within two months had the claimant self-funded.

**Irving v Morgan Sindall [2018] EWHC 1147**
At first instance, the judge found the claimant could have raised approximately £900 by depleting her accounts and could have bought a replacement at pre-accident value of £775. The High Court overturned this, holding it was wholly incorrect. The correct question was the total sum needed — the cost of hire over the period plus the cost of a replacement vehicle — not simply whether the claimant had enough to buy a car. The claimant would have been exposed to 'a serious financial challenge in the event of a modest but unexpected financial reverse'. A judge cannot simply reduce the analysis to minimum capital and maximum debt.

**Umerji — Identifying When Hire Became Unreasonable**
A pecunious claimant who continues to hire long after the point at which they should reasonably have replaced their vehicle may find recovery limited. In Umerji, hire charges were allowed only from the date of the accident to 8 March 2011 — not to November 2012 when the claimant received the write-off cheque. Defendants should identify the specific moment when it became unreasonable to continue hiring (e.g., the date a letter was sent denying liability, or the date by which a reasonable person would have sourced a replacement). The mere fact that hire charges exceed the pre-accident value is not, by itself, evidence that something unreasonable has happened.

**Opoku v Tintas [2013] EWCA Civ 1299**
An impecunious claimant who continued to hire for almost two years (total: £130,000, accruing at £5,000/month) was found to have failed to mitigate by not having his car repaired once T's insurer had inspected it. There was no inconsistency between a finding of impecuniosity (justifying the credit hire rate) and a finding that at some later point the claimant should reasonably have found means to pay for repair (by using credit cards or savings). The one-off cost of £3,400 for repair had to be weighed against £5,000/month in ongoing hire. Courts in credit hire cases must exercise particular control over both rate and period.

**Rashid v Naylor [2018]**
A taxi driver found to be impecunious nonetheless failed to mitigate. Once proved impecunious, a claimant is not immune from all mitigation obligations. He should have paid his policy excess and taken his insurer's write-off payment to replace the vehicle, rather than waiting 220 days for the defendant's insurers to pay. Recovery was limited to 35 days: the duty to mitigate by replacing the vehicle arose at that point.

**Pre-Action Disclosure of Financial Documents**
In EUI Ltd v Charles [2018], it was consistent with the overriding objective to order pre-action disclosure of financial status documents (bank statements, payslips) from individuals who had intimated credit hire claims, to enable early assessment of impecuniosity. Credit hire companies that refer claims to panel solicitors at an early stage without engaging with insurers on impecuniosity undermine the expectation in Lagden that the issue would be resolved before litigation.

**Fundamental Dishonesty — Haider v DSM Demolition [2019] EWHC 2712**
Failure to disclose credit cards or a second bank account in response to Part 18 questions on impecuniosity amounts to fundamental dishonesty under CPR r.44.16(1), with consequent costs implications. A claimant who answered 'I did not have any credit card accounts' when he held two cards was found to have made a false disclosure statement verified by a statement of truth. Non-disclosure of financial information in the context of an impecuniosity case is treated seriously by the courts.

**Allan v Amlin UK Ltd [2013] CSOH 156 (Court of Session)**
In a case where impecuniosity is not relied upon, the burden is on the defendant to prove that the credit hire charges were unreasonable. If no BHR evidence is led by the defendant, the claimant is entitled to recover the credit hire charges in full.
key_principles: Impecuniosity is the claimant's exception to justify recovering the full credit hire rate rather than BHR; the burden of proving it rests entirely on the claimant
The Lagden test: 'inability to pay car hire charges without making sacrifices the plaintiff could not reasonably be expected to make' — impecuniosity does not mean penury (Irving)
Financial disclosure must cover all bank accounts, credit cards, savings, and overdraft facilities for three months before and three months after the hire period
A claimant who fails to comply with a court order for financial disclosure is debarred from relying on impecuniosity for both rate and period (Umerji)
The court considers total funding required (hire + replacement vehicle cost), not just whether the claimant had enough capital for a cheap replacement (Irving v Morgan Sindall)
Impecuniosity does not immunise a claimant from all mitigation obligations; there may come a point when a reasonable impecunious person should have found other means to end the hire (Opoku, Rashid)
Defendants can seek pre-action disclosure of financial documents before issuing (EUI v Charles)
Non-disclosure of credit cards or bank accounts in Part 18 replies is potentially fundamental dishonesty under CPR r.44.16(1) (Haider)
If the defendant adduces no BHR evidence, the full credit hire rate is recoverable even without impecuniosity (Allan v Amlin)
cases_referenced: Lagden v O'Connor [2003] UKHL 64; [2004] 1 AC 1067
Irving v Morgan Sindall PLC [2018] EWHC 1147 (QB)
Putta v Royal Sun Alliance [2020] EWHC 117 (QB)
Zurich Insurance Plc v Umerji [2014] EWCA Civ 357
Opoku v Tintas [2013] EWCA Civ 1299
Rashid v Naylor [2018] 8 WLUK 363
EUI Ltd v Charles [2018] 9 WLUK 269
Haider v DSM Demolition Ltd [2019] EWHC 2712
Allan v Amlin UK Ltd [2013] CSOH 156
