The greater knowledge or commercial understanding of enablers such as accident management companies and hire companies is not brought into consideration when assessing whether the claimant mitigated reasonably.

**Judgment**

Judgment was given for the claimant for the pleaded claim in full, save for £40 administration charges, with credit given for an interim payment already made in respect of repair costs. The total credit hire award was effectively the full £50,371.20 less the £40 deduction.

**DJ Lumb's Wider Observations**

The judge's comments were widely reported by the BBC: 'What might come as a shock to the general public is how the Credit Hire Industry operates. In particular, some may consider the sums of money that motor insurers of culpable policyholders become liable for to be staggering. These are recoverable by operation of the existing common law determined by the Higher Courts and binding upon judges in the County Court. In an age where motor insurance premiums are reported to have risen to unprecedented levels some may find it surprising that there appears to be no real appetite in the insurance industry to campaign for reform, presumably by Parliament, to control the level of credit hire charges compared to the ordinary market basic hire rate.'

Weightmans note that despite the case generating significant publicity, DJ Lumb had little option but to allow the claim in full given the binding case law. The decision raises continued questions about whether Parliamentary reform or industry protocols are needed to control credit hire costs — a debate that remains live.

**Defendant Practice Points (Weightmans' Analysis)**

The case illustrates the central difficulty for defendants in roadworthy-vehicle cases where the claimant was not told about the roadworthiness. The defendant's burden of proving failure to mitigate is difficult — arguably impossible — if the claimant can credibly say they were kept in the dark and acted on the instructions of their agents (solicitors and hire company). To have any prospect of a period reduction in such cases defendants should:
- Communicate directly with the claimant (not just their solicitors) in writing about the roadworthiness of their vehicle at the earliest opportunity
- Ensure the claimant is informed promptly of any engineer's report finding the vehicle roadworthy
- Issue an early offer of a reasonable alternative (with the Copley requirements satisfied — stating the cost, not being threatening, advising the claimant to consult their solicitor)
- Challenge the agency relationship between the claimant and the hire company where there is evidence of the hire company acting in its own interests rather than the claimant's
key_principles: Mitigation is judged on what the claimant actually knew — not on what they could theoretically have done had they been better informed; delay not caused by the claimant's own conduct cannot be laid at their door (Mattocks v Mann [1973] RTR 13; Burdis v Livesey [2002] EWCA Civ 510; Piercy v Kangethe)

The standard of reasonableness for mitigation is deliberately not high — the defendant is the admitted wrongdoer and their breach created the difficulty; measures taken to escape it should not be weighed in nice scales (Banco de Portugal v Waterlow [1932] AC 452)

Only the claimant's own knowledge and conduct is assessed for mitigation — the greater commercial knowledge of hire companies and accident management companies as enablers is not brought into consideration

A hire company and its associated solicitors acting as the claimant's agents may have their conduct attributed to the claimant; but an independent repairer with no contractual relationship to the claimant does not — delays at the repairer are not the claimant's failure to mitigate

Impecuniosity is assessed by reference to the full cost of the realistic hire period — even a 'prudent' claimant with a £4,000 emergency fund was impecunious when even a reasonable 21-day hire at BHR would have consumed nearly all of it (Piercy v Kangethe)

A defendant seeking to argue a roadworthy vehicle should have been returned to the claimant must prove the claimant knew it was roadworthy — if the claimant was never told, the defendant cannot discharge the mitigation burden

DJ Lumb (Oxford CC, 2025) noted that credit hire charges may appear 'staggering' and that there is no appetite in the insurance industry to campaign for Parliamentary reform to control them — current law is binding on County Court judges regardless of the quantum
cases_referenced: Piercy v Kangethe (Oxford County Court, DJ Lumb, 2025)
Mattocks v Mann [1973] RTR 13
Burdis v Livesey [2002] EWCA Civ 510
Banco de Portugal v Waterlow [1932] AC 452
Copley v Lawn [2009] EWCA Civ 580
articles.source: Susan Carville and Sarah Cartlidge (Weightmans LLP), 'Credit Hire: A vehicle's roadworthiness and the claimant's duty to mitigate their loss', weightmans.com, 8 May 2025

articles.title: Taxi Drivers and Credit Hire — Loss of Profit vs Hire Charges: Hussain v EUI Ltd [2019] EWHC 2647 (QB)
articles.summary: Hussain v EUI Ltd [2019] EWHC 2647 (QB) is the leading High Court authority on the correct measure of loss for taxi drivers in credit hire claims. The High Court confirmed that where a claimant's vehicle is a pure profit-earning chattel with no social, domestic or pleasure (SD&P) use, damages are limited to loss of profit — not the credit hire rate. Need for SD&P use is not self-proving and cannot simply be inferred from owning, insuring and taxing a car primarily used for commercial purposes. Analysis by Weightmans (defendant-side) with a four-part framework for assessing taxi claims.
articles.content:
**The Case**

The claimant was a self-employed private hire taxi driver whose BMW 320D was damaged in a road traffic accident. He hired a Mercedes E220 (a plated private hire vehicle) for 18 days at a total cost of £6,596.50 — a sum almost equal to his full year's profit from taxi driving. He presented a claim for the full credit hire charges.

**First Instance**

The trial judge awarded only £423, assessed by reference to the loss of profit the claimant would have earned over the 18-day period. The court found:
- The claimant had not acted reasonably in hiring a replacement at a cost almost equal to a full year's profit
- Based on the claimant's own evidence, he did not need the vehicle for social, domestic and pleasure (SD&P) use — there was a second vehicle in the household
- The claimant's vehicle was a pure profit-earning chattel; loss of profit was therefore the correct measure of loss
- Alternatively, if wrong on loss of profit, hire would be limited to the BHR of £975

**The High Court Appeal**

The claimant appealed on two grounds: (i) damages should not have been limited to loss of profit; (ii) the defendant's BHR evidence was wrongly accepted. The High Court dismissed the appeal on the first ground and declined to consider the second.

On the SD&P point, the High Court held: 'Need for social and domestic purposes is not self-proving and, in this case, cannot simply be inferred from Mr Hussain's actions in acquiring, insuring, taxing and maintaining his BMW since the car was primarily required for business use.' There was no evidence from the claimant that the vehicle was needed for anything other than commercial taxi purposes. Loss of profit remained the correct measure.

**The Legal Framework — Pure Profit-Earning Chattels**

The loss of profit approach for pure profit-earning chattels derives from Commissioners for Executing the Office of Lord High Admiral of the United Kingdom v Owners of the Steamship Valeria [1922] 2 AC 242. Where a vehicle is used exclusively as a commercial/income-generating asset, the correct measure of damages for its loss of use is the profit that would have been earned — not the cost of hiring a replacement. The rationale is that the claimant's actual loss is the lost income, not the cost of maintaining an income stream through hire.

**Weightmans' Four-Part Framework for Taxi Claims**

Drawing on Hussain, Weightmans set out a structured approach for defendants assessing taxi credit hire claims:

1. *Cost of hire is less than hypothetical loss of profit:* The cost of hire will be awarded — it represents a reasonable attempt to mitigate (the claimant has mitigated more cheaply than their actual loss).

2. *Cost of hire significantly exceeds the avoided loss of profit:* The court will ordinarily limit damages to loss of profit. The hire charges are disproportionate to the actual commercial loss sustained.

3. *Claimant is genuinely impecunious and has evidenced this:* Loss of profit is not an appropriate assessment where the claimant could not afford to forego income. In that case, the cost of hire (at credit hire rate) is likely to be awarded. Note the emphasis on evidenced impecuniosity — bare assertion is insufficient (Diriye v Bojaj [2020] EWCA Civ 1400).

4. *Claimant establishes genuine SD&P use:* Where the vehicle is used for mixed purposes (commercial and social/domestic), the claimant will ordinarily be entitled to recover the cost of hire rather than merely loss of profit.

**The SD&P Rate Argument**

Weightmans note that even where a taxi driver successfully establishes SD&P use, the rate for the SD&P element should be assessed by reference to the BHR for a standard (non-taxi) vehicle — not the specialist plated taxi hire rate. A pecunious claimant with SD&P needs can afford to hire a standard vehicle from a mainstream provider for domestic use, while their commercial loss is separately assessed as loss of profit. This keeps the two heads of loss distinct and avoids awarding the full specialist taxi hire rate where only a modest domestic hire was actually needed.

**Practical Implications for Defendants**

- Always investigate whether the damaged vehicle was used for SD&P purposes — this is the claimant's burden to establish; it is not self-proving
- Obtain evidence of the claimant's actual profit from taxi driving (accounts, tax returns, private hire licence records) to establish the correct loss of profit figure
- Where hire charges substantially exceed annual profit, challenge proportionality directly
- Where the claimant asserts impecuniosity, require full financial disclosure and apply the Diriye pleading standards
- Even where SD&P is conceded, argue the rate for that element should be a standard BHR vehicle rather than a specialist taxi rate
- Note that Hussain is not a 'silver bullet' — each case turns on its own evidence; a claimant who provides strong evidence of both commercial loss and genuine domestic need for the taxi may recover more than loss of profit alone

**Interaction with Rashid v Naylor [2018]**

Rashid v Naylor [2018] 8 WLUK 363 confirmed that where a taxi driver is impecunious, the full credit hire rate may be recoverable — but the claimant's duty to mitigate (by replacing the vehicle earlier, for example by paying the policy excess and taking the insurer's write-off payment) is not extinguished by impecuniosity. Hussain and Rashid together define the outer limits of taxi driver claims: loss of profit is the default for pecunious drivers with no SD&P use; impecuniosity and/or SD&P need can expand recovery but are both subject to evidential requirements and ongoing mitigation obligations.
key_principles: Where a taxi driver's vehicle is a pure profit-earning chattel with no SD&P use, the correct measure of loss is loss of profit — not the credit hire rate (Hussain v EUI Ltd [2019] EWHC 2647; Valeria [1922] 2 AC 242)

Need for SD&P use is not self-proving and cannot be inferred merely from owning, insuring and maintaining a vehicle primarily used for business — the claimant must adduce positive evidence of domestic use (Hussain)

Where hire costs are less than the avoided loss of profit, hire costs are awarded as a reasonable mitigation measure; where hire costs significantly exceed avoided loss of profit, recovery is limited to loss of profit

Genuinely impecunious taxi drivers who evidence their impecuniosity are likely to recover the credit hire rate rather than loss of profit — but evidenced impecuniosity is required; bare assertion is insufficient

Even where SD&P use is established, the rate for that element should be assessed by reference to the BHR for a standard non-taxi vehicle, not the specialist plated taxi hire rate (Weightmans' argument)
Taxi claims are fact-sensitive and Hussain is not a universal rule — a claimant with strong evidence of both commercial loss and genuine domestic need may recover beyond loss of profit alone

Impecunious taxi drivers who prove impecuniosity still have ongoing mitigation obligations — they must take reasonable steps (such as paying their policy excess to replace the vehicle sooner) rather than hiring indefinitely (Rashid v Naylor)
cases_referenced: Hussain v EUI Ltd [2019] EWHC 2647 (QB)
Commissioners for Executing the Office of Lord High Admiral v Owners of the Steamship Valeria [1922] 2 AC 242
Rashid v Naylor [2018] 8 WLUK 363
Diriye v Kaltrina Bojaj; Quick-Sure Insurance Ltd [2020] EWCA Civ 1400
Humayum Hussain v EUI Ltd [2019] EWHC 2647 (QB)
articles.source: Adam Skelland (Weightmans LLP), 'Credit hire costs, taxi drivers, loss of profit and the correct assessment of loss', weightmans.com, 25 October 2019

articles.title: Challenging Engineering Evidence in Credit Hire Claims — Hodroje v Storm of London & Ageas (2021)
articles.summary: Hodroje v Storm of London Ltd & Ageas Insurance (2021) is a county court decision analysed by Weightmans that exposes how engineering reports in credit hire claims can be fundamentally flawed — where an engineer's handwritten notes differ from the typed report, findings are based on speculation rather than direct examination, and the report is produced and signed by the engineering company rather than the attending inspector. The vehicle was found to be roadworthy; the hire, recovery and storage claims fell away; and costs were awarded on the indemnity basis. The case highlights the importance of rigorously challenging engineering evidence in cases involving seemingly trivial damage declared a total loss.
articles.content:
**Background**

A minor collision in January 2019 caused what appeared to be two tramline scuffs to the rear bumper of the claimant's Mercedes. Despite the trivial nature of the damage, the claimant claimed for the pre-accident value (PAV) of the Mercedes as a total loss, together with credit hire charges, recovery and storage. The claimant instructed Evans Harding Engineers QCGS Ltd ('EH'), whose inspector Mr Simon Levitt ('SL') attended to inspect the Mercedes in February 2019. EH produced a report in SL's name carrying a CPR r.35 expert declaration. The report declared the Mercedes an undriveable write-off with a PAV (less salvage) of £2,394.

**What Emerged at Trial**

The first day of trial in April 2021 produced a series of extraordinary revelations. Under cross-examination:

*The engineer had not seen his own report before trial.* SL attended with only his handwritten notes and photographs — not the typed report produced in his name. The judge adjourned to allow service of the notes and photographs.

*The report differed materially from the handwritten notes.* The salvage figure in SL's handwritten notes was £665; in the typed report it was £266 — a lower salvage figure that increased the net PAV claim. Other discrepancies also emerged.

*The signatures were not signed by the engineer.* The two signatures on the report were electronic signatures placed on the document by EH without SL signing the report off. SL confirmed that his usual practice was to carry out an inspection, send his notes to EH, and EH would produce a report in his name which he would not see before it was sent to the defendants.

*The 'undriveable' finding was not based on direct examination.*
- The boot lid: SL's notes recorded that the boot would not open. In cross-examination he accepted that the battery was flat at inspection — he had not been able to attempt to open the boot electronically. The comment about the boot not opening came only from his 'instructions', not from his own testing.
- The exhaust muffler: recorded in the report as needing replacement. SL accepted the engine was never started during his inspection; his concern was purely an observation from the exterior that it might be too loud or unsafe. No direct testing was carried out.
- The write-off conclusion rested entirely on the boot and exhaust muffler points — neither of which had been directly tested.

SL also confirmed he carried out approximately 10 inspections a day, around 75% for claimants and mainly for Evans Harding.

**The Vehicle Was Found Intact**

The trial was adjourned and the defendants located the Mercedes at an address occupied by the claimant's wife. Photographs confirmed no repairs had been undertaken — directly contradicting the claimant's earlier evidence that the car had been repaired by his employer at a cost of around £2,000. The claimant subsequently served a supplementary statement claiming only partial repairs had been done and disclosed the original instructions to EH and SL, which did not suggest the engineer had been directed that the boot would not open.

At the resumed trial in June 2021 neither the claimant nor SL attended to give evidence despite being given the opportunity to do so.

**Judgment**

The judge found SL's evidential concessions 'surprising'. He was candid at the first hearing but candour and credibility are different things. The judge did not find SL dishonest, but concluded that he had conducted the most superficial inspection and put forward findings that could not have been supported by a proper expert examination. At best, his findings were 'speculative assertions upon which the court could not rely'.

The PAV claim failed — not proved. Since the Mercedes was roadworthy, the entire hire, recovery and storage claim fell away — there was no reasonable need to mitigate loss by hiring an alternative vehicle if the claimant's own vehicle was driveable. The claimant was ordered to pay the defendants' costs on the indemnity basis and to repay an interim payment already received.

Most significantly, the judge indicated an intention to join both SL and EH to the proceedings for the purposes of a non-party costs order, given the findings about how EH had altered SL's notes and produced a report not signed off by him. The claimant's ATE insurers subsequently agreed to pay the costs, making the non-party costs application unnecessary.

**Key Legal Principle**

A claim for credit hire is a claim in mitigation for loss of use of a vehicle. A claimant will only recover hire charges if they had a reasonable need to mitigate that loss by hiring an alternative vehicle. In a total loss/PAV case, this means the vehicle must actually be undriveable — which is precisely why the engineer's inspection is so important. If the vehicle is roadworthy, the hire claim fails entirely.

**Defendant Practice Points (Weightmans' Analysis)**

The case demonstrates the value of rigorously testing engineering evidence, particularly in cases where seemingly trivial damage has been declared a total loss. Defendants should consider:

- Requesting the engineer's original handwritten inspection notes and photographs under CPR disclosure, not just the typed report
- Checking whether the CPR r.35 declaration was actually signed by the attending inspector or electronically applied by the engineering company
- Probing at trial how many inspections the engineer carries out per day and what proportion are for claimants — relevant to independence and thoroughness
- Identifying whether findings in the report (particularly on roadworthiness) were based on direct testing or merely observation and inference
- Where the vehicle has been declared a write-off but not repaired, attempting to locate and photograph the vehicle — its actual condition may contradict the engineer's findings
- Where an engineer's report is based on speculative findings not supported by proper examination, apply to exclude or challenge the weight of the evidence; failure of the PAV claim brings down the entire hire claim with it
- Where engineering evidence has been manipulated or the report does not reflect the inspector's actual findings, consider a non-party costs application against the engineering company
key_principles: A credit hire claim is a claim in mitigation — a claimant only recovers hire charges if they had a reasonable need to hire; if the vehicle is roadworthy, that need does not arise and the hire claim fails entirely
In a total loss/PAV case the vehicle's roadworthiness is the threshold question — an engineering report declaring a vehicle undriveable is critical and must withstand scrutiny at trial

An engineer's CPR r.35 declaration must be personally signed by the attending inspector; a declaration signed electronically by the engineering company without the inspector's approval undermines the report's admissibility and weight

Discrepancies between an engineer's handwritten inspection notes and the typed report produced by their firm are a serious red flag — defendants should always request original notes and photographs under disclosure

Findings in an engineering report based on speculation or inference rather than direct testing (e.g., boot not tested because battery flat; exhaust not started) are 'speculative assertions upon which the court cannot rely' (Hodroje)
High inspection volumes (10 per day, 75% for claimants, mainly for one company) are relevant to questions of thoroughness and independence — a proper CPR r.35 expert should be genuinely independent

Where engineering evidence is found to be fundamentally flawed, a non-party costs order against the engineering company is available and the court may join the engineer and their firm to proceedings for that purpose
Failure of the PAV/total loss claim carries the hire, recovery and storage claims with it — all depend on the vehicle being undriveable
cases_referenced: Hodroje v Storm of London Ltd & Ageas Insurance (County Court, 2021)
Mattocks v Mann [1973] RTR 13
Burdis v Livesey [2002] EWCA Civ 510
articles.source: Weightmans LLP, 'When expert engineering evidence in a vehicle damage and credit hire claim is not what it seems', weightmans.com, 5 August 2021

articles.title: Impecuniosity Debarment: RA v Rhondda Cynon Taf CBC — Consequences of Failing to Prove Financial Need (2026)
articles.summary: RA v Rhondda Cynon Taf County Borough Council (2026) is a county court decision analysed by Tom Danter of Dolmans Solicitors in which a claimant who failed to comply with court directions requiring documentary evidence of impecuniosity was debarred from asserting impecuniosity for both rate and period. Applying Zurich v Umerji, the court limited the hire period to 42 days (reasonable for a non-impecunious claimant in a total loss case) and applied the basic hire rate — reducing a £21,811.20 credit hire claim to an award of £2,395.17, just over 10% of the sum claimed. The case is a primer on the obstacles to proving impecuniosity, the consequences of inadequate preparation, and the debarment mechanism as a defendant tool.
articles.content:
**Background**

The claimant's vehicle was a total loss following a road traffic accident for which the defendant local authority (represented by Dolmans Solicitors) admitted liability. The claimant hired a replacement vehicle for 71 days at a credit hire rate, producing a claim of £21,811.20 (plus £240 additional charges). The defendant challenged both the rate (seeking BHR) and the period (arguing 42 days was the reasonable limit for a non-impecunious claimant in a total loss case).

**The Impecuniosity Issue**

The claimant asserted impecuniosity — that they could not afford to hire at the basic hire rate and therefore the full credit hire rate was recoverable. Impecuniosity, if established, has two consequences: it entitles the claimant to the full credit hire rate (rather than the lower BHR) and it may also extend the reasonable hire period (because an impecunious claimant cannot be expected to fund a replacement vehicle from their own resources while awaiting resolution of the claim).

The defendant served requests for financial disclosure. The court made directions requiring the claimant to produce documentary evidence of impecuniosity by a specified date. The claimant failed to comply adequately with those directions — the documentary evidence provided was insufficient to support the impecuniosity assertion.

**Debarment from Asserting Impecuniosity**

The court applied the principle from Zurich Insurance Plc v Umerji [2014] EWCA Civ 357: a claimant who fails to comply with court directions requiring disclosure of financial information to support an impecuniosity plea may be debarred from asserting impecuniosity at trial. Critically, the court held that debarment applied not only to the rate question (whether the credit hire rate or BHR applied) but also to the period question — the length of hire that could reasonably be recovered.

This double application of Umerji is significant. In a total loss case, an impecunious claimant may reasonably need to hire for an extended period because they cannot afford to pay the policy excess or purchase a replacement vehicle without first receiving their claim settlement. A non-impecunious claimant is expected to take steps to replace their vehicle sooner, using their own resources if necessary. Debarring the claimant from asserting impecuniosity on the period question therefore had a major impact on the recoverable hire period.

**Period of Hire — 42 Days**

The court found that 42 days was a reasonable hire period for a non-impecunious claimant in a total loss case. The claimant had hired for 71 days — 29 days beyond what the court considered reasonable for someone without financial constraints on replacement. The additional period was irrecoverable.

**Rate — Basic Hire Rate Applied**

Debarred from asserting impecuniosity on the rate question, the claimant was limited to the BHR. The defendant had adduced BHR evidence. The court accepted the BHR as the applicable rate for the 42-day recoverable period.

**Last-Minute Relief from Sanctions Application Refused**

Before trial, the claimant applied for relief from sanctions (under CPR r.3.9 and the Denton principles) to be permitted to rely on the impecuniosity evidence despite the earlier non-compliance. The application was made late — close to the trial date. The court refused relief. The Denton three-stage test requires assessment of: (1) the seriousness and significance of the breach; (2) why the default occurred; (3) all the circumstances of the case, including the need for litigation to be conducted efficiently and at proportionate cost and the need to enforce compliance with rules, practice directions and court orders. On the facts, the breach was serious and significant, no adequate explanation was provided, and granting relief would have prejudiced the defendant and undermined the court's case management directions. Prior non-compliance in the proceedings was also noted against the claimant.

**The Result**

The final award was £2,395.17 — just over 10% of the £21,811.20 + £240 claimed. Need for a replacement vehicle was established; the claimant recovered something. But the combination of debarment from impecuniosity, a restricted period of 42 days, and BHR rather than credit hire rate reduced the claim by approximately 90%.

**Dolmans' Practical Analysis — Obstacles to Proving Impecuniosity**

Tom Danter's analysis for Local Government Lawyer (March 2026) identifies the key obstacles claimants face in proving impecuniosity and the corresponding opportunities for defendants:

- *Financial disclosure is the battleground.* Impecuniosity requires detailed financial evidence — bank statements, wage slips, tax returns, evidence of savings and credit availability. Vague assertions are wholly inadequate (Diriye v Bojaj [2020] EWCA Civ 1400). Courts will not draw inferences from the fact the claimant hired on credit terms.

- *Court directions must be complied with precisely and promptly.* Failure to produce the required documents by the court's deadline triggers the Umerji debarment mechanism. There is no second chance to cure deficient disclosure after the deadline has passed without a successful relief from sanctions application.

- *Relief from sanctions is not a safety net.* Post-Denton, courts take a stringent approach to late relief applications, particularly where the breach was serious, unexplained, and has caused prejudice to the other side. A late relief application shortly before trial is likely to fail.

- *Debarment affects both rate and period.* The Umerji debarment is not limited to the rate question. It extends to the period question wherever impecuniosity is the reason the claimant claims an extended hire period. Defendants should always argue for double debarment where the impecuniosity evidence is inadequate.

- *Reasonable period for non-impecunious claimants.* In total loss cases, 42 days was found to be the reasonable period for a claimant without financial constraints. Defendants should be prepared with evidence and argument on the appropriate period for each claim type (total loss, CAT C/D repair, category B write-off, etc.).

- *Local authorities and public bodies as defendants.* This case also illustrates that local authorities who are defendants in credit hire claims can and do successfully challenge inflated claims — the Dolmans approach of methodically attacking rate, period, and impecuniosity on evidence-based grounds is available to any defendant, not just insurers.

**Key Takeaway for Defendants**

A well-managed impecuniosity challenge — early requests for financial disclosure, timely applications to court if disclosure is inadequate, and a clear debarment argument at trial — can reduce a credit hire claim to a fraction of its pleaded value. RA v Rhondda Cynon Taf is an illustration of what a 90% reduction looks like in practice.
key_principles: A claimant who fails to comply with court directions requiring documentary evidence of impecuniosity may be debarred from asserting impecuniosity at trial (Zurich Insurance Plc v Umerji [2014] EWCA Civ 357; RA v Rhondda Cynon Taf CBC)

Umerji debarment applies to both the rate question (credit hire rate vs BHR) and the period question (reasonable length of hire) — a claimant debarred from asserting impecuniosity recovers at BHR for the period a non-impecunious claimant would reasonably have hired (RA v Rhondda Cynon Taf)

In a total loss case, 42 days was held to be a reasonable hire period for a non-impecunious claimant — a claimant who cannot establish impecuniosity is expected to replace their vehicle sooner using their own resources

A last-minute application for relief from sanctions to cure deficient impecuniosity disclosure is likely to fail under the Denton three-stage test where the breach was serious, unexplained, and prejudicial to the defendant

Impecuniosity pleadings must contain full income, expenditure and explanation of why the claimant could not hire at BHR — bare assertions are not sufficient; failure to plead properly from the outset risks both procedural sanctions and debarment (Diriye v Bojaj [2020] EWCA Civ 1400)

A 90% reduction in a credit hire claim (from £21,811.20 to £2,395.17) is achievable through effective challenge to rate, period, and impecuniosity where the claimant has failed to produce adequate financial evidence

The Umerji/debarment route is available to all defendants in credit hire claims, not just insurers — local authorities and public bodies can and should deploy the same evidence-based challenge methodology
cases_referenced: RA v Rhondda Cynon Taf County Borough Council (County Court, 2026)
Zurich Insurance Plc v Umerji [2014] EWCA Civ 357
Diriye v Kaltrina Bojaj; Quick-Sure Insurance Ltd [2020] EWCA Civ 1400
Denton v TH White Ltd [2014] EWCA Civ 906
Mattocks v Mann [1973] RTR 13
articles.source: Tom Danter (Dolmans Solicitors), 'Impecuniosity and other issues in credit hire claims', Local Government Lawyer, March 2026

articles.title: Scotland — Who Bears the Burden of BHR Evidence? The Pursuer Does Not Need to Prove Impecuniosity First (Callaghan, 2013)
articles.summary: Michael Callaghan of Irwin Mitchell Scotland challenges the widely held view in Scotland that a pursuer claiming credit hire charges must either produce a basic hire report (BHR) or prove impecuniosity in order to succeed. Drawing on Tierney v Tissington (Glasgow Sheriff Court, 2013), Liddle v Brit Insurance [2011] CSOH 145 and the Court of Appeal in Pattni v First Leicester Buses [2011] EWCA Civ 1384, he argues that the burden lies on the defender to produce BHR evidence showing the credit hire rate was excessive — and that the impecuniosity question only arises once the defender has discharged that evidential burden. Until a BHR is produced, the pursuer is entitled to recover the full credit hire rate regardless of their financial means.
articles.content:
**The Widely Held View — and Why It Is Wrong**

A commonly accepted position in Scottish credit hire litigation is that a pursuer's claim for credit hire charges will fail unless they can either: (a) produce a basic hire report (BHR) demonstrating the credit hire rate was reasonable, or (b) prove impecuniosity. Callaghan argues this view is simply wrong.

The position had been adopted in a handful of sheriff court decisions. In Walker v Chesapeake (Glasgow Sheriff Court, 11 October 2010) Sheriff Mitchell allowed a defender's motion to ordain the pursuer to aver whether she was impecunious, on the basis that 'the pursuer's claim for hire costs can only be relevant if he was impecunious at the relevant time.' In Gee v AXA Corporate Solutions Assurance (Edinburgh Sheriff Court, 27 August 2012) Sheriff Principal Stephen held that where a pursuer had not established impecuniosity and there were no findings on basic hire rates, the defender was entitled to absolvitor. But Callaghan submits these decisions were wrong.

**The Leading Authorities Properly Analysed**

The proper analysis of Dimond v Lovell [2002] 1 AC 384 and Lagden v O'Connor [2004] 1 AC 1067 does not support the view that the pursuer must come with BHR evidence or an impecuniosity plea. In Dimond, the defendants had led evidence of basic hire rates, showing they compared favourably with the credit hire rate — it was the defendant who produced that evidence. And Lord Hope in Lagden was explicit: 'If the defendant can show [emphasis added] that the cost that was incurred was more than was reasonable... the amount expended on the hire must be reduced.' The duty to show that the rate was excessive falls on the defender, not the pursuer.

**Scottish and English Authority Supporting the Pursuer's Position**

In the Outer House, Lord McEwan in Liddle v Brit Insurance [2011] CSOH 145 confirmed that it is for the defender to produce the necessary evidence. The Glasgow Sheriff Court in Tierney v Tissington (19 March 2013) also refused the defender's motion to ordain the pursuer to lodge a BHR, citing Lord Hope in Lagden and Lord McEwan in Liddle: 'the reality and substance of the situation in this case, as indeed in most, is that it is the defender who is challenging as excessive the actual sum expended by the pursuer and... it is for the defender to aver and prove such excess.' The English Court of Appeal in Pattni v First Leicester Buses [2011] EWCA Civ 1384 was equally clear: 'it is for a defendant to demonstrate, by evidence, that there is a difference between the credit hire charge agreed between the claimant and the credit hire company and the BHR.'

**The Practical Consequence — Impecuniosity Is Only Relevant After BHR Is Produced**

Callaghan's analysis has a vital practical consequence: the impecuniosity question does not arise until the defender produces BHR evidence showing that cheaper rates were available. Only once that evidential threshold is crossed does the pursuer need to consider whether they qualify for the Lagden exception (recovering the full credit hire rate despite the premium over BHR, by virtue of financial inability to hire upfront). If no BHR is produced by the defender, the pursuer recovers the full credit hire rate regardless of their financial position — whether they are Bill Gates or living in penury, as Callaghan puts it.

**The Practical Implication for Pursuers**

Pursuers' agents are frequently told by insurers and their solicitors that a credit hire claim will not be considered without a BHR and/or impecuniosity evidence. Callaghan submits this demand should be resisted. Provided a pursuer can prove need for hire, that the hire period was reasonable, and that the hire contract is valid, they are entitled to recover the full credit hire rate unless and until the defender produces BHR evidence demonstrating that cheaper rates were available. The burden of proof is on the defender.
key_principles: In Scotland, as in England and Wales, the burden of producing BHR evidence to show that credit hire rates were excessive lies on the defender — not the pursuer (Tierney v Tissington [2013]; Liddle v Brit Insurance [2011] CSOH 145; Lagden v O'Connor [2004] 1 AC 1067, Lord Hope at para 27)

A pursuer is not required to produce a BHR or plead impecuniosity to advance a credit hire claim — they are entitled to recover the full credit hire rate unless the defender discharges the evidential burden of showing cheaper rates were available

Impecuniosity only becomes relevant once the defender has produced BHR evidence demonstrating a cheaper rate was available — it is then, and only then, that the Lagden exception crystallises for the pursuer
The decisions in Walker v Chesapeake and Gee v AXA placing the burden on the pursuer to establish impecuniosity or produce a BHR are, on this analysis, wrongly decided

Pattni v First Leicester Buses [2011] EWCA Civ 1384 (Court of Appeal, England): 'it is for a defendant to demonstrate, by evidence, that there is a difference between the credit hire charge and the BHR' — persuasive in Scotland
Pursuers' agents should resist demands from insurers requiring BHR evidence or impecuniosity disclosure as a precondition to considering the claim — such demands have no proper legal foundation
cases_referenced: Tierney v Tissington (Glasgow Sheriff Court, 19 March 2013)
Liddle v Brit Insurance [2011] CSOH 145 (Outer House, Court of Session)
Walker v Chesapeake (Glasgow Sheriff Court, 11 October 2010)
Gee v AXA Corporate Solutions Assurance (Edinburgh Sheriff Court, 27 August 2012)
Pattni v First Leicester Buses [2011] EWCA Civ 1384
Lagden v O'Connor [2004] 1 AC 1067
Dimond v Lovell [2002] 1 AC 384
articles.source: Michael Callaghan (Irwin Mitchell Scotland LLP, Glasgow), 'Credit hire: back to basics', Law Society of Scotland Journal, July 2013

articles.title: Scotland — The Defendant/Insurer Perspective: Unsettled Case Law, Costs Regime and Strategic Litigation (Keoghs, 2020)
articles.summary: Fraser McAndry and Emily Jeffery of Keoghs (July 2020) provide an insurer/defender perspective on credit hire litigation in Scotland, highlighting that the legal landscape is considerably less settled than in England and Wales. Case precedent in Scotland is limited; BHR methodology is inconsistent across sheriffs; English Court of Appeal decisions such as Stevens and McBride are persuasive but not binding; the costs regime differs significantly from England; and QOCS was imminent at the date of writing. Keoghs identify opportunities for strategic litigation to drive a uniform approach. The article is an important counterpoint to the claimant-side Scottish materials.
articles.content:
**Scotland vs England and Wales: An Unsettled Landscape**

While credit hire law in England and Wales is largely settled, the same cannot be said for Scotland. Fraser McAndry and Emily Jeffery of Keoghs write that Scottish credit hire defence arguments are historically similar to those in England — covering impecuniosity, Consumer Credit Act issues, and rate challenges — but this is not always based on established case precedent. Scottish case law on credit hire is limited, and the courts have yet to decide some fundamental issues. Notably, the question of Intervention — the practice of insurers offering third-party claimants a replacement vehicle directly, potentially cutting off their entitlement to a credit hire rate — had not been authoritatively decided in Scotland at the time of writing.

**BHR Methodology: Inconsistency Between Sheriffs and English Courts**

The most common approach to BHR in Scotland has been for sheriffs to consider the average of the rates produced by the parties. This is reflected in written decisions including Clark v City of Edinburgh Council and Greenlees v Allianz. However, this approach of taking an arithmetic average is inconsistent with the more sophisticated methodology developed in England following Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management [2015] EWCA Civ 93 and McBride v UK Insurance Ltd [2017] EWCA Civ 144. In England, Courts take a nuanced approach to which BHR rates are admissible and how they should be assessed — a simple average is not sufficient. English Court of Appeal decisions are persuasive in Scotland but not binding, and individual sheriffs have taken varying approaches. This inconsistency can result in higher recoveries for pursuers than an English court would award.

**The Scottish Costs Regime**

The costs regime in Scotland is fundamentally different from England and Wales. Defence strategies must be tailored accordingly. At the time of writing, the position was:

- Simple Procedure (claims up to £5,000): For claims up to £1,500, costs are limited to £150. For claims between £1,500 and £3,000, costs are 10% of the sum sued for (though if a defence is lodged but not relied on, costs may revert to non-injury scale). For claims between £3,000 and £5,000, pursuers are entitled to scale costs per item of work carried out.
- Ordinary Cause (claims over £5,000): Pursuers are entitled to a higher scale of costs charged per item of work. This is more comparable to English county court costs.

These cost differentials have a direct bearing on the economics of defending Scottish credit hire claims. Low-value claims in Simple Procedure carry low costs exposure for defenders, which affects settlement decisions.

**Qualified One Way Costs Shifting (QOCS) Coming to Scotland**

At the date of writing (July 2020), QOCS was imminent in Scotland. QOCS prevents defendants from recovering their costs against claimants in personal injury cases even if the defendant wins, save in cases of fraud or fundamentally dishonest claims. Its introduction to Scotland was expected to change the landscape significantly, creating similar dynamics to those seen in England post-2013.

**Credit Hire Fraud in Scotland**

Keoghs note that the differing costs regime in Scotland had created opportunities for credit hire organisations to maximise revenue, including through fraudulent activity. Counter-fraud identification is therefore a key element of Scottish credit hire defence strategy.

**Strategic Litigation to Drive a Uniform Approach**

Given the limited and inconsistent case precedent in Scotland, Keoghs identified a clear market need for strategic litigation to drive a uniform judicial approach — comparable to the strategic appellate cases (Stevens, McBride, Hussain 2) that established the framework in England and Wales. Without such litigation, Scottish sheriffs will continue to take inconsistent approaches to BHR methodology, intervention, and other contested issues, creating unpredictability for insurers handling Scottish claims.
key_principles: Credit hire case precedent in Scotland is limited and less settled than in England and Wales — sheriffs have taken inconsistent approaches to BHR methodology, and key issues such as Intervention have not been authoritatively decided
English Court of Appeal decisions on credit hire (including Stevens and McBride) are persuasive but not binding in Scotland — Scottish courts may reach different outcomes on BHR methodology

Scottish BHR practice has tended towards arithmetic averaging of rates produced (Clark v Edinburgh; Greenlees v Allianz), which is inconsistent with the more nuanced English approach following Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management [2015] EWCA Civ 93

The Scottish costs regime differs materially from England and Wales — Simple Procedure costs are capped (£150 for claims under £1,500; 10% for £1,500–£3,000) while Ordinary Cause costs follow a scale; defence strategies must be tailored accordingly
QOCS was introduced in Scotland following the date of this article, creating similar claimant-side cost protection as in England and Wales post-2013
The absence of authoritative Scottish appellate decisions on credit hire creates an opportunity (and need) for strategic litigation to establish a uniform approach across the Scottish courts
cases_referenced: Clark v City of Edinburgh Council (Sheriff Court, Scotland)
Greenlees v Allianz (Sheriff Court, Scotland)
Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management Ltd [2015] EWCA Civ 93
McBride v UK Insurance Ltd [2017] EWCA Civ 144
Lagden v O'Connor [2004] 1 AC 1067
articles.source: Fraser McAndry and Emily Jeffery (Keoghs), 'The Future of Credit Hire in Scotland', Keoghs Insight, 27 July 2020

articles.title: Avis General Conditions of Rental — BHR-Relevant Provisions (GB-EN-03/2026)
articles.summary: The Avis General Conditions of Rental (March 2026) govern all Avis UK rentals and are directly relevant to BHR challenges in credit hire litigation. Key provisions establish the structure of the basic hire rate (vehicle rental price), what is and is not included, the no-refund-on-early-return rule, vehicle availability obligations, and prohibited uses.
articles.content:
The Avis General Conditions of Rental (version GB-EN-03/2026) apply to all Avis rentals across Europe and must be read alongside the Location Specific Conditions. The contract is with the vehicle rental provider named on the rental agreement — in the UK this is ordinarily Avis Budget UK Limited (00802486), though certain locations are operated by regional franchisees. The governing law is that of the country of collection.

RENTAL PERIOD AND EARLY RETURN (ss.3 and 12): The rental period runs from pick-up to acknowledged return. Crucially, there is no refund for unused days if the vehicle is returned early (s.12). This provision directly supports a claimant's contention that they needed the vehicle for the full hire period — returning early forfeits payment already committed.

COST STRUCTURE — WHAT IS THE BASIC HIRE RATE? (s.4): The 'vehicle rental price' is the base charge calculated on start/end dates, location, and vehicle type. The hirer must additionally pay: damage costs, specialist cleaning, towing charges, toll charges, parking, traffic or other fines, and related administration fees. Optional extras (CDW, SCDW, GPS, child seats, additional drivers etc.) are separate contractual add-ons and must not be conflated with the basic vehicle hire rate for BHR comparison purposes.

DAMAGE WAIVER (s.7): A basic level of damage waiver is included as standard within Europe — it reduces (not eliminates) the excess. The standard excess is not zero; SCDW must be purchased separately to reduce the excess to zero. This is significant for BHR comparisons: if the credit hire rate purports to include zero-excess protection, the defendant is entitled to strip out the cost of SCDW (up to £30.28/day from Avis UK) for comparison purposes.

VEHICLE AVAILABILITY (s.6): If Avis cannot provide the requested vehicle group at pick-up, they will provide a higher group at no extra cost, a lower group at a reduced charge, or if the hirer must rent from another company, Avis will pay the difference. This provision is relevant to impecuniosity arguments — it shows that a credit hire claimant would ordinarily have had access to a comparable vehicle had they been able to rent commercially.

PROHIBITED USES (s.5): The vehicle must not be used for commercial purposes, hire or reward, motorsport, off-road, towing, or transport of hazardous materials. Use for hire or reward voids all waivers. This provision mirrors the credit hire company's own restrictions and is relevant where the defendant argues the claimant was using the vehicle commercially.

LATE RETURN (s.13): Unless agreed otherwise the vehicle must be returned at the agreed time. Late return triggers additional daily charges at 'pay at location' rates plus a late return processing fee (£15/day in mainland UK under the Country Conditions). This supports the proposition that hire periods are contractually fixed and not open-ended.

FUEL (s.9): Fuel is not included in the vehicle rental price. Pay on Return charges are 1.5x to 3x the average fuel price. Fuel Up Front is at cost. EZ Fuel fixed charges apply if the vehicle is returned without a full tank and without a receipted fuel stop within 75 miles. Fuel costs are not part of the basic hire rate for comparison purposes.

DAMAGE PROCESSING FEE: In addition to damage costs, Avis charges a Damage/Loss/Repair Processing Fee of £66 for any damage incident. This is a separate charge from the excess and from optional waivers.
key_principles: The 'vehicle rental price' (basic hire rate) is distinguishable from optional extras — CDW, SCDW, GPS, child seats, and additional driver charges are all add-ons that must be stripped out for like-for-like BHR comparison
No refund for early return (s.12) supports the claimant's position that the full hire period was needed — returning early costs the hirer money
Basic damage waiver is included in the standard Avis rate but only reduces (not eliminates) the excess; zero-excess requires SCDW at up to £30.28/day
If the vehicle requested is unavailable, Avis provides a replacement or pays the difference with a third-party rental — relevant to availability arguments
Fuel is entirely excluded from the hire rate and must be assessed separately in any BHR comparison
Late return attracts additional charges, confirming that rental periods are contractually defined
articles.source: Avis Budget UK Limited, General Conditions of Rental EN, version GB-EN-03/2026 (March 2026). Retrieved 25 March 2026.

articles.title: Avis UK Country Conditions — Rates, Charges and Optional Extras (UK/EN-20/03/2026)
articles.summary: The Avis UK Country Conditions (last updated 20 March 2026) set out the specific charges, daily rates for optional extras, surcharges, and restrictions applicable to all UK rentals. For BHR litigation purposes this document provides precise GBP figures for all optional extras that may be included in a credit hire rate, enabling like-for-like rate stripping for BHR comparison.
articles.content:
The Avis UK Country Conditions (version UK/EN-20/03/2026, last updated 20 March 2026) apply to all Avis rentals in the United Kingdom and supplement the General Conditions of Rental. The principal UK operator is Avis Budget UK Limited (00802486), Avis Budget House, Park Road, Bracknell, RG12 2EW. Several locations are operated by regional franchisees including Regal Vehicle Rentals (Carlisle, Chester, Blackpool Airport, Barrow, Bolton/Warrington), Flynn Bros (Northern Ireland airports), Avis Prestige - Miles & Miles (London, SW7), Polar Cars (Jersey), Mylchreest (Isle of Man), Hewitt Hire (Torquay), Bolts Car Hire (Shetland), and W.R. Tullock (Orkney).

OPTIONAL EXTRAS — DAILY RATES (GBP) AS AT MARCH 2026:

Damage Waiver (DW): £7.76/day mainland UK; £10.12/day Northern Ireland (reduces but does not eliminate excess — often included as standard).

Super Collision Damage Waiver (SCDW): £6.21–£30.28/day mainland UK; £23.00–£27.00/day Northern Ireland. Reduces damage excess to zero on cars; £250 excess on vans. This is typically the largest optional extra and is most likely to be bundled into a credit hire rate.

Theft Protection Waiver (TP): £2.59/day mainland UK; £5.62/day Northern Ireland (often included as standard).

Super Theft Protection Waiver (STP): £1.53–£7.46/day mainland UK.

Windscreen Protection: £5.00–£6.50/day mainland UK; £5.00/day Northern Ireland.

Personal Accident Insurance (PAI): Northern Ireland only — £9.28/day (mainland UK) / £9.00/day (NI). Underwritten by Chubb Limited.

Roadside Assistance Plus: £6.00/day mainland UK (max 13 days / £78.00 for rental up to 28 days); £9.00/day Northern Ireland.

Protection Packages — Zero/Reduced Excess (SCDW + STP + Windscreen): £29.99–£42.99/day.

Protection Packages — Complete Protection (SCDW + STP + Windscreen + Roadside Assistance Plus): £39.99–£52.99/day.

Additional Driver: £14.99/day (max 10 days, £149.90 max for up to 28-day rental) mainland UK; £15.00/day (no daily maximum) Northern Ireland.

GPS/Satellite Navigation: £9.99/day (max 10 days, £99.90 cap) mainland UK; £15.00/day Northern Ireland.

Child Seat (mainland UK): £12.99/day, max 10 days. Northern Ireland: £15.00/day.

YOUNG DRIVER SURCHARGE: Drivers under 25 pay a surcharge; if added at pick-up the indicative charge is £40.00/day. Minimum age to hire is 23 years.

FUEL CHARGES: Pay on Return = 1.5x–3x average UK fuel price (reference: fleetnews.co.uk/costs/fuel-prices). EZ Fuel fixed charge for cars: £14.40–£16.70; for vans: £21.60–£25.06 (triggered if driven less than 75 miles without a receipted refuel). Fuel Up Front at average UK price (reference: confused.com/petrol-prices).

ADDITIONAL CHARGES: Late return processing fee: £15.00/day mainland UK. Damage/Loss/Repair Processing Fee: £66.00. Traffic fine administration: £30.00/fine mainland UK; £50.00 Northern Ireland. Accident report fee (if incomplete): £66.00. Specialist cleaning: up to £88.80 mainland UK; minimum £100.00 Northern Ireland. Breakdown callout (customer fault): £141.60 mainland UK. One-way rental (same country, unplanned): £10.00–£300.00.

ELECTRIC VEHICLE BATTERY CHARGES: Return at 70%+ charge = £0. Return at 11–69% = £43.00. Return at 10% or less = £53.00.

MILEAGE: Most vehicles unlimited. Excess mileage: 12p–21p per mile.

PRE-AUTHORISATION: Rental price + £300.00 mainland UK.

DRIVING RESTRICTIONS: Permitted territories are England, Scotland, Wales, Northern Ireland, and Republic of Ireland (with Continental Cover). Continental Cover for Republic of Ireland from mainland UK: £104.40 (1–7 days) to £250.56 (22–28 days).
key_principles: SCDW (zero-excess damage waiver) costs Avis customers up to £30.28/day on mainland UK — if a credit hire rate includes zero-excess protection, this figure provides the baseline for stripping out that component for BHR comparison
Complete protection packages (SCDW + STP + windscreen + roadside) cost £39.99–£52.99/day from Avis — if bundled into a credit hire rate, this total should be identifiable and separable
The Avis daily rate for the vehicle itself (the BHR proper) is distinct from the £30–£53/day of optional extras that inflate the apparent daily charge
Young driver surcharge of approximately £40/day is a legitimate add-on if the hirer was under 25 — claimants should identify this separately from the base hire rate
Fuel is entirely separate and should not feature in a BHR comparison — Pay on Return at 1.5–3x fuel price can add significant cost to an apparent hire rate
Additional driver charges (£14.99/day) should only be included in a BHR comparison if the need for an additional driver is specifically evidenced
Avis is a mainstream (non-niche) provider — its rates and T&Cs are directly usable as BHR comparators under the Stevens/McBride survey methodology
articles.source: Avis Budget UK Limited, Country Conditions — United Kingdom, version UK/EN-20/03/2026 (last updated 20 March 2026). Retrieved 25 March 2026.

articles.title: Europcar Terms and Conditions of Hire — BHR-Relevant Provisions (January 2026)
articles.summary: The Europcar Terms and Conditions of Hire (version 453B-40-01-26, updated 5 January 2026) govern all UK Europcar rentals. The critical BHR distinction from Avis is that Europcar includes Basic Protection (CDW + Theft Waiver) in the standard daily rate for most rentals. The no-refund-on-early-return provision (s.11.4) is explicit and directly supports need arguments. Maximum hire period is 84 days.
articles.content:
The Europcar Terms and Conditions of Hire (version 453B-40-01-26, updated 5 January 2026) apply to all Europcar rentals in the United Kingdom. Europcar Group UK Limited is registered in England and Wales (company number 01089053), with its registered office at 1 Great Central Square, Leicester LE1 4JS. The contract is governed by the law of the country of pick-up.

CRITICAL BHR DISTINCTION — WHAT IS INCLUDED IN THE BASIC HIRE RATE (s.6): Unlike Avis, Europcar's standard daily rate INCLUDES the Basic Protection Package, which comprises both Collision Damage Waiver (CDW) and Theft Waiver (TW). This means Europcar's published rates are NOT bare-metal rates — they already incorporate basic damage protection. For BHR comparison purposes, this is significant: a defendant comparing a credit hire rate to Europcar rates must acknowledge that Europcar's own rate already includes CDW/TW. The valid comparison is therefore between the credit hire rate (including whatever protection it provides) and the equivalent Europcar rate (including Basic Protection at the same protection level). If the credit hire rate includes zero-excess protection (Premium Protection equivalent), the defendant may still argue to strip out the Premium Protection uplift (£54.50–£80.00/day depending on location and vehicle size) above the basic included CDW.

Also included in the basic daily rate: third party liability insurance (mandatory); technical assistance for mechanical breakdown caused by non-driver fault; initial vehicle cleaning; and unlimited mileage for standard car rentals up to 27 consecutive days.

EARLY RETURN — NO REFUND (s.11.4): If the renter returns the vehicle before the agreed return date and time, 'the rental charges will remain the same (ie as if you had not returned the Vehicle to us before the return date and time specified in the Rental Agreement)'. This is an explicit contractual provision directly supporting a claimant's position that they needed the vehicle for the full hire period — returning early provides no financial benefit and the period of hire is therefore presumptively reasonable.

LATE RETURN (s.11.5 and s.5.12): A grace period of 29 minutes applies. Beyond that, Europcar charges the daily rate for each additional day (or part day) plus an unauthorised extension charge (£25 under the Tariff Guide). If Europcar cannot reach the renter within 24 hours of the expiry, the vehicle is reported as stolen. This confirms that the contractual hire period is precise and binding.

MAXIMUM HIRE PERIOD: 84 days. Any extension beyond 84 days requires return and a new rental agreement.

PROHIBITED USES (s.5.11): The vehicle must not be used for hire or reward; carrying passengers for hire or reward; racing, off-roading or speed testing; towing (except factory-fitted tow hook up to 1,000kg); carrying flammable, dangerous or illegal materials; or giving driving lessons. These restrictions mirror those in credit hire agreements and reinforce that the credit hire vehicle was for private use only.

STAND LIABLE OPTION (s.9.2.2): A renter may opt for a rate exclusive of Basic Protection ('Stand Liable'). In this case they must either confirm they have equivalent fleet insurance cover or complete a Stand Liable Declaration Form. Stand Liable renters assume full liability for damage without the CDW/TW cap. If a credit hire claimant's rental was Stand Liable, they had no basic protection and would be responsible for the full cost of any damage — relevant to assessing reasonableness of taking out credit hire instead.

VEHICLE AVAILABILITY AND BREAKDOWN (s.14.1.2): If a vehicle cannot be repaired during the hire period, Europcar will 'where possible provide an alternative Vehicle of an equivalent standard and size for the remainder of the Hire Period' and where it cannot do so 'will provide you with a refund for any part of the Hire Period that you have paid for but not received the benefit of.' This clause supports the proposition that the hirer had a contractual expectation of continuous vehicle availability.

GEOLOCATION TRACKING (s.22): All Europcar vehicles are fitted with electronic tracking devices that monitor the rental journey, border crossings, mileage, fuel levels, and accident data. This is relevant in cases where defendants allege excessive or unnecessary mileage during the credit hire period — Europcar's own data practices show that real-time mileage monitoring is standard industry practice.
key_principles: Europcar's standard daily rate INCLUDES Basic Protection (CDW + TW) — unlike Avis, there is no separate CDW charge to strip out for like-for-like comparison at basic protection level
Section 11.4 provides an explicit no-refund-on-early-return clause: 'rental charges will remain the same' regardless of early return — directly supports the full hire period need argument
The maximum hire period is 84 days; any extension requires a new contract — sets a natural ceiling for reasonable period arguments

Zero-excess (Premium Protection) is not included in the basic rate — if the credit hire rate provides zero-excess cover, the Europcar Premium Protection uplift (£54.50–£80.00/day) can be identified for stripping
Stand Liable option exists for corporate fleet hirers who have their own insurance — relevant context if the claimant is a business
Europcar is a mainstream BVRLA-member provider consistently featured in BHR surveys — its rates are accepted as valid BHR comparators under the Stevens/McBride methodology
articles.source: Europcar Group UK Limited, Terms and Conditions of Hire, version 453B-40-01-26 (updated 5 January 2026). Retrieved 25 March 2026.

articles.title: Europcar Tariff Guide — Charges, Protection Products and Excess Values (January 2026)
articles.summary: The Europcar Tariff Guide (version 453B1-23-01-26, updated 5 January 2026) provides all published GBP charges for optional extras, protection upgrades, additional services, and other fees. This is the primary source for stripping optional extra costs out of credit hire rates for BHR comparison. Critically: Medium Protection (CDW excess reduced to £350–£1,050) costs up to £58/day; Premium Protection (zero excess) costs up to £73.50/day at Category A branches.
articles.content:
The Europcar Tariff Guide (version 453B1-23-01-26, updated 5 January 2026) forms part of the Contract alongside the Terms and Conditions of Hire. It sets out all additional charges that are NOT included in the basic daily rental charge.

PROTECTION UPGRADE PRODUCTS (all inc. VAT):

IMPORTANT NOTE ON WHAT IS ALREADY INCLUDED: The standard basic daily rate includes Basic Protection (CDW + TW). The products below are UPGRADES above that included protection. Category A locations are typically city branches and some airports; Category B are other locations.

Medium Protection Package (CDW + TW + reduced excess to £350–£1,050 depending on vehicle): Standard cars at Category A branches — Small vehicle up to £47.00/day, Large vehicle up to £58.00/day. Standard cars at Category B branches — Small £42.50/day, Large £52.00/day. Stand Liable renters — Small £51.00/day, Large £62.50/day.

Premium Protection Package (CDW + TW + zero excess on standard cars): Available at participating branches only. Not available for Selection vehicles or vans. Category A — Small up to £60.50/day, Large up to £73.50/day. Category B — Small £54.50/day, Large £66.00/day. Stand Liable — Small £65.50/day, Large £80.00/day.

Value Cover — Glass, Lights and Tyre: Reduces excess to zero for glass, lights and tyre damage (over and above existing Protection level). Cars — up to £21.00/day. Vans — up to £22.00/day.

STAND LIABLE ONLY — EXCESS REDUCTION PRODUCTS (for renters without Basic Protection): CDW alone — all vehicles up to £26.00/day. Theft Waiver alone — all vehicles up to £26.00/day.

COLLISION DAMAGE WAIVER EXCESS VALUES (the 'skin in the game' the renter retains): Small vehicles (Mini/Economy/Compact): Basic Protection excess £2,000; Medium excess £350–£450; Premium excess £0. Large vehicles (Intermediate/Standard): Basic Protection excess £2,200; Medium excess £350–£450; Premium excess £0. Premium vehicles: Basic Protection excess up to £4,000; Medium excess £850–£1,050; Premium N/A. Vans: Basic Protection excess up to £1,900; Medium excess £350–£450; Premium N/A.

SUPER PERSONAL ACCIDENT INSURANCE (SPAI): Cars £9.52/day; Vans £11.90/day. Covers personal injury/death up to £200,000, medical expenses to £10,000, and baggage.

EMERGENCY TRAVEL SERVICE: £4.50/day.

ROADSIDE ASSISTANCE PLUS: up to £19.00/day (maximum daily rate, varies by hire length). ROADSIDE ASSISTANCE EXPRESS: up to £20.00/day.

GENERAL PRODUCTS (add-on, not included in basic daily rate):

Young Driver Surcharge (ages 22–25): up to £79.00/day — mandatory if applicable, not discretionary.

Additional Driver: £19.10/day, maximum £191.00/rental (includes Basic Protection for the additional driver).

DVLA Contact Charge: £4.75/incident (if Europcar must validate licence directly with DVLA).

Booster Cushion: £15.70/day, max £154.70/rental; replacement if lost/stolen: £84.00.

Child Seat: £15.70/day, max £154.70/rental; replacement if lost/stolen: £84.00.

Satellite Navigation: £17.84/day, max £176.40/rental; replacement: £84.00.

Late Check-In: up to £30.00/rental (extends return time by 2 hours).

Out of Hours Collection: £60.00/rental. Out of Hours Key Return Box: £20.00/rental.

Full Tank Option: Pre-purchase at average fuel price — see europcar.co.uk for current price.

Europdrive Pack (overseas travel): up to £312.00. Cross-Border Pack (Republic of Ireland): up to £187.43.

OTHER CHARGES (payable on events, not part of BHR):

Fuel charges: pump price per litre × 2.5 for missing fuel (Full-to-Full policy). Electric vehicle recharging: weighted average rapid/ultra-rapid charging rate + £0.15/kWh × kWh needed to reach 80% charge. AdBlue (diesel vans): 3.5p/mile driven.

Excess mileage (standard cars, rentals of 28+ consecutive days): 30p/mile over allowance (30, 90 or 120 miles/day depending on product). Vans (up to 6 days): 24p/mile over 250/day. Vans (7+ days): 24p/mile over 90/day.

One-way hire: Pre-authorised UK up to £357.00; Unauthorised UK up to £595.00; Unauthorised overseas up to £2,380.00.

Premium Location Surcharge: automatically added for rentals commencing at premium locations (airports, major ferry terminals, railway stations, some city locations) — up to 20% of rental cost.

Valet Charge: £45.00 (if specialist cleaning required beyond standard). Third Party Administration Charge: £50.00/notice. Unpaid Charges Admin Charge: £40.00. Unauthorised Extension Charge: £25.00. Extension Charge (pre-agreed): £10.00. Reservation Amendment: £5.00. Changeover Charge (vehicle swap, same group): £25.00. No Show Charge: £102.00. Late Cancellation: up to £54.00. Missing Geolocation Device: £235.00. Disconnected Geolocation Device: £75.00.
key_principles: Premium Protection (zero-excess) costs £54.50–£80.00/day above the basic rate that already includes CDW — this is the maximum defensible strip-out if a credit hire rate includes zero-excess protection when benchmarked against Europcar

Basic CDW excess without any upgrade is £2,000–£4,000 depending on vehicle type — if the credit hire renter had no excess protection, comparison should be to the base Europcar rate (which already includes CDW to this excess level)
Young driver surcharge can be up to £79.00/day — roughly double the Avis equivalent (£40/day). For young hirers this is a legitimate and significant charge

Additional driver at Europcar (£19.10/day) is higher than Avis (£14.99/day) and includes Basic Protection for the additional driver — relevant when defendants argue additional driver cost should be stripped out
Excess mileage (30p/mile for cars on 28+ day rentals) and Premium Location Surcharge (up to 20%) can significantly inflate the final charge — both are separable from the daily BHR
Fuel charges at pump price × 2.5 represent a punitive uplift not present in the basic hire rate and must be excluded from any BHR comparison
Satellite navigation at £17.84/day (max £176.40/rental) is significantly higher than Avis (£9.99/day) — demonstrates variability between mainstream providers on optional extras
articles.source: Europcar Group UK Limited, Tariff Guide, version 453B1-23-01-26 (updated 5 January 2026). Retrieved 25 March 2026.

articles.title: Thrifty Car & Van Rental — Rental Agreement and Terms of Hire: BHR-Relevant Provisions (©2022/©2026)
articles.summary: Thrifty Car & Van Rental (operated by Scot Group Limited, FCA reg 684351) imposes mileage limits on all vehicles and caps hire at 30 days maximum — two structural features that make it a materially different comparator from Avis and Europcar. CDW is not included in the base rate; DLR is an optional extra. Young drivers (22–24) pay a mandatory £35.00/day surcharge and cannot take DLR.
articles.content:
Thrifty Car & Van Rental is operated by Scot Group Limited, registered FCA number 684351, with its principal office at Scot House, Matford Park Road, Exeter EX2 8AW. Thrifty is a BVRLA member. The hire terms are governed by two documents: the Rental Agreement Terms and Conditions (©2022, 5 pages) and the Rental Terms and Conditions (©2026, 9 pages).

KEY STRUCTURAL DIFFERENCES FROM AVIS AND EUROPCAR:

1. MILEAGE LIMITS: All Thrifty rentals include mileage limits. There is no unlimited mileage option. Limits are: 100 miles/day (hires of 1–14 days); 70 miles/day (hires of 15–27 days); 2,000 miles per 28-day period (28+ days). Excess mileage is charged at 15p+VAT per mile for standard vehicles and 50p+VAT per mile for Luxury and Elite vehicles. This is a fundamental distinction from the unlimited mileage offered as standard by Avis and Europcar.

2. MAXIMUM HIRE PERIOD: 30 days. Thrifty cannot be used as a BHR comparator for any credit hire exceeding 30 days.

3. NO OVERSEAS USE: Vehicles may not be taken outside the UK.

CDW / DAMAGE LIABILITY:
CDW is NOT included in Thrifty's base rate. The renter is subject to a 'responsibility amount' (excess) that varies by vehicle category: Economy to Standard: £1,000; Full Size to Prestige/MPVs (excl Prestige 4x4): £1,250; Prestige 4x4 and Luxury Automatics: £1,500; Luxury 4x4 and Elite: £2,000–£5,000.

Damage Liability Reduction (DLR) is available as an optional extra to reduce the excess to £250. DLR is NOT available to drivers aged under 25 or aged 70 and over.

YOUNG DRIVERS:
Minimum age is 22. Drivers aged 22–24 must pay a mandatory young driver surcharge of £35.00/day inclusive of VAT. Critically, these drivers also cannot purchase DLR, meaning they face both an additional daily surcharge AND the full damage liability exposure without any means to reduce it. Prestige, Luxury and Elite vehicles require the driver to be at least 30 years old.

EARLY RETURN:
No refund is given if the vehicle is returned before the end of the agreed rental period.

FUEL:
Three options apply: (1) purchase a full tank at a preferential rate and return empty; (2) return with a full tank at no charge; (3) return part-full — charged for the missing fuel at a locally determined price plus a surcharge.

LATE RETURN:
Charged at the daily or hourly rate.

PENALTY CHARGES:
Failure to notify Thrifty of a dashboard warning light within a reasonable period: £1,000. Missed vehicle service interval: £2,000–£10,000 depending on vehicle category.

THIRD PARTY LIABILITY:
Property damage limit: £1,000,000.

ACCIDENT / INCIDENT:
Thrifty Assist: 0800 107 9944.
key_principles: Thrifty imposes mandatory mileage limits on all vehicles — 100 miles/day (1–14 days), 70 miles/day (15–27 days). Excess mileage at 15p+VAT/mile (standard) or 50p+VAT/mile (Luxury/Elite). A credit hire with unlimited mileage is not directly comparable to Thrifty without a mileage adjustment
Maximum hire period of 30 days means Thrifty cannot be used as a BHR comparator for credit hires lasting more than 30 days

CDW is not included in the base rate — unlike Europcar. Defendants cannot use Thrifty's base rate as a like-for-like comparison where the credit hire includes CDW/damage protection without stripping the DLR equivalent
DLR (optional extra to reduce excess to £250) is unavailable to drivers aged under 25 or 70+ — a significant restriction that affects comparability for young driver credit hire cases
Young drivers (22–24) face a double jeopardy: mandatory £35/day surcharge plus full damage liability exposure (cannot take DLR)
No early return refund — same position as Avis and Europcar. Defendants cannot argue that a claimant was over-charged by credit hire for retaining the vehicle to the end of the agreed period
No overseas use permitted — Thrifty is not a valid comparator where the claimant needed to travel abroad
articles.source: Scot Group Limited (trading as Thrifty Car & Van Rental). Rental Agreement Terms and Conditions (©2022) and Rental Terms and Conditions (©2026). Retrieved 25 March 2026. www.thrifty.co.uk

articles.title: Thrifty Car & Van Rental — Optional Extras, Protection Products and Charges: Rates and BHR Strip-Out Values
articles.summary: Published GBP rates for all Thrifty optional extras as at March 2026. DLR (reducing excess to £250) costs £9.50–£20.00/day depending on vehicle category and rental duration. Glass and Tyre Cover costs £6.05–£19.80/day. Young driver surcharge is £35.00/day incl VAT for drivers aged 22–24. Excess mileage at 15p+VAT/mile (standard) or 50p+VAT/mile (Luxury/Elite). These are the rates to use when stripping optional extras from a credit hire rate for BHR comparison against Thrifty.
articles.content:
The following rates apply to Thrifty Car & Van Rental optional extras and charges as at March 2026.

DAMAGE LIABILITY REDUCTION (DLR):
DLR reduces the renter's damage liability ('responsibility amount') to £250 for Economy–Prestige categories. NOT available to drivers aged under 25 or aged 70 and over.

Economy to Standard vehicles (reducing £1,000 excess to £250):
  1–3 days: £15.00/day
  4–13 days: £11.50/day
  14+ days: £9.50/day

Full Size to Prestige cars and vans (reducing £1,250 excess to £250):
  1–3 days: £20.00/day
  4–13 days: £17.00/day
  14+ days: £14.00/day

Vehicles with £1,500+ excess: location-dependent pricing — confirm at point of rental.

GLASS AND TYRE COVER:
Not available for Elite category vehicles.

Economy to Full Size cars and vans:
  1–3 days: £8.80/day
  4–13 days: £7.15/day
  14+ days: £6.05/day

Premium and Prestige vehicles:
  1–3 days: £19.80/day
  4–13 days: £16.50/day
  14+ days: £13.20/day

YOUNG DRIVER SURCHARGE:
£35.00/day inclusive of VAT for drivers aged 22–24. Mandatory — not waivable.

EXCESS MILEAGE CHARGES:
Standard vehicles: 15p per mile + VAT (approximately 18p/mile incl VAT).
Luxury and Elite vehicles: 50p per mile + VAT (approximately 60p/mile incl VAT).

FUEL SHORTFALL:
Charged at locally determined price per litre plus a surcharge for missing fuel. No fixed tariff published in T&Cs.

LATE RETURN:
Charged at the applicable daily or hourly rental rate.

PENALTY CHARGES (non-BHR, contractual):
Warning light notification failure: £1,000.
Missed service interval: £2,000–£10,000 depending on vehicle category.

BHR STRIP-OUT APPROACH:
When comparing a credit hire daily rate against Thrifty's BHR, the following extras should be stripped out from the credit hire rate before comparison (if not included in Thrifty's base rate): DLR (£9.50–£20.00/day); Glass and Tyre Cover (£6.05–£19.80/day); additional drivers (any charge); young driver surcharge if applicable (£35.00/day). Mileage must also be factored: if the claimant drove more than 100 miles/day, the Thrifty rate would need to be uplifted by the excess mileage charge to achieve a true comparison.
key_principles: DLR (reducing excess to £250) costs £9.50–£20.00/day depending on vehicle category and rental duration — this is the primary protection extra to strip from credit hire rates when comparing to Thrifty
Glass and Tyre Cover costs £6.05–£19.80/day — a separate optional extra not bundled in Thrifty's base rate
Young driver surcharge (£35.00/day incl VAT) is mandatory for 22–24 year olds and is a legitimate comparable surcharge when assessing credit hire costs for young claimants

Excess mileage charges (15p+VAT/mile standard; 50p+VAT/mile Luxury/Elite) mean that Thrifty's base rate understates the true comparable cost if the claimant drove beyond the daily cap — defendants relying on Thrifty BHR rates must account for this
Unlike Avis and Europcar, Thrifty does not publish a zero-excess protection equivalent — no 'top tier' protection product to strip out against a credit hire zero-excess rate
Fuel shortfall charges are at a locally determined price plus surcharge — no fixed rate, so cannot be precisely calculated but clearly separable from the basic hire rate
articles.source: Scot Group Limited (trading as Thrifty Car & Van Rental). Rental Terms and Conditions (©2026). Retrieved 25 March 2026. www.thrifty.co.uk

articles.title: Sixt Rent a Car — Great Britain General Terms and Conditions: BHR-Relevant Provisions (03/2026)
articles.summary: Sixt Rent a Car Ltd (company 00440897, Slough) operates across Great Britain under T&Cs version 03/2026. CDW is included in the base rate but with a large excess (£1,600–£3,000 depending on vehicle). Zero-excess LDW is an optional upgrade not available to under-25s. Young driver surcharge applies to all drivers under 28 — broader than most other mainstream providers. Maximum hire period 84 days. Northern Ireland is operated by a separate franchisee (Downeys Rental Ltd, NI636209) under materially different T&Cs.
articles.content:
Sixt Rent a Car Ltd (company number 00440897) operates car rental across Great Britain from Keypoint, 17-23 High Street, Slough, SL1 1DY. Sixt is a BVRLA member. The GB T&Cs (version 03/2026) govern all rentals in England, Scotland and Wales. Northern Ireland is operated under separate terms by Downeys Rental Ltd (NI636209) — see Article 80.

CDW / DAMAGE WAIVER POSITION:
Clause 4.10 of the GB T&Cs explicitly states that the Rental Fees include: 'vehicle tax, local taxes, third party liability insurance, daily rental charge, collision damage waiver with excess breakdown assistance and, limited/unlimited mileage (depending on the application rate).' This means CDW IS included in Sixt's base rate — unlike Avis and Thrifty where CDW is an optional extra. However, the CDW in the base rate comes with a substantial damage excess (deductible) of £1,600 to £3,000 depending on the vehicle group. A 'minimum excess' Loss Damage Waiver product is available to reduce the excess to £0–£150, but this product is NOT available to renters under 25 years of age.

YOUNG DRIVERS:
A young driver surcharge applies to all drivers under 28 years in Great Britain (Rental Information doc). The surcharge amount is not fixed in the T&Cs and varies by location and tariff. This is a notably broader threshold than Avis/Europcar/Thrifty which use 25 as the cut-off. Minimum driver ages by vehicle group: 21 years (Groups M, E, H, C); 23 years (Groups D, I, J); 25 years (Groups S, F, G, P, U, L, WV); 30 years (UVAR, W, X, Special Cars, Sports and Luxury). All require a minimum of 1 year's full driving licence.

MAXIMUM HIRE PERIOD:
