84 days — same as Europcar. Extensions beyond 84 days require a new contract (clause 7.4).

MILEAGE:
Limited or unlimited mileage depending on the applicable tariff (clause 4.10). Unlike Thrifty, Sixt does not automatically cap mileage on all vehicles.

EARLY RETURN:
Prepaid tariff: no refund for early return (clause 4.8; prepaid tariff section). Pay Later tariff: a partial discount is available for unused rental days under the Flexi Return Guarantee. If returned early without notification, a £10 (ex VAT) processing fee applies.

SECURITY DEPOSIT:
Varies by vehicle group. Standard cars (M to P): rental amount + £300. Premium cars (G, U, L, Special M to S): rental amount + £500. Prestige (WD, WFAV, WV, XD, XT, Special F to U): rental amount + £750. Luxury/Sports (UVAR, WF, XF, Special L to X): rental amount + £2,000.

FUEL:
Vehicle must be returned full. Missing fuel charged at the global petrol prices matrix rate (updated every 2 weeks) plus a 160% surcharge on the non-prepaid rate (10% surcharge on prepaid amount rate). EV charge level: must be returned at 80% if collected at 80%+; at matching level if collected below 80%.

PROTECTION PRODUCTS (optional extras):
1. Loss or Damage Protection Waiver (LDW): reduces excess from standard CDW level to £0–£150. NOT available to under-25s. Specific rates not published in T&Cs — vary by vehicle group, location and tariff.
2. Tyre and Glass Protection: waives liability for tyre, windscreen and glass damage to £0.
3. Roadside Protection: extended breakdown cover for locked keys, flat battery, fuel, stuck.
4. Interior Protection: covers interior soiling and damage.
5. Complementary Protection Plus: Sixt waives its right to claim damages up to £500 AND above £30,000 per event — covers the gap between standard damage excess and the top end of large claims.

DAMAGE ADMIN FEES (GB):
£0–£74.99: £20; £75–£499.99: £50; £500–£2,000: £80; over £2,000: £125.

THIRD PARTY LIABILITY:
Up to £5,000,000 property damage. This is significantly higher than Avis (£1m), Thrifty (£1m) and most other providers.

FINES / TRAFFIC PENALTIES:
£40 administration fee per transaction handled by Sixt.

OVERSEAS USE:
Zone 1 (most of Western Europe) with appropriate fees. Zone 2 not permitted. GB Sixt+ (subscription) vehicles restricted to Great Britain.

GOVERNING LAW:
England and Wales. Complaints: BVRLA conciliation (complaint@bvrla.co.uk).

NORTHERN IRELAND NOTE:
The GB T&Cs explicitly exclude Northern Ireland. The NI franchisee (Downeys Rental Ltd) operates under entirely separate T&Cs — see Article 80 for NI-specific provisions.
key_principles: CDW is included in Sixt's GB base rate (clause 4.10) — unlike Avis and Thrifty. However, the included CDW comes with a substantial excess of £1,600–£3,000. This positions Sixt between Europcar (CDW with lower excess) and Avis (no CDW at all) for BHR comparison purposes
Zero-excess LDW upgrade is not available to renters under 25 — for young driver credit hire claimants, the Sixt BHR comparator rate already excludes the best available protection product
Young driver surcharge applies to under-28s in GB (not under-25 as with Avis/Europcar/Thrifty) — relevant for claimants aged 25–27
Maximum hire period 84 days — Sixt is a valid BHR comparator for longer credit hires (unlike Thrifty at 30 days)

Mileage is limited or unlimited depending on tariff — defendants must check whether the specific Sixt tariff used in a BHR survey included mileage limits before comparing to an unlimited-mileage credit hire
Early return refund position depends on tariff: prepaid = no refund; pay later = partial discount. For BHR comparison purposes, the full rental period charge applies on prepaid tariffs
Third party liability of £5m is the highest of all mainstream BHR comparators reviewed — relevant only insofar as it confirms the base rate includes comprehensive third party cover
Northern Ireland is operated by a different company (Downeys Rental Ltd) under different T&Cs — Sixt GB rates should never be used for Northern Ireland BHR comparisons
articles.source: Sixt Rent a Car Ltd. General Terms and Conditions of Rental for Great Britain, version 03/2026. Sixt Rental Information United Kingdom, 25/03/2026. Retrieved 25 March 2026. www.sixt.co.uk

articles.title: Sixt Rent a Car Northern Ireland (Downeys Rental Ltd) — T&Cs: Key Differences from GB and BHR Implications
articles.summary: Sixt Northern Ireland is operated by Downeys Rental Ltd (NI636209), a franchisee of Sixt GmbH — a completely separate legal entity from Sixt Rent a Car Ltd (GB). The NI T&Cs contain several material differences relevant to BHR analysis: LDW is mandatory; minimum licence requirement is 3 years (not 1); young driver surcharge threshold is under-25 (not under-28); deposits are different; damage admin fees are higher; and jurisdiction is Northern Ireland courts. For Northern Ireland credit hire claims, only the NI franchisee rates and T&Cs should be used as the BHR comparator.
articles.content:
Sixt Rent a Car Northern Ireland is operated by Downeys Rental Ltd, registered in Northern Ireland with company number NI636209, at 39 Portaferry Road, Newtownards, County Down, BT23 8NN. This is a franchisee of Sixt GmbH — it is NOT the same company as Sixt Rent a Car Ltd (company 00440897) which operates in Great Britain. The NI entity operates under its own General Terms and Conditions (version Sept2020, current as at March 2026).

KEY DIFFERENCES FROM GB SIXT:

1. LDW IS MANDATORY IN NORTHERN IRELAND:
Clause 4.6 of the NI T&Cs states: 'Loss Damage Waiver (LDW) is mandatory in Northern Ireland.' US or Canadian customers may be able to decline if they provide an insurance cover letter from their credit card company confirming they will cover the full replacement value. This means that for all standard NI rentals, the base rate already includes LDW — there is no excess to strip out for BHR comparison purposes when using Sixt NI as the comparator.

2. MINIMUM LICENCE REQUIREMENT:
All drivers must have held their full licence for at least 3 years (NI T&Cs clause 10.2) — compared to 1 year in GB. UK licence holders must also have no endorsements under 25 years of age.

3. MINIMUM DRIVER AGES:
21 years (UK licence only): Groups M, E. 25 years: M, E, C, SDAR, SDMR, SFAR, SFMR, SWAR. 30 years: I, F, P, L, X, SVMR, SVAR. Age range: 21–75 (UK licence); 25–75 (non-UK licence).

4. YOUNG DRIVER SURCHARGE:
A young driver surcharge applies to UK licence holders under 25 years (not under 28 as in GB).

5. SECURITY DEPOSITS (NI):
Higher and differently structured than GB: M/E groups £600; C group £900; S/I groups £1,200; P/L/F/SV groups £1,500; X group £2,000. Trucks: A/V £500, B £750, W £1,000. If LDW is not accepted (US/Canadian exception): pre-auth of £3,000–£10,000 (excess multiplied by 2).

6. FUEL SURCHARGE:
150% surcharge on non-prepaid fuel matrix rate (vs 160% in GB). Fuel matrix from fleetnews.co.uk (updated six-monthly).

7. DAMAGE ADMIN FEES (NI — HIGHER THAN GB):
£0–£74.99: £30; £75–£499.99: £60; £500–£1,999.99: £90; £2,000+: £140. These are substantially higher than the GB rates.

8. FINES ADMIN FEE:
£45 per transaction (vs £40 in GB).

9. TERRITORIAL RESTRICTIONS:
Vehicles must not be driven outside Northern Ireland without prior written permission. Republic of Ireland: permitted with written authorisation at £24/day (max £48/28 days). Leaving the island of Ireland: £500 fine. Leaving NI without paying foreign use charge: £250 fine. Commercial vehicles and 10+ seat vehicles cannot be taken cross-border.

10. GOVERNING LAW AND JURISDICTION:
Northern Ireland law governs the contract. Courts of Northern Ireland have exclusive jurisdiction. This is a material difference from GB Sixt (England and Wales).

11. EARLY RETURN:
Clause 6.2 NI T&Cs: 'once a rental has commenced, no refund is made on any unused rental days or protection waivers.' No equivalent Flexi Return Guarantee as in GB.

12. ELIGIBLE RENTER RESTRICTIONS (NI ONLY):
The NI T&Cs list occupational exclusions not found in the GB T&Cs: rally drivers, hawkers/market traders, scrap merchants, professional gamblers, entertainment professionals, students (other than private cars), and unemployed persons. Persons involved in 2+ accidents in the preceding 3 years are also excluded.
key_principles: LDW is mandatory in Northern Ireland — the Sixt NI base rate already includes zero-excess LDW. Defendants cannot strip LDW costs when using Sixt NI as a BHR comparator for Northern Ireland credit hire claims
Sixt NI (Downeys Rental Ltd) is a completely different legal entity from Sixt GB (Sixt Rent a Car Ltd) — GB Sixt T&Cs and rates must never be used for Northern Ireland credit hire cases
The NI minimum licence requirement is 3 years (vs 1 year in GB) — relevant for claimants who have held their licence for 1–2 years
NI young driver surcharge threshold is under-25 (matching most other providers) — not under-28 as in GB
NI damage admin fees (£30–£140) are significantly higher than GB (£20–£125) and higher than all other providers in this database
Early return: no refund on any unused rental days in NI — explicitly stated in clause 6.2 NI T&Cs

Cross-border: NI vehicles restricted to Northern Ireland + Republic of Ireland (with charge). Travelling to Great Britain or beyond Ireland without authorisation is a breach — relevant where claimant needed a vehicle for travel to mainland GB

NI occupational and accident history exclusions are broader than GB — certain claimants may not have been eligible to rent from Sixt NI at all, which may undermine it as a valid comparator in their specific case
articles.source: Downeys Rental Ltd t/a Sixt Rent a Car Northern Ireland. General Terms and Conditions of Rental (version Sept2020, current as at March 2026). Retrieved 25 March 2026. www.sixt.co.uk / customerservices@sixtni.co.uk

articles.summary: Budget Rent a Car UK General Conditions of Rental and Country Conditions (UK): BHR-relevant provisions covering contract structure, CDW position, rental requirements, mileage policy, fuel policy, early return, sub-operators and NI/Jersey-specific rules.
articles.content: {"contract_structure": "Budget operates a unique dual-contract structure. The Booking Agreement is with AB Group Financial Services Limited (company 02059740) acting as central booking agent — this entity is NOT party to the Rental Agreement. The Rental Agreement is entered into at the time of vehicle pick-up with the local rental provider (member, agent or representative of the Budget Rent A Car System). The Rental Agreement is governed by the law of the country of pick-up (UK law for UK rentals). This structure is important for BHR purposes: the party with liability under the Rental Agreement is the local operator, not the booking agent.", "rental_providers": {"primary": "Avis Budget UK Limited (00802486) — Avis Budget House, Park Road, Bracknell, RG12 2EW. Provides rental at most UK locations.", "sub_operators": ["Regal Vehicle Rentals Limited (03465480), Kingfisher Court, Parkgate Road Mollington, Chester CH1 6RR — operates Barrow in Furness, Blackpool Airport, Bolton, Carlisle, Chester, Warrington", "Hewitt Hire Limited (07428136), 13 Hyde Road, Paignton, Devon TQ4 5BW — operates Cardiff Downtown, Exeter, Newton Abbot, Torquay, Totnes", "Polar Cars Limited — Jersey Airport, St. Peter, Jersey JE1 1BY — operates Jersey Airport", "Flynn Bros. Rent-a-Car Limited (NI023192) — Belfast International Airport and Belfast City Airport — operates Northern Ireland"], "bhr_note": "The identity of the operator must be confirmed from the Rental Agreement Form. Sub-operators are legally separate entities and may have distinct terms. For BHR comparisons, the correct entity for the rental location must be used."}, "cdw_position": "Damage Waiver (CDW) and Theft Protection are 'usually' included as standard in the rental price — this will be stated on the Rental Agreement Form. If not included as standard, CDW costs GBP 7.76/day (Mainland UK) or GBP 10.12/day (Northern Ireland). This places Budget in the same category as Europcar and Sixt (standard CDW inclusion) rather than Avis and Thrifty (CDW as optional extra). For BHR strip-out: where CDW is confirmed as included, the comparator rate includes damage cover and defendants cannot separately deduct CDW costs.", "early_return_policy": "Full rental period is charged regardless of early return — no refund for unused days. Continuous credit hires of over 28 days with Budget must have been on successive contracts.", "mileage": "Most vehicles provided with unlimited mileage. For BHR analysis, confirm from RAF whether a mileage limit applied and whether excess charges are embedded in the claimed rate.", "fuel_policy": "Standard full-to-full. Options: Fuel Up Front (average UK price), EZ Fuel (fixed fee GBP 14.40–16.70 cars / GBP 21.60–25.06 vans, if driven <75 miles without refuel receipt), Pay on Return (1.5–3x average UK fuel price). Electric vehicles: must return at 70%+ battery (no charge); 11–69% = GBP 43 charge; 10% or less = GBP 53 charge.", "young_driver_surcharge": "Drivers under 25 incur a young driver surcharge. NI: people carrier and premium/executive cars minimum age 30.", "minimum_rental_age": "23 years for most vehicles.", "minimum_licence_held": "1 year for cars (Mainland UK); 2 years for vans; 2 years in Northern Ireland.", "pre_authorisation": "Mainland UK: vehicle rental price + GBP 300 (minus any Pay Now amount). Northern Ireland: Excess Amount GBP 1,700–2,500 (depending on vehicle) + vehicle rental price + fuel deposit.", "overseas_use": "Permitted within England, Scotland, Wales, Northern Ireland. Republic of Ireland from Mainland UK: permitted with Continental Cover package (GBP 104.40 for 1–7 days, GBP 146.16 for 8–14 days, GBP 180.95 for 15–21 days, GBP 250.56 for 22–28 days). Republic of Ireland from NI: permitted with GBP 30.00 cross-border fee agreed in advance.", "late_return": "Late return processing fee: Mainland UK GBP 15.00 per day. NI: calculated by local partners based on recovery costs. In addition to late return fee, current pay-at-location rates apply for any extended period.", "damage_admin_fees": "Damage/Loss/Repair Processing Fee: GBP 66.00. Accident processing fee: GBP 66.00. Fines admin fee: GBP 30.00 (Mainland UK) / GBP 50.00 (NI). Dartford Crossing auto-payment admin: GBP 2.50.", "customer_fault_breakdown": "Mainland UK callout charge: GBP 141.60. NI: assistance provider cost plus extra day's rental at pay-at-location prices per day until vehicle returned.", "payment_at_location": "Visa debit and Mastercard debit accepted at location (unlike at booking stage where only credit cards accepted). Maestro, Visa Electron, Cirrus, JCB and prepaid cards not accepted at any stage.", "dispute_resolution": "BVRLA and ECRCS member.", "cancellation_fee_uk": "GBP 72.00 — applies to Pay Now bookings cancelled <48 hours before rental start, and to Pay at Location other cancellations (no-show, failure to meet requirements, day-of cancellation)."}

articles.title: Hertz Rent a Car — Rental Terms, CDW/TP Structure, Non-Waivable Excess and Protection Products: BHR-Relevant Provisions (03/2026)
articles.version_ref: Rental Terms November 2025 + Qualification pages March 2026
articles.summary: Hertz CDW and TP are 'often included' in rates — the same conditional position as Europcar, Sixt and Budget. A non-waivable excess applies per incident regardless of CDW/TP status; only SuperCover eliminates it. Damage Admin Fee £45 per event. SuperCover also waives the admin fee and reduces the pre-authorisation hold by €500. Glass & Tyre Cover is available where CDW/TP is in place.
articles.content:
Hertz UK Rental Terms (November 2025) and Qualification pages (March 2026) — BHR-relevant provisions:

1. THIRD PARTY LIABILITY: Unlimited coverage for bodily injury to third parties and property damage (other than the Hertz vehicle) is included as standard in all rates. Voided if geographic driving restrictions are breached.

2. CDW — CONDITIONAL INCLUSION: 'Collision Damage Waiver is often included in your rate.' This is conditional language — CDW is not guaranteed to be included and must be confirmed on the Rental Agreement. CDW may be mandatory in some contract or preferential rates. If CDW is declined, the renter is liable up to the full current replacement value of the vehicle.

3. THEFT PROTECTION — CONDITIONAL INCLUSION: TP is also 'often included' in the rate. Same conditional position as CDW. If TP is declined, the renter is liable up to the full replacement value.

4. NON-WAIVABLE CDW/TP EXCESS: Even where CDW and TP are accepted or included, all rentals carry a non-waivable excess per incident for which the renter remains responsible. This excess cannot be eliminated by purchasing CDW/TP alone — only SuperCover eliminates it. Typical ranges: economy/small (Groups A/B/S) £1,700; compact (Groups C/E/L/R) £1,900; mid/large (Groups D/F/K/V) £2,100; premium (Groups G/H/J/T/W/Q1/T1) £2,800; sports/prestige up to £5,250 (Audi R8/Bentley). Full group schedule published on hertz.co.uk.

5. DAMAGE ADMINISTRATION FEE: £45.00 (inc tax) per damage event, in addition to any excess. Waived if SuperCover is purchased.

6. SUPERCOVE (SC — EXCESS WAIVER): Optional product that eliminates the CDW and TP non-waivable excess (including the portion that survives standard CDW/TP purchase). Also waives the £45 Damage Administration Charge. If SuperCover is not purchased, an additional €500 is added to the pre-authorisation hold.

7. GLASS & TYRE COVER (G&T): Optional. Covers windscreen, side windows, rear window, sunroof and tyres. Exception: curbed tyres. Only available when CDW and TP are included or purchased. Cannot be purchased if SuperCover is active (glass/tyres already included). Rates: £16.07/day, £112.48/week, £449.90/month (all inc tax).

8. PERSONAL INSURANCE (PI): Optional. Covers occupants for death, injury and possessions. Voided if geographic restrictions are breached.

9. PREMIUM EMERGENCY ROADSIDE SERVICE (PERS): Optional. Basic breakdown service (mechanical faults, accidents) is included in all rentals. PERS covers fault call-outs (flat battery, lost keys, out-of-fuel, keys locked in vehicle). Excludes cost to repair the vehicle and travel costs. Rates: £10.50/day, £73.48/week, £293.88/month (inc tax). Purchase only at time of pick-up.

10. PRE-AUTHORISATION HOLD: All amounts owing for rental + fuel tank value + minimum €200 deposit. Additional €500 held if SuperCover not purchased.

11. GROSS NEGLIGENCE EXCLUSIONS (CDW/TP): Liability not reduced where renter is grossly negligent. Specific exclusions include: striking overhead objects; driving through flooded roads causing engine damage; wrong fuel; ignoring warning lights; burning clutch; driving on beach; keys locked in vehicle; leaving windows open.

12. DISPUTE RESOLUTION: Hertz is a member of ECRCS (European Car Rental Conciliation Service). Independent adjudicator's decision is binding on Hertz. Hertz is also a BVRLA member.
articles.source: bhr/terms/Hertz/202511-hertz-rental-terms.pdf; bhr/terms/Hertz/202603-hertz-cdw.pdf; bhr/terms/Hertz/202603-hertz-theft-protection.pdf; bhr/terms/Hertz/202603-hertz-windscreen-cover.pdf; bhr/terms/Hertz/202603-hertz-premium-roadside-service.pdf; bhr/terms/Hertz/202603-hertz-third-party-liability.pdf; bhr/terms/Hertz/202603-hertz-key-facts.pdf

articles.title: Hertz Rent a Car — Age Restrictions, Additional Driver Fees, Fuel, Mileage, Late Return, Geographic Restrictions and Key Rates (03/2026)
articles.version_ref: Rental Terms November 2025 + Qualification pages March 2026
articles.summary: Hertz minimum rental age is 25 (21 for selected economy groups with surcharge). Young driver surcharge: £61.43/day, max £1,719.98/rental. Additional driver: £20.60/day, max £206.04/rental. 29-minute late return grace period. Vehicles must be returned every 28 days for service check. Post-Brexit, UK rentals cannot enter EU countries. Republic of Ireland requires VE103B certificate.
articles.content:
Hertz UK — additional operational terms and rates (BHR-relevant):

1. AGE RESTRICTIONS: Standard minimum age is 25 years. Selected economy vehicle groups (A, A4, B, B1, C, C4, D4, E, E4, S) can be rented from age 21 on payment of a young driver surcharge of £61.43/day (inc tax), £430.00/week (inc tax), or a maximum of £1,719.98/rental (inc tax). The majority of vehicle groups have a strict minimum age of 25 with no under-25 option. Higher age bands apply for premium vehicles: strictly 27 for groups H5/I5/K5 (Audi Q7, Merc V Class, Land Rover Range Rover Velar); strictly 28 for prestige sports (Porsche Boxster/Cayenne S, Range Rover Sport Supercharged); strictly 30 for high-end exotics (Jaguar F-TYPE, Mercedes C63 AMG, Audi R8, Bentley, Porsche 992/Panamera 4S, Range Rover Autobiography LWB); strictly 35 for 17-seat minibus (Ford Transit M4 — also requires D1 licence held 3+ years).

2. ADDITIONAL DRIVER: £20.60/day, £144.23/week, maximum £206.04/rental (all inc tax). Can be added at any UK Hertz branch during rental. Requires: valid driver's licence + passport; main driver must be present.

3. DRIVING LICENCE: Must be held for at least 1 year. Digital licences not accepted in UK. For Northern Ireland rentals: photocard AND paper counterpart both required. UK licence holders must provide a DVLA/nidirect 'Check Code' (valid 21 days) to share driving record with Hertz.

4. FUEL POLICY: Full-to-full standard. Fuel Purchase Option (FPO) available at start of rental. Fuel is NOT included in standard rates.

5. MILEAGE: Not unlimited — maximum mileage stated on Rental Agreement. Varies by rate and location.

6. LATE RETURN: Charged by full 24-hour periods from the rental start time. A 29-minute grace period applies before a new 24-hour period is triggered. An additional Late Return Charge may also apply.

7. LONGER RENTALS: For rentals exceeding 28 days, the vehicle must be returned every 28th day for a service check and Rental Agreement renewal. 30 days' notice required to terminate a longer rental.

8. PRE-AUTHORISATION: Rental amounts owing + fuel tank value + minimum €200 deposit. Additional €500 if SuperCover not purchased.

9. GEOGRAPHIC RESTRICTIONS (POST-BREXIT): UK-rented Hertz vehicles may not be taken into any EU country (effective 1 January 2021). Driving between England/Scotland/Wales, Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland is permitted with Hertz's express permission. If a UK vehicle is taken into the Republic of Ireland, a VE103B certificate is legally required (proof of Hertz permission). VE103B charges (inc tax): 1-7 days £171.99; 14 days £209.90; 15-21 days £226.51; 22-28 days £301.73. VE103B not required for travel to Northern Ireland alone. Cannot drop off vehicle on Isle of Wight, Scottish Islands, Jersey or Guernsey. If restrictions are ignored, ALL insurance (TPL, CDW, TP, PI, SuperCover) is voided. Renter fully liable including vehicle repatriation costs.

10. DELIVERY AND COLLECTION: Available but cannot be booked online. Telephone the Hertz Reservation Centre.

11. EV RECHARGING POLICY: Vehicle provided with charge level recorded at start. Return required at same level or 80%, whichever is lower. Shortfall charged per kWh (rate on Rental Agreement). If returned at 10% battery or below: £22.68 administration fee. Network charging via Shell Recharge (and Tesla Superchargers for Tesla vehicles) using Hertz key fob/card provided. Third-party charging costs are renter's responsibility.

12. NO SMOKING: Prohibited in all vehicles. Standard cleaning charge up to £100 (inc tax). For premium/prestige vehicle groups (Porsche, Aston Martin, Bentley, Range Rover, Lamborghini, Audi Q7 etc.): up to £200 (inc tax) plus loss of use charges.

13. BHR RATE BENCHMARKING SUMMARY: Young driver surcharge £61.43/day (max £1,719.98/rental). Additional driver £20.60/day (max £206.04/rental). Glass & Tyre Cover £16.07/day. PERS £10.50/day. CDW non-waivable excess: typically £1,700-£2,800 depending on vehicle group. Damage Admin Fee £45 per incident.
articles.source: bhr/terms/Hertz/202511-hertz-rental-terms.pdf; bhr/terms/Hertz/202603-hertz-age-restrictions.pdf; bhr/terms/Hertz/202603-hertz-additional-driver.pdf; bhr/terms/Hertz/202603-hertz-driving-licence.pdf; bhr/terms/Hertz/202603-hertz-driving-restrictions.pdf; bhr/terms/Hertz/202603-hertz-recharging-policy.pdf; bhr/terms/Hertz/202603-hertz-no-smoking.pdf; bhr/terms/Hertz/202603-hertz-delivery-and-collection.pdf; bhr/terms/Hertz/202603-hertz-key-facts.pdf

articles.title: Enterprise Rent-A-Car — Rental Terms, Damage Waiver Inclusion, Protection Products and Credit Hire Provision: BHR-Relevant Provisions (01/2025)
articles.version_ref: Rental Terms and Conditions January 2025 + Extra Costs Guide March 2026
articles.summary: Enterprise is a critical BHR comparator because its Damage Waiver (DW) is included as standard in all UK rates — unlike Hertz, Europcar, Sixt and Budget where CDW/TP inclusion is conditional. DW excess is £1,750–£2,250 per incident. Unlimited mileage in UK. Maximum rental period 88 days. Section 8(d)(ii) explicitly provides for credit hire with deferred payment up to 11 months. Pick-up/drop-off service free within 10 miles of non-airport branches.
articles.content:
Enterprise Rent-A-Car UK (Rental Terms January 2025 + Costs Guide March 2026) — BHR-relevant provisions:

1. OPERATING ENTITY: Enterprise Rent-A-Car UK Limited, Enterprise House, Vicarage Road, Egham, Surrey, TW20 9FB. Part of Enterprise Mobility / Enterprise Holdings, Inc. Ireland rentals: ERAC Ireland Limited. Governing law for UK rentals: laws of England. BVRLA member — disputes via BVRLA Conciliation Service.

2. DAMAGE WAIVER (DW) — INCLUDED IN ALL RATES: This is Enterprise's most significant BHR distinguisher. The Extra Costs guide states: 'You don't need to buy insurance on your rental as all our cars come with Damage Waiver included.' The Rental Terms (s.6(a)) confirm DW reduces renter's liability for damage and loss/theft to the excess per incident. DW is not conditional — it is standard across all UK rates. This contrasts directly with Hertz, Europcar, Sixt and Budget where CDW/TP is 'often included' or subject to rate type.

3. DW EXCESS: £1,750–£2,250 depending on vehicle type (as of 09/07/2025). Default excess if not stated on the Rental Agreement Summary: £2,000. Excess applies per incident.

4. DW EXCLUSIONS: DW does not apply to: (a) theft/loss not supported by police crime reference; (b) damage caused by incorrect fuel or incorrect EV charging; (c) breach of ss.3 (prohibited use) or 4 (obligations); (d) non-payment of Rental Costs.

5. EXCESS PROTECTION (EP): Optional. Reduces DW excess to £100–£250 (per Extra Costs guide). Under the Rental Terms, if DW + EP are both selected and no excess stated on agreement, default reduced excess is £700. If EP selected without DW, renter remains responsible for losses above £900.

6. ROADSIDE ASSISTANCE PROTECTION (RAP): Optional. Covers: tyre repair/replacement (including rims, except as part of a larger repair); replacement keys; glass repair/replacement (except part of larger repair); recovery and call-out charges due to driver fault. Basic 24-hour breakdown assistance for mechanical faults is included free in all rentals. RAP excludes incorrect fuel/charging, running out of fuel more than once, and breach of agreement.

7. THIRD PARTY LIABILITY: Conditional — included if '3RD PARTY COVER INCLUDED' appears on the individual Rental Agreement Summary. If so, covered under Enterprise's motor fleet insurance policy (Road Traffic Act 1988 compliant). If not stated, renter must provide their own valid TPL insurance; Enterprise's fleet policy is primary and renter's insurer must be disclosed. Renter must cooperate with Enterprise's insurers.

8. CREDIT HIRE PROVISION (s.8(d)(ii)): Where the rental arises from the renter's own vehicle being involved in an accident, and the replacement vehicle is provided on a credit basis, payment is deferred for a period not exceeding 11 months from the date of the Rental Agreement. The credit amount is a fixed sum (daily charge × number of rental days). No interest or other charges during the deferment period. This confirms Enterprise operates formally in the credit hire market.

9. PICKUP/DROP-OFF SERVICE: Free within a 10-mile radius of non-airport branches. Must call in advance to confirm availability.

10. BVRLA MEMBERSHIP: Enterprise is a BVRLA member. UK disputes handled via the BVRLA Conciliation Service.
articles.source: bhr/terms/Enterprise/202501-enterprise-rental-agreement.pdf; bhr/terms/Enterprise/202603-enterprise-extra-costs.pdf

articles.title: Enterprise Rent-A-Car — Mileage, Fuel, Late Return, Maximum Rental Period, Age Restrictions, Fees and Prohibited Use: BHR-Relevant Rates and Provisions (01/2025)
articles.version_ref: Rental Terms and Conditions January 2025 + Extra Costs Guide March 2026
articles.summary: Enterprise UK cars come with unlimited mileage — a key differentiator from operators with mileage caps. Three fuel options. 29-minute late return grace period. Maximum rental period 88 calendar days in UK. Under-25 drivers may face class restrictions and additional fees. Fines admin fee up to £45. Loss of revenue claimable for up to 30 lost rental days.
articles.content:
Enterprise Rent-A-Car UK — additional operational terms and rates (BHR-relevant):

1. MILEAGE: UNLIMITED in the UK. No additional charge for distance covered. This is a significant differentiator from operators including Hertz where mileage is capped per the Rental Agreement. For BHR comparison purposes, any rate sourced from Enterprise should be understood to include unlimited mileage.

2. FUEL POLICY — THREE OPTIONS:
   (a) Like-for-like (standard): Return with same fuel level as at collection. Minimum quarter-tank at pick-up.
   (b) Pay for what you use: Return without refuelling. Charged per litre at a higher rate plus admin fee up to £15 for refuelling downtime.
   (c) Pre-pay: Pre-pay for a full tank at local pump rate (mainly available at airport branches). Return empty. No refund for unused fuel.

3. LATE RETURN: 29-minute grace period applies before a new 24-hour period is charged. A rental day is a complete or partial period of 24 consecutive hours from the rental start time.

4. MAXIMUM RENTAL PERIOD (UK): 88 calendar days. The overall Rental Period in the UK may never exceed this. Extended periods may attract higher charges and/or an additional security deposit.

5. EARLY RETURN: No penalty fee. Renter charged only for days used. However, the applicable day rate will be recalculated and may increase if early return means the renter no longer qualifies for 'special offer' discounts or preferential rates that applied to the original booking period.

6. ADDITIONAL DRIVER: Can be added at branch when collecting the vehicle. Fee covers adding driver to vehicle's insurance. Specific rates not published in standard T&Cs — will appear on the Rental Agreement Summary. Under-25 additional drivers may face vehicle class restrictions.

7. AGE RESTRICTIONS: Authorised drivers under 25 may face restrictions on available vehicle classes and an additional fee. Specific young driver surcharge rates are not published in the standard T&Cs — stated on the Rental Agreement Summary.

8. ADMIN FEES:
   - Fines/offences processing: up to £45 (UK) per incident
   - Damage admin fee (standard): £78 per incident
   - Damage admin fee (vehicle uneconomical to repair): £100 per incident

9. LOSS OF REVENUE: Enterprise can claim loss of revenue at the daily rate on the Rental Agreement Summary for the number of lost rental days caused by damage, capped at 30 days.

10. DAMAGE EVALUATOR: Enterprise uses a proprietary damage evaluator tool with size-threshold circles for different damage types (body, glass, alloy wheels, tyres, trim, mat). Damage smaller than the corresponding circle is not charged. Renter should walk round vehicle and log any pre-existing damage at collection.

11. PROHIBITED USE — KEY EXCLUSIONS: Vehicle must not be used: off-road (unpaved surfaces, beaches, racetracks); to drive through water above ground clearance; to take vehicle outside UK without written permission; whilst driver is under the influence of alcohol/drugs/narcotics; for racing or pacemaking; to transport dangerous substances beyond household minimum amounts; on airfield/aerodrome restricted areas.

12. NO SMOKING: Prohibited, including e-cigarettes and vaping devices. Cleaning fee applies for breach.

13. BHR RATE BENCHMARKING SUMMARY: DW excess (standard) £1,750–£2,250. EP reduces to £100–£250. Fines admin £45. Damage admin £78/£100. Unlimited mileage. 29-minute grace period. Maximum 88-day rental. No published young driver surcharge — check individual Rental Agreement Summary.
articles.source: bhr/terms/Enterprise/202501-enterprise-rental-agreement.pdf; bhr/terms/Enterprise/202603-enterprise-extra-costs.pdf

articles.title: ACRISS Codes and GTA Groups — Why GTA Is Irrelevant in Litigation and ACRISS Is the Proper Standard for Vehicle Comparability
articles.source: ABI/GTA Vehicle Grouping (Cars, 1 July 2025–30 June 2026); ACRISS (Association of Car Rental Industry Systems Standards)
articles.summary: The superior courts have repeatedly confirmed that the GTA has no relevance in litigation — it is nothing more than a commercial arrangement between the credit hire industry and insurers for managing claims efficiently. Despite this, every piece of BHR evidence relies on GTA groupings to match vehicles, translating the hire vehicle into a GTA group and then purporting to find an equivalent from mainstream rental companies. This is dishonest: GTA groups are ludicrously out of date, subjectively defined, with too few models in each group, and they do not reflect how the rental industry actually classifies vehicles. ACRISS is the standard classification system used by virtually every international rental company. The ACRISS Vehicle Lookup tool on credit-hire.ai queries a database of over 14,000 vehicle models, each classified by experts into groups based on value, size, engine capacity and utility. When assessing BHR comparability, the correct approach is to identify the ACRISS group of the hire vehicle and find the rate for that group — not to translate through GTA.
key_principles: The GTA has no relevance in litigation — the superior courts have confirmed this repeatedly. It is a commercial arrangement between the credit hire industry and insurers for managing claims efficiently, not a standard for measuring damages.

Despite the courts' position, every piece of BHR evidence relies on GTA groupings to match vehicles. This is dishonest: GTA groups are out of date, subjectively defined, with too few models, and do not reflect how the rental industry actually classifies vehicles.

ACRISS is the standard classification system used by virtually every international rental company. When a rental company adds a vehicle to its fleet, ACRISS determines its group. The ACRISS database contains over 14,000 vehicle models classified by experts based on value, size, engine capacity and utility.

The ACRISS Vehicle Lookup tool (Tools -> ACRISS Vehicle Lookup) is the primary method for determining vehicle equivalence. It queries the acriss_vehicles database by manufacturer, model, year and trim and returns the exact ACRISS code and every other vehicle in the same group.
The correct test for BHR comparability is: what ACRISS group does the hire vehicle sit in? Find the rate for that group from mainstream rental companies. Do not translate through GTA.

BHR reports routinely propose comparator vehicles that are in different (lower) ACRISS groups from the hire vehicle. Example: a Kia Sportage (Standard Crossover) compared with a Skoda Kamiq (Mid-Size), Nissan Juke (Compact SUV) or Vauxhall Crossland (Compact SUV). None are equivalent under ACRISS.
The ACRISS Vehicle Lookup tool shows every vehicle in the same group as the hire vehicle — providing an objective, industry-standard list of genuine equivalents that can be put before the court.

BHR evidence that relies on a witness of fact exercising their own judgement to match vehicles without reference to the rental industry's own classification system lacks credibility. The witness is asserting opinion without relevant expertise.

The question of whether the claimant hired a better or worse car than their own damaged vehicle is a separate issue from BHR comparability. The BHR test is about the rate for the vehicle actually hired, not whether the claimant should have hired that vehicle.

Practical workflow: (1) Look up hire vehicle in ACRISS Vehicle Lookup to get code and group. (2) Note all vehicles in the same ACRISS group — these are genuine equivalents. (3) Check each BHR comparator against ACRISS. (4) If comparators are in a different (lower) group, the BHR evidence is comparing unlike with unlike. (5) The correct BHR rate is the rate for the hire vehicle's ACRISS group.
cases_referenced: Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management Ltd [2015] EWCA Civ 93
McBride v UK Insurance Ltd [2017] EWCA Civ 144
Pattni v First Leicester Buses Ltd [2011] EWCA Civ 1384
Dimond v Lovell [2002] 1 AC 384
Bunting v First Central Insurance Management Ltd (2024)
Diriye v Bojaj [2020] EWCA Civ 1400
articles.content:
The superior courts have said often enough that the GTA has no relevance in litigation. It is nothing more than a commercial arrangement between the credit hire industry and insurers about managing claims more efficiently. And yet every piece of BHR evidence relies on GTA groupings in what is a deceitful and duplicitous way of attacking the claimant's right to be compensated based on the market rate for hiring a car comparable to the one hired.

The fundamental problem with GTA-based BHR evidence is this: the BHR report identifies the hire vehicle, asserts that a GTA grouping allows them to match and find an equivalent BHR, and then does so on the flimsiest of evidence and with a complete lack of honesty. GTA groups are ludicrously out of date, subjectively defined, with too few models in each group. They do not reflect how the rental industry actually classifies and prices its fleet.

ACRISS (Association of Car Rental Industry Systems Standards) is the standard classification system used by virtually every international rental company. When a rental company adds a vehicle to its fleet, ACRISS tells them which group it falls into. The ACRISS database contains over 14,000 vehicle models, each classified by experts into groups based on value, size, engine capacity and utility. This is the system the rental industry itself uses — not GTA.

The ACRISS Vehicle Lookup tool (Tools menu -> ACRISS Vehicle Lookup) on credit-hire.ai queries this database by manufacturer, model, year and trim and returns the exact 4-character ACRISS code, the ACRISS category description, body type, fuel type, seat count and door count. It also shows every other vehicle in the same ACRISS group — so you can see at a glance what the rental industry actually considers equivalent.

A real example illustrates the problem. A claimant hires a Kia Sportage 1.6, a model current since 2022. ACRISS identifies that vehicle as a Standard Crossover with a list price of 31,945 GBP. The BHR report suggests a Skoda Kamiq, Nissan Juke and Vauxhall Crossland as comparators. However: the only Skoda in the same ACRISS group as the Sportage is the Enyaq, not the Kamiq. The equivalent Nissan is the Qashqai, not the Juke. The equivalent Vauxhall is the Grandland, not the Crossland. Avis list the Skoda Kamiq as a Mid-Size group vehicle. Enterprise show the Nissan Juke as a Compact SUV. Europcar do the same with the Vauxhall Crossland. None of those vehicles are in the same rental group as the Kia Sportage.

This is not nitpicking. The BHR evidence fails to address the equivalence of the hire vehicle and the comparison vehicle on any credible, objective basis. Instead, it relies on a witness of fact exercising their own judgement or opinion without the relevant expertise or challenge. That goes directly to the credibility of their evidence, particularly when its role is to assist the court in finding the appropriate measure of damages.

To put it in terms claim handlers understand: if a claimant hired a P4 vehicle under the GTA and the insurer came back offering to settle at the S3 rate, you would reject it. That is exactly what is happening when BHR reports use GTA to downgrade the comparator — except that without the right tools, the data is not being scrutinised.

There is no place for GTA equivalence or reliance on GTA groups where a BHR witness encloses some images and suggests that different vehicles are the appropriate basis for assessing damages. If the claimant hired a vehicle from the Standard Crossover group, then — subject to the court accepting it was reasonable to hire that vehicle — the test for the BHR evidence is to find the rate for the ACRISS group in which the hire vehicle sits. The question of whether the claimant hired a better or worse car than his own is a separate issue.

Practical workflow: (1) Look up the hire vehicle in the ACRISS Vehicle Lookup tool to get its ACRISS code and group. (2) Note every other vehicle in the same ACRISS group — these are the genuine equivalents. (3) Check the BHR report's proposed comparators against the ACRISS database. (4) If the comparators are in a different (lower) ACRISS group, the BHR evidence is comparing unlike with unlike and should be challenged. (5) The correct BHR rate is the rate for a vehicle in the same ACRISS group as the hire vehicle, from a mainstream rental company, in the relevant geographic area.

articles.title: Claiming Credit Hire Charges as a Bailee — Iqbal v Dockray: Claim Dismissed (DAC Beachcroft, 03/2023)
articles.author: Helen Mason & David Fardy
articles.source: DAC Beachcroft — What We Think (online article, 6 March 2023)
articles.summary: A DAC Beachcroft case commentary on Iqbal v Dockray (County Court at Bradford, 15 July 2022), in which a taxi driver who hired his taxi from a private hire company sought to claim credit hire charges following a road traffic accident. The claim was dismissed on the basis that it was properly construed as the private hire company's (bailor's) claim rather than the driver's (bailee's), and no evidence of the bailor's loss was adduced. The article draws together the key legal principles governing credit hire claims brought by bailees and identifies the requirements a claimant must meet.
key_principles: A bailee is entitled to bring a claim for loss of use as against the tortfeasor, even though the loss of use is technically the bailor's: O'Sullivan v Williams [1992] RTR 309; Armstead v Royal and Sun Alliance Insurance Co Ltd [2022] EWCA Civ 497.
A claim for hire charges is a claim for expenditure incurred in mitigation of loss of use: Lagden v O'Connor [2003] UKHL 64.

The claimant must prove that such expenditure is reasonably incurred, measured with reference to the bailor's (owner's) standing and resources — not simply the bailee's: Zurich Insurance Plc v Umerji [2014] EWCA Civ 357.

Where a vehicle is used as a profit-earning chattel, the measure of loss is loss of profit rather than hire charges, unless one of the three Hussain v EUI Ltd [2019] EWHC 2647 (QB) exceptions is established: (i) business use established; (ii) personal use corroborated; (iii) impecuniosity proven.

In Iqbal v Dockray the court dismissed the claim because: (a) the claim was in substance the private hire company's loss, not the driver/bailee's; (b) some invoices were in the private hire company's name; (c) no evidence was provided by the bailor of their own losses; (d) the claimant had not addressed the Hussain exceptions; (e) the claimant was debarred from relying on impecuniosity.
The defendant recovered costs from expiry of a Stage 2 Part 36 offer, restricting the claimant to £960 of costs against a claim of £8,337.12; the defendant recovered their own costs of £7,368.40.

Claimants who are not the owners of the damaged vehicle face material difficulties in establishing entitlement to credit hire charges as bailee. Bailors (owners) should take an active role in the repair and replacement of the vehicle.
Note: Armstead v Royal and Sun Alliance Insurance Co Ltd [2022] EWCA Civ 497 was, at time of publication, subject to further appeal to the Supreme Court.

Practical point for defendants: challenge whose loss of use is actually being claimed and require the claimant to identify whether the claim is brought in the bailee's own right or as agent for the bailor — the answer determines what evidence of loss is required.
cases_referenced: Iqbal v Dockray (DDJ Rose, County Court at Bradford, 15 July 2022)
O'Sullivan v Williams [1992] RTR 309
Armstead v Royal and Sun Alliance Insurance Co Ltd [2022] EWCA Civ 497
Lagden v O'Connor [2003] UKHL 64
Zurich Insurance Plc v Umerji [2014] EWCA Civ 357
Hussain v EUI Ltd [2019] EWHC 2647 (QB)
articles.content: Case commentary on Iqbal v Dockray. Taxi driver (not owner) brought credit hire claim. Vehicle owned by private hire company and hired to claimant for weekly sum. Court held claim was properly the private hire company's. Dismissed for failure to prove bailor's loss, address Hussain exceptions and having been debarred on impecuniosity. Key principles: bailee can claim loss of use; must prove reasonableness by reference to bailor's resources; profit-earning chattel exception applies; Hussain exceptions must be addressed. Defendant costs recovered from Stage 2 offer.

articles.title: Credit Hire and Bailees in Possession — Foundational Legal Principles (Andrew Hogan, Credit Hire Barrister, 05/2016)
articles.author: Andrew Hogan
articles.source: Credit Hire Barrister blog — credithirebarrister.com (May 2016)
articles.summary: An analysis by Andrew Hogan of the foundational legal principles governing bailment in the context of credit hire claims. The article traces the doctrine from The Winkfield [1902] through H.L. Motorworks (Willesden) Ltd v Alwahbi [1977] and O'Sullivan v Williams [1992] to establish the propositions that a bailee in possession has the full rights of an owner against a wrongdoer, can recover full damages including hire charges, and cannot be defeated by the tortfeasor pleading the bailor's superior title.
key_principles: The foundational authority is The Winkfield [1902] P42 (Court of Appeal): a bailee in possession can recover the full value of goods damaged in his possession by a negligent third party, even though he has a liability to account to the bailor.
A claim for loss of use (including credit hire charges as a specific form of mitigation) is part and parcel of the damage suffered by a person deprived of a vehicle: The Mediana [1900] AC 113.

H.L. Motorworks (Willesden) Ltd v Alwahbi [1977] RTR 276: bailee of a customer's Rolls Royce struck by a negligent driver recovered the cost of a substitute Rolls Royce hired by the owner during repairs; no reduction for a cheaper alternative in the absence of evidence that a cheaper car should have been hired.

O'Sullivan v Williams [1992] (CA): a bailee has, as against the wrongdoer, all the rights of an owner. The wrongdoer cannot plead the bailor's superior title as a defence. The bailee must account to the bailor for amounts recovered.

Summary of propositions from O'Sullivan v Williams: (1) bailee can sue the wrongdoer by reason of possession alone; (2) possession is, against the wrongdoer, equivalent to full ownership; (3) bailee can recover the full value of the chattel; (4) wrongdoer cannot plead bailor's superior title; (5) total damages cannot exceed the full loss arising from destruction; (6) a settlement by the bailor bars a claim by the bailee, and vice versa.

Practical limits: (a) damages recovered by the bailee cannot exceed the total loss; (b) where the bailor has already sued and recovered, the wrongdoer is not exposed to a second claim by the bailee; (c) once the bailor or bailee settles, the other cannot maintain a separate claim.
Credit hire charges fall within the scope of full damages available to the bailee — they are the specific form of mitigation of loss of use that the law recognises as special damage.
The doctrine applies whether the non-owner is driving with permission or by virtue of having hired the vehicle — the key requirement is possession at the relevant time.
cases_referenced: The Winkfield [1902] P42
The Mediana [1900] AC 113
H.L. Motorworks (Willesden) Ltd v Alwahbi [1977] RTR 276
O'Sullivan v Williams [1992] RTR 309 (CA)
articles.content: Foundational analysis of bailment principles in credit hire. The Winkfield establishes bailee can recover full damages. The Mediana confirms loss of use is compensatable. H.L. Motorworks confirms bailee can recover cost of hire incurred by owner. O'Sullivan v Williams confirms bailee has full owner's rights against wrongdoer, wrongdoer cannot plead bailor's superior title, settlement by either bars the other. Credit hire charges are a recognised form of mitigation of loss of use within the bailee's recoverable special damages.

articles.title: Credit Hire: A Beginner's Guide — Comprehensive Practitioner Primer (Henry Hawkesworth, Pump Court Chambers)
articles.category: Credit Hire — General Principles and Practitioner Guide
articles.author: Henry Hawkesworth
articles.source: Pump Court Chambers — www.pumpcourtchambers.com
articles.summary: A comprehensive practitioner primer on credit hire law by Henry Hawkesworth of Pump Court Chambers. Covers the full lifecycle of a credit hire claim from the underlying facts through to assessment of quantum, addressing additional benefits (Diamond v Lovell), the general principle (spot/BHR rate for pecunious claimants), impecuniosity (Lagden v O'Connor), pleading requirements (CPR PD16 paras 6.3–6.4), track allocation (CPR 26.8(2)), illegality, enforceability (unsigned agreements, misrepresentation, past consideration), need (Giles v Thompson), like-for-like vehicle, period of hire, and the BHR rate assessment framework from Stevens [2015] through to McBride [2017] and nil-excess products.
key_principles: The underlying credit hire fact pattern: non-fault claimant directed to a credit hire management firm; vehicle repaired/written off; placed in a credit hire vehicle during the repair/write-off period; use on credit subject to claim against the at-fault driver; the cost invoiced by the firm is typically significantly higher than the spot market rate.

Additional benefits — Diamond v Lovell [2002] 1 AC 384: a claimant cannot recover the full amount charged by the credit hire company because the credit hire agreement confers additional benefits beyond use of the car (credit, relief from anxiety of pursuing the at-fault party, relief from litigation risk, etc.). The value of those benefits must be deducted. The equivalent spot rate, less an allowance for additional benefits, is the measure (Lord Hoffmann).

General principle for pecunious claimants (Copley v Lawn [2009] EWCA Civ 580; Dimond v Lovell [2000]): a claimant who could have hired on the spot market can recover only the reasonable market rate (spot/BHR rate), not the credit hire rate. This was confirmed in Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management [2015] EWCA Civ 93.

BHR assessment (Stevens [2015] EWCA Civ 93): the BHR is the lowest rate for the type of car hired, in the claimant's geographical area, from a mainstream or local reputable supplier. A judge faced with a range of rates should identify a reasonable approximation. Rates need not be exactly contemporaneous nor for the identical vehicle.

McBride v UK Insurance Ltd [2017] EWCA Civ 144: reaffirmed the 'lowest reasonable rate' rule. The credit hire company's nil-excess offer should not be used as a smokescreen to avoid comparison with BHR. Where nil excess is not available from BHR providers, the correct approach is to treat the nil excess separately and ask how much of the cost of purchasing a nil-excess product is recoverable.

McBride [2017] on nil-excess products: where nil excess is not available from car hire companies at BHR, the court should value the nil-excess benefit separately. In many cases the cost of a standalone excess insurance product (e.g. Questor, Insurance4carhire.com, at approximately £3.99–£10 per day) will be recoverable rather than the premium charged by the credit hire company.

Impecuniosity (Lagden v O'Connor [2003] UKHL 64, Lords Nicholls and Hope): the exception permits recovery of the credit hire rate where the claimant could not, without making sacrifices they could not reasonably be expected to make, have afforded to hire on the spot market. The test is practical inability, not merely convenience. Impecuniosity goes both to rate and to the period of hire.

Pleading requirements — CPR PD16 para 6.3 and 6.4: where a replacement vehicle hire claim is made following a road traffic accident, the claimant must plead in the particulars of claim: (1) need for a replacement vehicle at the relevant time; (2) period of hire (start and end dates); (3) rate of hire; (4) reasonableness of the period and rate; (5) impecuniosity (if credit hire is claimed). Para 6.4 requires a copy of the hire agreement to be served with or attached to the particulars.

Track allocation — CPR 26.8(2): the court assesses the financial value of the claim, disregarding any amount not in dispute. A defendant's admission of part of the claim can affect track allocation; Akhtar v Boland [2014] EWCA Civ 872 — admissions by one party are binding on that party and cannot be resisted by the other.

Diriye v Bojaj [2020] EWCA Civ 1400 (Coulson LJ): parties must disclose important elements of their case at an early stage. Gone are the days of ambush and keeping points up your sleeve. Delay in providing critical information will adversely affect the side causing the delay and may result in Unless Orders.

Illegality (ex turpi causa non oritur damnum): recovery of damages can be defeated on public policy grounds where the claim is so closely related to the claimant's substantial wrongdoing that the court cannot be seen to condone it. Hewison v Meridien Shipping PTE [2002] EWCA Civ 1821; Agheampong v Allied Manufacturing (London) Ltd [2009] Lloyd's Rep IR 379. Key questions: was the illegality central to the claim, or merely collateral and insignificant? The burden of proving illegality is on the defendant alleging it.

Enforceability — unsigned/late-signed agreements: credit hire companies rarely allow unsigned agreements; questions arise as to whether a binding agreement came into existence at all. Armstrong v Hussain (unrep, Newcastle CC, 2015); Borley v Reed (Winchester CC, 2005). Contracts can be formed without a signature on a document, with finer details ironed out at a later stage.
Enforceability — past consideration (Company Call Centre v Sheehan [2009], Birmingham CC, unrep): 'past consideration is not good consideration.'

Enforceability — misrepresentation (Kadir v Thompson [2016] Central London County Court, HHJ Luba QC): if the credit hire company made a false representation of fact or law ('free car', 'no liability to pay in any circumstances') that induced the claimant to enter the contract, the claimant may void the contract. The burden of proving each element is on the defendant.

Need (Giles v Thompson [1993] 1 AC 142): need for a replacement car is not self-proving. The inference of need arises from the fact that a person runs a private car, but ample scope remains for the defendant to displace that inference (e.g. claimant was in hospital, abroad, behind on hire payments).

Need — business use/companies (Singh v Yaqubi [2013] EWCA Civ 23): very large hire claims involving business claimants should be carefully scrutinised. For a business claim to succeed the judge is entitled to require specific evidence of need — for example, evidence that a particular vehicle was actually in use for business purposes before the accident, and that a hired replacement was used for the same purpose.

Like-for-like — mitigation point: there is inconsistent county court authority on whether a claimant should mitigate by hiring a lesser vehicle (Bent v Highways and Utilities Construction Ltd [2011] EWCA Civ 1384 No.2). Defendants should consider submitting a non-luxury alternative BHR when obtaining rate evidence for luxury vehicle claims.

Period of hire (Mattocks v Mann [1993] RTR 13; Burdis v Livsey [2002] EWCA CIV 510; Zurich Insurance Plc v Umerji [2014] EWCA Civ 357): delays by the garage are unlikely to be the claimant's responsibility. In Umerji it was reasonable for the claimant to wait until an assessment had been made, the total loss agreed, and the appellants had had an opportunity to inspect before purchasing a replacement (a process taking a couple of weeks).
cases_referenced: Diamond v Lovell [2002] 1 AC 384
Copley v Lawn [2009] EWCA Civ 580
Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management Ltd [2015] EWCA Civ 93
McBride v UK Insurance Ltd [2017] EWCA Civ 144
Clayton v EUI Ltd [2017] EWCA Civ 144
Lagden v O'Connor [2003] UKHL 64
Diriye v Bojaj [2020] EWCA Civ 1400
Hewison v Meridien Shipping PTE [2002] EWCA Civ 1821
Agheampong v Allied Manufacturing (London) Ltd [2009] Lloyd's Rep IR 379
Giles v Thompson [1993] 1 AC 142
Singh v Yaqubi [2013] EWCA Civ 23
Bent v Highways and Utilities Construction Ltd [2011] EWCA Civ 1384
Zurich Insurance Plc v Umerji [2014] EWCA Civ 357
Mattocks v Mann [1993] RTR 13
Burdis v Livsey [2002] EWCA CIV 510
Akhtar v Boland [2014] EWCA Civ 872
Irving v Morgan Sindall [2018] EWHC 1147 (QB)
Hussain v EUI Ltd [2019] EWHC 2647 (QB)
articles.content: Comprehensive primer covering: credit hire fact pattern; additional benefits (Diamond v Lovell); pecunious claimant rate (spot/BHR per Stevens); McBride nil-excess approach; impecuniosity (Lagden); pleading (CPR PD16 paras 6.3-6.4); track allocation (CPR 26.8(2)); Diriye disclosure obligations; illegality (Hewison, Agheampong); enforceability (unsigned agreements, past consideration, misrepresentation); need (Giles v Thompson, Singh v Yaqubi); like-for-like mitigation; period of hire (Mattocks, Burdis, Zurich v Umerji).

articles.title: National Car Rental — LDW Position, Rental Requirements, Payment and Age Restrictions: BHR-Relevant Provisions (03/2026)
articles.category: BHR — Rental Company Terms: National Car Rental
articles.summary: National Car Rental's Loss Damage Waiver (LDW) is explicitly optional and not included in the rental rate — a key distinction from Enterprise (which includes DW as standard). In the UK, National primarily targets transatlantic visitors and typically shares premises, staff and vehicle fleet with co-located Enterprise and Alamo branches — all three are Enterprise Holdings brands operating as effectively the same physical operation under different names. A BHR survey citing National, Enterprise and Alamo as three independent comparators is citing the same supplier three times. The 8 FAQ documents from nationalcar.co.uk reflect primarily US/Canada operational terms; no UK-specific GBP rates are published.
key_principles: CRITICAL — SHARED UK OPERATION: In the UK, National branches typically share premises, staff and vehicle fleet with co-located Enterprise and Alamo branches. All three are Enterprise Holdings brands. A BHR survey citing National, Enterprise and Alamo as three separate comparators is not producing three independent pieces of market evidence — it is citing the same physical operation under three brand names. This fundamentally undermines the independence of the survey methodology.

UK MARKET — TRANSATLANTIC FOCUS: National's UK presence primarily serves US and Canadian customers who pre-book before travelling. National's UK published tariff may therefore reflect pre-booked transatlantic corporate/leisure pricing rather than domestic spot-market rates available to a UK credit hire claimant. The BHR provider should be required to confirm the tariff type and booking conditions underlying any National UK rate quoted.

LDW position — OPTIONAL, NOT STANDARD: National's LDW is explicitly described as 'NOT INSURANCE' and 'OPTIONAL AND NOT REQUIRED IN ORDER TO RENT A VEHICLE.' This is materially different from Enterprise (DW included in all rates) and is an important BHR comparability point.

Two LDW tiers: (1) Full LDW — waives all loss or damage; (2) LDW 3000 — National pays the first $3,000, renter pays all damage above $3,000. A BHR quote for National rates must specify which product (if any) is included and whether the claimant's excess protection need is met.

LDW does not cover: lost keys/fobs/transponders, damage in Mexico, unauthorised driver use, impaired driving, criminal acts, racing, towing, use outside US/Canada, suspended licence, transporting for hire, reckless operation, deliberate damage, off-road use, hazardous materials, renter misrepresentation, interior theft when unlocked, failure to report accidents.

Enterprise Holdings group: National, Enterprise and Alamo are all part of Enterprise Holdings. Where a BHR survey relies on multiple Enterprise Holdings brands as comparators, this raises a question about whether those rates represent genuinely independent market evidence or internal group pricing.

Minimum rental age: 21 years. Young Renter Fee applies for ages 21–24. The published FAQ does not give a UK GBP rate for the Young Renter Fee — only USD rates for specific US states are quoted. Challenge point: if a BHR report relies on National UK rates, the actual age-related surcharge in GBP must be disclosed.

Credit card required: major credit card in renter's name with available credit must be presented at time of rental. Debit/check cards accepted as payment on return only — not as deposit. Cash not accepted as deposit at any location.

Debit card and cash policy — impecuniosity relevance: a claimant without a credit card cannot access National Car Rental at all (for standard UK locations). This is a relevant consideration in impecuniosity arguments: if the claimant had no credit card, National was not a realistic alternative regardless of their financial position.
Available payment cards: American Express, MasterCard, Visa, Discover Network. Gift/prepaid cards accepted for final payment on return only — not as deposit.

IMPORTANT LIMITATION on these documents: all 8 FAQs served from nationalcar.co.uk reflect US/Canada operational terms. UK-specific GBP rates for young driver surcharges, additional driver fees, and LDW pricing are not published in these documents. Any BHR report relying on National UK rates should be cross-checked against the UK booking engine.
articles.source: National Car Rental — nationalcar.co.uk FAQ pages (retrieved 26 March 2026)
articles.content: National LDW is optional/not standard. Two tiers: full LDW and LDW 3000 ($3k excess). Multiple exclusions. Enterprise Holdings group (with Enterprise and Alamo). Minimum age 21, young renter fee 21-24 (GBP rate not published). Credit card required at rental; debit cards/cash not accepted as deposit. FAQ content reflects US/Canada terms — UK GBP rates not published.

articles.title: National Car Rental — Additional Driver, Towing, Roadside Plus and Pet Policy: BHR-Relevant Provisions (03/2026)
articles.category: BHR — Rental Company Terms: National Car Rental
articles.summary: National Car Rental's additional driver fee averages $12/day in the US (UK GBP rate not published). Multiple waiver conditions apply (Emerald Club members, spouses, corporate rentals, disabled renters). Towing is strictly prohibited. Roadside Plus (RSP) is an optional paid product covering 24/7 roadside assistance in the US and Canada only — not described as available in the UK. Pets are permitted if crated; cleaning fees apply.
key_principles: ENTERPRISE HOLDINGS GROUP: National, Enterprise and Alamo are all Enterprise Holdings brands. UK National branches typically share premises, staff and vehicle fleet with co-located Enterprise and Alamo branches. Where a BHR report relies on all three as comparators, challenge the independence of that evidence. The BHR provider should confirm whether the quoted National, Enterprise and Alamo branches operate from the same site, share fleet management, and are commercially distinct entities for pricing purposes.

Additional driver fee: 'varies and currently averages $12 a day' (US rate). UK GBP rate not published in available FAQ documents. This is a documentation gap — any BHR report using National UK rates must disclose the actual UK additional driver fee charged.

Additional driver fee waiver conditions: (1) spouse/domestic partner in US states with automatic authorised driver laws (CA, IL, MO, NV, OR, WI); (2) Emerald Club member's spouse, common law spouse or same-sex domestic partner at same address; (3) Emerald Club member's immediate family (parent, child, sibling) at same address; (4) corporate/commercial rental with assigned National contract; (5) disabled renter who cannot drive — one fee waived.
Additional driver requirements: must meet all age requirements; must appear at the rental counter with the primary renter; must present a valid driver's licence.

Towing strictly prohibited: 'National does not allow a hitch to be attached or towing of any kind with the rental vehicle.' If the claimant's damaged vehicle was regularly used for towing, National is not an appropriate BHR comparator.

Roadside Plus (RSP): optional purchasable product. Covers 24/7 roadside assistance where available — lost key replacement (including remote entry devices), flat tyre service (tow if no inflated spare available; replacement tyre cost not covered), lockout service, jump-starts, fuel delivery up to 3 gallons. Available in US and Canada only. Not described as available in the UK. Without RSP, standard roadside assistance charges apply.
Pet policy: pets permitted if kept crated/in carrier. Vehicle must be returned clean and free of pet hair; cleaning/detailing fees apply if not. Service animals permitted without a carrier.

BHR challenge — RSP availability in UK: RSP is explicitly described as available in the US and Canada only. If a BHR report includes a National UK rate incorporating RSP, this product may not be validly available on UK rentals as described in the FAQ.

BHR challenge — documentation gap: the absence of published UK-specific terms covering CDW/LDW pricing, young driver surcharges in GBP, and additional driver fees in GBP is itself a challenge point if a BHR report relies on National UK without disclosing the specific rate components used.
articles.source: National Car Rental — nationalcar.co.uk FAQ pages (retrieved 26 March 2026)
articles.content: Additional driver fee averages $12/day US (UK GBP unpublished). Multiple waiver conditions including Emerald Club. Towing strictly prohibited. Roadside Plus optional, US/Canada only — not described as UK available. Pets permitted if crated. Significant documentation gap: UK-specific GBP rates for all chargeable items not published in available FAQs.

articles.title: ABP (Auto Body Professionals) Rate Card 2026: Vehicle Repair Costs, Coles v Hetherton, and BHR Intersection
articles.source: ABP Club — Guide to Retail and Non-Contract Charges (effective 1 January 2026)
articles.author: Auto Body Professionals Club
principles: The ABP rate card publishes 'Guide to Retail and Non-Contract Charges' — rates for work NOT performed under insurer volume/block agreements; it represents what a repairer without an insurer contract would charge at arm's length.
The standard bodyshop labour rate for 2026 is £83.28/hr for standard cars and vans; prestige vehicles and specialist work (e.g. aluminium repair) attract £103.06/hr.
A 15% regional uplift is expressly stated in the card footnote to reflect regional cost variations — particularly relevant for London/South East repairs.

Coles v Hetherton [2013] EWCA Civ 1704 is the primary authority on recovery of repair costs: the measure is the reasonable cost of repair at the time and place of repair — not what the insurer would have paid under a managed-repair arrangement.

Insurers routinely resist ABP rates by arguing that managed-repair rates (their own network tariffs) represent the correct measure of loss — but Coles v Hetherton expressly distinguishes the claimant's right to recover reasonable retail costs from the hypothetical cost of insurer-arranged repairs.

The ABP card is used by repairers as a negotiating base with insurers; it is not a binding contractual tariff, and individual repairers may apply it with or without a margin depending on location and complexity.

A tactical anomaly arises when insurers argue a claimant should recover only the ABP rate, while simultaneously refusing to offer to pay that rate — the ABP card itself is not an insurer-agreed schedule and insurers have no contractual relationship with ABP members.

The mobility solution charge in the ABP card (car: £46.84/day; EV: £62.46/day) provides a repairer-sourced credit hire comparator figure that is directly relevant to BHR evidence in claims where the bodyshop arranged the hire vehicle.

EV-specific charges are now substantial: risk assessment 0.5hr, battery quarantine 4.0hr (if damage suspected), power-down/reinstatement 3.0hr — these represent genuine additional cost unavailable under older insurer tariffs.

ADAS calibration charges (first calibration £312.30; each subsequent £156.15; plus extensive road test 0.5hr) reflect mandatory post-repair safety requirements under IIR verification rules; these are often contested by insurers as unnecessary but are required by regulation.

The BS 10125 compliance charge (£41.64/job) and vehicle manufacturer approval charge (£5/hr uplift) reflect genuine accreditation costs that repairers are entitled to recover on top of base labour rates.
The repair plan compilation fee (£176.96/job) covers methods research and ADAS identification — a pre-repair assessment cost that precedes any insurer authority and is recoverable as a head of loss.

Storage charges (£41.64/24hrs per bay) and salvage administration (£98.89) are standalone recoverable heads of loss independent of repair cost, relevant where insurer delays have prolonged the storage period.

The credit repair model (where CHOs refer claims to bodyshops for credit repair) creates a triangular dynamic: the claimant receives both a credit hire vehicle and credit repair, the repairer invoices the CHO, and the CHO presents the combined claim — all governed by Coles v Hetherton for the repair element.

Keoghs (Gary Herring, February 2026) argues that overturning Coles v Hetherton through the courts is effectively impossible. The Court of Appeal is bound to follow its own decisions under Young v Bristol Aeroplane Co Ltd [1944] KB 718, and only the Supreme Court could overturn Coles.

The Supreme Court refused permission to appeal in Coles in 2014 — without the usual caveat about timing. The refusal was by Lords Mance, Sumption, and Hodge on the basis of no arguable point of law. Returning 12 years later to argue fundamental error is described as incredible.

A challenge to Coles cannot be founded on public policy (consumer detriment) alone. A challenger would need to show the judgment was fundamentally wrong in law — a very high threshold given the resounding judgments at both High Court and Court of Appeal level.

The CMA concluded in 2014 that Coles causes no meaningful consumer detriment. The motor insurance market is highly competitive and any insurer margin flows back to consumers via lower premiums. The net annual cost of subrogated repair models was just £30 million across the entire industry — far too small to justify fundamental legal change.

Coles is underpinned by well-established principles affirmed in Jones v Stroud District Council [1986] 1 WLR 1141, The London Corporation [1935] P 70, Clark v Ardington [2002] EWCA Civ 510, and Bee v Jenson [2007] EWCA Civ 923: the loss crystallises at the moment of damage (diminution in value) and insurance arrangements are irrelevant (res inter alios acta).

Coles has been followed and cited with approval in Technip Saudi Arabia Ltd v Maridive & Oil Services SAE [2023] EWHC 1859 (Comm) and in Northern Ireland in Chivers v O'Loughlin [2017] NIQB 18, confirming its cross-jurisdictional authority.

The more realistic defendant strategy is to identify and challenge specific repair uplift models that fall outside what Coles reasonably permits, rather than seeking to overturn Coles itself. Keoghs successfully challenged the PEC credit repair uplift model — nearly 50% of cases dismissed in full and 65% overall savings — leading to the operator exiting the market.

Insurers pushing the envelope beyond standard ABP guide rates, or opportunistically applying uplifts to items beyond mere repairs, are vulnerable to targeted County Court challenges without needing to attack the Coles principle itself.

cases.name: Coles v Hetherton
cases.relevance: Primary authority on the measure of repair cost recovery. Court of Appeal confirmed the claimant is entitled to recover the reasonable retail cost of repair at the time and place of repair, not what the insurer would have paid through a managed-repair network. The distinction between the claimant's actual cost and the insurer's hypothetical managed cost is fundamental to resisting insurer challenges to ABP-rate invoices.
cases.key_principle: Measure of loss for repairs is reasonable retail cost — not insurer network rate.

cases.name: Darbishire v Warran
cases.relevance: The duty to mitigate does not require a claimant to use the cheapest available repairer if there are reasonable grounds for using a more expensive one. Relevant where insurers argue the claimant should have used an approved-repairer network charging below ABP rates.
cases.key_principle: Mitigation duty does not mandate cheapest repairer.

cases.name: Approved Repairer arguments post-Coles
cases.relevance: Attempts by insurers to argue that managed-repair network rates are the proper measure of loss have consistently failed where Coles v Hetherton is properly argued. The insurer's own unilateral pricing arrangements cannot cap the claimant's entitlement to reasonable retail repair costs.
cases.key_principle: Insurer tariff rates do not cap claimant's entitlement under Coles.

labour.regional_uplift: Up to 15% to reflect regional cost variations

compliance_charges.repair_plan_compilation_fee: £176.96 per job (includes methods research and ADAS identification)

bhr_relevance.credit_repair_model: CHOs frequently arrange credit repair as well as credit hire. The ABP rate card governs what the repairer invoices in non-contract situations. The combined credit hire + credit repair claim is then presented to the at-fault insurer under the credit hire agreement and Coles v Hetherton respectively.
bhr_relevance.insurer_resistance_pattern: Insurers argue: (1) the repairer should have used the insurer's approved network at lower tariff rates; (2) ABP rates are excessive; (3) specific line items (ADAS, EV charges, compliance fees) are not recoverable. Each of these arguments fails on Coles v Hetherton and Darbishire v Warran authority.
bhr_relevance.abp_rate_as_cap_argument: A tactical inconsistency sometimes deployed by insurers: they argue that recovery should be capped at the ABP rate (thereby treating the card as a ceiling rather than a floor), while simultaneously not being contractually bound to pay ABP rates and not offering to pay them. This argument should be challenged.
bhr_relevance.mobility_solution_as_bhr_evidence: The ABP card's mobility solution daily rate (£46.84/day for cars) provides a third-party comparator for BHR purposes where the repairer arranged the hire. It is repairer-derived, not CHO-derived, and may corroborate or challenge BHR evidence depending on the vehicle category hired.
bhr_relevance.ev_and_adas_line_items: As the vehicle fleet electrifies, ADAS and EV-specific repair line items are becoming standard. Insurers that fail to update their managed-repair tariffs to include these charges will create a growing gap between what they offer to pay and what arm's-length repair actually costs — widening the Coles v Hetherton argument.
bhr_relevance.regional_uplift: The expressly stated 15% regional uplift acknowledges that London and South East repair costs substantially exceed the national guide rate. Claims arising from accidents in these regions should apply the uplift as a matter of course.

articles.content: ABP (Auto Body Professionals Club) Rate Card 2026 — BHR and Repair Cost Recovery Analysis. ABP Club was established in September 2004 and now has over 2,500 individual members. It publishes an annual 'Guide to Retail and Non-Contract Charges' — rates applicable to vehicle body repair work performed outside insurer volume or managed-repair contract arrangements. The 2026 card is effective from 1 January 2026. All values exclude VAT. Key 2026 rates: Standard bodyshop labour £83.28/hr; prestige and specialist work (e.g. aluminium repair) £103.06/hr; vans up to 3.5t GVW £83.28/hr. Regional uplift of up to 15% applies. Compliance charges include BS 10125 compliance £41.64/job, vehicle manufacturer approval uplift £5/hr, and repair plan compilation £176.96/job. EV/hybrid charges: risk assessment 0.5hr, battery quarantine 4.0hr, power-down/reinstatement 3.0hr, full EV recharge £52.05. ADAS calibrations: first calibration £312.30, subsequent calibrations £156.15 each; these are mandatory under IIR verification requirements and insurers cannot unilaterally exclude them. Mobility solution charges: car £46.84/day, EV £62.46/day — directly comparable to BHR rates. Storage: £41.64/24hrs per bay. Coles v Hetherton [2013] EWCA Civ 1704 is the primary authority: the claimant is entitled to recover the reasonable retail cost of repair at the time and place of repair, not the insurer's managed-repair network rate. Insurers routinely resist ABP-rate invoices by arguing managed-repair tariffs are the correct measure of loss — this argument fails on Coles. Darbishire v Warran [1963] 1 WLR 1067 confirms that the duty to mitigate does not require the cheapest available repairer. The ABP card is used by repairers as a negotiating base; it is not a binding tariff. A key tactical anomaly arises when insurers argue for an ABP-rate cap while simultaneously not offering to pay that rate and having no contractual relationship with ABP members. In the credit repair model, CHOs refer claims to bodyshops; the ABP rate governs invoicing for non-contract repairs; the combined credit hire and credit repair claim is presented to the at-fault insurer. As vehicle fleets electrify, ADAS and EV repair line items create a growing gap between managed-repair tariffs (which often lag) and arm's-length costs under Coles. From a defendant perspective, Gary Herring of Keoghs (February 2026) argues that overturning Coles through the courts is effectively impossible: the Supreme Court refused permission to appeal without caveat in 2014 (Lords Mance, Sumption and Hodge), the Court of Appeal is bound by its own decisions under Young v Bristol Aeroplane [1944], and a challenge cannot be founded on public policy alone but would require showing fundamental error in law. The CMA 2014 investigation found no meaningful consumer detriment (net cost £30m/year industry-wide). The more effective strategy for defendants is to identify and challenge specific models that exceed what Coles reasonably permits — such as uplifts applied beyond repairs or rates exceeding ABP guide figures — rather than attacking the Coles principle itself.

articles.title: MID Entry Does Not Bind Motor Insurer — Nagorski v Nikolics and Tradewise Insurance (Weightmans, 2021)
