articles.source: Weightmans LLP — media centre article, 2 July 2021
articles.author: Weightmans LLP
principles: An entry on the Motor Insurance Database (MID) does not of itself bind the motor insurer to satisfy a judgment: the MID is a detection tool, not a conclusive statement of liability.

Section 151 of the Road Traffic Act 1988 sets out the preconditions for insurer liability: the vehicle use must be covered by the terms of the policy (s.151(2)(a)(a)) or would be covered if the policy insured all persons (s.151(2)(a)(b)) — a MID entry cannot override or extend those statutory preconditions.

Where a vehicle has been sold or transferred before the date of an accident, the policy description must still be satisfied at the date of the accident. If the vehicle no longer falls within the category of vehicles insured (e.g. 'property of the insured or in their custody or control for motor trade purposes'), there is no RTA liability even if the MID entry persists.

Bristol Alliance v Williams [2013] QB 806 confirms that s.151 RTA is not a 'catch all': the terms of the policy must be considered and cannot be bypassed by reference to database entries or procedural shortcuts.

For motor trade and fleet policies where the policyholder controls MID updates, insurers are not bound merely because the policyholder failed to remove a sold vehicle from the MID — the delegation of MID management to the policyholder does not create insurer liability beyond the policy terms.

Tattersall v Drysdale [1935] 2 KB 174 remains relevant authority for the proposition that sale and cessation of possession of a vehicle brings any insurable interest in it to an end — and thereby ends RTA insurer liability — where the policy covered a specific named vehicle.

The decision in Nagorski is fact-specific: because the Tradewise policy covered vehicles 'the property of the insured or in their custody or control', the transfer to Mr Nikolics removed the vehicle from the policy description. A policy covering a specific named vehicle would require the additional Tattersall v Drysdale insurable interest argument to achieve the same result.

Prompt and accurate removal of a vehicle from the MID on mid-term cancellation remains important: to avoid ongoing liability as the Article 75 insurer under MIB's Articles of Association, a cancellation entry must appear on the MID prior to the date of any subsequent accident.

The OIC Portal (Official Injury Claim) relies on the MID to auto-allocate cases to insurers. Nagorski signals that insurers should not simply accept OIC Portal allocations based on MID data alone — they must investigate whether the policy genuinely covered the vehicle at the time of the accident.

Credit hire practitioners routinely identify the at-fault insurer by MID search. Nagorski is a reminder that MID identification is a starting point, not conclusive proof of insurance cover: an insurer named on the MID may have no RTA liability if the vehicle had been sold, the policy had lapsed, or the use fell outside the policy description.

Where a MID search returns an insurer that declines liability, the claimant or CHO must consider: (a) whether another insurer applies; (b) whether the MIB (as insurer of last resort) should be notified; (c) whether the Article 75 liability route is arguable if cancellation was not properly recorded on the MID before the accident date.

cases.name: Nagorski v Nikolics and Tradewise Insurance
cases.citation: Liverpool County Court (Recorder Simon Parrington), preliminary issue decision, September 2018 accident / 2021 decision
cases.relevance: Core case: MID entry showing Tradewise Insurance as insurer did not bind Tradewise where the vehicle had been sold by the policyholder before the accident and no longer met the policy description. Recorder Parrington held that s.151 RTA preconditions were not satisfied.
cases.key_principle: MID entry does not bind insurer where policy description no longer satisfied.

cases.name: Bristol Alliance v Williams
cases.relevance: Court of Appeal authority confirming that s.151 RTA is not a 'catch all' provision — the terms of the policy must be examined. Cited by Tradewise to defeat the argument that any vehicle appearing on the MID automatically triggers insurer liability.
cases.key_principle: Section 151 RTA requires policy terms to be satisfied — it is not a blanket liability provision.

cases.name: Tattersall v Drysdale
cases.relevance: Classic authority that sale and cessation of possession of a vehicle ends the insurable interest in it, thereby ending any insurer RTA liability. Relevant where a policy covers a specific named vehicle (as opposed to a category of vehicles) — in that scenario the insurer would need to rely on Tattersall rather than the policy description argument used in Nagorski.
cases.key_principle: Sale of vehicle ends insurable interest and insurer's RTA liability for named-vehicle policies.

credit_hire_relevance.insurer_identification: CHOs and solicitors rely on MID searches to identify the at-fault insurer when pursuing credit hire recovery. Nagorski confirms this is a starting point only — the named insurer may contest liability under s.151 RTA if the policy did not cover the vehicle at the time of the accident.
credit_hire_relevance.oic_portal_risk: The OIC Portal auto-allocates claims to insurers based on MID data. An insurer allocated a claim via the portal has the right (and arguably the duty under Nagorski commentary) to investigate whether the allocation reflects genuine RTA liability before accepting the case.
credit_hire_relevance.mib_fallback: Where the MID-identified insurer has no liability, the MIB becomes relevant as insurer of last resort. A key distinction applies: if the insurer cancelled the policy and properly removed the vehicle from the MID before the accident date, Article 75 MIB liability does not arise. If removal preceded the accident but the entry remained, Nagorski suggests the insurer can still escape liability — but MIB exposure may arise.
credit_hire_relevance.motor_trade_and_fleet_policies: These policy types are the most common context for MID disputes in credit hire because replacement vehicles, courtesy cars, and fleet vehicles may appear on the MID under policies that do not actually cover the specific use at the time of the accident.
credit_hire_relevance.claimant_strategy: Where an insurer rejects a credit hire demand citing Nagorski-type arguments (MID entry does not bind), the claimant must: verify the policy terms in force at the date of loss; check whether the vehicle use fell within or outside the policy description; consider whether Tradewise-style delegation of MID management creates any equitable argument; and assess MIB notification as a precautionary step.

articles.content: Nagorski v Nikolics and Tradewise Insurance — Liverpool County Court (Recorder Simon Parrington). Commentary by Weightmans LLP (2 July 2021). Key holding: an entry on the Motor Insurance Database (MID) does not bind the motor insurer to satisfy a judgment under the Road Traffic Act 1988. Facts: a Vauxhall Zafira was involved in a collision on 26 September 2018. The MID showed the vehicle as covered under a Tradewise motor trade policy in the name of Tibor Kiss T/A Tibor Autos. However, Mr Kiss had transferred the vehicle to the defendant driver, Mr Nikolics, on approximately 6 August 2018 — some weeks before the accident — and the vehicle was not removed from the Tradewise policy on the MID. Tradewise argued: (1) the MID entry did not bind it; (2) after the transfer, the vehicle no longer met the policy description ('any motor vehicle the property of the insured or in their custody or control for motor trade purposes'). Recorder Parrington accepted both arguments. Section 151 RTA is not a catch-all; the policy terms must be satisfied (following Bristol Alliance v Williams [2013] QB 806). The MID entry — even with a policyholder 'warning' about leaving sold vehicles on it — had no binding effect on the insurer's liability. Credit hire relevance: MID searches to identify the at-fault insurer are a starting point only. An insurer shown on the MID may have no s.151 liability if the vehicle was sold, the policy lapsed, or the use fell outside the policy description. The OIC Portal auto-allocates claims by MID — insurers should not accept allocations uncritically. Where MID-identified insurer has no liability, MIB notification should follow. Article 75 MIB liability is avoided only if the insurer cancelled and properly recorded removal on the MID before the accident date. Motor trade and fleet policies (where policyholders control MID updates) are the most common context for these disputes in credit hire and personal injury motor claims.

articles.title: Motor Insurers' Bureau (MIB): Claims Process, Insurer of Last Resort, and Credit Hire Challenges
articles.source: Motor Insurers' Bureau — Your Guide to Making an MIB Claim, Issue 5 (September 2015)
articles.author: Motor Insurers' Bureau
principles: The MIB is a non-profit company funded by compulsory contributions from all motor insurers under the Road Traffic Act 1988. It operates as the insurer of last resort in three categories: (1) uninsured drivers, under the Uninsured Drivers Agreement with the Department for Transport; (2) untraced drivers, under the Untraced Drivers Agreement; (3) foreign-registered vehicles, under the Green Card System.

For vehicle damage claims arising from accidents on or after 1 August 2015: claimants who hold comprehensive motor insurance cannot make a vehicle damage claim to the MIB — they must claim from their own insurer. Only claimants without comprehensive cover may bring a vehicle damage claim to MIB. This is a critical threshold for credit hire recovery.

The comprehensive insurance exclusion (post-August 2015) creates a direct challenge to credit hire in MIB cases: if the claimant had comprehensive insurance, they should have used their own insurer's repair/courtesy car provision rather than entering a credit hire agreement. This directly engages the 'need' argument — a claimant who could have obtained a free or subsidised replacement vehicle through their own insurer may not establish need for a credit hire vehicle at a commercial rate.

MIB is not a party to the General Terms of Agreement (GTA) and does not accept GTA-rate credit hire charges or GTA methodology for BHR assessment. Unlike participating insurers, MIB will challenge credit hire costs from first principles — including hire rate, duration, need, and impecuniosity — without any GTA safe harbour.

In untraced driver claims, the claimant bears the burden of satisfying MIB that the accident occurred and that the untraced driver was at fault. MIB conducts its own investigation, including obtaining police reports where liability is in dispute, conflicting evidence exists, or serious injury is involved — investigations that can extend timescales well beyond the three-month target.

MIB's target decision timescale is three months on whether to pay compensation; for Low Value PI claims via the portal, six weeks. The actual quantum decision typically takes longer. These extended timescales mean CHOs face prolonged uncertainty over recovery, which bears on the question of what constitutes a reasonable period of hire while the claim is investigated.

A police report is required where: (a) liability is in dispute; (b) conflicting evidence is presented; or (c) any person is seriously injured. Police reports cannot be released until criminal prosecutions are concluded — in hit-and-run or uninsured driver scenarios, this can cause months of delay before MIB can conclude its investigation.

Compensation is only payable where fault can be established on the part of the at-fault driver. Where the claimant was partly at fault, the payment is reduced proportionately (contributory negligence). Where the claimant was wholly at fault, no compensation is payable. This affects the recoverable credit hire period in cases of split liability.

MIB deducts a property damage 'excess' from vehicle damage claims — the amount set in its Agreement with the Government. This excess is a net deduction from any credit repair or vehicle damage recovery and must be factored into the overall claim calculation.

MIB can consider interim payments during its investigation — relevant for CHOs managing cash flow during long-running uninsured or untraced driver investigations. Interim payments are discretionary and require supporting documentation.

In untraced driver claims, the Untraced Drivers Agreement requires the claimant to report the accident to the police and notify MIB within specified time limits. Failure to comply with these conditions is a ground for MIB to refuse or reduce compensation — including any credit hire element.

For foreign-registered vehicle accidents, if the foreign insurer has a UK representative under the Green Card System, that representative handles the claim (and remains answerable to MIB). If no UK representative exists, MIB compensates directly. In both cases, GTA arrangements do not apply and credit hire recovery is argued on retail/common law principles.

The Low Value PI Protocol (OIC/Claims Portal) applies to MIB cases involving uninsured drivers where the claim falls within the portal value thresholds. However, untraced driver claims are outside the portal — they proceed under the Untraced Drivers Agreement process, which has its own separate timescales and conditions.

Claims involving foreign-registered lorries require both front and rear registration numbers, as trailers may be registered separately in some countries. This practical requirement can frustrate credit hire recovery in HGV accident cases where the claimant failed to record both numbers at the scene.

cases.name: MIB Uninsured Drivers Agreement
cases.citation: Agreement between MIB and the Secretary of State for Transport (current version post-2015)
cases.relevance: Governs MIB's obligation to satisfy unsatisfied judgments against uninsured drivers. The Agreement sets out conditions precedent to MIB's liability — including notice requirements, cooperation obligations, and the exclusion for claimants with comprehensive insurance in vehicle damage claims post-August 2015.
cases.key_principle: MIB satisfies judgments against uninsured drivers subject to Agreement conditions — not a direct insurer.

cases.name: MIB Untraced Drivers Agreement
cases.citation: Agreement between MIB and the Secretary of State for Transport (current version)
cases.relevance: Governs claims where the at-fault driver cannot be identified (hit and run). MIB investigates and adjudicates the claim itself rather than awaiting a court judgment. Police reporting requirements and time limits are conditions precedent. Credit hire in untraced cases is particularly hard to recover — no insurer to serve proceedings on and MIB scrutinises all heads of loss.
cases.key_principle: Untraced claims require MIB investigation and satisfaction — not litigation against a named defendant.

cases.name: Bristol Alliance v Williams
cases.relevance: Court of Appeal authority on the scope of s.151 RTA — intersects with MIB cases where the question is whether a policy was in force at all. Relevant context alongside Nagorski v Nikolics (article 94) for understanding when MIB rather than a named insurer bears liability.
cases.key_principle: Section 151 RTA and MIB obligations are distinct routes — both require the underlying statutory and agreement conditions to be satisfied.

credit_hire_challenges.comprehensive_insurance_and_need: The most significant challenge in MIB credit hire cases: if the claimant held comprehensive motor insurance at the time of the accident, they cannot bring a vehicle damage claim to MIB (post-August 2015). This raises the immediate question of why they entered a credit hire agreement rather than using their own insurer's replacement vehicle provision — directly engaging the 'need' defence available to all defendants including MIB.
credit_hire_challenges.no_gta_protection: MIB is not a GTA signatory. There is no Basic Hire Rate methodology safe harbour, no agreed uplift structure, and no industry protocol governing how MIB assesses credit hire rates. Every element of the credit hire charge — rate, duration, vehicle category, need, impecuniosity — is challenged from scratch on common law principles.
credit_hire_challenges.extended_timescales: MIB's investigation timescale (3 months to liability decision; longer for quantum) means the hire period during investigation may itself become contested. MIB may argue that a reasonable claimant would not have continued hiring throughout a prolonged investigation and would have mitigated by returning the vehicle.
credit_hire_challenges.police_report_delays: In disputed liability or serious injury cases, a police report is required — but cannot be released until criminal proceedings conclude. Where the at-fault driver was prosecuted (common in uninsured driver cases), this can add months or years to the timeline. The hire period during police report delay is particularly vulnerable to MIB challenge.
credit_hire_challenges.untraced_driver_burden: In untraced cases, the claimant must prove the accident occurred and the untraced driver was at fault — all without a named defendant. Credit hire charges in untraced claims are subject to rigorous MIB scrutiny. CHOs must ensure the hire is properly evidenced, the need is clear, and the rate is demonstrably reasonable before presenting a credit hire element in an untraced claim.
credit_hire_challenges.excess_deduction: The property damage excess deducted from MIB vehicle damage settlements reduces the net recovery available to credit repair arrangements operating alongside the credit hire. CHOs must account for this in their financial modelling.
credit_hire_challenges.foreign_vehicle_cases: Credit hire recovery in Green Card/foreign vehicle cases involves engaging with the UK representative of the foreign insurer (if any) or MIB directly — neither of whom operates under GTA terms. Rate evidence must be presented on a retail comparator basis.

articles.content: Motor Insurers' Bureau (MIB) — Your Guide to Making an MIB Claim (Issue 5, September 2015). The MIB is a non-profit company funded by all UK motor insurers under the Road Traffic Act 1988. It operates as the insurer of last resort in three scenarios: (1) accidents with uninsured drivers, under the Uninsured Drivers Agreement with the Department for Transport; (2) accidents with untraced/hit-and-run drivers, under the Untraced Drivers Agreement; (3) accidents with foreign-registered vehicles, under the Green Card System. Credit hire challenges in MIB cases are substantial. First, the comprehensive insurance exclusion: claimants who held comprehensive motor insurance cannot bring a vehicle damage claim to MIB for accidents on or after 1 August 2015 — they must use their own insurer. This directly challenges the 'need' for credit hire, as a comprehensive insurance holder could have obtained a replacement vehicle through their own insurer. Second, MIB is not a GTA signatory — there is no safe harbour for Basic Hire Rate methodology and every element of the credit hire charge is challenged from scratch on common law principles. Third, MIB investigates rather than simply accepting claims: its 3-month liability decision target (6 weeks for portal) can extend significantly where police reports are required (triggered by disputed liability, conflicting evidence, or serious injury) — police reports being unavailable until criminal proceedings conclude. Fourth, compensation requires established fault; contributory negligence reduces the payment. Fifth, MIB deducts a property damage excess from vehicle damage settlements. Untraced driver claims are outside the OIC Portal and operate under the Untraced Drivers Agreement — the claimant must prove the accident and fault without a named defendant, making credit hire recovery particularly difficult. MIB can make interim payments during investigation. False information in the claim form risks prosecution and claim refusal. For foreign-registered lorries, both front and rear registration numbers should be obtained as trailers may be separately registered.

articles.title: Motorcycle Credit Hire BHR: ABI-GTA Category B2, Insurance Comparability, and Niche Market Challenges
articles.source: BHR expert reports and rebuttal statements in motorcycle credit hire cases (2020–2025)
articles.author: credit-hire.ai synthesis
principles: When an insurer offers a claimant a car as a replacement for a damaged motorbike, the correct framing is not whether car hire terms differ from motorcycle hire terms. The sole question is whether a car is a functional substitute for this particular claimant's motorbike. For many motorcycle claimants this will be obvious — a delivery rider cannot use a car for delivery work; a commuter reliant on filtering cannot replicate that journey in a car; a claimant with only a motorcycle licence cannot legally drive a car at all. But the answer must come from the claimant, not be assumed by the CHO.

Before declining an insurer's offer of a car in substitution for a motorcycle, the CHO must ask the claimant directly whether a car would meet their needs, and document the response in writing before any communication declining the offer is sent. If the claimant would in fact have been content with a car — for example, a claimant who uses the bike only occasionally for leisure and has access to a car — the refusal of a free or discounted car will be treated as a failure to mitigate, and hire charges will not be recoverable from the date of the declined offer.

Motorcycle hire is a niche sector that operates on fundamentally different commercial and insurance principles from car hire. The majority of motorcycle hire companies in the UK are independently owned, London-centric operators whose primary market is delivery and courier riders — not accident replacement customers. Car hire principles and methodologies cannot simply be transposed to motorcycle hire.

The ABI-GTA classification for motorcycles and scooters is Category B2. The Stevens mainstream/reputable local supplier test applies to motorcycle hire comparators in the same way as car hire: a comparator company must either be a mainstream national supplier or a reputable local supplier in the claimant's area. Niche, delivery-focused, London operators with limited or no national presence frequently fail this test for claimants outside London.

The single most important comparability issue in motorcycle BHR cases is insurance. Accident replacement CHOs typically provide comprehensive insurance with a zero excess (achieved through a Collision Damage and Theft Waiver). Most motorcycle hire comparators cited in BHR reports provide only third-party insurance, and some provide no insurance at all — requiring hirers to provide their own cover. These are not equivalent products: a claimant without their own comprehensive cover cannot place a hired motorcycle under their existing policy, and private motor insurers are generally unwilling to extend cover to profit-earning chattels.

No Excess Reimbursement Insurance (ERI) product exists for motorcycle or scooter hirers. This has been acknowledged in BHR expert evidence: where a motorcycle comparator imposes an irreducible excess, there is no stand-alone ERI market to plug the gap — unlike the car hire sector where products such as Questor, Insurance4CarHire, CoverForYou and ICar Hire Insurance are available. The absence of ERI for motorcycles means that any comparator imposing a non-zero excess cannot be made comparable to a zero-excess CHO product.

Common irreducible excess figures in motorcycle hire comparator evidence: About Town £600; Scooterised (uninsured option) £1,000; Ugoscooters £500. Where the claimant hired from a CHO with a zero excess obtained through a CDW product at a daily rate (e.g. £30.12/day), these excesses cannot be equivalenced by ERI — making these comparators structurally incomparable on insurance terms.

Use restriction is a critical eligibility filter for motorcycle comparators. About Town does not permit its scooters to be used for fast-food delivery — it operates on SDP (Social, Domestic and Pleasure) terms only. Where the claimant was a fast-food delivery rider (and the replacement scooter was used for that purpose), About Town rates are simply not available to that claimant. The appropriate BHR evidence must reflect delivery/courier-rated hire.

Driving licence requirements impose additional eligibility filters. Scooterised requires the hirer's licence to have been issued by authorised authorities at least 12 months before the commencement of hire, and the hirer must be aged 21 or over. A claimant whose licence was issued less than 12 months before hire cannot hire from Scooterised, regardless of impecuniosity considerations. These restrictions must be checked against the claimant's actual licence history before a BHR comparator is relied upon.

Ben Scooters imposes a minimum 4-week (28-day) hire period and provides no insurance — hirers must provide their own cover. Where the claimant did not hold comprehensive motor insurance and the hire period was unknown at the outset (as is normal in accident replacement), Ben Scooters is not a realistic comparator: (a) the claimant would have no cover for damage or theft; (b) the minimum hire commitment exposes the claimant to irrecoverable charges if the period is shorter; (c) private motor insurers will not typically extend cover to hired profit-earning chattels.

Geographic availability of motorcycle comparators requires careful scrutiny. Ugoscooters' registered address (Companies House) is in Saffron Walden, Essex — approximately 58 miles from a claimant with an NW10 postcode. BHR expert evidence presenting Ugoscooters as a comparator without disclosing this distance fails the Stevens geographic locality requirement. A claimant cannot reasonably be expected to travel 116 miles (round trip) to collect and return a hire scooter.

Delivery cost transparency is a specific weakness in motorcycle BHR reports. Where a comparator claims to offer delivery but the cost is not published on its website and is described in the report as 'not known', the BHR calculation is incomplete. The claimant is entitled to delivery and collection at no extra charge (or the equivalent cost added to the BHR) — an unknown delivery charge cannot be relied upon as a complete comparator.

The rolling hire period calculation creates a structural problem for motorcycle BHR reports. Where the hire period (e.g. 65 days) does not divide evenly into the tariff period (weeks), the claimant would need to commit to a longer hire than actually required. Fixed weekly tariffs that cannot be pro-rated mean a 65-day hire requires payment for 70 days (10 full weeks). Artificially dividing 1/7th of the weekly rate by the number of actual hire days does not reflect the contractual reality.

A top box (delivery container) is standard equipment for a delivery rider's scooter. Where the claimant hired with a top box included, comparators that do not include a top box are incomplete substitutes. Where the claimant is advised to purchase a top box rather than hire one, the defendant cannot demand the claimant bear that capital cost as a condition of mitigating loss — Lagden v O'Connor confirms defendants cannot demand claimants incur unreasonable sacrifices in mitigation.

The fixed tariff methodology used by motorcycle BHR experts (as opposed to dynamic pricing used in car hire) means availability after the event cannot be confirmed. Motorcycle hire companies are 'often unable or unwilling' to provide retrospective availability information. This differs from mainstream car hire where dynamic booking systems provide contemporaneous availability evidence.

Where a motorcycle BHR report is prepared by an expert who has not been provided with (or has not sought) the claimant's driving licence history, age, insurance status, or vehicle use purpose, the comparators selected may be entirely unavailable to that specific claimant. A BHR report that fails to verify the claimant's eligibility for each comparator company carries limited evidential weight.

cases.name: Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management Ltd
cases.relevance: The benchmark test for BHR comparators: the defendant must provide evidence of rates from a mainstream national supplier or, if none is available, a local reputable supplier in the claimant's area. Motorcycle hire companies that are London-centric, delivery-focused, independently owned operations will struggle to meet either limb of this test for claimants outside their geographic footprint.
cases.key_principle: BHR evidence must come from mainstream or reputable local supplier in claimant's area.

cases.name: Lagden v O'Connor
cases.relevance: Defendants cannot demand claimants incur a loss, bear a burden, or make unreasonable sacrifices in the mitigation of their damages. Applied in motorcycle BHR cases to: (a) requiring claimants to travel unreasonable distances to collect/return a comparator scooter; (b) requiring claimants to commit to minimum hire periods beyond their needs; (c) requiring claimants to purchase their own insurance cover or top boxes rather than having these included.
cases.key_principle: Mitigation cannot require the claimant to incur unreasonable burdens or sacrifices.

cases.name: McBride v UK Insurance Ltd
cases.relevance: Court of Appeal guidance on the treatment of excess reduction costs in credit hire. Where a CHO provides a zero excess through a CDW product, and the comparators impose irreducible excesses with no ERI product available, the full cost of the CHO's CDW is recoverable. The absence of any ERI market for motorcycles makes this principle particularly powerful in motorcycle cases.
cases.key_principle: Excess reduction costs form part of the recoverable hire cost where the CHO provides zero-excess cover.

About_Town.notes: Does not permit fast-food delivery use — SDP only. Imposes irreducible excess of £600. Offers comprehensive insurance upgrade at £20/day or £45/week. Registered in London. Delivers from Wandsworth (SW18). Not a mainstream national supplier.
eligibility_filters: Must not use for fast-food delivery
Comprehensive insurance upgrade required for zero-excess equivalence

Ben_Scooters.notes: Hires only to fast-food delivery riders. No insurance provided — hirers must supply their own. 4-week minimum hire period. No refund on early return. Not suitable for claimants without comprehensive motor insurance. Not a mainstream national supplier.

Scooterised.notes: Driving licence must have been held for at least 12 months at commencement of hire; minimum age 21. Offers uninsured option (£84/week) or third-party insured option (£145/week). Irreducible excess of £1,000. Registered at 12 Steele Road, Park Royal, London NW10 7AS. Primarily targets delivery and courier riders.

Ugoscooters.notes: Registered office Saffron Walden, Essex (approximately 58 miles from NW10 postcode). Offers third-party insurance including fast-food delivery cover. Irreducible excess of £500. Delivery available at unspecified/unlisted cost. Top box not included in standard rate. Not a mainstream national supplier and not geographically local to London claimants.

articles.content: Motorbike and scooter credit hire BHR challenges. Motorcycle credit hire BHR: ABI-GTA Category B2 and comparator challenges. Motorcycle hire is a niche sector fundamentally different from car hire. The primary market for UK motorcycle hire companies is delivery and courier riders, not accident replacement customers. The ABI-GTA Category B2 covers motorcycles and scooters. The Stevens mainstream/reputable local supplier test applies. Most motorcycle comparators cited in BHR reports are London-based, independently owned operations that fail the Stevens test for claimants outside London. The most critical comparability issue is insurance. CHOs such as McAMS provide comprehensive insurance with zero excess through a CDW product. Comparators About Town, Scooterised, and Ugoscooters offer only third-party insurance with irreducible excesses (£600, £1,000, and £500 respectively); Ben Scooters provides no insurance at all. No Excess Reimbursement Insurance product exists for motorcycle hirers — acknowledged by WhichRate in their own evidence. This makes all these comparators structurally incomparable to a zero-excess comprehensive insurance product. Eligibility filters: About Town prohibits fast-food delivery use; Scooterised requires 12 months' licence; Ben Scooters requires hirers to be fast-food delivery riders and imposes a 4-week minimum; Ugoscooters is registered 58 miles from the NW10 area. Rolling hire period calculation: fixed weekly tariffs mean a 65-day hire requires payment for 70 days (10 weeks), not 65 days divided by 7. Artificial 1/7th calculations do not reflect the contractual reality of fixed weekly tariffs. Under Lagden v O'Connor, defendants cannot require claimants to make unreasonable sacrifices in mitigation, including travelling unreasonable distances, providing their own insurance cover, or committing to minimum hire periods beyond their actual needs. Under McBride v UK Insurance, the full CDW cost from the CHO is recoverable where comparators impose irreducible excesses and no ERI market exists.

articles.title: BHR Expert Evidence: Automated Rate Collection Systems, Broker Comparators, Rolling Rate Calculations, and Excess Equivalence
articles.source: BHR expert reports and rebuttal statements in car and motorcycle credit hire cases (2024–2025)
articles.author: credit-hire.ai synthesis
principles: Automated rate capture systems used by some BHR experts (software bots that visit predetermined rental company websites and capture screen-prints) raise a specific evidential challenge: the date on which the website was visited is stored in the hypertext URL metadata but is not always shown on the captured screen-print. Where this date information is omitted from the exhibited screen-prints, the court cannot confirm when the enquiries were made — which matters because rental prices are dynamic and vary significantly with booking lead time.

Dynamic pricing in mainstream car rental means that earlier bookings almost always attract lower rates. An accident replacement claimant who commences hire within days of the accident could not have benefitted from advance booking rates — the comparator rates in a BHR report that were captured at some earlier date (rather than at the time the claimant needed a vehicle) may not represent what was actually available to that claimant. The screen-print capture date is therefore a critical piece of evidence in any BHR challenge.

Automated bot-scraping of rental company websites may violate the terms of use of those websites. This does not necessarily render the data inadmissible, but it is relevant to the weight to be given to automated database evidence compared with manually conducted, real-time searches. It may also be relevant to the reliability of the data captured.

Nationwide Hire is an online broker with no own fleet. It 'works with an extensive network of reliable suppliers' whose identity and terms it does not disclose. It is neither a mainstream supplier nor a local reputable supplier under Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management Ltd [2015] EWCA Civ 93. Under Putta v Royal Sun Alliance Insurance Plc [2020] EWHC 117, online brokers that merely introduce customers to deals negotiated with undisclosed third-party suppliers cannot satisfy the Stevens test. The absence of a physical location means distance from the claimant cannot be assessed — which is itself a symptom of its non-local character.

Geographic accessibility of BHR comparators is a mandatory element of the Stevens test: the comparator must be available in the claimant's geographical area. Where BHR evidence cites airport-branch rental companies (e.g. London Heathrow, 24.5 miles; London Gatwick, 29 miles from the claimant), and those companies do not offer delivery and collection, the claimant would be required to make a return journey of 49-58 miles to collect the vehicle and again to return it. Under Lagden v O'Connor [2003] UKHL 64, defendants cannot demand claimants incur unreasonable burdens in mitigation — a 50+ mile round trip to collect a hire car is not a reasonable expectation.

Payment method compatibility is a distinct eligibility filter separate from the impecuniosity or deposit question. Europcar's prepayment terms require the same credit card used for the online booking to be presented at the rental counter to cover potential additional charges. Where a claimant did not possess a credit card at the time of commencement of hire, hiring from Europcar was not available to them regardless of the funds they held in a bank account. Bunting v Zurich Insurance Plc [2020] EWHC 1807 is distinguished on this point because the card requirement goes beyond the deposit question.

Rolling 7-day rate calculations create a structural distortion in BHR reports. Where a BHR expert calculates the total cost of hire by multiplying 1/7th of the 7-day rate by the number of actual hire days, this methodology is only valid if the comparator company permits rolling 7-day rentals without restriction. In practice, most mainstream rental companies: (a) do not guarantee the vehicle will be available for rolling extensions; (b) charge extension days at 'pay at location' rates (approximately 10% higher than the pre-booked online rate); (c) require the hirer to return to the branch to sign a new agreement if the hire exceeds 28 days; and (d) do not refund unused days if the hire terminates early. None of these realities are reflected in the 1/7th calculation.

The 'no refund on early return' policy operated by most mainstream rental companies (Budget, Europcar, Nationwide Hire) creates a specific problem for accident replacement claimants: because the duration of hire is unknown at the outset, a claimant who hires on a pre-booked 7-day rate and terminates early will not receive a refund for unused days. This means the 1/7th calculation can only work if the claimant commits to a new 7-day booking each time — but those extensions are not guaranteed at the pre-booked rate and may not be available at all.

Budget's rental extension terms expressly state that extensions 'cannot be guaranteed' and that 'another customer may have already booked the vehicle'. Extension days are charged at 'pay at location' prices, approximately 10% above the online pre-booked rate. For a 40-day hire (or 65-day hire in motorcycle cases), this means the total cost calculated by multiplying 1/7th of the 7-day rate by the actual days is demonstrably not the cost the claimant would actually have incurred. A BHR report that does not account for these extension conditions is incomplete and likely understates the true comparator cost.

Where a hirer requires more than 28 days at Budget, the terms require them to return to the branch to sign a new rental agreement and possibly change vehicle. For a claimant at a branch 24.5 miles away without a delivery and collection service, this is not a practical option. The cumulative effect of geographic remoteness, extension uncertainty, and branch attendance requirements makes Budget an unrealistic comparator for many accident replacement scenarios.

Excess comparability must be assessed on a like-for-like basis. Where a claimant hired from a CHO with a zero excess obtained through a Collision Damage and Theft Waiver, the BHR comparator must offer equivalent excess protection. Where the comparator's excess cannot be reduced to zero (e.g. Europcar's £2,500 excess is irreducible), the comparator is not equivalent on insurance terms regardless of the daily rental rate. Stand-alone excess reimbursement insurance (ERI) from Questor, Insurance4CarHire, CoverForYou, or ICar Hire Insurance may be proposed as a substitute — but this analysis is only necessary where the comparator's CDW reduces the excess to zero first; where it does not, ERI merely recoups the excess after payment, which is a different and inferior product.

Questor Insurance's policy wording provides that 'this insurance policy in its entirety is invalid if the dates on the hire agreement and the dates on the certificate of insurance do not match'. In accident replacement scenarios where the hire period is unknown at the outset, a claimant cannot reliably specify the exact hire end date when purchasing ERI — making Questor cover (and similar products) impracticable for accident hire regardless of their daily cost. This argument applies equally in car and motorcycle cases.

The distinction between a standalone ERI product and a hire company CDW is substantive: a CDW from the hire company eliminates the excess contractually — the hirer never pays the excess. An ERI product from a third party requires the hirer to first pay the excess to the rental company in the event of damage, and then claim reimbursement from the ERI insurer, which may or may not meet the claim. These are not equivalent products and the court should not treat ERI as a perfect substitute for CDW when comparing hire terms.

Where a BHR expert includes Nationwide Hire as a comparator alongside mainstream companies, the inclusion distorts the BHR comparison. Nationwide's online broker pricing reflects undisclosed supplier terms negotiated at a particular moment in supply and demand — not a transparent, independently verifiable retail rate from a named supplier. Including it alongside Budget and Europcar as if it were equivalent misleads the court about the true range of available rates.

cases.name: Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management Ltd
cases.relevance: The foundational test for BHR comparators: rates from a mainstream national supplier or, if none, a local reputable supplier in the claimant's geographic area. Nationwide Hire fails both limbs — it is not a mainstream supplier (no fleet, no branches) and has no geographic location. Airport-branch comparators at 24-29 miles without delivery/collection may fail the geographical limb. Methodology: if the BHR report does not assess whether each comparator actually satisfies the Stevens criteria, the evidence is incomplete.
cases.key_principle: BHR comparators must satisfy mainstream or local reputable supplier test and be in claimant's area.

cases.name: Putta v Royal Sun Alliance Insurance Plc
cases.relevance: High Court guidance on the treatment of online brokers in BHR evidence. A broker that merely introduces consumers to deals from undisclosed suppliers, with rates dependent on supply and demand at any particular time, is not an appropriate comparator — it does not represent a stable, transparent rental market rate. Nationwide Hire falls squarely within this analysis.
cases.key_principle: Online brokers with no own fleet are not appropriate BHR comparators under Stevens.

cases.name: Lagden v O'Connor
cases.relevance: Defendants cannot demand claimants incur unreasonable losses, burdens, or sacrifices in mitigation. Applied to: requiring claimants to travel 25-30 miles each way to collect a hire vehicle from a company that does not deliver; requiring claimants to return to a branch 28 days into hire to sign a new agreement; requiring claimants to purchase their own insurance where the comparator provides none; requiring claimants to commit to minimum hire periods at risk of irrecoverable charges.
cases.key_principle: Mitigation duty does not require claimants to bear unreasonable burdens.

cases.name: McBride v UK Insurance Ltd
cases.relevance: Court of Appeal authority on the recoverability of excess reduction costs in credit hire. Where a claimant hired with zero excess through a CHO's CDW product, and comparator companies impose irreducible excesses (or excesses only reducible through impracticable ERI products), the full CDW cost from the CHO is a recoverable element of the hire charge.
cases.key_principle: CDW/excess reduction costs are recoverable where comparators cannot match the CHO's zero-excess terms.

cases.name: Bunting v Zurich Insurance Plc
cases.relevance: Bunting concerned deposit requirements and was distinguished in cases where the obstacle to hiring from a comparator company was not a deposit but a credit card requirement. A claimant without a credit card cannot hire from Europcar regardless of available funds — the credit card is required to be presented at the counter, not merely to make a payment, and this goes beyond the deposit/impecuniosity question.
cases.key_principle: Credit card counter-presentation requirements are distinct from deposit questions — Bunting does not resolve them.

methodology_challenges.automated_database_capture: The date of website capture is embedded in the hypertext URL but often excluded from exhibited screen-prints. Courts cannot verify when comparator rates were obtained. Rates captured in advance of the accident date benefit from advance-booking discounts unavailable to an accident replacement claimant who must hire at short notice.
methodology_challenges.predetermined_supplier_list: Automated BHR systems query a predetermined, fixed list of rental company websites. They do not replicate what a claimant would discover in a real-time manual internet search from their location. The results are not a reflection of market reality as the claimant would have experienced it.
methodology_challenges.nationwide_hire_broker_status: Nationwide Hire has no fleet, no branches, and no fixed location. Its rates reflect undisclosed supplier arrangements that vary with supply and demand. Its inclusion in BHR evidence alongside named mainstream suppliers misrepresents the comparator landscape.
methodology_challenges.one_seventh_calculation: Dividing 1/7th of the 7-day rate by the number of actual hire days only works if: the hire can be extended on rolling 7-day terms at the same rate; availability is guaranteed; no branch attendance is required for extensions; and early return results in a pro-rated refund. None of these conditions are satisfied by Budget, Europcar, or Nationwide Hire as a matter of their own terms and conditions.
methodology_challenges.excess_and_eri: Where a hire company cannot reduce its excess to zero through its own CDW product, a stand-alone ERI policy is not an equivalent substitute. ERI requires the hirer to pay the excess first and then claim back — a process that is also invalidated if the hire end date is not known at the time of purchasing the ERI policy.

articles.content: BHR expert evidence methodology — applies to car, motorbike and scooter credit hire cases. BHR expert evidence methodology: challenges to automated rate capture, broker comparators, rolling rate calculations, and excess equivalence. Two established methodologies are used by BHR expert companies: (1) automated systems that pre-populate databases by bot-scraping predetermined rental company websites at set intervals; (2) manual searches conducted contemporaneously to the hire period. Key challenges to automated database methodology: the screen-print capture date (embedded in the URL but often omitted from exhibits) cannot be confirmed; advance-booking rates captured before the accident are not available to an accident replacement claimant; automated bots may violate rental company website terms of use. Nationwide Hire is an online broker with no fleet and no branches. Under Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management Ltd [2015] EWCA Civ 93 and Putta v Royal Sun Alliance [2020] EWHC 117, it is neither a mainstream supplier nor a local reputable supplier. Its inclusion in BHR evidence alongside Budget and Europcar misrepresents the comparator landscape. Geographic accessibility: BHR evidence citing airport-branch comparators at 24-29 miles without delivery/collection requires the claimant to make 50+ mile round trips. Under Lagden v O'Connor [2003] UKHL 64, defendants cannot demand such unreasonable mitigation burdens. Credit card requirements at Europcar are distinct from the deposit/impecuniosity question in Bunting v Zurich [2020] — a claimant without a credit card cannot hire from Europcar regardless of available funds. Rolling 7-day calculations (1/7th of weekly rate × actual days) do not reflect the contractual reality: Budget does not guarantee extensions, charges extension days at 'pay at location' rates (~10% higher), requires branch attendance at 28 days, and imposes no-refund on early return. Europcar and Nationwide similarly impose no-refund early return policies. A 40-day hire using the 7-day rate requires payment for 42 days. Excess equivalence: Europcar's £2,500 excess is irreducible; Nationwide's £1,200 reduces only to £600. Where the claimant hired with zero excess through a CHO CDW, these comparators are not equivalent. Questor ERI is impracticable for accident hire as its validity requires matching hire agreement and certificate dates — unknown at the outset of accident replacement hire. Under McBride v UK Insurance [2017], the full CHO CDW cost is recoverable where comparators cannot match zero-excess terms.

articles.title: Calderbank Offers: Without Prejudice Save as to Costs — Principles, Procedure and Costs Consequences
articles.source: Lexlaw Solicitors (lexlaw.co.uk); Nicholas Dobbs, Temple Garden Chambers (PI Brief Update); Keoghs Solicitors; Lamb Chambers
articles.author: Lexlaw
tags: without prejudice save as to costs
principles: A Calderbank offer is a settlement proposal marked "without prejudice save as to costs", meaning it cannot be referred to on liability but can be placed before the court when determining who pays costs.

The principle originates from Calderbank v Calderbank [1975] 3 All ER 333 (EWCA), where the Court of Appeal held that a party refusing a reasonable settlement offer can bear the costs consequences of that refusal.

If a party rejects a Calderbank offer and later obtains a judgment less favourable than the offer, the court may order that party to pay the offeror's costs from the date of the offer — but this is a matter of discretion, not an automatic consequence.

Unlike Part 36, Calderbank offers carry NO automatic costs consequences. CPR 44.2 and CPR 36 are entirely separate regimes: Part 36 gives rigid, automatic consequences; CPR 44.2 gives the court a broad discretion to consider all circumstances including Calderbank offers (confirmed in Coward v Phaestos [2014] EWCA Civ 1536).

CPR 44.2 is the governing framework for Calderbank offers. The court must consider whether a party has succeeded on part of its case, the conduct of the parties, and whether a party has exaggerated its claim. A Calderbank offer is one circumstance the court will weigh.

In split proceedings — where liability is determined first and quantum separately, as is common in credit hire litigation — a global Calderbank offer does not automatically govern costs on both stages. The court retains broad discretion under CPR 44.2 at each stage ([2021] EWCA Civ 1792).

A Calderbank offer can be withdrawn at any time before a costs decision, unlike a Part 36 offer, giving the offeror greater flexibility. (By contrast, a Part 36 offer can only be withdrawn within its 21-day acceptance period if the offeror can show a sufficient change of circumstances — Evans v Royal Wolverhampton.)
There is no fixed timeframe for the validity of a Calderbank offer — it remains open until withdrawn or until the court rules on costs.
A counteroffer to a Calderbank offer nullifies the original proposal; engaging in reasonable counter-negotiation is advisable when declining the offer.
The party rejecting a Calderbank offer bears the burden of demonstrating why the offer was unreasonable if the court later scrutinises the costs position.

Calderbank offers are particularly useful where: the Part 36 regime is inapplicable (e.g. appeals, non-monetary claims, costs-only proceedings); split liability/quantum proceedings make a global Part 36 offer impractical; or where flexibility over timing and withdrawal is needed.

In credit hire litigation, a defendant insurer making a Calderbank offer puts the CHO or claimant solicitor on notice that continued litigation at inflated rates risks a costs penalty under CPR 44.2 if the court awards less than the offer figure. The court will consider whether the claimant acted reasonably in refusing the offer.

Practitioners should carefully evaluate whether to use a Calderbank offer or a Part 36 offer: Calderbank provides flexibility and discretion; Part 36 provides certainty and automatic cost consequences but is subject to strict procedural rules. Both have a strategic role in credit hire claims management.
cases: [2021] EWCA Civ 1792 (split proceedings, CPR 44.2 and Calderbank offers)
Evans v Royal Wolverhampton Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust (Part 36 withdrawal principles)
articles.content: A Calderbank offer is a without prejudice save as to costs settlement proposal used in credit hire and other civil litigation. The principle derives from Calderbank v Calderbank [1975] 3 All ER 333 (EWCA), where the Court of Appeal established that a party refusing a reasonable pre-trial settlement offer can bear adverse costs consequences if the court outcome is less favourable than the offer. The offer is protected from disclosure during liability proceedings but can be placed before the judge at the costs stage. The key governing provision is CPR 44.2, which gives the court a broad discretion over costs. Unlike Part 36, Calderbank offers carry no automatic consequences — CPR 36 and CPR 44 are entirely separate regimes for entirely separate purposes, as the Court of Appeal confirmed in Coward v Phaestos [2014] EWCA Civ 1536. The court exercises its discretion having regard to all the circumstances, including whether a party has succeeded on part of its case, the conduct of the parties, and whether a claim has been exaggerated. In split proceedings — where liability is resolved first and quantum subsequently, as is commonly the case in credit hire litigation — a global Calderbank offer does not automatically govern both stages. The Court of Appeal confirmed in [2021] EWCA Civ 1792 that CPR 44.2 confers a broad discretion at each stage, and that applying the rigid Part 36 approach to Calderbank offers would reward bad behaviour and encourage unmeritorious points. In credit hire claims, defendant insurers commonly use Calderbank offers to put claimants and their solicitors on risk: if the court awards less than the offer figure — whether on quantum, hire rate, duration or impecuniosity — the defendant can apply under CPR 44.2 for costs from the date of offer, and potentially on the indemnity basis where the claimant acted unreasonably in refusing. Key features of a valid Calderbank offer include: marking it without prejudice save as to costs; clarity of terms and quantum; a reasonable timeframe for response; reasons why acceptance is justified; and a statement that the offer will be relied upon at the costs hearing if rejected. Calderbank offers can be withdrawn at any time before a costs decision — unlike Part 36 offers which require a sufficient change of circumstances to be withdrawn within the 21-day period (Evans v Royal Wolverhampton). Practitioners must weigh the strategic choice: Calderbank gives flexibility and discretion; Part 36 gives certainty with automatic costs consequences but is subject to strict procedural rules. Both have an important role in credit hire claims management.

articles.title: Fleet Vehicle Loss of Use: Armstead Misapplied — ABI GTA Rate Not Evidence of Loss (Enterprise Rent A Car v Advantage Insurance)
articles.source: Keoghs (keoghs.co.uk)
articles.author: Gary Herring / Luke Matthews / Ieuan Poole, Keoghs
principles: Armstead v Royal & Sun Alliance [2024] UKSC 6 does NOT apply to fleet operators claiming loss of use at the ABI GTA credit hire rate. Armstead concerned a pre-existing credit hire contract (Auxillis) where the loss arose from a contract entered into before the incident. It did not invert the burden of proof or create a licence to claim any unsubstantiated sum applied after the fact.
The burden remains on the claimant to first prove their loss. Armstead did not shift the burden of proof onto defendants to disprove a claimant's claimed rate.

For fleet operators, the applicable law is West Midlands Travel v Aviva Insurance UK Ltd [2013] EWCA Civ 887, not Armstead. A fleet operator must evidence loss of use and demonstrate they could not have sufficed from their available fleet.
Reliance on vehicle availability at a single branch is an impermissibly narrow approach. The overall fleet should be considered, as vehicles are commonly redeployed between branches as need arises.

The ABI GTA credit hire rate is not evidence of loss of use for a fleet provider. It reflects credit hire charges including overheads, operational costs, provision of credit and profit — not the net loss suffered by a fleet operator whose vehicle is off the road.

The correct measure of loss for a fleet operator where no special damage is evidenced is interest on capital and depreciation on the value of the vehicle (West Midlands Travel v Aviva; Beechwood Birmingham v Hoyer Group UK Ltd [2010] EWCA Civ 647).

If a fleet operator refuses to provide the vehicle's value (necessary for calculating depreciation and interest on capital), and the court finds that is the correct measure, the claim will be dismissed for want of evidence — as happened in Enterprise Rent A Car UK Ltd v Advantage Insurance Company Limited.

Enterprise's own website advertised approximately 150,000 vehicles in the UK, fundamentally undermining its reliance on a single branch spreadsheet showing only 168 vehicles at approximately 86% utilisation.
cases: Enterprise Rent A Car UK Ltd v Advantage Insurance Company Limited (2024, first instance)
Armstead v Royal & Sun Alliance [2024] UKSC 6
West Midlands Travel v Aviva Insurance UK Ltd [2013] EWCA Civ 887
Beechwood Birmingham v Hoyer Group UK Ltd [2010] EWCA Civ 647
articles.content: In Enterprise Rent A Car UK Ltd v Advantage Insurance Company Limited, Enterprise sought to recover loss of use of a fleet vehicle (Nissan Qashqai, 34 days) at the ABI GTA credit hire rate rather than evidencing actual loss of use or loss of profit. Enterprise argued Armstead v Royal & Sun Alliance [2024] UKSC 6 applied and that the defendant bore the burden of disproving the rate claimed. The court rejected this misapplication. Armstead concerned a pre-existing credit hire contract where Auxillis's loss arose from a contractual arrangement entered into before the incident — it established that such a contractual loss is a reasonable pre-estimate recoverable as damages. It did not create a general licence for any claimant to claim an unsubstantiated sum, nor did it invert the burden of proof. The correct law for fleet operators is West Midlands Travel v Aviva Insurance UK Ltd [2013] EWCA Civ 887: a fleet operator must evidence their loss of use and show they could not have sufficed from their available fleet. Reliance on a single branch's availability is impermissibly narrow — the entire fleet must be considered, and vehicles are routinely redeployed between branches. The ABI GTA rate is agreed between subscribing insurers and CHOs within the GTA framework; it represents hire charges including credit provision and profit, not the net loss suffered by a fleet operator. The correct measure where loss of use cannot be established from fleet availability is depreciation and interest on the capital value of the damaged vehicle (Beechwood Birmingham v Hoyer Group [2010] EWCA Civ 647). Enterprise refused to provide the vehicle's value despite requests. Once the court found that depreciation/interest was the correct measure, it had no evidence on which to assess the sum, and dismissed the claim in its entirety. The defendant's case was assisted by Enterprise's own website advertising approximately 150,000 UK fleet vehicles, contrasting sharply with the 168-vehicle branch spreadsheet produced at trial.

articles.title: ABI GTA Commercial Vehicle Groups and Maximum Daily Rates — Effective 1 July 2025
articles.source: ABI GTA Commercial Vehicle Grouping and Rates Schedule, effective 1 July 2025–30 June 2026
articles.author: ABI / GTA
principles: The ABI GTA specifies maximum daily rates for commercial vehicle groups. CHOs may charge any rate up to but not exceeding the maximum. All rates exclude VAT. Effective 1 July 2025 to 30 June 2026.
Where no GTA group exists for a commercial vehicle (e.g. hearse, refrigerated vehicle, car transporter), the CHO should agree a rate with the insurer before hire commences.

PANEL VANS: PV1 £47.50/day (small vans: Ford Transit Connect, VW Caddy, Renault Kangoo, Mercedes Vito Compact, Peugeot Expert, Toyota Hiace SWB, Citroen Berlingo); PV2 £55.13/day (SWB standard roof: Ford Transit SWB Low Roof, Renault Traffic SWB, Vauxhall Vivaro SWB, Toyota Hiace LWB, Peugeot Boxer SWB); PV3 £55.13/day (MWB low/standard roof: Ford Transit MWB Low Roof, Ford Transit SWB Medium Roof, Renault Traffic LWB High Roof, Vauxhall Vivaro LWB High Roof); PV4 £61.14/day (LWB medium roof: Ford Transit LWB Medium Roof, Mercedes Sprinter SWB, Peugeot Boxer LWB High Roof, Renault Master SWB); PV5 £64.22/day (LWB high roof: Ford Transit LWB High Roof, Mercedes Sprinter MWB, Renault Master LWB, Iveco Daily Class C High Roof); PV6 £67.31/day (jumbo/extra-large: Ford Transit Jumbo, Mercedes Sprinter LWB High Roof, Vauxhall Movano Maxi Roof, Renault Master LWB High Roof).

CHASSIS-BASED VEHICLES: CV1 £69.92/day (SWB/MWB: Ford Transit LWB/MWB/XLWB Dropside, VW Transporter Crew Cab); CV2 £70.50/day (LWB/EXLWB: Ford Transit LWB Box, Double Cab, XLWB Double Cab); CV3 £74.12/day (Ford Transit LWB 3-way Tipper, XLWB Luton); CV4 £105.59/day (heavy: Iveco 7.5 Tonne Tilt and Slide, Iveco Eurocargo 75E — Box, Curtain-Sider, Dropside, Luton, Tipper).

PICKUP TRUCKS: CP1 £64.64/day (single cab: Ford Ranger, Mitsubishi L200 Single Cab, Toyota Hilux Single Cab); CP2 £73.34/day (extended/double cab: Ford Ranger Double Cab, Isuzu Rodeo Double Cab, Land Rover Defender 90, Nissan Navara, Toyota Hilux Extended Cab); CP3 £83.50/day (heavy double cab: Land Rover Defender 110 variants, Mitsubishi Barbarian/Warrior Double Cab, Nissan Navara Double Cab, Toyota Hi-Lux 270 Double Cab).

COMMERCIAL 4x4/SUV: CS1 £75.87/day (Land Rover Defender 90 Hard Top, Nissan Terrano II, Santana PS10); CS2 £81.14/day (Defender 110 Hard Top, Defender 90 Station Wagon, Freelander Commercial, Nissan Terrano II 3.0); CS3 £87.14/day (Defender 110 Station Wagon, County 110 Hard Top); CS4 £92.39/day (Defender 110 County Station Wagon, Defender 90 XS, Mitsubishi Shogun SWB); CS5 £98.39/day (Defender 110 XS Station Wagon, Mitsubishi Shogun LWB).
MINIBUSES: CM1 £84.04/day (Ford Transit 12-seat Minibus); CM2 £97.25/day (15-seat Minibus); CM3 £110.15/day (17-seat Minibus).

REFRIGERATED VEHICLES: RV1 £91.80/day (Ford Escort 75 Chilled 1.9); RV2 £126.98/day (Ford Transit Luton Chilled, Ford Transit Chilled, Mercedes Sprinter Chilled). These represent specialist vehicles — if no group applies, rate must be agreed with the insurer in advance.
Commercial vehicle groups are entirely separate from the ACRISS passenger car groups used in standard BHR analysis for car hire claims.
articles.content: The ABI GTA commercial vehicle rate schedule (effective 1 July 2025 to 30 June 2026) sets maximum daily credit hire rates across six category types, all excluding VAT. Panel Vans: PV1 £47.50/day (small compact vans), PV2/PV3 £55.13/day (SWB and MWB standard roof), PV4 £61.14/day (LWB medium roof), PV5 £64.22/day (LWB high roof), PV6 £67.31/day (jumbo). Chassis-based: CV1 £69.92, CV2 £70.50, CV3 £74.12, CV4 £105.59/day (Iveco Eurocargo 75E heavy range). Pickups: CP1 £64.64 (single cab), CP2 £73.34 (double cab), CP3 £83.50/day (heavy double cab). Commercial 4x4: CS1-CS5 from £75.87 to £98.39/day (predominantly Land Rover Defender variants). Minibuses: CM1-CM3 from £84.04 to £110.15/day. Refrigerated: RV1 £91.80, RV2 £126.98/day. Where no group applies, the CHO must agree a rate with the insurer before hire. These groups are separate from ACRISS passenger car groups used in standard BHR analysis.

articles.title: ABI GTA Motorcycle and Scooter Groups and Maximum Daily Rates — Effective 1 July 2025
articles.subcategory: GTA Rate Reference — Motorcycles and Scooters
articles.source: ABI GTA Motorcycle Grouping and Rates Schedule, effective 1 July 2025–30 June 2026
articles.author: ABI / GTA
principles: The ABI GTA specifies maximum daily rates for motorcycle and scooter hire by engine capacity (cc). All rates exclude VAT. Rates apply 1 July 2025 to 30 June 2026. Groups are B1 to B6.
B1: 0-50cc — £45.89/day (mopeds and small scooters).

B2: 51-125cc — £53.60/day (learner-legal motorcycles and scooters, e.g. Yamaha NMax 125, Honda CB125R). This is the most commonly contested group in credit hire claims involving delivery riders and commuter scooters.
B3: 126-500cc — £75.58/day (mid-range motorcycles).
B4: 501-650cc — £91.01/day (mid-to-large motorcycles).
B5: 651-1000cc — £106.34/day (large motorcycles).
B6: 1001cc and above — £107.97/day (superbikes and large-capacity motorcycles).

Top boxes and panniers: under GTA Section 5.4, a flat rate of £35 plus VAT per hire can be charged for top boxes and/or panniers where need AND regular use can be demonstrated — for example couriers, delivery riders, and commuters using storage.

Motorcycle GTA groups are not relevant to ACRISS passenger car BHR comparisons. In motorcycle BHR disputes the relevant B-group rate provides the maximum benchmark; comparator evidence must come from motorcycle hire companies offering like-for-like cc-bracket vehicles.

In B2 group cases involving delivery riders, common defendant challenges include: comparator companies prohibiting food delivery use, requiring minimum 12-month licence periods, being geographically impractical, or having no insurance provision. These must be weighed against the GTA B2 rate as the primary benchmark.
Unlike car hire groups where ACRISS codes establish vehicle comparability, motorcycle grouping is purely by engine capacity. A like-for-like replacement should fall within the same cc bracket.
articles.content: The ABI GTA motorcycle rate schedule (effective 1 July 2025 to 30 June 2026) classifies hire motorcycles and scooters into six groups by engine capacity. B1 (0-50cc): £45.89/day for mopeds and small scooters. B2 (51-125cc): £53.60/day for learner-legal machines including commuter scooters and delivery bikes. B3 (126-500cc): £75.58/day. B4 (501-650cc): £91.01/day. B5 (651-1000cc): £106.34/day. B6 (1001cc+): £107.97/day for superbikes. All rates exclude VAT. A GTA Section 5.4 provision allows a £35 plus VAT per hire flat fee for top boxes and panniers where need and regular use is shown, such as for couriers and delivery riders. The motorcycle groups are entirely separate from the ACRISS car system. In BHR disputes involving motorcycles, the B-group rate provides the maximum GTA benchmark and comparators must offer like-for-like cc-bracket machines. The B2 group is the most frequently contested in credit hire litigation, particularly where the claimant is a food delivery rider, as many comparator companies impose restrictions on commercial use.

articles.title: ABI GTA Taxi and Licensed Hire Vehicle Groups and Maximum Daily Rates — Effective 1 July 2025
articles.subcategory: GTA Rate Reference — Taxis and Licensed Hire Vehicles
articles.source: ABI GTA Taxi and Licensed Hire Vehicle Grouping and Rates Schedule, effective 1 July 2025–30 June 2026
articles.author: ABI / GTA
principles: The ABI GTA sets maximum daily rates for taxis and licensed hire vehicles grouped by registration date, vehicle age, vehicle type and whether insurance is included. All rates exclude VAT. Effective 1 July 2025 to 30 June 2026.

It is the age of the HIRED vehicle (not the claimant damaged vehicle) that determines the applicable rate group. No betterment argument applies — if the claimant vehicle is 5 years old but the hire vehicle is 1 year old, the younger hire vehicle rate applies.

SALOON/HATCHBACK TAXIS registered before 31 March 2011 (T1-T4, engine size matters): Under 3 years old: T1 (up to 1.7L excl insurance) £86.04/day; T2 (up to 1.7L incl insurance) £114.71/day; T3 (from 1.7L excl insurance) £107.53/day; T4 (from 1.7L incl insurance) £136.24/day. 3 years or older: T1 £58.56/day; T2 £80.29/day; T3 £71.69/day; T4 £94.63/day.

SALOON/HATCHBACK TAXIS registered on or after 1 April 2011 (NT3/NT4, engine size irrelevant, CHO must provide hire vehicle reg): Under 3 years old: NT3 (excl insurance) £107.53/day; NT4 (incl insurance) £142.88/day. 3 years or older: NT3 £71.69/day; NT4 £99.24/day.

PUBLIC HIRE/HACKNEY CARRIAGES (T5-T8, by vehicle age): Under 4 years old: T5 City of London excl insurance £97.51/day; T6 City of London incl insurance £131.91/day; T7 Public Hire/Hackney excl insurance £111.85/day; T8 incl insurance £146.24/day. 4 years or older: T5 £68.81/day; T6 £97.51/day; T7 £78.99/day; T8 £107.18/day.

SPECIALIST TAXI GROUPS (under 3 years old): PT9 prestige up to P6 (VW Tiguan, Mercedes Viano 7-seat, Mercedes V250 D AMG) £250.07/day; T10 people carrier 6/7 seats (Galaxy, Sharan, Alhambra, Vauxhall Zafira Life, LEVC TX5 hybrid) £188.21/day; T12 minibus 8/9 seats (Tourneo, Transporter, Mercedes Vito Taxi) £237.47/day; PT13 prestige P7 and above £291.88/day; T14 Taxi Bike £103.22/day. 3 years or older: PT9 £187.58; T10 £138.00; T12 £169.65; PT13 £215.09; T14 £74.56/day.

PERMITTED UPLIFTS (taxi groups only): +£5/day for automatic transmission where matching the claimant vehicle (semi-shift/DSG qualifies); +£5/day for estate body where matching the claimant vehicle; +£5/day for necessary extras such as tow bars and baby seats. No uplift for signage, radio hire, taxi display equipment.

PT9 and PT13 cross-reference the passenger car prestige group classification, linking the taxi rate schedule to ACRISS-based prestige groups for specialist vehicles. PT9 covers up to car group P6; PT13 covers P7 and above.
articles.content: The ABI GTA taxi rate schedule (effective 1 July 2025 to 30 June 2026) operates across multiple group series depending on registration date, vehicle age, vehicle type, and insurance inclusion. All rates exclude VAT. The rate group is determined by the age of the HIRED vehicle, not the claimant vehicle — no betterment argument applies. Pre-April 2011 taxis use T1-T4 (engine size above/below 1.7L and insurance inclusion); under 3 years rates run £86.04-£136.24/day, 3 years or older £58.56-£94.63/day. Post-March 2011 taxis use NT3/NT4 (engine size irrelevant; CHO must provide hire vehicle registration); under 3 years £107.53-£142.88/day, 3 years or older £71.69-£99.24/day. Black cabs and hackney carriages use T5-T8, with City of London vehicles separately rated; under 4 years £97.51-£146.24/day, 4 years or older £68.81-£107.18/day. Specialist groups cover prestige taxis (PT9 up to P6, PT13 P7+), people carriers (T10), minibuses (T12), and Taxi Bikes (T14). Permitted uplifts of £5/day apply for automatics, estates, and necessary extras. No uplift for signage or radio.

articles.title: AMT Auto-Rent Terms and Conditions of Hire
articles.source: AMT Auto-Rent Terms and Conditions of Hire (current); AMT Car Rental Pricing Guide (May 2025)
articles.author: AMT Auto-Rent
principles: Company identity: AMT Vehicle Rental Ltd is the legal entity (company number 03713753, registered in England and Wales). Parent company: AMT Global Investments Ltd (No. 05904790). VAT number GB721712857. All companies in the AMT group are subsidiaries of AMT Global Investments Ltd. This is relevant when checking whether a hire agreement was made with the correct legal entity and when examining any CHO's contractual documentation.

AMT Auto-Rent is a BVRLA member (No. 1559) and FCA authorised credit broker (FRN724010), headquartered at Bruntcliffe Lane, Morley, Leeds LS279LR, with additional locations in London, Birmingham and Edinburgh. They hold a 4.8-star rating from 484 reviews and are commonly instructed by insurers as a basic hire rate (BHR) comparator.
Driver age eligibility: drivers must be aged between 25 and 69 years inclusive. Drivers outside this range are ineligible and a hire to such a driver would not be on AMT's standard terms.
Licence requirement: the driver must hold a full UK driving licence for a minimum of 24 months. Provisional licence holders or those with less than two years' driving history are not eligible.

Endorsement restriction: a maximum of 6 penalty points is permitted, and those points must relate to minor convictions only. Any major conviction within the last 5 years renders the driver ineligible. This means a claimant with more than 6 points, or any serious motoring conviction, could not have hired from AMT.

Accident history: no more than 2 accidents in the preceding 3 years; or if any single accident exceeded £5,000 in damage, no more than 1 accident is permitted. A claimant with a poor accident history may fall outside AMT eligibility.

Occupation exclusions: the following occupations are expressly excluded — Non-UK Armed Forces personnel, Entertainers, Sports Professionals, Hawking/General Dealers, Scrap Merchants and Dismantlers, Professional Gamblers, and those in Modelling. Unemployed persons are excluded entirely. Students are restricted to cars only and may not hire vans or other vehicle categories.

Maximum hire period: AMT's standard terms cap hire at 84 days unless a longer period is agreed in writing or by email prior to the commencement of hire. Any hire extending beyond 84 days without prior written agreement would be in breach of AMT's standard conditions.

Mileage fair use policy: AMT imposes mileage caps — 300 miles per day for hires under 7 days; 1,000 miles per week for hires of 7 days or more; and 2,800 miles per 28-day period. Excess mileage beyond these allowances is charged at an additional rate. A claimant with high mileage needs may not be adequately served by AMT's standard product.

Insurance — AMT's own policy (Clause 2): the hirer may take insurance through AMT's own policy. The standard excess applies but can be reduced by payment of an additional charge. The hirer remains liable for the standard excess if they decline the excess reduction option.

Insurance — own fully comprehensive cover (Clause 3): alternatively, the hirer may use their own fully comprehensive motor insurance to cover the hire vehicle. In this case AMT must be satisfied that the hirer's own policy provides adequate cover for the hired vehicle.
Geographic restriction: the hire vehicle may only be used within the United Kingdom. Use outside the UK is prohibited under AMT's standard terms.

Use restrictions: the vehicle must not be used for carrying passengers for hire or reward, racing, any unlawful purpose, or food delivery, courier or taxi services — unless AMT's prior written permission has been obtained. These restrictions mean that delivery drivers, taxi drivers and similar occupations would require express written consent to use an AMT hire vehicle for their work.
Telematics: all AMT hire vehicles are fitted with telematics devices. This means vehicle usage, location and driving behaviour can be monitored throughout the hire period.

Litigation relevance — eligibility as a comparator: where an insurer or BHR expert cites AMT rates as a comparator, the claimant's specific circumstances must be checked against AMT's eligibility criteria. If the claimant falls outside AMT's age range, licence history, endorsement limit, accident history or occupation exclusions, AMT is not a valid comparator for that claimant and the BHR argument based on AMT rates should be challenged.

Litigation relevance — mileage and hire period: if the claimant's hire exceeded 84 days or involved mileage beyond AMT's fair use caps, the AMT product would not have met the claimant's needs. This supports the argument that a spot hire or credit hire with more favourable terms was the only available option.

Litigation relevance — insurance position: AMT's insurance options require either payment of an excess reduction premium or the claimant having fully comprehensive cover on their own policy. Where a claimant lacked comprehensive insurance (common in credit hire scenarios), the additional cost of AMT's excess or the unsuitability of their own insurance product is relevant to the comparison.

Towing restriction: AMT hire vehicles must not be used to tow any other vehicle without AMT's express prior permission. This is relevant where a claimant's vehicle (e.g. a van, 4x4 or pickup) was being used for towing at the time of the accident — if the claimant needed a replacement vehicle capable of towing, AMT's standard terms would not permit this without specific agreement, which undermines AMT as a like-for-like comparator in such cases.

Deposit policy: AMT charges no deposit for standard daily hire where a credit account has been opened. For long-term rental (1 to 6 months), one month's rental is required in advance. This is relevant where a claimant's financial impecuniosity argument is advanced — the absence of a deposit requirement for short-term daily hire weakens any argument that the claimant could not have used a spot hire company because of upfront deposit costs.

Bespoke pricing: AMT offers discounted rates for business account holders and regular users, with greater discounts available the more frequently a customer rents. Where an insurer's BHR expert relies on AMT's standard published rates as the comparator, it should be noted that AMT's own pricing guide confirms these are not necessarily the rates available to all customers — regular hirers or account holders may access lower rates, and conversely, a one-off hirer following an accident would be on the standard rate with no negotiated discount.
articles.content:
AMT Auto-Rent is a well-established vehicle hire company frequently cited by insurer-appointed BHR experts as a comparator for credit hire rates. They are a BVRLA member (No. 1559), FCA authorised (FRN724010), and operate from Leeds, London, Birmingham and Edinburgh.

Their terms of hire impose significant eligibility restrictions that must be checked whenever AMT is used as a BHR comparator.

DRIVER ELIGIBILITY: Drivers must be aged 25-69 with a full UK licence held for at least 24 months. No more than 6 minor penalty points are permitted; any major conviction in the last 5 years disqualifies the driver. Accident history is capped at 2 incidents in 3 years, or 1 if any incident exceeded £5,000. Excluded occupations include: non-UK Armed Forces, Entertainers, Sports Professionals, Hawking/General Dealers, Scrap Merchants/Dismantlers, Professional Gamblers, Modelling professionals, and Unemployed persons. Students may only hire cars, not other vehicle types.

HIRE PERIOD: Standard maximum is 84 days. Any extension requires prior written or email agreement.

MILEAGE: Fair use policy applies — 300 miles/day for hires under 7 days; 1,000 miles/week for 7+ day hires; 2,800 miles per 28-day period. Excess mileage is charged additionally.

INSURANCE: Hirers must either take AMT's own insurance (with a standard excess, reducible for an additional charge) or provide their own fully comprehensive cover extending to the hire vehicle.
