USE RESTRICTIONS: UK-only use. No hire/reward passenger carrying, no food delivery, no taxi use, no racing, no unlawful use — without prior written permission. Towing any other vehicle is also prohibited without AMT's express prior permission. All vehicles are fitted with telematics.

LITIGATION USE: Where AMT is advanced as a BHR comparator, claimants' solicitors should verify whether the claimant actually met AMT's eligibility criteria at the time of the accident. A claimant who was unemployed, had more than 6 points, exceeded the accident history threshold, or needed the vehicle for work purposes excluded by AMT's terms, would not have been able to hire from AMT. Similarly, hires exceeding 84 days or high mileage scenarios fall outside AMT's standard product, undermining any comparison based on AMT rates.

articles.title: Claims Handler Queries: GTA vs Non-GTA Framework — Understanding Which Regime Applies
articles.source: Steve Evans, The Credit Hire Forum
articles.author: Steve Evans
principles: This article is the framework article for a series of claims handler query responses. Each query in the series addresses a practical settlement issue that a claim handler managing a credit hire case is likely to encounter. Every query response is structured with two answers: one for claims proceeding under the ABI General Terms of Agreement (GTA) and one for claims proceeding outside the GTA. This article explains how to determine which regime applies and why it matters.

The ABI GTA is a bilateral protocol between subscribing insurers and subscribing CHOs. Its original purpose was to provide a fast, low-friction route to settlement for straightforward credit hire claims where liability was admitted, the at-fault insurer had engaged, and the CHO had complied with its notification, management and invoicing obligations under the protocol. In theory, case law was not intended to play any part in GTA-based settlement discussions — the protocol and its FAQs were supposed to provide all the answers.

In practice, the GTA has become more combative than its founders intended. Defendant lawyers — typically firms retained by insurers to manage their claim portfolios — increasingly assess individual credit hire claims and recommend declining GTA settlement when they believe the hire period, hire value, or BHR can be challenged to deliver a better financial outcome outside the GTA. This tactical use of the litigation threat has brought case law arguments into GTA claim discussions and has made it necessary for CHOs and their advisers to understand how those arguments play out both within and outside the protocol.

A GTA claim is one where: (a) both the CHO and the at-fault insurer are current signatories to the GTA; (b) the CHO has complied with its notification obligations (notifying the insurer of its involvement within the prescribed timeframe); (c) the insurer has acknowledged its engagement with the claim; and (d) the CHO has met its ongoing management and reporting obligations. Where all of these conditions are met, the GTA protocol and FAQs govern the claim and case law arguments are generally out of place — though increasingly raised.

A non-GTA claim arises in any of the following circumstances: the CHO is not a GTA subscriber; the at-fault insurer is not a GTA subscriber; the insurer has declined to engage under the GTA (whether or not it is a subscriber); the CHO has failed to comply with a notification obligation that takes the claim outside the protocol; or the insurer has decided to take a tactical position and assess the claim on a litigation basis regardless of GTA membership. In all of these situations, the claim falls to be resolved under general law, principally the law of mitigation, the principles from the House of Lords cases (Dimond v Lovell, Lagden v O'Connor) and the Court of Appeal authorities that followed (Clark v Ardington, Bent v Highways, Copley v Lawn, McBride v UK Insurance).

The GTA complaints and disputes process is limited in its effectiveness. Where an insurer or its appointed defendant firm is playing a tactical game — using litigation pressure to force a lower settlement than the GTA rate — the formal GTA complaints mechanism is unlikely to resolve the dispute in the CHO's favour within a timeframe that affects the settlement outcome. CHOs and their solicitors should be realistic about this limitation and not rely on the complaints process as a primary remedy when an insurer is acting tactically.

The practical significance of the GTA/non-GTA distinction for claims handlers: GTA responses should focus on protocol compliance, the FAQs, notification records and the insurer's obligations under the agreement. Non-GTA responses must engage with case law, the duty to mitigate, the claimant's individual circumstances, and the litigation risk assessment. Applying case law arguments to a GTA claim (or, conversely, assuming GTA protections apply when they do not) is a common error that can produce misleading advice.

All subsequent articles in the Claims Handler Query Series (articles 105 onwards) follow the same dual structure: GTA position first, then non-GTA position. Where a question has no meaningful GTA dimension (for example, because it arises only in contested litigation), the GTA section will say so. Where the answer is the same in both regimes, that will be stated explicitly.
articles.content: Framework article for the credit-hire.ai Claims Handler Query Series. Explains the GTA/non-GTA distinction that underpins every query response in the series. The GTA is a bilateral protocol between subscribing insurers and CHOs intended to provide fast, low-friction settlement for compliant credit hire claims. In practice, defendant lawyers retained by insurers increasingly use litigation threats to force sub-GTA settlements, bringing case law into GTA discussions. A GTA claim requires: mutual GTA membership, CHO notification compliance, insurer engagement, and ongoing management compliance. Non-GTA claims are governed by general law — mitigation duty, Dimond v Lovell, Lagden v O'Connor, Copley v Lawn, McBride v UK Insurance. The GTA complaints process offers limited protection against tactical insurer behaviour. All Claims Handler Query articles are structured: GTA position / non-GTA position.

articles.title: Claims Handler Query 1: Intervention — What Are the Consequences and How Should a CHO Respond?
articles.source: Steve Evans, The Credit Hire Forum
articles.author: Steve Evans
principles: CLAIMS HANDLER QUERY 1: What are the consequences of an intervention being made by an insurer, and how should a CHO respond? See article 104 for the GTA/non-GTA framework that underpins this and all Claims Handler Query responses.

GTA POSITION — primacy of the first engager: Under the ABI GTA, the principle is that the party first engaged by the claimant has primacy and should be permitted to manage the claim without challenge, provided it has complied with its notification obligations to the at-fault insurer. A subscribing insurer intervening against a subscribing CHO that was first engaged and has complied with the protocol is acting contrary to the spirit of the GTA. The correct response in this situation is to engage the at-fault insurer directly, confirm the CHO's GTA membership, evidence the date of first engagement with the claimant, confirm compliance with the notification obligation, and assert the CHO's right to manage the claim. In most cases where the CHO can demonstrate prior engagement and protocol compliance, the insurer will withdraw the intervention approach without escalation.

GTA POSITION — when the insurer persists: If an insurer persists with an intervention approach despite the CHO's GTA compliance being evidenced, the CHO can invoke the GTA complaints and disputes process. However, as noted in article 104, this process is unlikely to produce a rapid or decisive resolution. The more practical response is to continue managing the claim to conclusion while keeping contemporaneous records of the insurer's conduct, which can be deployed as evidence of unreasonable behaviour if the claim does proceed to litigation.

NON-GTA POSITION — the legal framework: Outside the GTA, an insurer's intervention offer engages the claimant's duty to mitigate under Payzu v Saunders [1919] and as developed in the credit hire context by Copley v Lawn [2009] EWCA Civ 580. The core principle is that if the insurer makes a genuine, clear and complete offer of a suitable replacement vehicle that the claimant unreasonably fails to accept, the claimant's hire charges will not be recoverable from the date on which it was reasonable to have accepted the offer. This risk falls on the CHO commercially if the CHO has indemnified itself against non-recovery.

NON-GTA POSITION — what makes an intervention offer valid: For an intervention offer to create the mitigation risk established in Copley, it must be sufficiently clear and complete for the claimant to act on it without uncertainty. The offer in Copley itself was a formal letter arriving by post, in relatively formal and arguably intimidatory terms. Modern intervention offers are far more varied in format and more carefully worded by insurers who have refined their approach over many years. An Admiral SMS example reads: 'Following the incident with our Insured, we can authorise your repairs where applicable and hire you a range rover sport or similar to save you claiming off your own policy, at a cost of £81.49 per day. We'll pay this. It will be completely free to you and is significantly less than any other provider or representative. As there is a legal duty to mitigate costs on a claim, please let your insurer or representative know that we have put forward this offer.' This message names a vehicle class, quotes a rate, invokes the duty to mitigate, and provides contact details — a court could find it meets the threshold for a valid intervention offer if left unanswered.

NON-GTA POSITION — modern delivery methods and the response challenge: Intervention approaches now arrive by SMS, WhatsApp, Facebook Messenger, email, direct telephone call, voicemail, and letter. A claimant receiving an unexpected text from an unknown number after an accident could be forgiven for treating it as spam — but the insurer's position at trial will be that the offer was made and the claimant failed to respond. The practical risk is that telephone calls are hardest to ignore and easiest for an insurer to evidence. The CHO must prepare the claimant for this before any approach is made.

NON-GTA POSITION — preparing the claimant before any intervention approach: The single most effective protection is preparation. The claimant should be told in writing and orally, at the outset of the hire, that the at-fault insurer may contact them directly by any means — text, email, phone, post or social media — and that if they are contacted they should not engage in any conversation, should refer the person to the CHO or appointed solicitor, should make no commitments, and should immediately forward any message or correspondence to the CHO. This instruction, and the claimant's acknowledgment of it, should be documented.

NON-GTA POSITION — responding to an intervention offer in writing: Where an intervention approach is received, the CHO should not ignore it and should not engage by telephone. All communications should be in writing, sent by 48-hour tracked delivery where letters are used. The written response should: (1) confirm that the CHO is managing the claimant's claim and that all communications should be directed to the CHO; (2) ask for confirmation in writing that the claimant's hire charges up to the date of the intervention approach will be paid in full; (3) ask for the identity and location of the hire company said to be able to provide the intervention vehicle, and confirmation that a suitable vehicle is actually available; (4) ask for a copy of the proposed hire agreement so that the claimant can review the terms before making any decision; (5) ask for confirmation of the insurance terms on the offered vehicle, including the excess and permitted use. This structured written response simultaneously protects the CHO and tests whether the insurer's offer is genuine and complete.

NON-GTA POSITION — the insurer's practical inability to respond: Experience shows that most insurers cannot respond substantively to a CHO's written enquiries within the timescale of a typical hire. Post backlogs, internal workflows, and the volume of claims handled by insurer operations teams mean that substantive written responses to a CHO's four or five specific questions often never arrive — or arrive after the hire has ended. When an insurer later raises the intervention in its defence, the CHO can demonstrate that it responded promptly and in good faith, that it sought specific information necessary to assess the offer, and that the insurer failed to provide that information. An incomplete or unanswered intervention approach is a defective intervention, and a court is unlikely to penalise the claimant for failing to accept an offer whose terms were never fully disclosed.

NON-GTA POSITION — the claimant must be asked about the suitability of the offered vehicle: Before any response declining or querying the intervention offer is sent, the CHO must ask the claimant directly whether the offered vehicle would meet their needs, and document the answer. This is the threshold question. If the claimant confirms that the offered vehicle would not suit them — because of use requirements, insurance incompatibility, vehicle type, or any other reason — that response should be captured in writing and referenced in the CHO's reply to the insurer. If the claimant would in fact have been content with the offered vehicle, continuing to reject the offer exposes the hire charges to mitigation challenge from the date of the offer. See also article 15 (Copley letters and intervention) for the detailed eligibility and suitability checklist.

NON-GTA POSITION — offering an intervention rate as a middle ground: If the insurer's written enquiries are answered promptly and the hire is still ongoing, the CHO may consider offering to continue managing the hire on revised terms — paying the pre-intervention charges in full at the credit hire rate, and applying an agreed reduced rate (an 'intervention rate') for the post-intervention period. This approach preserves the CHO's pre-offer position, reduces the live exposure, and is a demonstrably reasonable response that is difficult for a court to criticise. It also gives the insurer an incentive to confirm payment of the pre-intervention charges rather than risk a costs award for contested litigation.

NON-GTA POSITION — the Admiral SMS example analysed: The Admiral SMS illustrated in the intervention folder is typical of modern volume intervention. Key features: (a) sent as a text to the claimant's personal mobile, from a number not in their contacts; (b) names a specific vehicle class (Range Rover Sport or similar) and quotes a specific rate (£81.49/day); (c) explicitly invokes the legal duty to mitigate; (d) describes the offer as 'significantly less than any other provider or representative'; (e) asks the claimant to call or visit a website to arrange. The use of 'or similar' for the vehicle and the absence of any insurance or excess information means the offer is not fully specified — a CHO's written response asking for insurance details, hire agreement terms, and vehicle availability confirmation would likely expose these gaps. At £81.49/day, the rate is also itself potential BHR evidence for the claim.

The Admiral formal intervention letter to CHOs — structure and purpose: Admiral (registered as EUI Limited, FCA authorised No. 309378) has developed a formal intervention letter directed not to the claimant but to the CHO, instructing the CHO to 'please advise your client of this offer'. This is structurally different from the SMS approach (see article 105). When the letter is addressed to the CHO, the CHO is formally on notice from the date of receipt. The clock potentially starts running not when the claimant sees the offer but when the CHO receives it. A CHO that files this letter without acting on it, or that simply ignores it, is in a very exposed position.

What the Admiral CHO letter offers — designed for Copley compliance: The letter makes a comprehensive offer designed to anticipate and answer every Copley objection. It offers: a specific named vehicle (BMW 320D M Sport or equivalent) at a stated daily rate (£48.80 + VAT, paid by Admiral); no excess to pay; free pick-up and delivery; repair through approved repairers or a repairer of the claimant's choice; cash settlement if beyond economic repair; free storage; free vehicle recovery; free disposal if written off; and a separate taxi driver provision (BMW 320D M Sport or equivalent, £50.39 + VAT, with PCO plating). For claimants needing something different, the letter offers to source 'many different makes, models and classes of vehicle'. The breadth of the offer is deliberate — it is designed to make it very difficult for the CHO to argue the offer was inadequate without engaging in detail.

The FAQs section — Admiral's attempt to pre-answer every challenge: The letter includes an extensive FAQs section that addresses the most common grounds on which CHOs challenge the adequacy of an intervention offer. The questions answered include: whether the claimant is liable for hire costs (no, £1 refundable deposit); like-for-like entitlement (yes, needs discussed); mileage allowance; hire period (repair duration; total loss = 7 days after receipt of payment cheque); car seats (isofix fitted); insurance cover; excess on hire vehicle; named drivers; which company provides the vehicle; what to do if already in a hire vehicle; pick-up and drop-off arrangements; total loss process; use of own garage; and taxi driver provision. This level of detail is a direct response to the kind of written enquiry strategy described in article 105 — Admiral is trying to pre-empt the questions before the CHO can ask them.

Critical weakness 1 — the excess is NOT zero: The FAQ answer to 'In the event of an accident in the hire vehicle, would I be liable for the excess?' is: 'Yes, the excess on the hire vehicle will match that of what is stated on your own policy.' This is a fundamental comparability gap. A credit hire vehicle is typically provided with zero excess or a greatly reduced excess as part of the accident replacement package. The Admiral intervention vehicle carries the same excess as whatever the claimant has on their own policy — typically £200 to £500 or more. There is no ERI product available to reduce this in the credit hire context. This means the Admiral offer is not equivalent to the credit hire product in terms of financial exposure, and the CHO's response should specifically identify and quantify this difference.

Critical weakness 2 — mileage is vague and at provider's discretion: The FAQ states: 'No, there is not a mileage allowance. However, there are limitations around taking the vehicle aboard and is at the discretion of the vehicle provider who will discuss this with you directly.' This is not an unambiguous commitment to unlimited mileage. It explicitly acknowledges 'limitations' and defers to the vehicle provider. A claimant with high mileage needs — for example a travelling salesperson, delivery driver, or rural resident — cannot be certain from this letter that their mileage needs will be met. The CHO's written response should ask for a specific written confirmation of the mileage terms applicable to this claimant.

Critical weakness 3 — drivers under 21 may be restricted: The FAQ states: 'Please note, any drivers under the age of 21 years, may be limited to what hire vehicle they will be insured to drive.' This means that for a young claimant (or a claimant with a young named driver), the Admiral offer may not provide a suitable vehicle. The CHO should identify whether the claimant or any regular named driver is under 21 and, if so, request clarification of what vehicle would be available and on what terms.

Critical weakness 4 — conviction holders at provider's discretion: The FAQ states: 'Any drivers who hold a driving conviction may not be eligible to drive the hire vehicle. This will be at the discretion of the vehicle provider.' This is a vague non-commitment. A claimant with penalty points — not uncommon — cannot be certain they are eligible for the intervention vehicle without a specific response from the vehicle provider. The CHO should ask for written confirmation that the claimant is eligible to drive the offered vehicle given their licence history.

Critical weakness 5 — taxi/private hire excluded from business insurance: The letter offers a PCO-plated vehicle for taxi drivers. However, the FAQ states: 'Business insurance can be provided for the vehicle; however, this does not cover the use for taxiing/private hire services.' This appears contradictory — but the likely interpretation is that the PCO-plated vehicle comes with a specific arrangement for licensed taxi use, while general 'business insurance' on a standard vehicle does not extend to taxi use. The CHO managing a taxi or private hire claim should obtain explicit written confirmation of exactly what insurance cover applies to the offered vehicle for the claimant's specific licensed use.

The letter is directed to the CHO — the CHO cannot simply advise the client to ignore it: Because the letter is formally addressed to the CHO and asks the CHO to advise the client, the CHO is squarely in the frame. Unlike a text message sent directly to the claimant which the claimant might not see or might misunderstand, this letter puts the CHO on notice. A CHO that fails to engage — either by responding in writing or by advising the client to reject with documented reasons — is exposed to the argument that the CHO suppressed or ignored a valid offer. The CHO must act: acknowledge receipt, identify the specific questions and weaknesses, respond in writing and document that the client was informed of the offer and their response.

The proactive written response strategy — applied to the Admiral CHO letter: The written response strategy described in this article (tracked delivery, specific questions) applies with even greater force when the letter is directed to the CHO. The response should: (1) acknowledge receipt and confirm the CHO is managing the claim; (2) identify each of the specific weaknesses — excess matching own policy rather than zero excess, vague mileage terms, under-21 or endorsement uncertainty if applicable; (3) ask for written confirmation of the specific terms that will apply to this claimant, including the excess applicable, mileage terms, and confirmation of eligibility given the claimant's licence; (4) confirm the claimant's position on whether the offered vehicle type would meet their needs (this remains the threshold question — see article 105); (5) state that the CHO will advise the client once the outstanding questions have been answered. This approach is documented, professional, and places the evidential burden squarely on Admiral to clarify their offer.
cases: Smyth v Markerstudy Insurance Company Ltd [2022] NIQB
articles.content: Claims Handler Query 1: Intervention. GTA position: first-engaged CHO has primacy; subscribing insurer intervention against a compliant subscribing CHO is contrary to the GTA; respond by evidencing prior engagement and protocol compliance. Non-GTA position: Copley v Lawn [2009] EWCA Civ 580 governs — a valid, clear and complete intervention offer that the claimant unreasonably ignores puts hire charges at risk from the date of the offer. Modern intervention arrives by SMS, WhatsApp, email, phone and letter — the Admiral SMS example names a vehicle (Range Rover Sport or similar, £81.49/day) and invokes the duty to mitigate. Key responses: (1) prepare the claimant before any approach; (2) do not ignore and do not call — respond in writing by tracked post; (3) ask specific questions about pre-hire payment, vehicle availability, hire agreement terms and insurance; (4) ask the claimant whether the offered vehicle would actually suit them before declining; (5) most insurers cannot answer written enquiries in time — a defective or unanswered offer is not an effective intervention. Middle ground: offer to continue hire at an intervention rate for the post-offer period, with pre-offer charges settled at credit hire rate.

articles.title: Claims Handler Query 2: Total Loss — How Long Can a Claimant Continue to Hire After Their Vehicle is Declared Beyond Economic Repair?
articles.source: Steve Evans, The Credit Hire Forum
articles.author: Steve Evans
principles: CLAIMS HANDLER QUERY 2: Once a vehicle is declared beyond economic repair (BER / total loss), how long can the claimant continue to hire? See article 104 for the GTA/non-GTA framework.

GTA POSITION — express 7-day provision: The ABI GTA contains an express provision governing hire after a total loss declaration. Once the pre-accident value (PAV) has been agreed between the insurer and the claimant and the claimant has been put in funds — meaning the settlement payment has actually been made, not merely offered — the claimant is entitled to continue hiring for a further 7 days during which they can search for and purchase a replacement vehicle. At the expiry of that 7-day period, the hire should end. The GTA therefore provides a bright-line rule that removes most of the uncertainty present in non-GTA claims: agreement of PAV + payment of funds + 7 days = end of recoverable hire.

GTA POSITION — the trigger is being put in funds, not the declaration of total loss: The 7-day period does not run from the date on which the vehicle is assessed as a total loss or the date the insurer declares it a write-off. It runs from the date the claimant actually receives the settlement funds. An insurer that delays payment of the agreed PAV cannot argue that the 7-day period has already run. A CHO managing a GTA claim should record the date of PAV agreement and the date funds were received by the claimant separately, as both are relevant to the end-of-hire calculation.

GTA POSITION — disputed PAV: Where the claimant disputes the insurer's PAV assessment, the 7-day clock does not start until the dispute is resolved and the agreed sum is paid. A claimant who reasonably holds out for a higher PAV is not obliged to accept the insurer's initial offer and start searching for a replacement on the basis of an inadequate settlement.

NON-GTA POSITION — no fixed period, reasonableness governs: Outside the GTA there is no equivalent 7-day rule. The question is simply one of reasonableness: how long would it have been reasonable for this claimant to take to find and purchase a suitable replacement vehicle, given their specific circumstances? The burden of showing that the hire period was unreasonably long lies on the defendant, but the claimant must be able to explain and evidence why the replacement process took as long as it did.

NON-GTA POSITION — replacement vehicle type and availability: Where the claimant's vehicle was relatively new or a specific model that the claimant wished to replace on a like-for-like basis, a longer search period is entirely reasonable. Ordering a new car from a manufacturer or dealer involves lead times that can run to weeks or months. The reasonable hire period is the period up to the point at which the replacement vehicle could realistically be collected — not the point at which the claimant could theoretically have purchased any available car.

NON-GTA POSITION — seasonal and personal factors: Courts will take into account genuine practical obstacles to the replacement purchase. Where a total loss occurs around Christmas or other bank holiday periods, dealers are closed, delivery times are longer, and family commitments make the search process more difficult. A longer post-total-loss hire period in December and early January is correspondingly easier to justify. Evidence of the relevant period (dealer closure dates, order logs, delivery confirmations) should be preserved.

NON-GTA POSITION — adapted and accessible vehicles: Where the claimant's vehicle was adapted for a disability or other specific requirement — hand controls, hoists, lowered floors, wheelchair access — the reasonable hire period extends to the point at which a suitably adapted replacement vehicle is available and delivered. Adapted vehicles are not available from internet supermarkets or off the shelf; specialist lead times can be substantial. Hire during this period is plainly reasonable, and the claimant should obtain written evidence of lead times from the supplier of the adapted vehicle.

NON-GTA POSITION — shift workers and those with restricted availability: A claimant who works anti-social hours — night shifts, rotating shifts, early starts — cannot be expected to visit dealerships during normal business hours in the immediate aftermath of a total loss. If their working pattern genuinely prevents them from conducting a search during the period immediately following the total loss declaration, the reasonable period is extended to encompass a realistic window in which they could have made the purchase. Documentary evidence of the claimant's working pattern at the relevant time (pay slips, rota records, employer confirmation) significantly strengthens this argument.

NON-GTA POSITION — the insurer's internet supermarket argument: Insurers increasingly argue that online car supermarkets (Cazoo, Cinch, Motorpoint and similar) offer next-day or rapid delivery with a short-period return or exchange policy, meaning the claimant should have purchased a replacement vehicle within days of the total loss and could have returned it if it proved unsuitable. This argument should be met on two grounds: first, the law of mitigation does not require the innocent party to accept a sub-optimal solution that puts them at commercial or personal risk — a claimant who buys a car online without inspecting it may end up owning a car that does not meet their needs or has undisclosed faults, and is not obliged to put themselves in that position. Second, if the claimant exercises the return right, they are once again without a vehicle and must restart the search process, potentially extending the hire rather than reducing it. The at-fault party cannot dictate the manner in which the claimant is restored to their pre-accident position.

NON-GTA POSITION — evidencing the replacement timeline: In all non-GTA total loss hire cases, the claimant should be asked to retain evidence of the steps taken to find a replacement: dealer enquiries, online searches, test drive bookings, finance applications, order confirmations, and delivery correspondence. This evidence rebuts any insurer argument that the claimant was passive or dilatory. A contemporaneous record of the search process — even a brief chronology prepared by the claimant or their solicitor — is far more persuasive at trial than unsupported oral evidence given months or years later.

NON-GTA POSITION — when the funds are received matters here too: As with the GTA, the trigger for the post-total-loss hire period running is not the insurer's internal decision that the vehicle is a write-off, nor even the communication of that decision to the claimant. The claimant cannot reasonably be expected to purchase a replacement vehicle before they have received the funds to do so. A claimant who does not have the financial resources to bridge the gap between the total loss declaration and payment (which is common) should not be penalised for delays caused by the insurer's payment process. The hire period should be measured from the date of actual receipt of funds.
articles.content: Claims Handler Query 2: Total loss hire duration. GTA: express 7-day post-funds provision — hire ends 7 days after PAV is agreed and settlement funds are received by the claimant. The clock does not run from the write-off declaration, nor from a disputed PAV offer. Non-GTA: no fixed period; reasonableness governs on the claimant's specific facts. Relevant factors include: type of replacement vehicle sought (new vs used, specialist vs standard); seasonal and personal obstacles (Christmas, shift patterns, restricted availability); adapted or accessible vehicle lead times; and the date actual settlement funds were received. Insurers' internet supermarket argument (buy online, return if unsuitable) should be resisted on two grounds — the claimant is not obliged to accept a commercially risky substitution, and a returned vehicle leaves the claimant without transport again. Evidence of the search process (dealer enquiries, orders, delivery confirmations, rota records) is essential in non-GTA claims.

articles.title: Claims Handler Query 3: Own Insurance — Can the Insurer Argue the Claimant Should Have Used Their Own Comprehensive Policy?
articles.source: Steve Evans, The Credit Hire Forum
articles.author: Steve Evans
principles: CLAIMS HANDLER QUERY 3: The insurer argues that the claimant was wrong to pursue a claim against the third party insurer when the claimant had comprehensive insurance of their own, which could have covered the repairs promptly and potentially provided a courtesy car. See article 104 for the GTA/non-GTA framework.

GTA POSITION — the argument is unlikely to arise in practice: This challenge is uncommon in GTA claims because the CHO notifies the at-fault insurer of its involvement at the outset of the hire, giving the insurer early visibility of the claim and the opportunity to manage it. The insurer is not disadvantaged by the claimant coming directly to them through the CHO's notification process. In the GTA context, the question of whether the claimant could have used their own insurance is not a recognised basis for resisting settlement under the protocol.

NON-GTA POSITION — why this argument is raised: Outside the GTA, insurers are less likely to receive timely notification of a credit hire claim. They may first become aware of the claim when the hire is well advanced or the vehicle is close to repair, by which point they have limited ability to manage costs. The 'use your own insurance' argument is partly a tactical response to this information gap — an attempt to shift moral responsibility for the hire costs back to the claimant by pointing to an alternative remedy the claimant allegedly had available.

NON-GTA POSITION — the doctrine of res inter alios acta: The argument fails as a matter of law. The doctrine of res inter alios acta — 'a thing done between others' — provides that a contract, agreement or arrangement between two parties cannot be invoked by, or impose obligations on, a third party who is not a party to it. Applied to this context: the comprehensive insurance policy is a contract between the claimant and their own insurer. The negligent driver (and their insurer) is a stranger to that contract. The tortfeasor cannot rely on the existence of that policy to reduce their own liability or to dictate to the claimant that they must exercise rights under it.

NON-GTA POSITION — the tortfeasor cannot compel the claimant to claim on their own insurance: The negligent driver caused the loss. The claimant is entitled to be put back in the position they were in before the accident by the party responsible for that accident. The claimant's comprehensive insurance policy is a benefit the claimant has paid for independently. It is not an asset that belongs to the tortfeasor or that the tortfeasor can require the claimant to deplete. Requiring the claimant to claim on their own policy would expose the claimant to a potential excess payment, a potential loss of no-claims discount or bonus, and the administrative consequences of a first-party claim — none of which are the claimant's fault and all of which flow from the negligence of the third party.

NON-GTA POSITION — the courtesy car point: Even where the claimant's own comprehensive policy includes a courtesy car benefit, the tortfeasor cannot require the claimant to use it. The courtesy car benefit is part of the contract between the claimant and their own insurer. Whether and how the claimant uses that benefit is a matter between those two parties. Moreover, courtesy cars are typically basic, unspecified vehicles provided at the insurer's discretion — they may not be equivalent to the claimant's damaged vehicle in size, specification or capability. The claimant is not obliged to accept an inferior substitute when the party whose negligence caused the loss is capable of funding an adequate replacement.

NON-GTA POSITION — practical consequences of claiming on own insurance: The insurer's argument that the claimant should have used their own comprehensive insurance ignores the real-world consequences of doing so. A claimant who makes a first-party claim may: (a) pay an excess, typically £100-£500 or more; (b) lose some or all of their no-claims bonus or discount, potentially increasing their premiums for several years; (c) have the claim recorded on their insurance history, which affects future insurability and pricing. None of these consequences are outcomes the claimant should be required to accept when the loss was caused by someone else's negligence. The tortfeasor should bear the full cost of the harm they caused.

NON-GTA POSITION — no duty to mitigate by claiming on own insurance: The duty to mitigate loss (Payzu v Saunders [1919]) requires the claimant to take reasonable steps to reduce their loss — but reasonableness does not extend to requiring the claimant to exercise contractual rights against their own insurer at their own financial cost and risk. The steps that constitute reasonable mitigation must not themselves cause the claimant further loss. A step that triggers an excess payment, premium increase, or loss of no-claims protection is not a step that can reasonably be demanded of the claimant by the party who caused the loss in the first place.

NON-GTA POSITION — the position is well established: The principle that a defendant cannot rely on the claimant's insurance position to reduce their liability is long-standing in English tort law. In Bradburn v Great Western Railway (1874) LR 10 Ex 1, the court held that insurance monies received by an injured party could not be set off against damages owed by the tortfeasor — the claimant's insurance was a benefit of their own prudence and foresight, not a windfall that reduced the wrongdoer's obligation. This principle has been consistently applied. The comprehensive insurance argument raised by third party insurers in credit hire cases is a variant of the same discredited approach.

NON-GTA POSITION — summary response to the insurer: If an insurer raises this argument in correspondence or at trial, the response is: (1) the claimant's insurance policy is a res inter alios acta — it is a contract between the claimant and their own insurer to which the defendant is a stranger and upon which the defendant has no right to rely; (2) the claimant is entitled to pursue their remedy against the party responsible for the loss; (3) requiring the claimant to claim on their own insurance would impose financial penalties on an innocent party — excess, premium loading, no-claims discount loss — which are themselves consequential losses caused by the defendant's negligence; (4) the duty to mitigate does not require the claimant to suffer additional loss at their own expense in order to reduce the defendant's liability.
cases: Bradburn v Great Western Railway (1874) LR 10 Ex 1
articles.content: Claims Handler Query 3: Own insurance / courtesy car argument. GTA: unlikely to arise — CHO notification gives insurer early visibility. Non-GTA: insurers raise this tactically where they receive late notification of a claim. The argument fails in law under the doctrine of res inter alios acta — the claimant's comprehensive insurance policy is a contract between the claimant and their own insurer; the tortfeasor is a stranger to it and cannot compel the claimant to exercise rights under it. Requiring the claimant to use their own insurance exposes them to excess payments, no-claims discount loss and premium increases — all consequential losses caused by the defendant's negligence, not the claimant's obligation to absorb. The tortfeasor cannot dictate that the claimant must deplete a benefit they paid for in order to reduce the wrongdoer's liability. Bradburn v Great Western Railway (1874) establishes that a claimant's insurance receipts cannot reduce the tortfeasor's liability. The duty to mitigate under Payzu v Saunders does not require the claimant to incur further loss at their own expense.

articles.title: Claims Handler Query 4: Storage Charges — Is the Claimant Entitled to Recover the Cost of Storing a Damaged Vehicle?
articles.source: Steve Evans, The Credit Hire Forum
articles.author: Steve Evans
principles: CLAIMS HANDLER QUERY 4: The insurer argues that the claimant is not entitled to recover storage charges for a damaged vehicle that was undriveable and either awaiting repair or declared beyond economic repair, on the basis that the claimant should have kept it at home. See article 104 for the GTA/non-GTA framework.

GTA POSITION — storage rarely a live issue under the protocol: Storage charges do not typically fall within the scope of GTA settlement discussions, which focus primarily on hire charges and repair costs. However, storage may become relevant in a GTA-originated claim where the at-fault insurer decides that the total value of the claim — including storage — is sufficient to warrant declining protocol settlement and inviting litigation instead. In that event, the claim for storage falls to be assessed on general law principles as set out in the non-GTA position below.

NON-GTA POSITION — the insurer's argument: The insurer's position is that the claimant had a duty to mitigate by keeping the damaged vehicle at their home address — on a driveway or parked on the road outside — rather than incurring storage charges at a third party facility. This argument is advanced on the basis that storage is an avoidable cost that the claimant chose to incur unnecessarily.

NON-GTA POSITION — reasonableness is the governing test: The question is not whether the claimant could theoretically have kept the vehicle at home, but whether it was reasonable in the circumstances for them not to do so. The burden of showing that the storage was unreasonable lies with the insurer. Reasonableness is assessed on the specific facts of each claim, and there are several common categories of case in which storage away from the home address is plainly reasonable.

NON-GTA POSITION — no space at the home address: The most straightforward justification for third-party storage is lack of space. The claimant who hired a replacement vehicle now has two vehicles to accommodate — the damaged vehicle and the hire car. Most residential properties do not have sufficient driveway or off-street parking for two vehicles, and leaving the damaged vehicle on the public highway may create obstruction or road safety concerns of its own. Where the claimant has only one off-street parking space, or none at all, it is entirely reasonable for the damaged vehicle to go into storage to allow the hire vehicle to be kept securely.

NON-GTA POSITION — the vehicle poses a safety risk if left at home: Where the damaged vehicle is leaking fluids — oil, coolant, brake fluid or fuel — leaving it on a residential driveway or public road creates a genuine environmental and safety hazard. Similarly, where the vehicle has jagged or displaced bodywork, broken glass or unstable components, leaving it where members of the public or children could come into contact with it may expose the claimant to liability. In both situations, placing the vehicle in secure storage is not only reasonable but arguably the responsible course of action.

NON-GTA POSITION — the vehicle left on the road may constitute an offence: A damaged vehicle that is undriveable and without a current MOT, or that is so extensively damaged as to present a hazard to other road users, may be a candidate for a police notice or local authority action if left on the public highway. Where a constable or council officer has indicated that the vehicle should be removed, or where the claimant has reasonable grounds to believe it would be subject to enforcement action, storage is the only lawful alternative. Evidence of any such notice or communication from the police or local authority is strong support for the storage charge.

NON-GTA POSITION — security of the damaged vehicle: A significantly damaged vehicle — particularly one with broken windows, missing doors or non-functioning locks — is vulnerable to theft, vandalism or further damage if left on a public road or unsecured. The claimant has an obligation to take reasonable steps to preserve the vehicle pending the outcome of the claim, both for their own interests and because the at-fault insurer has an interest in the vehicle's condition (particularly where liability or quantum is disputed). Secure storage discharges that obligation. An insurer who argues against storage but then disputes the condition or value of the vehicle is in a difficult position.

NON-GTA POSITION — documentation is critical: The strength of the storage claim depends almost entirely on the quality of the contemporaneous evidence. The claimant and the CHO or solicitor should record: (a) the reason why home storage was not practicable — photographs of the home address showing parking availability, or a written note of the parking situation; (b) the condition of the vehicle at the time it was placed in storage — photographs showing fluid leaks, jagged bodywork, broken glass or other hazards; (c) any communication from the police, local authority or recovery company about the vehicle's condition or road-worthiness; (d) the daily storage rate charged and by whom; and (e) the date the vehicle was collected from storage (on repair, sale for salvage, or collection by the insurer following total loss settlement). Evidence captured at the time is worth considerably more than retrospective explanations at trial.

NON-GTA POSITION — the rate charged must itself be reasonable: Even where the decision to store is justified, the insurer may challenge the rate charged. Storage rates vary considerably between recovery and storage operators, and an unusually high daily rate without explanation may attract criticism. Where possible, the storage provider should be a reputable, established operator and the rate should be consistent with the market for that type and size of vehicle. If the vehicle is a large commercial vehicle or specialist vehicle, a higher rate is inherently more justifiable. The claimant or CHO should retain the storage invoices and be prepared to justify the rate if challenged.
articles.content: Claims Handler Query 4: Storage charges. GTA: not a standard protocol issue, but becomes relevant if the insurer declines settlement and invites litigation on the whole claim. Non-GTA: reasonableness governs. The insurer's argument — that the claimant should have kept the vehicle at home — fails where: (1) the claimant lacks space for both the damaged vehicle and the hire car; (2) the vehicle is leaking fluids or has bodywork hazards making home storage unsafe; (3) leaving the vehicle on the road risks police or local authority enforcement; (4) the vehicle is unsecured and vulnerable to theft or further damage. Documentation is critical: photographs of the home address showing parking constraints, photographs of the vehicle's condition, any police or local authority communications, and the storage invoices. The daily rate charged must itself be reasonable and consistent with the market for that vehicle type.

articles.title: Claims Handler Query 5: Cash In Lieu — How Long Can the Claimant Remain in Hire After the Insurer Makes a Cash Settlement Allowing the Claimant to Arrange Their Own Repairs?
articles.source: Steve Evans, The Credit Hire Forum
articles.author: Steve Evans
principles: CLAIMS HANDLER QUERY 5: The insurer has made a cash in lieu offer, allowing the claimant to keep their damaged vehicle and arrange repairs independently. How long after accepting that offer can the claimant remain in hire and recover the hire charges? See article 104 for the GTA/non-GTA framework.

GTA AND NON-GTA POSITION — the same rule applies in both regimes: Unlike most issues in this query series, the cash in lieu question is not resolved differently under the GTA and outside it. The GTA does not contain specific provisions governing the hire period following a cash in lieu settlement, and so the answer in both regimes is determined by the same general principle: reasonableness. The analysis below applies equally to GTA and non-GTA claims.

The nature of a cash in lieu settlement: A cash in lieu payment is the insurer's acknowledgment that the vehicle has suffered damage whose repair cost has been agreed, but instead of the insurer managing the repair directly, the claimant accepts a cash payment and takes on the responsibility of arranging the repairs themselves. In practical terms it is the equivalent of the insurer treating the vehicle as a constructive total loss and settling the physical damage claim in cash — but with the claimant retaining the vehicle and accepting the risk that the repair cost may differ from the agreed settlement sum. Critically, the cash in lieu payment settles the quantum of the physical damage claim only. It does not settle, extinguish or cap any other head of loss arising from the accident, including the claim for hire charges.

Hire continues until the vehicle is repaired and returned: Where the claimant accepts a cash in lieu offer, the hire period does not end on the date of the cash payment. The claimant still does not have a useable vehicle — the damaged car is yet to be repaired. The hire continues, and hire charges continue to accrue and remain recoverable, until the repairs have been completed and the claimant's vehicle is returned to them in a roadworthy condition. The acceptance of cash in lieu is not an event that terminates the need for hire; it merely changes the mechanism by which the physical damage will be addressed.

The claimant must act without undue delay: The hire period following a cash in lieu settlement is not open-ended. The claimant must take reasonable and prompt steps to arrange and complete the repair of their vehicle. The duty to mitigate applies: the claimant cannot allow the repair to drag on indefinitely while continuing to accrue hire charges at the defendant's expense. The question in any disputed case is whether the time taken to complete the repair was reasonable given the claimant's circumstances and the nature of the repairs required.

The risk of DIY or self-managed repairs: Where the claimant decides to carry out the repairs themselves rather than instructing a professional repairer, the hire period must reflect the realistic time it would take the claimant — not a trade repairer — to complete the work. If the repairs would take a competent repairer 7 working days, but the claimant can only work on the car one day per week, the reasonable hire period is not 7 weeks. The reasonable period is closer to the time a professional repairer would have taken, because the claimant has chosen a method of repair that extends the period beyond what was necessary. A 7-week hire period for repairs that would have taken a garage 7 days is unlikely to be recoverable in full. The claimant who chooses to repair their own vehicle accepts the consequences of that choice on the hire period calculation.

Professional repairs and reasonable lead times: Where the claimant instructs a professional repairer, hire charges are recoverable during any reasonable waiting period for a repair slot, the duration of the repair itself, and any period of reasonable delay caused by parts availability, specialist work, or unforeseen complications. The key is that each element of the repair timeline must be explicable. A claimant who instructs a repairer promptly and whose vehicle is returned to them within a normal commercial repair timescale will have no difficulty recovering the full hire period.

Maintaining a repair log — essential in all cash in lieu cases: Because the hire period following a cash in lieu settlement depends entirely on the progress and duration of the repair, a contemporaneous repair log is the single most important piece of evidence in any dispute. The log should record: (a) the date the cash in lieu offer was accepted and funds received; (b) the date the claimant instructed a repairer or began their own repair; (c) the reason for any gap between receipt of funds and commencement of repair; (d) the dates of any significant repair milestones; (e) the reason for any delays during the repair — parts on order, specialist work required, unexpected complications; (f) the date the repaired vehicle was returned to the claimant and confirmed roadworthy. Where the claimant is carrying out repairs personally, the log should also record the days worked, the hours spent, and the work completed on each occasion. This evidence transforms a bare assertion of reasonableness into a documented account that is far harder for an insurer to challenge.

The cash payment does not cap other heads of loss: An insurer who has made a cash in lieu payment may argue that all aspects of the claim have been resolved, and that no further charges are recoverable. This argument is wrong. The cash in lieu payment is consideration for the physical damage to the vehicle only. It does not discharge the tortfeasor's liability for other losses flowing from the accident, including the reasonable cost of a replacement vehicle for the period during which the claimant's vehicle remains unroadworthy. The heads of loss are separate and the settlement of one does not extinguish the other.

Practical advice for claims handlers: When a cash in lieu offer is accepted, the CHO or solicitor should immediately: (1) confirm in writing to the claimant that the cash payment relates to vehicle damage only and that hire charges continue; (2) advise the claimant to instruct a repairer promptly and to keep all receipts, correspondence and repair documentation; (3) set up a repair log or ask the claimant to maintain one; (4) if the claimant is doing the repair themselves, have an honest conversation about the realistic repair timeline and the impact on recoverable hire charges; (5) diarise regular check-ins on repair progress so that the hire period can be monitored and the file can be closed promptly when the repair is complete.
articles.content: Claims Handler Query 5: Cash in lieu. The same rule applies in GTA and non-GTA claims — the GTA has no specific provision. A cash in lieu payment settles the physical damage quantum only; it does not cap or extinguish the claim for hire charges. Hire continues and charges accrue until the vehicle is repaired and returned to the claimant in a roadworthy condition. The claimant must act without undue delay. DIY repairs are the principal risk: if the claimant can only work on the vehicle one day per week and the job would take a garage 7 days, a 7-week hire period is not recoverable. Professional repair timescales including reasonable waiting periods, parts delays and unforeseen complications are recoverable where properly documented. A contemporaneous repair log is essential: date of cash receipt, date repair instructed or started, reason for any gaps or delays, milestones, date vehicle returned roadworthy. Practical advice: confirm in writing that cash settlement is physical damage only; advise prompt instruction of a repairer; establish a repair log; have an honest conversation with self-repairing claimants about the hire period consequences.

articles.title: Claims Handler Query 6: Disputed Repair Costs — The Insurer's Staff Engineer Disputes Costs Agreed by an Independent Engineer. What Is the Position?
articles.source: Steve Evans, The Credit Hire Forum
articles.author: Steve Evans
principles: CLAIMS HANDLER QUERY 6: The insurer refuses to settle the repair account on the basis that its own staff engineer considers the costs agreed by an independent engineer to be too high. What is the position? See article 104 for the GTA/non-GTA framework.

GTA AND NON-GTA POSITION — the same rule applies in both regimes: The question of whether a repair account assessed and agreed by an independent engineer is recoverable is not resolved differently under the GTA and outside it. The GTA has no specific provision governing the resolution of a dispute between a CHO's independent engineer and an insurer's staff engineer. The answer in both regimes is determined by the general law on repair costs and expert evidence.

How the repair assessment process works: A repairer issues a repair estimate covering the scope of work required, the number of labour hours, parts costs, paint and materials. An independent engineer — instructed by or on behalf of the CHO or claimant — attends the vehicle, reviews the estimate, inspects the damage, and agrees a figure which in their professional judgment represents the reasonable cost of the repair in the open market. The agreed figure is then authorised, the repairs are carried out, and the CHO pays the account. It is that agreed and paid figure which the insurer then seeks to challenge.

The role and status of an insurer's staff engineer: An insurer's staff engineer performs a fundamentally different function from an independent engineer. Their primary role is to negotiate repair costs downward — typically with repairers on the insurer's own managed repair panel — and to protect the insurer's financial position. This is entirely appropriate in the context of first-party claims where the insurer is managing the repair under the claimant's own comprehensive policy. In that context, the staff engineer acts as a cost controller on behalf of the insurer who is paying the bill. Their expertise and value lies in negotiating volume discounts and efficiencies within the insurer's own repair network. They are not independent and are not held out as independent.

The independent engineer's role and standing: An independent engineer is instructed to provide an objective professional assessment of the reasonable retail cost of the repairs at the time and in the geographic area where the repairs were carried out. They have no financial interest in the insurer's outcome, no panel relationship with the repairer, and no incentive to inflate or deflate the figure. Their assessment is capable of being relied upon in court as the opinion of an independent professional. A court called upon to resolve a dispute about repair costs would give significant weight to the report of an independent engineer and would approach the opinion of a staff engineer with considerably more caution, given the potential for the latter's institutional role to introduce conscious or unconscious bias toward the insurer's commercial interests.

The legal measure of repair costs — Coles v Hetherton: The authoritative statement of the measure of recoverable repair costs is Coles v Hetherton [2013] EWCA Civ 1704. The Court of Appeal held that the measure of damage for a vehicle that has been repaired is the reasonable cost of repair at the time and place of repair — that is, the retail market rate for the repair, not the managed or contracted rate that the at-fault insurer would have paid through its own repair network. Paragraphs 28 and 29 of Coles directly support the claimant's entitlement to recover the rate agreed by the independent engineer, and are the primary authority for resisting the insurer's staff engineer challenge. See article 93 for the full analysis of Coles and its supporting case law.

The insurer cannot substitute its own preferred rate for the market rate: An insurer who says 'our engineer thinks the cost should be lower' is in effect arguing that the claimant should be confined to what the insurer would have spent through its managed repair network. Coles v Hetherton expressly rejects this approach. The relevant question is what the reasonable retail cost of the repair was — not what the insurer's contracted panel rate was, and not what the insurer would have been willing to pay. The at-fault insurer's internal cost preferences are irrelevant to the measure of the claimant's loss.

Courts would not ordinarily expect to adjudicate between two engineers: Where both an independent engineer and a staff engineer have assessed the same repair, a court would not routinely treat the dispute as a battle of experts in which each side is given equal weight. The independent engineer is a professional providing an impartial market assessment; the staff engineer is performing an internal cost-control function for the defendant. If the matter were to go to trial, the court would be likely to accept the independent engineer's assessment as the appropriate measure of the reasonable cost of repair, subject only to any specific item-by-item challenge that the insurer could demonstrate was genuinely above the market rate. A bare assertion by a staff engineer that the costs were too high, without detailed item-level justification, is unlikely to displace the independent engineer's report.

Practical recommendation — litigate if necessary: Where an insurer refuses to settle a repair account that has been assessed and agreed by an independent engineer and paid by the CHO, the appropriate response is to pursue the claim through litigation if the insurer does not resile from its position. The CHO's position is strong: the repair has been independently assessed, the cost has been agreed by a professional with no financial interest in the outcome, the work has been carried out, and the legal measure of recovery under Coles v Hetherton supports the amount claimed. The insurer's staff engineer is in a structurally weaker evidential position. The risk in litigation lies with the insurer, not with the claimant or CHO.

Protecting the position before litigation: In preparation for any dispute, the independent engineer's report should be retained along with the original repair estimate, the repairer's invoice, and any correspondence between the engineer and the repairer about the scope or cost of the repairs. If there is any item in the repair account that is unusual, involves a significant uplift, or relates to a specialist procedure (for example ADAS calibration, aluminium repair, or a manufacturer-approval process), those items should be specifically addressed in the engineer's report so that they are pre-emptively justified rather than left open to challenge. See article 93 on ABP rates and article 97 on repair cost evidence for additional context on specific cost items that frequently attract insurer challenge.
articles.content: Claims Handler Query 6: Disputed repair costs — staff engineer vs independent engineer. Same rule applies in GTA and non-GTA claims. An independent engineer provides an objective, impartial market assessment of the reasonable retail cost of repair; a staff engineer performs an internal cost-control function for the insurer and lacks independence. Coles v Hetherton [2013] EWCA Civ 1704, paragraphs 28-29, supports the claimant's entitlement to recover the market rate agreed by the independent engineer — the measure is the reasonable cost of repair at the time and place of repair, not the insurer's managed panel rate. Courts would give the independent engineer's report significantly more weight than the staff engineer's assessment due to the latter's institutional conflict of interest. Where the insurer refuses to pay a repair account assessed by an independent engineer, the recommendation is to litigate. The CHO's position is strong: independent assessment, payment made, Coles clearly in point. Protect the position by retaining the engineer's report, repair estimate, invoices, and justification for any specialist items.

articles.title: Claims Handler Query 7: Multiple Vehicles — The Claimant Has Access to Another Car. Does This Prevent Recovery of Credit Hire Charges?
articles.source: Steve Evans, The Credit Hire Forum
articles.author: Steve Evans
principles: CLAIMS HANDLER QUERY 7: The claimant has access to another vehicle. Does this automatically prevent them from recovering credit hire charges? See article 104 for the GTA/non-GTA framework.

GTA AND NON-GTA POSITION — the issue arises in both regimes: Unlike some questions in this series, the multiple-vehicle point is a live issue whether the claim is pursued under the GTA or outside it. The at-fault insurer can ascertain whether a claimant owns more than one vehicle by making enquiries with the claimant's own comprehensive insurer. This is a standard step in insurer claims handling and a common basis for challenging need. The answer in both regimes turns on whether the other vehicle was genuinely available and suitable as a substitute for the damaged car.

The general principle — a usable substitute vehicle defeats need: The cost of credit hire is the measure of damages for loss of use of the damaged vehicle. However, the claimant has a duty to mitigate their loss at common law. If the claimant owned another vehicle that was available to them and suitable for their needs, the starting point is that they should have used it rather than incurring hire charges. A court — and most likely a GTA insurer — will take the view that hiring a replacement vehicle when a suitable alternative was sitting on the driveway represents a failure to mitigate, and hire charges will not be recoverable.

Exception 1 — the other vehicle was not freely available to the claimant: Ownership of another vehicle does not establish availability. Where the other vehicle was in regular use by another family member — a spouse, partner, or adult child — and was not freely available to the claimant as and when they needed it, the existence of that vehicle does not defeat the need for hire. The claimant could not use a car that someone else was dependent on for their own travel needs. The CHO should establish and document who used the other vehicle, for what purpose, and whether that use would have been disrupted or displaced by the claimant taking the vehicle for themselves.

Exception 2 — the other vehicle was unroadworthy or incapable of being driven: If the other vehicle was off the road at the time of the accident — uninsured, untaxed, subject to a SORN declaration, awaiting repair, or mechanically incapable of being driven — it was not in practice available as a substitute. Evidence of the vehicle's status at the material time (DVLA records, insurance records, repair invoices, MOT history) should be obtained and preserved to demonstrate this.

Exception 3 — the other vehicle was not intended for regular everyday use: The existence of a vehicle that is kept for occasional or recreational purposes only does not defeat the need for hire. A high-value luxury car, a performance car, a classic or vintage vehicle, or a specialist vehicle kept for occasional weekend or recreational use is not a functional substitute for the claimant's everyday transport. Such vehicles are typically not insured for daily use, may carry higher excess levels, and represent a disproportionate risk to the owner if used as a daily runaround following an accident. The claimant is not required to put an appreciating classic or a high-value prestige vehicle into regular daily use to avoid hiring a replacement.

Exception 4 — the other vehicle lacked a feature essential to the claimant's needs: Even if the other vehicle was roadworthy, insured and available, it may simply not have been suitable for the purposes served by the damaged vehicle. A two-seater sports car cannot substitute for a four-door family saloon routinely used with child seats, to carry multiple passengers, or to transport family pets or goods. A small city car cannot substitute for a van used in the claimant's trade or business. The question is whether the other vehicle could have met the specific needs that the damaged vehicle was serving — not merely whether it was a driveable vehicle of some description.

Exception 5 — the damaged vehicle was a trade or commercial vehicle: Where the damaged vehicle was used in the claimant's business or trade — a van, a pickup, a licensed hire vehicle, a taxi, a specialist commercial vehicle — the existence of a private car in the claimant's ownership is almost entirely irrelevant. A plumber whose van is damaged cannot be expected to carry tools and materials in a family hatchback. A courier whose commercial vehicle is off the road cannot substitute a personal car for their business use. The CHO should identify at the outset whether the damaged vehicle had a commercial or trade function and, if so, record this clearly in the file so that any challenge based on another vehicle is met immediately.

The CHO's obligation at the point of hire — the need assessment: The multiple-vehicle point is one of the most common and most avoidable sources of exposure for CHOs. At the time of hire, the CHO must always ask the claimant whether they own or have access to any other vehicle, and if so, establish clearly and in writing why that vehicle cannot be used as a substitute for the damaged car. This conversation — and the claimant's explanation — should be documented on the file at the time, not reconstructed months later when the insurer raises the challenge. Failure to conduct this need assessment at the outset places the CHO at serious risk of under-recovering hire charges or failing to recover at all.

How the insurer identifies the issue: The at-fault insurer routinely makes enquiries with the claimant's own comprehensive insurer to identify whether the claimant has other vehicles registered to their policy or household. This is a standard early step in the insurer's claims investigation. The CHO should assume that any vehicle registered to the claimant's household or insurance policy will be identified and its existence used to challenge need. Where the claimant discloses another vehicle at the point of hire and a valid reason is documented, the challenge is easily met. Where no need assessment was carried out, the CHO is exposed.

Documenting the exception at point of hire — practical checklist: The file record should capture: (a) whether the claimant owns or has access to any other vehicle; (b) if yes, the make, model and registration of that vehicle; (c) the reason why it cannot be used as a substitute — who uses it, its condition, its type, its suitability for the claimant's needs; (d) if it is a trade or commercial vehicle claim, the nature of the damaged vehicle's use and why no private car could substitute for it; (e) the claimant's confirmation of the above, ideally in a signed statement or written record. This documentation, captured contemporaneously, is the CHO's primary defence against a need challenge based on the existence of another vehicle.
articles.content: Claims Handler Query 7: Multiple vehicles. Live issue in both GTA and non-GTA claims — insurer will identify other vehicles by enquiring with the claimant's own insurer. General principle: a usable substitute vehicle defeats the need for hire and the duty to mitigate will prevent recovery. Exceptions: (1) the other vehicle was in regular use by another family member and not freely available; (2) the vehicle was unroadworthy, uninsured, SORN'd or mechanically incapable; (3) the vehicle was a luxury, classic or recreational vehicle not intended for everyday use; (4) the vehicle lacked a feature essential to the claimant's needs (2-seater vs family car, small car vs trade van); (5) the damaged vehicle was a commercial or trade vehicle — a private car is an irrelevant substitute. CHO obligation: at the point of hire, always ask whether the claimant owns other vehicles and document the reason why any other vehicle cannot be used as a substitute. Failure to conduct this need assessment at the outset is the primary source of avoidable exposure on multiple-vehicle cases.

articles.title: Claims Handler Query 8: Hire Charges Exceeding Vehicle Value — Is the Insurer Right That This Makes the Claim Irrecoverable?
articles.source: Steve Evans, The Credit Hire Forum
articles.author: Steve Evans
principles: CLAIMS HANDLER QUERY 8: The third-party insurer argues that the hire charges exceed the value of the claimant's damaged vehicle and are therefore irrecoverable as excessive. Is this correct? See article 104 for the GTA/non-GTA framework.

GTA AND NON-GTA POSITION — the same answer applies in both regimes: The argument that hire charges cannot exceed the vehicle's value has no legal foundation, and it applies equally in GTA and non-GTA claims. It is, however, a common tactical position — deployed to prevent settlement in GTA claims and to justify a reduced offer or a defence in litigation outside the GTA. It is an argument that should be resisted directly and confidently.

The argument has no legal basis: The measure of damages for loss of use of a vehicle is the reasonable cost of hiring an equivalent replacement vehicle for the period during which the claimant reasonably needed one, at a reasonable rate. That measure is entirely independent of the value of the damaged vehicle. There is no legal rule, no case authority, and no GTA provision that caps hire charge recovery at the pre-accident value of the damaged car. The value of the vehicle and the cost of replacing its use are separate heads of loss that measure different things: one measures the capital value of the asset, the other measures the loss of the right to use it. A claimant with a low-value vehicle has just as much right to use a vehicle as a claimant with a high-value one.

The argument confuses two distinct heads of loss: The insurer's argument rests on a confusion between the repair or replacement value of the vehicle and the cost of the claimant's loss of use. These are different losses measured differently. A vehicle worth £1,500 might require hire of an equivalent vehicle for three weeks while repairs are completed — hire charges of £1,000 or more are entirely foreseeable and entirely recoverable provided the hire period and rate are reasonable. The fact that the hire charges approach or exceed the vehicle's value is a consequence of the vehicle's low capital value, not evidence that the hire claim is disproportionate or improper.

Hire charges are recoverable for the evidentially supportable period: The test is not whether the total hire bill is proportionate to the vehicle's value. The test is whether the hire was needed, whether the period was reasonable, and whether the rate was reasonable. A hire claim that passes all three of those tests is recoverable in full, regardless of how it compares to the vehicle's value. The CHO and claimant's solicitor should therefore ensure the file demonstrates: (a) need — that the claimant required the vehicle and had no suitable alternative; (b) duration — that the hire period reflects the actual repair or replacement timeline and not any avoidable delay; and (c) rate — that the daily rate charged is consistent with the appropriate BHR comparator evidence for that vehicle type and area.

The true exception — a vehicle that is beyond economic repair but still roadworthy: There is a genuine but narrow exception to recovery, and it is not the one the insurer is arguing. Where a vehicle is damaged to a point where the repair cost exceeds its economic justification but the vehicle remains roadworthy and driveable, the claimant does not need a replacement vehicle during the negotiation of the repair or total loss settlement — because they still have a car they can use. For example: a vehicle valued at £1,000 sustains damage costing £600 to repair. The car is still roadworthy. The claimant has a choice — accept the repair, accept a cash in lieu payment, or negotiate. During that negotiation period, the claimant can continue to drive their own vehicle. Hiring a replacement vehicle while the claimant's own car sits driveable on their driveway is, in those circumstances, a failure to mitigate. Hire charges accrued in that period would not be recoverable.

The exception explained — roadworthy but uneconomic to repair: The distinction is between a vehicle that is undriveable (where hire is clearly necessary) and a vehicle that is driveable but subject to a dispute about whether it is economic to repair. In the latter case, the claimant retains the use of the damaged vehicle until the point at which they surrender it for repair or accept a cash in lieu settlement. Hire only becomes justified — and recoverable — from the point at which the claimant no longer has use of their own vehicle: that is, when it enters a repairer's workshop or is handed over following a total loss or cash in lieu agreement. See article 109 for the cash in lieu position.

The exception does not apply where the vehicle is undriveable: Where the vehicle was rendered undriveable by the accident — whether or not its repair cost exceeds its value — the claimant immediately needs a replacement and is entitled to hire one. A low-value vehicle that is a write-off is not available to the claimant as transport during the settlement process. The hire charges in that period are recoverable in the ordinary way, subject to need, period and rate.

Responding to the insurer's argument in correspondence: Where an insurer raises the vehicle value/hire charges comparison as a reason to resist settlement, the response should: (1) reject the proposition that any such cap exists in law; (2) confirm the three elements of the recoverable claim — need, period, rate — and provide the evidential basis for each; (3) distinguish the genuine exception (driveable vehicle, no immediate need for hire) from the insurer's unfounded proportionality argument; (4) invite the insurer to identify with specificity any part of the claim that does not meet the need/period/rate tests — a general assertion of disproportionality is not a sufficient basis to resist settlement.
articles.content: Claims Handler Query 8: Hire charges exceeding vehicle value. Same answer in GTA and non-GTA — the argument has no legal basis. The measure of damages for loss of use is independent of the vehicle's capital value: it is the reasonable cost of hiring an equivalent vehicle for the period the claimant reasonably needed one, at a reasonable rate. No rule, case or GTA provision caps hire recovery at pre-accident value. The test is need, period and rate — all three must be evidenced. The genuine exception is different and narrower: where the vehicle is beyond economic repair but still roadworthy, the claimant can still drive it during negotiations and does not need a hire car until the vehicle is surrendered for repair or a cash in lieu agreement is made. Hiring while a driveable vehicle sits unused is a failure to mitigate. Where the vehicle is undriveable from the outset, the exception does not apply and hire charges accrue in the ordinary way. Respond to the insurer's proportionality argument by rejecting any legal cap, evidencing need/period/rate, and requiring the insurer to identify specific failings rather than asserting general disproportionality.
