articles.title: Arnold Clark Car & Van Rental — Terms and Conditions of Hire
articles.source: Arnold Clark Car & Van Rental — Terms of Hire, Insurance Guide, and FAQ pages (arnoldclarkrental.com, retrieved 28/03/2026)
articles.author: Arnold Clark Finance Limited
principles: Company identity: Arnold Clark Rental and Arnold Clark Car & Van Rental are trading styles of Arnold Clark Finance Limited, registered in Scotland (No. 039597). VAT Reg. No. GB334331879. Registered office: 454 Hillington Road, Glasgow, G52 4FH. Mailing address: Renault Complex, Kerse Road, Stirling FK7 7RU. Arnold Clark Finance Limited is an appointed representative of Arnold Clark Automobiles Limited, authorised and regulated by the FCA. Website: arnoldclarkrental.com. Tel: 0141 849 7170. Arnold Clark is a large, well-established mainstream car rental operator with branches across the UK and is regularly cited by insurer-appointed BHR experts as a spot hire comparator.

Driver age — minimum age thresholds: Drivers aged 23 or over may hire cars up to and including SUV-type vehicles, automatics and estates. Drivers aged 25 or over may hire all vehicle groups including prestige and luxury categories. Drivers aged 23 or over may hire all van sizes. There is no upper age limit for renting, though certain vehicle group restrictions may apply. A claimant under 23 cannot hire a standard vehicle from Arnold Clark under any circumstances, making Arnold Clark an unsuitable comparator for any claimant under that age threshold.

Young driver surcharge: Drivers aged 23 to 24 incur an additional surcharge of £25 per day. Where an insurer's BHR expert relies on Arnold Clark rates for a claimant aged 23 or 24, the correct comparator cost must include this £25/day surcharge — not the base rate alone. Failing to add this surcharge understates the true Arnold Clark cost for a young driver claimant.

Licence holding requirement: Drivers must hold a valid full driving licence for a minimum of four years. This reduces to one year if the driver is over 23 years of age. Drivers who passed their test within the last year (and are under 23) are ineligible. This is a stricter threshold than some credit hire operators — where a claimant has held their licence for less than four years and is under 23, Arnold Clark is not a valid comparator. Certain endorsed licences may be accepted at the sole discretion of the branch manager — acceptance is not guaranteed and cannot be assumed.

Insurance — coverage included: All Arnold Clark hire rates include VAT and insurance. Coverage extends to: full repairs to the rental vehicle in a road traffic accident (excluding the excess); unlimited third-party liability including passengers in the hired vehicle; and theft of the vehicle and its accessories (excluding the excess). Insurance is for UK mainland use only unless prior clearance is obtained. Cover defaults if basic security precautions are not observed (e.g. keys left in the ignition).

Insurance — the standard excess of £1,500: The standard insurance excess for all cars is £1,500. Arnold Clark reserves the right to increase this excess following each incident. This is a very significant figure in the BHR comparability analysis. Accident replacement CHOs typically provide zero-excess comprehensive cover as part of the credit hire package. A £1,500 excess is not comparable to zero excess — a claimant who would have faced a £1,500 exposure on an Arnold Clark vehicle but had zero excess on a credit hire vehicle is not in an equivalent position. The excess differential must be addressed in any BHR comparison.

Insurance — excess waiver: An optional excess waiver can be purchased from Arnold Clark, subject to qualification. The waiver covers damage to tyres, bodywork, glass and third-party damage. It does not cover: lost or damaged keys, interior damage, damage from hitting low-level objects (bridges, low branches), damage caused by failure to pay due care and attention to road hazards (deep water, debris), off-road recovery costs where no third party is involved, or wrong fuel. Even with the excess waiver, the hirer remains personally responsible for these categories. Where a BHR expert adds an excess waiver cost to Arnold Clark's base rate to achieve comparability with a zero-excess credit hire, the residual unwaivable exposures must also be factored in.

Insurance — hire and reward exclusion: Arnold Clark's insurance expressly does not cover vehicles used for hire and reward. This means taxi drivers, private hire drivers, delivery riders and couriers cannot use Arnold Clark vehicles for their work. Any Arnold Clark-based BHR comparison for a claimant whose damaged vehicle was used for hire and reward is invalid for the purpose of that vehicle's working use.

Insurance — no personal injury cover for the driver: Arnold Clark's insurance does not cover the driver's personal injury or loss of life. The driver's own insurance must cover this. This is a standard exclusion in rental car insurance and is noted here for completeness.

Insurance — own comprehensive cover option: Rate reductions are available for hirers who provide their own fully comprehensive insurance cover for the hire vehicle. Where a claimant's own policy covers hired vehicles, this option reduces the Arnold Clark daily rate. This is relevant to BHR comparisons where an insurer argues the claimant could have used Arnold Clark on their own insurance — the claimant's own policy terms, including whether it extends to hired vehicles and on what excess terms, must be checked.

Mileage policy — cars: For hires up to three days, 250 miles per day are included free of charge; additional mileage is charged at 10p per mile. For hires of four days or more, mileage is unlimited. For longer-term hires of 28 days or more, 2,500 miles are included free of charge, then 10p per mile for excess. A claimant with high daily mileage needs on a short hire would incur additional mileage charges with Arnold Clark that do not arise under a credit hire agreement with unlimited mileage — this affects the true cost comparison.

Mileage policy — vans: For van hires of 24 hours or longer, 250 miles per day are included free, with additional mileage at 10p per mile. For hires of four days or more, mileage is nominally unlimited but capped at 2,500 miles in any 28-day period. This hard cap means Arnold Clark is unsuitable as a van comparator for claimants with high commercial mileage needs.

Geographic restrictions — cars: Cars may only be taken to Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland, and only with prior arrangement and a minimum of one week's notice (additional charges apply). Travel is expressly not permitted to any other European country. Scottish Islands, Isle of Wight, Guernsey, Jersey, Alderney and the Isle of Man are accessible without additional written permission. Where a claimant's vehicle use involved travel outside these permitted areas, Arnold Clark is not a suitable comparator.

Geographic restrictions — vans: Vans are not permitted to be taken beyond the UK mainland under any circumstances. This means no van hire from Arnold Clark can be taken to Northern Ireland, the Republic of Ireland, or any island. For claimants requiring a replacement van for use in Northern Ireland or on islands, Arnold Clark is an entirely unsuitable comparator.

Payment requirements — no cash, physical card required: Arnold Clark does not accept cash at any branch. Payment is by major debit or credit card (not pre-paid cards), PayPal, Apple Pay or Google Pay. Crucially, the hirer must present a physical valid credit or debit card in their own name when collecting the hire vehicle, even if they paid online. A digital-only or pre-paid card is not accepted at collection. A claimant who does not hold a credit or debit card in their own name — a common feature of impecunious claimants — cannot in practice hire from Arnold Clark regardless of the daily rate advertised. This is relevant to impecuniosity arguments and the practical availability of Arnold Clark as a spot hire option.

Deposits: Some branches may request a security deposit of up to £150. For executive car hire, a deposit of £500 via credit or debit card, payable in branch, is required. The requirement for an upfront deposit at an unspecified number of branches is another practical barrier for impecunious claimants.

Luxury car hire — special terms: Luxury car hire is available only at Glasgow and Edinburgh Airport branches. A deposit of £1,000 is required. The damage excess is £3,000, reducible to £1,000 with an excess waiver at £25/day. Minimum rental is 2 days. Additional drivers cost £20/day. These terms make luxury hire from Arnold Clark significantly more onerous than standard hire and directly comparable to a prestige credit hire vehicle only with careful analysis of the total cost including deposit, excess and waiver.

Additional drivers: Additional drivers cost £15 per day per person. A maximum of 5 additional drivers are permitted on any agreement. Additional drivers must bring the card part of their driving licence and their DVLA access code.

Litigation relevance — Arnold Clark as a BHR comparator: Arnold Clark is a high-volume mainstream rental operator and is frequently cited in insurer BHR reports. When challenging an Arnold Clark-based comparator, the following points are most likely to be material: (a) the £1,500 standard excess versus zero excess on credit hire — the excess differential must be priced or acknowledged; (b) the young driver surcharge of £25/day for 23-24 year olds, which must be added to the base rate; (c) the 4-year licence requirement for under-23s; (d) the hire and reward exclusion for taxi, private hire and delivery claimants; (e) the physical card requirement at collection — impecunious claimants who lack a credit or debit card in their own name cannot in practice hire from Arnold Clark; (f) the mileage cap on short hires (250 miles/day for <4 days) affecting high-mileage claimants; (g) the van mileage cap of 2,500 miles in 28 days; and (h) geographic restrictions for van claimants in Northern Ireland or on islands.
articles.content:
Arnold Clark Finance Limited (trading as Arnold Clark Rental / Arnold Clark Car & Van Rental), registered Scotland No. 039597, FCA-regulated appointed representative, headquartered Glasgow. Major mainstream rental operator frequently cited as BHR comparator by insurer experts.

AGE: Min 23 for cars (up to SUV) and all vans; min 25 for all car groups. No upper limit. Young driver surcharge £25/day for ages 23-24.

LICENCE: Minimum 4 years full licence (1 year if over 23). Endorsed licences at branch manager discretion only.

INSURANCE: Included in rate. Standard excess £1,500 for cars (Arnold Clark may increase per incident). Excess waiver available subject to qualification — covers tyres/bodywork/glass/third-party but NOT keys, interior, low-object damage, off-road recovery, wrong fuel. No hire and reward cover. No driver personal injury cover. Own comprehensive insurance option available at reduced rate.

MILEAGE — CARS: 250 miles/day free for <4 days (10p/mile excess); unlimited for 4+ days; 2,500 miles free for 28-day+ hires. VANS: 250 miles/day for 24hr+; 4+ days unlimited but capped at 2,500 miles/28-day period.

GEOGRAPHY: Cars — NI and ROI only with 1 week notice and charges; no other Europe. Vans — UK mainland only, no NI/ROI.

PAYMENT: No cash. Physical credit/debit card (own name) required at collection. Pre-paid cards not accepted. Deposits: up to £150 at some branches; £500 for executive hire; £1,000 for luxury hire.

LITIGATION: Key BHR challenge points — £1,500 excess vs zero excess credit hire; £25/day young driver surcharge not always included in BHR reports; physical card requirement excludes impecunious claimants; hire and reward exclusion; mileage caps on short hires and vans; van geographic restrictions.

articles.title: Foreign National Claimants — Translation, Witness Statements and Access to Justice in Credit Hire Litigation
articles.source: Cathy Wrigglesworth, 'Translation Rules Impede Access to Justice', Law Society Gazette, 17 March 2023; CPR PD32.18; CPR PD1A; Correia v Williams [2022] EWHC 2824 (KB); Aldred v Cham [2019] EWCA Civ 1780
articles.author: Cathy Wrigglesworth, Parkers Solicitors (Stockport)
principles: CPR PD32.18 — the own language and own words requirement: Following the amendment to Civil Procedure Rule PD32 in April 2020, witness statements must (1) if practicable, be in the intended witness's own words; and (2) in any event be drafted in the witness's own language. These are mandatory requirements, not aspirational standards. A witness statement that does not comply exposes the claimant to the risk that it will be disregarded — leaving the claimant without any admissible evidence on which to rely.

Correia v Williams [2022] EWHC 2824 (KB) — the consequences of non-compliance: In Correia v Williams, the claimant was a Portuguese national whose witness statement was drafted in English and then translated into Portuguese. Mr Justice Garnham held that both the witness statement and the statement of truth must be in the witness's own language: 'If the witness statement is not in their own language, there can be no confidence that it is their own evidence rather than the evidence of the drafter.' The statement was found non-compliant with PD32. The claimant was left with no evidence on which he could rely and his claim was dismissed. This is a significant risk for credit hire CHOs whose claimants are foreign nationals — a non-compliant witness statement can result in complete claim failure at trial, regardless of the merits of the underlying hire.

The meaning of 'own language' — complexity in practice: The CPR does not define 'own language'. The concept is more complex than it appears. It may need to be considered carefully for: dual nationals; bilingual individuals; those raised in countries where formal education is delivered in English (including many African nations); those who settled in the UK at a young age and have spoken English for longer than their first language; and those who are conversationally competent in English but not sufficiently fluent for formal legal proceedings. A person may legitimately have two or more 'own languages'. The solicitor managing a credit hire file for a foreign national claimant should assess this at the outset — before drafting the witness statement — and document the assessment.

The practical burden — finding a solicitor who speaks the claimant's language: PD32.18 creates a significant access to justice problem in practice. The requirement to draft a statement in the claimant's own language means either finding a solicitor who speaks that language and practises in the relevant area of law — an increasingly unlikely combination for less common languages — or engaging a professional interpreter to sit with the solicitor and claimant through the entire statement-drafting process. The statement must then be signed by the claimant in their own language, and separately translated into English for the court. This is time-consuming and expensive.

The 'own words' requirement and lawyerly language: Since Correia v Williams, defendants increasingly challenge witness statements on the additional ground that they are not in the 'witness's own words' and include 'lawyerly language'. This criticism applies to all claimants, not only foreign nationals. Solicitors drafting witness statements must take particular care to ensure that the statement reflects the claimant's own account in their own voice — not a legally polished version of the facts. In credit hire cases, this requires the statement to describe the claimant's circumstances, needs and actions in their own terms, not in the language of a mitigation analysis.

Aldred v Cham [2019] EWCA Civ 1780 — translation costs historically not recoverable: Aldred v Cham is the Court of Appeal authority for the proposition that the costs of translation and interpretation are not recoverable from a paying party. This created a severe access to justice problem: the claimant's solicitors were obliged by PD32 to incur translation costs, but could not recover those costs from the defendant even if the claim succeeded. Interpretation and translation fees can run into thousands of pounds — on fixed costs cases involving small personal injury awards (potentially as low as £240 under the post-2021 whiplash reforms), those costs could extinguish the entire value of the award.

The updated position — courts now allow recovery of reasonable translation costs: Notwithstanding Aldred v Cham, courts have moved toward allowing the recovery of reasonable translation and interpretation costs where these are genuinely necessary to ensure the claimant can participate fully in proceedings and give their best evidence. Where translation costs are properly evidenced, reasonably incurred and necessary for compliance with PD32.18, they should be claimed and are increasingly awarded. The CHO and solicitor should ensure that: (a) the need for translation is identified and documented at the outset; (b) translation costs are properly invoiced and justified; and (c) the claim for those costs is included in any costs schedule or bill of costs.

CPR PD1A — vulnerable parties and the equal footing principle: PD1A was introduced in April 2021, a year after PD32 was amended. It requires courts to ensure that vulnerable parties 'are on an equal footing and can participate fully in proceedings... and can give their best evidence.' One of the factors identified as potentially causing vulnerability is communication or language difficulty. This provision creates a tension with the position in Aldred v Cham: a claimant whose own language is not English is plainly not 'on an equal footing' with an English-speaking party if they must bear non-recoverable translation costs to comply with PD32 while their English-speaking opponent bears no equivalent burden. PD1A supports the argument that translation costs must be recoverable as a matter of principle.

Article 6 ECHR — the right to a fair trial: The combined effect of PD32.18, Correia v Williams and Aldred v Cham arguably engages Article 6(1) of the Human Rights Act 1998 — the right to a fair trial. A regime that requires a non-English-speaking claimant to comply with procedural obligations that impose significant irrecoverable costs, and that dismisses claims where those obligations are not met, may amount to a denial of access to justice for those claimants. This argument, while not yet authoritatively resolved, provides an additional basis for arguing that translation costs should be treated as recoverable.

Practical guidance for CHOs and solicitors — managing foreign national claimants: (1) Identify the language issue at intake: when a claimant is a foreign national or has English as a second language, assess their linguistic competence and document the assessment. Do not assume that conversational English is sufficient for witness statement purposes — Correia shows the danger. (2) Instruct a professional interpreter at statement stage: engage a qualified interpreter (not a friend or family member) to attend the statement-drafting session. Ensure the statement is drafted in the claimant's own language and signed by the claimant in that language. (3) Obtain a certified English translation for the court. (4) Keep all interpreter and translation invoices and document why the costs were necessary. (5) Include translation and interpretation costs in the bill of costs or costs schedule — the trend is toward recovery where properly evidenced. (6) Be alert to the 'lawyerly language' challenge on statements — all witness statements, not only those for foreign nationals, should be drafted in a style that reflects the claimant's own voice.
articles.content: Foreign national claimants in credit hire litigation face specific procedural risks under CPR PD32.18 (amended April 2020), which requires witness statements to be in the witness's own words and own language. In Correia v Williams [2022] EWHC 2824 (KB), a Portuguese national's claim was dismissed because his statement was drafted in English rather than Portuguese — leaving him without any admissible evidence. Aldred v Cham [2019] EWCA Civ 1780 historically held that translation and interpretation costs were not recoverable from the paying party, creating an access to justice problem where costs could exceed the value of the award. However, courts now allow recovery of reasonable translation costs where properly evidenced and necessary for compliance with PD32.18. CPR PD1A (April 2021) requires vulnerable parties (including those with language difficulties) to be on an equal footing, which supports cost recovery. Article 6 HRA 1998 (fair trial) may also be engaged. Practical steps: identify language issues at intake; instruct professional interpreters at statement stage; draft in own language; certify English translation; retain all invoices; include costs in the bill.

articles.title: Cogent Hire — Industry Profile: An Insurer-Collaborative CHO Model and Its Significance
articles.source: Kirsty McKno interview, Auto Body Professional (ABP) magazine, Spring 2022
articles.author: Auto Body Professional (ABP) / Kirsty McKno, Cogent Hire
principles: Cogent Hire — company background: Cogent Hire was launched in 2021 by Kirsty McKno, a solicitor and former chair of the Credit Hire Organisation (CHO), the credit hire industry's trade body, where she served for four years. Cogent Hire is part of handl Group, described as a leading UK claims services provider. handl Group set the goal of building a new type of credit hire company — one that worked collaboratively with all stakeholders, including insurers, rather than treating the paying party as an adversary. McKno was attracted to the venture precisely because her years at the CHO gave her deep relationships with credit hire companies, insurers and supply chain partners, and she saw the opportunity to build a CHO without legacy practices that create friction.

The stated model — treating the insurer as a customer: Cogent Hire's founding principle is that all stakeholders are customers — not only the claimant needing mobility and the referrer of work, but also the paying party (the at-fault insurer). This is explicitly stated as a departure from industry norms. The business model is built on the proposition that reducing friction for insurers — in terms of transparency, process and pricing — produces better outcomes for all parties. Cogent describes its approach as 'collaborative and transparent', with supply chain partners working as extensions of the Cogent team rather than independent operators. Their first major contract win was with a major insurer for non-fault work, which McKno describes as a validation of the collaborative proposition.

Key vehicle supply partnership — Europcar: Cogent Hire uses Europcar as its credit hire vehicle provider. This is directly relevant to BHR comparability analysis. Where Cogent Hire is the CHO on a claim and the BHR expert or insurer seeks to compare the Cogent credit hire rate against a spot hire rate, the fact that Cogent's own vehicle provision is through Europcar — a mainstream supplier frequently used in BHR reports — is a material point. Europcar's own terms and conditions (including age restrictions, excess levels, mileage allowances and use restrictions) apply to the supply side of Cogent's operation.

Technology and process infrastructure: Cogent operates a bespoke case management system with customer access and portal-based tools with API connectivity. This removes the need to instruct, monitor and off-hire vehicles through manual processes. Key technology partner is Quantum Technical, who developed tools for vehicle imaging, estimating, assessment and booking into repairers. The stated objective is that repairers feel like an extension of the Cogent team rather than external contractors. For total loss handling, Cogent partners with Hills Salvage and Recycling.

Repair network model — no captive network: Cogent does not operate its own repair network. Instead it aligns with existing networks that have a strong focus on customer service and which have been put through rigorous testing. Payment terms for approved invoices are less than seven days — a meaningful differentiator given the payment delays that have historically characterised insurer-managed repair networks. Repair pricing is developed collaboratively with repairers, without discount and including payment terms, which McKno says reflects 'the costs and daily difficulties they face'. This model mirrors the ABP (Auto Body Professionals) approach to retail-rate pricing (see article 93) rather than the managed-rate discount model used by insurer repair panels.

EV repair preparedness: Cogent has invested significantly in understanding EV repair, working with Fix Auto Blackburn to understand the differences between ICE and EV repair. McKno flags this as identifying 'a wider industry issue relating to the lack of preparedness for EV' and states Cogent is collaborating with industry leaders on this. This is relevant context for credit hire cases involving electric vehicles, where the repair period may be longer and the repair costs higher than for equivalent ICE vehicles, supporting hire charges that extend beyond what an insurer might argue is reasonable for a conventional repair.

Significance for the CHO landscape — what Cogent represents: Cogent Hire represents one commercially viable response to the pressure that aggressive insurer litigation has placed on traditional CHO models. Whether its collaborative approach produces genuinely better outcomes for claimants — or simply makes it easier for insurers to manage and limit claims — is a matter on which the claimant credit hire community is sceptical. Critics would say that a CHO whose primary selling point to insurers is that it will not fight as hard as its competitors is not serving claimants' interests as effectively as it might. The involvement of handl Group as parent and Europcar as vehicle supplier situates Cogent firmly within the insurer and mainstream hire ecosystem rather than the independent credit hire market. Its significance lies in illustrating that the CHO sector contains a spectrum of approaches — from highly adversarial to explicitly collaborative — and that the model adopted by the CHO has direct implications for how aggressively claims are pursued and settled.

Significance for the CHO landscape — what Cogent represents: Cogent Hire represents a self-conscious attempt to reposition credit hire as a service that works with insurers rather than against them. Whether this model is genuinely different in outcome — in terms of hire rates charged, periods claimed and settlement approaches — or represents primarily a difference in tone and process, remains to be assessed over time. However, its existence and growing volume (McKno reports volumes growing week by week by Spring 2022, following an initial insurer contract win) reflects an acknowledgment within the credit hire industry that the adversarial model has driven insurers to litigate aggressively and that a more collaborative positioning may be commercially sustainable. The involvement of handl Group — an established claims services provider with insurer relationships — as the parent organisation adds credibility to the proposition.

Kirsty McKno — credentials, limitations and partisan positioning: Kirsty McKno is a solicitor with genuine expertise in credit hire, and her four years as chair of the Credit Hire Organisation gave her a broad understanding of the GTA and the industry's operational landscape. However, her explicit alignment with insurers as customers — and Cogent Hire's business model of positioning itself as the CHO that insurers prefer — means she is widely regarded within the claimant credit hire community as a partisan figure rather than an objective voice. Her views on industry practice reflect an insurer-friendly perspective and should be read in that context. The fact that Cogent's founding proposition is built on being acceptable to insurers rather than maximising recovery for claimants means that its model and McKno's commentary on credit hire should not be treated as a neutral or authoritative account of how the sector operates or should operate. Her opinions carry weight as a description of one commercially viable positioning within the CHO market, but they should not be overstated as representing industry consensus or the interests of claimant-side practitioners.
articles.content: Cogent Hire was launched in 2021 by Kirsty McKno (solicitor, former chair of the Credit Hire Organisation trade body) as part of handl Group. Its founding proposition is that all stakeholders — claimants, referrers and paying insurers — are customers, and that reducing friction through transparency and collaboration produces better outcomes. Key features: vehicle supply through Europcar (directly relevant to BHR comparability); bespoke case management with portal/API tools; repair network built on existing accredited networks without captive panel or volume discounts; payment of repair invoices in under 7 days; EV repair expertise developed with Fix Auto Blackburn; total loss handled via Hills Salvage and Recycling. First major contract was with a major insurer for non-fault work. Cogent represents a deliberate attempt within the CHO sector to reposition credit hire as insurer-collaborative rather than adversarial. The use of Europcar as vehicle supplier means Europcar's terms, eligibility criteria and rate structures are directly relevant to assessing Cogent's product comparability.

articles.title: The Damages Claims Portal (DCP) — PD51ZB, Procedure and Practical Operation for Credit Hire Claims
articles.source: Practice Direction 51ZB — The Damages Claims Pilot (as amended to 21 March 2024); Davies Group, 'Damages Claims Portal Pilot Scheme: An Overview' (September 2021)
articles.author: HMCTS / Ministry of Justice
principles: Background and status — from voluntary pilot to compulsory system: The Damages Claims Portal (DCP) was launched on 28 May 2021 as a voluntary pilot under Practice Direction 51ZB. The pilot was originally scheduled to run to 1 October 2026. It was initially accessible to 13 claimant firms: Admiral Law, Bakers, Charltons, Clarke Willmott, Express Solicitors, Hodge Jones & Allen, Irwin Mitchell, Lyons Davidson, Minster Law, Scott Rees, Slater & Gordon, Trav Law and Winn Solicitors. The DCP has since expanded significantly and is now the basis on which credit hire claims are routinely litigated. What began as an opt-in pilot is now effectively compulsory for eligible claims handled by registered firms. The system operates through HMCTS's MyHMCTS platform, with the Claims Number Business Centre (CNBC) as the administrative hub for claims that leave or are excluded from the portal.

Scope and eligibility — what can and cannot enter the DCP: PD51ZB sets out 14 conditions that must all be met for a claim to be started in the DCP. The key conditions are: the claim must seek damages only (not a Part 8 claim); the claimant must be legally represented by a registered DCP legal representative; the claimant must be aged 18 or over and not a protected party; proceedings must be conducted in English; there can be no more than two claimants and no more than two defendants; the court fee must have been paid; and any conditions specified in the DCP Order must be met. Claims for injunctive or declaratory relief, Part 8 proceedings, claims involving protected parties or unrepresented claimants, and proceedings not in English are all outside scope and must be issued through CNBC under CPR Part 7 as normal.

Starting a claim — issue and the notification longstop date: A claimant starts a DCP claim by completing the relevant screens and submitting the claim through the DCP system. The DCP generates the claim form. The single most critical deadline in the DCP is the notification longstop date: the claimant must notify the defendant of the claim and serve the claim form within 4 months of the date of issue. If the claimant fails to notify the defendant of the claim before the notification longstop date, the claim will be automatically dismissed without any further order — there is no court intervention and no warning. Any extension of time agreed by the parties before the longstop date causes the claim to leave the DCP and be transferred to the CNBC. This is a known hazard for CHOs: if a defendant agrees to extend time, the claim immediately exits the portal and the practitioners must manage the case under CPR Part 7 at the CNBC.

Notifying the defendant — Section 3 process: Once the claim is issued, the claimant must notify the defendant within the DCP. Where the defendant is represented by a DCP legal representative, notification is via the portal and the defendant's response process is conducted entirely online. Where the defendant is not represented by a DCP legal representative, the claimant must also serve a paper version of the claim form and use the DCP to notify that defendant within 14 days of service of the claim form. Notification of service of the claim form must reach the court before 4pm on the 14th day after service, or before the notification longstop date, whichever is earlier. If the defendant notifies the court on paper within 14 days of service of the claim details, the court must transfer the claim out of the DCP to the CNBC to continue as a CPR Part 7 claim. If the defendant does not notify the court within 14 days and gives no notice of representation, the claim transfers out to the CNBC in any event and continues as a Part 7 claim.

Defendant's acknowledgement — Section 5: The defendant may acknowledge the claim after being notified of the details of the claim. Acknowledgement must be given through the DCP. Acknowledgement is mandatory if the defendant wishes to challenge the court's jurisdiction, in which case the defendant must then apply to challenge jurisdiction within 14 days of acknowledging — upon which the claim transfers to the CNBC to continue under CPR Part 7. The deadline for acknowledgement is 4pm on the 14th day after notification of the details of the claim.

Defendant's response — Section 6 and default judgment: The defendant must respond to the claim by the time stated on the DCP. Response times are: (a) 4pm on the 14th day after notification of details of the claim if the defendant has not acknowledged and no alternative date has been set; (b) 4pm on the 28th day if the defendant has acknowledged under Section 5; or (c) such other date agreed by the parties (one agreed extension of up to 28 days is permitted, to be recorded on the DCP before the existing deadline expires) or ordered by the court. If the defendant admits all or part of the claim, or wishes to make a counterclaim, the claim automatically transfers to the CNBC. If defending in full, the defendant submits the defence details and directions questionnaire through the DCP. If the defendant does not respond by the required time, the claimant may request default judgment through the DCP. If the defendant does not respond within 6 months of notification of the details of the claim AND the claimant has not requested judgment or made an application, the claim is automatically dismissed.

Automatic dismissal — the twin dangers for CHOs: There are two automatic dismissal provisions that require careful diary management. First, under Section 4, if the claimant fails to notify the defendant of the claim before the notification longstop date (4 months from issue), the claim is dismissed automatically without any court order. Second, under Section 6.7, if 6 months pass from notification of the claim details to the defendant without the defendant responding and without the claimant requesting default judgment or making an application, the claim is again automatically dismissed. Once a claim is automatically dismissed, it leaves the DCP entirely and any application to reinstate must be made under CPR Part 23 on paper — which means a contested application to set aside dismissal, with adverse costs risk. The DCP does not give warnings before automatic dismissal occurs.

Initial case management — Section 7: Once the defendant has filed a defence, the claimant must indicate whether they wish to continue with the claim and, if so, provide hearing information through the DCP (including proposed track: fast, intermediate or multi-track) before 4pm on the 28th day after notification of the defendant's response. If the claimant fails to do this within the time limit, the claim automatically transfers to a County Court Hearing Centre and is treated as if the claimant had failed to comply with a CPR 26.4(1) notice — an order is then made under CPR 26.4(9). CPR 26.6 does not apply to DCP claims. For claims where cost budgeting applies under CPR Part 3 Section II and PD3D, cost budgets must be filed and exchanged not later than 21 days before the first case management conference. The DCP automatically creates a trial bundle for fast-track claims — a separate trial bundle only needs to be lodged if the parties consider it necessary given the automatic creation.

Trial readiness and pre-trial process: For fast-track claims, within 2 weeks of a court notice via the DCP, parties must submit a trial readiness certificate through the DCP indicating readiness for trial and updating hearing requirements. After all certificates are submitted, the court fixes the trial date, gives directions and specifies further steps — with at least 3 weeks' notice of the trial date unless exceptional circumstances apply. A judge may additionally direct a pre-trial checklist (listing questionnaire). For intermediate-track and multi-track claims, a judge may direct the parties to upload a pre-trial checklist (Form N170) to the DCP. The automatic creation of the trial bundle by the DCP is a specific DCP feature that modifies the standard CPR provisions about trial bundle lodging.

Settlement, discontinuance and transfer out: If the claim settles, the claimant uses the DCP to notify the court, which stays the proceedings. The defendant is then given 19 days to object. If the defendant objects, the claim transfers to the CNBC (or County Court hearing centre if applicable). Settlement notification is only valid if all matters are resolved including costs — if costs remain disputed, a separate application is needed to lift the stay. For discontinuance, the notice of discontinuance is filed at the CNBC (not through the DCP itself in most cases), which causes the claim to transfer out. A claim may be transferred out of the DCP at any time: by court order on application (Section 7A), automatically on various triggers (admission, counterclaim, jurisdiction challenge, failure to respond, failed case management steps) or where the court concludes the claim is no longer suitable for the DCP. When transferred out, the claim continues at the CNBC as if started under CPR Part 7, with any DCP directions questionnaire treated as a completed N180.

Applications for court orders — online and on-paper routes: Where all parties are represented, applications must ordinarily be made through the DCP as online applications. The online application is treated as an application notice under CPR Part 23 and the respondent is notified through the DCP immediately. The respondent must provide 'respondent information' by 4pm on the 5th day after notification. Legal advisers may exercise county court jurisdiction on online applications made before the claimant has provided hearing information; a decision by a legal adviser may be challenged by requesting reconsideration within 7 days through the DCP. Certain applications must always be made on paper to the CNBC: pre-issue applications; applications involving appeal, enforcement or insolvency; applications where the respondent is not already a party; and (in specific circumstances) applications for default judgment where a Debt Respite Scheme moratorium is in place.

Change of legal representation — a hidden transfer-out trigger: Change of solicitor is a frequently overlooked cause of cases leaving the DCP. Under Section 8A, if a new solicitor is not registered with MyHMCTS and does not have DCP access, the claim must transfer out of the DCP to the CNBC to continue as a Part 7 claim. CHOs should ensure that any new solicitors instructed mid-claim — whether through referral, firm merger or direct instruction — hold current DCP access credentials. A failure to check this can result in a case unexpectedly leaving the portal with attendant procedural disruption.

Document submission, timing and the 4pm rule: Under Section 10, documents submitted to the DCP are treated as received when recorded as received by the DCP. Documents received before 4pm are treated as submitted that day. Documents received after 4pm but before 11:59pm are treated as submitted before 4pm on the next court working day. The responsibility for timely submission rests on the submitting party — but where the DCP malfunctions and prevents timely upload, a judge must give directions to extend the time limit, and if the party retries as soon as practicable after a malfunction, they are treated as having submitted on time. This provision acknowledges the known technical fragility of the system.

Practical limitations — IT lagging operational demands: The DCP has been, since its launch, hampered by the gap between the operational demands of practitioners compelled to use it and the pace of IT development by HMCTS. Features expected at launch were delayed; workflows that existed in the paper/CNBC environment have not always been replicated digitally; and technical outages and malfunctions have caused procedural difficulties for practitioners. The automatic dismissal provisions (Section 4 and Section 6.7) are particularly unforgiving in a system that can malfunction: a portal outage at a critical deadline can result in claim dismissal if the party cannot demonstrate that it attempted timely submission. CHOs and their solicitors should maintain contemporaneous records of all DCP interactions, including upload attempts, timestamps and any error messages, to support any extension application necessitated by system failure.
articles.content: The Damages Claims Portal (DCP) is an online claims management system launched on 28 May 2021 under Practice Direction 51ZB as a pilot (to 1 October 2026) for County Court damages claims. Originally voluntary and limited to 13 claimant firms, it is now effectively the compulsory route for eligible credit hire claims. Claims must meet 14 conditions including: damages only, legally represented claimant aged 18+, not a protected party, English proceedings, maximum two claimants/two defendants, fee paid. The system operates through MyHMCTS with the CNBC as the fallback for cases that leave the portal. Critical timelines: (1) notification longstop date — 4 months from issue to notify defendant, or automatic dismissal; (2) 14 days to serve claim form on defendant; (3) 14 days for defendant acknowledgement after notification of details; (4) 28 days for defendant response if acknowledged (14 if not); (5) claimant must provide hearing information within 28 days of notification of defence; (6) if defendant doesn't respond within 6 months of notification of details and claimant has not acted, automatic dismissal. Many triggers cause transfer out to CNBC including: admission, counterclaim, jurisdiction challenge, agreed extensions of time, paper notifications from unrepresented defendants, change to solicitor without DCP access, and court order. Settlement notified via DCP with 19-day defendant objection window; discontinuance via CNBC. Applications between represented parties must be made online through the DCP. Documents received before 4pm treated as submitted that day; after 4pm treated as next court day. Party bears responsibility for timely submission but is protected where DCP malfunction prevents upload if retried promptly. The system has been consistently hampered by IT development lagging operational demands.

articles.title: Credit Hire Pleading and Directions — PD16, CPRC Model Order and the DCP Bastardisation Problem
articles.source: Practice Direction 16 — Statements of Case (CPR PD16), para 6.3–6.4; Civil Procedure Rule Committee, 'Model Order for Directions to be used in all Credit Hire Cases' (Consultation, June–August 2017); DCP fast track directions order (DJ Solomon, County Court, 14 June 2023, case 080DC540); Credit Hire Forum, DCP Directions circular note (22 October 2025); Tescher v DAML [para 91]
articles.author: HMCTS / Civil Procedure Rule Committee / Stephen Evans (Credit Hire Forum)
principles: PD16 para 6.3 — what must be pleaded in credit hire particulars of claim: Practice Direction 16 paragraph 6.3 sets out the mandatory pleading requirements where a claim includes the cost of hire of a replacement motor vehicle following a road traffic accident. The claimant must state in the particulars of claim: (1) the need for the replacement vehicle at the relevant time; (2) the period of hire claimed (providing the start and end of the period); (3) the rate of hire claimed; (4) the reasonableness of the period and rate of hire; and (5) if the claim relates to credit hire, whether the claimant could afford to pay in advance to hire a replacement car, and if not, why not ('impecuniosity'). The obligation under (1) to state need is an ongoing one: paragraph 6.4 confirms that 'relevant time' means at the start of the hire and throughout the period of hire, and that the obligation to state the matters in paragraph (3) [reasonableness] includes an obligation to state relevant facts.

PD16 para 6.3 — practical significance for CHOs and their solicitors: Each element of PD16 para 6.3 maps to a discrete legal issue that will be contested at trial. The pleading on need must address both initial need (was a replacement vehicle required at all?) and continuing need (was the claimant's need present throughout the full hire period?). The pleading on impecuniosity must go beyond a bare assertion: it should identify the claimant's financial position and explain why they could not afford spot hire. A bare or formulaic assertion of impecuniosity in the particulars of claim without supporting particulars is vulnerable to a direction requiring better particulars or to a finding at trial that the issue was not properly raised. CHOs should ensure their solicitors' standard particulars template covers all five elements.

The CPRC 2017 model order — origin and intention: In June 2017, the Civil Procedure Rule Committee (through its Sub-Committee on Model Orders, chaired by HH Judge McKenna) consulted on a model order for directions to be used in all credit hire cases. The CPRC identified that the existing draft directions were a cause of avoidable contested directions hearings and proposed consolidated directions to be used by the judiciary across all tracks. The preference was for credit hire provisions on disclosure and witness evidence to be treated as distinct steps from the rest of the directions. The CPRC emphasised that the directions were intended to encourage proportionate and efficient resolution of litigation — not to be used as tactical staging posts — and that it would be particularly attracted to wording reached through consensus by practitioners on both sides.

The CPRC 2017 model order — content of the draft directions: The draft credit hire directions produced by the 2017 CPRC consultation established a three-stage framework that became the template for credit hire case management. Stage 1 — the claimant witness statement: the claimant must serve a witness statement addressing (a) need for a replacement vehicle and (b) if relied upon, impecuniosity — within 14 days of the order. Stage 2 — impecuniosity financial disclosure: if impecuniosity is alleged and not admitted by the defendant, the claimant's disclosure must include (i) evidence of all income from all sources for 3 months prior to the commencement of hire to the earlier of 3 months after cessation of hire or repair/replacement; (ii) copy statements of all bank, credit card and savings accounts for the same period; and (iii) evidence of any loan, overdraft or other credit facilities available to the claimant during the same period — all within 14 days of service of the order. Failure to comply debarred the claimant from asserting impecuniosity at the final hearing, save with permission of the trial judge. Stage 3 — BHR evidence: the parties must liaise and use reasonable endeavours to agree the basic hire rate within 28 days of the order; if they fail to agree, each party may instruct a single witness to provide evidence of BHR available within the claimant's geographical location from a mainstream supplier (or local reputable supplier if none available), with the defendant's evidence served first and the claimant's reply within the timeframes specified.

The DCP standard order — what the centralised DCP directions look like in practice: An example DCP fast track order made by DJ Solomon on 14 June 2023 (County Court, case 080DC540) illustrates the directions that emerged from the DCP environment. The order broadly mirrors the CPRC model directions in its credit hire provisions (paragraphs 22–27) but embeds them within a generic DCP case management order covering: standard disclosure (list upload); inspection requests; trial documents upload; witnesses of fact (limited to 3 per party, max 10 A4 pages per statement, double-spaced, font 12); schedule and counter-schedule of loss; ADR engagement; trial window and time estimate; and trial bundle. The credit hire specific provisions include the same impecuniosity disclosure requirements as the CPRC model (income, bank statements, credit facilities — 3 months before hire to 3 months after cessation) with automatic debarment for non-compliance, plus the BHR agreement/single-witness evidence structure. Additionally, the order requires photographs of the accident location to be uploaded to the Digital Portal.

The DCP bastardisation problem — how the portal has undermined tailored credit hire directions: The DCP has introduced a centralised, formulaic approach to case management that has effectively bastardised the carefully negotiated credit hire standard directions. Pre-DCP, district judges with credit hire experience could adapt directions to the specific issues in dispute and apply the settled model in a flexible way; contested directions hearings allowed practitioners to argue for directions tailored to the actual issues. Under the DCP, directions are generated by the system on a standardised template following the submission of hearing information and directions questionnaires by the parties. The resulting orders vary in quality, sometimes omit credit hire specific provisions, sometimes duplicate them within the same order in inconsistent ways, and are made without hearing — removing the opportunity for practitioners to correct deficiencies or challenge inappropriate directions before they take effect. The Civil Procedure Rules Committee has acknowledged that work is needed on the standard directions for DCP credit hire cases, but as at March 2026 has given no indication of when that work will be completed.

The October 2025 Willesden example — judicial overreach in DCP directions: In October 2025, a Deputy District Judge at Willesden County Court issued directions in a DCP credit hire case that went beyond any recognised model and were made without any application or request from the defendant and without any basis in the pleadings. The judge added to paragraph 9 (witnesses of fact) a direction requiring the claimant's evidence to include a witness statement from a partner of the firm of the claimant's solicitors confirming: (a) what relationship, if any, existed between the claimant's solicitors' firm and the vehicle hire firm, including details of any shared ownership; (b) if applicable, what was disclosed to the claimant about that relationship and when; and (c) a breakdown of the daily hire charge including the basic hire rate and the amount charged for credit. Directions (a) and (b) constitute judicial overreach — they impose an obligation to investigate and disclose proprietary and structural information about the solicitors' own firm that was not pleaded, not in dispute, and not within the proper scope of case management directions. The defendant had not sought such an order and there was no basis in the statements of case for it.

Why direction (c) is intellectually dishonest and legally inarticulate: The direction requiring the claimant's solicitor to provide 'a breakdown of the daily hire charge including the basic hire rate and the amount charged for credit' is intellectually dishonest and legally inarticulate for two reasons. First, the order already contains the standard BHR evidence exchange directions — requiring the parties to liaise and agree the BHR, and if they fail, each party to provide a BHR witness statement. Adding a separate requirement for the claimant's solicitor to define or agree the BHR undermines the entire BHR exchange process and attempts to place the burden of establishing BHR on the claimant's solicitor, when it is the defendant who bears the burden of proving that the credit hire rate exceeded the BHR. Second, the concept of 'the amount charged for credit' has no settled legal meaning in credit hire: the credit element of a CHR is the difference between the CHR and the BHR, and the BHR is not agreed — it is precisely what the direction purports to require the solicitor to define. The direction is circular and unworkable.

The NPCO fishing concern — Tescher para 91 as the corrective: The October 2025 Willesden direction appears to have been intended to generate evidence that could support a future non-party costs order (NPCO) against the CHO or the solicitors' firm, by establishing a connection between them and identifying the profit element built into the credit hire rate. The credit hire forum analysis suggested the judge may have been trying to 'open the door for an NPCO later.' However, the judgment in Tescher v DAML addresses this directly. At paragraph 91, the court stated: 'A notable feature of the two cases is that in each of them there is an alleged link (to use a neutral term) between the credit hire company and the solicitors who represented the claimant in the original claim. DAML is in the same group as Bond Turner, while the directors of Spectra and of the company of which DGM was a trading name, were cousins. I mention this to make the point that I have not taken it into account. The non-party costs in the circumstances described above would be the likely result regardless, and so there should be no need for evidence of this kind to be given to the court deciding on the non-party costs order.' This passage directly counters the Willesden direction: the connection between a CHO and its associated solicitors' firm is not relevant to an NPCO determination and there is no need for evidence of it.

Practical guidance — managing DCP directions orders: Because DCP orders are made without hearing, practitioners receive the order after it has been made and have limited time to apply for variation. The DCP Directions order (DJ Solomon, para 29) specified that any application to vary must be received by the court within 7 days of the order. CHOs and their solicitors should therefore: (1) check every DCP directions order immediately upon receipt for non-standard or unusual directions that go beyond the settled model; (2) if the order contains directions that are unprecedented, go beyond the pleadings, or impose obligations on the solicitors as distinct from the parties, apply promptly to vary before the deadline expires; (3) specifically, if a direction purports to require the claimant's solicitors to give evidence about their firm's corporate structure, ownership, or relationship with the CHO, that direction should be challenged on the basis that it is outside the scope of case management, not grounded in the pleadings, and inconsistent with Tescher para 91. The absence of an active application for the direction is a strong ground for challenge.
articles.content: Practice Direction 16 paragraph 6.3 (CPR PD16) requires that particulars of claim in a credit hire case must state: the need for a replacement vehicle at the relevant time (at the start and throughout the hire — PD16 para 6.4); the period and rate of hire claimed; the reasonableness of the period and rate; and (for credit hire) whether the claimant could afford to pay in advance (impecuniosity). In 2017 the CPRC consulted on a model order for directions in all credit hire cases, producing a three-stage framework: (1) claimant witness statement on need and impecuniosity within 14 days; (2) if impecuniosity alleged and not admitted — 3-month income, bank statements and credit facilities disclosure, with automatic debarment for non-compliance; (3) parties liaise to agree BHR within 28 days; if not agreed, single witness per side with defendant's evidence first. This model was adopted in DCP orders (see DJ Solomon, 14 June 2023), embedded in a generic DCP template covering disclosure, witnesses (max 3 per party, max 10 pages), schedule of loss, ADR, trial window and bundle. However, the DCP's centralised approach has bastardised the tailored credit hire directions: orders are made without hearing, vary in quality, and cannot be corrected before they take effect. The CPRC has acknowledged work is needed but given no timeline (as at March 2026). In October 2025 a DDJ at Willesden issued a direction requiring a partner of the claimant's solicitors to file a witness statement about any ownership link between the solicitors' firm and the CHO — unprecedented judicial overreach, not sought by the defendant, not grounded in the pleadings. Tescher v DAML para 91 directly addresses this: the CHO/solicitor link is not relevant to NPCO and there is no need for evidence of it to be given to the court. Direction (c) (breakdown of daily hire charge including 'amount charged for credit') was also legally inarticulate: the standard BHR evidence exchange already in the order covered this, and the concept of 'amount charged for credit' has no settled legal meaning. Any DCP order containing non-standard directions must be challenged promptly within the variation deadline specified in the order.

articles.title: County Court Delays and Dysfunction — HC 677 and the Crisis in Civil Justice
articles.subcategory: Court Delays and Access to Justice
articles.source: HC 677 — House of Commons Justice Committee, 'Work of the County Court', Fourth Report of Session 2024-25, published 21 July 2025
articles.author: House of Commons Justice Committee (Chair: Andy Slaughter MP)
principles: 1. THE COMMITTEE'S OVERALL VERDICT — 'CINDERELLA SERVICE': The Justice Committee concluded that the County Court is the 'Cinderella service' of the justice system in England and Wales (Conclusion, para 157). It is beset by delays resulting from a failed attempt at digital reform, a recruitment and retention crisis in the judiciary and court staff, and a complex dysfunctional 'patchwork' of outdated paper-based and digital systems. The causes of these inefficiencies are well-established and openly recognised by Ministers and officials, yet there has been no credible plan to address them. The Committee found that the situation would be materially worse but for the highly commendable efforts of frontline court staff operating a system that ultimately fails to deliver civil justice (Conclusion, para 158). The recommendation is for an urgent, comprehensive root-and-branch review launched by Spring 2026 — concluding that it is 'not tenable to continue without fundamental reform' (Recommendation, para 159).

2. SCALE OF DELAYS — THE STATISTICAL PICTURE: The statistics are stark. In 2024 the average time from issue to trial in the small claims track was 50.7 weeks, up from 31.6 weeks in 2015 — a 60% deterioration over a decade. For fast track, intermediate track and multi-track cases, the average in 2024 was 79.4 weeks (up from 54.3 weeks in 2015 — a 46% increase). By Q1 2025 these figures had improved marginally to 49.8 weeks (small claims) and 74.7 weeks (FT/IT/MT). The Committee heard that these headline figures mask much worse experiences in specific courts, particularly London and the South East where delays are structurally worse. Central London County Court and the CNBC are identified as particular hotspots, and 8 of the bottom 20 courts for timeliness are in London and the South East.

3. SCALE OF DELAYS — EMPIRICAL EVIDENCE FROM CREDIT HIRE LITIGATION: The most compelling empirical evidence before the Committee came from Annexo Group plc, a credit hire company that submitted data from its own litigation portfolio. Annexo reported an average of 516 days from issue to trial excluding adjournments, rising to 786 days including adjournments. The worst individual case in its data was a claim issued at Maidstone County Court in August 2019 that was not heard until January 2025 — 1,624 days, representing more than four and a half years. This empirical data from credit hire practice directly illustrates the real-world consequences of systemic delay on claimants who are often pursuing relatively modest personal injury and vehicle-related claims. The credit hire sector is therefore acutely affected by the dysfunction the Committee identified.

4. BLOCK LISTING — SETTLEMENTS AT UNDERVALUE: The Committee examined block listing — the practice of listing more cases in a court session than can realistically be heard, in the knowledge that many will settle on the day. The Committee acknowledged the flexibility block listing provides given the County Court's high settlement rate, but concluded that it creates serious financial harm and frustration for all parties. The knowledge that a case may not be reached on the day it is listed causes parties to settle cases at undervalue as a means of ending the cycle of uncertainty (Conclusion, para 41). This is particularly acute in credit hire litigation where defendants routinely seek to use block listing as leverage. The HMCTS and Judiciary must collect data on listing and settlement rates to allow data-informed listing practices that minimise over-listing (Recommendation, para 42). Critically, litigants must be able to recover the legal, travel and subsistence costs wasted as a result of over-listing or poor court administration that prevents their case from being heard (Recommendation, para 43). No such compensation mechanism currently exists.

5. BLOCK LISTING INTERACTION WITH FIXED RECOVERABLE COSTS: The introduction of the extended fixed recoverable costs (FRC) regime from October 2023 has compounded the block listing problem for credit hire practitioners. Under FRC, only one set of costs is recoverable regardless of the number of hearings or adjournments. Where a credit hire trial is adjourned at court because it cannot be reached in the block list, the claimant's solicitors absorb the wasted costs of attendance, preparation and expert fees without any mechanism for recovery against HMCTS. The hearing fee is not refunded. The practical effect is that FRC creates a financial disincentive to hold out for trial in block-listed cases, reinforcing the pressure to settle at undervalue that the Committee identified. This interaction between FRC and block listing was not specifically addressed in HC 677 but is a direct consequence of the combined policy choices.

6. THE CNBC — A COMMUNICATIONS FAILURE: The Courts and Tribunals Service Centres (CTSC), primarily the Civil National Business Centre (CNBC) at Northampton, handles centralised court administration for money claims and the Damages Claims Portal. The Committee received detailed evidence of systemic failure. Between April 2024 and March 2025 the CNBC received approximately 865,000 calls, of which only 60% reached an operator, with an average wait time of over 24 minutes. Email inboxes reached a backlog of 10-12 weeks at peak, creating a situation where 80% of incoming emails were simply chasing updates on earlier emails — a vicious cycle of non-responsiveness feeding further non-responsiveness. The Committee concluded that centralisation has had a devastating impact on delivery of justice, entrenching the postcode lottery and resulting in debilitating delays for all parties (Conclusion, para 53). The CNBC must be integrated with local court case management systems, and allocated claims handlers introduced to give parties a responsible point of contact (Recommendation, para 54).

7. COURT CLOSURES AND PHYSICAL ESTATE DECAY: Since 1997 a total of 103 county court buildings have been closed — 25 between 1997 and 2009 and a further 78 since 2010, leaving 195 in operation. The court estate has a maintenance backlog of £1.3 billion. The Committee found unacceptable examples of poorly maintained court buildings including asbestos, rat infestations and collapsing ceilings. Half of salaried district judges rated their court building as in poor or unacceptable condition. The Committee concluded that it is unacceptable that the court estate has been allowed to enter such disrepair through years of capital underfunding (Conclusion, para 99) and was concerned by lack of transparency over the £1.3 billion backlog and how successful the Estate Strategy will be in addressing it (Conclusion, para 100). HMCTS and the Ministry of Justice were directed to publish a detailed breakdown of how the £220 million in capital funding spent between March 2023 and March 2025 was deployed, by end of March 2026 (Recommendation, para 101).

8. JUDICIAL CAPACITY CRISIS: Only 60% of the district bench is made up of salaried judges, against the desired target of 80%. A survey found that 39% of salaried district judges intended to leave the judiciary within five years. There are approximately 70 judicial vacancies in London and the South East alone. The Committee concluded that the civil judiciary is 'no longer an attractive profession' and that something vital must be done to attract high-performing candidates to the district bench (Conclusion, para 64). Any review of the County Court must include evaluation of the judicial recruitment offer, covering pay, progression, working conditions and regional recruitment initiatives (Recommendation, para 65). The HMCTS staffing crisis was separately identified as untenable and impairing the ability to support County Court users and efficient access to justice (Conclusion, para 74).

9. THE HMCTS REFORM PROGRAMME — OVER-AMBITIOUS AND UNDER-DELIVERED: The £1 billion HMCTS Reform programme, which ran from 2016 to March 2025, was extended four times and ultimately failed to deliver the promised digital transformation of the County Court. The Committee found HMCTS's evidence unconvincing: it offered weak justifications and failed to adequately acknowledge the extent of the programme's failure (Conclusion, para 126). The Master of the Rolls, Sir Geoffrey Vos, told the Committee that the programme had only managed to digitise approximately 23% of County Court cases. The main civil outputs were the Damages Claims Portal (DCP) and the Online Civil Money Claims (OCMC) service. The Committee found that civil projects under the Reform programme were not sufficiently tested with practitioners in mind, resulting in unacceptable levels of 'dual running' — where a single claim requires interaction with multiple systems simultaneously — creating further inefficiencies (Conclusion, para 127). All future digital reforms must be co-designed with users and stakeholders and should not be rolled out until proven reliable through extensive piloting and testing (Recommendation, para 128).

10. THE PAPER PROBLEM — DCP AND THE PARADOX OF DIGITISATION: One of the most striking findings of the Committee is that the DCP — ostensibly a digital platform — requires courts to print physical files. The County Court used 76,545 reams of A4 paper in 2024-25 at a cost of approximately £378,000. Over three years the courts processed approximately 116 million pages of paper. Approximately 2,000 paper files are transferred weekly via DX courier at a cost of around £350,000 per year. When cases leave the DCP, files are often lost or delayed for months in transit. HMCTS did not consult the Solicitors Regulation Authority when designing the DCP. The Committee concluded that it is very difficult to understand why the County Court continues to rely on paper files, which need to be shipped around the country at great cost, representing a 'patchwork' of systems standing in direct contradiction to the Ministry of Justice's stated commitment to digital transformation (Conclusion, para 136). A single case management system with real-time status updates integrating both Reform and legacy systems is required (Recommendation, para 130), and all claims must be issued a single reference number to eliminate the 'off-ramps and on-ramps' between paper and digital (Recommendation, para 137). Use of postal services for paper files must be replaced with electronic methods (Recommendation, para 138).

11. IMPLICATIONS FOR CREDIT HIRE PRACTITIONERS — MANAGING THE DYSFUNCTION: The practical consequences for credit hire solicitors and claims handlers of the dysfunction identified in HC 677 are significant and multi-layered. Delays of 50+ weeks to trial on small claims (and 74+ weeks on fast track) mean that credit hire claimants face protracted periods of uncertainty long after their accident and vehicle-related losses have crystallised. The Annexo data demonstrates that in the worst cases this extends to four-plus years. Block listing at chronically under-resourced courts creates pressure on clients to settle at undervalue — pressure that is compounded by FRC leaving no remedy for wasted hearing costs. CNBC non-responsiveness makes management of DCP cases extremely difficult: inability to contact the court and 10-12 week email backlogs mean that procedural queries, urgent applications and directions queries go unanswered for months. Practitioners should maintain detailed records of all CNBC contact attempts to support any future application for costs thrown away, and should consider applying for transfer out of DCP to a local court where the dysfunction materially impairs the case.

12. THE COMMITTEE'S RECOMMENDATIONS — WHAT MUST CHANGE: The Committee made 43 numbered conclusions and recommendations across six thematic areas. The overarching recommendation (Rec 43, para 159) is an urgent root-and-branch review of the County Court launched by Spring 2026 that must encompass judicial and staff recruitment and retention, a realistic and sustainable plan for digitisation and capital investment, and exploration of the future role of AI. More immediate recommendations of direct relevance to credit hire include: recovery of wasted costs from over-listing (Rec 8, para 43); data-informed listing to minimise over-listing (Rec 7, para 42); greater use of remote hearings with national guidance on when they should be deployed (Rec 9, para 44); CNBC integration with local systems and allocated claims handlers (Rec 11, para 54); a single unified case management system (Rec 31, para 130); elimination of paper file transfer (Recs 33-34, paras 137-138); and an AI consultation for the County Court to report by end of 2026 (Rec 40, para 156). As at March 2026 there has been no Government response to HC 677 and no root-and-branch review has been announced.
articles.content: HC 677, 'Work of the County Court' (House of Commons Justice Committee, Fourth Report of Session 2024-25, published 21 July 2025), is the most comprehensive parliamentary examination of County Court dysfunction in a generation. It delivers a damning verdict: the County Court is the 'Cinderella service' of the justice system, beset by delays from failed digitisation, a judicial and staff recruitment crisis, and a dysfunctional patchwork of paper-based and digital systems. For credit hire practitioners, the report confirms what daily practice has long demonstrated: it now takes an inordinate amount of time for a claim to reach trial, and the systemic pressures — block listing, CNBC unresponsiveness, DCP dysfunction, and the £1.3 billion decaying court estate — create a litigation environment in which claimants are routinely forced to settle at undervalue simply to end the cycle of uncertainty. The Committee's 43 conclusions and recommendations, anchored in an urgent call for root-and-branch reform by Spring 2026, represent the clearest parliamentary recognition that the current position is not tenable.

articles.title: Individual Voluntary Arrangements (IVAs) and Credit Hire — The Emerging Defence and How to Defeat It
articles.source: Steve Edmondson, 'Impecuniosity and Individual Voluntary Arrangements (IVAs)' — Credit Hire Forum paper
articles.author: Steve Edmondson
principles: 1. THE GROWING SCALE OF IMPECUNIOSITY: The cost-of-living crisis has substantially increased the proportion of motorists who will qualify as impecunious for credit hire purposes. House of Commons Library research ('Poverty in the UK: Statistics', 08/04/24) shows 8.1 million UK residents live in relative poverty and 6.9 million in absolute poverty, with this trend expected to worsen. PwC's 2022 report 'Overlooked and Financially Underserved' found 11 million adults in the UK do not have a credit card — a precondition that the House of Lords recognised in Lagden v O'Connor as making it effectively impossible to hire from a mainstream supplier. At least 1 in 3 adults has difficulty accessing credit from mainstream lenders, a 50% increase since 2016, and 8.9 million adults are financially fragile with 60% having no access to any lending product. 16 million adults could not meet an unexpected payment of £300 without borrowing. FCA research ('It's Time for an Enlightenment on Financial Inclusion', September 2023) found 1.1 million adults are unbanked. The Lagden v O'Connor exception is therefore not a narrow category — it applies to very large numbers of motorists. The proportion of credit hire claimants who qualify as impecunious will continue to grow.

2. THE EMERGING IVA CHALLENGE: An Individual Voluntary Arrangement (IVA) is a legally binding agreement under Part VIII of the Insolvency Act 1986 (ss.252-263) for an insolvent debtor to repay creditors some or all of what they are owed over a period of typically five or six years. In 2023 alone, 64,050 IVAs were registered in England and Wales and the number is growing. The new challenge being run, primarily by Keoghs, is to argue that where a credit hire claimant has entered an IVA — either before hire begins, during the hire period, or before settlement — the existence of the IVA defeats the enforceability or recoverability of the credit hire charges, or at least reduces them to the dividend payable under the IVA. ISAGI reports on the existence of an IVA in their £20 credit check report, giving CHOs an early warning. The author recommends engaging Ben Williams KC to answer any IVA-based defence raised by Keoghs.

3. WILLIAMS v LIVERPOOL VICTORIA — THE FIRST INSTANCE ERROR: The leading case is Williams v Liverpool Victoria Insurance, tried before HHJ Jarman KC on 8 February 2023 (judgment 23 February 2023). The claimant was injured in a Cardiff RTA caused by D's insured, needed a replacement vehicle, and hired on credit from Direct Accident Management Ltd ('DAML') as he could not afford to pay a mainstream rate. There were three hire agreements (24 March, 16 June and 2 September 2020) covering a total of 215 days. On 10 August 2020, during the second agreement, C entered an IVA with his creditors. DAML was not given notice, did not vote, and was not included in the creditor list. At first instance HHJ Jarman found D liable but held the first two hire agreements 'unenforceable' because of the IVA and because DAML had not proved its debt in the IVA — finding C had 'suffered no loss' in respect of them. He allowed only £13,624.56 for 28 days under the third agreement. Permission to appeal was granted by Nicklin J. The appeal was settled before the hearing. The judge's reasoning was wrong in law on multiple grounds.

4. THE IVA DOES NOT EXTINGUISH OR RENDER HIRE AGREEMENTS UNENFORCEABLE: The judge's fundamental error was to treat the IVA as rendering the first two hire agreements unenforceable by analogy with Dimond v Lovell [2000] UKHL 27, in which hire charges failed because the agreement was 'irredeemably unenforceable' due to breaches of the Consumer Credit Act 1974. That analogy is false. An IVA is a statutory mechanism under s.260(2) of the Insolvency Act 1986 by which the approved arrangement binds every unsecured creditor, whether or not they voted and whether or not they had notice, as if they were a party to the arrangement. The effect is NOT to extinguish C's debt to DAML or to make the hire agreements unenforceable. Rather, it contingently binds DAML, as an unsecured creditor, to accept a potentially discounted settlement once C's assets are fully realised. At the point of the claim, C's full indebtedness to DAML subsists. The hire agreements remain fully enforceable. The judge made a straightforward mistake of law as to how IVAs operate (Insolvency Act 1986 s.260(2)(b)): Muir Hunter on Personal Insolvency §3-143.

5. THE IVA OPERATES ONLY AS A MORATORIUM — NOT A DISCOUNT: During the currency of an IVA, the legal effect is simply to place a moratorium on enforcement by creditors while assets are collected: Green v Wright [2017] EWCA Civ 111, [2017] Bus LR 1070, §21. It does not permanently reduce C's liability to his creditors at this stage. Any discount on the debt payable to DAML is applicable only at the completion of the IVA, and after (inter alia) the proceeds of the RTA claim have been collected and paid to the supervisor for distribution: s.260(2A) of the Act. Furthermore, even where an IVA has been successfully completed, that completion operates as a release of the debtor from further enforcement by creditors but does NOT extinguish the underlying debts themselves: Green v Wright §§30-33. In Green, the debtor argued that post-completion PPI proceeds belonged to him because the IVA had extinguished his indebtedness. The Court of Appeal held to the contrary: even a completed IVA does not extinguish debts, so assets recovered post-completion remain payable to creditors. C's liability to DAML under all three agreements therefore subsisted throughout.

6. THE IVA IS RES INTER ALIOS ACTA — NOT A BENEFIT D CAN RELY ON: The independent and decisive answer to the IVA defence is that the IVA is res inter alios acta — a collateral arrangement between C and his creditors that D is not entitled to rely on to reduce its liability. McGregor on Damages (21st ed) §9.111 states that 'a matter completely collateral and merely res inter alios acta cannot be used in mitigation of damage.' The test for whether a defendant can take advantage of a subsequent benefit to a claimant is whether that benefit 'comes from an act taken in consequence of the wrong, either out of the act of mitigation itself or as part of a continuous transaction starting with the wrong': McGregor §9.112; Thai Airways v K I Holdings [2015] EWHC 1250, [2016] 1 All ER (Comm) 675, §46 (Leggatt J).
