The IVA did not arise in any way out of or in connection with D's breach of duty. It is an extraneous, ex post facto, and entirely separate composition between C and his creditors. It is therefore res inter alios acta and left out of account. AssetCo v Grant Thornton [2019] EWHC 150, [2019] Bus LR 2291 provides the closest parallel: a company's scheme of arrangement with creditors was held to be 'an independent bargain between AssetCo and creditors' and res inter alios acta in a claim against negligent auditors (§904).

7. DISTINGUISHING DIMOND v LOVELL ON THE RES INTER ALIOS ACTA POINT: In Dimond v Lovell [2000] UKHL 27 the House of Lords rejected an argument that the fact that the hire agreement was unenforceable was res inter alios acta. But that conclusion followed from the specific facts: the unenforceability arose directly from the act of mitigation itself — the very act of renting the replacement car created the unenforceable agreement. In Leggatt J's language in Thai Airways (§46), that benefit was 'a consequence of the mitigating step and hence of the defendant's breach.' The IVA in the present case cannot possibly be so described. It is the result of a subsequent, and entirely separate, composition between C and his creditors, having no connection whatsoever with D's breach or the act of mitigating by hiring. The Dimond v Lovell analogy therefore entirely fails.

8. D IS NOT A BENEFICIARY OF THE IVA — THREE FURTHER BASES: The appeal advanced three independent further grounds why D cannot take advantage of the IVA. (a) The IVA was not caused by the tort and was an entirely collateral ex post facto arrangement left out of account. (b) The IVA's express purpose was to preserve C's legal claims against third parties (including D) in order to maximise recovery for creditors — not to release third parties from liability. The IVA expressly contemplated that extant claims would continue to be pursued with the supervisor's agreement (§4.5-4.10 of the IVA proposal). DAML/the supervisor was informed of the post-accident RTA claim, and the supervisor confirmed she would liaise with Bond Turner and that proceeds would be assets of the IVA. For D (a debtor, not a creditor) to appropriate the benefit of the IVA would actively defeat its very purpose and constitute an adventitious and uncovenanted windfall for a wrongdoer at the expense of C's creditors: Merrett v Capitol Indemnity [1991] 1 Lloyd's Rep 169 (Steyn J — arrangement intended solely for benefit of insured must be disregarded in assessing recoverable loss). (c) On policy grounds, the partial release of debts under an IVA should be treated as res inter alios acta because any other conclusion would incentivise creditors to bankrupt debtors rather than allow IVAs — since a trustee in bankruptcy would be able to pursue claims against D in full. IVA and bankruptcy should be treated analogously: Green v Wright §32. The justice and public policy imperatives stated in Parry v Cleaver [1970] AC 1, 13 (Lord Reid) strongly favour this outcome.

9. THE AGREEMENTS REMAIN ENFORCEABLE IN ANY EVENT: Even if the IVA were treated as affecting enforceability, the judge should still have allowed the claims under the first two agreements in full because: (i) there was no risk of double recovery — C cannot keep the proceeds of the claim for himself; they go to the supervisor to distribute to creditors (including DAML); (ii) there was no risk of over-payment by D — D's liability was already limited by the doctrine of mitigation; any IVA windfall would have been wholly adventitious and unmerited. The IVA operates only as a contingent and uncertain moratorium on enforcement (Green v Wright §21), which would lapse if the IVA were breached, terminated, or if C became bankrupt. The court should not put an injured party at risk of unquantifiable future uncertainties to relieve a wrongdoer of the consequences of its breach: Freeman v Lockett [2006] EWHC 102, [2006] PIQR P23, §35; Pilkington v Wood [1953] Ch 770. The judge should have held that the law permitted undiscounted recovery in respect of C's potential liabilities under the first two agreements.

10. PROCEDURAL SAFEGUARD — RAISING RES INTER ALIOS ACTA: The paper specifically warns that the first instance judge in Williams v Liverpool Victoria entirely failed to address the res inter alios acta argument — despite it having been raised in the Reply, in C's trial skeleton, in closing submissions, and on three subsequent occasions when C drew the judge's attention to the omission (upon receipt of the draft judgment, in the written PTA application, and upon receipt of the draft order). A failure by a judge to address key arguments raised by the parties and give reasons for rejecting them is itself a ground of appeal: English v Emery Reimbold & Strick [2002] EWCA Civ 605, [2002] 1 WLR 2409. Credit hire practitioners facing an IVA-based defence must ensure that res inter alios acta is pleaded in the Reply, included in the trial skeleton, and pressed in closing. If the judgment does not address it, the omission should be raised promptly with the judge before seeking permission to appeal.
cases: Williams v Liverpool Victoria Insurance (HHJ Jarman KC, 23 February 2023)
Green v Wright [2017] EWCA Civ 111, [2017] Bus LR 1070
AssetCo v Grant Thornton [2019] EWHC 150, [2019] Bus LR 2291
Merrett v Capitol Indemnity [1991] 1 Lloyd's Rep 169
Thai Airways v K I Holdings [2015] EWHC 1250, [2016] 1 All ER (Comm) 675
British Westinghouse v Underground Electric Railways [1912] AC 673
Freeman v Lockett [2006] EWHC 102, [2006] PIQR P23
English v Emery Reimbold & Strick [2002] EWCA Civ 605, [2002] 1 WLR 2409
articles.content: An emerging tactic — primarily deployed by Keoghs — is to argue that where a credit hire claimant has entered an Individual Voluntary Arrangement (IVA), the IVA defeats the enforceability or recoverability of credit hire charges. The first instance decision in Williams v Liverpool Victoria Insurance (HHJ Jarman KC, February 2023) wrongly accepted this argument, holding that the first two of three hire agreements were 'unenforceable' because of the claimant's IVA. Permission to appeal was given by Nicklin J and the appeal was settled before the hearing. This paper analyses the multiple grounds on which the first instance decision was wrong. An IVA is not analogous to an irremediably unenforceable consumer credit agreement (Dimond v Lovell). It operates only as a moratorium on enforcement, not as an extinguishment or reduction of the debt. Even if it did reduce the debt, the IVA is res inter alios acta — a collateral arrangement between C and his creditors that D cannot exploit as a windfall. The correct result is full recovery of hire charges under all agreements, with the proceeds inuring to C's creditors through the IVA machinery. Credit hire practitioners should ensure res inter alios acta is squarely pleaded and pressed, and should engage Ben Williams KC for any case where Keoghs runs this defence.

articles.title: Northern Ireland Pre-Action Protocol — Personal Injury and Damage-Only RTA Claims in the County Court
articles.source: County Court of Northern Ireland, Pre-Action Protocol for Personal Injury and Damage Only Road Traffic Accident Claims, effective 6 February 2023 (signed by Lady Chief Justice Dame Siobhan Roisin Keegan)
articles.author: County Court of Northern Ireland (Lady Chief Justice Dame Siobhan Roisin Keegan)
principles: 1. APPLICATION, SCOPE AND JURISDICTION: The County Court of Northern Ireland Pre-Action Protocol for Personal Injury and Damage Only Road Traffic Accident Claims has effect from 6 February 2023. It applies to all cases involving claims in personal injury litigation and damage-only road traffic accidents brought in the County Court of Northern Ireland. The protocol does not alter the parties' rights and obligations. Northern Ireland has a separate and distinct legal jurisdiction from England and Wales, and this protocol is entirely independent of the Pre-Action Protocol for Personal Injury Claims and the Pre-Action Protocol for Low Value Personal Injury Claims in Road Traffic Accidents that govern proceedings in England and Wales. NI terminology uses 'plaintiff' for the injured party and 'defendant' for the tortfeasor/insurer — not the E&W 'claimant/defendant' convention. The overriding objective is drawn from Order 58 Rule 1 of the County Court Rules [Northern Ireland] 1981 and mirrors the E&W overriding objective in requiring the court to deal with cases justly, proportionately, and efficiently.

2. AIMS OF THE PROTOCOL: The protocol aims to achieve best litigation practice by encouraging more pre-action contact between the parties, better and earlier exchange of information, better pre-action investigation by both sides, placing the parties in a position where they may be able to settle cases fairly and early without litigation, enabling proceedings to proceed according to the court's timetable efficiently if litigation does become necessary, and promoting a 'cards on the table' approach to litigation. The court will treat the standards set out in the protocol as the normal reasonable approach of parties and will expect the parties to have complied before proceedings are issued. Litigants in person must still comply as far as reasonably possible; where a party is aware the other side is a LiP, they should send a copy of the protocol at the earliest opportunity.

3. CONTENT OF THE PLAINTIFF'S LETTER OF CLAIM — GENERAL REQUIREMENTS: After any preliminary notification letter, the plaintiff's solicitors must send a detailed letter of claim to the defendant (or their legal/corporate representative) as soon as sufficient information is available to substantiate a realistic claim and before issues of quantum are addressed in detail. The letter must contain: (a) a clear summary of the facts; (b) an indication of the nature of any injuries; (c) details of any financial loss incurred, even if provisional; (d) the plaintiff's full address and postcode (which may be withheld if there are genuine personal security concerns); and (e) the name and address of any hospital attended by the plaintiff, with the hospital reference number where available. A template letter is provided at Appendix A to the protocol which can be amended to suit the particular case. If the identity of the insurer is known, a copy of the letter of claim should be sent directly to the insurer.

4. CREDIT HIRE AND VEHICLE DAMAGE — SPECIFIC LETTER OF CLAIM REQUIREMENTS: Where the plaintiff is claiming vehicle damage and/or the cost of a replacement vehicle, the letter of claim must additionally provide: (a) the plaintiff's date of birth; (b) the details of heads of claim to be presented if available; (c) the plaintiff's impecuniosity position if ascertainable; (d) details of whether the vehicle is roadworthy or driveable; and (e) the plaintiff's occupation if relevant — for example if the plaintiff uses their vehicle as a taxi or primarily for business purposes. The explicit requirement to state the plaintiff's impecuniosity position at the pre-action stage is noteworthy: it means CHOs operating in Northern Ireland should conduct impecuniosity checks at the earliest possible stage so the information can be included in or promptly following the letter of claim, rather than being left to emerge only at disclosure.

5. DEFENDANT'S RESPONSE TIMESCALES — PERSONAL INJURY CLAIMS: In a personal injury claim, the defendant's solicitor/insurer has a maximum of 3 months from the date of acknowledgement of the letter of claim to investigate. No later than the end of that period, the defendant's insurer/solicitors must reply stating whether liability and/or causation is admitted or denied, and if denied stating with sufficient clarity and detail the basis of the defendant's case (including any allegation that fault lies with or an indemnity is claimed from another). Any contributory negligence allegation must be set out with details of what is alleged and upon what basis. These time periods also apply to any allegations of fraud that the defendant intends to make (para 7.6). For cross-border cases (accident outside Northern Ireland and/or defendant outside the jurisdiction), the acknowledgement period extends from 21 days to 42 days and the investigation period from 3 months to 6 months.

6. DEFENDANT'S RESPONSE TIMESCALES — DAMAGE-ONLY CLAIMS: In a road traffic damage-only claim (including a pure credit hire claim with no personal injury element), the defendant's solicitor/insurer has a maximum of 2 months from the date of acknowledgement of the letter of claim to investigate and respond, stating whether liability is admitted or denied with sufficient clarity and detail. This is a shorter window than for PI claims. For cross-border damage-only cases, the periods extend from 21 days to 42 days and from 2 months to 4 months. A defendant may also state in their response whether they intend to dispute the plaintiff's need for a replacement vehicle and, if so, the reasons why (para 7.8). Where liability is admitted, the defendant/insurer is bound by that admission — the sole exception being where evidence subsequently emerges that the claim is fraudulent (para 7.7).

7. PROVISION OF DOCUMENTS — CREDIT HIRE CASES: Where the plaintiff is claiming vehicle damage and/or the cost of a replacement vehicle, the plaintiff will provide the following documents if available: the repair invoice; any depreciation report; the recovery agreement and invoice; the storage agreement and invoice; confirmation of whether the plaintiff has transferred their own comprehensive insurance and, if so, temporary insurance invoices; the hire invoice and hire agreements; proof that the plaintiff held a valid licence for 3 years prior to the accident; confirmation of whether they held any penalty points at the time of hire; confirmation if they need to potentially travel outside Northern Ireland; confirmation of whether the vehicle was required for purposes of taxiing, towing, or storing livestock; the details of any additional drivers (relationship to plaintiff, convictions, penalty points and length of licence); and reasons for high-risk charges if applicable. The NI protocol therefore requires early and comprehensive disclosure of the hire file by the plaintiff, going beyond what is routinely expected in England and Wales at the pre-action stage.

8. PROVISION OF DOCUMENTS — CREDIT REPAIR AND TOTAL LOSS: For credit repair cases, additional documentation is required if available: the date the plaintiff's engineer was instructed; the date of inspection; the date the report was completed; vehicle details (make, model, engine size, mileage at date of accident); MOT certificate expiry; confirmation the vehicle was roadworthy and driveable at inspection; a breakdown of hourly rate, hours spent, parts, paint/materials, specialist costs and VAT; the repair invoice; hire start and end dates; date the plaintiff collected the vehicle if different from hire end; and VAT registration status. For total loss cases handled by an accident management company, the engineer's report must address: the date the engineer was notified; date of inspection and report completion; date the pre-accident value figure was received; mileage at date of accident; a cost breakdown; pre-accident value, salvage value at time of accident, and salvage category code; salvage figure details; and VAT registration status.

9. IMPECUNIOSITY DOCUMENTATION REQUIREMENTS: If the plaintiff is relying on impecuniosity to justify credit hire rates under the Lagden v O'Connor principle, they will provide the following information if available: (a) three months' bank and/or building society statements; (b) three months' credit union savings statements or the equivalent; (c) the monthly cost of servicing any debts/loans; (d) details of outgoings at the time of the accident; and (e) details of earnings at the time of the accident (or if the plaintiff's earnings are not paid into a bank account, three months' pay slips). The NI protocol's requirement for credit union savings statements reflects the significantly higher penetration of credit unions in Northern Ireland compared to England and Wales, where credit unions are less common. The standard impecuniosity financial disclosure package required by the NI protocol closely tracks what is routinely exchanged in England and Wales litigation but is given formal pre-action protocol status in Northern Ireland.

10. REHABILITATION AND ADR: The parties are encouraged to jointly explore rehabilitation at an early stage in appropriate cases, without prejudice to liability (para 9). On settlement and ADR, the parties should consider whether some form of alternative dispute resolution would be more suitable than litigation, and if so endeavour to agree which form to adopt. During any litigation, either party may be required by the court to produce evidence that alternative means of resolving the dispute have been considered. A mediation service is provided by the Law Society of Northern Ireland. The protocol expressly recognises that no party can or should be forced to mediate or enter into any form of ADR. These provisions mirror the spirit, if not the exact detail, of the post-Churchill v Merthyr Tydfil position in England and Wales where courts have broader discretion to encourage or order ADR.
articles.content: The County Court of Northern Ireland Pre-Action Protocol for Personal Injury and Damage Only Road Traffic Accident Claims, effective from 6 February 2023 (signed by Lady Chief Justice Dame Siobhan Roisin Keegan), governs pre-action conduct for all PI and damage-only RTA claims in the County Court of Northern Ireland. It is wholly distinct from the England and Wales pre-action protocols. Key differences of practical importance for credit hire include: mandatory early disclosure of the plaintiff's impecuniosity position in the letter of claim (para 6.1(c)); a shorter 2-month response window for damage-only/credit hire claims compared to 3 months for PI; an unusually comprehensive list of pre-action document obligations for credit hire and credit repair cases; an explicit requirement for credit union savings statements in impecuniosity disclosure; and the use of 'plaintiff/defendant' terminology derived from Order 58 of the County Court Rules [NI] 1981. Practitioners operating in Northern Ireland must follow this protocol; non-compliance may attract adverse costs consequences.

articles.title: Keoghs' Lobbying Strategy and the MoJ Part II Whiplash Consultation Response -- Understanding the Insurer Reform Agenda
articles.source: Keoghs LLP, 'MoJ Part II Whiplash Consultation Response: What's Missing?' (c.2022)
articles.author: Keoghs LLP (Gary Herring, Ruth Needham, Mark Hall, Howard Dean, Matthew Ruck, Natalie Larnder)
principles: 1. THE DOCUMENT AND ITS CONTEXT: This Keoghs publication responds to the Ministry of Justice's Part II response to the 'Reforming the soft tissue injury (whiplash) claims process' consultation (which had closed on 6 January 2017). Five years elapsed between the Part I and Part II responses. The Part II response was characterised by Keoghs as essentially saying 'we're taking no action yet'. Rather than summarising the MoJ's response, Keoghs used the document to set out their view of what was missing -- identifying what meaningful reform they believed should still be taken forward across five areas: fraud/QOCS, credit hire, early notification, recoverability of disbursements, and rehabilitation. The document is authored by multiple Keoghs partners and is representative of the firm's institutional approach to regulatory influence: identifying the gap between what the MoJ did and what insurers want, framing that gap as 'loopholes', and explicitly committing to pursue change through judicial precedent and direct lobbying. The conclusion, by Head of Market Affairs Natalie Larnder, states: 'Judging by the Government's latest response to the consultation, little to no further reform is required, however we hope we have demonstrated above how much further we have to go as an industry to close the current loopholes and create a level playing field for whiplash and credit hire claims.'

2. THE MoJ'S POSITION ON CREDIT HIRE -- AND WHY KEOGHS FOUND IT INADEQUATE: The consultation considered five potential models for reforming how credit hire agreements are dealt with: a First Party Model, a Regulatory Model, an Industry Code of Conduct, a Competitive Offer Model, and other options. The consultation response from respondents favoured an Industry Code of Conduct (183 supporting respondents). The MoJ's response was to confirm that Government would continue to work with key stakeholders in the credit hire sector to monitor and improve the use of industry agreements including the GTA, with further consideration to be given to whether it would be beneficial to make use of such agreements mandatory. The MoJ acknowledged that any mandatory changes would require primary legislation and -- in Keoghs' parenthetical observation -- given other Government commitments and strains on parliamentary time, 'such reform is unlikely to happen.' Keoghs regarded this response as wholly insufficient.

3. KEOGHS' CREDIT HIRE REFORM AGENDA -- THE EXPLICIT WISH LIST: Gary Herring, Keoghs' credit hire partner, set out the firm's substantive reform agenda in unambiguous terms. First, they called for a dedicated pre-action protocol for credit hire claims -- describing this as 'clearly desirable for the purposes of reducing the substantial volume of contested litigation where the only issue is an assessment of damages.' Second, they identified what they regard as anomalies between the legislative and procedural frameworks governing credit hire claims versus other similar claim types: specifically, that FCA regulation, specific pre-action protocols, fixed costs, Qualified One Way Costs Shifting (QOCS), and fundamental dishonesty sanctions all apply to personal injury claims but do not apply to claims for credit hire. Third, they anticipated that higher volumes of credit hire claims would be driven by: (a) migration from the PI space following the introduction of the Civil Liability Act; (b) the continued availability of standard basis legal costs for credit hire and vehicle damage only claims exceeding 10,000 pounds; (c) the absence of any prescribed sanctions in respect of fundamentally dishonest credit hire claims; and (d) the rapid expansion of certain industry participants. The document concludes that the current consultation parameters do not go far enough to tackle the impact credit hire will continue to have on insurance premiums.

4. THE GTA AND BI-LATERAL AGREEMENTS -- KEOGHS' ASSESSMENT OF SELF-REGULATION: Keoghs expressed scepticism about the long-term viability of the GTA and other voluntary bi-lateral agreements as a means of self-regulating the credit hire market, stating that market opinion is divided on whether their effectiveness has been overestimated. They reported that insurer clients were experiencing what appeared to be a concerted drive from a cross-section of the claimant market to renegotiate bi-lateral agreements, attributable to then-current inflationary pressures. Any renegotiated agreements might themselves become unfeasible once conditions normalised. The number of GTA signatories had already reduced following high-profile exits over the previous decade. The overall consequence anticipated was that a far higher proportion of the credit hire market would operate outside any protocol arrangement at all -- the opposite of the Government's ambition. Keoghs therefore regarded the Government's reliance on self-regulation as misplaced.

5. THE REFORM STRATEGY -- JUDICIAL PRECEDENT AND LOBBYING: The explicit strategy set out by Keoghs for achieving the reform the Government refused to deliver is twofold: 'We will continue to attempt to drive positive change in this area, by way of judicial precedent and by making the case for reform.' This is an unusually candid statement of a law firm's dual role as both litigation practitioner and regulatory lobbyist. The judicial precedent route means running cases designed to establish principles adverse to CHOs -- extending arguments about need, impecuniosity, fundamental dishonesty, and the appropriate BHR test in ways not currently supported by the leading authorities. The political lobbying route means directly pressing Government and the CPRC for specific changes: a credit hire PAP, extension of QOCS to credit hire, FCA regulation, and fundamental dishonesty sanctions for dishonest credit hire claims. This document should be read as a roadmap of the arguments and regulatory pressure Keoghs intends to bring to bear on the credit hire market over the medium term.

6. QOCS, HO v ADELEKUN AND THE FRAUD FRAMING: Ruth Needham, Keoghs' Partner and Director of Fraud Rings, addressed QOCS reform. She noted that following Ho v Adelekun, insurers are in a worse position when making Part 36 offers to settle grey-area fraud cases because claimants have double protection: they face no costs under QOCS and can accept a Part 36 offer at the last moment without adverse consequences. Keoghs regard this as creating a 'have a go' culture that all consumers ultimately pay for. Although this section addresses PI fraud rather than credit hire directly, the underlying theme -- that claimants in motor claims face insufficient procedural disincentives to pursue weak or fraudulent claims -- is integral to the insurer lobby's broader argument for treating credit hire and PI claims equivalently for costs and dishonesty purposes.

7. DISBURSEMENTS AND REHABILITATION: Howard Dean, Head of Costs, argued that the consultation asked the wrong question on disbursements -- the issue is not whether reasonably incurred disbursements are recoverable but the amount of each disbursement, which FRC has still not resolved twenty years after its introduction. He suggested the CJC should fix capped disbursement levels. Matthew Ruck on rehabilitation argued for a mandatory rehabilitation process integrated with the OIC portal and MedCo, with wasted and non-party costs orders against those responsible for adverse behaviours. Both positions signal Keoghs' support for extending mandatory processes and capped recoveries into areas currently governed by voluntary frameworks -- consistent with their broader credit hire agenda.

8. READING KEOGHS' MODUS OPERANDI -- IMPLICATIONS FOR CHO STRATEGY: This document illuminates four features of Keoghs' institutional approach that credit hire organisations should understand. (a) They operate simultaneously as litigators and lobbyists, treating individual cases as vehicles for generating the judicial precedents that advance the broader reform agenda. Arguments that appear novel or disproportionate in individual credit hire cases may be part of a long-game strategy to shift the law rather than to win any particular case. (b) They regard the absence of FCA regulation, QOCS, fixed costs and fundamental dishonesty sanctions from credit hire as a structural anomaly to be corrected, not an accepted feature of the legal landscape. (c) They monitor the GTA and bi-lateral agreements with a view to exploiting any breakdown in self-regulation as evidence that statutory intervention is required. (d) They have a dedicated Head of Market Affairs whose explicit function is making the regulatory and parliamentary case for the changes the courts have not yet delivered. CHOs should engage with their own trade and lobbying infrastructure -- the CHF, ABI/CHO liaison, and parliamentary contacts -- to counter these arguments at the political level with the same rigour applied to them in the courtroom.
articles.content: This Keoghs publication, responding to the MoJ's Part II response to the whiplash consultation (closed January 2017), reveals with unusual candour the insurer lobby's reform agenda for credit hire. Gary Herring explicitly identifies Keoghs' credit hire wish-list: a dedicated pre-action protocol; extension of QOCS and fundamental dishonesty sanctions to credit hire; FCA regulation; and fixed recoverable costs. He predicts growing credit hire volumes driven by Civil Liability Act migration, standard-basis legal costs above 10,000 pounds, and the absence of dishonesty sanctions. The document commits to pursuing change by way of judicial precedent and lobbying -- a clear statement that Keoghs regards individual credit hire litigation as a vehicle for achieving the regulatory outcomes the Government declined to deliver. Credit hire practitioners should read this document as a strategic roadmap of the arguments and lobbying pressure they will face from the insurer side.

articles.title: BHR Supplier Profile — Ugo Scooters (North London / Saffron Walden)
articles.subcategory: Supplier Profiles — Scooter and Moped Hire
articles.source: Provider profile compiled from ugoscooters.com; rates valid at 01/12/2025
articles.author: credit-hire.ai research
principles: SUPPLIER IDENTITY: Ugo Scooters operates as a regional scooter hire provider. Its administrative address is 16 High Street, Saffron Walden, Essex CB10 1AX. The website is ugoscooters.com. No fixed depot or collection locations are identified. Geographic coverage is claimed for London, Hounslow, Tower Hamlets, Cambridge, Chelmsford, Twickenham, Watford, St Albans, Hemel Hempstead, Brentford, Basildon and Slough, but without identified premises in those areas. Contact is by email or webform only — no telephone number is advertised.

ELIGIBILITY CONSTRAINTS: Registration is required online before hire is confirmed. The provider states: 'Once you register, we will check your eligibility to hire a moped from Ugo Scooters and will contact you as soon as possible. The time of year and demand for scooters can affect how long this takes.' No fixed approval timescale is stated. Eligibility criteria include: age 21 to 65; current provisional licence with CBT pass or full UK licence; no more than 6 penalty points endorsed; proof of identity by bank statement or utility bill; riders may not carry passengers.

MINIMUM TERM AND FINANCIAL REQUIREMENTS: The minimum rental period is 1 week. A deposit of £300 and advance rental payment are required at the start of hire. A claimant who cannot fund these upfront costs — or whose credit hire period is shorter than 1 week — cannot realistically be regarded as having had access to this provider.

DELIVERY AND COLLECTION: The website does not advertise a cost, qualification, or geographical limit for delivery or collection. The terms of any delivery service (if available) are wholly unspecified.

INSURANCE POSITION: Third-party only insurance is included. The hirer remains fully liable for damage to the hired vehicle including fair wear and tear, and fully liable to reimburse in the event of theft. No comprehensive cover is offered. An unspecified damage fee applies for dents, scratches, collisions, flat tyres, battery discharge, engine problems, vandalism, theft, and third-party damages. Social, Domestic and Pleasure cover is offered where the vehicle is not used for courier or delivery work; food delivery and courier use is permitted under a separate policy basis.

ON-HIRE ACCIDENT RESTRICTION: If the hirer is involved in an accident whilst on hire, hire will be suspended while Ugo makes enquiries into liability, which may take 2 to 3 weeks. This suspension clause has direct relevance in credit hire cases where the claimant has been involved in a further incident during the hire period.
MILEAGE: No mileage limitation is stated on the website.
HIRE RATES (valid 01/12/2025): Honda Vision 110cc: £105/week (deposit £300). Honda PCX 125cc: £119/week (deposit £300).

COMPARATOR VALIDITY ASSESSMENT: This supplier may constitute a valid BHR comparator only where all of the following conditions are met: (1) the claimant falls within the age range 21-65; (2) the claimant holds a qualifying licence (CBT provisional or full UK) with no more than 6 penalty points; (3) the claimant can provide the required proof of identity; (4) the claimant can fund £300 deposit plus advance payment at commencement; (5) the hire period is at least 1 week; (6) the use intended matches the insurance basis offered; (7) the claimant's location falls within the claimed coverage area and delivery/collection terms are consistent with the actual hire. Where any of these conditions is not met, rates derived from this provider do not reflect a realistic alternative available to this claimant.

FINANCIAL STANDING (contextual only): Companies House accounts to 31/03/2025 show net assets of approximately £2,900. This field is contextual and should not be relied upon as a standalone eligibility criterion unless separately justified.
articles.content:
Ugo Scooters – North London is a regional scooter hire provider with administrative headquarters in Saffron Walden. The website does not identify fixed depot locations but advertises coverage across multiple areas including London, Hounslow, Tower Hamlets, Cambridge, Chelmsford, Twickenham, Watford, St Albans, Hemel Hempstead, Brentford, Basildon and Slough. No telephone contact details are advertised; customer contact is limited to email or webform submission, and availability is stated to be subject to demand.

Hire is subject to a minimum one-week term, advance payment and deposit, and eligibility criteria including age limits, licence type, a maximum of six endorsed points, and proof of address. Registration and eligibility approval are required before hire is confirmed, with no stated timescale. Delivery and collection arrangements are not described on the website, and no costs or geographic limits are specified.

Insurance provided is third-party only. The hirer retains liability for damage, theft, and fair wear and tear, with damage fees applying; no comprehensive cover is offered. Food delivery and courier use is permitted, while Social, Domestic and Pleasure cover applies where the vehicle is not used for delivery or courier work. The website does not stipulate any mileage limitation or excess figure.

Where a claimant satisfies the eligibility criteria, is able to meet the financial requirements, the minimum hire period aligns with the credit hire duration, and the intended use is permitted under the provider's insurance terms, this supplier may represent a valid basic hire comparator. In the absence of any of these conditions, rates derived from this provider may not reflect a realistic alternative.
articles.summary:

The website does not identify fixed depot locations but advertises coverage across multiple areas including London, Hounslow, Tower Hamlets, Cambridge, Chelmsford, Twickenham, Watford, St Albans, Hemel Hempstead, Brentford, Basildon and Slough. No telephone contact details are advertised; customer contact is limited to email or webform submission, and availability is stated to be subject to demand.

Hire is subject to a minimum one-week term, advance payment and deposit, and eligibility criteria including age limits, licence type, a maximum of six endorsed points, and proof of address. Delivery and collection arrangements are not described on the website, and no costs or geographic limits are specified.

Insurance provided is third-party only. The hirer retains liability for damage, theft, and fair wear and tear, with damage fees applying; no comprehensive cover is offered. Food delivery and courier use is permitted, while Social, Domestic and Pleasure cover applies where the vehicle is not used for delivery or courier work. The website does not stipulate any mileage limitation or excess figure.

Where a claimant satisfies the eligibility criteria, is able to meet the financial requirements, the minimum hire period aligns with the credit hire duration, and the intended use is permitted under the provider's insurance terms, this supplier may represent a valid basic hire comparator. articles.title: BHR Supplier Profile — Omega Bikes (Park Royal, London)
articles.subcategory: Supplier Profiles — Scooter and Moped Hire
articles.source: Provider profile compiled from omegabikes.com; rates valid at 01/12/2025
articles.author: credit-hire.ai research
principles: SUPPLIER IDENTITY: Omega Bikes – Park Royal is a motorcycle dealer and rental provider. Address: 11-12 Steele Road, Park Royal, London NW10 7AS. Website: omegabikes.com/bikerentals. The provider operates from fixed premises; hire vehicles must be collected from and returned to Park Royal at the commencement and conclusion of the rental period.

ELIGIBILITY CONSTRAINTS: Booking is through an online form with confirmation required by the provider. Eligibility criteria include: minimum age 21; current provisional licence with CBT pass or full UK licence; verified proof of address.

MINIMUM TERM AND FINANCIAL REQUIREMENTS: Minimum rental period is 3 days. A deposit of £300 and advance rental payment are required at the commencement of hire. A claimant who cannot attend in person to collect, or who cannot fund the deposit and advance payment, cannot realistically be regarded as having had access to this provider.

DELIVERY AND COLLECTION: No delivery or collection service is advertised. The claimant must attend the Park Royal premises in person to collect and return the vehicle. This is a material constraint for any claimant who does not live or work in proximity to NW10.

INSURANCE POSITION: The insurance type provided by Omega Bikes is not specified on the website. A £1,000 excess applies to all hire vehicles. Courier use is permitted. WARNING: The insurance provision is unclear with this supplier but we suspect that it only provides Third Party cover leaving a hirer fully exposed for the value of the bike in the event of a theft or fault accident. This uncertainty is itself a material distinction from a credit hire agreement that provides comprehensive cover, and should be raised as a point of differentiation when this supplier is cited as a BHR comparator.
MILEAGE: No mileage limitation is stated on the website.

HIRE RATES (valid 01/12/2025): Peugeot Tweet 125cc £69/week (deposit £300, excess £1,000). Piaggio Liberty 125cc £69/week (deposit £300, excess £1,000). Aprilia SR GT 125cc £90/week (deposit £300, excess £1,000). Yamaha Delight 125cc £69/week (deposit £300, excess £1,000). Honda SH 125cc £90/week (deposit £300, excess £1,000). Tricity Yamaha 125cc £109/week (deposit £300, excess £1,000).

COMPARATOR VALIDITY ASSESSMENT: This supplier may constitute a valid BHR comparator only where all of the following conditions are met: (1) the claimant is aged 21 or over; (2) the claimant holds a qualifying licence (CBT provisional or full UK); (3) the claimant can provide proof of address; (4) the claimant can fund £300 deposit plus advance payment at commencement; (5) the claimant can attend Park Royal in person for collection and return; (6) the £1,000 excess is consistent with (or no more burdensome than) the terms of the credit hire agreement; (7) the hire period is at least 3 days. Where any of these conditions is not met, rates derived from this provider do not reflect a realistic alternative available to this claimant. The personal collection requirement is a particularly significant barrier for claimants who have lost use of their vehicle as a result of accident damage.
articles.content: Omega Bikes – Park Royal is a motorcycle dealer and rental provider operating from a fixed premises in Park Royal, London. Hire vehicles must be collected from, and returned to, the provider's premises at the commencement and conclusion of the rental period; no delivery or collection services are advertised. Availability is subject to completion of an online booking process and confirmation by the provider. Hire is subject to eligibility criteria including a minimum age requirement, licence type, and provision of proof of address. Rentals are offered for relatively short minimum terms, with advance payment and a deposit required at the outset. Courier use is permitted. Insurance terms include a substantial excess, and the website does not specify any mileage limitation. Where a claimant satisfies the eligibility requirements, is able to collect and return the vehicle in person, accepts the excess terms, and the rental period aligns with the credit hire duration, this provider may represent a valid basic hire comparator. In circumstances where personal collection is impractical, excess terms are materially different, or other eligibility requirements are not met, rates derived from this provider may not reflect a realistic alternative.
articles.insurance_warning: WARNING: The insurance provision is unclear with this supplier but we suspect that it only provides Third Party cover leaving a hirer fully exposed for the value of the bike in the event of a theft or fault accident.
articles.summary: Omega Bikes – Park Royal is a motorcycle dealer and rental provider operating from a fixed premises in Park Royal, London. Hire vehicles must be collected from, and returned to, the provider's premises at the commencement and conclusion of the rental period; no delivery or collection services are advertised. Availability is subject to completion of an online booking process and confirmation by the provider. Hire is subject to eligibility criteria including a minimum age requirement, licence type, and provision of proof of address. Rentals are offered for relatively short minimum terms, with advance payment and a deposit required at the outset. Courier use is permitted. Insurance terms include a substantial excess, and the website does not specify any mileage limitation. Where a claimant satisfies the eligibility requirements, is able to collect and return the vehicle in person, accepts the excess terms, and the rental period aligns with the credit hire duration, this provider may represent a valid basic hire comparator. In circumstances where personal collection is impractical, excess terms are materially different, or other eligibility requirements are not met, rates derived from this provider may not reflect a realistic alternative.
key_points: Omega Bikes – Park Royal is a motorcycle dealer and rental provider operating from a fixed premises in Park Royal, London.
Hire vehicles must be collected from, and returned to, the provider's premises at the commencement and conclusion of the rental period; no delivery or collection services are advertised.
Availability is subject to completion of an online booking process and confirmation by the provider.
Hire is subject to eligibility criteria including a minimum age requirement, licence type, and provision of proof of address.
Rentals are offered for relatively short minimum terms, with advance payment and a deposit required at the outset.

articles.title: BHR Supplier Profile — Rent a Scoot (Morden, Surrey)
articles.subcategory: Supplier Profiles — Scooter and Moped Hire
articles.source: Provider profile compiled from rentascoot.co.uk; rates valid at 01/12/2025
articles.author: credit-hire.ai research
principles: SUPPLIER IDENTITY: Rent a Scoot – Morden is a regional scooter hire provider. Address: 248 Martin Way, Morden SM4 4AW. Website: rentascoot.co.uk. Operations are limited to Surrey and Greater London. The provider has fixed premises and is closed on Fridays and Sundays.

ELIGIBILITY CONSTRAINTS: Strict eligibility criteria apply: minimum age 21; current provisional licence with CBT pass or full UK licence; no current penalty points endorsed on licence (any points endorsed is an absolute exclusion — stricter than Ugo Scooters which permits up to 6); verified identity by utility bill or bank statement not older than 3 months.

MINIMUM TERM AND FINANCIAL REQUIREMENTS: The minimum rental period is 4 weeks — the longest of the three profiled providers and significantly more restrictive than a typical credit hire period. Advance payment of 4 weeks' hire plus a £300 deposit is required at the start of hire. A claimant whose credit hire period is shorter than 4 weeks cannot realistically be regarded as having had access to this provider on equivalent terms.

DELIVERY AND COLLECTION: Delivery is not routinely available. Where delivery is offered it is chargeable as an extra. Critically, even if delivery is arranged, the client must first attend the depot in person to complete documentation and show identification before the vehicle can be delivered. This effectively requires a depot visit regardless of whether delivery is ultimately used.

INSURANCE POSITION: Third-party only insurance is included. The hirer remains fully liable for damage to the hired vehicle and fully liable to reimburse in the event of theft. No comprehensive cover is offered. Food delivery is permitted under the insurance basis.

MILEAGE: 300 miles per week are included. Additional miles are charged at £0.10/mile. This is the only one of the three profiled providers to impose a mileage limitation. In cases where the claimant's use pattern exceeds 300 miles/week, the effective weekly cost rises accordingly and the comparison rate must be adjusted.
NOTICE PERIOD ON TERMINATION: After the 4-week minimum term, 7 days' notice is required to terminate the hire. Early return within the minimum period does not entitle the hirer to a refund.

HIRE RATES (valid 01/12/2025): E-Skuta SX-250 (electric): £60/week (deposit £200). Piaggio Liberty: £110/week (deposit £300). Honda Vision: £110/week (deposit £300). Piaggio Medley: £125/week (deposit £300).

COMPARATOR VALIDITY ASSESSMENT: This supplier may constitute a valid BHR comparator only where all of the following conditions are met: (1) the claimant is aged 21 or over; (2) the claimant holds a qualifying licence with no endorsed penalty points whatsoever; (3) the claimant can provide identity documentation no older than 3 months; (4) the claimant can fund a £300 deposit plus 4 weeks' advance hire payment at commencement; (5) the hire period is at least 4 weeks; (6) the claimant resides or operates within Surrey or Greater London; (7) the claimant is able to attend the Morden depot in person before delivery or collection is arranged; (8) the mileage usage is consistent with the 300 miles/week allowance (or the excess mileage charge has been factored into the comparison rate). The zero-points licence requirement is the most frequently operative exclusion. Any claimant with even a single SP30 endorsement cannot hire from this provider and the comparator accordingly falls away.

FINANCIAL STANDING (contextual only): Companies House accounts to 30/06/2024 show approximately £2,000 annual profit. This field is contextual and should not be relied upon as a standalone eligibility criterion unless separately justified.
articles.content: Rent a Scoot – Morden is a regional scooter hire provider operating within Surrey and Greater London. Hire is subject to a minimum four-week term, advance payment and deposit, and strict eligibility criteria including age, licence type, absence of endorsements, and proof of address. Insurance provided is third-party only, with the hirer retaining full liability for damage and theft. Delivery is not routinely available and is chargeable if provided although the client still must visit the premises to complete documentation beforehand. Where a claimant satisfies these criteria and the minimum hire period aligns with the credit hire duration, the provider may represent a valid comparator; otherwise, rates derived from this supplier may not reflect a realistic alternative.
articles.summary: Rent a Scoot – Morden is a regional scooter hire provider operating within Surrey and Greater London. Hire is subject to a minimum four-week term, advance payment and deposit, and strict eligibility criteria including age, licence type, absence of endorsements, and proof of address. Insurance provided is third-party only, with the hirer retaining full liability for damage and theft. Delivery is not routinely available and is chargeable if provided although the client still must visit the premises to complete documentation beforehand. Where a claimant satisfies these criteria and the minimum hire period aligns with the credit hire duration, the provider may represent a valid comparator; otherwise, rates derived from this supplier may not reflect a realistic alternative.

articles.title: Claims Handler Query 9: Bank Statements — Why Do Insurers Ask Whether the Claimant Pays Bills Through His Bank Account?
articles.source: Steve Evans, The Credit Hire Forum
articles.author: Steve Evans
principles: THE QUESTION IN CONTEXT: Where a claimant asserts impecuniosity — that is, that he was unable to fund the cost of hire or repair from a mainstream supplier without making unreasonable sacrifice — the third-party insurer will require financial disclosure to test that assertion. The most common form of disclosure is bank statements for the three months preceding and covering the period of hire. The insurer's claims handler will then conduct a detailed review of those statements to identify whether the claimant genuinely lacks the financial resources he claims to lack. A central line of enquiry is whether the statements show evidence of the claimant paying his ordinary bills — rent, mortgage, council tax, utilities, phone, subscriptions and other regular outgoings — through the disclosed account.

WHY THE BILLS QUESTION MATTERS: If a claimant's bank statements show that he receives income and makes regular payments for everyday living expenses, those statements are likely to be a complete picture of his financial life. But if income is received without any visible evidence of rent, council tax, utility payments or loan repayments — expenditure that almost every adult incurs and almost always pays through a bank — the insurer will draw one of three inferences: (1) the claimant has an undisclosed bank or building society account through which he manages his everyday financial affairs; (2) the claimant has undisclosed cash or savings from which those expenses are met; or (3) the expenses are not being paid at all, which may indicate that someone else pays them (a parent, partner or landlord) in which case the claimant's true financial position may be very different from the picture presented. Any of these inferences, if substantiated, may undermine the impecuniosity case and, if the claimant has signed a statement of truth in support of financial disclosure, potentially give rise to an allegation of fundamental dishonesty.

THE OPEN BANKING RESPONSE — WHY CLAIMANT FIRMS USE IT: To counter this risk, experienced credit hire solicitors will use open banking software at the point of financial disclosure rather than simply requesting paper statements from the client. Open banking platforms — the principal providers in the credit hire market being ISAGI, Atto, and Experian — connect directly to the claimant's bank and financial institution accounts with the claimant's consent and pull a complete, verified record of all transactions across all connected accounts. This serves two purposes. First, it identifies whether the claimant has additional accounts that he may not have volunteered — a savings account, a credit union account, a second current account — so that these can be disclosed proactively rather than discovered adversarially. Second, it enables a forensic review of the full transaction history before disclosure, allowing the solicitor to identify and explain any transactions that may look suspicious or inconsistent — large cash withdrawals, irregular credits, gaps in regular outgoings — before the insurer raises them.

FORENSIC REVIEW — WHAT TO LOOK FOR: The forensic review of bank statements in an impecuniosity case should address the following categories of potential problem: (a) Rogue credits — unexplained deposits that may suggest undisclosed income, a transfer from a savings account, or receipts the claimant cannot account for; (b) Missing outgoings — the absence of any rent, mortgage, council tax, utility or loan payments, which raises the undisclosed account question; (c) Cash withdrawals — large or regular cash withdrawals may suggest the claimant operates partly in cash and has resources not visible on the statements; (d) Gambling transactions — deposits to or withdrawals from gambling platforms are frequently seized upon by defendant insurers and require careful handling; (e) Irregular patterns — large balances followed by sudden reduction, or periods of apparent affluence, that are inconsistent with the claimed financial position; (f) High-value discretionary spending — holidays, luxury goods purchases, or subscription services that are inconsistent with a claimant who genuinely lacks the means to fund hire or repair.

THE UNDISCLOSED ACCOUNT PROBLEM: The most damaging scenario for a claimant's impecuniosity case is the discovery by the defendant of a bank or savings account that was not disclosed. Open banking platforms address this by pulling a credit profile that identifies all financial accounts in the claimant's name at the time the consent is given — including dormant accounts, credit union accounts, and savings products that the claimant may simply have forgotten about or may have hoped would not be noticed. Where an undisclosed account is identified and not volunteered in disclosure, the defendant will argue that the claimant has deliberately concealed assets, that the impecuniosity claim is false, and that the claim is fundamentally dishonest. A finding of fundamental dishonesty under s.57 of the Criminal Justice and Courts Act 2015 results in dismissal of the entire claim including any personal injury element and an order for the defendant's costs.

PRACTICAL GUIDANCE FOR CLAIMS HANDLERS: When a claimant's bank statements are received, the claims handler should check: (1) whether regular bill payments (rent/mortgage, council tax, utilities, phone) are visible — if not, raise the undisclosed account point; (2) whether all income sources are consistent with the claimant's stated employment and financial position; (3) whether there are cash withdrawals of a pattern or scale inconsistent with genuine impecuniosity; (4) whether the balance at the time of hire is genuinely insufficient to fund a mainstream hire deposit and rental advance; (5) whether the statements cover the full three-month period requested — gaps or missing months should be queried. Where any of these issues is identified, the handler should request an explanation in writing before accepting the impecuniosity position or authorising settlement at credit hire rates.

THE ISAGI, ATTO AND EXPERIAN PLATFORMS: Open banking in the credit hire market is currently provided principally by three platforms. ISAGI provides a credit check report (currently priced at approximately £20) which also flags IVAs and other insolvency markers. Atto and Experian provide broader open banking and financial data services. All three operate under FCA authorisation and require the claimant's explicit consent before accessing account data. The data is provided in real time and reflects live balances and transaction histories rather than retrospective paper statements, which makes it significantly harder for a claimant to manage what is disclosed. Claimant solicitors who use open banking effectively are in a stronger position to proactively identify and address vulnerabilities before disclosure rather than having them surfaced for the first time in the defendant's Part 18 questions or at trial.
cases: Criminal Justice and Courts Act 2015 s.57 (fundamental dishonesty)
articles.content: Where a claimant asserts impecuniosity, the third-party insurer will scrutinise financial disclosure to identify whether he has undisclosed accounts, cash, savings, or is simply not telling the truth. The key investigative tool is the bank statement review: if rent, council tax, utilities and other everyday bills are not visible on the disclosed account, the inference is that a second account exists or that cash is being used — either of which undermines the impecuniosity case and may support a fundamental dishonesty allegation. Claimant solicitors counter this through open banking platforms (ISAGI, Atto, Experian) which pull a complete verified picture of all accounts, enabling a forensic review before disclosure to identify and address rogue transactions, missing outgoings, undisclosed accounts and inconsistent patterns before the defendant can raise them.

articles.title: BHR Supplier Profile — Hertz (UK)
articles.source: Hertz UK Rental Terms and Conditions (composite of multiple topic PDFs)
articles.author: credit-hire.ai
principles: Standard minimum age is 25; drivers aged 21-24 may hire subject to a young driver surcharge of £61.43 per day / £430 per week
Higher minimum ages apply to premium and prestige vehicles: 27 for Audi Q7/Mercedes V Class; 28 for Porsche/Range Rover Sport; 30 for Jaguar F-Type/Bentley; 35 for minibus (M4)
Driving licence must have been held for at least one year; three years for the M4 minibus group
A DVLA check code (Share Driving Licence) is required and must be obtained within 21 days of rental start
Endorsements DD, DR, UT and IN with disqualifications of 12 months or more must have expired at least 5 years before the rental date
Short disqualifications, speeding, and points accumulation: renter may hire from the date the endorsement expires
All other codes: if 6 or fewer points accrued in any 3-year period, the licence may be accepted — unless points relate to code IN
Third party liability insurance is included in all rental rates as standard
CDW is optional; if declined the renter is liable for the full current replacement value of the vehicle
A non-waivable excess applies regardless of CDW: ranges from £1,470 to £5,250 depending on vehicle group
A damage administration fee of £45 applies to all damage claims
Deposit/pre-authorisation: minimum £200 plus £500 if SuperCover is not purchased; may include prepaid fuel
Additional drivers: £20.60 per day / £144.23 per week; main driver must be present at time of addition
European cross-border travel is prohibited for UK rentals following Brexit; travel between Great Britain, Northern Ireland and Republic of Ireland is permitted subject to VE103B certificate
Delivery and collection: available but not bookable online; charges apply
Prohibited uses include carriage of passengers for hire or reward, motor racing, and driving under the influence
articles.content:
Hertz is one of the principal car hire comparators cited by defendants in BHR challenges. The following eligibility criteria are drawn from Hertz UK published terms and conditions.

AGE REQUIREMENTS
The standard minimum age for most vehicle groups is 25 years. Drivers aged 21-24 may hire but attract a young driver surcharge of £61.43 per day, £430 per week or £1,719.98 maximum per rental. The minimum age rises to 27 for Audi Q7, Mercedes V Class Extra LWB and Land Rover Range Rover Velar; to 28 for Porsche Cayenne S, Range Rover Sport Supercharged and Porsche Boxster 718; to 30 for Jaguar F-TYPE, Mercedes C63 AMG, Audi R8 Spider, Bentley GTC Speed, Porsche 992 4S and Porsche Panamera 4S; and to 35 for the Ford Transit 17-seat minibus (M4).

DRIVING LICENCE
A valid licence held for at least one year is required. The M4 minibus requires three years and a D1 entitlement. A DVLA Share Driving Licence check code (valid 21 days) is required. Laminated paper licences and digital licences are not accepted. Northern Ireland holders must produce both the photocard and paper counterpart.

ENDORSEMENTS AND CONVICTIONS
Codes DD, DR, UT and IN where the disqualification was 12 months or more: the endorsement expiry must be at least 5 years before the rental. For short disqualifications, speeding and points accumulation, hiring may proceed once the endorsement has expired. For all other codes, if 6 or fewer points have been accrued in any 3-year period the licence may be accepted, provided none relate to code IN.

INSURANCE
Third party liability is included as standard. CDW is an optional extra; if declined the renter bears the full current replacement value of the vehicle for any damage. A non-waivable excess applies in any event, ranging from £1,470 for a standard hatchback to £5,250 for high-performance prestige vehicles. A £45 damage administration fee is charged on all claims. SuperCover (excess waiver) and Glass & Tyres are available as optional products.

DEPOSIT
A deposit or pre-authorisation is taken at collection: minimum £200, plus £500 if SuperCover is not purchased, plus the cost of fuel and any optional extras. The deposit may take up to 30 days to be released after the rental ends.

ADDITIONAL DRIVERS
Additional drivers cost £20.60 per day or £144.23 per week (maximum £206.04 per rental). The main renter must be present at the branch when adding additional drivers.

GEOGRAPHIC RESTRICTIONS
Since 1 January 2021 UK-renting customers are not permitted to drive into any European country. Cross-border travel between Great Britain, Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland is permitted with a VE103B certificate from Hertz (charged separately). Travel to the Isle of Wight and Scottish Islands is also restricted.

PROHIBITED USES
The vehicle may not be used for carriage of passengers for hire or reward, motor racing, driving under the influence of alcohol or drugs, or by any driver not named on the rental agreement.

articles.title: BHR Supplier Profile — Enterprise Rent-A-Car (UK)
articles.source: Enterprise UK Rental Agreement (January 2025) and Extra Costs Guide (March 2026)
articles.author: credit-hire.ai
principles: Minimum age is 21 years; no maximum age is specified
The driving licence must have been held for at least 12 months
Licence validation is conducted via the DVLA Share Driving Licence online service
A Damage Waiver (DW) is included as standard in all UK rentals; excess applies per incident: £1,750-£2,250 depending on vehicle type
Excess Protection (EP) is available optionally to reduce the excess to £100-£250
Third party liability is included when '3RD PARTY COVER INCLUDED' appears on the rental agreement
Cash, cheques and prepaid/preloaded cards are not accepted for payment
A security deposit is required by credit or debit card at commencement
UK mainland rentals include unlimited mileage
A free pickup service within 10 miles of the branch is available at non-airport locations
Travel outside the UK without prior written consent and a VE103B form is prohibited
Premium location surcharge of up to 20% applies at airports, major terminals and railway stations
Administration fee of up to £45 for processing traffic fines and offences
Prohibited uses include carriage for hire or reward, racing, driving under the influence and off-road use
articles.content:
Enterprise Rent-A-Car is a principal BHR comparator cited by defendants. The following eligibility criteria are drawn from Enterprise UK published terms effective January 2025.

AGE REQUIREMENTS
Minimum age is 21 years. No maximum age is stated. A young driver policy applies for drivers aged 21-24; additional charges may apply.

DRIVING LICENCE
A full and valid driving licence held for at least 12 months is required. Validation is via the DVLA Share Driving Licence service. The penalty point policy is not explicitly quantified in the published terms; the DVLA check is used to assess the driving record at the branch.

INSURANCE
A Damage Waiver (DW) is included as standard in all UK rental rates. The excess per incident is £1,750-£2,250 depending on vehicle type. Excess Protection (EP) is available as an optional extra to reduce the excess to £100-£250. Third party liability cover is included and must appear on the rental agreement.

PAYMENT AND DEPOSIT
A security deposit is required at rental commencement. Cash, cheques and prepaid/preloaded cards are not accepted. Debit cards, credit cards and hire vouchers are all accepted.

MILEAGE
UK mainland rentals include unlimited mileage as standard.

DELIVERY
A free pickup service is available at non-airport locations, within a 10-mile radius of the branch. One-way drop-offs to a different branch attract an additional charge.

GEOGRAPHIC RESTRICTIONS
Vehicles may not be taken outside the UK without prior written consent and a VE103B form. Cross-Border and Europrodrive packs are available at additional cost.

PROHIBITED USES
Carriage for hire or reward, racing, off-road driving, driving under the influence, and smoking in the vehicle are all prohibited.

articles.title: BHR Supplier Profile — Europcar (UK)
articles.source: Europcar UK Terms and Conditions (effective 5 January 2026) and Tariff Guide
articles.author: credit-hire.ai
principles: Minimum age is 22 years (higher than most mainstream competitors at 21)
A maximum age of 75 applies at York, Jersey, Guernsey and Orkney locations
Driving licence must have been held for at least 12 months
DVLA Share Driving Licence validation is mandatory; NI number and home postcode required; code valid for 21 days
CDW and Theft Waiver are included in the basic hire charge, reducing liability to the stated excess
Default UK excess is £2,000 where no excess is stated on the rental agreement
Excess Protection (EP) is available optionally; without EP the renter is liable for first £900 above the standard excess
Third party liability insurance is included in the basic hire charge
Physical payment card required — digital/contactless versions (Apple Pay, Samsung Pay) not acceptable
Animals (except Assistance Dogs) require prior written consent under the Animal Policy
Vehicles fitted with electronic devices detecting unauthorised cross-border travel
Travel to Europe requires Europrodrive Pack; travel to Republic of Ireland requires Cross-Border Pack; travel to UK islands requires Island Explorer Pack
Premium location surcharge of up to 20% applies at airports, ferry terminals and major city stations
Selection group vehicles require a minimum driver age of 25 or 30 and credit card only payment
articles.content:
Europcar is a principal BHR comparator. The following eligibility criteria are drawn from Europcar UK Terms and Conditions effective 5 January 2026.

AGE REQUIREMENTS
The minimum age is 22 years — one year higher than most mainstream competitors. A maximum age of 75 applies at York, Jersey, Guernsey and Orkney locations. Young driver surcharges apply for drivers aged 22-25. Selection range premium vehicles have minimum ages of 25 or 30.

DRIVING LICENCE
A full and valid licence held for at least 12 months is required. DVLA validation via Share Driving Licence is mandatory: the renter must provide their NI number and home postcode. The check code is valid for 21 days only. Non-UK licences must be clearly identifiable as a driving licence; an IDP may also be required.

INSURANCE
CDW and Theft Waiver are included in the basic hire package. These reduce the renter's liability to the stated excess — where not specified, the UK default excess is £2,000. Without Excess Protection (EP), the renter remains liable for the first £900 above any excess threshold. Third party liability is included.

PAYMENT
Payment must be made by physical card presented in person — digital wallet representations (Apple Pay, Samsung Pay) are not acceptable. Cash and cheques are not accepted. Selection group vehicles accept credit card only.

PET POLICY
Live animals are permitted (other than Assistance Dogs) but require prior written consent under Europcar's Animal Policy.

GEOGRAPHIC RESTRICTIONS
All vehicles are fitted with electronic tracking devices. Unauthorised cross-border travel triggers automatic charges for the relevant pack. European travel requires the Europrodrive Pack; Republic of Ireland travel requires the Cross-Border Pack; UK island travel requires the Island Explorer Pack.

PROHIBITED USES
Carriage for hire or reward, racing, off-road driving, driving under the influence, fitting roof racks or tow bars without consent, and smoking in the vehicle are all prohibited.

articles.title: BHR Supplier Profile — Avis (UK)
articles.source: Avis UK General Conditions of Rental and UK Country Conditions (March 2026)
articles.author: credit-hire.ai
principles: Minimum age is 21 years; no standard maximum age (affiliate locations may impose a limit of 75-80)
Driving licence must have been held for at least 1 year for cars and 2 years for vans
DVLA licence check is conducted at the rental counter
Endorsements for careless/dangerous driving, drink-driving, uninsured driving and insurance offences may result in refusal
Third party liability insurance is included in the standard hire rate
CDW is an optional extra; if declined the renter bears full liability for vehicle damage
Non-waivable excess is approximately £300 on UK mainland; £1,700-£2,500 in Northern Ireland
A credit card pre-authorisation of approximately £300-£2,500 is required at vehicle collection
Young driver surcharge applies to drivers aged 21-24 at approximately £40 per day
Geographic restrictions: UK only; cross-border use requires prior permission and additional documentation
Prohibited uses include carriage for hire or reward, racing, off-road driving and driving under the influence
articles.content:
Avis is a principal BHR comparator cited by defendants. The following eligibility criteria are drawn from Avis UK General Conditions of Rental and UK Country Conditions dated March 2026.

AGE REQUIREMENTS
The minimum age is 21 years. No standard maximum age applies, though certain affiliate locations may set a maximum of 75 to 80 years. A young driver surcharge of approximately £40 per day applies to drivers aged 21-24.

DRIVING LICENCE
A full and valid licence held for at least one year (cars) or two years (vans) is required. Endorsements for careless or dangerous driving, drink-driving, being uninsured or insurance offences may result in rental refusal. An DVLA licence check is conducted at the rental counter.

INSURANCE
Third party liability insurance is included as standard. CDW is an optional extra. If CDW is declined the renter bears full liability for vehicle damage. A non-waivable excess of approximately £300 applies on UK mainland; in Northern Ireland this rises to approximately £1,700-£2,500 depending on location.

DEPOSIT
A credit card pre-authorisation of approximately £300-£2,500 is required at collection, depending on vehicle type and protection products selected.

GEOGRAPHIC RESTRICTIONS
The vehicle may not be taken outside the UK without prior permission and appropriate cross-border documentation.

PROHIBITED USES
Carriage for hire or reward, racing, off-road use and driving under the influence are all prohibited.

articles.title: BHR Supplier Profile — Budget (UK)
articles.source: Budget UK General Conditions of Rental and Booking Conditions (November 2025 and March 2026)
articles.author: credit-hire.ai
principles: Minimum age is 21 years; no maximum age specified
Driving licence must have been held for at least 1 year
DVLA licence validation is conducted at the rental counter
Serious conviction endorsements may result in rental refusal
Third party liability insurance is included in the standard hire rate
CDW is an optional extra; if not selected the renter bears full liability for vehicle damage
Non-waivable excess varies by vehicle type and protection package selected
Credit card pre-authorisation is required at vehicle collection
Young driver surcharge applies to drivers aged 21-24; rate varies by location
Geographic restrictions: UK only; cross-border use requires prior permission
Prohibited uses include carriage for hire or reward, racing, off-road use and driving under the influence
articles.content:
Budget is a principal BHR comparator cited by defendants. The following eligibility criteria are drawn from Budget UK General Conditions of Rental (March 2026) and Booking Conditions (November 2025).

AGE REQUIREMENTS
The minimum age is 21 years. No maximum age is stated. A young driver surcharge applies to drivers aged 21-24; the rate varies by location.

DRIVING LICENCE
A full and valid licence held for at least one year is required. Serious conviction endorsements may result in rental refusal. DVLA validation is conducted at the rental counter.

INSURANCE
Third party liability insurance is included as standard. CDW is an optional extra; if not selected the renter is liable for the full cost of any damage to the vehicle. The non-waivable excess varies by vehicle type and the protection package chosen.

DEPOSIT
A credit card pre-authorisation is required at collection; the amount varies according to vehicle type and options selected.

GEOGRAPHIC RESTRICTIONS
The vehicle may not be taken outside the UK without prior permission and appropriate documentation.

PROHIBITED USES
Carriage for hire or reward, racing, off-road use and driving under the influence are all prohibited.

articles.title: BHR Supplier Profile — Sixt (UK)
articles.source: Sixt GB General Terms and Conditions and UK Rental Information (March 2026)
articles.author: credit-hire.ai
principles: Minimum age is 21 years for standard vehicles; no maximum age stated
Driving licence must have been held for at least 1 year; DVLA check required
Young driver surcharge applies to drivers under 25 years
CDW is not included in the standard rate; multiple protection product tiers are available
Geographic restriction: vehicles may be used in Great Britain only; travel to Northern Ireland, Republic of Ireland and Europe requires additional packs and fees
Sixt+ subscription (minimum 30-day term) offers unlimited mileage
Standard short-term rentals: mileage limit varies by vehicle class
Corporate customers may be required to present a company credit card
Prohibited uses include carriage for hire or reward, racing, off-road use and driving under the influence
Occupation restrictions may apply for certain high-risk professions
articles.content:
Sixt is a principal BHR comparator cited by defendants. The following eligibility criteria are drawn from Sixt GB General Terms and Conditions and UK Rental Information dated March 2026.

AGE REQUIREMENTS
Minimum age is 21 years for standard vehicles. A young driver surcharge applies to drivers under 25. No maximum age is specified.

DRIVING LICENCE
A full and valid licence held for at least one year is required. DVLA validation is conducted at the rental counter.

INSURANCE
CDW is not included in the standard hire rate; Sixt offers a tiered range of protection products. Third party liability insurance is included as standard. Without an appropriate protection product the renter is liable for the full cost of damage.

GEOGRAPHIC RESTRICTIONS
Sixt UK vehicles may be used in Great Britain only. Travel to Northern Ireland, the Republic of Ireland or continental Europe requires separate packs purchased in advance and attracts additional charges. This geographic restriction is notably more limited than most competitors (e.g. Enterprise and Enterprise include Northern Ireland as standard territory).

MILEAGE
The Sixt+ subscription product (minimum 30-day term) includes unlimited mileage. Standard short-term rental mileage limits vary by vehicle class and are set out in the rental agreement.

PROHIBITED USES
Carriage for hire or reward, racing, off-road use and driving under the influence are all prohibited. Certain occupation restrictions may apply.

articles.title: BHR Supplier Profile — Thrifty (UK)
articles.source: Thrifty UK Rental Agreement Terms and Conditions (March 2026)
articles.author: credit-hire.ai
principles: Minimum age is 22 years — higher than Avis, Budget, Enterprise, Hertz, National and Sixt which all permit hiring from age 21
No maximum age for standard vehicles; drivers aged 70 or over cannot purchase the Damage Liability Reduction product
Insurance with damage liability cover is included in the standard hire rate (unlike most competitors where CDW is optional)
Non-waivable excess: £1,000 (Economy to Standard group); £1,250 (Full Size to Prestige); £1,500-£5,000 (Luxury and Elite classes)
Mandatory mileage limits: 100 miles per day (rentals of 1-14 days); 70 miles per day (15-27 days); 2,000 miles per 28-day period
Excess mileage charged at 15p + VAT per mile (standard vehicles) and 50p + VAT per mile (Luxury and Elite)
Young driver surcharge of £35.00 per day (including VAT) applies to drivers aged 22-24
Credit or debit card required; cash and cheques not accepted
Geographic restriction: UK only; overseas use is not available
Prohibited uses include carriage for hire or reward, racing, off-road use and driving under the influence
articles.content:
Thrifty is a BHR comparator cited by defendants. The following eligibility criteria are drawn from Thrifty UK Rental Agreement Terms and Conditions reviewed in March 2026.

AGE REQUIREMENTS
The minimum age is 22 years — one year higher than most mainstream competitors. No maximum age applies for standard vehicles, but drivers aged 70 or over cannot purchase the Damage Liability Reduction product, leaving them exposed to the full excess. A young driver surcharge of £35.00 per day (including VAT) applies to all drivers aged 22-24.

INSURANCE
Thrifty is notable among mainstream comparators in that insurance with damage liability cover is included in the standard hire rate. A non-waivable excess remains: £1,000 for Economy to Standard group vehicles; £1,250 for Full Size to Prestige; £1,500 to £5,000 for Luxury and Elite classes.

MILEAGE RESTRICTIONS
Thrifty imposes mandatory mileage limits: 100 miles per day for rentals of 1-14 days; 70 miles per day for rentals of 15-27 days; and 2,000 miles for any 28-day period. Excess mileage is charged at 15p + VAT per mile (standard) or 50p + VAT per mile (Luxury and Elite). This restriction is significant in BHR disputes where high mileage usage is at issue.

PAYMENT
A credit or debit card is required at collection. Cash and cheques are not accepted.

GEOGRAPHIC RESTRICTIONS
Vehicles may not be taken outside the UK under any circumstances.

PROHIBITED USES
Carriage for hire or reward, racing, off-road use and driving under the influence are all prohibited.

articles.title: BHR Supplier Profile — National Car Rental (UK)
articles.source: National Car Rental UK website guidance pages (26 March 2026)
articles.author: credit-hire.ai
principles: Minimum age is 21 years; no maximum age stated
Driving licence must be full and valid; DVLA check conducted at rental counter
Loss Damage Waiver (LDW) is an optional product; not included in the standard hire rate
LDW options include full coverage or LDW 3000 (renter pays first £3,000 above the standard excess)
A major credit card is required at rental commencement; debit cards may not be accepted at all locations
Young driver fee applies to renters aged 21-24; rate varies by location
Rental requirements include a valid driving licence, a major credit card and, where applicable, proof of identity
Additional drivers must meet the same eligibility requirements as the main renter and are charged at an additional daily rate
No towing is permitted unless Thrifty/National express permission is granted
Pets: policy not specified in published materials reviewed
Geographic restrictions: UK locations only; cross-border restrictions apply
articles.content:
National Car Rental is a BHR comparator cited by defendants. The following eligibility criteria are drawn from National Car Rental UK published guidance pages reviewed on 26 March 2026.

AGE REQUIREMENTS
The minimum age is 21 years. No maximum age is stated. A young renter fee applies to drivers aged 21-24; the specific rate varies by location.

DRIVING LICENCE
