A full and valid driving licence is required. DVLA validation is conducted at the rental counter. No specific penalty point threshold is published; National's DVLA check will assess the driving record.

INSURANCE
Loss Damage Waiver (LDW) is an optional product, not included in the standard hire rate. Options include full LDW coverage or LDW 3000, under which the renter remains responsible for the first £3,000 above the standard excess threshold. Third party liability insurance is included as standard.

PAYMENT
A major credit card is required at rental commencement. Debit card acceptance varies by location.

ADDITIONAL DRIVERS
Additional drivers must meet the same eligibility requirements as the main renter and attract an additional daily charge.

GEOGRAPHIC RESTRICTIONS
Vehicles may only be used within the UK. Cross-border restrictions apply and prior written permission is required for travel outside the UK.

PROHIBITED USES
Towing is prohibited without express permission. Carriage for hire or reward and driving under the influence are prohibited.

articles.title: BHR Supplier Profile — AMT Auto (UK)
articles.source: AMT Auto Pricing Guide and Terms and Conditions (March 2026)
articles.author: credit-hire.ai
principles: Strict age window: minimum 25 years, maximum 69 years — significantly more restrictive than mainstream competitors
Minimum licence holding period is 24 months (2 years) — double the 12-month requirement of most mainstream suppliers
Maximum 6 penalty points permitted; all points must be from minor conviction codes only (within last 5 years)

Major conviction codes are an absolute bar: AC10-30 (accident offences), DD10-80 (dangerous driving), DR10-90 (drink/drug driving), IN10 (no insurance), BA10-30 (disqualified driving), LC30-50 (licence offences), MS40-90 (miscellaneous serious), UT10-50 (TWOC/aggravated TWOC), CD40-70 (causing death by careless driving)
Maximum 2 accidents in last 3 years; or maximum 1 accident with costs exceeding £5,000 in last 3 years

Occupation restrictions exclude: hawkers, scrap merchants, vehicle breakers, professional gamblers, professional sportspeople and entertainers, models, non-UK armed forces, students (except private cars only), and unemployed persons
Comprehensive insurance is provided by AMT or renter's own fully comprehensive insurance may be used
Mileage fair use policy: 300 miles per day (up to 7-day rentals); 1,000 miles per week (7-28 days)
Maximum hire period: 84 days unless otherwise agreed in writing
Vehicle fitted with telematics device; mileage and location data collected and may be shared with finance companies
Geographic restriction: UK only; prior written permission required to take vehicle outside the United Kingdom
articles.content:
AMT Auto is a BHR comparator cited by defendants, particularly in longer-term credit hire cases. The following eligibility criteria are drawn from AMT Auto Terms and Conditions and Pricing Guide reviewed in March 2026. AMT's eligibility criteria are considerably more restrictive than those of mainstream rental companies.

AGE REQUIREMENTS
AMT imposes a strict age window of 25 to 69 years. Hirers below 25 or above 69 years are ineligible as a standard rule; exceptional cases may be considered on a case-by-case basis at AMT's discretion.

DRIVING LICENCE
The licence must have been held for at least 24 months (2 years). The maximum permitted penalty points is 6, and all points must arise from minor conviction codes only within the past 5 years. The following codes are an absolute bar within the last 5 years: AC10-AC30, DD10-DD80, LC30-LC50, BA10-BA30, DR10-DR90, MS40-MS90, UT10-UT50, CD40-CD70, IN10, NE99 and XX99.

ACCIDENT HISTORY
A maximum of 2 at-fault accidents in the last 3 years is permitted, or only 1 accident if the repair costs exceeded £5,000.

OCCUPATION RESTRICTIONS
AMT explicitly excludes the following occupational categories: hawkers, market traders and general dealers; scrap merchants, vehicle dismantlers and breakers; professional gamblers (except clerical staff); professional sportspeople and entertainers (including part-time); models; members of non-UK armed forces; students (private cars are an exception); and unemployed persons.

INSURANCE
Comprehensive insurance is provided by AMT for an additional charge. Alternatively, the renter may use their own fully comprehensive insurance, provided it is endorsed to cover the hired vehicle to its full value and AMT's interest is noted.

MILEAGE
AMT applies a fair use mileage policy: 300 miles per day for rentals up to 7 days, and 1,000 miles per week (up to 2,800 miles per 28-day period) for longer rentals. Excess mileage attracts additional charges per the published tariff.

MINIMUM AND MAXIMUM HIRE PERIODS
The maximum hire period is 84 days without written agreement to extend. A minimum period is not specified for daily hire.

TELEMATICS
All AMT vehicles are fitted with telematics tracking devices. Data on vehicle location and mileage is collected and may be transferred to finance companies and their worldwide group companies.

GEOGRAPHIC RESTRICTIONS
Vehicles may not be taken outside the United Kingdom without prior written permission.

NOTE FOR CREDIT HIRE CASES
AMT's occupation and accident history restrictions mean that a substantial proportion of claimants who are employed in certain industries, unemployed at the time of hire, students, or who have been in a recent fault accident may not qualify. The 25-year minimum age and 69-year maximum also eliminate younger and older claimants. These restrictions are material to any BHR challenge citing AMT as a comparator.

articles.title: BHR Supplier Profile — Arnold Clark Car & Van Rental (UK)
articles.source: Arnold Clark Car & Van Rental Full Terms & Conditions, Terms of Hire and Insurance Information (March 2026)
articles.author: credit-hire.ai
principles: Standard minimum age is 23 years for most car groups (up to SUV/estate/automatic); 25 years required for all vehicle groups
Young driver surcharge of £25 per day applies to all drivers under 25 years
Minimum licence holding period: 4 years for drivers under 25 years; 1 year if the driver is 23 or older
Licence must be clean; penalty points should be declared and acceptance is at the rental location manager's discretion
Comprehensive insurance is included in the rental price for UK mainland only; Northern Ireland and Republic of Ireland require one week's advance notice and attract additional charges
Standard excess for cars: £1,500; Arnold Clark reserves the right to increase the excess after each incident

An excess waiver product is available but does not cover: lost/damaged keys, interior damage, damage from hitting low-level objects (bridges, branches), driving through water, and recovery from off-road locations
Mileage: 250 miles per day included (rentals up to 3 days); unlimited mileage for rentals of 4 or more days; excess mileage charged at 10p per mile
Long-term hire (28+ days): 2,500 miles included; excess at 10p per mile
Deposit: up to £150 at standard branches; £500 for executive cars; £1,000 for luxury vehicles — all payable by credit or debit card
Pre-paid credit cards and cash not accepted; PayPal, Apple Pay and Google Pay are accepted online but physical card must be produced at collection
Vans may not be taken outside UK mainland; cars may travel to Republic of Ireland with one week's notice
Pet policy: pets allowed with precautions; renter charged for any valeting, odour treatment or loss of rental revenue if vehicle is returned in unacceptable condition
Airport security charge of £20 applies at all airport branch locations
articles.content:
Arnold Clark Car & Van Rental is a BHR comparator, particularly in Scottish and northern English credit hire disputes. The following eligibility criteria are drawn from Arnold Clark published terms reviewed in March 2026.

AGE REQUIREMENTS
The minimum age is 23 years for standard cars (including automatics, estates and SUVs). The full range of vehicle groups is only available to drivers aged 25 and over. A young driver surcharge of £25 per day applies to all drivers under 25 years of age. Luxury car hire (available at Glasgow and Edinburgh Airport only) requires a minimum of 2 days.

DRIVING LICENCE
The licence must have been held for at least 4 years if the driver is under 25, or at least 1 year if the driver is 23 or older. The licence must be clean; any penalty points must be declared. Acceptance of an endorsed licence is at the discretion of the individual rental location manager. EU and non-EU licence holders must produce a physical licence; digital licences are not accepted.

INSURANCE
Comprehensive insurance is included in the rental price for UK mainland only. Travel to Northern Ireland or the Republic of Ireland requires at least one week's advance notice and will attract additional charges. Vans may not leave UK mainland under any circumstances. The standard excess for cars is £1,500; Arnold Clark reserves the right to increase the excess following each damage incident. An optional excess waiver is available but has significant exclusions including keys, interior damage, low-level object strikes, water crossing damage and off-road recovery.

MILEAGE
Rentals up to 3 days include 250 miles per day; excess mileage is charged at 10p per mile. Rentals of 4 or more days include unlimited mileage. Long-term hires (28+ days) include 2,500 miles with excess at 10p per mile.

DEPOSIT
A security deposit of up to £150 is required at standard branches; £500 for executive cars; £1,000 for luxury vehicles. Cash and pre-paid credit cards are not accepted.

PET POLICY
Pets may be transported with appropriate precautions. If the vehicle is returned with animal hair or odours, the renter is charged for valeting, specialist odour treatment and any loss of rental revenue during reconditioning.

GEOGRAPHIC RESTRICTIONS
Vans are restricted to UK mainland. Cars may travel to Northern Ireland and Republic of Ireland with at least one week's advance notice and additional charges. Travel to other European countries is not permitted.

NOTE FOR CREDIT HIRE CASES
Arnold Clark's primary network is Scotland and northern England. Arnold Clark is therefore most relevant as a BHR comparator in cases involving claimants in those regions. The 23-year minimum age, clean licence requirement and 4-year licence holding period for younger drivers are all potentially significant eligibility restrictions.

articles.title: GTA Technical Committee FAQs — Official Guidance
articles.source: GTA Technical Committee (www.gtacredithire.com/faqs/)
articles.author: GTA Joint Technical Committee
principles: The GTA only applies where a CHO believes the customer has good prospects of full recovery against the at-fault insurer's liability (para 1.3)
Whoever is first to a customer with a suitable offer they can understand wins; no subscriber may intervene (paras 3.1–3.2)
GTA rates exclude VAT and include unlimited mileage
The daily settlement rate covers each 24-hour period; a hire ending at 17:00 on day 8 = 8 days
Additional charges (sat nav, extras standard in vehicle) are not permitted under the GTA
CDW is not claimable within the GTA
No extra charge for additional drivers unless they are a non-standard driver
Non-standard insurance premium: £5.50/day capped at £110 (£148.50 for bike hires)
Automatics: £5/day extra only where the customer's vehicle was automatic and the hired model is not manufactured solely as automatic
Estate cars: £5/day extra only where customer's vehicle was an estate and hired model is not already an estate
Convertibles: placed one vehicle group higher than the hardtop equivalent
Dual control vehicles: £12/day add-on (£7 if no insurance cover provided)
Motorcycle topboxes: £35 + VAT where regular use demonstrated (clause 5.4)
Bike hire extras (helmets, leathers, etc.): no extra charge permitted
Administration fee: £30 before 1 Jul 2011; £31 from 1 Jul 2011 to 14 Jun 2012; £37 from 15 Jun 2012. Apportioned on split liability basis
Penalty interest applies to the administration fee
Penalty clock (para 6.8): 0% in month 1; 7.5%/12.5% in months 1–2; 15%/20% in months 2–3 (hire commencing before/after 15 Jun 2012). Percentages not cumulative
Penalty start date: date CHO receives payment (not insurer authorisation date)
Penalty payments still apply if liability not challenged within 21 days (para 6.10)
If claim unresolved within 60 days (dispute over liability, length, rate, penalty failure) CHO may remove claim from GTA
CHO can remove claim from GTA 90 days after payment pack first sent if unresolved
Payment pack: must be complete and correct; penalty clock starts when correct pack received; insurer has 30 days to raise queries (para 6.4)
CHO delays answering insurer questions: must reply within 10 working days or penalty clock stops
Loss of use dates: proof should be the rare exception, not the rule
Hire period ends no later than 24 calendar hours after repair completion (not working hours); repair complete Friday = off-hire Saturday
Hire continuing after repair: no entitlement to extend; negotiate at lower rate as exception
Total loss: hire may continue up to 7 calendar days after receipt of FINAL settlement cheque; interim cheques do not start the clock; size of hire claim irrelevant provided GTA requirements met
Total loss monitoring: para 4.14 applies; if monitoring falls down, para 6.15 applies
ABI Salvage Code does not require withholding total loss payment pending physical inspection; hire caused by CHO obstructing payment is not recoverable
Parts delay: if CHO monitors and reports correctly, insurer accepts responsibility (para 4.12); if CHO fails to monitor, para 6.15 applies

Repair monitoring (para 4.10): CHO checks garage authorisation within 3 working days; further check at lesser of 5 working days or 3 working days before hire should have ended; check 3 working days before hire should have ended
Unreasonable delays (para 4.11): 2+ working days longer than expected OR 20%+ over estimated hire period — must report to insurer
Credit repair — 66% rule: CHO must advise insurer within 1 working day of engineer's report when total loss or repair costs exceed 66% of pre-accident vehicle value
Credit repair — fraud checks should not delay repair authorisation
Credit repair — engineer should be instructed when CHO receives the claim so inspection coincides with vehicle going into repairers
Non-deliberate GTA breach (para 6.15): insurer pays GTA claim reduced only by savings that would have been achieved had breach not occurred
New Claims Advice Form (from 17 Mar 2025, clause 4.1): must be submitted within 1 working day of agreeing services; hire not recoverable beyond 1 working day until correct NCA Form submitted
Sub-hired vehicles: perfectly proper; payment pack is proof CHO provided the vehicle
Selectivity: subscriber CHOs must comply with GTA in full for all hires
GTA only applies to incidents in England, Wales, Scotland, Northern Ireland, Isle of Man and Channel Islands
Like for likes: communicate with TPI early where doubt exists over vehicle group (old/prestige/unusual vehicles)
Older prestige vehicles (FAQ 61): 'prestige' applies to any prestige marque over 6 years old across all vehicle categories, not just 'P' group
Disabled driver vehicles: reasonable direct costs + £10 admin; special adaptations to be discussed with insurer at commencement
Taxi/PHV extras: no extra additions for signage, radio hire, taxi display; actual council costs for replating/fitting different radio etc. are recoverable
Taxi insurance: CHO decides whether to apply insurance included or excluded rate; if insurance on client's own policy, £10 admin charge + excluded rate
Premium rate telephone lines not in spirit of GTA
Vehicle group disputes: contact chair@thegta.onmicrosoft.com with subject 'Vehicle group request' (GTA subscribers only; sub-committee meets 1–2 times/month)
Mitigation: CHO must confirm in mitigation statement that client had not received an insurer offer, or give reason for not accepting it

Complaints procedure: only after deadlock; formal complaint to Independent Secretary (indsec@gtacredithire.com); for principle/interpretation disputes only — not simple value arguments; GTA Chair has discretion to reject complaints
If insurer doesn't support credit repair protocol, CHO under no obligation beyond mitigating loss
Excesses / TPCHA: GTA terms only apply where insurer's policy applies; CHO may ask handling agent if they will apply GTA terms

Repair monitoring when hire and repairs managed separately (FAQ 65, 25.3.26): CHC must actively monitor even without direct control; must obtain updates, challenge delays, inform TPI promptly; if proper oversight demonstrated, hire period should not be reduced solely because it appears long
articles.content:
This article contains the complete official guidance from the GTA Joint Technical Committee FAQs (www.gtacredithire.com/faqs/), current as at 28 March 2026. There are 65 numbered FAQs (some removed). The document is only relevant to claims pursued within the GTA. Claims pursued outside the GTA are not governed by this guidance.

The FAQs represent the Technical Committee's authoritative interpretation of the GTA and are binding on all subscribers. Key topics are summarised below by FAQ number.

FAQ 1 – ADDITIONAL CHARGES (23.7.07)
Extra charges such as sat nav are not allowed under the GTA. Items provided as standard in a vehicle (e.g. automatic gearbox) can never be charged as extras.

FAQ 2 – ADDITIONAL DRIVER CHARGES (1.6.08)
No charge may be included for additional drivers unless the additional driver is a non-standard driver.

FAQ 3 – ADMINISTRATION FEE FOR SPLIT LIABILITY CASES (1.6.08, updated 15.6.12)
The administration fee is apportioned on the same basis as liability. Fee history: before 1 July 2011: £30; 1 July 2011 to 14 June 2012: £31; from 15 June 2012: £37.

FAQ 4 – AUTOMATIC VEHICLES (23.7.07)
An additional £5 per day may be added for automatics where the customer's vehicle was automatic, unless the hired model is manufactured as automatic only.

FAQ 5 – BIKE HIRE EXTRAS (23.7.07)
No extra charge for any motorcycle extras (crash helmets, leathers, etc.) within the GTA.

FAQ 6 – CHO DELAYS IN ANSWERING INSURER QUESTIONS ON PAYMENT PACK (1.6.08)
If the TPI asks reasonable questions, the CHO must reply within 10 working days or the penalty clock stops until the CHO responds. This does not affect the CHO's right to remove the claim from the GTA 90 days after the payment pack was first sent.

FAQ 7 – COLLISION DAMAGE WAIVER RECOVERY (23.7.07)
CDW is not claimable within the GTA.

FAQ 8 – COMPLAINTS AGAINST SUBSCRIBERS (28.8.07)
Formal complaints may only be made after deadlock. Send to: Stewart McCulloch, Independent Secretary, indsec@gtacredithire.com with supporting documentation. The Independent Secretary refines the complaint and refers to the other party for written response within approximately 2 weeks. If unresolved, the matter goes to the Technical Committee.

FAQ 9 – CONVERTIBLES (23.7.07)
Convertibles are placed in the group one higher than the hardtop equivalent (e.g. BMW M3 in SP9 → convertible in SP10).

FAQ 10 – CREDIT REPAIR ENGINEER DISCOUNTS (23.7.07)
The engineer must negotiate appropriate market discounts (labour, parts, paint, materials, engineer's fee). If the engineer fails to do so and this appears deliberate, a complaint should be made to the Technical Committee that the CHO is in breach of the GTA.

FAQ 11 – DEFINITION OF 24 HOURS (23.7.07)
The daily settlement rate applies for each 24-hour period. Example: 10:00 Monday 1 July to 17:00 Monday 8 July = 8 days.

FAQ 12 – DELAYS BY THIRD PARTY SOLICITORS (28.8.07)
The GTA applies in full regardless of whether solicitors are used. Any agents acting for subscribers on GTA claims must be aware of and abide by GTA terms.

FAQ 13 – DISABLED DRIVER VEHICLES (23.7.07)
Reasonable direct costs + £10 administration charge is acceptable in most instances. Special adaptations should be discussed with the insurer, preferably at hire commencement.

FAQ 14 – DISPUTES AND REMOVING CLAIMS FROM THE GTA (27.2.08)
If a GTA claim is not fully resolved within 60 days (e.g. dispute over liability, length of hire, rate offered, or failure to pay penalty charge) the CHO may remove it from the GTA.

FAQ 15 – DUAL CONTROLLED VEHICLE RATES (23.7.07)
£12 per day add-on to the agreed daily settlement rate for dual control vehicles (£7 per day if insurance cover is not provided).

FAQ 16 – END OF HIRE — MEANING OF 24 HOURS (27.2.08)
Hire ends not later than 24 hours after repair completion — this is calendar hours, not working hours. If repairs are completed on a Friday, the vehicle should be off-hire by Saturday.

FAQ 17 – ESTATE CARS (23.7.07)
An additional £5 per day may be added for estate vehicles where the customer's vehicle was an estate, unless the hired model is already listed as an estate.

FAQ 18 – EXCESSIVE HIRE PERIODS (23.7.07)
The GTA requires detailed monitoring and reporting. If hire continues beyond repair completion, the insurer is entitled to ask why. If there is no satisfactory answer, the TPI should not pay. Negotiation at a lower (near-intervention) rate for any extension period is recommended.

FAQ 19 – FIRST TO A CUSTOMER (23.7.07)
Para 3.1: whoever is first to a customer and obtains their agreement provides the service; all other subscribers must not intervene. Para 3.2: 'first to a customer' means receipt by the customer of a suitable offer they can understand. Communicating by letter alone risks it not being received or understood.

FAQ 20 – GEOGRAPHIC COVERAGE OF THE GTA
The GTA applies only to claims arising from incidents occurring in England, Wales, Scotland, Northern Ireland, the Isle of Man and the Channel Islands.

FAQ 21 – HIRE CONTINUING AFTER REPAIRS COMPLETED (28.8.07)
Para 4.8: hire ends no later than 24 hours after repair completion. CHO cannot extend for a customer being away on business. CHO or client must either off-hire within 24 hours of repair completion or not charge the at-fault insurer beyond that point.

FAQ 22 – HIRE DAYS AFTER TOTAL LOSS CHEQUE RECEIVED (23.7.07)
Hire may continue until 7 calendar days after receipt of the FINAL settlement cheque by the customer or representative. This is the maximum, not the norm. Interim cheques do not start the 7-day clock. Negotiations based on 'what the claimant can find to replace their vehicle' are not a recognised basis for extending total loss settlement; vehicle value is what the vehicle was worth, not what a replacement costs.

FAQ 23 – INCORRECT PAYMENT PACKS (28.8.07)
A payment pack must be complete and correct before the penalty clock starts. The insurer must notify the CHO of any key missing information or material errors within 30 days (para 6.4). The penalty clock starts from receipt of the correct payment pack.

FAQ 24 – INDIVIDUAL CHO DAILY HIRE RATES (23.7.07)
Individual CHO rates are not published; CHOs may set their own rates up to the published maximum for each group. Maximum rates are listed at the GTA website. All published rates exclude VAT. CHOs should use the current vehicle group letters/numbers when submitting invoices.

FAQ 25 – LIABILITY (23.7.07)
Para 1.3 (revised 2005): the GTA only applies where a CHO believes the customer has good prospects of full recovery against the at-fault driver's insurer. In all other cases, subscribers may elect to follow the same principles provided they comply with the spirit and terms of the GTA, including settlement rates.

FAQ 26 – LIKE FOR LIKES (28.8.07)
It is preferable to communicate with the TPI where there is any doubt over a vehicle group — e.g. old prestige vehicles, unusual vehicles. Communication should start as early in the process as possible.

FAQ 27 – LOSS OF USE DATES (28.8.07)
Requiring proof of loss of use dates should be the rare exception, not the rule, for the hire period validation form.

FAQ 29 – MITIGATION — CHECKING FOR INSURER OFFER (28.8.07)
The CHO is required to check that the client has not received an offer for a replacement vehicle from the at-fault insurer. The mitigation statement signed by the customer must confirm this (or give the reason for not accepting the insurer's offer).

FAQ 31 – MOTORCYCLE TOPBOXES (1.7.10)
A charge of £35 plus VAT may be made for a top box where the client had one fitted to their damaged motorcycle and regular use can be demonstrated (clause 5.4).

FAQ 32 – NEGOTIATION (23.7.07)
All insurers and CHOs are encouraged to communicate and negotiate as quickly as possible. This must be in the best interests of both parties.

FAQ 33 – NON-STANDARD DRIVERS AND ADDITIONAL INSURANCE PREMIUMS (revised 1.2.11)
Non-standard risk is defined as: under 25 or over 70 years old; full driving licence held for less than 12 months; certain occupations (professional sportsmen, actors, entertainers, gamblers, musicians, publicans, journalists); convictions resulting in an unspent ban or 7 or more outstanding points in the last 4 years. Proof is the mitigation questionnaire/statement of truth signed by the hirer. The additional insurance premium is £5.50 per day, capped at £110 for all hire claims other than bike hires where the cap is £148.50.

FAQ 34 – PARTS DELAY (28.8.07)
Para 4.12: when a hire has been correctly monitored and reported, insurers accept responsibility for delays including unavoidable parts delays. If the CHO has not monitored correctly, para 6.15 applies (payment in full less savings the insurer would reasonably have achieved had the breach not occurred).

FAQ 35 – PENALTIES ON PENALTIES (23.7.07)
The GTA penalty rates (para 6.8) are not cumulative. Late payment additions to total invoice: 0% in month 1; 7.5% (hires before 15 Jun 2012) / 12.5% (hires on or after 15 Jun 2012) in months 1–2; 15% / 20% in months 2–3. For repairs: 2.5% in months 1–2; 5% in months 2–3. If payment is made after 6 weeks the penalty is 7.5%; after 10 weeks it is 15%; after 3 months it remains 15% unless the claim is taken outside the GTA.

FAQ 37 – PENALTY INTEREST AND THE ADMIN FEE (23.7.07)
Yes, penalty interest can be charged on the administration fee.

FAQ 38 – PENALTY PAYMENTS WHEN LIABILITY NOT CHALLENGED WITHIN 21 DAYS (27.2.08)
Penalty payments still apply even where liability has not been challenged within the first 21 days of submission of the payment pack. See para 6.10 of the GTA.

FAQ 39 – PENALTY PAYMENTS START DATE (20.2.09)
The relevant date for penalty purposes is the date the CHO receives the payment (para 6.8), not the date the insurer authorises it or dispatches it.

FAQ 40 – PRIVATE OR COMMERCIAL HIRE (23.7.07)
Vehicle classification depends on ownership, use and circumstances. A privately owned vehicle used substantially for private purposes should attract a private hire replacement even if also used for mixed purposes.

FAQ 41 – REPAIR MONITORING (23.7.07)
Para 4.10: CHO must check authorisation within 3 working days of vehicle going in; further check at the lesser of 5 working days or 3 working days before hire should have ended; final check 3 working days before hire should have ended. Para 4.11: report unreasonable delays (2+ working days longer than expected or 20%+ over estimated period) to the insurer. Where CHO undertakes both hire and repair, it is held to a higher standard of diligence. Failure to monitor = CHO responsible for delays, subject to para 6.15.

FAQ 42 – ROADWORTHY OR NOT? (23.7.07)
Para 4.6: 'roadworthy' includes usable and roadworthy and embraces aspects such as deficient locks, panel damage or need for a prestige vehicle. If the vehicle was roadworthy it should have been used until the repair garage was ready. If the vehicle was not roadworthy it should have been off the road for the entire period.

FAQ 43 – SELECTIVITY IN IMPLEMENTING THE GTA (23.7.07, amended 1.1.13)
Subscriber CHOs must comply fully with the GTA for all their hires. The same obligation applies to insurers.

FAQ 44 – SUB-HIRED VEHICLES (27.2.08)
It is perfectly proper for CHOs to sub-hire vehicles in order to provide a client with a suitable replacement. The payment pack information should usually be accepted as proof that the CHO provided the vehicle.

FAQ 45 – TAXI AND PRIVATE HIRE EXTRAS (23.7.07)
No extra additions are allowed for signage, radio hire, taxi display, etc. However, actual costs incurred for council replating requirements, fitting a different radio, etc. are real costs that can be recovered.

FAQ 46 – TAXI INSURANCE CLARIFICATION (20.2.09)
The CHO decides whether to use the insurance included or excluded rate. If the CHO includes insurance for the client on its own policy, the insurance included rate applies. If insurance is arranged on the client's own policy, the insurance excluded rate applies with a £10 administration charge.

FAQ 47 – TELEPHONE LINES (23.7.07)
Premium rate telephone lines are not in the spirit of the GTA. Advance notice should be given of any changes to telephone numbers or closure of call/claim centres.

FAQ 48 – TOTAL LOSS AND RECEIPT OF CHEQUE (23.7.07)
The maximum 7-day post-settlement hire period starts from receipt of the final settlement cheque by the customer or their representative — not the date of agreement or dispatch.

FAQ 49 – TOTAL LOSSES — MONITORING (27.2.08)
A CHO is required to monitor as per the GTA. For total losses, para 4.14 sets out the details. Where monitoring has been correct, para 4.12 requires the insurer to pay. Where monitoring has fallen short, para 6.15 is relevant.

FAQ 50 – TOTAL LOSS — SIZE OF HIRE PAYMENT (27.2.08)
The size of the hire claim has no bearing on recovery, provided the GTA requirements have been met, even if the hire total exceeds the pre-accident value of the vehicle.

FAQ 51 – UNLIMITED MILEAGE (23.7.07)
GTA settlement rates include unlimited mileage.

FAQ 52 – VAT (23.7.07)
The daily settlement rates exclude VAT.

FAQ 53 – VEHICLE GROUP DISPUTE (23.7.07)
For disputes about vehicle group not listed in the GTA tables, contact chair@thegta.onmicrosoft.com using 'Vehicle group request' as the subject heading. This service is only available to GTA subscribers. The Vehicle Group Sub-committee meets once or twice a month. The grouping process is to ascertain the group of the CHO's hire vehicle, not the claimant's pre-accident vehicle.

FAQ 54 – CREDIT REPAIR PROTOCOL — APPLICATION (27.2.08)
If an insurer does not support the credit repair protocol, the CHO is under no obligation (other than to mitigate the loss) to comply with its standards, timetables and procedures.

FAQ 55 – CREDIT REPAIR — FRAUD CHECKS AND REPAIR AUTHORISATION (1.6.08)
For credit repairs (Option 2 — Independent Repairer), the engineer will normally authorise the repair at the time of inspection. Fraud checks should not delay authorisation.

FAQ 56 – CREDIT REPAIR — MONITORING (27.2.08)
Para 4.12: when a hire has been correctly monitored and reported, insurers accept responsibility for delays. Conversely, where a hire has not been correctly monitored and reported, the CHO is responsible for any delays.

FAQ 57 – CREDIT REPAIR — ROADWORTHY VEHICLES (27.2.08)
Where the vehicle is roadworthy, the CHO should instruct an engineer when the claim is received so that the inspection coincides with the day the vehicle goes into the repairers, minimising unnecessary hire extensions.

FAQ 58 – CREDIT REPAIR — 66% RULE (27.2.08)
The repair extension requires the CHO to advise the at-fault driver's insurer within one working day of receipt of the independent engineer's report when a vehicle is a total loss or repair costs exceed 66% of the pre-accident value. The report must be faxed/emailed. Repeated failure is a breach of the engineer's service standards.

FAQ 60 – EXCESSES AND THIRD PARTY CLAIMS HANDLING AGENTS (1.2.21)
GTA terms only apply where an insurer's policy applies. Where a claim falls within the policyholder's excess, the CHO may at their discretion ask the policyholder or their handling agent whether they are prepared to apply GTA terms.

FAQ 61 – OLDER PRESTIGE VEHICLES (18.10.23)
The Technical Committee has confirmed that 'prestige' as used in GTA clause 4.5 is not limited to vehicles in the 'P' category but applies to any prestige marque vehicle over 6 years old across all vehicle categories. Need will then be determined by make, model, value and use of the vehicle. For prestige vehicles over 6 years old, it is the exception rather than the rule that a similar prestige replacement is required. These comments should be read in conjunction with GTA clause 4.4 and FAQ 26.

FAQ 62 – SUBMISSION OF NEW CLAIMS ADVICE FORM FROM 17 MARCH 2025 (26.2.25)
Clause 4.1 (as amended): the CHO must advise the at-fault driver's insurer within one working day of agreeing services, using a New Claim Advice Form (NCAF or Appendix A). The clock starts from when the customer understands and agrees the hire (clauses 3.1 and 3.2). Where the CHO is asking the at-fault insurer to deal with vehicle damage, the NCA Form MUST include: name; address and/or e-mail; phone number of the repairer, storage yard or recovery company. Hire is not recoverable beyond one working day until this information is supplied on a completed NCA Form sent to the email address on the GTA website. Where relevant information is missing, hire charges will not ordinarily be recoverable beyond the one working day allowed until the date the information is provided.

FAQ 63 – ABI SALVAGE CODE OF PRACTICE AND TOTAL LOSS PAYMENTS (26.11.25)
CHOs cannot refuse to facilitate total loss payment (e.g. by withholding BACS details) pending a physical inspection of the vehicle. The ABI Salvage Code of Practice does not require withholding total loss payments until a physical inspection has taken place. The purpose of salvage categorisation is to provide guidance to potential purchasers, not to delay settlement. Any hire period caused by a CHO deliberately obstructing or failing to facilitate payment will not be recoverable.

FAQ 64 – MAKING A COMPLAINT UNDER THE GTA (26.11.25)
The GTA complaints procedure exists to resolve disputes on issues of principle or interpretation of the GTA — not to resolve simple arguments over the value of a claim. The escalation procedure under clause 2.7(b) must be followed before a formal complaint is submitted. The GTA Chair has complete discretion to reject a complaint or batch of complaints if the complainant has failed to communicate and act collaboratively. Complaints should be a last resort. The GTA authorises the Technical Committee to require an audit of a subscriber in appropriate cases.

FAQ 65 – REPAIR MONITORING WHEN HIRE AND REPAIRS ARE MANAGED SEPARATELY (25.3.26)
Where a Credit Hire Company (CHC) has no direct control over the repairs process, both the CHC and the claimant still have a responsibility to actively monitor repair progress, obtain regular meaningful updates, address delays, and inform the Third Party Insurer (TPI) promptly when issues arise. If the CHC can provide evidence that: regular repair updates were obtained and followed up; any unreasonable delays were identified and challenged; and the TPI was kept informed (including details of any parts delay) — then the hire period should not be reduced solely because it appears long. However, if monitoring was not proactive or avoidable delays were allowed to continue without challenge, it is reasonable for the TPI to adjust the hire period.

articles.title: CPR Part 35 — Experts and Assessors
articles.source: Civil Procedure Rules, Ministry of Justice (www.justice.gov.uk/courts/procedure-rules/civil/rules/part35)
articles.author: Ministry of Justice
principles: Rule 35.1: Expert evidence shall be restricted to that which is reasonably required to resolve the proceedings

Rule 35.2: An 'expert' is a person instructed to give or prepare expert evidence for the purpose of proceedings; a 'single joint expert' (SJE) is an expert instructed to prepare a report for the court on behalf of two or more parties
Rule 35.3: The expert's overriding duty is to the court, not to the party instructing them or paying them. This duty overrides any obligation to the instructing party
Rule 35.4(1): No party may call an expert or put in evidence an expert's report without the court's permission
Rule 35.4(2): When applying for permission, parties must provide a costs estimate and identify the field and issues the expert evidence will address, and where practicable the expert's name
Rule 35.4(3): Permission is limited to the named expert or identified field; the court may specify the issues the expert evidence should address
Rule 35.4(3A): On small claims or fast track, permission will normally only be for one expert on a particular issue
Rule 35.4(3B)/(3C): In soft tissue injury or whiplash claims, permission may normally only be given for one expert medical report; the report must initially be a fixed cost medical report
Rule 35.4(4): The court may limit the amount of the expert's fees and expenses recoverable from the other party

Rule 35.5: Expert evidence is to be given in a written report unless the court directs otherwise; on small claims or fast track, the court will not direct an expert to attend a hearing unless necessary in the interests of justice

Rule 35.6: Written questions to experts must be proportionate, put once only, within 28 days of service of the report, and for the purpose only of clarification, unless the court gives permission or the other party agrees. Answers form part of the expert's report. If the expert fails to answer, the court may order that the instructing party may not rely on the expert or recover their fees

Rule 35.7: Where two or more parties wish to submit expert evidence on an issue, the court may direct a single joint expert. If parties cannot agree, the court selects from a list or directs otherwise

Rule 35.8: Any relevant party may give instructions to an SJE but must copy instructions to all other relevant parties at the same time. Relevant parties are jointly and severally liable for the SJE's fees and expenses unless the court directs otherwise
Rule 35.9: Where a party has access to information not reasonably available to another party, the court may direct that party to prepare and serve a document recording the information
Rule 35.10(1): An expert's report must comply with Practice Direction 35
Rule 35.10(2): An expert's report must end with a statement that the expert understands and has complied with their duty to the court
Rule 35.10(3): The report must state the substance of all material instructions (written or oral) on the basis of which it was written

Rule 35.10(4): Instructions are not privileged against disclosure, but the court will not order disclosure of specific documents or permit questioning (other than by the instructing party) unless satisfied there are reasonable grounds to consider the statement of instructions to be inaccurate or incomplete
Rule 35.11: Where a party has disclosed an expert's report, any party may use it as evidence at trial

Rule 35.12: The court may direct a discussion between experts at any stage to identify and discuss expert issues and, where possible, reach an agreed opinion. The court may direct a joint statement setting out agreed and disagreed issues with reasons. Discussion content is not referred to at trial unless parties agree. Expert agreement does not bind the parties unless expressly agreed
Rule 35.13: A party who fails to disclose an expert's report may not use it at trial or call the expert to give evidence orally without court permission
Rule 35.14: Experts may file written requests for directions. They must provide copies to the instructing party at least 7 days before filing, and to all other parties at least 4 days before filing

Rule 35.15: Assessors appointed under s.70 Senior Courts Act 1981 or s.63 County Courts Act 1984 assist the court in matters within their skill and experience. They may prepare a report (copies sent to all parties before trial) and/or attend trial to advise. Their remuneration is determined by the court and forms part of the costs of the proceedings
articles.content:
CPR Part 35 governs the use of expert evidence in civil proceedings in England and Wales. It is directly relevant to credit hire litigation where expert evidence on the Basic Hire Rate (BHR) is in issue. The following sets out the full text of each rule.

RULE 35.1 — DUTY TO RESTRICT EXPERT EVIDENCE
Expert evidence shall be restricted to that which is reasonably required to resolve the proceedings.

RULE 35.2 — INTERPRETATION AND DEFINITIONS
(1) A reference to an 'expert' in this Part is a reference to a person who has been instructed to give or prepare expert evidence for the purpose of proceedings.
(2) 'Single joint expert' means an expert instructed to prepare a report for the court on behalf of two or more of the parties (including the claimant) to the proceedings.

RULE 35.3 — EXPERTS: OVERRIDING DUTY TO THE COURT
(1) It is the duty of experts to help the court on matters within their expertise.
(2) This duty overrides any obligation to the person from whom experts have received instructions or by whom they are paid.

RULE 35.4 — COURT'S POWER TO RESTRICT EXPERT EVIDENCE
(1) No party may call an expert or put in evidence an expert's report without the court's permission.
(2) When parties apply for permission they must provide an estimate of the costs of the proposed expert evidence and identify: (a) the field in which expert evidence is required and the issues which the expert evidence will address; and (b) where practicable, the name of the proposed expert.
(3) If permission is granted it shall be in relation only to the expert named or the field identified under paragraph (2). The order granting permission may specify the issues which the expert evidence should address.
(3A) Where a claim has been allocated to the small claims track or the fast track, if permission is given for expert evidence, it will normally be given for evidence from only one expert on a particular issue.
(3B) In a soft tissue injury claim, permission may normally only be given for one expert medical report; the report must initially be a fixed cost medical report. Further medical reports from Consultant Orthopaedic Surgeons, Consultant A&E Medicine specialists, GPs registered with the GMC, or Physiotherapists registered with the HCPC must also be fixed cost medical reports.
(3C) In a claim for a whiplash injury (whether or not part of a wider injury claim), permission may normally only be given for one expert medical report in respect of the whiplash injury, and may not be given initially unless the medical report is a fixed cost medical report. Further whiplash reports from the specified disciplines must also be fixed cost medical reports.
(3D) Where, in respect of a whiplash injury claim, the claimant lives outside England and Wales but chooses to be examined in England or Wales, paragraph (3C) applies.
(3E) Where the claimant obtains a medical report in respect of a more serious injury suffered on the same occasion as the whiplash injury, the claimant may use that report instead of a fixed cost medical report under (3C) provided: (a) the report is from a doctor on the GMC Specialist Register; and (b) the report provides evidence of the whiplash injury.
(3F) Where the claimant lives outside England and Wales and permission is given for a medical report in a whiplash claim (and (3D) does not apply), the first report must be from a person recognised in the country of practice as a medical expert with required qualifications for whiplash diagnosis and prognosis.
(3G) 'Fixed cost medical report' and 'soft tissue injury claim' have the meanings in the Pre-Action Protocol for Low Value Personal Injury Claims in Road Traffic Accidents; 'fixed cost medical report' and 'whiplash injury' have the meanings in the Pre-Action Protocol for Personal Injury Claims Below the Small Claims Limit in Road Traffic Accidents.
(4) The court may limit the amount of a party's expert's fees and expenses that may be recovered from any other party.

RULE 35.5 — GENERAL REQUIREMENT FOR EXPERT EVIDENCE TO BE GIVEN IN A WRITTEN REPORT
(1) Expert evidence is to be given in a written report unless the court directs otherwise.
(2) If a claim is on the small claims track or the fast track, the court will not direct an expert to attend a hearing unless it is necessary to do so in the interests of justice.

RULE 35.6 — WRITTEN QUESTIONS TO EXPERTS
(1) A party may put written questions (which must be proportionate) about an expert's report to: (a) an expert instructed by another party; or (b) a single joint expert appointed under rule 35.7.
(2) Written questions under paragraph (1): (a) may be put once only; (b) must be put within 28 days of service of the expert's report; and (c) must be for the purpose only of clarification of the report, unless the court gives permission or the other party agrees.
(3) An expert's answers to questions put in accordance with paragraph (1) shall be treated as part of the expert's report.
(4) Where a party has put a written question to an expert instructed by another party and the expert does not answer that question, the court may order in relation to the party who instructed the expert: (i) that the party may not rely on the evidence of that expert; or (ii) that the party may not recover the fees and expenses of that expert from any other party.

RULE 35.7 — COURT'S POWER TO DIRECT THAT EVIDENCE IS TO BE GIVEN BY A SINGLE JOINT EXPERT
(1) Where two or more parties wish to submit expert evidence on a particular issue, the court may direct that the evidence on that issue is to be given by a single joint expert.
(2) Where the relevant parties cannot agree who should be the single joint expert, the court may: (a) select the expert from a list prepared or identified by the relevant parties; or (b) direct that the expert be selected in such other manner as the court may direct.

RULE 35.8 — INSTRUCTIONS TO A SINGLE JOINT EXPERT
(1) Where the court gives a direction under rule 35.7, any relevant party may give instructions to the expert.
(2) When a party gives instructions to the expert that party must, at the same time, send a copy to the other relevant parties.
(3) The court may give directions about: (a) the payment of the expert's fees and expenses; and (b) any inspection, examination or experiments which the expert wishes to carry out.
(4) The court may, before an expert is instructed: (a) limit the amount that can be paid by way of fees and expenses to the expert; and (b) direct that some or all of the relevant parties pay that amount into court.
(5) Unless the court otherwise directs, the relevant parties are jointly and severally liable for the payment of the expert's fees and expenses.

RULE 35.9 — POWER OF COURT TO DIRECT A PARTY TO PROVIDE INFORMATION
Where a party has access to information which is not reasonably available to another party, the court may direct the party who has access to the information to: (a) prepare and file a document recording the information; and (b) serve a copy of that document on the other party.

RULE 35.10 — CONTENTS OF REPORT
(1) An expert's report must comply with the requirements set out in Practice Direction 35.
(2) At the end of an expert's report there must be a statement that the expert understands and has complied with their duty to the court.
(3) The expert's report must state the substance of all material instructions, whether written or oral, on the basis of which the report was written.
(4) The instructions referred to in paragraph (3) shall not be privileged against disclosure but the court will not, in relation to those instructions: (a) order disclosure of any specific document; or (b) permit any questioning in court, other than by the party who instructed the expert, unless it is satisfied that there are reasonable grounds to consider the statement of instructions given under paragraph (3) to be inaccurate or incomplete.

RULE 35.11 — USE BY ONE PARTY OF EXPERT'S REPORT DISCLOSED BY ANOTHER
Where a party has disclosed an expert's report, any party may use that expert's report as evidence at the trial.

RULE 35.12 — DISCUSSIONS BETWEEN EXPERTS
(1) The court may, at any stage, direct a discussion between experts for the purpose of requiring the experts to: (a) identify and discuss the expert issues in the proceedings; and (b) where possible, reach an agreed opinion on those issues.
(2) The court may specify the issues which the experts must discuss.
(3) The court may direct that following a discussion between the experts they must prepare a statement for the court setting out those issues on which: (a) they agree; and (b) they disagree, with a summary of their reasons for disagreeing.
(4) The content of the discussion between the experts shall not be referred to at the trial unless the parties agree.
(5) Where experts reach agreement on an issue during their discussions, the agreement shall not bind the parties unless the parties expressly agree to be bound by the agreement.

RULE 35.13 — CONSEQUENCE OF FAILURE TO DISCLOSE EXPERT'S REPORT
A party who fails to disclose an expert's report may not use the report at the trial or call the expert to give evidence orally unless the court gives permission.

RULE 35.14 — EXPERT'S RIGHT TO ASK COURT FOR DIRECTIONS
(1) Experts may file written requests for directions for the purpose of assisting them in carrying out their functions.
(2) Experts must, unless the court orders otherwise, provide copies of the proposed requests for directions: (a) to the party instructing them, at least 7 days before they file the requests; and (b) to all other parties, at least 4 days before they file them.
(3) The court, when it gives directions, may also direct that a party be served with a copy of the directions.

RULE 35.15 — ASSESSORS
(1) This rule applies where the court appoints one or more persons under section 70 of the Senior Courts Act 1981 or section 63 of the County Courts Act 1984 as an assessor.
(2) An assessor will assist the court in dealing with a matter in which the assessor has skill and experience.
(3) An assessor will take such part in the proceedings as the court may direct and in particular the court may direct an assessor to: (a) prepare a report for the court on any matter at issue in the proceedings; and (b) attend the whole or any part of the trial to advise the court on any such matter.
(4) If an assessor prepares a report for the court before the trial has begun: (a) the court will send a copy to each of the parties; and (b) the parties may use it at trial.
(5) The remuneration to be paid to an assessor is to be determined by the court and will form part of the costs of the proceedings.
(6) The court may order any party to deposit in the court office a specified sum in respect of an assessor's fees, and where it does so the assessor will not be asked to act until the sum has been deposited.
(7) Paragraphs (5) and (6) do not apply where the remuneration of the assessor is to be paid out of money provided by Parliament.

APPLICATION IN CREDIT HIRE CASES
CPR Part 35 is directly relevant to credit hire disputes in several ways. First, insurers frequently rely on expert BHR evidence — typically a market survey of mainstream vehicle hire companies — to challenge the rate charged by the CHO. This expert evidence must be obtained with court permission under rule 35.4 and the report must comply with rule 35.10 and Practice Direction 35, including the statement of truth as to the expert's duty to the court. Second, the court often directs a single joint expert (SJE) under rule 35.7 to produce a BHR survey, in which case both parties may give instructions (rule 35.8) and are jointly and severally liable for fees. Third, where an insurer's BHR expert has been instructed, the CHO's lawyers may put written questions within 28 days under rule 35.6. Fourth, the court may direct a discussion between the parties' experts under rule 35.12, leading to a joint statement identifying agreed and disputed issues. Expert agreement in that discussion does not bind the parties unless expressly agreed. Fifth, an expert's overriding duty under rule 35.3 is to the court; an expert who simply advocates for the instructing party's position risks challenge under this rule. This is particularly relevant where insurer-instructed BHR experts consistently produce surveys that the CHO contends are not representative of the true market.

articles.title: Diminution in Value — Vehicle Depreciation Claims Following a Road Traffic Accident
articles.source: Payton v Brooks [1974]; Coles v Hetherton [2013]; JMK Solicitors; Lacey Solicitors; Clifford James Consultants
articles.author: Various
principles: Diminution in value is a recoverable head of damage — a vehicle that has been involved in a road traffic accident will be worth less on the open market than an equivalent vehicle with no accident history, even if repaired to a high standard
The legal basis is the principle of restitution ad integrum: to restore the claimant to their pre-accident position so far as money can do it

The leading authority is Payton v Brooks [1974] 1 Lloyd's Rep 241 (CA): where evidence justifies a finding that there has been, on top of the cost of repairs, some diminution in market value, there is no reason why the plaintiff should be deprived of recovery under that head of damage

Payton v Brooks was reaffirmed in Coles v Hetherton [2013] EWCA Civ 1704 per Aikens LJ: the reasonable cost of repair is a rule of thumb representing the diminution in value, but may not always represent the full amount of diminution
The cost of repairs and any residual depreciation are both evidentiary mechanisms to quantify a single head of loss — diminution in value (Coles v Hetherton)
Compensation for diminution may exceed the cost of repairs where the court is satisfied the evidence justifies it
The point of loss is the date of the damage, not the date the vehicle is sold — Coles per Aikens LJ: loss is suffered as soon as the chattel is damaged

Events occurring after the date of damage are irrelevant to calculating diminution — subsequent destruction, delay in repair, or ability to have repairs done cheaply or for nothing will not prevent recovery
A claimant does not need to have sold the vehicle to bring a diminution claim; equally, a claimant may bring the claim even after selling, with the sale price used as evidence of the loss

Even repairs carried out to the best possible standard do not defeat a diminution claim — Payton: if a diminution in market value can be established despite good repairs, the diminution is a recoverable item of damage
The claim is NOT restricted to vehicles with structural damage — the argument that diminution only arises from structural or semi-structural repairs is incorrect in law
Hussain v Dhawan (Newcastle CC, 7 November 2008, unreported) argued the claim requires damage beyond the norm — this case is widely regarded as wrong in law and has not been followed

Lex Autolease Limited v Susan Bartlett (Stockport CC, 9 May 2011) expressly rejected the Hussain approach: Payton v Brooks laid down no limitation requiring damage to be out of the norm — what must be proved is simply that diminution exists
The burden of proof lies with the claimant — the claimant must establish on a balance of probabilities (1) that diminution exists, and (2) the quantum of that loss
There is no mathematical formula for calculating diminution — the loss is fact-specific and no fixed percentage applies
The commonly cited figure of 10% of repair costs is not a rule of law and is incorrect as a universal approach
It is equally incorrect to say diminution is always between 2.5% and 15%, or that a vehicle over a certain age (eg four years) will not qualify — every case turns on its own facts
Glass's Guide is not designed to assess post-accident diminution and cannot be used as a substitute for expert evidence
Expert engineering evidence from a suitably qualified motor assessor is required in virtually all contested diminution cases — this is opinion evidence and the court cannot assess quantum without it

The court cannot dismiss expert evidence without giving reasons, and must explain why it prefers one expert over another — Sewell v Electrolux (1997); Flannery v Halifax Estate Agencies [2000] 1 All ER 373
A judge has a duty to give a coherent reasoned explanation for rejecting expert evidence; failure to do so may be grounds for appeal — Flannery v Halifax Estate Agencies [2000]
Where there is conflicting expert opinion, the judge must address and resolve it and make proper findings of fact — Cooper v Floor Cleaning Machines [2003] EWCA Civ 1649
The insurer for the at-fault party will frequently appoint their own motor engineer to contest quantum — the claimant must have strong expert evidence in response

The modern used car market means buyers are increasingly reluctant to purchase vehicles with an accident history even where fully repaired, strengthening the case for diminution particularly on newer and higher-value vehicles

Factors relevant to the quantum assessment include: type of vehicle; age; mileage; pre-accident condition; severity of damage; who carried out repairs and whether manufacturer methods were followed; whether repairs have invalidated the vehicle's warranty; quality of repairs post-repair; and any special attributes or qualities of the vehicle

cases.name: Payton v Brooks
cases.principle: Established that diminution in value is recoverable as a head of damage on top of repair costs. Roskill LJ: where the loss to the plaintiff has not been fully compensated by the cost of repair because of a resultant diminution in market value, there is no reason to deprive the plaintiff of recovery under that head of damage. Also confirmed that even good quality repairs do not defeat the claim.

cases.name: Coles v Hetherton
cases.principle: Reaffirmed Payton v Brooks. Aikens LJ confirmed: loss is suffered as soon as the chattel is damaged; the proper measure is the diminution in value caused by the defendant's negligence; events after the damage are irrelevant to calculating this measure; subsequent destruction, delayed repair, or cheap or free repair will not prevent recovery. Cost of repairs is a rule of thumb only and may not represent the full diminution.

cases.name: Hussain v Dhawan
cases.principle: Argued that a diminution claim requires damage that is unusual or beyond the norm. This restriction has no basis in Payton v Brooks, which placed no such limitation. The case has not been followed and is widely regarded as wrong in law.

cases.name: Lex Autolease Limited v Susan Bartlett
cases.principle: DJ Jones expressly rejected the Hussain v Dhawan approach: Payton v Brooks laid down no limitation requiring the repair or damages to be out of the norm. What has to be proved is that diminution exists.

cases.name: Cooper v Floor Cleaning Machines Limited
cases.principle: A judge cannot dismiss expert evidence and rely merely on the burden of proof. The court does not have the expertise to assess the value of loss on its own; if it rejects expert evidence it must go on to explain why.

cases.name: Sewell v Electrolux Limited
cases.principle: If a judge rejects expert evidence, the judge must explain why. Failure to give reasons for rejecting expert evidence is an error of law.

cases.name: Flannery v Halifax Estate Agencies Limited
cases.principle: A judge has a duty to give a coherent reasoned explanation for rejecting any expert evidence and for preferring one expert over another. Failure to do so may be grounds for appeal.

cases.name: The Mediana
cases.principle: Established the general principle that difficulty in valuing a loss does not defeat a claim for general damages. Halsbury LC: the law recognises that general damages may be awarded even where no arithmetical calculation can establish the exact sum representing the loss.

articles.content:
DIMINUTION IN VALUE — OVERVIEW

When a vehicle is involved in a road traffic accident it may suffer two distinct financial consequences: the cost of physical repair, and a permanent reduction in its open market value. The second of these — diminution in value — arises because a vehicle with an accident history will be worth less to a potential buyer than an equivalent vehicle without that history, even if the repairs have been carried out to the highest possible standard. Diminution in value is an established and recoverable head of general damages in English law.

THE LEGAL BASIS — RESTITUTION AD INTEGRUM

The underlying principle is restitution ad integrum: the court's aim is to restore the claimant to the position they were in before the negligence of the defendant, as far as money can do it. In the context of vehicle damage, this means compensating not just for the physical cost of repair but also for any residual loss of value that repair cannot cure.

As Aikens LJ explained in Coles v Hetherton [2013] EWCA Civ 1704: the reasonable cost of repair is a rule of thumb representing the diminution in value, but may not always represent the full amount of the diminution, as made clear in Payton v Brooks. Both the cost of repairs and any residual depreciation are evidentiary mechanisms for quantifying the single head of loss — diminution in value.

KEY CASE LAW

Payton v Brooks [1974] 1 Lloyd's Rep 241 is the foundational authority. In the Court of Appeal, Roskill LJ held that where the evidence justifies a finding that there has been, on top of the cost of repairs, some diminution in market value — or to put it another way, that the loss to the plaintiff has not been fully compensated by the receipt of the costs of complete and adequate repairs because of a resultant diminution in market value — there is no reason why the plaintiff should be deprived of recovery under that head of damage. Payton also confirmed that even repairs carried out to the highest standard do not extinguish the claim: if a diminution in market value can be established despite good repairs, the diminution is a recoverable item of damage.

Payton was reaffirmed by the Court of Appeal in Coles v Hetherton [2013] EWCA Civ 1704. Aikens LJ confirmed the following propositions: (1) where a chattel is damaged by the negligence of another, the loss is suffered as soon as the chattel is damaged; (2) the proper measure of that loss is the diminution in value the chattel has suffered as a result of the defendant's negligence; (3) events occurring after the infliction of damage are irrelevant to calculating the diminution in value — subsequent destruction, a decision to delay repairs, or the ability to have repairs done at less than cost or for nothing will not prevent recovery.

The attempt in Hussain v Dhawan (Newcastle CC, 7 November 2008, unreported) to restrict diminution claims to damage that is unusual or beyond the norm has no foundation in Payton v Brooks and has not been followed. In Lex Autolease Limited v Susan Bartlett (Stockport CC, 9 May 2011), DJ Jones expressly rejected the Hussain approach: it seems to the court that the authority of Payton v Brooks laid down no such limitation. What has to be proved is that diminution exists.

POINT OF LOSS

A common misconception is that diminution cannot be quantified or claimed until the vehicle is sold. This is wrong. The point of loss is the date the damage was caused and quantification must be assessed as of that time. A claimant may bring the claim while still in possession of the vehicle, assessed on the basis of expert evidence as to market value. Equally, a claimant may still bring the claim after selling, with the actual sale price serving as evidence of the loss, provided it can be compared with the vehicle's pre-accident value.

BURDEN OF PROOF

The burden of proof lies with the claimant. The claimant must establish on a balance of probabilities: (1) that diminution exists — that is, that a potential buyer would pay less for the vehicle knowing of its accident history; and (2) the quantum of that loss. If the first question is answered yes, the court must go on to do its best to assess the loss, even where precise figures are difficult to establish. As Halsbury LC observed in The Mediana [1900] AC 113, difficulty in valuation has never been a bar to the award of general damages.

NO FIXED FORMULA OR PERCENTAGE

There is no mathematical formula for calculating diminution in value in English law. The commonly cited approach of awarding 10% of repair costs is not a rule of law. It is equally incorrect to state that the award will always fall between 2.5% and 15%, or that a vehicle over a certain age (such as four years) will not qualify for a diminution claim. Every case is fact-specific. These figures may be relevant as a starting point for negotiation but should not be treated as definitive limits.

For reference, in the United States the 17c Diminished Value Formula is widely used: it takes 10% of the vehicle's pre-accident value, applies a damage multiplier of 0.00 to 1.00 (0.00 = no structural damage, 0.25 = minor, 0.50 = moderate, 0.75 = major, 1.00 = severe), and then applies a mileage multiplier. This formula has no legal standing in the UK but may serve as a rough illustrative starting point.

FACTORS AFFECTING QUANTUM

A suitably qualified motor assessor will consider the following factors when assessing the extent of diminution: type of vehicle (make, model, prestige value); age and registration year; mileage; pre-accident condition; severity of the damage sustained; who carried out the repairs and whether manufacturer repair methods were followed; whether the repairs have invalidated the vehicle's warranty; quality of the repairs post-completion; whether the vehicle has sophisticated technology making it more susceptible to perceived loss of value; and any special attributes or qualities of the vehicle.

Vehicles with sophisticated technology are increasingly relevant — modern cars are more complex and even apparently minor accidents may require extensive repairs, amplifying the impact on perceived resale value. The modern used car market is highly competitive and buyers are often reluctant to purchase vehicles with any accident history, even where fully and professionally repaired.

GLASS'S GUIDE

Glass's Guide provides second-hand vehicle valuations without reference to accident history and is not designed to assess post-accident diminution. Reference is sometimes made to an article by a Director of Glass's Guide in Bodyshop Magazine (April 2014) suggesting that high-quality repairs eliminate any loss of market value. This view ignores the established case law and should be rejected.

EXPERT EVIDENCE

Because diminution involves an opinion as to market value, expert evidence from a suitably qualified motor assessor is essential in virtually all contested cases. Car dealers may also provide opinions, but independent engineering evidence from a motor assessor will carry greater weight in proceedings. Where a claim is in dispute, the defendant insurer will typically appoint their own engineer, and the strength of the case will turn on the quality and credibility of the competing expert opinions.

The court cannot dismiss expert evidence without giving reasons, and must explain why it prefers one expert to another (Sewell v Electrolux (1997); Flannery v Halifax Estate Agencies [2000] 1 All ER 373). A judge cannot simply rely on the burden of proof to dismiss expert evidence without engaging with it (Cooper v Floor Cleaning Machines [2003] EWCA Civ 1649). Failure to provide a coherent reasoned explanation for rejecting expert evidence may be grounds for appeal.

COMPENSATION EXCEEDING REPAIR COSTS

Where expert evidence justifies it, the court may award compensation for diminution that exceeds the cost of the vehicle repairs. The repair cost is a starting point, not a ceiling.

MAKING A CLAIM — PRACTICAL STEPS

A claimant wishing to pursue a diminution claim should instruct an experienced motor assessor as early as possible, gather supporting documentation including the repair report and receipts and photographs of the accident damage, and register the claim as soon as possible after the accident. Where the vehicle has already been sold, evidence of the sale price compared with the pre-accident value can be used to establish the loss.

Documentation that may be required includes: police report; repair reports and receipts; photographs of the accident and damage; witness statements and CCTV evidence; where the vehicle has been sold, proof of sale and evidence it sold for less than its pre-accident value.

Insurers will frequently contest diminution claims and may push back regardless of the strength of the evidence. This is precisely why motor assessor evidence is important and why a claimant should not assume that providing documentation alone will lead to a straightforward settlement.

articles.title: Enforceability of Credit Hire Agreements — Consumer Credit Act 1974, Exempt Agreements and Consumer Contracts Regulations
articles.source: Dimond v Lovell [2002]; Clark v Tull [2002]; Salat v Barutis [2013]; Irving v Morgan Sindall [2010]; Kadir v Thompson [2016]; Consumer Credit Act 1974; CCR 2013
articles.author: Various
principles: Credit hire agreements are credit agreements subject to the Consumer Credit Act 1974 (as amended by the CCA 2006) — confirmed by the House of Lords in Dimond v Lovell [2002] 1 AC 384

If a credit hire agreement is not exempt from the CCA 1974, it is likely to be improperly executed under CCA 1974 s.61(1)(a) for failing to state the total cash price for the services — a failure that is inevitable because the total cannot be known at the time of signing

An improperly executed agreement means the hirer has suffered no loss and cannot recover hire charges from the defendant — the enforceability of the agreement is therefore a fundamental threshold issue

Clark v Tull (trading as Ardington Electrical Services) [2002] EWCA Civ 510: a credit hire agreement is an exempt agreement if it requires no more than four payments and limits the period of hire to no more than 12 months — even if the liability to pay may stretch over a longer period, this does not remove the exemption
Credit hire companies have therefore structured their agreements to ensure they are exempt — typically by limiting the number of instalments and the hire period to fall within the exemption
The claimant must produce a copy of the credit hire agreement signed by the parties — failure to produce a signed agreement is fatal to the claim

More fundamentally, the claimant must satisfy the court that they are actually obliged to pay the hire charges — in Irving v Morgan Sindall [2010] the claim failed because the claimant's own evidence was that she was not obliged to pay

In a minority of cases, defendant insurers may assert that the claimant has voided the agreement due to misrepresentation — Kadir v Thompson (Central London CC, 25 August 2016) sets out the three elements the defendant must establish: (1) a false representation (of fact or law); (2) the claimant was induced to enter the contract in reliance on the representation; (3) the claimant then voided the contract (which had been rendered voidable)

Under the Cancellation of Contracts made in a Consumer's Home or Place of Work etc Regulations 2008 (SI 2008/1816), where an agreement was reached in the consumer's home or place of work, failure by the CHO to include a Notice of the Right to Cancel rendered the agreement unenforceable — Salat v Barutis [2013] confirmed such a breach meant the hirer suffered no loss
The 2008 Regulations were superseded by the Consumer Contracts (Information, Cancellation and Additional Charges) Regulations 2013 (SI 2013/3134) (CCR 2013) for agreements on or after 13 June 2014

Under CCR 2013 reg 10(1) there is a similar right to cancel; the scope is wider — it applies wherever the consumer and trader are simultaneously present other than at the trader's business premises (an 'off-premises contract'), not just in the consumer's home or place of work

However, CCR 2013 contains two provisions which may exempt credit hire agreements from its scope: (1) reg 6(1)(b) excludes agreements of a credit nature — this may take both credit hire and credit repair out of the CCR 2013 entirely; (2) reg 28(1)(h) excludes vehicle rental services contracts from the cancellation right requirement where the agreement gives a specific date or period for performance

The practical effect is that well-drafted credit hire agreements made on or after 13 June 2014 should be exempt from the CCR 2013 cancellation requirements by virtue of either reg 6(1)(b) or reg 28(1)(h), or both

Practitioners on both sides should check whether the specific agreement in question falls within the CCR 2013 exemptions and, if not, whether the required cancellation notice was given — its absence could render the agreement unenforceable

cases.name: Dimond v Lovell
cases.principle: Confirmed that credit hire agreements are credit agreements subject to the Consumer Credit Act 1974. The House of Lords held that the CHO's agreement was a regulated agreement under the CCA and, because it was improperly executed, was unenforceable. As it was unenforceable against the hirer, the hirer had suffered no loss and could not recover the hire charges from the tortfeasor.

cases.name: Clark v Tull (trading as Ardington Electrical Services)
cases.principle: A credit hire agreement requiring no more than four payments and limiting the hire period to no more than 12 months is an exempt agreement under the CCA 1974. The exemption is not lost merely because the total liability to pay may ultimately stretch over a longer period. CHOs have relied on this authority to structure their agreements so as to qualify for the exemption, avoiding the CCA's formality requirements.

cases.name: Salat v Barutis
cases.principle: Under the Cancellation of Contracts Regulations 2008 (SI 2008/1816), where a credit hire agreement was concluded in the consumer's home or place of work and the CHO failed to include a Notice of the Right to Cancel, the agreement was unenforceable. As a result, the hirer suffered no loss and could not recover the hire charges from the defendant.

cases.name: Irving v Morgan Sindall
cases.principle: The credit hire claim failed because the claimant's own evidence was that she was not obliged to pay the hire charges. The obligation to pay is an essential element of the claim — a claimant who does not intend or expect to pay cannot show a relevant loss recoverable from the defendant.

cases.name: Kadir v Thompson
cases.principle: Set out the three elements a defendant must establish to void a credit hire agreement for misrepresentation: (1) there was a false representation, which could go to fact or law; (2) the claimant was induced to enter the contract in reliance on the representation; (3) the claimant then voided the contract (which had been rendered voidable by the misrepresentation).

articles.content:
OVERVIEW — ENFORCEABILITY AS A THRESHOLD ISSUE

Enforceability of the credit hire agreement is a threshold issue that must be established before any question of quantum arises. If the agreement is unenforceable against the hirer, the hirer has suffered no loss and cannot recover any hire charges from the defendant tortfeasor. Defendant insurers regularly scrutinise agreements for enforceability defects.

CONSUMER CREDIT ACT 1974 — DIMOND v LOVELL

The House of Lords in Dimond v Lovell [2002] 1 AC 384 confirmed that credit hire agreements are credit agreements subject to the Consumer Credit Act 1974 (as amended by the CCA 2006). An agreement that is a regulated agreement under the CCA, but fails to comply with the statutory formalities (in particular s.61(1)(a) — the requirement to state the total cash price for the services), will be improperly executed and therefore unenforceable. Because the total cost of a credit hire arrangement cannot be known at the time the agreement is entered into (the hire period being open), CHOs that do not fall within an exemption will inevitably fail to state the total cash price.

THE EXEMPTION — CLARK v TULL (ARDINGTON ELECTRICAL SERVICES)

The Court of Appeal in Clark v Tull (trading as Ardington Electrical Services) [2002] EWCA Civ 510 confirmed that a credit hire agreement is an exempt agreement, and therefore falls outside the CCA's formality requirements, if:
(1) it requires no more than four payments; and
(2) it limits the period of hire to no more than 12 months.

Critically, even if the hirer's total liability to pay may ultimately stretch over a longer period (for example, because the hire is prolonged beyond 12 months), this does not remove the exemption — it is the agreement's terms that matter, not the ultimate outcome. CHOs have therefore structured their standard agreements so as to satisfy these conditions and qualify for the exemption. In practice, the vast majority of mainstream credit hire agreements are drafted to be exempt.

SIGNED AGREEMENT AND OBLIGATION TO PAY

Even where an agreement is exempt from the CCA formalities, two basic requirements must still be met. First, the claimant must produce a credit hire agreement signed by the parties — county court decisions regularly require practitioners to note that an unsigned agreement or an inability to produce the agreement is fatal to the claim. Second, and more fundamentally, the claimant must satisfy the court that they are actually obliged to pay the hire charges. In Irving v Morgan Sindall [2010], the credit hire claim failed entirely because the claimant's own evidence was that she was not obliged to pay — there was therefore no loss.

MISREPRESENTATION — KADIR v THOMPSON

In a minority of cases, defendant insurers assert that the claimant voided the agreement due to a misrepresentation by the CHO upon which the claimant relied. This most commonly arises where the claimant was told they would not have to pay anything (see also Parker v Skyfire [2024]). Kadir v Thompson (Central London CC, 25 August 2016) sets out the three elements the defendant must establish:
(1) there was a false representation — which may go to fact or law;
(2) the claimant was induced to enter the contract in reliance on that representation;
(3) the claimant voided the contract (which had been rendered voidable).

RIGHT TO CANCEL — 2008 REGULATIONS

The Cancellation of Contracts made in a Consumer's Home or Place of Work etc Regulations 2008 (SI 2008/1816) required, where an agreement was reached in the consumer's home or place of work, that the CHO include a Notice of the Right to Cancel. In Salat v Barutis [2013] EWCA Civ 1499, the Court of Appeal confirmed that failure to comply with this requirement rendered the agreement unenforceable, meaning the hirer suffered no loss. This was a frequently deployed defence by insurers against agreements concluded at the claimant's home or roadside.

CONSUMER CONTRACTS REGULATIONS 2013 (CCR 2013)

The 2008 Regulations were superseded by the Consumer Contracts (Information, Cancellation and Additional Charges) Regulations 2013 (SI 2013/3134) for agreements concluded on or after 13 June 2014. The CCR 2013 introduce a similar right to cancel under reg 10(1), but with a wider scope: the regulations apply to any 'off-premises contract' — that is, any agreement reached where the consumer and trader are simultaneously present somewhere other than the trader's business premises, which is broader than merely the consumer's home or place of work.

However, two provisions may exempt credit hire agreements from the CCR 2013's application:
