(1) CCR 2013 reg 6(1)(b): The regulations do not apply to agreements of a credit nature. Since credit hire is a credit agreement (confirmed in Dimond v Lovell), this provision may take credit hire entirely outside the CCR 2013's scope. The same argument applies to credit repair.

(2) CCR 2013 reg 28(1)(h): Vehicle rental services contracts are specifically excluded from the cancellation right if the agreement gives a specific date or period for performance. A credit hire agreement that specifies the hire period (or its start date) should therefore be excluded from the requirement to carry a cancellation notice.

The practical effect is that a well-drafted credit hire agreement made on or after 13 June 2014 should be exempt from the CCR 2013 cancellation requirements by virtue of one or both of these provisions. Defendant insurers seeking to deploy the CCR 2013 against a credit hire agreement must demonstrate that neither exemption applies. Practitioners should check the terms of the specific agreement in issue.

articles.title: RTA Driver Liability — General Principles: Speed, Overtaking, Junctions, Braking and Multi-Vehicle Accidents
key_points: Road traffic accident claims are predominantly brought in negligence. A breach of the Highway Code may be relied upon as tending to establish liability, though it is not conclusive (s.38(7) Road Traffic Act 1988). A road traffic criminal conviction shifts the burden of proof onto the defendant to disprove civil liability.

A driver must not assume that another driver will be sensible and careful (Truscott v McLaren [1982] RTR 34). The legal test is whether conduct was unreasonable taking into account the exigencies of the particular situation — not the counsel of perfection using hindsight (Greene v Sookdeo, Privy Council). However, courts impose a high burden on drivers to reflect the fact that a car is a potentially dangerous weapon.

The Highway Code hierarchy (Rules H1–H3) places pedestrians, cyclists, horse riders and motorcyclists at greatest risk and requires those controlling vehicles capable of causing the greatest harm (LGVs, vans, cars/taxis, motorcycles) to bear the greatest responsibility for taking care.

SPEED — High speed alone is not necessarily negligence; the question is whether speed was appropriate for road conditions at the time (Rules 124–125). A speeding claimant will not automatically have damages reduced unless the excess speed was causative of their own loss. A finding of 25% contributory negligence has been applied where there was 'reckless flagrancy' in excess of the limit. In Lamoon v Fry the defendant was 40% to blame for colliding with a cyclist on the wrong side of a country lane bend despite keeping to his side, because his speed of 40mph was excessive for those conditions. In Puffett v Hayfield excessive speed was found where the defendant knew children played in the road.

OVERTAKING — Rules 166–167 of the Highway Code set out circumstances in which overtaking is prohibited (bends, brows of hills, junctions, narrow roads, school crossings, queuing traffic, level crossings, a vehicle signalling right, before a left turn where a cyclist is present). An overtaking manoeuvre warrants a very high degree of care because the driver may be blind to circumstances on the left-hand side of the road.

OVERTAKING — A motorcyclist who drove on the wrong side of the road to jump a queue and collided with a car edging out from a side turning was 80% to blame; the emerging driver who had been waved out was 20% to blame (Powell v Moody (1966) 110 Sol Jo 215). When being overtaken, a driver should not accelerate, swerve outward or change course without warning (Holdack v Bullock Bros Electrical Ltd (1964); Ogden v Barber). The Court of Appeal has held it is not negligent for the driver being overtaken to fail to decelerate, and imposing such a duty would place too high a burden on the reasonable driver (Smith v Cribben). In Pykett v Clement the overtaken driver was not negligent for failing to slow down; the overtaking driver's misjudged attempt was the cause.

EMERGING FROM MINOR ROAD — The general rule is that the onus is on the emerging driver to give way and take care. There is no duty on the correctly proceeding main road driver to guard against the possibility of the emerging driver's negligence by slowing down (Humphrey v Leigh [1971] RTR 363). However, once the main road driver has time to see the side road driver emerge and perceive a danger that is more than a mere possibility, a duty arises to take reasonable evasive action (Truscott v McLaren — 20% contribution imposed).

EMERGING FROM MINOR ROAD — The main road driver may bear partial liability where speeding even modestly: 20% contribution upheld against a driver going 35–40mph in a 30mph zone in good conditions on a wide road (Hamied v Estwick); similarly in Dolby v Milner. In Taylor v Raspin the emerging driver had a duty to look left a second time before pulling out; the breach was the primary cause of a collision with a motorcyclist; but the motorcyclist was 45% contributorily negligent for excessive speed.

EMERGING — Clarke v Winchurch: where a car driver emerged extremely slowly and carefully into slow/stationary traffic, it would be a counsel of perfection to require them only to poke their nose out then stop; fault lay entirely with a moped rider who had overtaken a line of traffic. Worsfold v Howe: Clarke lays down no principle of law that the overtaking vehicle in such a scenario is always liable. Farley v Buckley: each case is fact-sensitive; neither the emerging driver nor the overtaking vehicle is an insurer for the other; 'nose poking' does not protect an emerging driver from liability.

EMERGING — Where the waiting driver's vision is partly blocked (e.g. by a large bus or lorry), they should wait the extra seconds to guard against the possibility of another vehicle or motorcycle masked behind the large vehicle (Harding v Hinchcliffe (1964)).

MISLEADING SIGNALS — Where a motorist on a main road gives a misleading signal that contributes to another motorist emerging into their path, the signaller will be partially to blame. A one-third/two-thirds split was imposed where the signalling driver was one-third responsible and the emerging driver two-thirds (Wadsworth v Gillespie [1978] CLY 764). This contrasts with Clarke v Winchurch where flashing headlights by a bus driver to invite a car to emerge meant only 'come on as far as I am concerned' and was not negligent even though it encouraged the car to pull out in front of a moped. Rule 170 cautions against assuming a vehicle indicating left will actually turn left.

BRAKING AND SKIDDING — A following driver must drive at a distance enabling them to deal with all urgent situations that may reasonably be anticipated (Highway Code Rule 126). Where a vehicle strikes the vehicle it is following, this strongly suggests negligence on the part of the following driver. To stop sharply without apparent reason gives rise to liability: in Elizabeth v Motor Insurers' Bureau [1981] RTR 405 (CA) the burden of proof was reversed where a vehicle braked suddenly, requiring the driver to explain why. In Gussman v Gratton-Storey (1968) braking sharply to avoid a pheasant was insufficient to discharge that burden — a good reason, not merely a reason, is required.

BRAKING — Where a lead driver stopped suddenly for no reason on a dual carriageway, the Court of Appeal declined to hold that driver wholly responsible, preferring to apportion liability 60% (following driver) / 40% (lead driver) as the following driver was within half a car's length at 40mph.

SKIDDING — Rule 119 of the Highway Code states skidding is usually the fault of the driver. In Richley v Faull the presence of a skid reverses the burden of proof: the driver must prove the skid occurred without negligence on their part (see also Custine v Nottingham Corp [1970] RTR 365). The presumption may be rebutted where the driver has kept a proper look out, driven very slowly and applied brakes gently on ice.

VEHICLE LIGHTING — During darkness and reduced visibility there is an obligation to use headlights (defined as half an hour after sunset to half an hour before sunrise). Failure to use headlights in poor weather or darkness gives rise to a rebuttable presumption of negligence (Burgess v Hearn (1965) — motorcyclist in thick fog without dipped headlights held negligent). Unlit parked vehicles will usually attract a share of liability apportioned with the moving vehicle driver.

MULTI-VEHICLE ACCIDENTS — In a concertina collision causation can be difficult where a vehicle is hit repeatedly. Key points: (1) a driver responsible for some damage will, subject to causation and remoteness, be liable for all damage sustained; claimants need not claim against every tortfeasor; (2) a defendant not fully at fault should bring Part 20 proceedings for contribution or indemnity; (3) where there are multiple claimants, it is best practice for liability to be determined in one judgment and consideration should be given to consolidating proceedings.

EXCESSIVE CITATION OF AUTHORITY — The court has expressed disapproval of the excessive citation of authority in RTA cases (Foskett v Mistry [1984] RTR 1). Most RTA cases turn on their particular facts. Provided prior decisions are not mistaken for principles of law, they give helpful guidance as to how similar issues are likely to be resolved.

articles.summary: General principles governing road traffic accident liability in negligence, covering the role of the Highway Code, speed, overtaking, emerging from minor roads, misleading signals, braking and skidding, vehicle lighting, and multi-vehicle accidents. Synthesised from LexisNexis Practice Note with reference to 25 leading cases.
articles.source: LexisNexis Practice Note — Driver Liability in Common Types of Road Traffic Accidents

articles.title: Fundamental Dishonesty — The Legal Framework: Dishonesty Test, Section 57 CJCA 2015, QOCS and Critical Distinctions for Credit Hire
key_points: OVERVIEW — The term 'fundamental dishonesty' appears in two distinct legal instruments: (1) CJCA 2015, s.57, which empowers a court to dismiss an entire personal injury claim, including honest elements, where the claimant has been fundamentally dishonest; and (2) CPR 44.16(1), which strips a claimant of QOCS protection allowing adverse costs orders to be enforced in full. The term is undefined in either instrument and has been developed entirely through judicial decisions.

DISHONESTY TEST (IVEY v GENTING) — Dishonesty is determined by a two-stage test from Ivey v Genting Casinos (UK) Ltd [2017] UKSC 67: (1) establish the actual state of the individual's knowledge or belief as to the facts (subjective); (2) once that state of mind is established, determine whether the conduct was honest or dishonest by reference to the standards of ordinary people (objective). The old Ghosh direction on dishonesty no longer applies in civil proceedings.

RELIABILITY vs DISHONESTY — Unreliable evidence is not necessarily dishonest evidence. The High Court in Molodi v Cambridge Vibration Maintenance Service acknowledged that claimants will sometimes make errors or forget relevant matters; 100% consistency cannot reasonably be expected. Dishonesty requires a deliberate intent; a claimant who has overstated their case without deliberately intending to do so may not be fundamentally dishonest (Wright v Satellite Information Services Ltd — care claim reduced from £73,000+ to £2,100 but no FD found; claimant attempting to convince rather than deceive). In Smith v Ashwell Maintenance Ltd: 'Faking pain would almost undoubtedly amount to fundamental dishonesty. Exaggeration, with mixed motives of attempting to convince or deceive, is not.'

WHAT IS 'FUNDAMENTAL'? — The dishonesty must go to the root of the claim or a substantial part of it; it must not be merely collateral or incidental (Gosling v Hailo and Screwfix Direct, approved by the Court of Appeal in Howlett v Davies [2017] EWCA Civ 1696). The distinction drawn in Gosling is between (a) dishonesty which is incidental/collateral (does not expose claimant to costs under QOCS) and (b) dishonesty which is fundamental — a claim that depended as to a substantial or important part of itself upon dishonesty. In Sinfield (LOCOG v Sinfield [2018] EWHC 51 (QB)), the two-stage test is: (1) has the claimant acted dishonestly? (2) has the dishonesty substantially affected the presentation of their case in respect of liability or quantum in a way that potentially adversely affected the defendant in a significant way? 'Substantially affects' = going to the root or heart of the claim.

MUYEPA THREE-FACTOR ANALYSIS — In Muyepa v Ministry of Defence, the High Court identified three helpful questions: (1) At what stage and in what circumstances did the dishonest conduct start? (2) Does the dishonesty taint the whole claim or is it limited to a divisible element? (3) How does the value of the valid underlying claim compare to that of the dishonestly inflated claim? In Muyepa, dishonesty was found fundamental because it started early, tainted the whole claim, and the genuine award was only 3–5.5% of the claim as advanced. The Muyepa approach applies equally to both s.57 and CPR 44.16(1), though post-discontinuance CPR 44.16(1) applications do not require the same precision in assessing the valid claim value.

CRITICAL DISTINCTION — S.57 vs CPR 44.16: (1) Under CJCA 2015, s.57, it is the CLAIMANT who must be found to have been fundamentally dishonest. (2) Under CPR 44.16(1), it is the CLAIM which must be fundamentally dishonest. In practice it will rarely be the case that a claim is fundamentally dishonest without the claimant also being fundamentally dishonest (Sinfield, obiter), but the distinction can matter in certain contexts — particularly credit hire and other service-provider claims where the benefit does not flow to the claimant personally.

CRITICAL FOR CREDIT HIRE — MICHAEL v HURFORD — In Michael v I E & D Hurford Ltd (trading as Rainbow), the High Court confirmed that where the disputed elements of a claim (specifically credit hire fees, physiotherapy, vehicle storage and transportation) are not paid to the claimant for their own benefit but to third-party service providers, it may be less rare than expected that the claim could be fundamentally dishonest without the claimant personally being so. In Michael, discrepancies in the claim for physiotherapy (which had not actually been provided) were attributed to the claimant's lack of understanding of the basis on which their solicitors had advanced the claim. The County Court was entitled to conclude that if there had been dishonesty, it was not on the part of the claimant, and the High Court upheld that finding. This principle is directly applicable to credit hire: where a credit hire company or its associated solicitors inflate or misrepresent hire charges or hire period, but the claimant does not personally understand or direct that misrepresentation, a s.57 application against the claimant is unlikely to succeed even if the claim as a whole has dishonest elements.

S.57 — WHEN IT APPLIES — s.57 came into force on 13 April 2015 and does not apply to proceedings issued before that date (pre-2015 cases: rely on Summers v Fairclough Homes). It applies where a court finds the claimant entitled to damages in respect of personal injury ('the primary claim') but the claimant has been fundamentally dishonest. Key boundaries: it does NOT apply to claims for false imprisonment (Reynolds v Chief Constable of Kent Police — false imprisonment is not a PI claim); it DOES apply to claims for anxiety under the Protection from Harassment Act 1997 (Taiwo v Homelets of Bath — 'personal injury' includes impairment of mental condition under CJCA 2015, s.57(8), identical to Limitation Act 1980, s.38(1)). s.57 does not apply where there is no cause of action, no injury, or a staged accident — the court must first find the claimant entitled to damages.

S.57 — RELATED CLAIMS — A 'related claim' under s.57(8) is another claim for damages in respect of personal injury made: (a) based on the same incident(s) as the primary claim, and (b) by a person other than the person who made the primary claim. An honest driver who assists a fundamentally dishonest passenger to make a claim may therefore fall within this definition and have their own honest PI claim dismissed. The related claims provision is confined to personal injury — it cannot sweep in a credit hire or property damage claim arising from the same accident.

S.57 — THE THREE-STAGE SINFIELD PROCEDURE — When a defendant applies under s.57: (1) Is the claimant entitled to damages? If not, end of matter (though QOCS under CPR 44.16 may still require consideration). (2) Has the defendant proved on the balance of probabilities that the claimant has been fundamentally dishonest in relation to the primary/related claim? (3) If yes, dismiss the claim including honest elements — unless the claimant would suffer 'substantial injustice'. The court must record the amount of damages it would have awarded and deduct that figure from any costs awarded to the defendant.

S.57 — SUBSTANTIAL INJUSTICE — The court must dismiss the claim unless it is satisfied that the claimant would suffer 'substantial injustice'. Courts have been reluctant to define the test. It is clear that: (a) losing entitlement to damages for honest parts of the claim is not itself substantial injustice — that is the purpose of the section; (b) a change of position arising from an interim payment is not sufficient; (c) the court may consider the claimant's personal circumstances. In Williams-Henry, the claimant was found FD; the court declined to find substantial injustice despite the claimant's assertion she would commit suicide if unable to fund therapy, but held she need not repay a £75,000 interim payment as repayment plus dismissal together would render her homeless, jobless and suicidal.

S.57 — COSTS MECHANIC ON DISMISSAL — Where the court dismisses a claim under s.57, it must record the damages that would have been awarded and deduct that sum from the defendant's costs order. This means: if the genuine damages exceed the defendant's awarded costs, the net result may be that no costs are payable — which can itself be a factor pointing towards substantial injustice. Criminal proceedings or contempt of court proceedings may still be brought notwithstanding the s.57 dismissal; on sentencing, the court must have regard to the dismissal.

CPR 44.16 — QOCS FRAMEWORK — QOCS under CPR 44.14 protects personal injury claimants from adverse costs orders in most circumstances. Under QOCS (proceedings issued before 6 April 2023): adverse costs orders against a claimant may be enforced without permission only to the extent that the aggregate costs do not exceed the aggregate damages and interest awarded in favour of the claimant. Under QOCS (proceedings issued on or after 6 April 2023): the enforcement cap has been extended to include the aggregate of orders for damages, costs AND interest — including agreed settlements — made in favour of the claimant. QOCS does not apply to claims where a CFA or ATE policy pre-dates 1 April 2013, or to mesothelioma claims with a pre-2013 CFA.

CPR 44.16(1) — FUNDAMENTAL DISHONESTY EXCEPTION — Where a claim is found on the balance of probabilities to be fundamentally dishonest, CPR 44.16(1) allows costs orders to be enforced against the claimant to their full extent, subject to the permission of the court. This exception is broader than s.57: it speaks of the 'claim' (not the 'claimant') being fundamentally dishonest, meaning it could in principle be engaged even where the claimant did not personally direct or understand the dishonest elements. The procedure is: issues of FD are ordinarily determined at trial; where proceedings have settled, FD issues will not in general be determined save in exceptional circumstances; where the claimant has served a notice of discontinuance, the court may nonetheless direct that FD be determined; a FD application can be made post-discontinuance.

CPR 44.16 — KEY RTA CASES — (1) Zurich Insurance plc v Bain: claimant admitted in a recorded call that she was uninjured and had 'succumbed to the temptation' of cold-call claims management companies; no injury, no claim — on appeal QOCS protection stripped. (2) Waggett v Warchalowski: first defendant's counterclaim for PI including credit hire, storage, recovery and repair showed numerous inconsistencies; nine previous RTAs; only one disclosed to medical expert; FD found in absence at trial — indemnity costs awarded. (3) Howlett v Davies: FD upheld on appeal even though fraud had not been specifically pleaded in the defence. (4) Pegg v Webb: defendant appealed the trial judge's failure to find FD; High Court allowed the appeal, made a FD finding and applied CPR 44.16 — claimant to pay costs subject to adjustment for defendant's failure to prove the accident was staged.

CPR 44.16 — WHERE ALLEGATIONS FAIL — FD must be proved on the balance of probabilities. The more serious the allegation the stronger the evidence required (Re H (Minors), Lord Nicholls). In Meadows v La Tasca: trial judge found the accident had not taken place at all — this went too far; the proper finding was that the evidence was too weak to prove the claim; FD finding therefore overturned. Quantum reduction alone does not establish FD. Roberts v Kesson: persistence in dishonesty is not the correct test — a global assessment of the claim is required by reference to its root and importance (applying Howlett and Sinfield). Cojanu v Essex: claimant's conviction for attempted murder of his wife was not FD going to root of his clinical negligence claim because the mechanism of injury was determined on expert evidence, not the claimant's credibility.

PLEADING REQUIREMENTS — A defendant does not need to plead FD to make an application under s.57 or to disapply QOCS under CPR 44.16(1); it is not appropriate to plead FD on a contingent or speculative basis. However, it is sensible and in keeping with the overriding objective to plead the allegation so that parties know the case they must meet. Critically: a claimant must have sufficient notice and opportunity to deal with credibility issues before trial — matters which could lead to a FD finding cannot be sprung on the claimant at trial if they might have been countered with further evidence. Allegations should be set out within the statements of case. No FD allegation needs to be specifically pleaded to permit a finding (Howlett) but adequate notice is required.

PRACTICAL IMPLICATIONS — Track allocation: FD allegations typically extend trial time beyond a day; cases may need multi-track allocation even where otherwise suitable for the fast track. Evidence to gather: social media history; contemporaneous medical records compared with the expert's history; independent witness statements (especially in low-velocity RTAs); employment/colleague evidence in loss of earnings claims. FD can be found on liability or quantum or both. FD can be found in absence if the evidence warrants it (Waggett). A generally honest person is not thereby immune from a FD finding in relation to a specific claim (Conclusions in FD.docx).
cases_cited: Ivey v Genting Casinos (UK) Ltd (trading as Crockfords Club) [2017] UKSC 67
Molodi v Cambridge Vibration Maintenance Service
Howlett v Davies; Ageas Insurance Ltd [2017] EWCA Civ 1696
Gosling v Hailo and Screwfix Direct (County Court)
London Organising Committee of the Olympic and Paralympic Games (in liquidation) v Sinfield [2018] EWHC 51 (QB)
Muyepa v Ministry of Defence
Michael v I E & D Hurford Ltd (trading as Rainbow)
Cojanu v Essex Partnership University NHS Trust
Roberts v Kesson
Wright v Satellite Information Services Ltd
Smith v Ashwell Maintenance Ltd
Williams-Henry (County Court)
Reynolds v Chief Constable of Kent Police
Taiwo v Homelets of Bath
Zurich Insurance plc v Bain
Waggett v Warchalowski
Pegg v Webb
Meadows v La Tasca Restaurants Limited
Re H (Minors)

articles.summary: Comprehensive analysis of the fundamental dishonesty framework: the Ivey v Genting dishonesty test; the meaning of 'fundamental' (Gosling/Howlett/Sinfield); the critical procedural and conceptual distinction between s.57 CJCA 2015 (claim dismissal — the CLAIMANT must be FD) and CPR 44.16(1) (QOCS stripping — the CLAIM must be FD); the s.57 three-stage Sinfield procedure; substantial injustice; QOCS mechanics (including the post-April 2023 extension); and the key RTA cases. Includes the critical credit hire application: Michael v Hurford confirms that where credit hire or other service-provider charges are paid to third parties and the claimant does not understand the solicitor's claim, the claimant may not personally be FD under s.57 even if the claim contains dishonest elements.
articles.source: LexisNexis Practice Note — What is Fundamental Dishonesty?; Howlett v Davies [2017] EWCA Civ 1696; LOCOG v Sinfield [2018] EWHC 51 (QB); Michael v Hurford

articles.title: MIB Agreements 2015/2017 — Legal Framework, Uninsured vs Untraced Driver Distinctions, and Credit Hire Recovery
articles.source: LexisNexis Practice Note — Motor Insurers' Bureau (MIB) 2015/2017 Agreements; Patrick Vincent KC and Isaac Hogarth, 12 King's Bench Walk — 'Motor Insurance and Indemnity Update' Presentation
key_points: THE MIB AS FUND OF LAST RESORT — THE HIERARCHY — Before the MIB is engaged, practitioners must consider the full hierarchy of liability: (1) Is there an insurer willing to indemnify in the normal course? If not, (2) Is there an insurer liable under RTA 1988? If not, (3) Is there an Article 75 insurer? If not, (4) MIB deals with the claim subject to Agreement conditions. The MIB is a fund of last resort, not an insurer. Understanding whether liability falls on a named insurer, an Article 75 insurer, or the MIB directly determines the procedural route — and crucially for credit hire — whether the GTA or a direct litigation route applies.

ARTICLE 75 INSURERS — An Article 75 insurer is the insurer who, at the time of the accident, was providing insurance in respect of the vehicle from the use of which liability arose. Critically, an insurer remains the Article 75 insurer notwithstanding that: (a) the insurance was obtained by fraud, misrepresentation, non-disclosure or mistake; (b) the cover was backdated; or (c) the use of the vehicle was other than that permitted under the policy. The MIB may itself settle or compromise any claim in respect of Road Traffic Act liability and then recover from the Article 75 insurer. This means that in many cases where a defendant appears 'uninsured', a policy may exist on the vehicle even if void or voidable — and the insurer (not MIB) will be the primary target. Article 75 insurers are bound by the MIB Uninsured Agreement terms.

UNINSURED DRIVER (2015 AGREEMENT) — PROCEDURE AND CREDIT HIRE RECOVERY — A claim against an uninsured driver proceeds in the normal litigation way with two key modifications: (1) the MIB's application form must be completed and submitted; and (2) the MIB must be joined as an additional defendant at the outset. The MIB will satisfy any unsatisfied judgment (including costs) that has not been met within seven days. The relevant sum covers death, personal injury AND property damage — which includes credit hire charges. This is the critical point: credit hire is recoverable in uninsured driver claims as part of the judgment against the first defendant, with the MIB satisfying the judgment. There is no GTA framework, no GTA safe harbour, and no GTA rate: recovery is on common law principles, meaning BHR is the benchmark and the full suite of challenges (need, impecuniosity, rate, duration) applies.

UNINSURED DRIVER — OBLIGATIONS AND JOINING MIB — The MIB incurs no liability unless joined as an additional defendant from the outset. The uninsured driver (first defendant) must also be served — the MIB's liability extends no further than the uninsured driver's. If the driver cannot be served, neither the driver nor the MIB will have liability. The MIB may consent to substituted service on itself where proper attempts to locate and serve the first defendant have been made. The MIB may require the claimant to pursue other potentially liable parties, indemnifying the claimant for reasonable litigation costs. In practice the MIB often settles before judgment and then takes an assignment of the claimant's rights to pursue the uninsured driver.

UNINSURED DRIVER — KEY EXCLUSIONS AFFECTING CREDIT HIRE RECOVERY — The MIB's liability is limited to that which would have been covered by compulsory road traffic insurance under RTA 1988, Pt VI. Relevant exclusions: (1) Vehicle not a 'mechanically propelled vehicle intended or adapted for use on roads' — excludes farm machinery, mobility scooters and similar (see Motor Vehicles (Compulsory Insurance) Act 2022 below); (2) Vehicle not used on a 'road or other public place' — private land incidents excluded (subject to MIB v Lewis / Vnuk position pre-28 June 2022); (3) Crown, local authority, police and NHS vehicles — generally excluded unless separately insured; (4) Property damage cap of £1.2m (increased from £1m by Supplementary Agreement 2017, applying to accidents from 1 March 2017); (5) Claimant has another source of recovery — MIB will not indemnify where the claimant is entitled to payment from any other person or organisation (excluding CICA); (6) For accidents between 1 August 2015 and 1 March 2017: MIB not liable for vehicle damage claims where the claimant knew or had reason to believe the vehicle was uninsured (removed by Supplementary Agreement 2017 for accidents from 1 March 2017 onwards).

UNINSURED DRIVER — PASSENGER EXCLUSION AND KNOWLEDGE TEST — Claims are excluded where the claimant was a passenger who voluntarily travelled and 'knew or had reason to believe' the vehicle was stolen/unlawfully taken or was being used without valid insurance. The burden of proving knowledge rests with the MIB but reverses to the claimant if the MIB establishes any of: (i) the claimant was the owner or registered keeper or had permitted use; (ii) the claimant knew the driver was too young to hold a licence; or (iii) the claimant knew the driver was disqualified. Impairment from drink or drugs does not negate knowledge. In Colley v MIB (Court of Appeal), the MIB was liable where the claimant knew the driver (rather than the vehicle) was uninsured. The exclusion applies only where the claimant knew the vehicle was uninsured or stolen — not merely that the driver personally had no insurance. This is a significant protection for innocent passengers.

UNTRACED DRIVER (2017 AGREEMENT) — PROCEDURE — Where the at-fault driver cannot be identified, the claimant applies directly to the MIB (not through the courts). The MIB investigates and adjudicates the claim. The Untraced Drivers Agreement 2017 applies to accidents from 1 March 2017; the 2003 Agreement applies to accidents from 14 February 2003 to 28 February 2017. Time limits mirror the Limitation Act 1980 (three years from accident for death and PI). The previous 14-day police reporting requirement has been removed — replaced by a requirement to report to police as soon as reasonably possible when requested by the MIB. The claimant must cooperate with any police investigation.

UNTRACED DRIVER — THE CRITICAL CREDIT HIRE RESTRICTION: SIGNIFICANT PERSONAL INJURY THRESHOLD — This is the most important distinction for credit hire recovery. Under the Untraced Drivers Agreement 2017, the MIB has NO LIABILITY for property damage caused by an unidentified vehicle UNLESS the MIB has already paid compensation for a 'significant personal injury'. Significant personal injury means: (a) death of a victim; or (b) injury requiring at least two nights of in-patient hospital treatment; or (c) injury requiring three or more sessions of out-patient hospital treatment. Practical consequence: in a straightforward hit-and-run where the claimant suffers soft-tissue injuries or minor injuries not meeting this threshold, credit hire charges (as property-related loss) will be entirely irrecoverable under the Untraced Drivers Agreement. Even where injury is serious enough to qualify, the credit hire claim must still be established on its merits. This is fundamentally different from uninsured driver claims where property damage including credit hire is recoverable as part of the judgment without any injury threshold.

UNTRACED DRIVER — CONTRIBUTORY NEGLIGENCE AND THIRD PARTIES — The MIB may reduce compensation for contributory negligence (without the benefit of the untraced driver's evidence). Where an identified party is partly or wholly responsible for the accident, they will be liable for the whole loss notwithstanding an untraced driver's fault — in which case the Untraced Drivers Agreement will not apply (the claim proceeds under the Uninsured Drivers Agreement or against the identified party direct). MIB may require the claimant to proceed against a third party, indemnifying the claimant against costs.

UNTRACED DRIVER — COSTS REGIME: A MAJOR DISADVANTAGE — In untraced driver claims, the MIB pays only a contribution towards the claimant's legal costs (not a full costs order). Costs are limited to what has been specifically pre-authorised by the MIB plus reasonably incurred counsel's fees. A claimant may request a higher costs contribution only by demonstrating exceptional complexity. This is in stark contrast to uninsured driver proceedings where, as MIB satisfies a court judgment including costs, normal recoverable costs apply. The unfavourable costs regime in untraced driver claims is an additional reason why claimants prefer to proceed under the Uninsured Agreement wherever possible.

UNINSURED OR UNTRACED? — THE CRITICAL STRATEGIC CHOICE FOR CREDIT HIRE CLAIMANTS — The determination of which Agreement governs has profound consequences for credit hire recovery. The correct analysis: (1) If the claimant can prove on the balance of probabilities that a named defendant was the driver → Uninsured Drivers Agreement (better: full property damage recovery, normal costs regime). (2) If the driver cannot be identified → Untraced Drivers Agreement (worse: property damage only recoverable if significant PI established, limited costs contribution). Where a name was provided but may be false, practitioners should register under both Agreements, especially as the third anniversary approaches. Where an identified vehicle has a known owner but unidentified driver, there is a rebuttable presumption the owner was driving → Uninsured Drivers Agreement. In Cameron (Supreme Court), an identified vehicle with an unidentified driver: a claim under a pseudonym cannot proceed; the claim falls under the Untraced Drivers Agreement — a significant restriction. In Gurtner, a defendant who gave his name and insurance details to police but emigrated to Canada was dealt with under the Uninsured Agreement (not Untraced) — the driver had been identified at the scene.

CAMERON (SUPREME COURT) — IDENTIFIED VEHICLE, UNKNOWN DRIVER — In Cameron, a claimant injured in a hit-and-run collision where the car was identified but the driver was not was permitted by the Court of Appeal to bring an action against 'the person unknown'. The Supreme Court allowed the insurer's appeal: a person who cannot be identified cannot be sued under a pseudonym or description unless the claim form can be served or service properly dispensed with. The claim therefore fell within the Untraced Drivers Agreement — triggering the significant personal injury threshold for property damage and the limited costs regime, rather than the normal litigation route applicable to uninsured drivers. Cameron is important for credit hire practitioners: where a vehicle is identified at the scene but the driver's identity is never confirmed, the credit hire claim faces the untraced driver's property damage restriction.

BREXIT AND THE MIB — POST-IP COMPLETION DAY POSITION — The MIB was formerly the designated compensation body under EU Motor Insurance Directives, meaning that where the Agreements fell below the Directive standard, claimants could sue the MIB as an emanation of the state. Following UK withdrawal from the EU, it is no longer possible to bring a claim against the MIB for incompatibility with the Directives. This rule is both prospective and retrospective — it applies to cases where damage was suffered both before and after the end of the transition period (11 pm, 31 December 2020). However: rights arising under EU Directives that were recognised by the Court of Justice or by UK courts in cases decided before IP completion day are preserved under section 4 of the European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018. MIB v Lewis (Court of Appeal — MIB liable as emanation of state for uninsured vehicle on private land) was decided before IP completion day and so that right was preserved for accidents pre-28 June 2022.

VNUK, MIB v LEWIS AND THE MOTOR VEHICLES (COMPULSORY INSURANCE) ACT 2022 — In Vnuk, the CJEU held that EU Motor Insurance Directives required compulsory insurance for vehicles on private land (a tractor in a farmyard). As RTA 1988 had not been amended to implement this, the Court of Appeal in MIB v Lewis held MIB was liable as an emanation of the state for a claimant injured by an uninsured vehicle on private land — a right preserved post-Brexit by s.4 EU (Withdrawal) Act 2018 for accidents before 28 June 2022. However, the Motor Vehicles (Compulsory Insurance) Act 2022 means that for accidents occurring on or after 28 June 2022, MIB is no longer liable for: (a) vehicles such as mobility scooters, go-karts and farm machinery that are mechanically propelled but not intended for road use; or (b) accidents occurring on private land. This restores the pre-Vnuk position domestically for post-28 June 2022 accidents.

UNTRACED DRIVER — APPEAL AND ARBITRATION PROCEDURE — If MIB rejects a claim or makes an unsatisfactory award, the claimant has six weeks to appeal. The appeal is decided by a KC arbitrator (the former right of appeal to the Secretary of State has been removed). The arbitrator may make a preliminary written decision on the papers; parties then have 28 days to accept, make written observations, or request an oral hearing — failure to respond within 28 days constitutes deemed acceptance of the preliminary decision (a potential trap for practitioners). Oral hearings take place in private; parties may be represented, call witnesses and cross-examine. The arbitrator may: determine whether the Agreement applies; consider s.33 Limitation Act discretion; determine quantum; and award costs. Arbitrator fees are paid by the MIB unless the arbitrator finds there were no reasonable grounds for the appeal, in which case claimant or their solicitors may be ordered to pay.

INTEREST IN UNTRACED DRIVER CLAIMS — The MIB adopts the same method of calculating interest as a court would, with one exception: interest on general damages runs from the date the award is made or from the date three years after the accident, whichever is the sooner. This means that in long-running untraced driver claims, the claimant may lose interest on general damages for the period beyond three years if the MIB delays its award.

PRACTICAL IMPLICATIONS FOR CREDIT HIRE ORGANISATIONS — CHOs taking on claims from innocent motorists hit by uninsured or untraced drivers need to apply a different risk assessment depending on which Agreement applies. (1) Uninsured driver: credit hire is recoverable in principle as part of the court judgment; MIB is joined; full common law challenge applies (no GTA); ensure MIB application form is submitted correctly and MIB joined at outset — failure to join MIB from the outset is a complete bar to recovery. (2) Untraced driver: assess at intake whether the significant personal injury threshold (death, 2 nights in-patient, or 3+ outpatient sessions) is likely to be met — if not, property damage including credit hire is irrecoverable under the Agreement; no GTA; limited costs contribution; arbitration not litigation; consider whether the claim should instead be routed under the Uninsured Agreement (e.g., is there a rebuttable presumption the vehicle owner was driving?). (3) In both cases: comprehensive insurance exclusion (post-August 2015) for vehicle damage directly engages need — the claimant with comprehensive cover should be using their own insurer's provision. (4) Register under both Agreements if uncertain which applies.

SECTION 152 RTA AMENDMENTS AND DOWNGRADING STATUS — As a result of amendments to section 152 of the RTA effective 1 November 2019, an insurer can no longer obtain a section 152 declaration that it is entitled to avoid a policy for misrepresentation or non-disclosure and thereby reduce its status from section 151 insurer to Article 75 insurer. Since 1 November 2019 an insurer can only avoid a section 151 liability and downgrade its status to Article 75 if: (1) before the accident the policy has been cancelled by mutual consent or by virtue of a provision contained in it (section 152(1)), OR (2) the use of the vehicle at the time of the accident fell outside the use permitted by the policy. This is a critical factor in the ongoing battle between different insurers potentially involved after an accident to determine which should pay the claim. Reduction of status is a critical factor because it determines whether the insurer has a direct contractual or section 151 liability or merely an Article 75 obligation via MIB.

EFFECT OF DOWNGRADING TO ARTICLE 75 STATUS — If a contractual or section 151 insurer can downgrade its status to Article 75 insurer then it no longer has any direct liability to meet a judgment obtained by a claimant. That is because after the downgrading of status there is no longer any insurance covering the driver at the time of the accident with the consequence that the liability to meet a judgment devolves on MIB under the Uninsured Drivers Agreement. The fact that the insurer will, in fact, have to pay the claim as Article 75 insurer through its Articles of Association is a consequence of the internal arrangements of the members of MIB — it does not affect the fact that the legal obligation to meet the judgment is on MIB not the Article 75 insurer. This means that where a claimant is pursuing an Article 75 insurer the correct defendant is MIB not the insurer, although MIB will often agree to act as agent for MIB so that it can be sued in its own name and then deal with the claim as Article 75 insurer.

FOUR BENEFITS OF DOWNGRADING TO ARTICLE 75 STATUS — There are four principal benefits for an insurer that downgrades to Article 75 status: (1) FIRSTLY, since the liability of the Article 75 insurer is legally the liability of MIB the Article 75 insurer can take the benefit of the fact that MIB is not liable if there is another liable insurer — so if there are two insurers who are liable in respect of the same defendant (e.g. one in respect of the vehicle and one who insured the driver), the Article 75 insurer can avoid any liability and the claim will have to be met by the other non-contractual or section 151 insurer. (2) SECONDLY, if there is more than one tortfeasor and the insurer (or the tortfeasor) who has contractual liability is mainly to blame for the accident, the Article 75 insurer gets off the hook because as liability is really an MIB liability and MIB has no contractual or section 151 liability on another tortfeasor whose insurer has contractual liability. (3) THIRDLY, the Article 75 insurer can rely on all the terms and exclusions of the Uninsured Drivers Agreement (UDA), in the same way MIB itself can — in particular the recent case of Colley v Shuker [2020] EWHC 3435 (QB) found that an Article 75 insurer who can prove that the claimant knew the driver was uninsured can rely on the exclusion in Clause 8(1)(b) of the UDA. (4) FOURTHLY, the Article 75 insurer can rely on Clause 14 of the UDA and require the claimant to join another driver or another insurer or the police in a car chase case, in order to exert tactical pressure or argue for a meaningful degree of negligence in circumstances where the claimant would not otherwise do so because he has a cast iron case against D1.

POLICY CANCELLATION AND SECTION 152(1) — Cancellation of the policy before the accident under section 152(1) by mutual consent or by virtue of any provision contained in it will reduce the insurer's status from section 151 to Article 75 insurer. Now that there is no possibility of downgrading status by obtaining a declaration, insurers will have to look much more carefully at the wording of their policies in relation to cancellation. The provisions of section 152(1) only apply if the cancellation has taken place before the accident and has been by mutual consent of the insured and insurer (e.g. the insured wanting to cancel the policy because they have found a cheaper one) or where the cancellation was by virtue of any provision contained in the policy. This means that the term of the policy which gives the insurer the right to cancel must be clear (a) as to the circumstances in which the right can be exercised (e.g. for failure to pay the premium) and (b) as to how the cancellation is to be effected and communicated to the insured. If the term is not clear or the cancellation is not strictly in accordance with how the cancellation should be achieved then section 152(1) will not be satisfied.

CANCELLATION TRAP FOR CLAIMS HANDLERS — It is important to remember that if the cancellation is done before the accident (and it is done properly) then if the MID has been updated before the accident to show that the policy has been cancelled the insurer will not be an Article 75 insurer and will be free of any liability (see Article 75(2)(iv)). The liability to meet any judgment will then devolve upon the MIB central fund. For that reason it is vital that insurers maintain in-house procedures for cancellation and also make provision for updating the MID. Beware the trap for claims handlers of avoiding the policy as a knee jerk reaction and then trying to cancel it — the amendments to section 152 mean that merely avoiding policies will only prevent insurers from obtaining declarations to avoid their section 151 liability to the claimant. The trap is that if a policy has been avoided then there is no policy to cancel, so the insurer cannot then rely on the cancellation provisions of section 152(1) to get out of its section 151 obligations.

STATUS OF MID — NAGORSKI v NIKOLICS — In the recent County Court decision of Recorder Parrington at Liverpool, the question arose as to whether an insurer on the MID remains liable as section 151 insurer after a vehicle has been sold. The insurer argued that the MID is a useful tool but is only a database and is not conclusive. C argued that the entry of a vehicle on the MID places automatic and unconditional obligations on insurers to satisfy judgments under section 151. The Court held that the MID is a database designed to assist the police and others in identifying insurers but it is not conclusive. Under the terms of the policy there was no cover (although there is surprisingly little in the judgment about this or the relevant policy terms), and so there was no section 151 liability. The Court did not follow Tattersall v Drysdale [1935] 2 KB 174, finding there was no insurable interest in a vehicle which has been sold. The Court of Appeal held that a DOV extension on a policy did not come to an end on the sale of the insured vehicle.

COLLEY v SHUKER (2019-2020) — INSURER STATUS AND MIB LIABILITY — In Colley v Shuker the Claimant (C) was injured in a vehicle driven by Daniel Shuker (D1). The vehicle had been insured to drive under the policy with UKI but D1 was not insured to drive and C knew that he was uninsured. UKI obtained a section 152 declaration. C brought a claim against UKI on the basis that following Fidelidade the Court should determine (a) that UKI's declaration under section 152 was incompatible with the Directive and (b) use Marleasing principles to prevent UKI avoiding its liability to C under section 151. The claim was dismissed by O'Farrell J (see [2019] EWHC 781 (QB)) on the basis that whilst it is well arguable that section 152 is incompatible with the Directive, any such incompatibility cannot be resolved by any permissible Marleasing interpretation of the state, and since MIB is a private body and not an emanation of the state the court cannot disapply the domestic legislation in relation to a claim against it.

COLLEY v SHUKER (2020) — MIB LIABILITY CONTINUED — In the original action MIB had been D3. When the claim against UKI (D1) failed C then pursued D2 on the basis of the state's (following Lewis v Tindale) failure to implement the Directive, which made MIB liable to compensate as an emanation of the state. MIB argued that it had no liability to compensate as an emanation of the state where the obligation to insure the vehicle was met at the time of the accident, and that Freedman J found that MIB was obligated to compensate in any scenario where the system has broken down whether because the vehicle is uninsured or because of the provisions of national legislation (e.g. a section 152 declaration). MIB also argued that it was not obliged to compensate C because C knew that D1 was uninsured — that argument also failed because Freedman J found that in order to be able to rely on the exclusion permitted by the Directive it was necessary for MIB to show that at the time of the accident the vehicle was insured and it was not necessary for MIB to show that the vehicle was insured at the time of the accident and only became 'uninsured' when UKI later avoided the policy. So again MIB had to pay in circumstances arising from the government's failure properly to implement its EU obligations. MIB has sought permission to appeal but this decision is now retained EU law and will continue to apply post-Brexit. MIB has continuing litigation against the Secretary of State in Colley to get an indemnity or contribution.

THE OVERALL POSITION ON MOTOR INSURANCE AND INDEMNITY — The underlying purpose of the Motor Directives is that no innocent victim of a car accident will go uncompensated. That purpose is part of retained EU law and will continue to form the framework of English insurance law unless and until it is changed by statute or (more likely) the CA or Supreme Court. The rule of thumb is that if an insurer has taken a premium (or was entitled to take one even if one was not paid) then it will have to pay any judgment obtained against the driver of the insured vehicle as contractual, section 151 or Article 75 insurer. Where there is more than one potential insurer then the liability to pay is likely to depend upon the relative positions of each insurer with the insurer being in the best position which can downgrade its status to Article 75 to avoid section 151 liability. Where all else fails because of incompatibility between the Directive obligations and English law, MIB is likely to have to meet the claim as an emanation of the state.

articles.summary: Comprehensive analysis of the MIB Uninsured Drivers Agreement 2015 and Untraced Drivers Agreement 2017, with particular focus on the consequences for credit hire recovery. The critical distinction: in uninsured driver claims, property damage including credit hire is recoverable as part of the court judgment with MIB satisfying; in untraced driver (hit-and-run) claims, property damage is only recoverable if MIB has paid for significant personal injury (death, 2 nights in-patient or 3+ outpatient sessions). Covers the Article 75 insurer hierarchy, procedural requirements, passenger exclusion/knowledge test (Colley v MIB), the Cameron Supreme Court decision on identified-vehicle/unknown-driver claims, the post-Brexit position (no more EU Directive incompatibility claims), the Vnuk/MIB v Lewis position and its limitation by the Motor Vehicles (Compulsory Insurance) Act 2022, the untraced costs regime disadvantage, and practical intake guidance for CHOs. UPDATED with material from '12 King's Bench Walk Motor Insurance and Indemnity Update' presentation by Patrick Vincent KC and Isaac Hogarth, covering: section 152 RTA amendments effective 1 November 2019 and their impact on downgrading insurer status; the four benefits of Article 75 status for insurers; policy cancellation mechanics and traps for claims handlers; Colley v Shuker (insurer status, MIB liability as emanation of state, and the continuing significance post-Brexit); Nagorski v Nikolics (MID entry not conclusive for s.151); and the overall position on motor insurance indemnity.

articles.title: Novus Actus Interveniens in Credit Hire — Break in the Chain of Causation: Repair Delays, Vehicle Condition, Hire Agreement Entry and Claimant Conduct
articles.source: LexisNexis Practice Note — Defences to Tort Claims; Majid Ali v HSF Logistics [2024] EWCA Civ 1479; Chatterton v AXA Corporate Solutions [2016]
key_points: THE GENERAL DOCTRINE — A subsequent extraneous event occurring after the defendant's wrongful act may prevent any further loss being attributed to that wrong, 'breaking the chain of causation'. The test is whether the subsequent event was 'ultroneous, something unwarrantable, a new cause which disturbs the sequence of events' — a new and independent cause — or whether the loss is better regarded as continuing to accrue from the defendant's wrong and the subsequent event together. This is ultimately determined as a matter of common sense. In credit hire, defendants invoke novus actus to limit the hire period or the recoverable charges by arguing that some post-accident event severed causation between the collision and the ongoing hire.

THE ENTRY INTO THE CREDIT HIRE AGREEMENT — A defendant's argument that the claimant's own decision to enter a credit hire agreement (at a rate above the basic hire rate) constitutes a novus actus breaking the chain is rejected as a matter of law. The natural and direct consequence of being deprived of a vehicle through the defendant's tort is the need to hire a replacement. The act of entering the hire agreement is a reasonable response to that deprivation; it is not 'unwarrantable' or 'ultroneous'. The correct analysis is that the credit hire charges are the measure of the loss caused by the tort, subject to the claimant's duty to mitigate. Overpricing or an unreasonably long agreement goes to mitigation, not causation: the chain is not broken.

REPAIR DELAYS — WHERE NOVUS ACTUS IS MOST COMMONLY ARGUED — In credit hire, the most frequently argued novus actus scenario is an allegedly unreasonable delay in the repair or total loss settlement of the claimant's vehicle, which prolongs the hire period. The test is whether the delay was so inordinate and unforeseeable as to constitute a new independent cause severing the causal link. In practice, some delay in repair is always foreseeable (parts availability, insurer approval, paint, courtesy complications); a mere delay does not break the chain. In Chatterton v AXA Corporate Solutions [2016], the court noted that if a defendant can demonstrate that a repair delay arose from a cause wholly independent of the accident and unforeseeable, it may amount to a novus actus breaking the causal chain beyond that point. In Scotland (see Article 65), the Sheriff court has held that the onus is on the defender; delays are foreseeable; only inordinate and unforeseeable delay amounts to novus actus.

REPAIR DELAYS — PRACTICAL APPLICATION — Where an insurer argues that repair delays break the chain (and thus limit the recoverable hire period), the claimant must show: (a) the repair was being pursued diligently; (b) any delay was attributable to the repairers, parts supply, or the insurer/engineer themselves (not the claimant's own inaction); and (c) the delay was within the range of foreseeable delays for that type of repair. A claimant who abandons a repair instruction or fails to authorise a repair for an unexplained period may face the argument that their own inaction broke the chain — but this is more naturally analysed as failure to mitigate (proportionate reduction) than as novus actus (complete cut-off beyond a date).

VEHICLE CONDITION AND MOT — THE REJECTED 'CAUSATION DEFENCE' — Defendants have argued that where the claimant's vehicle lacked a valid MOT certificate or was otherwise unroadworthy, the defendant's tort did not cause the claimant to lose the use of a roadworthy vehicle — and therefore caused no actionable loss for which credit hire charges are recoverable. This is framed as a causation argument: the claimant was already unable to drive their vehicle lawfully, so the tort did not deprive them of anything they could use. In Majid Ali v HSF Logistics Polska [2024] EWCA Civ 1479, the Court of Appeal rejected this argument comprehensively. The 'causation defence' was an ex turpi causa argument dressed in causation clothing, and that defence had already been rightly rejected as disproportionate (see Article 34 and Article 39). Stuart-Smith LJ: 'When stripped to its bare essentials, the argument underlying the causation defence is not that the claimant has suffered no loss of use, but that damages ought not to be recovered for loss of use where the use of the original vehicle would have had adverse legal consequences for the claimant as a matter of criminal law. This is the stuff of ex turpi causa, not causation.' The claimant had suffered a real loss — the inconvenience of not having a private car — and the hire charges were the appropriate measure of that loss.

THIRD PARTY INTERVENTION — COPLEY LETTERS AND INSURER OFFERS — Where a defendant insurer writes to the claimant offering a replacement vehicle at a lower rate (a 'Copley letter' — see Article 15), and the claimant unreasonably ignores the offer and continues to hire at a higher credit hire rate, the defendant may argue that the claimant's refusal constitutes a failure to mitigate or — in more extreme formulations — a novus actus. The law's response: an unreasonable refusal of a Copley offer goes to mitigation (the hire charges recoverable are capped at what a reasonable claimant would have paid following the offer), but it does not break the chain of causation entirely. The claimant retains their entitlement to reasonable hire charges. Where the Copley offer is itself unreasonable (inadequate vehicle, unreasonable terms), the claimant is not obliged to accept it and no reduction flows from the refusal.

CLAIMANT'S OWN CONDUCT AS NOVUS ACTUS — The claimant's own act post-accident can only amount to novus actus if it was so unreasonable as to constitute a new and independent cause — setting a very high bar. Ordinary failures to mitigate (failure to check online rates, failure to return a hire vehicle when repair was complete, failure to pursue repairs promptly) are treated as mitigation issues attracting a proportionate reduction in damages rather than as acts breaking the chain entirely. For a claimant's own conduct to constitute novus actus, it must be something wholly outside the range of reasonable responses to the situation created by the defendant's tort — essentially irrational or perverse conduct. Courts are reluctant to treat a claimant's reasonable (if not optimal) responses to their situation as chain-breaking events.

THE DISTINCTION BETWEEN NOVUS ACTUS AND MITIGATION — In credit hire, the practical effect of a novus actus finding is binary: if the chain is broken at date X, no hire charges are recoverable beyond that date regardless of the reason the claimant continued to hire. By contrast, a mitigation finding adjusts the quantum — the hire charges are reduced to what a reasonable claimant would have incurred. Defendants often argue novus actus when the correct analysis is mitigation, because the former has a more severe effect. Practitioners should identify which analysis is truly in play: if the defendant's real complaint is that the hire period was excessive, the answer lies in mitigation, not causation. Only where a truly independent, unforeseeable event supervenes does novus actus properly apply.

EX TURPI CAUSA AS A FLEXIBLE PARTIAL BAR — Where the ex turpi causa defence is engaged (claimant's own illegal conduct, e.g., no MOT or unroadworthy vehicle), the doctrine may bar the action entirely ('non oritur actio'), bar a specific head of claim ('non oritur damnum'), or bar part only of a head of claim. Stuart-Smith LJ in Ali v HSF, adopting Hewison v Meridian Shipping, confirmed that ex turpi causa is a flexible doctrine: where the illegality ceases to be operative or effective at a certain point (e.g., once the vehicle would have been repaired and made roadworthy), the defence may affect only part of the period for which hire charges are claimed. This avoids the disproportionate all-or-nothing outcome. The correct approach is not to dress an ex turpi argument as causation, but to apply ex turpi itself to the part of the period genuinely affected by the illegality.

Chatterton v AXA Corporate Solutions [2016]

Article 62: Credit Hire — Duration: Reasonable Hire Period and Mitigation of Loss

articles.summary: Standalone treatment of the novus actus interveniens doctrine as applied in credit hire claims. Covers: (1) the general legal test (ultroneous/unwarrantable new independent cause); (2) entry into the hire agreement — not novus actus; (3) repair delays — the foreseeability test and the burden on defendants; (4) the rejected vehicle condition/MOT causation defence (Ali v HSF); (5) Copley letter refusals — mitigation not novus actus; (6) the crucial distinction between novus actus (binary cut-off) and mitigation (proportionate reduction); and (7) ex turpi causa as a flexible partial bar (Stuart-Smith LJ in Ali v HSF).

articles.title: Res Inter Alios Acta and UCTA 1977 / Consumer Rights Act 2015 in Credit Hire — Collateral Benefits, Hire Agreement Terms and the Reasonableness Test
articles.source: LexisNexis Practice Note — Defences to Tort Claims; Bradburn v Great Western Railway Co (1874); Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977; Consumer Rights Act 2015
key_points: THE RES INTER ALIOS ACTA DOCTRINE — GENERAL STATEMENT — The Latin maxim 'res inter alios acta alteri nocere non debet' — a thing done between others ought not to harm someone else — is a foundational principle of damages law. Its effect is that collateral benefits received by the claimant from third-party sources (independent of the defendant's liability) are not brought into account to reduce the claimant's damages. The tortfeasor/defendant takes the claimant as they find them and cannot take credit for advantageous arrangements the claimant has made with others. The doctrine applies in both contract and tort. Conversely, it also means that a contract between two parties (claimant and CHO) cannot impose obligations on or be invoked against a third party (the defendant).

THE LEADING AUTHORITY — BRADBURN v GREAT WESTERN RAILWAY (1874) — In Bradburn v Great Western Railway Co (1874-75) LR 10 Ex 1, the claimant received monies under an accident insurance policy following an injury caused by the defendant railway company's negligence. The court held that the insurance monies were not to be deducted from the damages recoverable from the defendant. The claimant had paid for the policy and the fruits of that insurance were res inter alios acta — a thing done between the claimant and their insurer, which the defendant could not exploit as a windfall. Bradburn established the principle that independently acquired collateral benefits are disregarded when assessing damages against a tortfeasor. This remains the foundational authority: confirmed in Parry v Cleaver [1970] AC 1 (HL) and applied consistently across credit hire.

RES INTER ALIOS ACTA AND OWN INSURANCE COURTESY CARS — The most frequently litigated credit hire application. A defendant argues: 'The claimant had comprehensive motor insurance entitling them to a courtesy vehicle. They should have used it. Having failed to do so, their credit hire charges are irrecoverable as a failure to mitigate.' The claimant's answer: the claimant's own insurance policy is a contract between the claimant and their insurer to which the defendant is a stranger. The defendant cannot invoke the terms of that policy to reduce their liability. The courtesy car entitlement is the fruit of the claimant's own insurance premiums — res inter alios acta. The defendant does not become entitled to take credit for a benefit the claimant procured independently and at their own cost. This principle is established in English credit hire law and has been repeatedly applied (see Article 9 for comprehensive treatment; Article 107 for the claims handler response).

RES INTER ALIOS ACTA AND IVAs — In Individual Voluntary Arrangements (IVAs), the defendant insurer has argued that where a claimant's IVA has reduced or extinguished the credit hire debt owed to the CHO, the defendant benefits: if the claimant owes nothing to the CHO, they have suffered no loss and the credit hire claim fails. The answer is res inter alios acta: the IVA is a collateral arrangement between the claimant and their creditors (including the CHO). The defendant is a stranger to that arrangement and cannot exploit it as a windfall. Just as Bradburn prevented the railway company from taking credit for the claimant's accident insurance, the defendant here cannot take credit for the IVA reducing the claimant's debt. The IVA does not extinguish the cause of action against the defendant (see Article 119 for full treatment).

THE DIMOND v LOVELL DISTINCTION — WHEN RES INTER ALIOS ACTA DOES NOT APPLY — Res inter alios acta protects the claimant where the hire agreement is valid and enforceable. In Dimond v Lovell [2000] UKHL 27, the House of Lords held that where the credit hire agreement was unenforceable (because it failed to comply with the Consumer Credit Act 1974), the claimant could not recover the hire charges from the defendant. The res inter alios acta defence failed here because the agreement was not a valid contractual benefit in the claimant's hands — having no enforceable claim against the CHO (since the agreement was void), there was no loss to compensate. This is the critical limit: the doctrine protects a claimant who has a valid hire agreement and has incurred a real liability; it does not create a claim out of an unenforceable or sham arrangement. Contrast with enforceable IVA cases where the debt remains real.

UCTA 1977 — SCOPE AND APPLICATION TO CREDIT HIRE — The Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977 restricts the ability of a party to exclude or limit liability for negligence by express contractual term, notice, or disclaimer. UCTA 1977, s.1(3) applies to things done in the course of business or from occupation of business premises. Section 2(1) absolutely prohibits the exclusion of liability for death or personal injury caused by negligence (cannot be excluded regardless of reasonableness). Section 2(2) permits restriction of liability for other negligence-caused loss only in so far as it satisfies the requirement of reasonableness (UCTA s.11). IMPORTANT — from 1 October 2015, UCTA 1977 NO LONGER APPLIES to consumer contracts; consumer hire agreements made on or after that date are governed by the Consumer Rights Act 2015 (CRA 2015), ss.61–76. UCTA 1977 now applies only to business-to-business contracts.

UCTA 1977 IN CREDIT HIRE — HIRE AGREEMENT TERMS — In credit hire, UCTA arguments arise in two directions. First, a defendant or their insurer cannot use a notice or disclaimer to restrict their tortious liability to the claimant for property damage and loss of use — any such attempt (e.g., a letter asserting they will not pay credit hire charges whatever the agreement says) does not bind the claimant. Second, the CHO's own terms in the hire agreement may be subject to UCTA/CRA challenge if the agreement is a business-to-business contract (UCTA) or a consumer contract (CRA 2015). Terms that may be challenged include: (a) rate escalation clauses; (b) open-ended duration terms with no mechanism for the claimant to terminate; (c) cooperation/assignment obligations requiring the claimant to prosecute claims actively; (d) clauses restricting the claimant's ability to settle the underlying tort claim without the CHO's consent. Note that s.2(3) UCTA provides that a term or notice purporting to restrict or exclude liability for negligence is not in itself conclusive evidence of volenti non fit injuria.

UCTA — THE REASONABLENESS TEST: SMITH v ERIC S BUSH FACTORS — Under UCTA 1977, s.11, a term is reasonable if it was a fair and reasonable one to include having regard to the circumstances known (or reasonably knowable) to the parties at the time of contracting. Lord Griffiths in Smith v Eric S Bush [1990] 1 AC 831 identified relevant factors: (1) the relative bargaining power of the parties — where there is unequal bargaining power or no realistic ability to negotiate, terms are more likely to be unreasonable; (2) the difficulty of the task for which liability is excluded — if the service is specialist and hard to source elsewhere, exclusion is more likely unreasonable; (3) whether the same service was available without the exclusion from another source; (4) the ability of the parties to bear the loss in light of available insurance. In consumer credit hire: the claimant is typically a consumer in a vulnerable position following an accident with no real ability to shop around or negotiate — this weighs against upholding harsh exclusion or cooperation terms.

CONSUMER RIGHTS ACT 2015 — CONSUMER HIRE AGREEMENTS FROM OCTOBER 2015 — For credit hire agreements made with consumers on or after 1 October 2015, the Consumer Rights Act 2015 (CRA 2015), ss.61–76 replaces UCTA 1977. Under the CRA 2015: (a) a term is unfair if, contrary to the requirement of good faith, it causes a significant imbalance in the parties' rights and obligations to the detriment of the consumer (s.62); (b) unfair terms are not binding on the consumer (s.62(1)); (c) the fairness assessment does not apply to the main subject matter of the contract or price terms if they are transparent and prominent (s.64); (d) terms that restrict or modify the consumer's remedies, impose disproportionate sanctions, or permit the supplier to determine the characteristics of the service after conclusion are particularly susceptible to challenge (Schedule 2, indicative list). In credit hire, a term requiring the claimant to cooperate in recovery proceedings on pain of liability for the hire charges may be challengeable as unfair if the burden imposed on the consumer is disproportionate.

UCTA/CRA AND THE HIRE AGREEMENT'S ASSIGNMENT CLAUSE — Most credit hire agreements contain an assignment of (or a charge over) the claimant's right of action against the defendant, or an obligation to assign. These are the mechanism by which the CHO recovers its charges from the defendant (either in the claimant's name or via an assignment). UCTA/CRA do not generally invalidate these clauses because they do not restrict or exclude the claimant's rights — they confer rights on the CHO. However, where an assignment clause purports to take away the claimant's ability to settle the underlying claim without consent, it may be challenged: (a) under CRA 2015 as a potentially unfair term restricting the consumer's freedom to contract; or (b) under the common law rule against champerty if the CHO is acting in a way that amounts to trafficking in litigation. The CCA 1974 regime (Article 139) separately governs the formality requirements for any such assignment.

VOLENTI AND UCTA — THE CRITICAL NOTE — A contractual term or notice that purports to exclude or restrict liability for negligence is not by itself evidence of volenti non fit injuria (UCTA 1977, s.2(3)). This is directly relevant in credit hire where a hire agreement or related document might purport to acknowledge that the hire rate represents the claimant's assessed loss. Such an acknowledgment does not prevent the claimant from arguing for a different (lower) basic hire rate if the credit hire rate is challenged — the defendant cannot rely on the contractual acknowledgment as evidence that the claimant voluntarily agreed the rate was the true measure of loss. The UCTA s.2(3) provision therefore supports the claimant's right to have hire rate assessed independently of the agreement terms.
