articles.summary: Standalone treatment of two related tort defences as they apply specifically in credit hire: (1) Res inter alios acta — the doctrine (Bradburn, Parry v Cleaver) preventing defendants from taking credit for collateral benefits the claimant has independently acquired, covering courtesy car insurance, IVAs, and the Dimond v Lovell limit; (2) UCTA 1977 and Consumer Rights Act 2015 — scope, the reasonableness test (Smith v Eric S Bush factors), application to hire agreement terms including cooperation/assignment clauses, the consumer/business split from October 2015, and the volenti/UCTA s.2(3) interaction. Cross-references existing detailed articles on specific applications (Articles 9, 107, 119, 139).

articles.title: QOCS in Mixed Personal Injury and Credit Hire Claims — Risk Management and Costs Protection: A Claimant Practitioner's Guide
articles.source: LexisNexis Practice Note — Credit Hire Common Liability Issues; CPR 44.16; Brown v Commissioner of Police [2019] EWCA Civ 1724; Tescher v DAML / AXA v Spectra [2025] EWCA Civ 733
key_points: THE QOCS FRAMEWORK IN A CREDIT HIRE CONTEXT — QOCS (Qualified One-Way Costs Shifting) under CPR 44.14 protects personal injury claimants from adverse costs orders in most circumstances. A claimant with a CFA and/or ATE policy entered on or after 1 April 2013 should in principle benefit from QOCS. The starting position — costs orders against the claimant may not be enforced beyond the aggregate of the claimant's damages and interest — provides significant protection. However, where a credit hire claim is brought alongside the personal injury claim, that protection is at risk through three distinct mechanisms under CPR 44.16. Claimant practitioners must identify this risk at the outset, not after the claim has failed.

MECHANISM 1 — CPR 44.16(2)(a): CLAIM FOR THE FINANCIAL BENEFIT OF ANOTHER — CPR 44.16(2)(a) provides that costs orders against the claimant may be enforced to their full extent where proceedings include a claim made for the financial benefit of a person other than the claimant. CPR Practice Direction 44, paragraph 12.2 specifically identifies credit hire as an example of this category. The underlying logic is that a credit hire claim is, in substance, a recovery vehicle for the CHO's charges: the claimant claims the hire costs on behalf of the CHO who provided the vehicle on credit terms. CPR 44.16(3) goes further: where CPR 44.16(2)(a) applies, the court may make a costs order against the non-party (the CHO) for whose financial benefit the whole or part of the claim was made. This is the statutory foundation for Non-Party Costs Orders (NPCOs) against credit hire companies — see Articles 29 and 35.

MECHANISM 2 — CPR 44.16(2)(b): THE MIXED CLAIM EXCEPTION — Credit hire claims fall within the mixed claim exception at CPR 44.16(2)(b) because they arise from property damage, not personal injury. QOCS does not automatically apply to the credit hire element. Where the credit hire claim fails and the defendant obtains a costs order, that order may be enforced against the claimant 'to the extent the court considers just'. The practical risk: even where the PI element succeeds, the credit hire failure generates a costs order that the claimant may have to meet personally. Claimant practitioners must understand that QOCS is not a blanket shield across a mixed claim.

THE BROWN v COMMISSIONER OF POLICE STARTING POINT — The Court of Appeal in Brown v The Commissioner of Police [2019] EWCA Civ 1724 (Coulson LJ at para [57]) confirmed that in a mixed PI/credit hire claim, the starting point — and in most cases the finishing point — is that the QOCS regime continues to apply: 'the fact that there is a claim for damages in respect of personal injury, and a claim for damage to property, does not mean that the QOCS regime suddenly becomes irrelevant.' Where the proceedings can fairly be described in the round as a personal injury case, a court should endeavour to achieve a 'cost-neutral' result unless there are exceptional features of the non-PI claim. 'Exceptional features' means something like gross exaggeration of the credit hire claim, not merely that the credit hire claim failed. This is the claimant's principal protection in mixed claim litigation.

WHEN BROWN PROTECTION IS LOST — EXCEPTIONAL FEATURES OF THE CREDIT HIRE CLAIM — The Brown protection is not unlimited. Coulson LJ was explicit that it would be wrong for claimants to make personal injury claims merely to hide behind QOCS protection for inflated credit hire claims. Where a court finds gross exaggeration, dishonesty or abuse in the credit hire element, or where the credit hire is so large relative to the PI damages as to make the proceedings really a credit hire dispute with a PI element attached, the court will be more willing to enforce costs against the claimant through CPR 44.16(2)(b). Importantly, this is a matter of judicial discretion on the facts: there is no bright line. Claimant practitioners should ensure the credit hire claim is properly calibrated and proportionate — exaggerated or inflated hire periods are the biggest trigger for loss of Brown protection.

THE ENFORCEABILITY LINK — WHEN THE AGREEMENT IS UNENFORCEABLE — Where the credit hire agreement itself is found to be unenforceable (for failure to comply with the CCA 1974, the CCR 2013, or for misrepresentation — see Article 139), the QOCS/costs position becomes significantly worse. An unenforceable agreement means the claimant has suffered no loss and the credit hire claim fails entirely. This in turn: (a) removes the claim from the category of a 'mixed PI/credit hire' case (the credit hire claim never properly existed), weakening the Brown v Commissioner of Police protection; (b) makes an NPCO against the CHO under CPR 44.16(2)(a)/(3) more attractive to the defendant because the claim was purely for the CHO's benefit from the start; and (c) may expose the claimant's PI costs recovery to a set-off if costs are awarded against them. Claimant practitioners must check enforceability (CCA exemption status, signed agreement, obligation to pay under Irving v Morgan Sindall) before issuing proceedings.

THE NPCO CASCADE — HOW CREDIT HIRE FAILURE MIGRATES COSTS FROM CLAIMANT TO CHO — Where a credit hire claim fails and costs are ordered against the claimant but the claimant benefits from QOCS, the costs remain unrecoverable from the claimant — which is precisely what drives NPCO applications against the CHO. In Kozaihi, a CHO was made the subject of an NPCO after the claimant lost QOCS protection (through strike-out). In Kindertons Ltd v Murtagh [2024] EWHC 471, the CHO was liable as a non-party where the claim was found not to have been caused by the accident. In Tescher v DAML / AXA v Spectra [2025] EWCA Civ 733, the Court of Appeal established a near-presumption that where a credit hire claim fails, an NPCO against the CHO follows. The practical implication for claimant practitioners: where QOCS protects the claimant, the CHO bears the costs risk — but this depends on the CHO remaining financially capable of meeting the order and not having indemnified itself through the claimant's cooperation obligations.

THE CLAIMANT'S OWN POSITION — PRACTICAL PROTECTION STEPS — Claimant practitioners instructed to run a combined PI and credit hire claim should take the following steps at the outset: (1) Verify agreement enforceability: check the number of payments, hire period length (Clark v Tull exemption), whether a signed copy exists, and whether the claimant understands and accepts an obligation to pay — an unenforceable agreement exposes the claimant to all the costs risks without any credit hire damages to offset them. (2) Calibrate the hire period: an excessive hire period relative to a modest PI claim dramatically increases the risk that the court will treat the proceedings as a credit hire dispute and apply CPR 44.16(2)(b) costs enforcement. (3) Consider GTA: if both parties' insurers are GTA members, using the GTA avoids litigation and the associated QOCS complexity. (4) ATE insurance: consider whether separate ATE cover for the credit hire element is available and proportionate; the standard PI ATE policy may not cover credit hire failure. (5) Advise the client in writing: if the credit hire claim carries a material risk of costs exposure, advise the claimant in writing so they can make an informed decision about whether to pursue it.

THE OBLIGATION TO PAY — A PRE-ISSUE CHECK THAT IS OFTEN OVERLOOKED — As confirmed in Irving v Morgan Sindall [2010], the claimant must demonstrate not only that the agreement is formally enforceable but also that they personally understood themselves to be obliged to pay the hire charges. Where the claimant's witness statement or pre-issue evidence inadvertently suggests they believed the credit hire was free or that the CHO would recover from the insurer without recourse to them, the claim fails at this threshold — regardless of whether the agreement is formally valid. This is a particular risk in claims handled by CHOs that market themselves as 'no out of pocket expense' services: the claimant may genuinely not appreciate that a legal obligation to pay exists. Claimant practitioners should take a specific proof-of-obligation instruction from the client before issue.

POST-APRIL 2023 QOCS POSITION — Since 6 April 2023, the QOCS enforcement cap has been extended: costs orders against a claimant may be enforced without permission up to the aggregate of all orders for damages, costs AND interest (including agreed settlements) in the claimant's favour. The pre-2023 cap was limited to damages and interest only. The post-2023 position is more favourable to claimants with successful PI damages: a credit hire failure generates a costs order that the defendant can set off against the PI award, but the broader cap makes it less likely that full adverse costs exceed the claimant's net recovery. However, in cases where PI damages are modest and credit hire charges are large, even the extended cap may not be sufficient protection — and the risk of a net loss to the claimant remains real.

articles.summary: Claimant-practitioner-focused analysis of the QOCS risks arising from a combined personal injury and credit hire claim. Covers the three CPR 44.16 mechanisms by which QOCS protection is displaced (CPR 44.16(2)(a) claim for CHO's benefit; CPR 44.16(2)(b) mixed claim; CPR 44.16(1) fundamental dishonesty); the Brown v Commissioner of Police starting-point protection and when it fails; the enforceability link (unenforceable agreement worsens QOCS position); the NPCO cascade from claimant to CHO (Kozaihi, Kindertons, Tescher); and nine practical pre-issue steps including the Irving v Morgan Sindall obligation-to-pay check. Complements Article 37 (CHO/insurer perspective) with equivalent guidance for claimant solicitors.
topics: mixed claim PI and credit hire

articles.title: Dimond v Lovell [2000] UKHL 27 — CCA 1974, Improperly Executed Agreements and the No-Loss Principle
articles.source: House of Lords, 11 May 2000 — [2000] UKHL 27; [2002] 1 AC 384
key_points: BACKGROUND AND FACTS — Mrs Dimond's Suzuki Vitara was damaged in a road traffic accident on 30 December 1996 through the fault of Mr Lovell. She hired a replacement Ford Mondeo from 1st Automotive Ltd at £30 per day, plus a £5 collision damage waiver and £15 delivery charge, totalling £346.63 for an 8-day hire period. The hire agreement was executed on the standard 1st Automotive form. The central issue was whether Mrs Dimond could recover these hire charges from Mr Lovell as part of her damages claim.

THE CCA 1974 ENFORCEABILITY ISSUE — The hire agreement provided financial accommodation within the meaning of the Consumer Credit Act 1974. Under s.8(2) and s.9(1), it constituted a regulated consumer credit agreement because it allowed Mrs Dimond to obtain the car now and pay later, deferring a financial obligation. Under s.61(1)(a) of the CCA 1974 read with the Consumer Credit (Agreements) Regulations 1983 (Schedule 6), a properly executed regulated agreement must contain the prescribed term stating the amount of credit. The 1st Automotive agreement failed to include this prescribed term. The consequence under s.65(1) is that the agreement is enforceable against the debtor only on a court order. However, s.127(3) provides that where s.61(1)(a) has not been complied with, the court 'shall not' make an enforcement order. The agreement was therefore irredeemably unenforceable against Mrs Dimond as debtor.

THE NO-LOSS CONCLUSION — The irredeemable unenforceability of the agreement was fatal to Mrs Dimond's damages claim. Because the agreement could not be enforced against her, she was under no legal obligation to pay the hire charges. Since she bore no liability to pay, she had suffered no loss. The basic tort principle that damages compensate for actual loss meant that nothing was recoverable. Lord Hoffmann (with whom the majority agreed) held that Mrs Dimond could not recover the hire charges from Mr Lovell. The House of Lords allowed Mr Lovell's appeal and set aside the award of £346.63.

REJECTION OF RES INTER ALIOS ACTA AND UNJUST ENRICHMENT ARGUMENTS — Mrs Dimond attempted two alternative routes to recovery. First, she argued that the unenforceability of the agreement was res inter alios acta — a matter between herself and 1st Automotive that should not affect her claim against the tortfeasor. The House of Lords rejected this: it was not a collateral benefit case. Hunt v Severs [1994] 2 AC 350 was applied — a claimant cannot claim as trustee for a third party where there is no enforceable obligation to account. Mrs Dimond had no enforceable obligation to pay 1st Automotive; therefore she could not sue Mr Lovell as trustee for 1st Automotive's benefit. Second, she argued that allowing Mr Lovell to escape liability would confer an unjust enrichment on him. This too was rejected: Parliament's purpose in enacting s.61(1) and s.127(3) was precisely to confer a benefit on the debtor. It would be contrary to that statutory purpose to allow the debtor to recover damages representing charges she cannot lawfully be compelled to pay.

THE SPOT RATE PRINCIPLE (OBITER — MAJORITY) — The most significant obiter element of Dimond v Lovell concerns the measure of damages in credit hire cases generally, where enforceability is not in issue. Lord Hoffmann, with the support of the majority, applied the British Westinghouse principle: where a claimant takes steps in mitigation of loss and those steps produce additional benefits beyond mere replacement, the value of those additional benefits must be deducted from the award. A credit hire agreement typically provides services beyond a spot market hire — deferred payment, credit facility, claims management services. The claimant who hires on credit terms obtains something more than an equivalent spot rate rental. Accordingly, the correct measure of damages for credit hire is the equivalent basic hire rate (the spot rate) that would have been charged for an equivalent vehicle, with the additional CHO services deducted. This principle has become the practical foundation of the BHR/spot rate defence in credit hire litigation. Lord Nicholls dissented on this point, arguing that credit hire charges as a whole should be recoverable. Lord Saville expressly declined to express a view.

PRACTICAL IMPORTANCE FOR CREDIT HIRE PRACTITIONERS — Dimond v Lovell has two distinct practical legacies. First, any credit hire agreement that is a regulated consumer credit agreement under the CCA 1974 must be properly executed in accordance with the prescribed terms under s.61(1) and the 1983 Regulations. Failure to include the credit amount as a prescribed term renders the agreement irredeemably unenforceable and the hirer recovers nothing. Practitioners acting for claimants must scrutinise CHO agreements for CCA compliance before issuing proceedings. Second, the obiter spot rate principle — now well-established — means that even in enforceable cases, the defendant can challenge the credit hire rate and seek a reduction to the basic hire rate for a comparable spot market vehicle. The CHO's additional services (credit facility, claims management, free replacement vehicle without upfront payment) are not recoverable as damages. Articles 3, 7, 14 and 19 of this knowledge base develop the spot rate principles in detail.

British Westinghouse Electric and Manufacturing Co Ltd v Underground Electric Railways Co of London Ltd [1912] AC 673
Burdis v Livsey [2002] EWCA Civ 510
Lagden v O'Connor [2003] UKHL 64

Article 7 — CCA 1974 Compliance in Credit Hire Agreements
Article 14 — Mitigation and the Deduction of Additional Benefits

articles.summary: The House of Lords held in Dimond v Lovell that a credit hire claimant whose agreement is irredeemably unenforceable under the Consumer Credit Act 1974 (due to failure to include the prescribed credit amount term under s.61(1)(a)) has suffered no loss and recovers nothing. The majority also held (obiter) that where a CHR agreement is enforceable, only the equivalent spot rate for a comparable vehicle is recoverable — the value of additional CHO services must be deducted under the British Westinghouse principle. This dual holding defines both the CCA compliance obligation and the spot rate measure of damages in credit hire litigation.

articles.title: Lagden v O'Connor [2003] UKHL 64 — Impecuniosity, the Full Credit Hire Rate and the Overruling of Liesbosch
articles.source: House of Lords, 4 December 2003 — [2003] UKHL 64; [2004] 1 AC 1067
key_points: BACKGROUND AND FACTS — Mr Lagden's Ford Granada (a 10-year-old vehicle) was damaged in a road traffic accident on 29 November 1999 while parked and unoccupied, through the fault of Ms O'Connor. Mr Lagden was unemployed, in poor health, and had very little money. He signed a Helphire UK Ltd agreement on 6 December 1999 for a replacement Ford Mondeo with a 26-week credit facility. The total Helphire charges were £659.76. The judge at Oxford County Court (Judge Harris) held that the full Helphire cost was recoverable because Mr Lagden was impecunious and had no choice but to use a credit hire arrangement. The Court of Appeal agreed. Spot market rates would have been approximately 70% of the Helphire charges. The House of Lords dismissed the defendant's appeal.

THE IMPECUNIOSITY TEST — Lord Nicholls (§9) formulated the definitive test for impecuniosity in the credit hire context: impecuniosity means 'inability to pay car hire charges without making sacrifices the plaintiff could not reasonably be expected to make.' This is a practical, fact-sensitive standard. It is not limited to those who are destitute or in receipt of benefits. A claimant is impecunious for these purposes if paying spot market rates would require them to divert funds in a way that would cause real hardship or unreasonable disruption to their financial position. Lord Hope (§36–37) suggested a practical dividing line: those who cannot produce a credit or debit card to pay upfront for a hire vehicle at the spot rate are likely to be impecunious. Evidence of income, assets, existing financial commitments and any real burden that spot market hire would impose is relevant.

THE NO-CHOICE PRINCIPLE AND BRITISH WESTINGHOUSE — Lord Hope (§34–35) addressed the relationship between the no-choice principle and the British Westinghouse deduction. The general rule in Dimond v Lovell is that additional benefits obtained through credit hire beyond mere vehicle replacement must be deducted, leaving only the spot rate recoverable. However, this deduction operates only where the claimant had a genuine choice: if the claimant could have hired at the spot rate, then choosing the more expensive credit hire arrangement generates a benefit that must be netted off. Where the claimant had no choice — where the only practical option was credit hire because they lacked the means to pay spot market rates upfront — no deduction is made. In such cases the entire credit hire rate is recoverable because the additional cost is attributable to the tortfeasor's wrong, not to any voluntary choice by the claimant. Lord Slynn (§11–13) agreed: where no choice exists, there can be no windfall to deduct.

LIESBOSCH OVERRULED — The Liesbosch [1933] AC 449 had established that a claimant's impecuniosity (lack of funds to mitigate) could not increase the defendant's liability — poverty was a personal circumstance that broke the chain of causation and was too remote a consequence of the defendant's wrong. Lord Nicholls held (§9) that these observations by Lord Wright in The Liesbosch were no longer authoritative. They had been overtaken by subsequent legal developments, including Dodd Properties (Kent) Ltd v Canterbury City Council [1980] 1 WLR 433 and Alcoa Minerals of Jamaica Inc v Broderick [2002] 1 AC 371. The general principle in tort that the defendant must take the victim as found — including their financial circumstances — applies. A claimant who is impecunious, and who reasonably has no choice but to use a credit hire arrangement, is entitled to recover the full credit hire charges. The tortfeasor cannot avoid liability on the ground that a wealthier victim could have mitigated more cheaply.

REJECTION OF POLICY OBJECTIONS — The defendant raised various policy objections: that allowing full CHR recovery would encourage expensive arrangements; that CHOs were profiting at insurers' expense; that it created inconsistency between impecunious and solvent claimants. Lord Nicholls rejected these arguments (§38–43). CHOs provide a real and valuable service. The asymmetry between solvent and impecunious claimants is a consequence of applying normal tort principles fairly. A solvent claimant who voluntarily chose credit hire when spot hire was available would not recover more than spot rate — but an impecunious claimant who genuinely had no choice is entitled to full recovery. This is not special treatment for the poor; it is the general principle that the tortfeasor takes the victim as they find them.

PRACTICAL IMPORTANCE FOR CREDIT HIRE PRACTITIONERS — Lagden v O'Connor remains the foundation of the impecuniosity argument in credit hire litigation. To recover the full credit hire rate rather than being limited to the spot rate under Dimond v Lovell, the claimant must prove impecuniosity using the Lord Nicholls/Lord Hope test. The evidential burden falls on the claimant to establish inability to pay without unreasonable sacrifice. Common evidence includes: bank statements demonstrating limited funds, income and expenditure schedules, evidence of no credit facility, evidence of inability to produce a credit or debit card, inability to access a conventional hire without payment upfront. Defendants will frequently challenge impecuniosity and call for disclosure of financial documents. The argument that the claimant 'could have used a credit card' or 'could have taken out a loan' is a common line of attack. Articles 1, 11 and 20 of this knowledge base address impecuniosity evidence and procedure in detail.

articles.summary: The House of Lords in Lagden v O'Connor held that an impecunious claimant who has no choice but to use a credit hire arrangement is entitled to recover the full credit hire rate, without the British Westinghouse deduction to spot rate that applies to solvent claimants. Impecuniosity is defined as inability to pay car hire charges without making sacrifices the claimant could not reasonably be expected to make. The Liesbosch observations on impecuniosity as a cause-breaking factor were overruled. The decision establishes the foundational exception to the Dimond v Lovell spot rate limitation.

articles.title: Giles v Thompson [1994] 1 AC 142 — Champerty, Need and the Gap in Remedies for Private Motorists
articles.source: House of Lords, 26 May 1993 — [1994] 1 AC 142
key_points: BACKGROUND AND CONJOINED APPEALS — Giles v Thompson was heard alongside Devlin v Baslington as two conjoined appeals raising common issues about the legality of credit hire schemes and the recoverability of credit hire charges. In Giles v Thompson, the motorist hired a replacement vehicle through the Forward Hire scheme. In Devlin v Baslington, the scheme used was operated by 1st AutoMotive Ltd. In both cases the credit hire company had involvement in the litigation — in some form providing vehicles to accident victims on the basis that the hire charges would be claimed as damages from the at-fault insurer. The House of Lords (Lord Mustill giving the leading judgment) dismissed the appeals and held the arrangements were lawful.

THE THREE-STAGE CHAMPERTY TEST (LORD MUSTILL) — Lord Mustill formulated a structured three-stage analysis for champerty challenges. Stage one: does the arrangement bear the marks of champerty — in particular, does the company have a financial interest in the outcome of the litigation (i.e., does it benefit from the claimant succeeding)? Stage two: if so, does the company have a legitimate commercial interest in the litigation that renders the arrangement lawful despite the financial stake? Stage three: are there any other public policy objections that would nonetheless make the arrangement unlawful? The test for champerty is not the mere existence of a financial interest but whether the arrangement crosses the line into 'wanton and officious intermeddling' — the test from British Cash and Parcel Conveyors v Lamson Store Service Co [1908] 1 KB 1006. A person who has a legitimate commercial interest in a dispute does not become a champertous intermeddler simply by financing or supporting litigation.

APPLICATION TO CREDIT HIRE SCHEMES — In both appeals, the CHO had a direct commercial interest in the hire charges being recovered in litigation — those charges represented its trading income. Lord Mustill held this was a legitimate commercial interest: the company was not a stranger to the dispute but was a direct commercial party whose income depended on the recovery of its charges. The motorist retained a residual personal liability for the hire charges in the event of litigation failure (Devlin) or was the dominus litis (Giles) with ultimate control over the proceedings. The risk of conflicting interests between the motorist and the CHO in a mixed claim (personal injury + property damage + hire) was recognised but characterised as minimal in practice. The arrangements were lawful.

THE GAP IN REMEDIES — One of the most important passages in the judgment is Lord Mustill's identification of what he called 'the gap in remedies available to the motorist.' He observed that where a motorist's vehicle is damaged by a negligent defendant, the motorist faces a practical problem: if they cannot afford to rent a replacement vehicle at spot market rates and pay upfront, and if they cannot get a vehicle from the tortfeasor's insurer directly, they are left without transport while the claim is resolved. This gap between the legal remedy (damages, payable only after judgment or settlement) and the practical need (transport now) was being filled by credit hire companies. Lord Mustill's observation — that 'there exists in practical terms a gap in the remedies available to the motorist, from which the errant driver, and hence his insurers, frequently profit' — has become a foundational passage relied on by courts to justify the CHO model as serving a legitimate need.

NEED: BURDEN OF PROOF AND THE INFERENCE PRINCIPLE — Lord Mustill also addressed the requirement that the claimant establish a genuine need for the replacement vehicle. He confirmed that need is 'not self-proving' — the claimant bears the legal burden of establishing need. However, he recognised that it is not difficult to infer that a private motorist who incurs the considerable expense of running a car does so because they have a need for it — an inference that arises simply from the fact of ownership. This inference is not conclusive: the defendant has 'ample scope' to displace it by showing the claimant did not in fact need the vehicle during the hire period. Examples of circumstances defeating need: the claimant was in hospital during the hire, was on a long holiday abroad, had another vehicle readily available, or had no driving licence. The principle that need creates an inference from vehicle ownership but that inference is rebuttable is now the established framework applied in all credit hire litigation.

LASTING SIGNIFICANCE — Giles v Thompson resolved the validity of the credit hire model at the highest level and the decision remains unchallenged. The champerty analysis in Lord Mustill's judgment is still applied whenever the lawfulness of CHO involvement in litigation is challenged. The 'gap in remedies' passage is regularly cited as the moral and commercial justification for the existence of credit hire companies. The need principle — need not self-proving, inference from vehicle ownership, defendant can displace — is cited in every credit hire trial. Article 1 of this knowledge base (by Azeem Ali, Barrister) is substantially an exposition of the need principles first articulated in Giles v Thompson and their application in modern practice.

Devlin v Baslington (conjoined appeal)
Dimond v Lovell [2000] UKHL 27; [2002] 1 AC 384
Lagden v O'Connor [2003] UKHL 64
cross_references: Article 1 — Credit Hire and Need
Article 2 — Champerty and CHO Agreements
Article 146 — Dimond v Lovell [2000] UKHL 27
Article 147 — Lagden v O'Connor [2003] UKHL 64
articles.summary: The House of Lords in Giles v Thompson upheld the lawfulness of credit hire schemes against champerty challenges, applying a three-stage test that found CHOs to have a legitimate commercial interest in the outcome of hire charge litigation. Lord Mustill identified the 'gap in remedies' that credit hire companies fill for motorists who cannot afford upfront spot market rates. He also confirmed that need is 'not self-proving,' that vehicle ownership creates an inference of need, and that the defendant has ample scope to displace that inference. These principles remain the foundational framework for need and champerty in credit hire litigation.

articles.title: Armstead v Royal & Sun Alliance Insurance Co Ltd [2024] UKSC 6 — Bailee's Right to Sue, Pure Economic Loss and the Reasonable Pre-Estimate of Loss
articles.source: UK Supreme Court, 14 February 2024 — [2024] UKSC 6; on appeal from [2022] EWCA Civ 497
key_points: BACKGROUND AND FACTS — Lorna Armstead hired a Mini Cooper from Helphire Ltd on credit hire terms on 11 November 2015. The hire agreement included Clause 16 which provided: 'You will on demand pay to [Helphire] an amount equal to the daily rental rate...up to a maximum of 30 days in respect of damages for loss of use for each calendar day...when the vehicle is unavailable to Helphire for hire because...the Hire Vehicle has been damaged.' On 23 November 2015, a Ford Transit Connect van driven by an RSA-insured driver collided with and damaged the Mini Cooper. The Mini Cooper was repaired between 8 and 21 January 2016 (12 days). At the daily rate of £130, the Clause 16 sum was £1,560. Ms Armstead brought proceedings against RSA under the European Communities (Rights Against Insurers) Regulations 2002 for this sum. Three courts below dismissed the claim. The Supreme Court unanimously reversed and allowed it (subject to remission on the reasonable pre-estimate question).

BAILEE HAS THE RIGHT TO SUE: POSSESSORY TITLE SUFFICIENT — The Deputy District Judge held that Ms Armstead had no claim because she had no proprietary interest in the Mini Cooper — only Helphire had ownership. The Supreme Court held this was wrong. The principle established in The Winkfield [1902] P 42 is that a bailee in possession has possessory title and can sue a stranger (including the tortfeasor who damaged the property) in negligence for loss or damage to the property. Possessory title is sufficient to found a negligence claim; proprietary/ownership title is not required. Ms Armstead, as the hirer in possession of the Mini Cooper, had possessory title and was therefore entitled to bring a claim in tort against RSA's insured for the damage caused to the vehicle she held.

NOT PURE ECONOMIC LOSS — THE RELATIONAL ECONOMIC LOSS LABEL REJECTED — The courts below characterised Ms Armstead's Clause 16 liability as 'pure economic loss' or 'relational economic loss,' arguing it was too remote to be recoverable. The Supreme Court rejected these characterisations. The loss was not pure economic loss: it was financial loss consequential upon physical damage to property in Ms Armstead's possession. The 'relational economic loss' label — applied in the Court of Appeal following Beechwood Birmingham v Hoyer Group [2010] EWCA Civ 647 — was described as unhelpful and misleading. The correct analysis begins from the fact that physical damage to property (the Mini Cooper) had occurred and Ms Armstead had possessory title to it. The financial loss she suffered through triggering Clause 16 was consequential loss flowing from that physical damage, not free-standing economic loss.

THE INTERNAL ARRANGEMENT PRINCIPLE REJECTED — The Court of Appeal (Dingemans LJ) held that Ms Armstead could not recover the Clause 16 sum because it arose from an 'internal arrangement' between her and Helphire — the hire agreement was not an arm's length commercial arrangement and so the loss it created should not be visited on RSA. The Supreme Court rejected this entirely. There is no 'internal arrangement' principle as a limitation on recoverability. The Winkfield itself involved the bailee (the Post Office) suing the third party tortfeasor for the loss suffered by reason of damage to letters in its possession — nobody suggested the Post Office's internal arrangements with letter owners were relevant. Dingemans LJ had misread Collins MR's judgment in The Winkfield. The fact that the contractual terms between Ms Armstead and Helphire were not negotiated at arm's length creates no legal distinction from Network Rail Infrastructure Ltd v Conarken Group Ltd [2012] EWCA Civ 644 and Ehmler v Hall [1993] 1 EGLR 137.

THE REAL ISSUE: REMOTENESS AND THE WAGON MOUND TEST — Having cleared away the incorrect reasons for dismissal, the Supreme Court identified the real question as one of remoteness of damage under the Wagon Mound test: was the type of loss suffered by Ms Armstead (the Clause 16 liability) reasonably foreseeable as a consequence of the defendant's negligent driving? The type of loss was financial loss from inability to use a damaged hire vehicle — loss of use income for Helphire and liability for that lost income by Ms Armstead under Clause 16. The Supreme Court held that this type of loss was reasonably foreseeable. A driver who damages a hire vehicle can reasonably foresee that the owner/hire company will suffer loss of use during the repair period, and that the hirer may be contractually liable for that loss. Applying Network Rail and Ehmler v Hall: contractual liability to pay a sum representing loss of use is recoverable provided the sum is a reasonable pre-estimate of the actual loss suffered by Helphire.

REASONABLE PRE-ESTIMATE — THE REMITTED QUESTION — The Supreme Court did not finally determine whether Clause 16 constituted a reasonable pre-estimate of Helphire's loss of use. This question was remitted for determination at first instance. The issue is whether the daily rate of £130 in Clause 16 genuinely represented Helphire's expected lost revenue from the Mini Cooper being off the road for 12 days, or whether it was a punitive/inflated sum that went beyond compensating Helphire for its actual loss. If it is a reasonable pre-estimate, the full £1,560 is recoverable. If it is not — if it is in substance a penalty or an overstatement — the sum recoverable will be limited to Helphire's actual proven loss. This question is of broader significance to the credit hire industry because many CHO agreements contain similar loss-of-use clauses; the reasonable pre-estimate requirement applies to all of them.

PRACTICAL IMPORTANCE — Armstead v RSA [2024] UKSC 6 is the most recent Supreme Court authority on credit hire and has significant practical consequences. It confirms: (1) that a credit hire claimant in possession of a hire vehicle has possessory title and can sue for damage to it; (2) that loss flowing from hire agreement obligations triggered by damage to the hire vehicle is consequential loss, not pure economic loss, and is in principle recoverable; (3) that there is no 'internal arrangement' defence for tortfeasors relying on the nature of the CHO agreement; (4) that remoteness and reasonable pre-estimate are the correct controlling principles; and (5) that contractual loss-of-use provisions in hire agreements are recoverable if they represent a genuine, reasonable pre-estimate of the owner's loss. Practitioners should review their clients' hire agreement loss-of-use clauses against this standard.

Wagon Mound (No 1) [1961] AC 388

articles.summary: The UK Supreme Court in Armstead v RSA unanimously reversed three lower court decisions and held that a credit hire claimant in possession of a hire vehicle has possessory title to sue for damage to it (The Winkfield applied); that financial loss triggered by hire agreement obligations following physical damage is consequential loss, not pure economic loss; and that there is no 'internal arrangement' principle preventing recovery. The correct analysis is remoteness under the Wagon Mound test, and contractual loss-of-use provisions are recoverable if they constitute a reasonable pre-estimate of the owner's actual loss. The reasonable pre-estimate question was remitted.

articles.title: Ho v Adelekun [2021] UKSC 43 — QOCS, the CPR 44.14 Monetary Cap and Set-Off as Enforcement
articles.source: UK Supreme Court, 6 October 2021 — [2021] UKSC 43; on appeal from [2020] EWCA Civ 517
key_points: BACKGROUND AND FACTS — Ms Adelekun was injured in a road traffic accident on 26 June 2012. Her claim was issued on 7 January 2015. Ms Ho (the defendant) made a Part 36 offer of £30,000 plus standard costs, which Ms Adelekun accepted by Tomlin order on 24 April 2017. A dispute arose over costs assessment: the defendant contended that fixed costs (~£16,700) applied, while Ms Adelekun sought standard basis costs (~£42,000). The Court of Appeal upheld fixed costs in November 2019. However, Ms Ho had lost the costs assessment dispute itself and was ordered to pay Ms Adelekun's costs of that dispute (~£48,600). Ms Ho therefore owed Ms Adelekun ~£48,600 in costs while Ms Adelekun owed Ms Ho ~£16,700 in fixed costs. Ms Ho sought to set off the £16,700 she was owed against the £48,600 she owed — in effect using her costs order as partial satisfaction of Ms Adelekun's costs order. The Supreme Court held she could not.

QOCS AS A COMPLETE CODE — The Supreme Court (Lord Briggs and Lady Rose, with whom the other Justices agreed) held that the QOCS provisions in CPR 44.13–44.16 constitute a complete code governing what defendants may do with costs orders made in their favour against claimants in personal injury proceedings. CPR 44.14(1) provides: 'orders for costs made against a claimant may be enforced without the permission of the court but only to the extent that the aggregate amount in money terms of such orders does not exceed the aggregate amount in money terms of any orders for damages and interest made in favour of the claimant.' This is a monetary cap (limit B), not merely a procedural rule requiring permission. The complete code analysis means that CPR 44.12 (the general costs set-off jurisdiction) does not operate independently of QOCS: set-off is a form of enforcement and is subject to the same cap.

SET-OFF IS ENFORCEMENT — SUBJECT TO THE CAP — The central issue in Ho v Adelekun was whether set-off of costs against costs (Ms Ho's costs order being set off against Ms Adelekun's costs order) was 'enforcement' for the purposes of CPR 44.14(1). The Court of Appeal in Howe v Motor Insurers' Bureau [2020] Costs LR 297 had held that set-off was not enforcement. The Supreme Court disagreed and overruled Howe. Set-off operates to discharge or reduce a liability — it has the practical effect of enforcement. To exclude it from the QOCS cap would allow defendants to circumvent the cap entirely by the device of set-off. The Supreme Court confirmed: set-off of costs against costs is a form of enforcement and is subject to the CPR 44.14(1) monetary cap.

THE LIMIT B FORMULA — The Supreme Court articulated the operative formula: (A) = the aggregate of all costs orders made against the claimant in favour of the defendant; (B) = the aggregate of all orders for damages and interest made in favour of the claimant. If A does not exceed B, enforcement (including set-off) is unlimited. If A exceeds B, enforcement (including set-off) is capped at B. In Ho v Adelekun, the settlement was by Tomlin order. Following Cartwright v Venduct Engineering Ltd [2018] EWCA Civ 1654, a Tomlin order is not a court order for damages and interest — it is a stay on agreed terms. Therefore, limit B = zero. Ms Ho could not enforce or set off anything against Ms Adelekun's costs order because there was no headroom. The result: Ms Ho was liable to pay the full ~£48,600 costs of the assessment dispute without any set-off.

CARTWRIGHT v VENDUCT CONFIRMED — Ho v Adelekun expressly confirmed the principle in Cartwright v Venduct Engineering [2018] EWCA Civ 1654: where a credit hire or personal injury claim settles by Tomlin order (as is extremely common), the settlement sum does not constitute a 'court order for damages and interest' for the purposes of CPR 44.14(1). Limit B is therefore zero. No QOCS enforcement — including set-off — is possible. This has profound implications for defendants who obtain costs orders against QOCS-protected claimants whose claims settled by Tomlin order. The costs order is, in practice, unenforceable unless the claim proceeded to judgment or a consent order specifically recording an award of damages and interest was made.

PRACTICAL IMPORTANCE FOR CREDIT HIRE LITIGATION — QOCS and its mechanics arise regularly in mixed personal injury and credit hire claims. The Ho v Adelekun principles govern three critical scenarios for credit hire practitioners: (1) where the claim fails and the defendant has a costs order — enforcement is capped at the claimant's aggregate damages and interest awards; (2) where the settlement is by Tomlin order — limit B = zero, and no enforcement or set-off of costs is possible; (3) where the credit hire element alone fails (and the personal injury element succeeds with a damages award) — the defendant may enforce costs relating to the credit hire failure only up to the value of the damages awarded for personal injury. Article 145 of this knowledge base develops the QOCS framework specifically in the mixed PI/credit hire context and should be read alongside this authority.
cases_cited: Cartwright v Venduct Engineering Ltd [2018] EWCA Civ 1654
Howe v Motor Insurers' Bureau [2020] Costs LR 297
Ho v Adelekun [2020] EWCA Civ 517 (CA decision below)
cross_references: Article 145 — QOCS in Mixed PI and Credit Hire Claims
Article 29 — Non-Party Costs Orders against CHOs
Article 35 — QOCS and CPR 44.16 Exceptions
articles.summary: The UK Supreme Court in Ho v Adelekun held that QOCS constitutes a complete code; that set-off of costs against costs is a form of enforcement subject to the CPR 44.14(1) monetary cap; and confirmed the Cartwright v Venduct principle that a Tomlin order settlement generates no 'court order for damages and interest,' leaving limit B at zero and rendering all costs enforcement and set-off impossible where the claim settled on Tomlin terms. The decision is essential reading for any practitioner managing costs risks in QOCS-protected credit hire or personal injury claims.

articles.title: Clarke v Ardington [2002] EWCA Civ 510 — Helphire Scheme Validity, CCA 1974 Exemptions and Impecuniosity
articles.source: Court of Appeal transcript [2002] EWCA Civ 510
key_points: OVERVIEW — Clarke v Ardington [2002] EWCA Civ 510 is a landmark Court of Appeal decision determining the legal validity of the Helphire credit hire scheme and resolving five conjoined appeals: Clark, Dennard, Sen, Lagden and Burdis. The panel comprised Aldous, Tuckey and Jonathan Parker LJJ. The judgment addresses six distinct issues of wide application across all credit hire litigation: (1) whether the Helphire scheme was a pretence designed to avoid the Consumer Credit Act 1974; (2) whether the hire agreements were consumer hire agreements within s.15 CCA 1974; (3) whether the agreements were exempt from CCA regulation; (4) whether repair costs could be recovered even if the hire agreement were unenforceable; (5) the position of impecunious claimants; and (6) the correct calculation of the spot rate.

ISSUE 1 — WHETHER HELPHIRE SCHEME WAS A PRETENCE — The defendants argued that the entire Helphire scheme was a sham: that the documentation was designed to create the appearance of a credit hire agreement while in substance simply providing claimants with free cars at the defendants' expense, thereby evading the netting-off and mitigation principles established in Giles v Thompson. The Court rejected this argument. The scheme was not a pretence. The obligations created — including the claimant's obligation to repay hire charges — were genuine. The fact that Helphire had a commercial arrangement with insurers and was unlikely to pursue individual claimants did not make the agreement a nullity. The agreement created genuine rights and genuine liabilities, and could not be characterised as a device to deprive the defendant of the protection of the market rate cap.

ISSUE 2 — CONSUMER HIRE AGREEMENTS UNDER s.15 CCA 1974 — The Court held that the Helphire agreements were consumer hire agreements within the meaning of s.15 of the Consumer Credit Act 1974 because they were agreements by which goods (a motor vehicle) were bailed to the hirer in return for periodic payments. The hire period exceeded three months. The question was whether regulation 4 or other exemptions applied.

ISSUE 3 — EXEMPTIONS FROM CCA REGULATION — The Court held that the Helphire agreements were exempt from CCA regulation under two separate routes: (a) the 'four payments rule' — where the agreement provided for not more than four payments, the agreement fell outside the CCA; and (b) the '12 months rule' — where the agreement was for a period not exceeding 12 months in combination with other conditions, the agreement was exempt. The Court held that Helphire's agreements qualified for exemption under the relevant statutory instrument (SI 1983/1878 as amended). This exemption conclusion was important: it meant the CCA provided no route for defendants to have hire agreements declared unenforceable.

ISSUE 4 — REPAIR COSTS WHERE CCA POTENTIALLY UNENFORCEABLE — In the Burdis sub-appeal (which concerned repair costs rather than hire charges), the question arose whether repair costs could be recovered as damages even if the agreement under which repairs were performed was unenforceable under the CCA. The Court held yes, applying Jones v Stroud District Council. A defendant tortfeasor cannot take advantage of the unenforceability of a contract to which it is not a party. The claimant's loss (the damaged vehicle) was a real loss. The repair costs were an objective measure of that loss. The fact that the contract for repairs might not be enforceable against the claimant does not mean the claimant has suffered no recoverable loss.

ISSUE 5 — IMPECUNIOUS CLAIMANTS — The Lagden sub-appeal raised the question of whether an impecunious claimant who could not afford to pay for a comparable vehicle at the spot/market rate, and who therefore had no alternative but to use a credit hire agreement at a higher rate, could recover the full credit hire rate from the defendant. The Court of Appeal held that an impecunious claimant can recover the credit hire rate. The defendant's argument — that the claimant should recover only the market (spot) rate because that is what any claimant could have paid — was rejected. The Court applied the principle that a defendant must take his victim as he finds him. If the claimant's financial circumstances are such that he cannot pay upfront for a replacement vehicle at the spot rate and must use a credit hire company, the additional cost attributable to the credit hire arrangement is recoverable as part of the damages flowing naturally from the defendant's wrong. Note: Lagden v O'Connor was subsequently appealed to the House of Lords, which confirmed this principle in [2003] UKHL 64 (see Article 147 of this knowledge base).

ISSUE 6 — SPOT RATE CALCULATION — For those claimants who were not impecunious, the Court confirmed that the recoverable hire charge is the market (spot) rate for an equivalent vehicle — the amount the claimant would have paid had he hired a comparable vehicle from a high-street hire company without a credit facility. This is sometimes described as the 'basic hire rate' (BHR) or the 'comparable spot hire rate.' Where the credit hire rate exceeded the spot rate, only the spot rate was recoverable (absent impecuniosity). The Court approved the use of surveys and market evidence to establish the applicable spot rate.

SIGNIFICANCE AND ONGOING RELEVANCE — Clarke v Ardington remains foundational authority in credit hire litigation for: (a) the validity of the Helphire-style scheme and the genuine nature of the claimant's hire obligations; (b) the CCA exemption analysis which applies to many modern CHO agreements; (c) the recovery of repair costs notwithstanding potential CCA unenforceability; (d) the impecuniosity principle (see also Lagden v O'Connor [2003] UKHL 64, Article 147); and (e) the spot rate benchmark. The decision was handed down in the same year as Giles v Thompson and provides the CCA and impecuniosity gloss on the core principles established in that earlier decision.

Jones v Stroud District Council [1986] 1 WLR 1141
Lagden v O'Connor [2003] UKHL 64

Article 147 — Lagden v O'Connor [2003] UKHL 64 (impecuniosity confirmed by HL)

articles.summary: Clarke v Ardington [2002] EWCA Civ 510 resolved five conjoined appeals concerning the Helphire credit hire scheme. The Court of Appeal held: (1) the scheme was not a pretence; (2) the agreements were consumer hire agreements under CCA s.15 but were exempt from regulation; (3) repair costs were recoverable even if the underlying contract were unenforceable, applying Jones v Stroud; (4) impecunious claimants can recover the full credit hire rate; and (5) for non-impecunious claimants, recovery is limited to the market (spot) rate. The decision underpins modern credit hire practice on Helphire-style arrangements, CCA exemption arguments and the impecuniosity doctrine.

articles.title: Bee v Jenson [2007] EWCA Civ 923 — Recovery of Hire Costs, Bradburn Principle and Inducement Payments as Res Inter Alios Acta
articles.subcategory: Recovery of Hire Costs / Res Inter Alios Acta / Inducement
articles.source: Court of Appeal transcript [2007] EWCA Civ 923
key_points: OVERVIEW — Bee v Jenson [2007] EWCA Civ 923 is a Court of Appeal authority on three distinct but interrelated issues arising in credit hire litigation: (1) whether a claimant can recover the cost of hire even if he has not personally incurred liability to pay the hire charges; (2) whether an inducement or referral payment made between the hire company (Helphire) and an affiliate of the claimant's legal expenses insurer (DAS/Counselline) falls to be deducted from the damages; and (3) the correct measure of damages for deprivation of use of a chattel. The panel comprised Tuckey and Longmore LJJ and Kennedy LJ. The judgment is of general application beyond Helphire cases.

FACTS — Mr Bee's vehicle was damaged in a road traffic accident caused by Mr Jenson. Mr Bee was insured with DAS for legal expenses. DAS referred him to Helphire, who provided a replacement vehicle at a daily rate of £24.74 for 21 days, giving a total hire charge of £610.46. Helphire paid an inducement (referral fee) to Counselline, an affiliate company of DAS. The defendant argued that this payment should be deducted from, or set off against, the damages representing the hire charges because it meant the effective cost to the claimant (or to those standing behind him) was lower than the headline hire rate.

ISSUE 1 — RECOVERY WHERE CLAIMANT NOT PERSONALLY LIABLE — The defendant argued that if Mr Bee was not personally liable to pay the hire charges (e.g. because DAS or Helphire had agreed to bear the cost in certain circumstances), he had suffered no loss and was not entitled to damages representing the hire charges. The Court emphatically rejected this argument. Longmore LJ at paragraph 15 stated: 'If he has in fact reasonably made arrangements for a hire car, there is no reason why he should not recover the cost of hire, whether or not he has rendered himself liable for the hire charges and whether or not the actual cost has been paid by him or somebody else such as an insurer.' The principle is that a tortfeasor cannot benefit from the fact that the claimant was fortunate enough to have insurance, an insurer, or a hire company willing to bear costs on his behalf.

ISSUE 2 — BRADBURN PRINCIPLE — The Court applied the well-established Bradburn v Great Western Railway (1874) principle that a defendant cannot reduce the damages he must pay by reference to moneys received by the claimant from independent sources such as insurance. Insurance benefits are res inter alios acta — acts between other parties — and are irrelevant to the measure of the tortfeasor's liability. The same principle applies where the benefit has been received by an insurer or associated party rather than the claimant personally. The defendant's tort did not become less harmful merely because the claimant was prudent enough to have legal expenses insurance.

ISSUE 3 — INDUCEMENT PAYMENT AS RES INTER ALIOS ACTA — The core argument on appeal was that the inducement payment from Helphire to Counselline (DAS's affiliate) should reduce the damages because it effectively subsidised the hire transaction and meant the true 'net cost' to the DAS group was lower than £610.46. The Court held this argument failed at the first hurdle. The payment from Helphire to Counselline was a transaction between two commercial entities wholly independent of Mr Bee's hire contract. It was res inter alios acta — a transaction between other parties to which Mr Bee was not party and by which he was unaffected. Whatever commercial benefit DAS or Counselline derived from the arrangement had no bearing on Mr Bee's loss, which was the cost of replacing the use of his vehicle for 21 days.

ISSUE 4 — MEASURE OF DAMAGES: THE MEDIANA PRINCIPLE — The Court confirmed that where a claimant is deprived of the use of a chattel (here, his car), the appropriate measure of general damages is the cost of hiring a comparable chattel for the period of deprivation. This flows from The Mediana [1900] AC 113 (HL), which established that the loss of use of a vessel is compensatable even without proof of special damage, and that the cost of hiring a substitute is the natural measure of that loss. Where the claimant has hired a replacement vehicle, that hire cost (subject to reasonableness and any market rate cap) is therefore the appropriate measure of his damages.

ISSUE 5 — SPOT RATE AS THE MEASURE — The Court confirmed that damages for hire costs are assessed by reference to the spot hire rate — the amount a claimant in the market (without credit) would have paid for a comparable vehicle. The fact that the claimant paid a higher credit hire rate, or arranged hire through an insurer-referred scheme at an unconventional rate, does not alter the measure. The correct comparator is the open-market daily rate for a comparable vehicle, as established in Giles v Thompson and confirmed in Clarke v Ardington.

PRACTICAL SIGNIFICANCE — Bee v Jenson is important for three categories of case: (1) cases where the CHO has agreed to defer charges or look to insurers rather than the claimant, and the defendant argues the claimant has 'no liability' and therefore no loss — this argument fails per paragraph 15; (2) cases where the defendant seeks to unpick referral or inducement payment arrangements as reducing damages — these payments are res inter alios acta; (3) cases where the Mediana/loss-of-use principle is engaged. The paragraph 15 dictum of Longmore LJ is widely cited in credit hire litigation to defeat 'no personal liability = no loss' arguments.
cases_cited: Bradburn v Great Western Railway (1874) LR 10 Ex 1
The Mediana [1900] AC 113
Giles v Thompson [1994] 1 AC 142
Clarke v Ardington [2002] EWCA Civ 510
Dimond v Lovell [2002] 1 AC 384
cross_references: Article 148 — Giles v Thompson [1994] 1 AC 142 (spot rate benchmark)
Article 151 — Clarke v Ardington [2002] EWCA Civ 510 (Helphire scheme validity)
Article 146 — Dimond v Lovell [2002] 1 AC 384 (CCA and credit hire)
articles.summary: Bee v Jenson [2007] EWCA Civ 923 held: (1) a claimant can recover the reasonable cost of hire even if not personally liable for the hire charges — Longmore LJ at para 15 is the leading statement of this principle; (2) the Bradburn principle prevents deduction of benefits received from independent sources; (3) an inducement/referral payment between the hire company and an insurer affiliate is res inter alios acta and cannot reduce the claimant's damages; (4) the Mediana principle applies — loss of use of a vehicle is compensated by the cost of hiring a substitute; (5) the applicable measure is the spot hire rate for a comparable vehicle.

articles.title: Copley v Lawn [2009] EWCA Civ 580 — Mitigation, Defendant's Offer of Replacement Vehicle and the Disclosure Requirement
articles.source: Court of Appeal transcript [2009] EWCA Civ 580
key_points: OVERVIEW — Copley v Lawn [2009] EWCA Civ 580 is the leading Court of Appeal authority on the mitigation argument in credit hire cases where the defendant or defendant's insurer offers a 'free' replacement vehicle to the claimant. The appeal resolved two conjoined appeals — Mrs Copley and Captain Maden — both of whom had rejected replacement vehicle offers from KGM Insurance (the defendant's insurer) and had instead hired from Helphire at credit hire rates. The panel comprised Waller VP and Longmore and Jacob LJJ. The decision defines the conditions which a defendant's offer must satisfy before its rejection can constitute a failure to mitigate.

FACTS — In both appeals, KGM (acting on behalf of the defendant insurer) contacted the claimants shortly after the accident and offered to provide a comparable replacement vehicle at no cost to the claimant. Both claimants declined the offers and instead hired replacement vehicles through Helphire at credit hire rates substantially higher than any commercial spot rate. At trial the defendants argued that by unreasonably rejecting a reasonable offer of a free car, the claimants had failed to mitigate their loss and should recover nothing (or very little) for their hire charges. The county court agreed in both cases.

THE DISCLOSURE REQUIREMENT — THE CORE PRINCIPLE — The Court of Appeal held that for a defendant's offer of a replacement vehicle to be one that a claimant is required to accept (on pain of being found to have failed to mitigate), the offer must disclose the true cost to the defendant of providing the vehicle. This is the 'disclosure requirement.' A claimant cannot make a properly informed decision about whether the defendant's offer is the equivalent of, or superior to, going to the open market, unless the claimant knows what the defendant will have to pay for the provision. Waller VP emphasised that the defendant's offer is not truly comparable with a spot hire unless the claimant can verify what the actual cost to the defendant would be. Without that information, rejecting the offer is not unreasonable.

WHERE DEFENDANT CANNOT SHOW CHEAPER ALTERNATIVE — The Court held that where the defendant cannot demonstrate that it could and would have provided a comparable vehicle more cheaply than the credit hire rate actually incurred, the mitigation argument cannot even get off the ground. The defendant bears the burden of showing: (a) that it made a genuine offer; (b) what that offer would have cost the defendant; and (c) that that cost was less than the credit hire rate claimed. Absent that evidence, the claimant's conduct in using a credit hire company cannot be impugned.

CONSEQUENCE OF UNREASONABLE REJECTION — The Court addressed the position where a claimant does unreasonably reject a genuine, adequately disclosed defendant offer. Contrary to the county court approach in both cases (which had awarded nil or minimal hire charges), the Court held that an unreasonable rejection of a reasonable offer does not forfeit the claimant's entire damages claim for hire. Rather, the claimant's damages are limited to the cost the defendant could show it would have reasonably incurred in providing the replacement vehicle. This is the 'floor' below which damages cannot fall even where mitigation has failed. The principle is that the defendant's measure of loss is capped at what the defendant would have spent anyway, not extinguished.

COLD CALLING — INAPPROPRIATE CONDUCT — Waller VP delivered a strongly-worded passage (at paragraph 32 of the judgment) criticising the practice of insurance companies contacting accident victims within 24 hours of an accident, sometimes from the scene, to offer replacement vehicles before any solicitor has been instructed. The Court characterised this as inappropriate and expressed the view that such letters 'should be discontinued forthwith.' The conduct was described as a form of commercial pressure placed on a vulnerable party at a stressful moment. Whilst not determinative of the mitigation question, the Court indicated that the circumstances of the offer (including how it was made) are relevant to the reasonableness of the claimant's response.

THE ADEQUATE INFORMATION TEST — The principle flowing from Copley v Lawn is that a defendant's offer must satisfy an information test: the claimant must be told (a) who is providing the vehicle; (b) the cost to the defendant; and (c) the nature of the vehicle being offered. Without this information the claimant is not in a position to compare the defendant's offer with the open market and cannot be said to have acted unreasonably in declining it. Modern defendant offer letters that do not specify what the defendant will pay for the provision of the replacement vehicle are defective under Copley v Lawn principles.

PRACTICAL SIGNIFICANCE — Copley v Lawn is essential for any credit hire case where the defendant raises a mitigation/alternative vehicle offer argument. The key points for practice are: (1) obtain from the defendant evidence of what its offer would have cost it; (2) if that evidence is absent or the offer letter is silent on cost, the mitigation argument should fail entirely; (3) even if the claimant is found to have unreasonably rejected a valid offer, the remedy is reduction to what the defendant would have paid — not nil damages; (4) cold calling by insurers should be challenged as inappropriately prejudicing the claimant's freedom of choice; (5) the decision does not disturb the spot rate cap for non-impecunious claimants but addresses the separate mitigation dimension.
cases_cited: Giles v Thompson [1994] 1 AC 142
Clarke v Ardington [2002] EWCA Civ 510
Lagden v O'Connor [2003] UKHL 64
cross_references: Article 148 — Giles v Thompson [1994] 1 AC 142 (spot rate and mitigation)
Article 151 — Clarke v Ardington [2002] EWCA Civ 510 (Helphire validity)
Article 147 — Lagden v O'Connor [2003] UKHL 64 (impecuniosity)
articles.summary: Copley v Lawn [2009] EWCA Civ 580 established that a defendant's offer of a free replacement vehicle must disclose its true cost to the defendant before its rejection can constitute a failure to mitigate. Where the defendant cannot show it could provide the car more cheaply, the mitigation argument fails. Even where a claimant unreasonably rejects a valid offer, damages are limited to the cost the defendant would have incurred — not forfeited entirely. Cold-calling accident victims within 24 hours to offer replacement vehicles was criticised as inappropriate and 'should be discontinued forthwith.'

articles.title: Diriye v Bojaj [2020] EWCA Civ 1400 — Impecuniosity Pleading, Unless Orders, CPR 6.26 Service and the Denton Test
articles.subcategory: Impecuniosity / Pleading / CPR 6.26 / Denton / Unless Orders
articles.source: Court of Appeal transcript [2020] EWCA Civ 1400
key_points: OVERVIEW — Diriye v Bojaj [2020] EWCA Civ 1400 is a Court of Appeal decision of critical importance to credit hire practitioners on two distinct but related issues: (1) the procedural requirements for service of documents by 'Signed For 1st Class' post and its classification under CPR 6.26; and (2) the substantive requirements for pleading and evidencing impecuniosity in compliance with an Unless Order. The panel comprised Coulson, Nicola Davies and Rose LJJ. The decision is a stark practical warning that impecuniosity assertions must be specifically pleaded with income and expenditure detail, not advanced by bare assertion.

FACTS — Mr Diriye, a minicab driver, was involved in a road traffic accident on 30 May 2014 and issued proceedings in 2017. The credit hire element of his claim was £12,048.29 — the largest component. A DJ made an Unless Order requiring impecuniosity to be pleaded and evidenced by 4 April 2018, failing which Mr Diriye would be debarred from advancing impecuniosity at trial. Mr Diriye's solicitors served the Reply (which was intended to address impecuniosity) by 'Signed For 1st Class' post. The Reply was physically received two days after the Unless Order deadline. Whether it was served in time turned on whether 'Signed For 1st Class' post was treated as effecting service on the day of posting plus one day (next business day), or on a later deemed date.

CPR 6.26 — DEEMED DATE OF SERVICE BY 'SIGNED FOR 1ST CLASS' POST — CPR 6.26 provides a table of deemed service dates for different methods of service. The relevant entry provides that service by 'first class post or other service providing next business day delivery' is deemed to take effect on the second business day after posting. The question was whether 'Signed For 1st Class' Royal Mail post falls within this category or a different, later-deemed category. The Court held that 'Signed For 1st Class' post IS 'first class post' for the purposes of CPR 6.26. It is not a separate or distinct category. The deemed service date is therefore the second business day after posting. On the facts, this meant service was two days late (not five as the defendant had argued using a different category). This analysis by Coulson LJ has general application to all service-by-post questions under CPR.

DENTON THREE-STAGE TEST — STAGE 1: SERIOUSNESS OF BREACH — Having established that service was two days late, the Court applied the Denton v TH White [2014] EWCA Civ 906 three-stage test for relief from sanctions. At the first stage (seriousness of breach), Coulson LJ held the breach was serious and significant. Although the breach in terms of delay was only two days, the Court treated it as serious in context because: (a) it was a breach of an Unless Order, which is by its nature a sanction of last resort reflecting the court's assessment that compliance is essential; and (b) the Reply itself was substantively deficient.

DENTON THREE-STAGE TEST — STAGE 2 AND 3: REASON FOR BREACH AND ALL THE CIRCUMSTANCES — No compelling reason was identified for the late service. At stage 3, the Court considered all the circumstances. The critical finding was that the Reply did not in fact comply with the terms of the Unless Order even on its face: it contained only a bare assertion that Mr Diriye 'earned cash as a minicab driver and expended same on bills.' This failed to provide the specific income and expenditure particulars required by the Unless Order and by the established jurisprudence on impecuniosity pleading. Relief from sanctions was refused.

IMPECUNIOSITY — WHAT MUST BE PLEADED AND EVIDENCED — The decision confirms and applies the principle from Zurich Insurance plc v Umerji [2014] EWCA Civ 357 (Underhill LJ at paragraph 37) that a claimant who needs to rely on impecuniosity to justify recovery at the credit hire rate must: (a) properly plead the financial circumstances said to give rise to impecuniosity; (b) identify specific income figures; (c) identify specific expenditure commitments; and (d) thereby demonstrate that the claimant genuinely lacked the means to fund a spot hire at the time of the accident. A bare or bald assertion — such as 'earned cash, expended same' — is wholly insufficient. The Court endorsed the approach in Zurich v Umerji that the defendant is entitled to investigate and challenge impecuniosity, and that the claimant must lay out the factual substratum to permit that investigation.

CONSEQUENCE OF UNLESS ORDER BREACH — Mr Diriye was debarred from relying on impecuniosity at trial. This meant his credit hire claim was assessed on the spot rate basis, reducing it substantially from £12,048.29. The decision illustrates the high-stakes nature of compliance with case management orders in credit hire claims. An Unless Order relating to impecuniosity is not a technical nicety: its breach can eliminate a five-figure element of the claim.

PRACTICAL SIGNIFICANCE FOR CREDIT HIRE PRACTITIONERS — Diriye v Bojaj is an essential reference for the following practical scenarios: (1) when serving documents by 'Signed For 1st Class' post, the deemed service date is the second business day after posting under CPR 6.26, not a later date; (2) Unless Orders requiring impecuniosity to be pleaded must be complied with on time and in substance; (3) a Reply or pleading that purports to address impecuniosity but contains only a bare assertion will not satisfy the Unless Order and will not survive a Denton application; (4) claimant solicitors must obtain, at an early stage, documentary evidence of the claimant's financial position (bank statements, income records, evidence of benefit receipts, outstanding credit commitments) sufficient to plead specific income and expenditure figures; (5) a two-day breach of an Unless Order will be treated as serious and significant in the Denton analysis where the underlying pleading is also deficient.
cases_cited: Denton v TH White Ltd [2014] EWCA Civ 906
Zurich Insurance plc v Umerji [2014] EWCA Civ 357
Lagden v O'Connor [2003] UKHL 64
Mitchell v News Group Newspapers Ltd [2013] EWCA Civ 1537
cross_references: Article 147 — Lagden v O'Connor [2003] UKHL 64 (impecuniosity doctrine)
Article 151 — Clarke v Ardington [2002] EWCA Civ 510 (impecuniosity in context of Helphire)
Article 128 — Denton v TH White [2014] EWCA Civ 906 (relief from sanctions framework)
articles.summary: Diriye v Bojaj [2020] EWCA Civ 1400 held: (1) 'Signed For 1st Class' post is first class post under CPR 6.26 and service is deemed the second business day after posting; (2) a two-day breach of an Unless Order requiring impecuniosity to be pleaded was serious and significant under the Denton test; (3) a Reply containing only bare assertion of impecuniosity ('earned cash, expended same on bills') does not comply with the Unless Order; (4) relief from sanctions was refused; (5) the claimant was debarred from relying on impecuniosity, reducing a £12,048 credit hire claim to spot rate. The decision is a practical warning that impecuniosity must be specifically pleaded with income and expenditure particulars.

articles.title: Opoku v Tintas [2013] EWCA Civ 1299
articles.source: Court of Appeal (Civil Division)
key_points: BACKGROUND AND FACTS: Mr Opoku's vehicle was damaged in a road traffic accident caused by Ms Tintas. He hired a replacement vehicle on credit hire terms and claimed the cost from the defendant insurer. The hire period was lengthy and the claimant relied on impecuniosity both to justify the credit hire rate (rather than the basic hire rate) and to explain the extended duration of hire while his own vehicle remained unrepaired. The trial judge found that the claimant was impecunious for the purposes of the hire rate but declined to allow the full period of hire, finding that the claimant could have funded repair within a shorter period. The defendant appealed on the rate issue; the claimant cross-appealed on the period.
COURT OF APPEAL

DECISION: The Court of Appeal dismissed both the appeal and the cross-appeal. The judgment addresses two analytically distinct questions: (1) whether a finding of impecuniosity for hire rate purposes automatically carries through to impecuniosity for repair funding purposes; and (2) whether the judge's assessment of the hire period was open to challenge.

IMPECUNIOSITY FOR RATE VERSUS REPAIR FUNDING: The Court held that a finding of impecuniosity entitling a claimant to recover the credit hire rate rather than the BHR does not automatically mean that the same claimant is impecunious for the purpose of funding vehicle repairs. The two questions are analytically distinct. Impecuniosity for hire rate purposes asks whether the claimant could have hired a substitute vehicle at the basic hire rate – that is, whether he had or could obtain the means (including credit) to pay the upfront deposit or pre-authorisation required by mainstream hire companies. Impecuniosity for repair funding asks a different question: whether the claimant could have funded or financed the cost of repairs to his damaged vehicle. These are different financial transactions with different characteristics. A claimant may be unable to fund a deposit for a mainstream hire car (and so be entitled to the credit hire rate) but may nonetheless be able to fund or finance repair costs – for example through insurance, a repair credit scheme, or other means – within a reasonable period. The judge was entitled to make separate findings on each aspect.

PERIOD OF HIRE AND MITIGATION: The period of hire is subject to the general principle of mitigation. The claimant is under a duty to take reasonable steps to have his vehicle repaired within a reasonable time. Where the delay in repair is attributable to the claimant's impecuniosity, that impecuniosity must be pleaded and proved as a continuing condition throughout the hire period. The judge found on the facts that the claimant could have funded repairs within approximately 8 months, and accordingly limited the allowable hire period. The Court of Appeal held this was a finding open to the judge on the evidence and declined to interfere. The claimant's cross-appeal on the period therefore failed.

EVIDENTIAL REQUIREMENTS: The burden of proving impecuniosity lies on the claimant. The claimant must provide cogent evidence of his financial position sufficient to satisfy the court: (1) that he lacked the means to hire at the basic hire rate; and/or (2) that he lacked the means to fund repairs and that this continued throughout the hire period claimed. General assertions of financial difficulty are insufficient; the court requires detailed evidence of income, outgoings, savings, available credit, and any insurance or other funding mechanisms available.

PRACTICAL SIGNIFICANCE FOR CREDIT HIRE HANDLERS: This case is important for practitioners because it prevents a claimant from using a single broad impecuniosity finding as a blanket justification for both an inflated rate and an extended period. Handlers should always analyse the two issues separately: (a) Can the claimant truly be shown to have been unable to fund mainstream hire? If not, the BHR applies. (b) Even if the credit hire rate is conceded or established, was the period of hire reasonable and was any delay in repair genuinely attributable to financial incapacity throughout? If the claimant could have funded repairs earlier, the period should be curtailed. The case reinforces that impecuniosity is not a single monolithic concept but must be considered in relation to each specific financial obligation it is relied upon to excuse.
cases_cited: Lagden v O'Connor [2003] UKHL 64
Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management Ltd [2015] EWCA Civ 93
articles.summary: A finding of impecuniosity entitling a claimant to the credit hire rate does not automatically establish impecuniosity for repair funding purposes. The two questions are analytically distinct. The period of hire is subject to mitigation principles: if the claimant could have funded repairs within a shorter period, the hire period is curtailed accordingly. Burden of proof throughout lies on the claimant.

articles.title: Zurich Insurance plc v Umerji [2014] EWCA Civ 357
articles.source: Court of Appeal (Civil Division)
key_points:
BACKGROUND AND FACTS: Mr Umerji was involved in a road traffic accident and hired a replacement vehicle on credit hire terms. He claimed impecuniosity both to justify the credit hire rate over the basic hire rate and to justify the length of the hire period (contending he could not afford to purchase a replacement vehicle). During the litigation, the defendant insurers Zurich obtained a debarring order against Mr Umerji preventing him from relying on impecuniosity, because of his failure to comply with disclosure obligations relating to his financial position. Mr Umerji also claimed storage charges for his written-off vehicle. The trial judge allowed hire charges up to the date of the debarring order and dismissed the storage charges claim. Both parties appealed to the Court of Appeal.

COURT OF APPEAL DECISION (Moses, Underhill and Jacob LJJ): The Court of Appeal dismissed both appeals, affirming the trial judge's approach in all material respects.

EFFECT OF DEBARRING ORDER – ALL PURPOSES: The most significant holding is that a debarring order preventing the claimant from asserting impecuniosity operates for ALL purposes for which impecuniosity might otherwise have been relevant – not merely for the purpose of the hire rate. The Court rejected the argument that the debarring order only prevented the claimant from claiming the credit hire rate over the BHR, while leaving him free to rely on impecuniosity to justify the length of the hire period (i.e. that he couldn't afford to buy a replacement car and so needed to hire for longer). The Court held that the debarring order meant Mr Umerji was treated as if he could afford to hire at the basic hire rate AND as if he could afford to purchase a replacement vehicle. Consequently, the hire period was curtailed to 8 March 2011, the date of the debarring order (or more precisely, the date from which the claimant was debarred), not extended on grounds of inability to buy a replacement.

IMPECUNIOSITY GOES TO DURATION AS WELL AS RATE: The Court confirmed (para 9(4)) that impecuniosity is relevant not only to the rate of hire but also to the duration of hire. A claimant who genuinely cannot afford to purchase a replacement vehicle may be entitled to an extended hire period as a consequence of their impecuniosity. This is a two-way principle: impecuniosity, if proved, can justify both the credit hire rate AND the period; equally, if a claimant is debarred from asserting impecuniosity, they lose both advantages.
