BURDEN OF PLEADING AND PROVING IMPECUNIOSITY: The Court confirmed that the burden of pleading and proving impecuniosity rests on the claimant. Impecuniosity is not to be assumed; it must be positively asserted and supported with evidence. Where a claimant fails to provide adequate financial disclosure to enable the defendant to assess and contest the impecuniosity case, the court has power to make a debarring order.

STORAGE CHARGES: The defendant's appeal against the storage charges was dismissed. The judge had found that it was reasonable for Mr Umerji to retain the written-off vehicle in storage while waiting for Zurich to exercise its option to inspect the vehicle. Until the insurer had had a reasonable opportunity to inspect the write-off, the claimant could not be criticised for incurring storage costs. This aspect of the decision has practical importance for handlers dealing with storage charge disputes: where the insurer has been given a reasonable opportunity to inspect and has failed to do so, storage costs accruing in the meantime may be recoverable.

PRACTICAL SIGNIFICANCE FOR CREDIT HIRE HANDLERS: (1) Always seek disclosure of the claimant's financial position where impecuniosity is relied upon for either rate or duration. Non-disclosure can and should lead to an application for a debarring order. (2) A debarring order is a powerful weapon: it removes the claimant's ability to rely on impecuniosity for both rate AND period/duration purposes. (3) Where a debarring order is obtained, the hire period should be assessed on the basis that the claimant could have hired at the BHR (i.e. had the financial means to do so) AND could have purchased a replacement vehicle. This can substantially reduce the recoverable hire period. (4) On storage charges, arrange inspection of write-offs promptly to prevent ongoing storage costs crystallising into a recoverable debt.
cases_cited: Lagden v O'Connor [2003] UKHL 64
Opoku v Tintas [2013] EWCA Civ 1299
articles.summary: A debarring order preventing a claimant from asserting impecuniosity operates for ALL purposes – both for the hire rate and for the duration/period of hire. The claimant could not rely on inability to purchase a replacement vehicle (to justify extended hire) once debarred. Impecuniosity goes to both rate and duration. Storage charges during reasonable inspection period are recoverable.

articles.title: Beechwood Birmingham Ltd v Hoyer Group UK Ltd [2010] EWCA Civ 647
articles.source: Court of Appeal (Civil Division)
key_points:
BACKGROUND AND FACTS: Beechwood Birmingham Ltd was an Audi dealership with a pool of approximately 64 vehicles available for use. One of the company's vehicles was damaged in an accident caused by Hoyer Group UK Ltd. Rather than using one of the vehicles in its own pool as a replacement, Beechwood engaged an employee to hire a replacement vehicle on credit hire terms. The trial judge awarded the credit hire charges. Hoyer appealed, arguing both that there was no need for hire (given the pool of available vehicles) and that the measure of damages for a corporate claimant was in any event not the spot rate.

COURT OF APPEAL DECISION (Sir Mark Potter, Dyson LJ, Maurice Kay LJ): The Court of Appeal allowed the appeal on both grounds.

NO NEED FOR HIRE: The Court held that there was no genuine need for outside hire in the circumstances. Beechwood had a substantial pool of 64 vehicles, and the evidence demonstrated that the company had sufficient spare capacity to have absorbed the loss of one vehicle without needing to hire externally. The employee's decision to engage a credit hire vehicle was not a reasonable act of mitigation but an unnecessary additional expense. The "need" requirement for credit hire is a real and substantive one: the claimant must demonstrate that the hired vehicle was genuinely required and that the hire was reasonable in the circumstances, including whether the claimant's own resources could have met the need. For corporate fleet operators, this means showing that the lost vehicle genuinely could not be covered from within the existing fleet, having regard to operational requirements.

CORRECT MEASURE OF DAMAGES FOR CORPORATE CLAIMANT: Even if there had been a need for a replacement, the correct measure of general damages for a corporate claimant who uses its own fleet to provide a replacement vehicle (or who has spare fleet capacity) is NOT the spot hire rate. The Court rejected the approach of awarding the spot rate as a proxy for loss of use, following the analysis in Lord Scott's speech in Lagden v O'Connor (which had been directed at the individual private motorist who actually hired a replacement, not the corporate claimant with a fleet). The correct measure for a corporate claimant is: (a) interest on the capital value of the damaged vehicle during the period it was off the road; plus (b) wasted expenses (costs thrown away during the repair period – e.g. road tax, insurance attributable to that vehicle); plus (c) a depreciation element. This is sometimes called the "Admiralty" or "Sowsberry accounting" approach. In the present case, the trial judge's award of £12,000 based on the spot rate was set aside and the matter remitted for re-assessment on the correct basis.

WHY THE SPOT RATE IS INAPPROPRIATE FOR CORPORATE CLAIMANTS: The spot rate as a measure of loss of use was endorsed in Lagden in the context of a private individual who could not afford to hire a replacement and was therefore confined to credit hire. Lord Scott indicated that the spot rate was a "fair approach" in those circumstances. However, this reasoning does not extend to corporate claimants who maintain a fleet of vehicles, can absorb the impact of losing one vehicle from within the fleet, and do not actually go out and hire a replacement at spot rates. For such claimants, the true loss is the economic cost of having one fewer asset available – which is measured by the carrying cost of that asset (interest on capital and depreciation), not by the market cost of hiring an equivalent vehicle.

SIGNIFICANCE FOR FLEET AND CORPORATE CREDIT HIRE CLAIMS: This case is highly significant for credit hire handlers dealing with fleet or business claimants. Key points are: (1) Where the claimant is a business with its own fleet or pool of vehicles, investigate carefully whether external hire was genuinely necessary – if spare capacity existed, the hire claim may fail entirely. (2) Even if a corporate claimant can show some need for replacement (e.g. a very small fleet with no spare vehicles), the measure of damages is capital value interest plus depreciation, not the spot rate and certainly not the credit hire rate. (3) The spot rate as a surrogate measure for loss of use is confined to private individuals who actually hire a replacement; it does not apply to businesses with vehicle fleets. (4) Challenge any credit hire claim by a corporate or fleet claimant on both need and measure.
cases_cited: Lagden v O'Connor [2003] UKHL 64
West Midlands Travel Ltd v Aviva Insurance [2013] EWCA Civ 887
Singh v Yaqubi [2013] EWCA Civ 23
articles.summary: Corporate fleet operator with 64 vehicles had no genuine need to externally hire a replacement. Even if need were established, the correct measure of damages for a corporate claimant is interest on capital value plus depreciation – NOT the spot hire rate. The spot rate as a loss of use proxy is limited to private individuals who actually hire replacements.

articles.title: McBride v UK Insurance Ltd (and Clayton v EUI Ltd) [2017] EWCA Civ 144
articles.source: Court of Appeal (Civil Division)
key_points:
BACKGROUND AND FACTS: Two conjoined appeals raising important questions about the methodology for assessing the basic hire rate (BHR) and the treatment of nil excess/collision damage waiver charges. McBride v UK Insurance: Mr McBride hired a replacement vehicle from a credit hire organisation. The judge calculated the BHR from mainstream/local suppliers and held the credit hire organisation's nil excess charge (£10/day) was separately recoverable because mainstream suppliers did not provide a nil excess option. UK Insurance appealed. Clayton v EUI Ltd (Admiral): Mr Clayton hired at credit hire rates. The judge allowed a 15% uplift for a 7-day rate (because mainstream rates were quoted on a weekly basis while the hire ran for slightly fewer than 7 days) and a 10% uplift for nil excess cover. EUI/Admiral appealed.

COURT OF APPEAL DECISION (Flaux LJ, Timothy Lloyd LJ, Stanley Burnton LJ): The Court dismissed UK Insurance's appeal (affirmed the nil excess treatment) and dismissed EUI/Admiral's appeal (affirmed the 7-day rate uplift and nil excess uplift).

STEVENS V EQUITY CORRECTLY DECIDED – BHR METHODOLOGY: The Court confirmed that Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management Ltd [2015] EWCA Civ 93 was correctly decided. The BHR is the lowest reasonable rate available from a mainstream or reputable local supplier. In assessing the BHR, evidence must be obtained of rates from mainstream hire companies (such as Enterprise, Hertz, Europcar, Avis, etc.) operating in the locality. The court should identify the lowest reasonable rate available from such suppliers, and that rate is used as the ceiling for recovery of credit hire charges.

NIL EXCESS / COLLISION DAMAGE WAIVER – SEPARATE ASSESSMENT: Where the claimant hired at nil excess (i.e. with no excess liability in the event of damage to the hire vehicle), but mainstream suppliers do not routinely provide a nil excess option (they typically provide standard CDW with an excess of several hundred or several thousand pounds), the nil excess element should be assessed separately from the BHR comparison exercise. The Court endorsed the approach that: (a) the BHR is assessed from mainstream suppliers on a like-for-like basis (i.e. on the same excess terms as those suppliers offer); and (b) the cost of nil excess cover is then assessed separately as a genuine additional benefit obtained. Where the credit hire company's own nil excess charge was the only viable option (i.e. stand-alone excess elimination products were not shown to be locally available), the CHO's nil excess charge (in McBride, £10/day = £888/£740+VAT) was the appropriate comparator. Ground 3A of UK Insurance's appeal was accordingly allowed to the extent of reinstating the nil excess upward adjustment.

STAND-ALONE EXCESS ELIMINATION PRODUCTS: The Court confirmed that stand-alone excess elimination policies (such as Questor and Insurance4carhire.com products) should be admitted as evidence and that such products represent the norm in the market. In an appropriate case, the cost of a stand-alone policy may be the correct measure of the additional benefit of nil excess cover, rather than the credit hire company's own nil excess charge. Parties should put in evidence of available stand-alone products and their costs. This is a potentially significant cost-saving for defendants.

7-DAY / WEEKLY RATE UPLIFT (CLAYTON): Where mainstream suppliers quote rates on a weekly or 7-day basis, and the hire period is slightly less than 7 days, a judge is entitled to apply a modest uplift to reflect the practical reality that the claimant would have had to pay for a full 7-day period. The judge's 15% uplift for this purpose in Clayton was upheld as a reasonable exercise of judicial discretion based on judicial knowledge and experience of the hire market. A 10% uplift for nil excess was also upheld.

PRACTICAL SIGNIFICANCE FOR CREDIT HIRE HANDLERS: (1) BHR methodology: always obtain evidence from multiple mainstream local suppliers; the lowest reasonable rate from those suppliers is the ceiling. (2) Nil excess: do not treat nil excess as automatically recoverable at the CHO's own rate. Put in evidence of stand-alone excess elimination products (Questor, Insurance4carhire.com) as these will typically be cheaper than the CHO's nil excess charge and will reduce the uplift available. (3) Weekly rates: where hire is for fewer than 7 days but mainstream suppliers only quote weekly rates, a modest uplift may be conceded; but scrutinise the claimed uplift percentage. (4) This case, together with Stevens v Equity, provides the definitive framework for BHR assessment that applies in virtually all credit hire disputes.
cases_cited: Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management Ltd [2015] EWCA Civ 93
Lagden v O'Connor [2003] UKHL 64
Copley v Lawn [2009] EWCA Civ 580
articles.summary: Confirms Stevens v Equity BHR methodology (lowest reasonable rate from mainstream/reputable local supplier). Nil excess/CDW treated separately from BHR comparison: assessed as additional benefit, with stand-alone excess elimination products (Questor, Insurance4carhire.com) being the appropriate benchmark. Weekly rate uplift of 15% upheld where mainstream suppliers only quote 7-day rates.

articles.title: West Midlands Travel Ltd v Aviva Insurance UK Ltd [2013] EWCA Civ 887
articles.source: Court of Appeal (Civil Division)
key_points:
BACKGROUND AND FACTS: West Midlands Travel Ltd operated a large bus fleet. One of its buses was damaged in an accident caused by Aviva's insured and was off the road for 31 days during repair. West Midlands Travel had spare capacity within its fleet and used other buses to cover the route, so it did not in fact hire a replacement. However it claimed general damages for loss of use. West Midlands Travel quantified its claim using the Confederation of Passenger Transport (CPT) "standing charge" formula, which produces a daily rate by dividing total annual overhead costs attributable to a vehicle by 365 days, giving a per-day figure (approximately £106.80/day = £3,310.80 for 31 days). The trial judge accepted this methodology and awarded £3,310.80. Aviva appealed.

COURT OF APPEAL DECISION (Moore-Bick, Rimer, Underhill LJJ): The Court of Appeal allowed the appeal on the measure of damages. The CPT standing charge formula was held to be the wrong methodology, and the case was remitted for re-assessment.

CPT STANDING CHARGE FORMULA – WRONG MEASURE: The Court held that the CPT standing charge, which is essentially an overhead allocation formula (taking the total overheads of running the fleet and dividing them proportionately among the fleet), is not an appropriate measure of loss of use for a vehicle that is off the road for a temporary period. The formula is designed for management accounting purposes to allocate costs across a fleet – it is not designed to measure the loss actually suffered when one vehicle is temporarily unavailable. The overhead costs allocated to the damaged bus (insurance, depot costs, administration, etc.) would have been incurred regardless of whether that particular bus was running or not, and they did not increase because one bus was off the road. Awarding the CPT standing charge would therefore overcompensate the claimant.

CORRECT MEASURE – ADMIRALTY APPROACH: The correct measure of loss of use for a vehicle owner with spare capacity is the "Admiralty" approach: (a) interest on the capital value of the damaged vehicle during the period of loss of use; plus (b) wasted expenses thrown away during that period (e.g. expenses specific to that vehicle – road tax, licence, insurance referable to that vehicle); plus (c) a depreciation element for the vehicle not being used productively. This approach, drawn from Admiralty cases concerning vessels, is adopted because it reflects the actual economic cost of having a capital asset temporarily unavailable – the cost of tying up capital that cannot be productively employed.

WHERE THERE IS NO SPARE CAPACITY: The Court distinguished cases where the claimant has no spare capacity and is genuinely unable to provide the service without a replacement vehicle. In such cases, the measure may appropriately be the cost of hiring a replacement or equivalent damages for loss of business. But where the claimant absorbs the loss within its existing resources, the Admiralty capital-cost approach applies.

CONSISTENCY WITH BEECHWOOD V HOYER: This decision is consistent with and complementary to Beechwood Birmingham Ltd v Hoyer Group UK Ltd [2010] EWCA Civ 647, which applied similar principles to a fleet of cars. Together, the two cases establish that for corporate and institutional fleet operators, loss of use is measured by the carrying cost of the capital asset (interest + depreciation + wasted expenses), not by reference to market hire rates or overhead allocation formulae.

PRACTICAL SIGNIFICANCE FOR CREDIT HIRE HANDLERS: (1) Where a corporate or fleet claimant seeks general damages for loss of use (rather than hire charges), challenge both the need for replacement and the measure. (2) The CPT standing charge formula (or any equivalent overhead allocation formula used by bus operators, logistics companies, or other fleet operators) should be challenged as the wrong measure. (3) Insist on the Admiralty approach: capital value, interest rate, period, plus wasted expenses. For most vehicles this will produce a significantly lower figure than overhead allocation formulae. (4) The principles in this case apply to any corporate vehicle owner or fleet operator, not just bus companies.
cases_cited: Beechwood Birmingham Ltd v Hoyer Group UK Ltd [2010] EWCA Civ 647
The Mediana [1900] AC 113
Lagden v O'Connor [2003] UKHL 64
articles.summary: The CPT standing charge formula (overhead allocation) is not the correct measure of loss of use for a bus operator with spare fleet capacity. The correct measure is the Admiralty approach: interest on capital value of the damaged vehicle plus wasted expenses plus depreciation. Case remitted for re-assessment. Consistent with Beechwood v Hoyer.

articles.title: Singh v Yaqubi [2013] EWCA Civ 23
articles.source: Court of Appeal (Civil Division)
key_points:
BACKGROUND AND FACTS: Mr Singh was a partner in a business that owned a fleet of approximately 7 prestigious vehicles including Rolls Royces and Bentleys. His Rolls Royce was damaged in an accident (a rear door dent) caused by the defendant Mr Yaqubi. During the repair period, Mr Singh hired firstly a Bentley and then a Rolls Royce from AEL on credit hire terms at approximately £2,000 per day for a total of 54 days, generating a claim of £92,953.90. The trial judge dismissed the claim for hire charges on the basis that the claimant had not proved a genuine need for the hire vehicle. Mr Singh appealed to the Court of Appeal.

COURT OF APPEAL DECISION (Pill, Black LJJ and Burnton LJ): The appeal was dismissed. The judge's finding that need for hire had not been proved was upheld.

NEED IS NOT SELF-PROVING: The central holding is that the need for a replacement hire vehicle is not self-proving, even where a valuable vehicle has been damaged and taken off the road. The burden of proving need for hire lies on the claimant. The mere fact that a vehicle is off the road does not, without more, prove that the claimant needed to hire a replacement at great expense. This principle applies with particular force where: (a) the claimant is a business or partnership with multiple vehicles in its fleet; (b) the claim is of very high value; and/or (c) the vehicle is a high-value, non-standard prestige vehicle that would not ordinarily be used for everyday commercial purposes.

SUFFICIENCY OF EVIDENCE: The Court held that the judge was entitled to require specific evidence of: (a) the actual use to which the Rolls Royce was put before the accident (what trips were made, what journeys, what purposes); and (b) the actual use made of the hire vehicle during the hire period. Vague and general evidence that the vehicle was used "for business" or for "client entertainment" or that the claimant "needed" a prestige vehicle was insufficient to discharge the burden of proof on a claim of nearly £93,000. The partnership had other prestigious vehicles in its fleet, and no adequate explanation was given for why those vehicles could not have served the claimant's needs during the repair period.

INFERENCE OF NEED: The Court acknowledged that in some cases an inference of need may legitimately be drawn – for example, where a private individual has their only vehicle damaged and obviously needs personal transport. However, this inference is available only in straightforward cases. For complex cases involving business claimants, multiple vehicles, very high-value claims, or unusual circumstances, the inference cannot simply be drawn without evidence. The more unusual or expensive the claim, the more the court is entitled to expect specific evidential support.

APPARENT BIAS (REJECTED): Mr Singh also argued that the trial judge had demonstrated apparent bias through various colourful observations during the trial about the claimant's lifestyle and the nature of the claim. The Court of Appeal rejected this. The judge's comments, while vivid, were made consciously and in the course of open judicial reasoning; they did not indicate pre-judgment or an inability to decide the matter impartially. An unusual turn of phrase or judicial colour in commentary does not constitute apparent bias.

PRACTICAL SIGNIFICANCE FOR CREDIT HIRE HANDLERS: (1) Always challenge need, especially for high-value claims by business or fleet claimants. Need is not established by the mere fact of vehicle damage. (2) Require the claimant to provide specific evidence of actual pre-accident use of the damaged vehicle and actual use of the hire vehicle during the hire period. (3) For partnerships, companies or individuals with multiple vehicles, investigate and put in evidence regarding the availability of other vehicles in the fleet. (4) For prestige vehicles hired at very high daily rates, the evidential threshold for need is commensurably higher. (5) This case and Beechwood v Hoyer together establish that business and fleet claimants cannot rely on inference of need in the way a private individual might; they must prove it specifically.
cases_cited: Beechwood Birmingham Ltd v Hoyer Group UK Ltd [2010] EWCA Civ 647
Lagden v O'Connor [2003] UKHL 64
articles.summary: Need for hire is not self-proving. For business/fleet claimants making high-value claims, specific evidence of actual pre-accident use and actual use of the hire vehicle is required. General assertions are insufficient. Rolls Royce hire at £2,000/day for 54 days (£92,953.90) failed because need was not established. Partnership had 7 vehicles and could have covered its needs from within the fleet.

articles.title: Akhtar v Boland [2014] EWCA Civ 943
articles.source: Court of Appeal (Civil Division)
key_points:
BACKGROUND AND FACTS: Mr Pervez Akhtar was involved in a road traffic accident with Mr Jordan Boland. The credit hire claim was allocated to the small claims track. Mr Akhtar sought to have the claim reallocated to the fast track, which was refused by District Judge Fox. He appealed to the Circuit Judge (HHJ Platts) who dismissed the appeal. He then appealed to the Court of Appeal. Permission to appeal was granted on the basis that the case raised an issue of general practical importance. In reality, a credit hire company stood behind Mr Akhtar and an insurer behind Mr Boland. The Court of Appeal dismissed the appeal. The separate question of costs of the Court of Appeal appeal was decided by written submissions.

COURT OF APPEAL DECISION (Gloster, Floyd LJJ and Sir Stanley Burnton): The Court held that no order for costs could be made in respect of the appeal to the Court of Appeal, because the no-costs rule in CPR 27.14 applied and precluded any costs order.

CPR 27.14 – THE NO-COSTS RULE EXTENDS TO ALL APPEALS INCLUDING COURT OF APPEAL: CPR 27.14 provides, in clear and unambiguous terms, that the court may not order a party to pay another party's costs, fees and expenses "including those relating to an appeal" in any case allocated to the small claims track, subject to limited exceptions (none of which applied here). Sir Stanley Burnton held that this provision is clear and extends to appeals at every level, including to the Court of Appeal itself. The provision cannot be construed as inapplicable to Court of Appeal proceedings. If it did not apply to Court of Appeal appeals, there would be power to award costs even where the allocation to the small claims track was undisputed and indisputable – an absurd result.

CPR 52.9A CANNOT OVERRIDE CPR 27.14: CPR 52.9A provides that, in any proceedings where costs recovery is normally limited or excluded at first instance, an appeal court may make an order LIMITING the recoverable costs of an appeal. This is a power to limit (or reduce to nil), not a power to award costs where CPR 27.14 otherwise prohibits a costs order. CPR 52.9A confers no power to award costs in circumstances where CPR 27.14 applies. The note in the White Book (Supreme Court Practice) to the effect that "second appeals against small claims decisions in the Court of Appeal are subject to full costs shifting" (as stated in the Jackson Review) was held to be incorrect. Rule 52.9A was introduced to mitigate costs exposure on appeal, not to enable courts to award full costs against the loser.
PRACTICAL

SIGNIFICANCE: This decision has important practical implications for credit hire litigation. The great majority of lower-value credit hire claims are allocated to the small claims track. The no-costs rule under CPR 27.14 means that: (1) Claimants can bring small claims appeals through to the Court of Appeal without risk of a full adverse costs order if they lose. (2) Defendants who successfully defend small claims appeals cannot recover their legal costs, even from appeals reaching the Court of Appeal. (3) The decision creates an asymmetry which favours claimants (typically credit hire companies acting in the claimant's name) who can pursue appeals at low financial risk. (4) A defendant insurer that successfully resists a Court of Appeal appeal in a small claims track credit hire case will receive no costs for doing so. This structural feature of the small claims regime has influenced the conduct of credit hire appeals litigation, particularly by credit hire companies pursuing test cases at appellate level with limited downside risk.

CONTEXTUAL NOTE: The appeal itself (on track allocation) was dismissed. The substantive principles about when small credit hire claims should be reallocated to the fast track were not developed in this judgment, which concerned only costs. The Court acknowledged the manifestly unfair position that if the appellant's appeal on track allocation had succeeded, costs would have followed, but since it failed, no costs order could be made – a "one way" risk for the appellant – but held it was powerless to remedy this given the clear terms of CPR 27.14.
cases_cited: Dimond v Lovell [2002] 1 AC 384
articles.summary: CPR 27.14 (the no-costs rule for small claims) extends to all appeals including to the Court of Appeal itself. CPR 52.9A allows courts to limit appeal costs but does not override CPR 27.14 to award costs where that rule prohibits them. Credit hire cases in the small claims track therefore carry no adverse costs risk even on Court of Appeal appeal.

articles.title: Aldred v Cham [2019] EWCA Civ 1780
articles.source: Court of Appeal (Civil Division)
key_points:
BACKGROUND AND FACTS: Master Tyreese Cham (a 7-year-old child) was injured in a road traffic accident on 5 September 2015 caused by Mr Aldred. The claim was started under the RTA Pre-Action Protocol (PAP) but fell out of the PAP process when the defendant denied liability. Liability was subsequently accepted and the claim settled for £2,000. Because the claimant was a child, counsel's advice on the settlement was required under CPR Part 21 and Practice Direction 21 (which requires a merits opinion on any proposed settlement involving a child or protected party, unless the case is very clear). The advice cost £150. The settlement was subsequently approved by the court. The defendant was ordered to pay costs. The dispute concerned whether the £150 counsel's fee was recoverable as a disbursement in addition to the fixed recoverable costs under CPR Part 45 Section IIIA (which applies to cases that began under the PAP but no longer continue thereunder). Section IIIA (rule 45.29I) contained a catch-all provision at r.45.29I(2)(h) for "any other disbursement reasonably incurred due to a particular feature of the dispute".

COURT OF APPEAL DECISION (McCombe, Coulson, Nicola Davies LJJ, judgment by Coulson LJ): The Court of Appeal allowed the appeal by Mr Aldred, holding that the counsel's fee was NOT recoverable as a disbursement in addition to the fixed recoverable costs.

ISSUE 1 – "PARTICULAR FEATURE OF THE DISPUTE": The first and determinative issue was whether the claimant being a child constituted a "particular feature of the dispute" within r.45.29I(2)(h). Coulson LJ (with whom Nicola Davies LJ agreed) held that it did not. The "particular features of the dispute" are features of the accident and litigation itself – matters such as how the accident happened, whether the defendant was to blame, the nature, scope and extent of the injuries and their consequences. Examples of disbursements that might arise from particular features of the dispute would be: an accident reconstruction expert (because of unusual circumstances of the accident), multiple medical expert reports (because of complex injuries), or specialist legal advice (because of unusual legal complexity in the claim). In contrast, the fact that the claimant is a child, or cannot speak English, or requires an intermediary, are characteristics of the claimant as an individual. They are not generated by or linked in any way to the dispute itself. Accordingly, the requirement to obtain counsel's advice because the claimant is a child is caused by a characteristic of the claimant, not by a particular feature of the dispute, and falls outside the exception.

ISSUE 2 – FEE NOT SEPARATELY RECOVERABLE IN ANY EVENT: Even if the claimant's age were treated as a "particular feature" (i.e. if Issue 1 were decided in the respondent's favour), the counsel's fee would still not be recoverable in addition to the fixed recoverable costs. The item of work (advising on a child settlement) is a routine, predictable step that arises in every Section IIIA case where the claimant is a child. It must therefore be treated as within the global sum of fixed recoverable costs in Table 6B, even though Table 6B does not expressly identify it. Fixed costs operate on the assumption that all work ordinarily required up to a given stage is included in the stated sum; it is not possible to cherry-pick particular work items as disbursements merely because Table 6B does not expressly mention them. The "catch-all" disbursements provision is for genuinely unusual and case-specific items, not for standard steps in common claim types.

SIGNIFICANCE OF THE "PARTICULAR FEATURE OF THE DISPUTE" TEST: This decision establishes that the "particular feature of the dispute" exception in the fixed recoverable costs regime must be interpreted narrowly. It refers to features of the dispute in the sense of the specific accident, injuries and contested issues between the parties – not to characteristics of the claimant. The correct focus is always on what makes THIS dispute unusual, not on who the claimant is. Disbursements that arise because of routine characteristics of claimants (e.g. being a child, being a non-English speaker) are not recoverable as additional disbursements under the catch-all provision in cases falling under Sections II, III or IIIA of CPR Part 45.

PRACTICAL SIGNIFICANCE FOR CREDIT HIRE HANDLERS: While this case does not concern credit hire rates directly, it is highly relevant to the costs recovery framework for RTA claims, including credit hire claims. It confirms that: (1) the fixed recoverable costs regime must be applied strictly and the catch-all disbursement exceptions are narrow; (2) characteristic features of a claimant (including being a child or a vulnerable person) are not "particular features of the dispute" for the purpose of recovering additional disbursements; and (3) routine steps in common claim types are deemed included in the fixed costs, even if not expressly itemised.
cases_cited: Qader v Esure Services Ltd [2016] EWCA Civ 1109
Sharpe v Leeds City Council [2017] 4 WLR 98
Dockerill v Tullett [2012] 1 WLR 2092
articles.summary: A claimant being a child is not a 'particular feature of the dispute' within CPR r.45.29I(2)(h). The 'particular features of the dispute' are features of the accident/litigation itself, not personal characteristics of the claimant. Counsel's fee for advising on a child settlement under Section IIIA is not recoverable as an additional disbursement – it is deemed within the fixed recoverable costs in Table 6B.

articles.title: Dickinson v Tesco plc (and Simmonds, Verley, Moonsam) [2013] EWCA Civ 36
articles.source: Court of Appeal (Civil Division)
key_points:
BACKGROUND AND FACTS: These four conjoined appeals/applications (Simmonds v Stewart Alexander Group; Dickinson v Tesco; Verley v O'Neill; Moonsam v Axa) arose from the activities of Autofocus Limited ("AF"), a company that provided "expert" evidence to motor insurers about basic hire rates in credit hire cases. AF maintained a computer database called the "Instant Rates Information Service" (IRIS) from which it produced three types of report: (1) Case Specific Reports (CSRs) – purported telephone surveys of local car hire companies; (2) Small Claims Rate Information Packs (SCRIPs) – combining IRIS data with internet printouts; and (3) Detailed Rates Surveys (DRSs) – more elaborate reports also using IRIS data and website research. Between 2002 and 2010, AF provided reports in over 100 such cases per month, which were widely accepted by courts as honest evidence of the BHR.

THE AUTOFOCUS FRAUD EXPOSED: Mr Steven Evans, CEO of Accident Exchange Limited (AEL – the credit hire company whose rates were being challenged by AF evidence), became suspicious about AF's methodology after the Glossop v Salvesen case in 2009. Investigation revealed that: (1) AF's IRIS database was "mostly nothing more than a fabrication and concoction based on a far more limited and restricted set of survey-based figures extrapolated (and often simply invented)"; (2) AF surveyors routinely did not make the telephone calls they claimed to have made; (3) reports were prepared by one person but signed off as the work of another; (4) AF employees habitually excluded "unhelpful" (higher) rates from their reports; (5) internet material was manipulated to support lower BHR figures; and (6) AF's management were aware of the manipulation. Ms Helen Wysall (an AF rate surveyor) pleaded guilty to contempt of court for submitting false reports. AF went into liquidation in 2010. AEL subsequently obtained access to AF's complete business records on a "Mirror Disk" and commissioned full analysis of 867,521 items, completing that analysis in September 2012.

COURT OF APPEAL DECISION ON FRESH EVIDENCE (Aikens LJ, Moore-Bick LJ, Rimer LJ; judgment by Aikens LJ): In Simmonds, Verley and Moonsam, the fresh evidence from the Mirror Disk analysis was admitted and the appeals allowed. In Dickinson, the appeal was also allowed because the fresh evidence showed the excess waiver figures in the SCRIP report were manipulated, even though the judge had primarily relied on an independent internet quote from Enterprise rather than on AF's telephone survey evidence.

LADD V MARSHALL AND ADMISSION OF FRESH EVIDENCE: The conditions for admitting fresh evidence (Ladd v Marshall [1954] 1 WLR 1489) are: (1) the evidence could not have been obtained with reasonable diligence at trial; (2) it would probably have had an important influence on the result; and (3) it must be apparently credible. The Court held all three criteria were satisfied. The full picture of AF's dishonesty only emerged from analysis of the Mirror Disk material, which was not available at the earlier trials. Although AEL had suspected AF's dishonesty from 2009 and had made allegations in the Glossop case, it did not have, and could not have had, the depth and breadth of evidence of systematic fraud that the Mirror Disk provided. The defendants had paid the penalty for relying on AF's dishonest evidence; the overriding objective required that the fresh evidence be admitted.

FORM OF RETRIALS: In all four cases retrials were ordered, limited to the quantum of recoverable hire charges. Both parties were permitted to adduce evidence at the retrials on the applicable hire rate – the defendants were not debarred from putting in fresh BHR evidence merely because the original AF evidence had been dishonest (that would have been disproportionate). Cases were re-allocated to the multi-track for the purposes of the retrials.

BHR EVIDENCE – INTEGRITY REQUIREMENTS: The judgment contains important guidance on the proper approach to BHR evidence. BHR evidence must be genuinely independent and honestly prepared. Internet quotations taken directly from third-party provider websites are generally reliable (and may stand even where AF telephone survey evidence is discredited). However, where AF manipulated or selectively presented internet material (as with excess waiver charge figures in Attachment 1 to SCRIP reports), even the internet-based elements may be tainted. Defendants putting forward BHR surveys must ensure their witnesses are genuinely responsible for the surveys they describe: evidence prepared by one person but attributed to another (as happened repeatedly in AF reports) is deeply unsatisfactory.

PRACTICAL SIGNIFICANCE FOR CREDIT HIRE HANDLERS: (1) Any historic BHR evidence produced by Autofocus Limited should be treated as unreliable and should not be relied upon. (2) Rate survey evidence must be genuinely independent, honestly researched and properly attributed to the actual author. (3) Internet quotations from mainstream hire companies (Enterprise, Hertz, etc.) are generally reliable, but only if the excess waiver/CDW elements are also accurately reflected. (4) The "Mirror Disk" litigation has now concluded but the principles it established remain important: courts will not tolerate fabricated or manipulated BHR evidence, and where such evidence is proved, appeals will be allowed and cases retried even years after judgment. (5) This case, together with Stevens v Equity and McBride v UKI, forms part of the definitive framework for BHR evidence integrity.
cases_cited: Dimond v Lovell [2002] 1 AC 384
Lagden v O'Connor [2003] UKHL 64
Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management Ltd [2015] EWCA Civ 93
McBride v UK Insurance Ltd [2017] EWCA Civ 144
Noble v Owens [2010] 1 WLR 2491
Ladd v Marshall [1954] 1 WLR 1489
articles.summary: The Autofocus fraud case. Autofocus Ltd systematically fabricated BHR evidence (IRIS database, SCRIP and DRS reports) used in hundreds of credit hire cases. Mirror Disk analysis proved systemic dishonesty. Fresh evidence admitted (Ladd v Marshall satisfied). Appeals allowed in all four cases; retrials ordered limited to quantum of hire charges. BHR evidence must be genuinely independent, honestly prepared and properly attributed.

articles.title: Bent v Highways & Utilities Construction [2010] EWCA Civ 292 and Pattni v First Leicester Buses / Bent No 2 [2011] EWCA Civ 1384
articles.source: Court of Appeal (Civil Division)
key_points:
BACKGROUND AND CASES: These are two connected Court of Appeal decisions arising from the same underlying Bent v Highways litigation, with Pattni v First Leicester Buses decided in the same conjoined judgment as Bent No 2.

BENT NO 1 [2010] EWCA CIV 292 – FACTS: In February 2007, Mr Darren Bent (the professional footballer, then playing for Tottenham Hotspur), aged 23, had his Mercedes V12 6.3 AMG CLS (£72,000) damaged in an accident caused by the defendants. Although Mr Bent had another car (a left-hand drive Cadillac), the judge accepted his reason for not using it. Mr Bent hired an Aston Martin DB9 (worth ~£105,000) from AEL on credit hire terms for 94 days. Total hire charges: £63,406.90. Mr Bent was pecunious (could have hired without credit terms). The trial judge (HHJ Yelton) awarded the full credit hire sum because he concluded he had no reliable evidence of the spot rate for an equivalent car in February 2007: the only evidence was for 2008/2009 rates, and he declined to extrapolate. The defendants appealed.

BENT NO 1 – HELD (Jacob, Leveson LJJ, Briggs J): Appeal allowed; retrial ordered. The trial judge was wrong to refuse to use evidence of spot rates from a later date. Evidence of hire rates from a year or so later than the relevant date is likely to throw considerable light on what the spot rate would have been at the time. Working with comparables and making appropriate adjustments for the passage of time is a standard judicial exercise in valuation matters. A judge should not be hypnotised by the need to find an exact spot rate for an almost exactly comparable car. The replacement car need only be in the "same broad range of quality and nature" as the damaged car. There may be a bracket of spot rates for cars rather "better" and rather "worse"; a judge who considered that bracket and aimed for a reasonable average would not be going wrong.

BENT NO 2 / PATTNI [2011] EWCA CIV 1384 – PANEL (Pill, Moore-Bick, Aikens LJJ; judgment by Aikens LJ). This is the second Court of Appeal hearing in the Bent litigation (after a retrial before HHJ Plumstead) decided together with Pattni v First Leicester Buses.

BENT NO 2 – FACTS: At the retrial, HHJ Plumstead assessed the BHR at £396/day (taking a midway range from 2009 evidence for comparable cars, applying a 12% discount for a 28-day hire period). AEL/Mr Bent appealed, arguing the judge should have used direct 2007 evidence for Aston Martin DB9s (which showed a range of £470–£695/day) and that the claimant was entitled to the actual BHR for the car actually hired, not an average of a broader range.

BENT NO 2 – HELD: Appeal allowed; case remitted AGAIN. The judge erred. There WAS direct evidence in 2007 of BHR figures for Aston Martin DB9s: a range of £470–£695/day from car hire company archives and internet pages. The judge should have focused on this direct evidence. The aim of the BHR assessment is always to find the BHR for the type of car actually hired, not to average across a broader range. Aikens LJ clarified the correct methodology: the BHR is an objective figure – what would the claimant have paid had he gone to a non-credit car hire company for the same or broadly equivalent car in the same geographical area? This is not a subjective enquiry (what Mr Bent himself would have obtained); it is an objective finding of fact based on the available evidence. Jacob LJ's "bracket" approach from Bent No 1 was clarified: it is a technique for finding the BHR for the ACTUAL car hired where exact evidence is unavailable, not a licence to substitute a different (cheaper) car or to use an average. The aim is always to find the BHR for the car actually hired.

PATTNI – FACTS: Mr Pattni hired an Audi R8 (~£500/day, credit hire) as a substitute for his Porsche 911 Coupé. BHR found to be £370.50/day. Mr Pattni claimed interest on the BHR sum as an additional element of loss, relying on the credit hire agreement which imposed an obligation to pay interest on outstanding hire charges from the end of the hire until the claim was settled.

PATTNI – HELD: Interest on credit hire charges is NOT recoverable as a separate head of loss by a pecunious claimant. The interest charge in the credit hire agreement is the cost of the "additional benefit" of credit – being relieved of the necessity to pay the hire charges upfront. Under the analysis of Lords Hoffmann and Hobhouse in Dimond v Lovell, such additional benefits (credit, claim handling, risk-bearing) are not part of the recoverable loss for loss of use; they must be stripped out of the credit hire rate in arriving at the BHR. A pecunious claimant who is assumed to have hired at the BHR (paying in advance) would not incur any interest liability because he would have paid for the hire as he went. No actual interest payments had been made by Mr Pattni; there was no proved loss. Neither contractual interest liability (no actual payment made; no proved loss flowing from the liability) nor statutory interest under s.69 CCA 1984 (discretionary, and the discretion was properly exercised against awarding it) was recoverable.

THE PREFERRED TERM IS "BHR" NOT "SPOT RATE": Aikens LJ (at para 34 of Pattni/Bent No 2) recommended that the term "spot rate" should no longer be used in credit hire cases because it is a misnomer properly applicable to commodity and freight markets. The term "basic hire rate" or "BHR" more accurately describes what is being assessed: the rate at which a broadly equivalent car could be hired by paying in advance (without credit).

PRACTICAL SIGNIFICANCE FOR CREDIT HIRE HANDLERS: (1) Where the BHR for a high-value or prestige vehicle is in issue, direct evidence of rates for that specific type of car at the relevant date should be sought; approximate comparisons with different (cheaper) car types should be challenged. (2) Evidence from dates slightly before or after the hire period can be used with appropriate adjustment – the judge is not required to refuse assessment in the absence of exact contemporaneous data. (3) The BHR assessment is objective: what would a notional hirer have paid at a non-credit car hire company for the same type of car in the same area? (4) Interest charges built into credit hire agreements are NOT separately recoverable as an additional head of loss from the defendant. (5) "Spot rate" and "BHR" are used interchangeably in older cases but the preferred modern term is BHR.
cases_cited: Dimond v Lovell [2002] 1 AC 384
Lagden v O'Connor [2003] UKHL 64
Burdis v Livsey [2003] QB 36
Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management Ltd [2015] EWCA Civ 93
McBride v UK Insurance Ltd [2017] EWCA Civ 144
articles.summary: Bent No 1: evidence of BHR from dates shortly after the hire period can be used with adjustment; a 'bracket' approach is permissible where exact data is unavailable; replacement car need only be in the same broad range of quality. Bent No 2/Pattni: BHR assessment is objective (find the BHR for the car actually hired); claimant entitled to actual BHR not an average; interest on credit hire charges is NOT recoverable by a pecunious claimant as it is a non-recoverable 'additional benefit'. Preferred term is BHR not 'spot rate'.

articles.title: Tescher v Direct Accident Management Ltd & AXA v Spectra Drive Ltd [2025] EWCA Civ 733 – Comprehensive Guidance on Non-Party Costs Orders Against Credit Hire Companies
articles.source: Tescher v Direct Accident Management Ltd & AXA Insurance UK PLC v Spectra Drive Ltd [2025] EWCA Civ 733, Coulson LJ, Lady Justice Nicola Davies and Lord Justice Birss, judgment by Birss LJ, 13 June 2025
articles.key_points:
BACKGROUND AND TWO CONJOINED APPEALS. These two conjoined Court of Appeal appeals raised the question: when a credit hire case fails, in what circumstances should the non-party credit hire company be made liable for the defendant's costs? In each case, following a road accident, a claim was issued including personal injury and credit hire costs. QOCS protection under CPR Part 44 prevented enforcement of the costs orders against the claimants. The defendants applied for non-party costs orders against the credit hire companies (DAML in Tescher; Spectra Drive in AXA v Spectra). In DAML, DJ Jeffs refused the order on the basis that DAML was not the "real party" and had not caused additional costs. In Spectra, HHJ Gargan on appeal refused the non-party costs order partly on the basis that AXA had had "good fortune" in escaping a judgment because the claimant had been pressured into discontinuing. Coulson LJ gave permission for both appeals to be heard together by the Court of Appeal. Lord Justice Birss gave the leading judgment; Lady Justice Nicola Davies and Lord Justice Coulson agreed.

THE LEGAL FRAMEWORK. Birss LJ reviewed the authorities chronologically: (a) Giles v Thompson [1994] 1 AC 142 — credit hire agreements are lawful; claimant has a real legal liability for hire charges even if suspended as to enforcement; company makes profits from hiring, not litigation; (b) Lagden v O'Connor [2004] 1 AC 1067 — credit hire charges recoverable for impecunious claimants at rates above BHR; (c) Symphony v Hodgson [1994] QB 179 — history and principles of non-party costs orders under s.51 SCA 1981; (d) Dymocks Franchise Systems v Todd [2004] UKPC 39 (Lord Brown) — "exceptional" simply means outside the ordinary run of cases; ultimate question is whether it is just to make the order; a non-party who not merely funds but substantially controls proceedings or is to benefit from them will ordinarily be required to pay costs if the proceedings fail; (e) Farrell v Birmingham City Council [2009] EWCA Civ 769 — Court of Appeal upheld non-party costs order against a credit hire company: the hire was the essential catalyst of the proceedings and the company was in a real sense the instigator of the litigation; (f) Deutsche Bank v Sebastian [2016] EWCA Civ 23 — the only immutable principle in non-party costs orders is that the discretion must be exercised justly; (g) Excalibur Ventures v Texas Keystone [2016] EWCA Civ 1144 — commercial funders who invest expecting a return will ordinarily pay costs if the funded claim fails; (h) XYZ v Travelers Insurance [2019] UKSC 48 (Lord Briggs) — the focus of XYZ v Travelers is expressly the liability insurance context; its causation analysis at [80] was directed at intermeddler cases, not the real party context; not directly applicable to credit hire; (i) Goknur v Aytacli [2021] EWCA Civ 1031 (Coulson LJ) — two real parties can coexist; look at the matter in the round and consider the underlying economic reality; (j) Mee v Jones (Select Car Rentals) [2017] EWHC 1434 QB (Turner J) — r.44.16(2)(a) applies to credit hire; PD44 para 12.2 is no more than a statement of the obvious; (k) Kindertons v Murtagh [2024] EWHC 471 (KB) (Turner J) — non-party costs order at 80% upheld on appeal.

CPR r.44.16(2)(a), (3) AND PD44 PARAGRAPH 12. CPR r.44.16(2)(a) identifies proceedings including a claim made for the financial benefit of a person other than the claimant as an exception to QOCS enforcement restrictions. CPR r.44.16(3) expressly contemplates a non-party costs order against such a person. PD44 paragraph 12.2 identifies credit hire as an example of such a claim. Paras 12.5 and 12.6 provide practical guidance: para 12.5(a) — where r.44.16(2)(a) applies the court will usually make an order that the other person pays costs; para 12.6 — the QOCS cap (aggregate of damages and interest) will not normally apply in such cases.

THE TWO-STAGE APPROACH. Birss LJ adopted a two-stage approach: Stage 1 — whether a non-party costs order of some kind should be made (jurisdiction); Stage 2 — the appropriate costs order including quantum.

STAGE 1 — KEY PRINCIPLES. (i) Credit hire claims are by nature claims for the financial benefit of the credit hire company (r.44.16(2)(a)). (ii) The deferral arrangement — where payment of hire charges is deferred until damages are recovered — combined with the claimant's impecuniosity, makes litigation (or settlement) the only realistic means by which the credit hire company will be paid. The credit hire agreement is therefore a fundamental cause of the defendant's legal costs. This satisfies the causation requirement. A strict "but for" test is not required. (iii) The credit hire company satisfies the real party test: it need not be "the only real party" — it is sufficient that it is "a real party" in very important and critical respects (Dymocks v Todd, Lord Brown). (iv) Who appoints the solicitors is generally irrelevant. Control derives from the structure of the hire arrangements themselves. (v) The credit hire company is NOT analogous to lawyers acting under a CFA: Hodgson v Imperial Tobacco [1998] 1 WLR 1056 — the CFA does not bind the client to pursue the claim, and the lawyer's fees are not the subject of the claim. (vi) XYZ v Travelers is focused specifically on liability insurers; Lord Briggs' causation analysis was directed at intermeddler cases, not real party cases. (vii) PD44 para 12.2 accurately states that credit hire falls within r.44.16(2)(a). (viii) Absent some special reason, where a credit hire claim fails and QOCS applies, a non-party costs order against the credit hire company is likely.

STAGE 2 — QUANTUM. The three obvious options are: (a) all the costs of the litigation; (b) apportionment based on the relative sizes of the credit hire and PI claims; (c) the extra costs attributable to the credit hire claim. Where the credit hire claim is several times larger than the PI claim, an order for all costs would be likely absent a special feature.

DAML OUTCOME. Credit hire charges (£19,633) were over 85% of total special damages and several times the value of the PI claim. The real party test and causation were satisfied. DJ Jeffs was wrong. DAML was ordered to pay all the defendant's costs.

SPECTRA OUTCOME. HHJ Gargan's conclusion that Spectra was the principal beneficiary and a cause of AXA's costs was correct. The "good fortune" point was wrong: applying Nelson's Yard Management Co v Eziefula [2013] EWCA Civ 235, the prospect that the claim might have succeeded does not justify refusing a non-party costs order on discontinuance. The DDJ's original order (Spectra to pay 65% of AXA's costs) was restored.

CONCLUSION. Both appeals allowed: DAML to pay all defendant's costs; Spectra to pay 65% of AXA's costs. The alleged link between credit hire companies and solicitors (DAML/Bond Turner; Spectra/DGM) was noted but not taken into account — non-party costs orders would be the likely result in the circumstances described regardless of such links.
