articles.summary: Landmark Court of Appeal guidance (Birss LJ, June 2025) on non-party costs orders against credit hire companies when credit hire claims fail and QOCS protects the claimant. Two-stage test: (1) jurisdiction — credit hire claims are by nature for the financial benefit of the hire company (r.44.16(2)(a)); the deferral structure makes litigation inevitable and constitutes a fundamental cause of the defendant's costs; hire company is a 'real party' in important respects; absence of express control over solicitor appointment is irrelevant. (2) Quantum — where credit hire claim is several times larger than PI claim, order for all costs likely. Both appeals allowed: DAML ordered to pay all defendant's costs; Spectra ordered to pay 65% of defendant's costs. Non-party costs orders will be 'likely' in such cases absent special circumstances.

articles.title: Irving v Morgan Sindall PLC [2018] EWHC 1147 (QB) – Contingent Liability and the Impecuniosity Test
articles.source: Irving v Morgan Sindall PLC [2018] EWHC 1147 (QB), Mr Justice Turner, 15 May 2018
articles.key_points:
BACKGROUND AND FACTS. The claimant's Ford Ka was written off in a road accident caused entirely by the defendant's negligence. She hired a replacement vehicle on credit for 132 days at £152/day, accumulating hire charges of £20,109.60. The credit hire company's terms deferred her liability to pay until her damages claim was resolved; she was told that if she lost there would be "nothing to pay" — consistent with a no win no fee arrangement explained by her solicitors. At trial the judge dismissed the credit hire claim: first, on the basis that the claimant had no liability for the charges (they were contingent and she had been told she would never have to pay); second, in any event, she was not impecunious. Turner J allowed the appeal on both grounds.

ISSUE 1 — CONTINGENT LIABILITY. A contingent liability — one contingent upon recovering damages — is nonetheless a real liability the incurring of which constitutes a real loss to the motorist. Turner J reviewed: Giles v Thompson [1994] 1 AC 142 (Lord Mustill at p154C–155A and p155B–E: liability for car hire rests on the motorist throughout, albeit suspended as to enforcement; it is "a real liability, the incurring of which constitutes a real loss to the motorist"); Giles v Thompson [1993] CA (Sir Thomas Bingham MR at p349); Harlow & Jones Ltd v Panex (International) Ltd [1967] 2 Lloyd's Rep 509; Donnelly v Joyce [1974] QB 454; McAll v Brooks [1984] RTR 99; Cosemar SA v Marimarna Shipping [1990] 2 Lloyd's Rep 323; and Wakeling v Harrington [2007] EWCH 1184 (Ch) (Mann J — a non-recourse loan remains a loan even though repayment is restricted to designated funds). None of these authorities supported the proposition that a contingent debt cannot be recovered as damages. There is no double recovery: monies recovered go to the credit hire company to extinguish the debt; the only windfall from non-recovery would be enjoyed by the defendant's insurer. The assurances given to the claimant that she would not have to pay if she lost did not make the hire "free" — the liability was real and contingent, not a gratuitous benefit.

ISSUE 2 — IMPECUNIOSITY: TEST AND APPLICATION. The test for impecuniosity derives from Lagden v O'Connor [2004] 1 AC 1067 (Lord Nicholls): inability to pay car hire charges without making sacrifices the claimant could not reasonably be expected to make. The claimant earned £472/month basic (up to £700/month with overtime), had ISA savings of about £250, a Graduate Bank Account overdrawn by over £700, and a credit card with a limit of £500. Her written-off car had been worth £775. The judge below concluded she could have raised about £900 and bought a replacement, so she was pecunious. Turner J held this was wrong for two reasons: First, the claimant could not be expected to have bought a replacement car immediately — at least a fortnight would be needed before the car was written off and confirmed, then a further fortnight to source a replacement. Over those four weeks, even a pecunious claimant would have been entitled to hire (cost approximately £700). Capital plus hire costs combined far exceeded £900. Second, expecting the claimant to deplete all her savings and maximise credit left her dangerously exposed to any unexpected financial difficulty during the four-plus months the hire continued. "Impecuniosity need not amount to penury." The judge below erred and the claimant was properly to be regarded as impecunious.

RESULT. Appeal allowed. The judgment was increased by £20,109.60 to reflect full recovery of credit hire charges.
cases_cited: Irving v Morgan Sindall PLC [2018] EWHC 1147 (QB)
Giles v Thompson [1994] 1 AC 142
Giles v Thompson [1993] CA (Sir Thomas Bingham MR)
Lagden v O'Connor [2004] 1 AC 1067
Harlow & Jones Ltd v Panex (International) Ltd [1967] 2 Lloyd's Rep 509
Donnelly v Joyce [1974] QB 454
McAll v Brooks [1984] RTR 99
Cosemar SA v Marimarna Shipping Co Ltd [1990] 2 Lloyd's Rep 323
Wakeling v Harrington [2007] EWCH 1184 (Ch)
Zurich Insurance plc v Umerji [2014] EWCA Civ 357
Beechwood Birmingham Ltd v Hoyer Group UK Ltd [2010] RTR 33
articles.summary: Turner J allowed the appeal on two grounds: (1) a contingent credit hire liability (where charges are only payable if damages are recovered) is still a real loss recoverable in damages — the assurance that charges would be written off on a failed claim did not make the hire 'free'; (2) the claimant was impecunious — the judge below erred by not accounting for the time lag before a replacement car could be purchased and hire costs in the interim; combining capital and hire costs exceeded what she could raise. 'Impecuniosity need not amount to penury.' Full hire charges of £20,109.60 awarded.

articles.title: Howlett v Davies [2017] EWCA Civ 1696 – Fundamental Dishonesty: Meaning and Pleading Requirements
articles.source: Howlett v Davies [2017] EWCA Civ 1696, Lewison, Beatson and Newey LJJ, judgment by Newey LJ, 21 November 2017
articles.key_points:
BACKGROUND. The claimants alleged they were injured in a road traffic accident. Ageas Insurance defended on behalf of the defendant driver. The defence did not positively plead fraud but adopted a hybrid approach: it denied the accident occurred as described, denied the claimed injuries, and put the claimants' credibility squarely in issue by listing a number of matters indicative of a staged or contrived accident. At first instance the district judge found the claim to be fundamentally dishonest under CPR 44.16(1) and displaced QOCS protection. The claimants appealed on two grounds: (a) that "fundamentally dishonest" required dishonesty going to the root of the entire claim (not merely a significant part); and (b) that a finding of fundamental dishonesty could not properly be made on a defence that did not expressly plead fraud.

MEANING OF "FUNDAMENTALLY DISHONEST". Newey LJ confirmed and elaborated on the meaning given to "fundamentally dishonest" by Judge Moloney QC in Gosling v Hailo and Screwfix [2014]. The word "fundamentally" is used to distinguish between dishonesty that goes to the root of the whole claim or a substantial part of it (which is fundamental) and dishonesty that is merely incidental or collateral to the claim (which is not). In a claim where the accident itself is fraudulent — staged or contrived — any dishonesty necessarily goes to the root of the entire claim and is fundamental. The court rejected the argument that "fundamentally dishonest" required the entirety of the claim to be dishonest; dishonesty affecting a substantial part suffices.

PLEADING REQUIREMENTS — HYBRID DEFENCE. The claimants argued that a finding of fundamental dishonesty was impermissible because the defence had not expressly pleaded fraud. Newey LJ rejected this, following Kearsley v Klarfeld [2005] EWCA Civ 1510. The overriding principle is whether the defence adequately forewarns the claimant of the case they are required to meet. A defendant is not compelled to use the word "fraud" or to make an express allegation of fundamental dishonesty as long as the pleaded defence is sufficient to put the claimant on notice that their credibility and the circumstances of the accident are fundamentally in issue. Ageas' hybrid defence — which denied the accident occurred as alleged, put all aspects of the claims in issue, and listed specific matters indicative of a staged accident — was sufficient. The fact that it did not use the word "fraud" did not preclude a finding of fundamental dishonesty. This is consistent with CPR r.16.5(2) and the general principle that parties must know the case against them, without requiring particularity beyond what is necessary.

IMPLICATIONS. Defendants do not need to use the word "fraud" to obtain a finding of fundamental dishonesty. A well-drafted denial and hybrid defence which puts credibility squarely in issue and particularises the matters relied upon as indicators of dishonesty (inconsistencies in accounts of the accident, prior relationships between witnesses, inconsistencies in claimed injuries) will suffice. This reflects the practical reality that defendants often cannot prove fraud affirmatively at the pleading stage but have legitimate grounds to challenge the claim's credibility.

RESULT. Appeal dismissed. The finding of fundamental dishonesty was upheld.
cases_cited: Howlett v Davies [2017] EWCA Civ 1696
Gosling v Hailo and Screwfix Direct [2014] (Judge Moloney QC)
Kearsley v Klarfeld [2005] EWCA Civ 1510
Wagenaar v Weekend Travel Ltd [2014] EWCA Civ 1105
legislation_cited: CPR r.44.16(1) (fundamental dishonesty exception to QOCS)
articles.summary: The Court of Appeal (Newey LJ) confirmed: (1) 'fundamentally dishonest' means dishonesty going to the root of the whole claim or a substantial part of it — not merely collateral dishonesty; (2) a defence need not expressly plead 'fraud' to support a finding of fundamental dishonesty — it suffices that the defence adequately forewarns the claimant of the case to be met (Kearsley v Klarfeld). A hybrid defence denying the accident occurred as alleged, putting credibility in issue and particularising indicators of staging, was sufficient. Appeal dismissed.

articles.title: Correia v Williams [2022] EWHC 2824 (KB) – Witness Statement Compliance for Non-English Speakers
articles.source: Correia v Williams [2022] EWHC 2824 (KB), Mr Justice Garnham, 9 November 2022
articles.key_points:
BACKGROUND AND FACTS. Mr Correia, a Portuguese national who had lived in the UK since 2002, brought a road traffic personal injury claim. His solicitor drafted a 126-paragraph, 36-page witness statement in English based on instructions given in Portuguese. The statement included a statement of truth signed by Mr Correia in English, and a Certificate of Translation from the principal of his firm certifying that it had been read back in Portuguese. At trial before HHJ Gerald, the defendant objected to admissibility on the ground that the statement had been drafted in English rather than in Mr Correia's own language (Portuguese) contrary to CPR PD32.

THE CPR REQUIREMENTS. Garnham J set out the relevant rules. CPR r.22.1(1)(c): witness statements must be verified by a statement of truth. CPR PD22 para 2.4: the statement of truth must be in the witness's own language. CPR r.32.4(1): a witness statement must be in the witness's own words. CPR PD32 para 18.1: a witness statement must be in the witness's own words and drafted in their own language "in any event". PD32 para 19.1(8) reinforces the own-language requirement. PD32 para 20.1 requires a statement in the witness's own language that they believe the facts stated are true. PD32 para 23.2 requires a foreign language witness statement to be accompanied by a translation. The consequences of non-compliance are addressed in PD32 para 25.1 — the court may refuse to admit the statement; para 25.2 — a defective statement may be used with the judge's permission.

NATURE OF THE DEFICIENCY — FORM OR SUBSTANCE. The judge below characterised the statement as "simply not a witness statement." Garnham J held that went too far; the statement was intended as the equivalent of what Mr Correia would give in evidence. However, the deficiencies were ones of substance, not merely form: the statement of truth was in English, not Portuguese; it was unclear whether the content accurately represented what Mr Correia had said; the defendant could not be confident it reflected Mr Correia's evidence rather than the drafter's interpretation. As a deficiency of substance the statement required the judge's permission to be used under para 25.2. The default position for errors of form is admission (subject to the court's discretion); the default position for errors of substance is that the statement is NOT admitted unless the court agrees. The judge had a discretion either way.

JUDGE'S DISCRETION UPHELD. Garnham J upheld the trial judge's exercise of discretion to refuse admission. The rules about witness statements from non-English speakers provide an important discipline. To have admitted this statement would have been grossly unfair to the defendant, who had provided a compliant statement and only had the solicitor's English version of the claimant's Portuguese instructions. The purposes of requiring witness statements in the witness's own language are: (a) fairness to the witness — tied to a single account of events; (b) fairness to the opposing party — they must know the evidence against them; (c) reliability — the statement must reliably represent the witness's own evidence, not the drafter's interpretation. The judge was entitled to reject all alternatives proposed: allowing Mr Correia to give oral evidence in English; back-translating the statement into Portuguese for him to sign; or adjourning. Each of these would have created the very difficulties the rules are intended to prevent.

GROUND 2 — SOLICITOR'S HEARSAY NOTES. Solicitor's contemporaneous notes of Magistrates' Court proceedings, annexed to her witness statement, were hearsay. The solicitor was not being called as a witness; counsel had not at trial invited the judge to admit the notes as hearsay under the Civil Evidence Act 1995; and counsel ultimately confirmed she had no further evidence on liability. No ground for interference.

GROUND 3 — UROLOGICAL EVIDENCE AND ADJOURNMENT. The claimant's application to adjourn the trial (made on its first day) to enable a new urological expert to be instructed was refused. The application was "simply too late": the possibility of urological evidence had been raised at the CCMC in April 2021 but not pursued; the claimant had had incontinence problems since at least 2019; the application was made just five working days before the trial date listed since July 2021. No good reason for the late application. Prejudice to defendant and court administration clear. Robust case management decisions are entitled to deference on appeal (Piglowska v Piglowski [1999] UKHL 27; Chartwell Estate Agents v Fergies Properties [2014] EWCA Civ 506, Davis LJ).

RESULT. Appeal dismissed on all three grounds. The claim was dismissed due to the absence of any admissible evidence from the claimant on liability.
cases_cited: Correia v Williams [2022] EWHC 2824 (KB)
Denton v TH White [2014] EWCA Civ 906
British Airways PLC v Spencer [2015] EWHC 2477
Chartwell Estate Agents Ltd v Fergies Properties SA [2014] EWCA Civ 506
Piglowska v Piglowski [1999] UKHL 27
Aleksej Gubarev v Orbis Business Intelligence Ltd [2020] EWHC 2812 (QB)
legislation_cited: CPR PD22 para 2.4 (statement of truth in witness's own language)
CPR r.32.4(1) (witness statement in witness's own words)
articles.summary: Mr Justice Garnham dismissed all grounds of appeal in a Portuguese national's RTA claim. A 126-paragraph witness statement drafted in English (from Portuguese instructions) was properly excluded at trial: CPR PD32 requires witness statements to be in the witness's own language in any event; the statement of truth must also be in the witness's own language (PD22 para 2.4). The deficiency was one of substance (not mere form), requiring the judge's permission under PD32 para 25.2. The trial judge was entitled to refuse admission — to admit would have been grossly unfair. Grounds on hearsay notes and refusal of adjournment for urological expert also dismissed.

articles.title: Wagenaar v Weekend Travel Ltd [2014] EWCA Civ 1105 – QOCS Intra Vires and Inapplicable to Part 20 Claims
articles.source: Wagenaar v Weekend Travel Ltd [2014] EWCA Civ 1105, Laws, Floyd and Vos LJJ, judgment by Vos LJ, 31 July 2014
articles.key_points:
BACKGROUND. This Court of Appeal decision resolved two issues arising from QOCS: (1) whether the QOCS rules in CPR rr.44.13–44.17 are intra vires the rule-making power in s.51(3) of the Senior Courts Act 1981; and (2) whether QOCS applies to CPR Part 20 additional claims (contribution/indemnity proceedings) between a defendant and a third party.

ISSUE 1 — QOCS INTRA VIRES. The defendant raised an ultra vires challenge, arguing the QOCS rules exceeded the rule-making power in s.51(3) SCA 1981 because they altered the substantive right of a successful party to recover costs. Vos LJ rejected this. Section 51(3) gives a wide power to make rules of court governing costs. The QOCS regime is a procedural rule about the enforcement of costs orders, not a substantive abrogation of the right to costs. Parliament specifically empowered such changes through LASPO 2012. QOCS rules are therefore intra vires. Defendant's appeal on this point dismissed.

ISSUE 2 — QOCS AND PART 20 CLAIMS. The defendant argued that QOCS applied in its favour to the Part 20 contribution/indemnity claim it had brought against a third party (a tour operator). The third party argued QOCS only protects claimants in personal injury claims, not defendants who bring Part 20 proceedings. Vos LJ held that QOCS does NOT apply to CPR Part 20 additional claims between a defendant and a third party. The QOCS scheme under CPR rr.44.13–44.17 applies only to "proceedings which include a claim for damages for personal injuries" brought by the claimant; it was introduced to protect claimants in personal injury litigation, not to create a one-way costs shield for defendants in contribution or indemnity claims they have themselves chosen to bring against third parties. Third party's appeal allowed.

PRACTICAL SIGNIFICANCE. QOCS does not extend horizontally to Part 20 proceedings. A defendant who brings a contribution or indemnity claim against a third party will not benefit from QOCS protection if that Part 20 claim fails. A successful third party is entitled to enforce its costs order against the defendant without QOCS restriction. This prevents defendants from using QOCS to bring speculative Part 20 claims without costs risk.

RESULT. Defendant's appeal dismissed (ultra vires argument rejected). Third party's appeal allowed (QOCS does not apply to Part 20 proceedings).
cases_cited: Wagenaar v Weekend Travel Ltd [2014] EWCA Civ 1105
legislation_cited: Legal Aid, Sentencing and Punishment of Offenders Act 2012 (LASPO)
articles.summary: The Court of Appeal (Vos LJ) held: (1) the QOCS rules (CPR rr.44.13–44.17) are intra vires s.51(3) SCA 1981 — the ultra vires challenge was rejected; (2) QOCS does NOT apply to CPR Part 20 additional claims between a defendant and a third party — it protects claimants in personal injury proceedings only, not defendants who bring contribution or indemnity claims. Defendant's appeal dismissed; third party's appeal allowed.

articles.title: Denton v TH White Ltd [2014] EWCA Civ 906 – Relief from Sanctions: The Three-Stage Test
articles.source: Denton v TH White Ltd; Decadent Vapours Ltd v Bevan & Ors; Utilise TDS Ltd v Davies & Ors [2014] EWCA Civ 906, Master of the Rolls (Lord Dyson MR), Lord Justice Jackson and Lord Justice Vos, joint judgment by MR and Vos LJ, 4 July 2014
articles.key_points:
BACKGROUND AND PURPOSE. Denton v TH White consolidates and clarifies the guidance given in Mitchell v News Group Newspapers Ltd [2013] EWCA Civ 1537 on applications for relief from sanctions under CPR r.3.9. Mitchell had been misunderstood by many courts, leading to an unduly draconian approach in some cases and an unduly relaxed approach in others. The Master of the Rolls and Vos LJ gave a joint judgment (Jackson LJ agreed). The court acknowledged that Mitchell had generated excessive satellite litigation and unreasonable non-cooperation between litigants.

THE RULE. CPR r.3.9(1) provides that on an application for relief from any sanction imposed for a failure to comply with any rule, practice direction or court order, the court will consider all the circumstances of the case so as to enable it to deal justly with the application, including the need: (a) for litigation to be conducted efficiently and at proportionate cost; and (b) to enforce compliance with rules, practice directions and orders.

THE THREE-STAGE TEST. A judge should address an application in three sequential stages.

STAGE 1 — SERIOUSNESS AND SIGNIFICANCE OF THE BREACH. Identify and assess the seriousness and significance of the failure to comply. Mitchell used "trivial" — Denton replaced this with whether the breach is "serious or significant". This avoids semantic disputes. If the breach is neither serious nor significant the court will usually grant relief without spending much time on Stages 2 and 3. Mere lateness measured in minutes or hours, or a breach that has no practical effect on the progress of the litigation, will usually not be serious or significant. The seriousness of the breach should be assessed on its own at Stage 1 without regard to other unrelated past failures (which may be considered at Stage 3).

STAGE 2 — REASON FOR THE DEFAULT. Consider why the default occurred. A good reason (e.g. debilitating illness, an accident involving the solicitor, later developments showing the original time period was unreasonably short) may lead to relief being granted even for a serious breach. Mere overlooking of deadlines, or having too much work, will rarely be a good reason. Solicitors cannot take on too much work and then rely on overwork as justification. Applications made before the deadline expires are viewed more favourably than applications made after the event.

STAGE 3 — ALL CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE. Even if the breach is serious or significant and there is no good reason for it, relief is not automatically refused. Rule 3.9(1) requires the court to consider all the circumstances so as to deal justly with the application. Factors (a) and (b) must be given particular weight at this stage — they were singled out for specific mention in the rule and form part of the overriding objective. But they are not of "paramount" importance (which was the word used in Mitchell and which Denton corrected to "particular" importance). Other circumstances remain relevant and must be considered. The more serious the breach and the weaker the reason, the less likely relief will be granted; but the third stage requires a holistic assessment.

SATELLITE LITIGATION AND NON-COOPERATION. Parties who opportunistically seek to take advantage of minor procedural errors in the hope of obtaining a windfall strike-out are acting contrary to the overriding objective (CPR r.1.3). The court will penalise opportunism; parties who unreasonably refuse to agree extensions of time or unreasonably oppose relief applications may face adverse costs consequences including indemnity costs. The court can record that opposition was unreasonable under CPR r.44.11 for use at the costs stage at the end of proceedings.

APPLICATION TO THE THREE CASES. In Denton (milking parlour farm case): claimants served six late witness statements very close to the trial date without good reason, causing the trial to be vacated. Stages 1 and 2 pointed strongly against relief; Stage 3 also pointed against relief — factors (a) and (b) militated heavily in favour of refusal. The judge below who granted relief was wrong; his order was set aside. In Decadent (fees by DX): failure to pay court fees was near the bottom of the range of seriousness; no good reason; but at Stage 3 both factors (a) and (b) pointed in favour of relief since the late payment had not prevented efficient litigation or disrupted the trial timetable. Relief granted. In Utilise (costs budget 45 minutes late): breach was trivial (not serious or significant); Stage 3 also supported relief. Relief granted.

KEY PROPOSITIONS. (i) Replace "trivial" with "neither serious nor significant" at Stage 1. (ii) Factors (a) and (b) are of particular importance at Stage 3 but are not automatically determinative. (iii) Even a serious breach with no good reason does not automatically result in refusal. (iv) Opportunistic opposition to relief applications contrary to overriding objective and will be penalised. (v) Applications made before time expires viewed more favourably than applications made after the event. (vi) "Unless" orders should be reserved for situations where truly required.
cases_cited: Denton v TH White Ltd [2014] EWCA Civ 906
Mitchell v News Group Newspapers Ltd [2013] EWCA Civ 1537
Adlington v ELS International Lawyers LLP [2013] EWHC B29 (QB)
Durrant v Chief Constable of Avon and Somerset Constabulary [2013] EWCA Civ 1624
Newland Shipping & Forwarding Ltd v Toba Trading FZC [2014] EWHC 210 (Comm)
Lakatamia Shipping Co Ltd v Su [2014] EWHC 275 (Comm)
Summit Navigation Ltd v Generali Romania Asigurare Reasigurare SA [2014] EWHC 398 (Comm)
Chartwell Estate Agents Ltd v Fergies Properties SA [2014] EWCA Civ 506
Hallam Estates Ltd v Baker [2014] EWCA Civ 661
legislation_cited: CPR r.1.3 (parties' duty to help court further overriding objective)
articles.summary: Denton v TH White (2014) clarified and amplified Mitchell by establishing the three-stage test for relief from sanctions under CPR r.3.9: Stage 1 — assess seriousness and significance of breach (replacing 'trivial' with 'neither serious nor significant'); Stage 2 — consider reason for default; Stage 3 — evaluate all circumstances, giving particular (not paramount) weight to factors (a) (efficient litigation) and (b) (compliance culture). Even a serious breach without good reason does not automatically result in refusal. Opportunistic opposition to relief will be penalised. Applied: Denton — relief refused; Decadent — relief granted; Utilise — relief granted.

articles.title: Diaw v ERS Syndicate Management Ltd (Manchester CC, 25 February 2020)
articles.source: Manchester County Court
key_points: BACKGROUND AND FACTS: Mr Hamath Diaw, Mrs Fatima Kane, and their son Ibrahim claimed damages following a low-impact road traffic accident on 12 November 2017 between a Mercedes Sprinter Van and a Renault Clio. The defendant admitted liability but disputed quantum, challenging the personal injury claims as fundamentally dishonest and claiming the claimants exaggerated or fabricated their injuries. Mr Diaw also claimed £1,527.16 for vehicle repairs. The case involved a low-speed, low-impact collision occurring at approximately 5 mph.
COURT

DECISION: Recorder Mark Jones found that Mr Diaw and Mrs Kane were dishonest in connection with their claims and that the entirety of the personal injury claims were shot through with dishonesty on both claimants' parts. Mr Diaw was entitled to £975 for damage to his car. However, the claimants' personal injury claims were dismissed. The judge applied section 57 of the Criminal Justice and Courts Act 2015 (the 'primary claim' dismissal mechanism) to dismiss the personal injury claims made by all three claimants.

KEY LEGAL PRINCIPLES: Recorder Jones conducted a detailed textual analysis of section 57 CJCA 2015, determining that 'primary claim' refers specifically to proceedings on a claim for damages in respect of personal injury, not to the proceedings as a whole or to any other claims in those proceedings. The court established that the 'claim for damages in respect of personal injury' is defined as the 'primary claim', and the word 'claim' in section 57(2) refers only to that personal injury claim element. The judgment clarified that section 57 does not require dismissal of the entire proceedings if they include non-personal injury claims such as property damage. The court rejected the wider interpretation that would have extended section 57 to encompass all claims in proceedings containing a personal injury claim. Significantly, the judgment addressed 'mixed claims' (actions involving both personal injury and property damage claims), establishing that section 57 applies only to the personal injury elements of such claims. PRACTICAL SIGNIFICANCE FOR CREDIT HIRE: This case is of critical importance to credit hire law because it determines the scope of section 57 dismissal powers in 'mixed claim' road traffic accident cases. Most road traffic accident claims involving credit hire include both a personal injury claim and a property damage claim. This judgment establishes that if a claimant is found to be fundamentally dishonest about personal injury, their property damage claim (including credit hire costs) may not automatically be dismissed under section 57. The claimant must be fundamentally dishonest about the personal injury claim itself for section 57 to apply. This provides important protection for credit hire claims as separate property damage claims. However, the decision emphasizes judicial caution about fundamental dishonesty findings in contexts of whiplash and low-impact accident claims, requiring careful scrutiny of evidence.
cases_cited: Molodi v Cambridge Vibration Maintenance Service [2018] EWHC 1288 (QB)
LOCOG v Sinfield [2018] EWHC 51 (QB)
Stanton v Hunter [2017] LTL (12 June 2017)
Pinkus v Direct Line [2018] EWHC 1671 (QB)
Haider v DSM Demolition Ltd [2019] EWHC 2712 (QB)
Basir v Larizadeh [2020] unreported
Ivey v Genting Casinos (UK) Ltd [2018] AC 391 SC

Qualified One-Way Costs Shifting (QOCS) CPR 44.13 to 44.16

articles.title: Bunting v Zurich Insurance Plc [2020] EWHC 1807 (QB)
articles.subcategory: Basic Hire Rate Assessment and Credit Hire
articles.source: High Court
key_points: BACKGROUND AND FACTS: On 24 June 2015, Andrew Bunting was involved in a road traffic accident in which his Lotus Exige Coupe sports car was damaged. While the vehicle was being repaired over several months, Mr Bunting hired a replacement Mercedes C220 Sport from Helphire on credit hire terms at a total cost of £28,551.84. Mr Bunting brought proceedings to recover the hire costs together with repair costs. The negligent driver's insurer, Zurich Insurance plc, admitted liability for repair costs but challenged the credit hire charges. At trial before Recorder Le Poidevin QC in November 2018, the parties agreed that Mr Bunting had reasonably hired a replacement car for 78 days.
COURT

DECISION: The Recorder rejected the insurer's arguments that the hire agreement was not binding and that a cheaper car should have been hired. The judge found Mr Bunting entitled to hire a comparable prestige car (Mercedes) rather than a cheaper vehicle. Since Mr Bunting did not claim to be impecunious, he was entitled to recover only the basic hire rate for a comparable prestige car, not the premium charged for credit hire. The judge accepted evidence from Philip Rose as to the basic hire rate using a database of rates, identifying Thrifty as offering a BMW 420 at £250.24 per week. After adjustments to address concerns about availability and terms, the judgment was awarded on the basis of the Thrifty rate at £260 per week plus £98 per week for collision damage waiver, representing a small fraction of the total credit hire costs incurred. Mr Bunting appealed, and Justice Pepperall upheld the Recorder's decision.

KEY LEGAL PRINCIPLES: The judgment applied the structured approach established in Pattni v First Leicester Buses Ltd, requiring consideration of: (i) whether the claimant needed to hire a replacement car; (ii) whether it was reasonable to hire the particular type of car at the agreed rate; (iii) whether the claimant was impecunious; (iv) whether the defendant proved a difference between the credit hire rate and the basic hire rate; and (v) what that difference was. Justice Pepperall endorsed the principle that judges should be encouraged to do their best with imperfect evidence, relying on case law establishing that in valuations generally, evidence of prices at different dates requires appropriate adjustments, and judges need not find an exact spot rate for an exactly comparable car. The court confirmed that evidence of availability need not be at precisely the time of the accident, and that a bracket of spot rates for cars is acceptable with the judge aiming for a reasonable average. The judgment emphasized that judges are entitled to make rough and ready adjustments using experience and that appellate courts should be slow to interfere with first instance factual assessments. PRACTICAL SIGNIFICANCE FOR CREDIT HIRE: This decision is fundamental to credit hire law as it establishes the parameters for assessing reasonable hire rates. It confirms that claimants not claiming to be impecunious must prove their entitlement to credit hire costs by establishing the basic hire rate differential. The case demonstrates judicial acceptance of database evidence of rates and willingness to make adjustments for practical factors like hire term limitations. However, it establishes a restrictive approach to credit hire recovery where the insurer successfully relies on comparable prestige vehicle evidence. The decision reinforces that credit hire claims must be carefully structured with evidence of comparable prestige vehicles and their market rates, and that presumptions about availability based on online evidence can be challenged. The case is critical authority for insurers seeking to limit credit hire recovery to basic hire rates.

Dimond v Lovell [2000] RTR 243
Lagden v O'Connor [2004] RTR 24
Bent v Highways & Utilities Construction Ltd [2010] EWCA Civ 292
Crewe Services v Silk [1997] CA (unreported)

articles.title: Mattocks v Mann [1993] RTR 13
articles.source: Court of Appeal (Civil Division)
key_points: BACKGROUND AND FACTS: Wendy Ann Mattocks' Peugeot 205 GTi was damaged in an accident caused by the defendant on 10 August 1989. The plaintiff, a young woman in her mid-twenties expecting her second child, required a replacement vehicle. She initially hired a Lancia Fila runabout at £99.47 per week for two weeks, then changed to a Ford Sierra at £130 per week for 20 weeks (total £2,600), and subsequently hired a Nissan Micra at £85 per week for 20 weeks (total £1,700). An equivalent Peugeot 205 GTi would have cost £354 per week. The defendant's insurers refused to pay for all hire costs, arguing the Ford Sierra at £130 per week was unreasonable and that the plaintiff should have hired a two-door Nissan Micra equivalent throughout. The Master awarded damages of £5,191.14 but refused the full credit hire claim, reducing it by £1,750 by disallowing hire costs during certain periods.
COURT

DECISION: The Court of Appeal allowed the appeal in its entirety. Lord Justice Beldam held that the correct test was whether the plaintiff had acted reasonably in all the circumstances, not whether she had hired a reasonable replacement for her original car. He rejected the defendant's argument that the plaintiff should have managed with a two-door hatchback, noting that the plaintiff had actually demonstrated through her subsequent hiring of the Nissan Micra that such cars were a reasonable replacement. However, the question was not whether the Ford Sierra was a reasonable replacement in general, but whether it was reasonable for her to hire it during the specific period of January to June 1990 given all prevailing circumstances. Lord Justice Beldam found it impossible to argue that hiring a car at £130 per week when equivalent cars cost £354 per week constituted unreasonable conduct. He allowed the appeal on the hire cost issue and increased the award by £1,750. Lord Justice Stocker agreed, emphasizing that the correct test was whether the plaintiff acted reasonably, not whether alternative arrangements were theoretically possible. Lord Justice Nourse similarly allowed the appeal, applying the principle from Banco de Portugal v. Waterlow that the plaintiff is entitled to be put in the position she would have been in but for the breach, and assessing whether she acted reasonably in all circumstances and with commercial prudence.

KEY LEGAL PRINCIPLES: (1) The test for assessing the reasonableness of hire costs is whether the plaintiff acted reasonably in all the circumstances, not whether the vehicle hired was the cheapest reasonable replacement. (2) A claimant is not required to mitigate by hiring an unreasonably inconvenient or unsuitable replacement merely because it is cheaper. (3) The principle established in Banco de Portugal v. Waterlow applies: the plaintiff must have acted reasonably and with commercial prudence in the adoption of remedial measures. (4) Once a large repair organization is engaged on repairs, reassurance may be given from day to day that completion is imminent, and delay in obtaining parts (particularly for foreign make vehicles) may be reasonable and should not be held against the plaintiff. (5) The plaintiff's inability to pay for repairs from her own resources does not constitute an independent cause terminating the defendant's liability, provided the defendant's insurers instructed the repairs and agreed to pay (application of the Liesbosch principle narrowly construed). (6) Delay in providing repair estimates or notification to the defendant should be evaluated carefully; unexplained delay may be attributed to circumstances for which the defendant is responsible, and the plaintiff should not be penalized for unavoidable delays in the claims process.
PRACTICAL

SIGNIFICANCE: This is a landmark decision establishing that credit hire claims must be assessed by reference to reasonableness of conduct rather than by identifying the absolute minimum cost vehicle that could theoretically have been hired. The case provides strong authority that where a defendant's insurers have agreed liability and authorized repairs, subsequent hiring of a higher-specification vehicle is recoverable if reasonable in the circumstances. The decision significantly expanded recovery for credit hire costs by shifting focus from the nature of the replacement vehicle to the plaintiff's overall conduct and the resources available to her. The court's treatment of the 'knock for knock' arrangements and insurance industry practice is also significant, establishing that once insurers indicate liability is not in issue and repairs will be paid for, the plaintiff can rely on that representation. The case has become a key authority for establishing that claimants need not shop for the cheapest replacement vehicle if a more suitable one is reasonable given their circumstances, resources, and the defendant's representations about liability and payment.

Payzu v. Saunders [1919]

Monarch Steamship Company v. Karlshamns [1949] A.C. 196
Perry v. Sydney Phillips

articles.title: Darbishire v Warran [1963] 3 All ER 310
articles.source: Court of Appeal
key_points: BACKGROUND AND FACTS: This case involved a motor vehicle damaged in a negligent accident. The defendant's liability for the damage was not in issue. The key issue concerned the measure of damages recoverable where the cost of repairing the vehicle substantially exceeded the market value of the vehicle at the time of the accident.
COURT

DECISION: The Court of Appeal held that when the cost of repairs greatly exceeds the market value of the vehicle, the plaintiff has a duty to mitigate damages. Rather than claiming the full repair costs, the plaintiff should be limited to recovering the diminution in the vehicle's value (the difference between its pre-accident market value and its post-accident salvage value), together with any genuine consequential losses. The court established that it would be unreasonable for a plaintiff to insist on the vehicle being repaired when the repair costs far exceed what the vehicle is worth.

KEY LEGAL PRINCIPLES: (1) The plaintiff has a duty to mitigate losses arising from a defendant's negligence. (2) This duty applies to the election between different remedial measures; the plaintiff must not claim the cost of repairing a vehicle when repair is disproportionately expensive compared to the vehicle's market value. (3) Where repair costs exceed market value, the proper measure of damages is the diminution in value (pre-accident market value less post-accident salvage value). (4) The duty to mitigate is an objective test based on what a reasonable person would do in the circumstances. (5) The concept of market value is central to assessing the reasonableness of repair claims; this principle has become foundational in credit hire cases where arguments are made about the proportionality of hire costs to actual vehicle value.
PRACTICAL

SIGNIFICANCE: Darbishire v Warran establishes the fundamental principle that repair costs can be challenged on the basis of disproportionality to market value. This principle is frequently cited in credit hire disputes where defendants argue that the cost of hiring a replacement vehicle for an extended period is disproportionate to the value of the original vehicle. While the case pre-dates the modern credit hire industry, its reasoning about comparative proportionality between loss and remedial cost remains highly relevant to determining whether hire charges are reasonably incurred.

articles.title: Payzu v Saunders [1919]
articles.source: Court of Appeal
key_points: BACKGROUND AND FACTS: This 1919 case established a foundational principle in the law of damages concerning the plaintiff's obligation to minimize losses resulting from a defendant's breach.
COURT

DECISION: The court held that where a defendant breaches a contract, the plaintiff is not entitled to sit back and allow losses to accumulate. Instead, the plaintiff has an affirmative duty to take reasonable steps to minimize or mitigate the loss caused by the breach.

KEY LEGAL PRINCIPLES: (1) A party who suffers loss due to a defendant's breach of contract must take all reasonable steps to mitigate that loss. (2) The duty to mitigate is not a duty to take extraordinary or unreasonable measures, but rather to act as a reasonable person would in the circumstances. (3) A plaintiff who fails to mitigate will have damages reduced to the extent the loss could have been avoided by taking reasonable steps. (4) The onus is on the defendant to prove that the plaintiff failed to mitigate.
PRACTICAL

SIGNIFICANCE: Payzu v Saunders provides the foundational authority for the duty to mitigate in contract law. This principle has been extended into tort and is central to all credit hire disputes, as defendants routinely argue that plaintiffs should have taken steps to minimize hire costs by selecting cheaper vehicles or shorter hire periods. The case establishes that mitigation is not about choosing the cheapest option available, but about taking reasonable steps in the circumstances.

articles.title: British Westinghouse v Underground Electric [1912]
articles.source: House of Lords
key_points: BACKGROUND AND FACTS: This 1912 House of Lords case dealt with mitigation of loss principles in a commercial context.
COURT

DECISION: The House of Lords, in a judgment by Viscount Haldane LC, established the foundational principle that a plaintiff who has suffered loss through breach of duty has a duty to take reasonable steps to mitigate that loss.

KEY LEGAL PRINCIPLES: (1) The fundamental rule is that damages are assessed as at the time of the breach, but the injured party must take reasonable steps to mitigate the consequences of the breach. (2) The injured party is not required to take exceptional or extraordinary steps, only reasonable ones. (3) The test is objective: what would a reasonable person have done in similar circumstances. (4) Mitigation applies not only to deliberate steps but also to the failure to take steps that would have avoided or reduced loss.
PRACTICAL

SIGNIFICANCE: British Westinghouse provides the foundational House of Lords authority for the doctrine of mitigation. Viscount Haldane LC's articulation of the principle has been quoted extensively in subsequent cases and provides the primary statement of the mitigation principle applicable across both contract and tort. This case is cited as the ultimate authority for mitigation duties in credit hire disputes.

articles.title: Banco de Portugal v Waterlow & Sons Ltd. [1932] A.C. 452
articles.subcategory: Measure of Damages and Market Value
articles.source: House of Lords
key_points: BACKGROUND AND FACTS: This 1932 House of Lords case involved a claim for damages arising from breach of duty in the provision of financial services.
COURT

DECISION: The House of Lords, in a judgment by Lord Macmillan, established the principle that where a party has been put in a difficult situation by reason of breach of duty, the law is satisfied if that party has acted reasonably in adopting remedial measures. The injured party will not be held disentitled to recover the cost of such measures merely because other measures less burdensome to the wrongdoer might have been taken.

KEY LEGAL PRINCIPLES: (1) The test for reasonableness in mitigation is whether the plaintiff acted reasonably in adopting remedial measures, not whether alternative measures were theoretically available. (2) A plaintiff is entitled to be put into the position they would have been in but for the breach. (3) Once the plaintiff has acted reasonably, they are entitled to recover the cost of the reasonable measures taken, even if less burdensome alternatives existed. (4) The defendant cannot shift the burden to the plaintiff by suggesting less expensive alternatives after the breach has occurred.
PRACTICAL

SIGNIFICANCE: Banco de Portugal v Waterlow is the leading House of Lords authority establishing that reasonableness is the correct test in mitigation, not the selection of the cheapest possible option. This case has been applied repeatedly in credit hire disputes to establish that if a plaintiff has reasonably hired a vehicle at a certain cost, the defendant cannot argue that a cheaper vehicle should have been hired. The principle is that the plaintiff must act reasonably, but once they have done so, they are entitled to recover the cost of their reasonable choice.

articles.title: The Mediana [1900] A.C. 113
articles.subcategory: Loss of Use and Damages Principles
articles.source: House of Lords
key_points: BACKGROUND AND FACTS: This was a House of Lords admiralty case involving damage to a lightship. The lightship was damaged through collision and rendered unusable. The owner sought damages for loss of use of the vessel during the period it was out of service, and the cost of hiring a substitute vessel during the repair period.
COURT

DECISION: The House of Lords held that damages for loss of use are recoverable as a head of loss. The court approved recovery of the cost of hiring a substitute vessel as a measure of the loss of use sustained. The principle established was that where a vessel or other asset is damaged and rendered unusable, the owner is entitled to recover the reasonable cost of obtaining a substitute during the period of non-availability.

KEY LEGAL PRINCIPLES: (1) Loss of use is a recognized head of damages in tort. (2) The cost of hiring a substitute to replace an unavailable asset is a proper measure of loss of use damages. (3) The cost incurred in obtaining a substitute demonstrates the real loss suffered by the plaintiff in being deprived of use of the asset. (4) This principle applies whether the substitute is hired at market rates or at rates reflecting the plaintiff's own assessment of their needs.
PRACTICAL

SIGNIFICANCE: The Mediana is a foundational authority establishing that credit hire losses (as a form of loss of use) are legitimate damages in tort. The case provides the historical basis in admiralty law for the modern credit hire industry. By establishing that the cost of a substitute is the proper measure of loss of use, the case has been cited as authority that credit hire charges reflecting the cost of hiring a substitute vehicle are recoverable damages. The principle that 'loss of use' is itself compensable (rather than requiring proof of an alternative quantifiable loss) is central to credit hire theory.

articles.title: Hewison v Meridian Shipping Services Inc. [2002] EWCA Civ 1821
articles.source: Court of Appeal (Civil Division)
key_points: BACKGROUND AND FACTS: This 2002 Court of Appeal case involved a claim for damages by a claimant who had failed to disclose material information (epilepsy) when applying for employment as a ship's officer. The claimant subsequently suffered injury and sought damages for losses including loss of earnings and other consequential losses.
COURT

DECISION: The Court of Appeal held that on public policy grounds, the claimant was barred from recovering damages for losses that were causally connected to their own criminal conduct (the making of false statements in failing to disclose material information). The court established that damages claims may be defeated on public policy grounds where the plaintiff's own illegal conduct is sufficiently connected to the loss claimed.

KEY LEGAL PRINCIPLES: (1) Public policy can operate as a complete bar to recovery in damages in exceptional cases where the plaintiff's own criminal conduct is sufficiently causally connected to the loss. (2) The doctrine is not limited to situations where the claimant sues on an illegal transaction, but extends to cases where the claimant's illegal conduct is causally relevant to the loss claimed. (3) The application of the public policy bar is exceptional and fact-specific, requiring clear connection between the criminal conduct and the loss. (4) Courts will not lightly apply the public policy bar, as it is a drastic remedy that defeats an otherwise legitimate claim.
PRACTICAL

SIGNIFICANCE: While this case does not directly address credit hire, it establishes important principles about limitations on recovery based on public policy grounds. In credit hire disputes, the case is relevant to arguments about whether certain losses should be recoverable where the claimant's own conduct has contributed to those losses or where public policy considerations might limit recovery. The case demonstrates that courts will consider public policy objections to damage claims in appropriate circumstances, though such objections are applied sparingly.

articles.title: Bradburn v Great Western Railway Co. (1874-75) LR 10 Ex. 1
articles.source: Court of Exchequer
key_points: BACKGROUND AND FACTS: This 1874 case involved a passenger injured on the Great Western Railway who received damages for injuries sustained through the railway company's negligence. The plaintiff had also received a sum of £31 from an Accidental Insurance Company on a policy he had previously taken out with them. The defendants sought to reduce the damages award by the amount of the insurance payout, arguing that the plaintiff's loss had been partly compensated by the insurance proceeds.
COURT

DECISION: The Court of Exchequer unanimously rejected the defendants' argument and held that the insurance proceeds could not be deducted from the damages award. Baron Bramwell stated that the plaintiff was entitled to retain both the benefit of the insurance policy he had purchased and the full damages he recovered on account of the defendants' negligence. The court distinguished this case from situations involving indemnity insurance policies and established that private insurance benefits are not deductible from tort damages.

KEY LEGAL PRINCIPLES: (1) A plaintiff who has insured himself against a loss by paying premiums is entitled to the benefit of that insurance in addition to damages recovered for the tort. (2) The fact that an accident triggers an insurance payment does not mean the insurance proceeds are the cause of receipt; rather, the private contract of insurance is the cause. (3) Insurance benefits purchased voluntarily by a plaintiff as a quid pro quo for premiums paid are not deductible from damages. (4) This principle applies to personal accident insurance and similar policies where the plaintiff is the sole beneficiary under the contract. (5) The rule differs from Lord Campbell's Act contexts where certain statutory deductions may apply.
PRACTICAL

SIGNIFICANCE: Bradburn established the foundational common law principle that collateral benefits in the form of voluntary personal insurance are not deductible from tort damages. This principle has been applied for nearly 150 years and remains the baseline rule from which modern collateral benefits doctrine operates. For credit hire, the case is significant because it establishes that if a claimant has incurred losses despite having insurance coverage, those losses remain recoverable in full. The principle underpins the notion that insurance is separate from tort compensation and addresses different risks. Courts have not extended this principle to require reduction in credit hire charges based on insurance cover, particularly where the claimant has actually incurred the hire charges and they are not covered by insurance benefits.

Yates v Whyte (1851)
Hicks v Newport Railway Co. (1856)

Dalton v South Eastern Railway Co. (1860)
Pym v Great Northern Railway Co. (1863)

articles.title: Parry v Cleaver [1970] AC 1
articles.source: House of Lords
key_points: BACKGROUND AND FACTS: This landmark House of Lords case involved a police constable severely injured in a road accident caused by the defendant's negligence. The plaintiff had been disabled and discharged from the police force and was entitled to a police disability pension under the Police Pensions Act 1948 and Police Pensions Regulations 1962. At trial, damages were assessed at £16,580 15s. 2d., but the question arose whether certain pension and other benefits received by the plaintiff should be deducted from the award.
COURT

DECISION: The House of Lords held that the police disability pension should not be deducted from the damages award. Lord Reid gave the leading judgment, holding that pension benefits secured by the plaintiff's own contributions and employment service are not deductible from tort damages. The court emphasised that the pension was earned through the plaintiff's services and contributions, forming part of his employment contract terms, and therefore represents a separate source of compensation unrelated to the tort. The court also rejected arguments that the plaintiff should be denied damages based on public policy grounds or that insurance principles should mandate deductions.

KEY LEGAL PRINCIPLES: (1) Benefits arising from pension rights earned through an employment contract and secured by the employee's own contributions should generally not be deducted from tort damages. (2) The distinction between earned benefits (wages, pensions, insurance) and gratuitous benefits is crucial; earned benefits secured by the plaintiff's efforts are typically not deductible. (3) The source and nature of the receipt is critical—if a benefit is the fruit of the plaintiff's own earnings or premiums previously paid, it should not be deducted. (4) Public policy principles and insurance doctrine do not automatically require deduction of all benefits; the focus must be on the fairness and justice of the particular case. (5) Benevolent payments and gifts from third parties may be treated differently from earned benefits. (6) The principle applies even where benefits have a connection to the employment that caused the injury.
PRACTICAL

SIGNIFICANCE: Parry v Cleaver established the modern collateral benefits principle that earned benefits are generally not deductible from tort damages, particularly where the plaintiff has contributed to or earned those benefits through their own efforts and resources. For credit hire claims, this principle supports the recovery of hire charges where the claimant has actually incurred those charges (whether through credit arrangements or direct hire). The case established that where a claimant has taken reasonable steps to mitigate loss through hire arrangements, those arrangements should not be defeated simply because insurance or other benefits exist. The principle reflects the common law approach that tort damages compensate for actual loss caused by the tort, not hypothetical losses that might have occurred if the claimant had made different choices. Courts have extended this reasoning to credit hire, holding that claimants are entitled to recover the reasonable cost of replacement vehicles even where insurance exists, provided the hire was reasonable and necessary.

articles.title: Chatterton v AXA Corporate Solutions [2016] EWHC 2016 WL 04772382
articles.subcategory: Reasonable Transport Needs and Hire Rates
articles.source: Stoke-on-Trent County Court
tags: Watson Norie Limited v Shaw & Nelson
key_points: BACKGROUND AND FACTS: This 2016 County Court case involved a collision on 24 December 2013 between vehicles owned by the claimant, Mr Chatterton. His BMW X5 motor car was damaged, and he hired a replacement vehicle through Enterprise Rent-A-Car (a Mercedes Benz ML350). His Ford Transit van was also damaged and was replaced through Kindertons at a claimed cost of £19,425.06 covering the period from 30 April 2013 to 24 December 2014. The defendant (Axa) was the insurer of the vehicle that caused the collision. The key issues were: (1) whether replacement vehicles were necessary and for what period; (2) the type of replacement vehicle the claimant was entitled to recover for; (3) whether the hire rates charged were reasonable; and (4) whether the claimant could recover costs for recovering the Ford Transit van.
COURT

DECISION: HHJ Rawlings held that: (1) Mr Chatterton was entitled to hire a replacement vehicle for his Ford Transit van only for the 6 days it took to repair the van, as it was drivable and roadworthy immediately following the collision. (2) For the BMW X5, Mr Chatterton was entitled to recover only the cost of hiring a Volkswagen Passat estate (or equivalent), not a Mercedes Benz ML350, as this met his reasonable transport needs—a standard vehicle of equivalent functionality to the damaged BMW was sufficient. (3) The basic hire rates for the Passat estate were £7,377.37 from Sixt (the lowest reasonable mainstream supplier) for the 91 days of agreed hire, and for the van, the rate of £28.85 per day from Enterprise for 6 days totalling £173.10. (4) The £300 recovery charge for the Ford Transit van could not be recovered as the van was roadworthy and could have been driven immediately to storage without professional recovery.

KEY LEGAL PRINCIPLES: (1) A claimant is entitled to hire a replacement vehicle only where there is a genuine need for that vehicle during the period of repair or replacement. (2) The reasonable transport needs test is objective, focusing on what would reasonably be necessary to mitigate loss, not the claimant's subjective preferences or the insurer's available options. (3) An impecunious claimant (one who cannot afford to hire on spot terms) is entitled to recover credit hire charges on the basis of reasonable necessity, following the principle in Langdon v O'Connor and Watson Norie Limited v Shaw & Nelson. (4) However, the principle does not extend to allowing recovery of the cost of hiring a vehicle more expensive or prestigious than necessary to meet genuine transport needs. (5) The basic hire rate is the appropriate measure of reasonable hire costs, not the credit hire rate; where credit hire has been incurred, the defendant can argue that a cheaper basic rate vehicle was available. (6) The court should identify the basic hire rate by looking at mainstream suppliers in the relevant geographic area and at the relevant time. (7) Excess charges and administrative costs may be recoverable where they are reasonably incurred to mitigate loss.
PRACTICAL

SIGNIFICANCE: This case significantly clarifies the principles governing credit hire recovery in English law. While confirming that impecunious claimants can recover credit hire charges on reasonable necessity grounds, the case establishes important limitations. First, the claimant must have a genuine need for a replacement vehicle—if the vehicle can be repaired and driven within days, lengthy hire periods cannot be justified. Second, the type of vehicle hired must be reasonably necessary to meet transport needs; hiring an expensive vehicle beyond what is needed for ordinary purposes will not be recoverable in full. Third, the court will determine a basic hire rate and may not allow full credit hire rates if equivalent basic rate vehicles were available. This represents a middle ground: rejecting the strict 'like for like' requirement but also rejecting unlimited credit hire recovery. For practitioners, the case emphasises that evidence of actual transport needs (family size, business use, specific requirements) is crucial to establishing whether a particular vehicle was reasonably necessary. The case also confirms that even where an insurer has conceded that hire was necessary, the court retains discretion to assess the appropriate level of hire charges and may reduce claims where evidence shows cheaper alternatives would have met the claimant's genuine needs.
cases_cited: Langdon v O'Connor [2004] 1 App Cas 1067
Watson Norie Limited v Shaw & Nelson [1967] 1 Lloyd's Rep 515
Dennard v Plant [2003] QB 36
Singh v Yagubi [2013] EWCA Civ 23
Bent v Highways & Utilities Construction [2011] EWHC Civ 1384
Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management Limited (cited at page 599)

articles.title: Haider v DSM Demolition Ltd [2019] EWHC 2712 (QB)
articles.subcategory: High Court — Fundamental Dishonesty and Appeal
articles.source: High Court of Justice, Queen's Bench Division
key_points: BACKGROUND AND FACTS: This 2019 High Court case involved an appeal from a County Court judgment dismissing a credit hire claim on the ground that the underlying accident was staged and the claimant had been fundamentally dishonest. A taxi driver, Mr Haider, had been involved in a road traffic accident on 10 July 2014 on the Worcester Road (A450) at approximately 5.45 pm. The accident involved the claimant's BMW and a vehicle driven by Mr O'Sullivan. The defendant, DSM Demolition Ltd, argued that the accident was deliberately staged and that the claimant had acted dishonestly in presenting the claim. At trial, the judge found that the claimant had stopped his vehicle unnecessarily, and while there may have been some negligence in the claimant's over-braking, the core issue was whether the claimant had been fundamentally dishonest in his claim.
COURT

DECISION: On appeal, Mr Justice Julian Knowles allowed the defendant's appeal in part. The High Court held: (1) The judge failed to give adequate reasons for finding that Mr O'Sullivan's driving was not negligent and at least partially causative of the collision; (2) However, the judge's finding that the claimant was fundamentally dishonest was not clearly wrong and was supported by the evidence, particularly regarding Mr Beech's credibility regarding whether the claimant had stopped; (3) While the claimant's evidence at trial was honest regarding key matters, his failure to disclose credit cards and a second bank account constituted a fundamentally dishonest disclosure, which warranted disqualification of Qualified One-Way Costs Shifting (QOCS) under CPR r 44.16(1); (4) The defendant's Notice of Cross-Appeal was served out of time but relief from sanctions was granted given the circumstances.

KEY LEGAL PRINCIPLES: (1) Fundamental dishonesty in civil litigation is a serious matter that can result in disqualification from QOCS protection, exposing the claimant to adverse costs orders. (2) Dishonesty must be established by the civil standard of proof and requires objective evaluation of the defendant's state of mind and honest belief—not merely subjective honesty. (3) The distinction between collateral dishonesty (dishonesty regarding minor, peripheral matters) and fundamental dishonesty (dishonesty regarding matters central to the claim or substantial parts thereof) is critical. (4) Full disclosure requirements apply in civil litigation, and deliberate failures to disclose financial information constitute dishonesty; excuses based on forgetfulness or claimed bank errors are not credible if the information was within the claimant's knowledge and capacity to disclose. (5) Trial judges must give adequate reasons for key findings, particularly on critical issues such as negligence and causation; reasoning that permits appellate courts to understand how the judge reached their conclusion is essential. (6) The burden of proving negligence rests on the claimant, and where a defendant raises allegations of staged accidents or fundamental dishonesty, the judge must clearly address whether the defendant has properly discharged the evidential burden or whether the claimant has failed to prove their case. (7) Relief from sanctions under CPR r 3.9 may be granted for late service of cross-appeal notices where justice requires it, even where procedural breaches have occurred.
PRACTICAL

SIGNIFICANCE: This case is highly significant for credit hire practice at the High Court level. While the case itself ultimately resulted in the claim being dismissed on fundamental dishonesty grounds, the High Court's reasoning provides important guidance on the standards applicable to dishonesty allegations in civil litigation. The judgment emphasises that while a trial judge is well-placed to assess witness credibility, appellate courts will carefully scrutinise findings of dishonesty, particularly where they are based on inferences about a claimant's knowledge and intent. The case demonstrates that failure to disclose financial information in disclosure lists, even where explanations are offered (such as bank error excuses), can constitute fundamental dishonesty that justifies removal of QOCS protection. For credit hire claimants, the judgment serves as a warning that courts will examine financial disclosures carefully, and counsel must ensure that all relevant financial information is properly disclosed, including details of credit facilities, bank accounts, and financial resources. The case also illustrates the importance of clear findings on causation and negligence; while the defendant may raise allegations of staged accidents, these remain allegations, and the court must clearly determine whether the claimant has proved the defendant's negligence and whether the defendant has effectively challenged that negligence. The High Court's willingness to examine the judge's reasoning in detail suggests that appellate courts will not readily defer to trial judges on dishonesty findings, particularly where the dishonesty finding is based on inferences about the claimant's state of mind rather than clear documentary or testimonial evidence.
