articles.title: Rashid v Naylor [2018] 8 WLUK 363
articles.source: Barnsley County Court
key_points: BACKGROUND AND FACTS: Taxi driver brought credit hire claim following road traffic accident. Defendant disputed various elements of the claim including the need for hire, impecuniosity of claimant (inability to afford basic hire rate), length of hire period, and mitigation of loss. The case involved assessment of whether the claimant could have obtained alternative transport at a lower cost and whether hire period extended unreasonably.
COURT

DECISION: Court found balance between need for replacement vehicle and reasonableness of hire charges claimed. Judgment addressed whether claimant genuinely required hire at credit rate or could have arranged alternative transport. Court considered evidence of claimant's financial position and business reliance on vehicle.

KEY LEGAL PRINCIPLES: Need for hire is not self-proving and burden falls on claimant to demonstrate genuine necessity for replacement vehicle following accident. Impecuniosity (lack of funds) is relevant factor in establishing need for credit hire at rates exceeding basic hire rate. Duration of hire period must be reasonable and proportionate to repair time. Claimant has duty to mitigate loss by considering cheaper alternatives. Reasonableness of hire rate must be assessed in context of market rates for comparable vehicles.
PRACTICAL

SIGNIFICANCE: Case illustrates that credit hire claims require careful evidencing of need, financial position of claimant, and reasonableness of charges. Defendants can challenge claims by establishing claimant could have used alternative cheaper transport options or had access to own vehicles.
cases_cited: Singh v Yaqubi [2013] EWCA Civ 23
Giles v Thompson [1994] 1 AC 142
legislation_cited: Pre-Action Protocol for Low Value Personal Injury Claims in Road Traffic Accidents

articles.title: Hillier v Southern Rock Insurance Co Ltd
articles.subcategory: Abuse of Process and Strike Out Applications
articles.source: Northampton County Court
key_points: BACKGROUND AND FACTS: Application to strike out credit hire claim on basis of abuse of process under Henderson abuse of process principles. Case involved procedural issues relating to how credit hire claim was formulated and pursued. Defendant argued that manner of bringing claim and conduct of parties amounted to abuse of court's process. Case examined threshold for striking out claims as abusive and what conduct warrants such drastic remedy.
COURT

DECISION: Court applied Henderson test for abuse of process, examining whether manner of conducting claim was such as to prevent fair trial or bring justice system into disrepute. Court considered proportionality of strike out remedy and whether lesser procedural sanctions would suffice. Decision addressed whether procedural defects in bringing credit hire claim warranted complete dismissal or could be remedied through case management.

KEY LEGAL PRINCIPLES: Abuse of process strike out is exceptionally serious remedy only available where conduct is so egregious it prevents fair trial or brings justice into disrepute. Henderson criteria require court to demonstrate clear connection between alleged abuse and trial fairness. Procedural irregularities in credit hire claims do not automatically justify strike out. Courts prefer proportionate remedies through case management and directions. Fair trial considerations are paramount in determining whether abuse has occurred.
PRACTICAL

SIGNIFICANCE: Case establishes that even serious procedural defects in credit hire claims typically require lesser remedies than strike out. Practitioners must understand high threshold for abuse of process strike out and that courts will generally prefer procedural directions to dismissal.
cases_cited: Henderson v Henderson [1843] 3 Hare 100
Three Rivers District Council v Bank of England [2003] 2 AC 1
legislation_cited: Pre-Action Protocol for Low Value Personal Injury Claims in Road Traffic Accidents

articles.title: Basir v Lazarideh
articles.subcategory: Section 57 Criminal Justice and Courts Act 2015
articles.source: Central London County Court
key_points: BACKGROUND AND FACTS: Appeal regarding application of section 57 of Criminal Justice and Courts Act 2015 in context of credit hire claim. Case involved findings that claimant had been fundamentally dishonest in pursuit of claim. Lower court applied s.57 to strike out entire claim including credit hire element. Appeal considered whether fundamental dishonesty findings were properly made and whether proportionate remedy was striking out all claims.
COURT

DECISION: Court examined whether claimant's dishonesty extended to credit hire element of claim or was confined to other aspects. Appeal addressed whether striking out entire claim was proportionate response to dishonesty in other elements. Court considered whether credit hire element could be severed from dishonest elements of claim. Decision reviewed standard of proof and evidence required to establish fundamental dishonesty.

KEY LEGAL PRINCIPLES: Section 57 Criminal Justice and Courts Act 2015 permits court to dismiss claim where claimant has been found fundamentally dishonest in prosecuting it. Fundamental dishonesty is serious allegation requiring clear and cogent evidence. Dishonesty in one element of claim does not automatically invalidate all other elements. Court should consider proportionality and whether dismissal of entire claim is proportionate remedy to dishonesty found. Claimant has right to appeal fundamental dishonesty findings on basis of evidential assessment and legal application.
PRACTICAL

SIGNIFICANCE: Case illustrates that s.57 findings require careful factual analysis and cannot be assumed across all elements of multi-faceted claims. Credit hire claims require honesty regarding need, rates, and period of hire. Dishonest evidence on one aspect may not necessarily taint credit hire element if separately established.
cases_cited: Owens v Brimmell [2016] EWCA Civ 1075
legislation_cited: Section 57 Criminal Justice and Courts Act 2015

articles.title: Bricklebank v Quinn
articles.subcategory: CPR Requirements for Credit Hire Claims
articles.source: Central London County Court
key_points: BACKGROUND AND FACTS: Judgment addressing pleading requirements for credit hire claims under Civil Procedure Rules. Case examined whether claimant had properly pleaded credit hire claim with necessary particularity and evidence. Defendant disputed adequacy of pleading and supporting evidence for credit hire element. Court considered what information must be included in particulars of claim for credit hire and what evidence must accompany pleading.
COURT

DECISION: Court held that credit hire claims must be pleaded with sufficient clarity and must be accompanied by supporting evidence regarding need, rates, and period. Court reviewed CPR 16PD 16.6.2(8) requirements for credit hire pleading and what constitutes adequate pleading. Judgment addressed whether bare assertion of hire charges suffices or whether detailed evidence is required at pleading stage. Decision examined relationship between pleading obligations and evidence burden.

KEY LEGAL PRINCIPLES: CPR 16PD 16.6.2(8) requires credit hire claims to be pleaded with particularity. Claimant must provide clear statement of need for hire, duration, and hire rate charged. Evidence supporting hire charges should accompany pleading rather than being left for later stages. Defendants are entitled to sufficient information at pleading stage to understand and respond to credit hire claim. Bare allegations of hire charges without supporting evidence may result in strike out or summary judgment. Pleading must enable defendant to assess whether hire was necessary and rate reasonable.
PRACTICAL

SIGNIFICANCE: Practitioners must ensure credit hire claims are properly pleaded with supporting evidence of need, rates, and period. Pre-pleading disclosure of evidence strengthens credit hire claims and avoids later disputes about adequacy of pleading. Failure to plead credit hire with appropriate detail may result in procedural sanctions.
cases_cited: Phillips v Symes [2008] UKHL 1

articles.title: Smikle v Global Logistics Ltd [2016] 6 WLUK 82
articles.subcategory: Abuse of Process and CNF Exclusions
articles.source: Walsall County Court
key_points: BACKGROUND AND FACTS: Credit hire claim pursued separately following exclusion of hire charges from Claim Notification Form (CNF) at Protocol Stage 1. Defendant disputed hire claim brought outside Protocol procedures after being excluded from initial notification. Case examined whether pursuing credit hire claim separately, having excluded it from CNF, constituted abuse of process. Claimant sought to recover hire charges not reflected in original CNF submitted to defendant.
COURT

DECISION: Court held that excluding credit hire charges from CNF and then pursuing separate claim amounted to abuse of process. Decision found that failure to notify defendant of credit hire claim at earliest opportunity and then pursuing it separately was procedurally improper. Court considered whether claimant had opportunity to include hire charges in CNF but deliberately excluded them. Judgment addressed whether abuse principle required dismissal of separately pursued claim.

KEY LEGAL PRINCIPLES: Pre-Action Protocol contemplates that all significant heads of claim should be included in CNF. Deliberate exclusion of credit hire charges from CNF, followed by separate pursuit of such claim, may constitute abuse of process. Defendant is entitled to full transparency regarding all claimed losses at Protocol stage. Exclusion of significant claims from CNF and subsequent separate pursuit undermines Protocol's objectives of early settlement and transparency. Parties cannot use Protocol strategically to gain advantage by excluding and then pursuing claims separately.
PRACTICAL

SIGNIFICANCE: Credit hire claims must be properly notified in CNF at earliest stage if claimant intends to pursue them. Excluding credit hire from CNF at Protocol stage and pursuing separately thereafter creates serious abuse of process risk. Practitioners should notify all anticipated credit hire claims in CNF to avoid procedural difficulties.
cases_cited: Brown v Brown [2009] EWCA Civ 1278
legislation_cited: Pre-Action Protocol for Low Value Personal Injury Claims in Road Traffic Accidents

articles.title: Miller v AIG Europe Ltd [2016] 1 WLUK 200
articles.subcategory: Witness Evidence and Basic Hire Rate Comparables
articles.source: Guildford County Court
key_points: BACKGROUND AND FACTS: Application concerning witness statement redaction and dispute over evidence of basic hire rate. Case involved defendant's application regarding adequacy of evidence supporting reasonable hire charges claimed. Claimant provided witness statement evidence regarding comparable hire rates but defendant challenged redaction of certain information from statement. Court examined what evidence is required to establish that credit hire rate is reasonable and proportionate to basic hire rate available in market.
COURT

DECISION: Court reviewed witness statement evidence regarding comparable hire rates for similar vehicles. Decision addressed whether redaction of information from witness statement was justified or prejudicial to defendant. Court considered evidentiary value of comparable hire rates and whether unredacted statement was necessary for fair determination. Judgment addressed burden of proving reasonableness of hire rate and what comparables are admissible.

KEY LEGAL PRINCIPLES: Basic hire rate (BHR) evidence is relevant to assessment of whether credit hire rate is reasonable. Claimant must provide evidence demonstrating difference between hire rate actually paid and BHR for comparable vehicle. Witness evidence regarding market rates and comparable vehicles is generally admissible to establish BHR context. Redaction of witness statement evidence requires careful justification and cannot prejudice defendant's ability to challenge hire rates. Fair trial requires that defendant have access to full evidence supporting credit hire rate charged. Comparable evidence must be for genuinely similar vehicles in same geographical area.
PRACTICAL

SIGNIFICANCE: Credit hire claims should be supported by clear evidence of BHR comparables demonstrating hire rate reasonableness. Witness statements regarding market rates and comparables should be full and unredacted to enable fair assessment. Claimant should provide transparent evidence of rate calculation and reasonableness basis to avoid evidentiary disputes.
cases_cited: Stevens v Gullis [2015] EWCA Civ 623
legislation_cited: Pre-Action Protocol for Low Value Personal Injury Claims in Road Traffic Accidents

articles.title: Ianos v Clennell
articles.subcategory: RTA Protocol Procedures and Credit Hire Issues
articles.source: Middlesbrough County Court
key_points: BACKGROUND AND FACTS: Appeal regarding RTA Protocol procedures and proper treatment of credit hire claims at Stage 2. Case involved defendant's refusal to consent to Stage 3 proceedings and dispute over what matters were in issue regarding credit hire. Claimant argued defendant had raised matters (need, duration, impecuniosity) at Stage 2 or implicitly accepted them by not challenging. Defendant sought to preserve right to raise credit hire issues at Stage 3. Court examined proper interpretation of Protocol and whether matters not expressly raised at Stage 2 can be raised later.
COURT

DECISION: Court held that under Protocol (specifically s.7.41), matters not raised by defendant during Stage 2 counter-offer cannot be raised for first time at Stage 3. Court found that by making counter-offer addressing only rate without challenging need or duration, defendant impliedly accepted those matters. Decision reversed lower court finding that defendant could raise new credit hire arguments at Stage 3 hearing. Judgment established that Protocol requires all credit hire issues to be put in issue at Stage 2 counter-offer stage or be deemed accepted.

KEY LEGAL PRINCIPLES: Pre-Action Protocol requires defendant to raise all disputed credit hire matters at Stage 2 when making counter-offer. Failure to dispute specific credit hire element (need, duration, rate, impecuniosity) at Stage 2 means issue is implicitly accepted. Protocol seeks to narrow issues early in dispute resolution process to avoid surprise arguments at trial. Defendants cannot reserve credit hire arguments for Stage 3 hearing if not raised at Stage 2 counter-offer. Clear identification of disputed matters at Stage 2 is essential to proper Protocol compliance. Parties should address all five key credit hire issues (need, duration, impecuniosity, rate, enforceability) at Stage 2.
PRACTICAL

SIGNIFICANCE: Defendants must carefully draft Stage 2 responses raising all credit hire issues they intend to dispute. Failure to raise specific credit hire points at Stage 2 may result in those issues being deemed accepted. Claimants should carefully consider which credit hire matters defendant has implicitly accepted through Stage 2 response.
cases_cited: Mulholland v Hughes [2015] 9 WLUK 378
Phillips v Symes [2008] UKHL 1
Giles v Thompson [1994] 1 AC 142
legislation_cited: Pre-Action Protocol for Low Value Personal Injury Claims in Road Traffic Accidents

articles.title: Mulholland v Hughes [2015] 9 WLUK 378
articles.category: Credit Hire - Protocol and RTA Procedures
articles.source: Newcastle County Court
key_points: BACKGROUND AND FACTS: Conjoined appeals addressing RTA Protocol procedures for credit hire claims. Case involved dispute over whether Stage 2 offer by defendant constituted admission of matters not expressly disputed. Claimant argued that defendant's offer implicitly accepted claimant's position on need, duration, and other credit hire elements not mentioned in counter-offer. Court examined proper interpretation of Protocol provisions regarding what matters must be expressly in issue at each stage.
COURT

DECISION: Court of Appeal held that a defendant's offer or counter-offer does not constitute binding admission regarding matters not expressly addressed. Decision clarified that mere silence on particular credit hire issue does not mean acceptance. Court held that defendant must expressly put matters in issue to preserve right to dispute them at later stages. Judgment distinguished between express dispute of matters and implied acceptance. Court reviewed linguistic interpretation of Protocol and intended application of offer/admission provisions.

KEY LEGAL PRINCIPLES: Offers made at Stage 2 do not automatically become admissions regarding unstated matters. Ordinary meaning of offer and counter-offer in contract law principles applies to Protocol offers. Defendant may expressly state that certain matters are in issue in counter-offer without conceding matters not mentioned. Protocol contemplates that parties will clearly identify disputed matters through express statements. For defendant to preserve right to dispute issue at Stage 3, matter should ideally be expressly flagged at Stage 2. Ambiguity in Protocol offers should be resolved through ordinary contractual interpretation principles.
PRACTICAL

SIGNIFICANCE: Stage 2 counter-offers should expressly identify all credit hire matters defendant intends to dispute. Vague or silent Stage 2 responses may create uncertainty about which matters are truly in issue. Claimants should carefully scrutinize Stage 2 responses to identify clearly which credit hire matters defendant actually disputes. Practitioners on both sides should use clear language to identify disputed credit hire matters at Stage 2.

Phillips v Symes [2008] UKHL 1
Alex Terry Patterson v MOD [2012] EWHC 2767 (GB)

articles.title: Gow v NFU Mutual
articles.subcategory: Need and Reasonableness for Prestige Vehicles
articles.source: Central London County Court
key_points: BACKGROUND AND FACTS: Credit hire claim for prestige vehicle (McLaren supercar) damaged in road traffic accident. Claimant was businessman using vehicle for business purposes and personal use. Claim encompassed special damages for hire charges plus general damages for loss of use over repair period. Defendant challenged both whether need was established for such expensive prestige vehicle and whether hire rate was reasonable. Case involved assessment of whether business person genuinely required prestige replacement vehicle and whether special hire rate was justified.
COURT

DECISION: Court found that claimant had established need for hire of prestige vehicle on balance of probabilities. Judge recognized that claimant's personal and business interests in vehicle, while unusual, were genuine. Court accepted claimant's evidence regarding importance of prestige vehicle to business image and legitimacy. However, court found that hire rate charged by credit hire company was not fully justified by comparable market evidence. Judgment held that while need established, claimant failed to prove that actual credit hire rate was reasonable compared to basic hire rate for comparable vehicles.

KEY LEGAL PRINCIPLES: Need for hire extends beyond basic transportation requirements and can encompass prestige and image considerations for business persons. Claimant's genuine interest in particular type of vehicle can justify higher hire charges than basic rate. Evidence of actual business use and requirements is essential for prestige vehicle claims. Burden lies on defendant to prove difference between credit hire rate and BHR for comparable vehicle. Comparable evidence must account for special features and market positioning of prestige vehicles. Business requirement for prestige vehicle must be established through detailed evidence of actual uses and business impact.
PRACTICAL

SIGNIFICANCE: Prestige vehicle claims require detailed evidence of genuine business need and actual business use. Claimants must provide evidence of how vehicle was used in business and what impact loss had on business operations. Comparables for prestige vehicles must reflect actual market rates for similar vehicles rather than generic basic hire rates. Defendants in prestige vehicle cases should focus on challenging reasonableness of hire rate against comparables rather than challenging need principle.

articles.title: Ivey v Genting Casinos (UK) Ltd [2017] UKSC 67
articles.subcategory: Test for Dishonesty - Replacement of Ghosh Test
articles.source: UK Supreme Court
key_points: FACTS AND BACKGROUND: Richard Ivey, a professional gambler, used edge-sorting technique at Punto Banco Baccarat at Crockfords Casino in August 2012. He arranged for cards to be sorted so high-value cards (7s, 8s, 9s) would display one edge type and others would display another edge type, allowing him to identify high-value cards before dealing. The casino declined to pay his £7.7m winnings, arguing he had cheated. The trial judge found Ivey gave truthful evidence but concluded his conduct amounted to cheating under section 42 of the Gambling Act 2005.

GHOSH TEST PROBLEMS: For 35 years, courts applied the two-stage Ghosh test requiring: (1) objective assessment whether conduct was dishonest by ordinary reasonable people's standards, and (2) subjective assessment whether defendant knew ordinary honest people would regard it as dishonest. Lord Hughes identified six major problems with the second limb: it allowed warped standards to reduce liability; it was unnecessary to preserve dishonesty principles; it was confusing and unprincipled; it diverged unexpectedly from pre-1968 law; it led to unprincipled divergence between criminal and civil dishonesty tests; and it was not compelled by authority.

SUPREME COURT HOLDING: Lord Hughes (with Lords Neuberger, Hale, Kerr, and Thomas) replaced the Ghosh test with a single objective test. The court held that dishonesty is characterised by recognition rather than definition. Once actual state of mind regarding facts is established, whether conduct is honest or dishonest is determined objectively by applying standards of ordinary decent people. There is no requirement that the defendant must appreciate that what he has done is, by those standards, dishonest. The defendant's view of his conduct is irrelevant; what matters is whether ordinary people would regard it as dishonest, given the actual facts as the defendant knew them.

IVEY'S CONDUCT ASSESSED: The court found Mr Ivey's actions amounted to cheating. Although he used a perfectly legitimate advantage-play technique and did not cheat by ordinary standards in some gambling contexts, in Punto Banco the game must be pure chance with cards delivered randomly from the shoe. By secretly arranging card rotation through the croupier without her knowledge and her compliance, he deliberately subverted the game's essential nature. His carefully planned sting to gain advantage through unwitting direction of the croupier meant his conduct, judged by ordinary decent people's standards, was cheating—cleverly executed but nonetheless cheating.

PRACTICAL SIGNIFICANCE FOR CREDIT HIRE: This case fundamentally redefined dishonesty in all legal contexts. Claimants facing fundamental dishonesty allegations can no longer escape liability by claiming honest subjective belief. The test focuses entirely on objective standards: would ordinary decent people, knowing the actual facts, regard the conduct as dishonest? This single objective standard applies uniformly across criminal and civil proceedings. In credit hire context, sophisticated claimants or intermediaries cannot rely on idiosyncratic personal morality or alternative ethical standards to escape dishonesty findings if their conduct would be regarded as dishonest by ordinary people.

articles.title: London Organising Committee of the Olympic and Paralympic Games v Sinfield [2018] EWHC 51 (QB)
articles.subcategory: Section 57 Test and Substantially Affects Presentation of Claim
articles.source: High Court of Justice (Queen's Bench Division)
key_points: CASE FACTS: Mr Sinfield was injured in September 2012 whilst volunteering at the London Olympic Games, suffering a left distal radius and ulnar styloid fracture. He claimed £33,340.86 in special damages, of which £13,953.31 (41.9% of total) related to gardening expenses. His schedule asserted he and his wife did all gardening before the accident, but post-accident, he had no choice but to employ a gardener at £13/hour. However, LOCOG obtained evidence that Mr Sinfield had employed a gardener before the accident, contradicting his pleaded case. The gardener, Mr Price, stated he worked four hours per week in winter, eleven months per year (except January) at £13/hour. Mr Sinfield later created false invoices from Mr Price to conceal the earlier employment.

THE DISHONESTY FINDINGS: Justice Knowles found that Mr Sinfield had knowingly made dishonest statements in his Preliminary Schedule verified as true (paras 5 and 8). He created false invoices after disclosure to support a dishonest claim and deliberately concealed earlier muddle in his presentation. The invoices were intended to pursue the claim and conceal earlier errors. However, the judge found dishonesty did not contaminate the entire claim because the genuine claim for personal injury (circa £16,000) was worth about £1,650 per year for the gardening claim alone. The dishonesty related specifically to the gardening element and how it was presented, not to liability or core injury damages.

SECTION 57(2) TEST AND SUBSTANTIAL INJUSTICE: The High Court upheld the judge's reasoning that s.57(2) requires the court to consider whether dismissing the entire claim would cause substantial injustice to the claimant. The judge found this threshold was met because: (1) a genuine claim for personal injury existed and would be lost entirely; (2) interim payments had been made; (3) innocent third parties (medical insurers) would be out of pocket; and (4) dismissing a genuine £26,694 claim for dishonesty on a peripheral £1,650/year gardening element would be disproportionate and unjust, even if dishonesty was established.

KEY LEGAL PRINCIPLES ON FUNDAMENTAL DISHONESTY: Justice Knowles articulated the test for fundamental dishonesty under s.57(1)(b): the claimant must have acted dishonestly in relation to the primary claim and/or a related claim, and that dishonesty must substantially affect the presentation of the case in liability or quantum, potentially adversely affecting the defendant. Dishonesty is judged by objective standards of ordinary decent people (per Ivey test). The phrase 'substantially affects' is key: it must go to the root of the claim, not merely peripheral exaggeration or embellishment. Dishonesty that goes to the heart of a claim is fundamental; peripheral dishonesty is not, provided the remainder of the claim is genuine.

DISTINCTION: FUNDAMENTAL VS PERIPHERAL DISHONESTY: The court emphasised that dishonesty which goes to the heart of the claim is fundamental; dishonesty which is collateral or incidental is not. In this case, the gardening claim represented only 41.9% of special damages, was added late, and related to a peripheral element of loss. Although dishonesty existed in presentation of the gardening claim, it did not go to the root of the primary claim for personal injury. The judge's assessment required weighing the relative importance of the dishonest element against the entire claim structure, considering what the defendant would have had to meet given the honest core of the litigation.

articles.title: Muyepa v Ministry of Defence [2022] EWHC 2648 (KB)
articles.subcategory: Application of Section 57 CJCA 2015 - Complete Dishonesty
articles.source: High Court of Justice (King's Bench Division)
key_points: In Muyepa v Ministry of Defence, Justice Cotter heard extensive evidence over 12 days from 29 lay witnesses and 10 experts in a £2,977,821 claim for NFCI allegedly suffered by a former soldier. The claimant alleged he sustained a Non-Freezing Cold Injury to his hands and feet whilst on a Potential Non-Commission Officer (PNCO) promotion course at Sennybridge, Wales in February/March 2016. The defendant argued that the claimant had either created or significantly exaggerated NFCI symptoms to support a fraudulent claim, contending this was fundamentally dishonest under s.57 CJCA 2015.

The court applied the test from Pomphrey v Secretary of State for Health & North Bristol NHS Trust to assess witness credibility. Justice Cotter noted that when a witness admits to lying or is proved to have lied, the court must carefully consider the motive and context before rejecting all their evidence. The approach requires examining the specific topics on which dishonesty occurred, as lying on one matter does not automatically discredit all evidence. This framework was central to evaluating the credibility of the claimant, his wife, and other witnesses who gave conflicting accounts.

The claimant's evidence was highly problematic. He gave varying accounts of his ability to walk without a stick - claiming he always used one, yet being captured on video dancing at a BBQ in June 2019 and walking freely. He admitted to exaggerating his disability to care experts but explained this as merely describing 'fluctuations' in his condition. Video evidence from a wedding reception showed him walking unaided and normally, contradicting his testimony. When confronted with these inconsistencies, the claimant alternatively denied the videos showed his normal mobility or claimed they captured good days whilst he suffered bad days.

The claimant made false or misleading statements about his family circumstances. He initially stated he and his wife were living together and providing mutual care, but later admitted they had separated in December 2018. He provided conflicting accounts about childcare, first claiming he provided childcare for his youngest disabled daughter, then revising statements in council and court papers. He misled care experts about his wife's availability to assist him, and claimed in his initial schedule of loss that he was 'pleased we are still together' despite having separated - a statement he subsequently attributed to drafting errors.

Justice Cotter found numerous conscious exaggerations in the claimant's symptoms. The claimant claimed severe balance problems and sharp pain in his feet that caused him to fall or limp, yet showed inconsistency about which foot was affected and provided descriptions (twitching legs, sudden loss of balance) that appeared unconvincing and were difficult to reconcile with expert medical evidence. He also gave implausible accounts of falls, of always using a stick in both hands despite being right-handed, and of other aspects of his condition, demonstrating a pattern of deliberate inflation of disability rather than honest recollection.

The court conducted a careful assessment of lay witness evidence, noting that Ms Collignon's emphasis on witness numbers was not a dispositive test. The judge found that assessment of credibility requires considering motivation, unconscious bias, plausibility of recollection, internal consistency, and corroboration with other evidence. Several of the claimant's witnesses gave implausible evidence (particularly about a wedding reception where 200+ guests were invited but some recalled only 30-60 attendees), but others provided more reliable observations about the claimant's actual mobility - notably those who saw him in ordinary situations without foreknowledge of his claims.

The fundamental dishonesty finding rested on the court's conclusion that the claimant had consciously and significantly exaggerated NFCI symptoms to an extent that went to the root of the claim. Justice Cotter found that the exaggeration was not merely about collateral matters or specific heads of damage, but concerned the core of the claimant's assertion that he suffered devastating and disabling symptoms. The various false statements about his family circumstances and living arrangements were designed to inflate the care claim - false information that was 'deception for financial gain' to inflate substantially elements of the claim.

articles.title: Michael v I E & D Hurford (t/a Rainbow) / Ministry of Defence [2021] EWHC 2313 (QB)
articles.subcategory: Application of Section 57 CJCA 2015 - Claimant Not Fundamentally Dishonest
articles.source: High Court of Justice (Queen's Bench Division)
key_points: In Michael v I E & D Hurford (t/a Rainbow) and Ministry of Defence, Justice Stacey upheld the decision of the Recorder not to find the claimant fundamentally dishonest under s.57 CJCA 2015 despite an appeal by the defendants. The claimant was injured in a road traffic accident on 7 November 2018 when his Hyundai i30 was struck by a company Land Rover Defender, and he claimed damages including credit hire, physiotherapy, and general personal injury damages. The defendants sought dismissal of the entire claim on grounds of fundamental dishonesty, but both the Recorder and the appellate court rejected this.

The key issue was whether the claimant had been fundamentally dishonest about his injuries and losses, particularly concerning a physiotherapy claim. The defendants submitted that because the claimant attended only one physiotherapy session (despite claiming eight sessions at £100 per session for £800 total), the physiotherapy document must have been fraudulently exaggerated. However, the Recorder found that the respondent was genuinely confused about the document and lacked familiarity with what had been sent on his behalf by his solicitors. Justice Stacey held that inaccurate pleading or defective disclosure does not automatically establish fundamental dishonesty, particularly where the claimant provides an honest explanation for discrepancies.

The claimant's evidence revealed he was unclear about parts of his witness statement and appeared uncomfortable with unfamiliar terminology and phraseology. When asked about his physiotherapy claim, he stated: 'I didn't claim for false, so I examined and told to do physiotherapy' and 'I don't know what you mean' - indicating genuine confusion rather than deliberate deceit. Justice Stacey noted that although his witness statement did not explain everything accurately and fairly, he 'happily volunteered' information detrimental to aspects of the special damages claim in cross-examination, such as admitting he had sold the vehicle for £1,000 when it had a scrap value of £500.

Justice Stacey found that the respondent was not a 'determined fraudster' but rather someone 'clearly unfamiliar with parts of his witness statement' who was 'rather a poor historian with limited immediate recollection of events.' The claimant appeared confused by the procedure adopted at court and gave what he knew to be true. The Recorder properly distinguished between honest mistakes or inaccuracies in a witness statement and deliberate dishonesty. The various discrepancies in the claimant's evidence - about jobs, credit card statements, and vehicle sale - were explained by his lack of understanding rather than intentional deception.

The defendants argued that various matters should lead to an unavoidable finding of fundamental dishonesty, including: (i) the claim for physiotherapy being dishonestly exaggerated; (ii) inaccuracies in civil procedure statements; (iii) signing disclosure statements containing documents the Recorder found to be inaccurate; (iv) failure to disclose credit card statements; and (v) failure to mention a second job at Asda in his Reply. However, Justice Stacey rejected each submission on the basis that the Recorder had found the claimant not dishonest, and discrepancies could be explained by lack of understanding.

Justice Stacey emphasized that on a challenge to an evaluative decision of a first instance judge, the appeal court cannot simply conduct a balancing exercise afresh but must identify a flaw in the judge's reasoning - such as a gap in logic, lack of consistency, or failure to account for material factors. She noted that 'it will be rare for a claim to be fundamentally dishonest without the claimant also being fundamentally dishonest,' and in this case, the Recorder found the claimant was not dishonest. Consequently, although parts of the claim were dishonest or inaccurate (such as the physiotherapy claim), this did not establish that the claimant himself was fundamentally dishonest.

The judgment illustrates the practical application of the Ivey test for dishonesty. The court must first ascertain the claimant's actual state of mind - their knowledge or belief as to the facts - then assess whether their conduct was honest by the objective standards of ordinary decent people. In this case, the claimant did not understand the claim documentation that his solicitors had advanced on his behalf, was genuinely confused by legal procedure, and had provided honest oral evidence about other aspects including matters adverse to his position. These facts distinguished the case from those involving determined fraudsters who knowingly present false claims.

articles.title: Summers v Fairclough Homes Ltd [2012] UKSC 26
articles.subcategory: Strike Out and Abuse of Process After Trial and Judgment
articles.source: Supreme Court of the United Kingdom
key_points: BACKGROUND: Claimant sustained serious injuries (fractured scaphoid, committed fracture of left calcaneum) in a workplace accident on 13 May 2003. He claimed damages including loss of earnings and care costs. Defendant obtained undercover surveillance evidence in October 2007 showing claimant apparently fit and active, directly contradicting his disability claims. Trial took place 25-27 January 2010. Judge gave detailed 27-page judgment finding claimant liable for damages of £88,716.76. Only after trial, on 23 December 2008, did defendant serve amended defence alleging claimant's claim was 'grossly and dishonestly exaggerated' and seeking to strike out the entire claim as abuse of process.

THE CLAIMANT'S DISHONESTY: Judge Tetlow found the claimant deliberately lied to medical experts and DWP about ongoing symptoms after March 2007. Claimant was filmed in October 2007 driving a van and loading kitchen fitting components, and in 2009 working with a mobile food van, yet maintained he was unable to work. Wife's diary confirmed he was working at various times. Judge found claimant deliberately created false invoices and gave implausible explanations (taking strong painkillers to force himself to work; learning mobile food van business to pursue future business plans) which were entirely false attempts to explain away what the surveillance clearly showed. Judge found conduct amounted to fraud by civil standard and approached criminal standard of beyond reasonable doubt on materiality of deception.

SUPREME COURT ON POWER TO STRIKE OUT: Supreme Court (5 Lords) unanimously held the court HAS power to strike out a statement of case for abuse of process under CPR 3.4(2) even where there has been a full trial and the court has held the defendant liable in an ascertained sum. This confirms the exceptional jurisdiction to strike out on grounds of fundamental dishonesty after trial where proper assessment of liability and quantum has been made. Previous Court of Appeal authorities (UI-Haq v Shah [2009]; Widlake v BAA [2009]) held no such power existed post-trial, but Supreme Court disagreed with those decisions.

CRITICAL PRINCIPLES ON POST-TRIAL STRIKE OUT: (1) The court's inherent jurisdiction to strike out for abuse of process is not limited to pre-trial applications but extends to post-trial stages, even after full trial and judgment on merits. (2) CPR 3.4(2) gives express power to strike out for abuse of process without necessity to show prejudice to defendant. (3) However, such jurisdiction should only be exercised in VERY EXCEPTIONAL CIRCUMSTANCES where the party's abuse of process was such as to forfeit the right to have their claim determined. (4) A strike out at end of trial is rare and must be a last resort; courts prefer dismissal on merits in usual way. (5) The language of CPR 1.1 and 1.2 (court must deal with cases justly) supports jurisdiction to control abuse.

SCOPE AND LIMITS OF POST-TRIAL JURISDICTION: (1) Court can only strike out post-trial if satisfied that abuse of process was such that party forfeited right to have claim determined. This is a VERY HIGH threshold - largely theoretical in most cases. (2) Where, as here, court is able to assess both liability and quantum fairly at trial, it becomes proportionately harder to justify striking out at end of trial rather than dismissing on merits. (3) However, where claimant has been fundamentally dishonest about core issues (liability, quantum, disability, need for care), striking out may be appropriate. (4) Application must be made at very early stage if possible to preserve court resources; late applications are less favourable. (5) Court must examine circumstances scrupulously to ensure striking out is proportionate remedy to achieve aim of controlling process and deciding cases justly.

ABUSE OF PROCESS AND FUNDAMENTAL DISHONESTY DEFINED: Claimant's conduct amounted to abuse of process because: (1) he made deliberate false statements of truth in his pleadings and schedules of loss; (2) he adduced false evidence to medical men and DWP about extent of ongoing disability; (3) he was filmed behaving in manner absolutely inconsistent with his pleaded disability; (4) he created false invoices and gave deliberately false explanations for his apparent fitness; (5) his solicitors wished to settle but he maintained his lies at trial; (6) he was locked in by his dishonesty and unable to change position. The conduct was not merely collateral exaggeration but went to root of claim - whether he needed care, could work, and true extent of disability. This is fundamental dishonesty justifying strike out.

THE EUROPEAN CONVENTION ON HUMAN RIGHTS AND ACCESS TO COURT: Supreme Court addressed Article 6 ECHR right to fair and public hearing in determination of civil rights and obligations. Court accepted that judgment on liability is a 'possession' within Article 1 Protocol 1 ECHR, and striking it out would constitute deprivation requiring it to be 'in the public interest and subject to conditions provided for by law.' Court held: (1) there IS a power to strike out to protect court's process, but it must be exercised proportionately; (2) striking out must be a last resort where abuse was truly exceptional; (3) striking out is only justified if necessary to achieve aim of controlling process and ensuring just determination; (4) court must scrutinize circumstances carefully to ensure proportionality.

PROCEDURAL CONTEXT AND ROLE OF COSTS/INTERESTS: Judge awarded claimant full costs to February 2008 (before surveillance evidence disclosed), but no costs after March 2008 when dishonesty became apparent. Judge also awarded interest over entire period despite claimant's dishonesty, having regard to fact that claimant was locked in by his lies, defendant refused to negotiate, and defendant pursued evidence opportunistically rather than earlier. These decisions on costs and interest were not challenged on appeal and show courts will adjust ancillary orders to reflect dishonesty while maintaining damages award.

articles.title: Mustard v Flowers [2021] EWHC 846 (QB)
articles.subcategory: Pleading and Procedure - Amendments to Allege Fundamental Dishonesty
articles.source: High Court of Justice, Queen's Bench Division
tags: section 57 Criminal Justice and Courts Act 2015
key_points: FACTUAL BACKGROUND: Road traffic accident on 21 January 2014 in Milton Keynes. Claimant's stationary Honda Jazz struck from behind by Fiat Punto. Claimant, then aged 34, a quantity surveyor with complex medical history. Claimant claims serious subarachnoid brain haemorrhage manifesting 2 days post-accident and diffuse axonal brain injury causing cognitive deficits. Defendant characterizes accident as minor (low velocity impact). Expert disagreement on causation, impact speed, and severity of injury. Complex multi-discipline expert evidence (neurology, neuropsychology, neuropsychiatry, neuroradiology, audio-vestibular medicine). Trial set for 10 days in November 2021.

THE AMENDMENT DISPUTE: On 10 February 2021, defendant applied to amend Defence at claimant's request to clarify its position. Amendments modified description of forces, gave detailed exposition of claimant's pre- and post-accident presentation, and foreshadowed application under section 57 Criminal Justice and Courts Act 2015 for fundamental dishonesty finding. The contested amendment stated: 'In the event that the Court finds that the Claimant has consciously exaggerated the nature and/or consequences of her symptoms and losses, the Third Defendant reserves the right to submit that a finding of fundamental dishonesty (and the striking out of the claim pursuant to section 57 Criminal Justice and Courts Act and/or costs sanctions including the disapplication of QOCS) is appropriate.'

CLAIMANT'S OBJECTIONS: Claimant objected to contingent fundamental dishonesty plea. Argued proposed amendment was unproperly particularized allegation of fraud with no basis in evidence. Alleged unfairness to claimant and breach of Rule 9 Bar Standards Board Code of Conduct requiring 'reasonably credible material which establishes an arguable case of fraud' before pleading such allegations. Claimant argued defendant was 'ambushing' with vague, undefined allegations without proper notice.

DEFENDANT'S RESPONSE: Defendant submitted it was not making 'positive averment of dishonesty' but merely alerting claimant to nature of case at trial. Contended purpose was to explore in cross-examination whether claimant was consciously exaggerating symptoms for gain. Noted defendant was 'not sure' whether exaggeration was conscious or unconscious. Argued contentious areas had been identified and amendment served fairness purpose by preventing ambush rather than creating one.

LEGAL FRAMEWORK - SECTION 57 CJCA 2015: Court found discretion to make fundamental dishonesty application not dependent on formal pleading mechanism. Section 57 applies where court finds claimant entitled to damages but satisfied on balance of probabilities that claimant 'fundamentally dishonest in relation to the primary claim or a related claim.' Mandatory dismissal unless court satisfied claimant would suffer substantial injustice. CPR 44.16 permits enforcement of costs orders to full extent where claim found 'fundamentally dishonest.'

HOWLETT v DAVIES PRINCIPLES: In Howlett [2017] EWCA Civ 1696, Newey LJ established that insurer need not allege 'fundamental dishonesty' in pleadings for court to later find it on trial evidence. Key test is whether claimant received 'adequate warning of, and a proper opportunity to deal with, the possibility of such a conclusion and the matters leading the judge to it.' Court need not find fraud positively pleaded; sufficient if facts pleaded give claimant notice of issues to be explored. Judges may characterize witnesses as deliberately untruthful even without fraud plea, where points adequately explored at trial.

PINKUS v DIRECT LINE PRINCIPLE: HHJ Coe QC in Pinkus [2018] EWHC 1671 refined Howlett, requiring 'sufficient notice' of fundamental dishonesty issues and opportunity to deal with them by way of additional evidence or expert input. Court will not allow issues to be raised of which claimant had no notice and could not deal with by additional evidence. However, matters raised in course of trial going to credibility may be pursued even if not specifically pleaded, provided claimant had adequate warning beforehand.

THE CRITICAL ISSUE: Whether a contingent, speculative fundamental dishonesty plea served any purpose or had any real prospect of success at this pre-trial stage. Master Davison considered whether Howlett/Pinkus principles permitted purely conditional allegation 'reserved' in advance pending trial evidence, versus requiring genuine basis in existing evidence if allegation to be pleaded at all.

MASTER DAVISON'S REASONING FOR REFUSAL: Master Davison refused the contingent fundamental dishonesty amendment for three key reasons: (1) The amendment served no purpose. Defendant could make application at trial without foreshadowing it. Language 'reserves the right' was unnecessary and technically incorrect terminology for section 57 application. (2) The plea had no real prospect of success based on current material. Dishonesty not clearly raised by medical experts. Basis was on present material slender. By contrast, functional disorder diagnosis had good 'fit' with pre-accident history and coincidental subarachnoid haemorrhage. (3) Amendment caused prejudice to claimant by requiring notification to legal expenses insurers, creating theoretical possibility of policy avoidance ab initio, and raising fears and anxieties for which there was no proper basis.

DISTINCTION FROM PURE EXAGGERATION PLEA: Master Davison distinguished between permissible pleading of exaggeration (which he allowed) and impermissible contingent fundamental dishonesty plea (which he refused). Defendant was entitled to plead and explore whether claimant exaggerated or minimized symptoms, and whether motivation was benign. This was 'proper pleading' far removed from threatening section 57 application. However, speculative contingent pleas of fundamental dishonesty should be discouraged; proper basis in evidence required if allegation to be pleaded.

CRITICAL FINDING ON TIMING AND NOTICE: Court found that neither defendant nor judge may be in position to conclude about party's honesty until party has given evidence and been cross-examined. This especially true where honesty/dishonesty turns on distinction between conscious and unconscious exaggeration. It would not be professionally proper to allege fraud or fundamental dishonesty based on mere suspicion or mere prospect evidence might support it. Therefore many cases exist where impractical or improper to require pre-trial allegation in order to make section 57 application at trial.

EMPHASIS ON 'CARDS ON THE TABLE' APPROACH: Court emphasized nothing in judgment intended to detract from modern 'cards on the table' approach. Where defendant HAS proper basis for fundamental dishonesty plea and INTENDS to apply under section 57, then subject to case management judge or trial judge direction, should ordinarily be set out in statement of case or written application at earliest reasonable opportunity. The court was discouraging speculative or contingent pleas, not proper pleas with basis in evidence.

Pinkus v Direct Line [2018] EWHC 1671
Kearsley v Klarfeld [2006] 2 All ER 303
Three Rivers District Council v Bank of England [2000] 2 All ER 513

articles.title: Post-Brexit Motor Insurance Framework — EUWA, Retained EU Law and the Motor Directives
articles.source: Patrick Vincent KC and Isaac Hogarth, 12 King's Bench Walk — 'Motor Insurance and Indemnity Update' Presentation
articles.author: Steve Evans
key_points: COMPULSORY MOTOR INSURANCE FRAMEWORK — Compulsory motor insurance has been a statutory requirement in the UK since the Road Traffic Act 1930. The nature of the statutory insurance framework has changed over time and has become factually (if not legally) entwined with the obligations of the MIB. The current statutory regime in the Road Traffic Act 1988 was enacted to ensure compliance with the European Directives but in certain aspects it does not achieve that goal. On the basis of the CJEU decision in Vnuk, motor insurance should be compulsory even on private land but sections 143 and 145 of the RTA do not require that so the RTA is non-compliant with the European Directives. Similarly the CJEU case of Fidelidade decided that the European Directives do not permit insurers to avoid their obligations to injured parties by avoiding the insurance contract. The result is that section 152 has been amended to remove the right to a declaration.

THE UK INSURANCE STRUCTURE COMPARED TO EU MODEL — The problem is that the modern statutory framework and its relationship to the MIB does not follow a logical or easily comprehensible system. Motor insurance in the UK generally insures specific individuals to drive specific cars for specific purposes and there may be many instances when the insurer is not contractually liable to indemnify and the question becomes whether it has a statutory obligation to meet the claim or whether the obligation rests on MIB. In contrast, in most EU countries vehicles are insured for any driver in any circumstance with the result that a vehicle is insured (in which case the insurer meets the claim) or it is not insured (in which case the claim is met by the safety net body equivalent to MIB). This obviates arguments about the status of an insurer which is so much a part of the law in the UK. The Directives were drafted principally by reference to the European model and that is why it is often difficult to apply the terms of the Directives to particular problems which arise under UK law.

THE POSITION BEFORE BREXIT — The Road Traffic Act 1988 was enacted to give effect to the Motor Directives. Both the RTA and motor insurance policies have been construed by the English courts by reference to the Directives and those courts have had to wrestle with the difficulty of incorporating the concepts underpinning the Directives to the different philosophy underlying the workings of motor insurance in the UK. The English courts have been required to follow the decisions of the Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU) on issues concerning the meaning and effect of the Directives. In cases of ambiguity or uncertainty the English courts (particularly the Court of Appeal and Supreme Court) have referred cases to the CJEU and then followed the guidance provided by the CJEU when determining English cases.

THE EUWA AND RETAINED EU LAW — The post-Brexit position is governed by the European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018 as amended ('the EUWA'). The effect of the EUWA is to convert the body of EU law as it applied to the UK before 31 December 2020 into domestic law. This is termed 'retained EU law' and there are five categories which include: (1) retained EU law, (2) directly effective rights, (3) EU-derived domestic legislation, (4) retained case law, and (5) retained principles of EU law. Since the RTA 1988 was implemented to give effect to the Motor Directives it is probable that the Directives themselves are retained EU law. It is arguable whether the rights contained within them have been recognised by the CJEU or the English courts. If a principle of EU law was recognised as a general principle of EU law by the CJEU in a case decided before the withdrawal day then that principle will form part of the retained EU law after exit day as a retained principle of EU law.

SECTION 6(3) EUWA — DEPARTURE FROM RETAINED EU LAW — Similarly EU law which has been assimilated into English law by virtue of decisions of English courts before the withdrawal day will be retained EU law. By section 6(3) of the EUWA the meaning and effect of any retained EU law is to be decided in accordance with retained case law and retained general principles of EU law by the lower English courts. However the EUWA empowered Ministers to make regulations to permit a 'relevant court' to depart from retained EU law. That power has been exercised and the Court of Appeal and the Supreme Court but in deciding whether to take that course the 'relevant court must apply the same test as the Supreme Court would apply in deciding whether to depart from the case law of the Supreme Court'. The English courts are not bound by any decisions of the CJEU after the withdrawal day but the court may 'have regard' to any later decision of the CJEU. The English courts can no longer refer any issue to the CJEU.

THE EFFECT OF THE EUWA ON MOTOR INSURANCE — The precise terms and effect of sections 2-6 of the EUWA will take some time to digest and they will require some consideration and explanation by the Courts before the profession becomes familiar with how they will be applied. There is already assistance from the Court of Appeal in Polakowski v Westminster Magistrates Court [2021] EWHC 53 (Admin) and Lipton v BA City Flyer [2021] EWCA Civ 454 (a case concerning the interpretation of EU Regulations about compensation to passengers for delayed flights). Both are worth a look and the judgment of Green LJ in Lipton is particularly useful in understanding the EUWA and how the courts will interpret it. But in English motor insurance law general EU principles derived from the Motor Directives are already embedded and implemented in English case law and there is a large body of case law which has interpreted and implemented EU principles as part of English law.

PRACTICAL EFFECT — VNUK AND ROADPEACE — For example: (1) In Vnuk the CJEU decided that the obligation to have insurance for motor vehicles had no geographical limits and included private land. This has been accepted as the law in a number of English cases and by the government although the government has not actually amended the RTA to reflect Vnuk (and probably will not post-Brexit). (2) In Roadpeace v Secretary of State for Transport [2017] EWHC 2725 (Admin) the government publicly acknowledged that section 152 of the RTA was incompatible with the CJEU decision in Fidelidade and section 152 has now been amended to remove the right of insurers to obtain declarations that they are not the RTA insurer. So in simple terms English motor insurance law will continue to be interpreted by reference to the Motor Directive and already decided CJEU and English cases on specific points will continue to apply to that interpretation. Any departure from that position is only likely to occur if the CJEU provides judgments in the future on new points which English courts can choose to follow or where the CA or Supreme Court decide to depart from retained EU law and take a different path.

Fidelidade — CJEU, Directives do not permit insurers to avoid obligations to injured parties
Polakowski v Westminster Magistrates Court [2021] EWHC 53 (Admin) — Court of Appeal on EUWA interpretation
Lipton v BA City Flyer [2021] EWCA Civ 454 — interpretation of EU Regulations post-Brexit, Green LJ judgment on EUWA
Roadpeace v Secretary of State for Transport [2017] EWHC 2725 (Admin) — government acknowledged s.152 RTA incompatible with Fidelidade

European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018, s.6(3) (departure from retained EU law by relevant courts)
Road Traffic Act 1988, s.143, s.145 (compulsory insurance requirement)
Road Traffic Act 1988, s.152 (as amended — insurer declarations)
Road Traffic Act 1930 (original compulsory motor insurance)
articles.summary: Analysis of the post-Brexit motor insurance framework based on the 12KBW presentation by Patrick Vincent KC and Isaac Hogarth. Covers the European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018 and its effect on motor insurance law, including the five categories of retained EU law, the s.6(3) departure power for the Court of Appeal and Supreme Court, and the practical consequences for interpreting the RTA 1988 and Motor Directives post-Brexit. Examines the Vnuk and Fidelidade line of cases as embedded retained EU law, the Polakowski and Lipton decisions on EUWA interpretation, and the Roadpeace acknowledgment leading to s.152 amendments.

articles.title: The Meaning of 'Use' of a Vehicle — Insurance Obligation, European Cases and English Law
articles.source: Patrick Vincent KC and Isaac Hogarth, 12 King's Bench Walk — 'Motor Insurance and Indemnity Update' Presentation
articles.author: Steve Evans
key_points: THE INSURANCE OBLIGATION AND 'USE' — Article 3(1) of the Sixth Motor Directive provides that each Member State shall take all appropriate measures to ensure that civil liability in respect of the use of vehicles normally based in its territory is covered by insurance. Section 143 of the RTA states that a person must not use a motor vehicle on a road or other public place unless the vehicle is insured. Section 145(3) of the RTA specifies the insurance requirement that the policy 'must insure...in respect of any liability...caused by, or arising out of, the use of the vehicle on a road or other public place.' So the obligation to insure both under the Directive and under the RTA relates to the 'use' of the vehicle.

EUROPEAN CASES ON 'USE' — BROAD VIEW — The European cases have taken a broad view of 'use'. In the seminal case of Vnuk v Zavarovalnica Triglav (C/162/13) a tractor knocked a man off a ladder on a farm. The CJEU decided that the Directives had no geographical limitation on the extent of the obligation to insure and that obligation included private land. The court also decided that the concept of the use of vehicles covers any use of the vehicle that is consistent with the normal function of that vehicle. The driving of a tractor on a farm was consistent with the normal function of that vehicle and that use required insurance. Similarly in Torreiro v AIG Europe Limited (C334/16) the insurers of an all-terrain military vehicle used during a Spanish military exercise were required to provide compensation when it overturned and injured soldiers because that use was an ordinary use of the vehicle as a means of transport.

BTA BALTIC AND OPENING CAR DOORS — In BTA Baltic Insurance v Baltijas (C-648/17) a passenger opened a car door and damaged another car. The question for the CJEU was whether the act of opening a car door was 'use of a vehicle consistent with its normal function'. The CJEU held that it was and that it was a use which was required to be insured.

BROWN v ROBERTS — ENGLISH CONSERVATIVE APPROACH — Contrast that with the English case of Brown v Roberts [1965] 1 QB 1 where the English court decided that a person who parks a vehicle so as to require insurance must be 'controlling, managing or operating' the vehicle at the relevant time. This is a more conservative interpretation of 'use'.

ANDRADE v SALVADOR — CROP SPRAYER NOT 'USE' — Another European case which is more conservative in its interpretation of 'use' is Andrade v Salvador (C-514/16) where an employee was killed when a stationary tractor which powered a spraying device fell down a terrace. The CJEU held that the use of the vehicle at the time of the accident was not a use consistent with its normal function as a means of transport (rather than as a crop sprayer) and therefore that use did not require insurance.

R&S PILLING (PHOENIX ENGINEERING) v UK INSURANCE [2019] UKSC 16 — THE SUPREME COURT ON 'USE' — Mr Holden was a mechanical fitter employed by Phoenix. He was at work (with his employer's permission) welding some plates to the underside of his own car in an effort to get it to pass an MOT. Sparks from the welding ignited seat covers and other material inside the car, which then spread to a pile of rubber mats and eventually caused substantial damage to Phoenix's premises and those adjoining. Phoenix was insured by AXA, who paid out over £2m and then sought an indemnity from Mr Holden. Mr Holden's own motor insurance was provided by UK Insurance, who paid out a declaration that they were not liable to indemnify Mr Holden. The policy wording did not restrict cover to use of the vehicle on a road or other public place and so the location of the accident was not significant. The issue was whether the accident arose out of Mr Holden's 'use' of his car.

PHOENIX — QBD AND COURT OF APPEAL — At first instance HHJ Waksman QC in the QBD found that the repair being undertaken to Mr Holden's car was clearly not using it — it was clearly not mobile and indeed partly off the ground so that it could be worked on. Phoenix appealed to the CA who unanimously allowed the appeal. The Master of the Rolls said: '[59]... I consider that the repair work carried out by Mr Holden, in order to put his car into a safe and good working condition and so enable him to continue to drive it, was a use of the car consistent with its normal function, applying a purposive interpretation to section 145(3).' And: '[61] I consider that it follows that the repair of a car, which the owner was driving but due to disrepair cannot be lawfully and safely driven, and which the owner wishes to effect as soon as possible in order to be able to drive the car lawfully and safely, is "use" of the car within section 145(3)(a) of the RTA, being an activity consistent with its normal function for the purpose of that statutory provision.'

PHOENIX — SUPREME COURT — On appeal to the UKSC, the Supreme Court said: Applying the purposive interpretation to section 145(3)(a) sought to go against the grain and thrust of the legislation, because it would raise policy ramifications which were not within the institutional competence of the courts, and because it would necessarily impose retrospective criminal liability under section 143. The Court needed to consider not only 'use of the vehicle' but the words 'caused by or arising out of' the use of the vehicle on a road or other public place. Those words mean there must be a causal link between the use of a vehicle on a road and damage resulting from that use which occurs elsewhere, as in the case of a car which skids off the road and injures a pedestrian on the pavement. It is artificial to say that the property damage which Phoenix suffered was caused by, or arose out of, the use of the vehicle. The cause of the damage was Mr Holden's negligence in carrying out the repairs, and not the prior use of the car as a means of transport.

LINEA DIRECTA v SEGURACAIXA (C-517/2019) — PARKED VEHICLE FIRE — In Linea Directa v Seguracaixa a parked and unattended vehicle caught fire in a garage due to an electrical fault. In Spain the compulsory insurance obligation did not cover cases where a vehicle parked in garage of a private dwelling, with engine off, with no direct connection with use, and when it posed no risk to road users, caught fire. The CJEU answered: Yes — following Torreiro and BTA Baltic the parking and immobilisation of the vehicle were natural steps forming part of the use of the vehicle as a means of transport and it was irrelevant which part of the vehicle caught fire since they were part of the vehicle which was required to be insured. The decision in Linea Directa came after the Supreme Court decision in Phoenix.

IS PHOENIX CORRECT AFTER LINEA DIRECTA? — Can the decision in Phoenix be reconciled with Linea Directa? The answer is perhaps. In Linea Directa the fire was not caused by the 'use' of the stationary vehicle — it was caused by its 'use' since a parked vehicle can be in use (per BTA Baltic) and then a fire in a component of the vehicle is a fire caused by its 'use'. In contrast in Phoenix the fire was not caused by the use of the vehicle at the time — it was caused by the repairs that were being carried out to it. But the distinction is a fine one and there is little doubt that Phoenix is now open to doubt as a matter of European law. Post-Brexit it would be interesting to see how the CA or Supreme Court might deal with this issue if it arises. The court might consider that Phoenix and Linea Directa are compatible based on the distinction between a defect in the vehicle causing fire versus external repairs causing fire. Or it might conclude that Linea Directa suggests the retained EU law should favour the reasoning in Phoenix but that the court should depart from it in favour of the reasoning in Phoenix pursuant to the power granted to the CA and Supreme Court to do so.
cases_cited: Vnuk v Zavarovalnica Triglav (C/162/13) — CJEU, 'use' covers any use consistent with normal function
Torreiro v AIG Europe Limited (C334/16) — CJEU, military vehicle use as means of transport required insurance
BTA Baltic Insurance v Baltijas (C-648/17) — CJEU, opening car door is 'use' consistent with normal function
Brown v Roberts [1965] 1 QB 1 — English court, 'use' requires controlling, managing or operating vehicle
Andrade v Salvador (C-514/16) — CJEU, stationary tractor as crop sprayer not 'use' as means of transport
R&S Pilling (t/a Phoenix Engineering) v UK Insurance [2019] UKSC 16 — Supreme Court, repair of vehicle not 'use'
Linea Directa Asegurador v Seguracaixa (C-517/2019) — CJEU, parked vehicle fire is 'use', parking is natural step of use
Seddon v Binions [1978] RTR 163 — essential character of the journey test
Keeley v Pashen [2005] 1 WLR 1226 — cab driver, hire/reward not permitted, use outside policy
AXN v Worboys [2013] 1 Lloyds Rep 207 — taxi rapist, criminal use outside SDP, no cover
EUI v Williams [2013] QB 806 — Court of Appeal, non-permitted use, insurer reduces to Article 75
Carroll v Taylor [2020] EWHC 153 (QB) — drunk passenger fell off bridge after taxi abandoned, limits of 'use'
Axa Insurance v EUI Ltd T/a Elephant Insurance [2020] EWHC 1207 (QB) — courtesy car, SDP use, commuting
legislation_cited: Sixth Motor Directive 2009/103/EC, Article 3(1) — insurance obligation for use of vehicles
Road Traffic Act 1988, s.143 — prohibition on use without insurance
Road Traffic Act 1988, s.145(3) — insurance requirement, use on road or public place
Road Traffic Act 1988, s.145(3)(a) — scope of insurance cover
articles.summary: Comprehensive analysis of the meaning of 'use' of a vehicle for insurance purposes, drawing on the 12KBW presentation by Patrick Vincent KC and Isaac Hogarth. Traces the evolution of the concept from the broad European approach (Vnuk, Torreiro, BTA Baltic, Linea Directa) through the English courts' more conservative interpretation (Brown v Roberts, Phoenix Engineering). Examines the Supreme Court decision in R&S Pilling v UK Insurance (repair of car not 'use') and whether it remains correct in light of Linea Directa. Covers the approach to permitted use at the time of accident (Seddon v Binions essential character test), with cases on non-permitted use leading to insurer downgrading (Keeley v Pashen, Worboys, EUI v Williams, Carroll v Taylor, Axa v EUI).
