Article 201: Section 152 RTA Amendments — Downgrading Insurance Status and Practical Implications

articles.title: Section 152 RTA Amendments — Downgrading Insurance Status, Policy Cancellation and Practical Implications
articles.source: Patrick Vincent KC and Isaac Hogarth, 12 King's Bench Walk — 'Motor Insurance and Indemnity Update' Presentation
articles.author: Steve Evans
key_points: SECTION 152 AMENDMENTS EFFECTIVE 1 NOVEMBER 2019 — As a result of amendments to section 152 of the RTA since 1 November 2019 an insurer can no longer obtain a section 152 declaration that it is entitled to avoid a policy for misrepresentation or non-disclosure and thereby reduce its status from section 151 insurer to Article 75 insurer. The amendments mean that an insurer can now only avoid its section 151 liability and downgrade to Article 75 status in two ways: (1) before the accident the policy has been cancelled by mutual consent or by virtue of a provision contained in it (section 152(1)); or (2) the use of the vehicle at the time of the accident fell outside the use permitted by the policy.

DOWNGRADING ON BASIS OF TYPE OF USE — The inability of insurers to obtain section 152 declarations to reduce status to Article 75 insurer will mean a far greater consideration of whether the use at the time of the accident (whether by an insured or uninsured driver) was within the use permitted by the policy. The certificate will describe the use of the vehicle which is permitted by the policy (e.g. social, domestic and pleasure ('SDP'), commuting, business use etc). The terms of the policy also expand the meaning and extent of purposes that are permitted and those which are prohibited. So the policy may stipulate that any use which is not a use expressly permitted by the certificate is an excluded or prohibited use. Or the policy may say that use for particular purposes (e.g. to cause deliberate injury or damage) is excluded.

EUI v WILLIAMS — NON-PERMITTED USE REDUCES TO ARTICLE 75 — In EUI v Williams [2013] QB 806 the Court of Appeal held that if the use of the vehicle at the time of the accident was not a use permitted by the policy, in that case the use was SDP and the accident occurred during what the insurer said was not SDP, then the insurer had neither a contractual liability to indemnify nor a section 151 liability, thereby automatically reducing its status to Article 75. So if the use at the time of the accident is outside the terms of the policy the insurer can automatically reduce its status to Article 75.

APPROACH TO DETERMINING USE — SEDDON v BINIONS TEST — To determine whether the use to which the vehicle is being put at the time of the accident is a use permitted by the policy it is necessary to examine the facts to decide on the overall or primary purpose of the journey. The leading case is Seddon v Binions [1978] RTR 163 in which the CA said that it is necessary to examine 'the essential character of which the...accident occurred'. What is important is the insured/driver's purpose at the time of the accident.

KEELEY v PASHEN — HIRE/REWARD USE — In Keeley v Pashen [2005] 1 WLR 1226 a cab driver ran over some passengers. His insurance policy did not permit use for hire or reward. However the driver had finished his shift at the time of the accident and was returning home. The CA held that since he was no longer working and was on his way home the use was SDP use which was a permitted use under the policy.

WORBOYS — CRIMINAL USE — In AXN v Worboys [2013] 1 Lloyds Rep 207 (the taxi rapist) Silber J held that the essential character of the journey (looked at from Worboys' point of view) changed when he sedated his passengers and assaulted them. The judge found that the use of the vehicle for those purposes did not fall within either of the uses permitted by Worboys' policy ('public hire' or 'SDP') and so there was no cover.

CARROLL v TAYLOR — LIMITS OF USE — In the recent case of Carroll v Taylor [2020] EWHC 153 (QB) a drunk passenger had been robbed and then abandoned by a taxi driver. He then tried to walk home, but fell off a motorway bridge and sustained catastrophic injury. There were two questions: (1) Did C's injuries arise out of the use of the taxi on a road or other public place? and (2) What was the relevance of D1's deliberate criminal acts? Tipples J held that 'once a journey is at an end, what may or may not happen to a passenger after dejourneying has been completed is not a relevant use of the vehicle' — the Claimant's injuries had nothing whatsoever to do with 'the use of the vehicle on a road'. The taxi was being used for criminal enterprise throughout and the policy did not engage (per Keeley v Pashen).

AXA v EUI (ELEPHANT INSURANCE) — COURTESY CAR AND COMMUTING — In Axa Insurance v EUI Ltd T/a Elephant Insurance [2020] EWHC 1207 (QB) the Defendant had a policy with Elephant. His car was damaged and he was lent a courtesy car by the garage insured by Axa. The Elephant policy had a DOC extension which provided cover for SDP only and only covered the driving of a 'private car'. The Axa policy covered SDP and business use. The Defendant had been working as a security guard on a night shift and at the time of the accident he was on his way home but was going to divert to pick up a friend. Foster J held that the essential character of the accident was driving home from work and that this was not altered by the intention to divert to pick up the friend. That essential character was not social, domestic or pleasure but 'commuting' — home from work, and was covered by the SDP extension in the Elephant policy. The judge also found that a courtesy car owned by a garage was not a 'private' car for the purposes of the DOC extension in the Elephant policy. Both points are interesting: Axa was solely liable to meet the claim because the SDP cover only provided by the Elephant policy did not extend to commuting use.

RACING EXCLUSION — PINN v GUO — The importance of policy use exclusions as a way of downgrading status has meant that some insurance policies have been rewritten to extend the variety and type of excluded use and where there is more than one potential insurer both will look very carefully at the facts of particular cases and at the wording of their rivals' policies. Most policies have a racing exclusion. In Pinn v Guo, Zenith & AIG (Swansea County Court, 11 April 2014) Zenith insured D1 and AIG insured D3. Zenith obtained a section 152 declaration and downgraded its status to Article 75 but the AIG policy had a clause excluding cover for 'racing, pacemaking, speed-testing...' which is a fairly standard clause in many certificates and policies. D1 and D3 arranged a race with about 100 spectators. The vehicles lined up side by side, there was a countdown and they started the race. D1 lost control and hit some pedestrians. Perhaps not surprisingly the judge found that this was 'racing' as defined in the AIG policy and there was no cover. So AIG's status was reduced to Article 75 and they shared the claim with Zenith since both were Article 75 insurers.

WHAT USE CAN BE EXCLUDED POST-BREXIT? — Pinn is a relatively easy case to understand but many insurance policies now have extended exclusions which typically exclude use 'for racing, whether formal or informal, against another motorist and whether on a track or on a road'. Does this permit the insurer to refuse cover after an accident resulting from a 'burn up' away from traffic lights between 2 motorists who are strangers when one or both of them crashes and injures pedestrians? If one insurer has such a clause in its policy and the other does not, successful reliance on that clause would reduce one insurer's status to Article 75. As yet there is no authority for this. Is such a clause a proper and permissible exclusion of use or is it directly an impermissible exclusion in the guise of a use exclusion? How would such a clause be interpreted by reference to the Motor Directive post-Brexit? Many policies also have specific exclusions in respect of use 'for criminal purposes' and some extend the exclusion to say 'including avoiding lawful apprehension'. Does this mean that if the vehicle crashes whilst the driver is trying to escape from the police that the insurer can downgrade its status to Article 75 and then blame the accident on the police? A criminal purpose exclusion incompatible with the terms of the Directive was held by the CA to be incompatible with MIB in relation to Delaney v Secretary of State for Transport [2015] 1 WLR 5177 — but that was a case concerning use in the course or furtherance of a crime and was about a vehicle which was 'used in the course of a crime'. It is unclear how the courts will deal with these issues post-Brexit but the expansion of the concept of 'use' and excluded use beyond the well-recognised categories of SDP, business use, motor trade use etc does not make it easy to resolve the issues by reference to older authorities and the concept of 'the essential character of the journey'.

SARFRAZ v AKHTAR — 'ALLOWING' VEHICLE TO BE UNLAWFULLY TAKEN — In Sarfraz v Akhtar [2020] EWHC 782 (QB) C and D1 had been drinking with friends. D1 then drove C's car (not insured to drive). D1 took the keys from C's pocket. C travelled as front seat passenger. It was agreed that C not give D1 the keys but that D1 took them from his pocket, but C was too drunk to resist and reluctantly got into the car as an unwilling passenger. The insurer (D2) argued that because C had not consented to D1 taking the keys and driving, that he got into the car knowing that it had been unlawfully taken until it was driven away and that C was 'allowing himself to be carried' in a car knowing that it had been 'unlawfully taken' per section 151(4) RTA 1988. Pepperall J held that it was properly arguable that the car cannot be said to have been 'unlawfully taken' until it was driven away and that taking possession of the keys, sitting in the driver's seat and even turning the key are all acts that fall short of actually taking the car. The test for 'allowing' oneself to be driven is not mere presence in the car. For the purposes of a SJ application the judge accepted that C's sole purpose in getting into the car was to prevent D1 from taking it and was not therefore allowing himself to be driven.
cases_cited: EUI v Williams [2013] QB 806 — Court of Appeal, non-permitted use reduces insurer to Article 75
Seddon v Binions [1978] RTR 163 — essential character of the journey test
Keeley v Pashen [2005] 1 WLR 1226 — hire/reward use outside policy, returning home was SDP
AXN v Worboys [2013] 1 Lloyds Rep 207 — criminal use of taxi outside permitted uses
Carroll v Taylor [2020] EWHC 153 (QB) — limits of 'use', passenger abandoned by taxi
Axa Insurance v EUI Ltd T/a Elephant Insurance [2020] EWHC 1207 (QB) — courtesy car, SDP, commuting
Pinn v Guo, Zenith & AIG (Swansea County Court, 11 April 2014) — racing exclusion
Sarfraz v Akhtar [2020] EWHC 782 (QB) — 'allowing' vehicle to be unlawfully taken, s.151(4)
Delaney v Secretary of State for Transport [2015] 1 WLR 5177 — criminal purpose exclusion incompatible with Directive
Nagorski v Nikolics — County Court, MID entry not conclusive for s.151 liability
legislation_cited: Road Traffic Act 1988, s.152 (as amended 1 November 2019)
Road Traffic Act 1988, s.151 (duty of insurers to satisfy judgments)
Road Traffic Act 1988, s.151(4) (allowing oneself to be carried in unlawfully taken vehicle)
Road Traffic Act 1988, s.145(3) (scope of insurance — use on road or public place)
articles.summary: Detailed analysis from the 12KBW presentation by Patrick Vincent KC and Isaac Hogarth on the section 152 RTA amendments effective 1 November 2019 and their practical consequences for insurer status. Covers the two remaining routes to downgrade from section 151 to Article 75 (policy cancellation and non-permitted use), the Seddon v Binions essential character test, and key cases on permitted use (Keeley v Pashen, Worboys, Carroll v Taylor, Axa v EUI, Pinn v Guo racing exclusion). Examines unresolved questions about expanding use exclusions post-Brexit and the Sarfraz v Akhtar decision on 'allowing' vehicle to be unlawfully taken.

articles.title: Greenaway v Parrish & Covea & MIB — 'Stolen' Vehicle Definition Post-Brexit and the Effect of the EUWA
articles.source: Patrick Vincent KC and Isaac Hogarth, 12 King's Bench Walk — 'Motor Insurance and Indemnity Update' Presentation
articles.author: Steve Evans
key_points: GREENAWAY v PARRISH FACTS — In Greenaway v Parrish & Covea & MIB [2021] 4 WLR 97, four lads aged 16 took one of their father's cars without permission onto a road and D1 crashed it, injuring two of the lads (Greenaway and Rocks). In the claim against the insurers of the car under section 151 the claim was common ground that the vehicle had not been 'stolen' within the meaning of the Theft Act 1968 because there was no intention permanently to deprive.

CLAIMANTS' ARGUMENT — MOTOR DIRECTIVE DEFINITION — The Claimants argued that the only permitted exclusion in the Motor Directive is if the vehicle has been 'stolen' and that this word should be interpreted narrowly and in accordance with the Theft Act definition — i.e. there must be an intention permanently to deprive. Since there was no such intention, the vehicle had not been 'stolen' and the Directive exclusion did not apply.

COVEA'S ARGUMENT — POST-BREXIT INTERPRETATION — Covea disagreed and contended that pre-Brexit the issue would almost certainly have been referred to the CJEU but since that is no longer possible the Court should permit the parties to call expert evidence as to the meaning of the word 'stolen' under the law of different EU states (evidence which the CJEU would have had to consider). The Master refused Covea's application but the judge allowed Covea's appeal and permitted both parties to adduce the evidence of foreign law in four EU states.

SIGNIFICANCE FOR POST-BREXIT MOTOR INSURANCE — So in simple terms English motor insurance law will continue to be interpreted by reference to the Motor Directive and already decided CJEU and English cases on specific points will continue to apply to that interpretation. Any departure from that position is only likely to occur if the CJEU provides judgments in the future on new points which English courts can choose to follow or where the CA or Supreme Court decide to depart from retained EU law and take a different path. But the recent case of Greenaway v Parrish & Covea & MIB [2021] 4 WLR 97 illustrates one issue which has already arisen post-Brexit — the definition of 'stolen' vehicle for the purposes of the permitted exclusion under the Motor Directive, and the court's willingness to consider comparative European law evidence in the absence of the ability to refer to the CJEU.
cases_cited: Greenaway v Parrish & Covea & MIB [2021] 4 WLR 97 — stolen vehicle definition post-Brexit, comparative EU law evidence
legislation_cited: Theft Act 1968 (definition of 'stolen' — intention to permanently deprive)
Sixth Motor Directive 2009/103/EC (permitted exclusion for stolen vehicles)
European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018, s.6(3) (departure from retained EU law)
articles.summary: Analysis of Greenaway v Parrish & Covea & MIB [2021] 4 WLR 97, a significant post-Brexit case examining how the definition of 'stolen' vehicle in the Motor Directive's permitted exclusion should be interpreted now that the UK courts can no longer refer to the CJEU. The case involved youths taking a vehicle without permission (TWOC, not theft under the Theft Act 1968 as there was no intention to permanently deprive). The court allowed comparative European law evidence from four EU states to be adduced, illustrating the new approach to interpreting retained EU motor insurance law post-Brexit.

articles.title: Lewis v Tindale, Colley v Shuker and Recent MIB Liability Developments — Insurer Status, Emanation of State and Post-Brexit Position
articles.source: Patrick Vincent KC and Isaac Hogarth, 12 King's Bench Walk — 'Motor Insurance and Indemnity Update' Presentation
articles.author: Steve Evans
key_points: LEWIS v TINDALE — PRIVATE LAND ACCIDENT — In Lewis v Tindale [2019] 1 WLR 6229 the Claimant was seriously injured when walking on private land and when D1 ran him down. D1 was uninsured. MIB did not contest D1's liability but it contended that it had no contingent liability to the Claimant pursuant to the Uninsured Drivers Agreement 1999 because the accident and injuries were not caused by or arose out of the use of the vehicle on a road or other public place with the result that insurance under the RTA was not compulsory. The judge at first instance and the CA held that the accident did not fall within the compulsory requirements for insurance under section 145 of the RTA and nor was it possible to apply Marleasing principles to read down section 145(3) in order to comply with EU law following Vnuk.

LEWIS — MIB AS EMANATION OF STATE — But applying Farrell v Whitty (No 2) in which the CJEU had decided that MIB Ireland was also an emanation of the state, MIB was also an emanation of the state and its failure to implement the Directive as a result of the UK government's failure properly to make motor insurance on private land compulsory meant that MIB was liable to compensate the claimant.

IMPACT OF LEWIS — MAJOR IMPLICATIONS — This decision has major implications for MIB and its members (the motor insurance industry). In any off-road accident and in respect of any vehicle which is a 'vehicle' as defined in the Directives (which includes ride-on lawn mowers and golf carts) claimants will probably pursue MIB directly. The obligation on MIB to pay those claims will not be an obligation under the UDA or UTDA because those agreements only apply to liabilities which are required to be covered by the RTA (which only includes on-road accidents). MIB is likely to try and pass this liability onto the Secretary of State for Transport but the outcome of that is uncertain. The insurance industry might reasonably say: why should we pay for liabilities outwith the English law embodied in the RTA 1988 when we did not contract to do so and have not taken any premium for the risk?

OTHER IMPLICATIONS OF LEWIS — What is the position of the insurer who insures the vehicle only for use on the road which then causes an accident when being used off-road? If MIB has to pay the Article 75 insurer, can it look to the individual insurer to pay as Article 75 insurer? Probably No: the obligations of an Article 75 insurer derive solely from the requirement of the insurance to satisfy the RTA. Offroad use is not required to be insured under the RTA so any liability for offroad use is not an Article 75 liability. MIB's liability as a result of Lewis is a liability consequent upon the Directives and its position as an emanation of the state. It is arguable that it cannot pass that liability on to an Article 75 insurer. What is the limit of the amount for which MIB can be liable following Lewis? Under the RTA the liability of the insurer is unlimited in relation to personal injury. Under Art.9 of the Directive (as amended), the requirements are at least €1.22m per injury or €6.07m per claim (whatever the number of victims). In a claim against MIB on a Lewis basis can MIB rely on the minimum requirements under the Directive? Uncertain: Watch this space!

COLLEY v SHUKER (2019) — UKI DECLARATION AND DIRECTIVE INCOMPATIBILITY — In Colley v Shuker the Claimant (C) was injured in a vehicle driven by Daniel Shuker (D1). The vehicle had been insured to drive under the policy with UKI but D1 was not insured to drive and C knew that he was uninsured. UKI obtained a section 152 declaration. C brought a claim against UKI on the basis that following Fidelidade the Court should determine (a) that UKI's declaration under section 152 was incompatible with the Directive and (b) use Marleasing principles to prevent UKI avoiding its liability to C under section 151. The claim was dismissed by O'Farrell J on the basis that whilst it is well arguable that section 152 is incompatible with the Directive, any such incompatibility cannot be resolved by any permissible Marleasing interpretation of the state, and since MIB is a private body and not an emanation of the state the court cannot disapply the domestic legislation in relation to a claim against it. It is unclear why C did this rather than proceeding directly against MIB but the reason may have been that C's legal advisers were concerned that the claim against MIB would be defeated by the exclusion in the UDA where the claimant knows that D1 is uninsured.

COLLEY v SHUKER (2020) — MIB AS EMANATION OF STATE — In the original action MIB had been D3. When the claim against UKI (D1) failed C then pursued D2 (the state, following Lewis v Tindale) on the basis of the state's failure to implement the Directive which made MIB liable to compensate as an emanation of the state. MIB argued that it had no liability to compensate as an emanation of the state where the obligation to insure the vehicle was met at the time of the accident. Freedman J found against MIB, holding that MIB is obligated to compensate in any scenario where the system has broken down — whether because the vehicle is uninsured or because of the provisions of national legislation (e.g. a section 152 declaration). MIB also argued that it was not obliged to compensate C because C knew that D1 was uninsured. That argument also failed: Freedman J found that in order to be able to rely on the exclusion permitted by the Directive it was necessary for MIB to show that at the time of the accident the vehicle was insured. It was not necessary to show the vehicle was insured only because it later became 'uninsured' when UKI avoided the policy. So again MIB had to pay in circumstances arising from the government's failure properly to implement its EU obligations.

COLLEY v SHUKER — SIGNIFICANCE FOR ARTICLE 75 INSURERS — The second limb of the decision of Freedman J in Colley has potentially significant implications for Article 75 insurers. An Article 75 insurer cannot be in a better position than MIB because the Article 75 liability is, in reality, an MIB liability. If MIB cannot rely on the claimant's knowledge of no insurance for the purposes of Clause 8(1) of the UDA when the vehicle was insured then it is arguable that nor can the Article 75 insurer. If Article 75 status is achieved on the basis that the use at the time of the accident was not permitted or was excluded then the vehicle was nonetheless insured (even if not for the use at the time of the accident) and the insurer cannot rely on the claimant's knowledge that the driver was uninsured because of the decision in Colley. The question of whether, in those circumstances, the liability should be met by the Article 75 insurer or by the MIB central fund because the liability arises as a result of the fact that MIB is an emanation of the state is something that may need to be resolved by further procedure.

MIB POST-BREXIT — CONTINUING LITIGATION — MIB has sought permission to appeal in Colley but this decision is now retained EU law and will continue to apply post-Brexit. MIB has continuing litigation against the Secretary of State in Colley to get an indemnity or contribution. The short point is that where an insurer downgrades its status to Article 75 insurer on the basis of use it may find that a claimant will refuse to accept that he is precluded from compensation even if he knew that the driver was uninsured.

Fidelidade — CJEU, Directives do not permit insurers to avoid obligations to injured parties
Vnuk v Zavarovalnica Triglav — CJEU, insurance obligation extends to private land
legislation_cited: Road Traffic Act 1988, s.145(3) — compulsory insurance requirement
Road Traffic Act 1988, s.152 (as amended — insurer declarations)
Road Traffic Act 1988, s.151 — insurer's duty to satisfy judgments
MIB Uninsured Drivers Agreement, Clause 8(1) — knowledge exclusion
MIB Uninsured Drivers Agreement, Clause 14 — requirement to join other parties

articles.summary: Detailed analysis from the 12KBW presentation by Patrick Vincent KC and Isaac Hogarth on the landmark cases of Lewis v Tindale and Colley v Shuker and their implications for MIB liability and insurer status. Lewis established MIB's liability as emanation of the state for off-road accidents following Vnuk, with major implications for the insurance industry regarding non-RTA covered liabilities. Colley v Shuker (in two stages) established that (1) section 152 incompatibility with the Directive cannot be resolved by Marleasing against a private body, but (2) MIB as emanation of the state must compensate where the system has broken down, and cannot rely on the claimant's knowledge of no insurance where the vehicle was in fact insured at the time of the accident. These decisions are now retained EU law post-Brexit. Covers significant implications for Article 75 insurers and MIB's continuing litigation against the Secretary of State.

articles.title: Pre-Litigation Impecuniosity: The BHR 'Chicken and Egg' Problem — EUI v Charles, TPI Demands and the Claimant's Correct Response
articles.source: Steve Evans, The Credit Hire Forum
articles.author: Steve Evans
key_points: THE QUESTION MISUNDERSTOOD — When a TPI (third party insurer or their representative) demands that the claimant proves impecuniosity before the TPI will provide BHR evidence, the real question is not about the burden of proof in litigation but about the pre-litigation position: is the claimant obliged to disclose financial records before proceedings have been issued, and before the defendant has shared the BHR rate it contends for? The answer is no — but the position requires careful handling.

EUI v CHARLES — THE BENCHMARK DECISION — The decision in EUI v Charles is the benchmark authority on pre-litigation impecuniosity notification. The case establishes that the onus is on the claimant to inform the defendant that impecuniosity is being asserted, so that the defendant knows how to address the claim and can understand the basis on which the credit hire rate (rather than BHR) is being claimed. This is a notification obligation — it requires the claimant to put the defendant on notice that impecuniosity will be relied upon. It does NOT require the claimant to prove impecuniosity or to disclose financial records before proceedings are issued.

THE 'CHICKEN AND EGG' PROBLEM — THE CRITICAL PRACTICAL POINT — The fundamental difficulty with a TPI demand for impecuniosity evidence before providing BHR is that it puts the cart before the horse. The test for impecuniosity (Lagden v O'Connor) is whether the claimant could not have afforded to hire a replacement vehicle at the basic hire rate without making unreasonable sacrifices. In order for the claimant to assess whether they meet that test, they must first know what the defendant says the BHR rate is. If the defendant has not shared evidence of the BHR rate it contends for, it is simply not possible for the claimant to review their finances, calculate the potential cash outlay they would have had to make, and determine whether that outlay was within their means. The impecuniosity assessment is necessarily dependent on the BHR figure — you cannot assess whether you could afford something without knowing how much it costs.

THE CLAIMANT'S CORRECT RESPONSE TO A TPI DEMAND — When a TPI demands impecuniosity evidence before providing BHR, the claimant's correct response is threefold: (1) REFER TO EUI v CHARLES — Acknowledge the notification obligation by confirming that impecuniosity is asserted and will be relied upon. This satisfies the EUI v Charles requirement. (2) POINT OUT THE BHR DEFICIT — Make clear that if the TPI or putative defendant has not shared evidence of the BHR rate they contend for, it is not possible for the claimant to respond to the impecuniosity challenge in any meaningful way. The claimant cannot determine whether they could have afforded the BHR without knowing what the BHR figure is. (3) ASSERT THE PRE-LITIGATION BOUNDARY — There is no obligation on the claimant to share financial records ahead of litigation when the defendant has not yet established evidence of the BHR. Financial disclosure obligations arise under court directions (typically as part of a disclosure order or in compliance with Part 18 requests after proceedings are issued), not as a pre-condition to the defendant engaging with the claim at all.

NO PRE-LITIGATION OBLIGATION TO DISCLOSE FINANCIAL RECORDS — The obligation to provide full financial disclosure (bank statements, credit card records, savings, overdraft facilities) arises in the context of litigation, typically pursuant to court directions or Part 18 requests. The cases on impecuniosity evidence — Diriye v Bojaj on pleading requirements, Umerji on debarment for non-compliance, Haider on non-disclosure consequences — all operate within the litigation framework. There is no free-standing pre-action obligation on a claimant to open their financial life to a TPI who has not even identified what rate they say was available in the market. The Pre-Action Protocol for Personal Injury Claims requires exchange of information and evidence to narrow issues, but it does not create a one-sided obligation for the claimant to prove impecuniosity to the defendant's satisfaction before the defendant engages with BHR.

THE TIMING AND ASSEMBLY POINT — Even where a claimant is willing in principle to evidence impecuniosity, the practical reality is that it takes considerable time to assemble the necessary financial documents. The standard disclosure requirement covers bank statements, credit card statements and evidence of savings and financial commitments from 90 days before the hire commenced through to the end of the hire period. If the request for impecuniosity evidence is made during the hire or immediately after the hire has ended, it is premature — the claimant cannot provide a complete financial picture because the relevant period has not yet concluded. The correct response in those circumstances is to advise the defendant that their request is premature and that the claimant will be in a position to address impecuniosity once the hire has ended and a reasonable period has elapsed to allow for the assembly of financial records covering the full relevant period.

THE TPI TACTIC — ORIGIN AND CONTEXT — This tactical demand for pre-action impecuniosity evidence was originally championed by Keoghs on behalf of defendant insurers. It followed a raft of applications for pre-action disclosure heard in the EUI v Charles line of cases, where the defendant was using the question as a device to frustrate the CHO, increase costs and delay engagement with the claim. The tactic was largely abandoned against larger CHOs — who had the resources and experience to resist it — but has been retained as a tactical ploy against smaller CHOs where the defendant feels it can use the demand as leverage to pressure early and unfavourable settlement. The demand is designed to: (a) deter the claimant from pursuing the credit hire claim at all by creating an impression that impecuniosity is an insurmountable evidential hurdle; (b) obtain financial information that can be used to resist the claim or to argue pecuniosity; and (c) delay engagement with the claim on the merits. It is not a legitimate procedural requirement and should not be treated as one. The correct approach is to assert impecuniosity, invite the TPI to share their BHR evidence, and make clear that financial disclosure will follow in due course in accordance with court directions if proceedings become necessary.

STRATEGIC SUMMARY FOR CREDIT HIRE PRACTITIONERS — (1) Always notify the TPI/defendant that impecuniosity is asserted — this satisfies EUI v Charles. (2) If the TPI demands financial evidence before sharing BHR, respond that the claimant cannot meaningfully assess or evidence impecuniosity without knowing the BHR rate contended for. (3) Do not volunteer financial records pre-litigation — disclosure obligations arise under court orders, not TPI demands. (4) If the TPI refuses to engage unless financial records are provided first, proceed to litigation — the court will manage disclosure properly under its case management powers. (5) Remember that if the defendant adduces no BHR evidence at all, the full credit hire rate is recoverable even without impecuniosity (Allan v Amlin).
cases_cited: EUI v Charles — pre-litigation notification of impecuniosity, onus on claimant to inform defendant
Lagden v O'Connor [2004] UKHL 36 — test for impecuniosity, inability to hire without unreasonable sacrifice
Diriye v Bojaj [2020] EWCA Civ 1400 — impecuniosity must be properly pleaded with financial detail
Zurich Insurance plc v Umerji [2014] EWCA Civ 357 — debarment for non-compliance with disclosure orders
Allan v Amlin — if defendant adduces no BHR evidence, full credit hire rate recoverable without impecuniosity
legislation_cited: Pre-Action Protocol for Personal Injury Claims
articles.summary: Expert commentary on the pre-litigation impecuniosity problem in credit hire claims. When a TPI demands impecuniosity evidence before providing BHR, the claimant faces a 'chicken and egg' problem: they cannot assess whether they could afford the BHR without knowing what rate the defendant contends for. The benchmark is EUI v Charles, which requires notification of impecuniosity (not proof). The correct response is to confirm impecuniosity is asserted, point out that the BHR rate has not been shared so the claimant cannot meaningfully respond, and make clear there is no pre-litigation obligation to disclose financial records when the defendant has not established its BHR evidence. This article addresses the common TPI tactic of demanding financial disclosure before engaging with the claim.

Article 26: The CMA Report: Legal Framework for Credit Hire Recovery — BHR, Impecuniosity and Copley
Article 30: Pre-Action Disclosure in Credit Hire: Holt v Allianz [2023] EWHC 790 — Impecuniosity Documents
Article 12: Pre-Action Disclosure and Credit Hire

articles.title: GTA Vehicle Groups, Rate Structure and Maximum Daily Settlement Rates — Cars and Commercial Vehicles (2019–2026)
articles.source: GTA Technical Committee — Official Rate Schedules 2019–2026
articles.author: Steve Evans
key_points: GTA VEHICLE GROUP STRUCTURE — The GTA Technical Committee classifies vehicles into groups by type and size. Within the CARS category, the groups are: S (Standard cars, groups S1–S7), P (Prestige vehicles, groups P1–P13), SP (Sports type vehicles, groups SP1–SP13), F (4x4/SUV vehicles, groups F1–F9), and M (MPV vehicles, groups M–M6). Each group has a maximum daily settlement rate. The number generally increases with engine size and vehicle value. Rates are reviewed periodically and new rate schedules are issued with effective dates.

RATE PERIODS FOR CARS — The available rate periods for Cars are: (1) 1 July 2019 to 30 September 2022; (2) 1 October 2022 to 30 June 2024; (3) 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025; (4) 1 July 2025 to 30 June 2026. To determine the applicable rate for a hire, identify the date the hire started and find the rate period that covers that date.

STANDARD CAR RATES (S GROUPS) — S1 is the smallest/cheapest standard car (e.g. Citroen C1, Peugeot 107, Smart, Toyota Yaris 1.0). S7 is the largest standard car. Current rates (from 1 July 2025): S1 = £42.75, S2 = £48.47, S3 = £51.70, S4 = £55.43, S5 = £58.64, S6 = £62.48, S7 = £87.62 per day (excl. VAT). Previous rates (1 July 2024): S1 = £38.17, S2 = £43.28, S3 = £46.16, S4 = £49.49, S5 = £52.36, S6 = £55.79, S7 = £78.23.

PRESTIGE VEHICLE RATES (P GROUPS) — P1 is the entry-level prestige (e.g. Alfa Romeo 147 Lusso 1.6). P13 is the highest prestige group (e.g. Bentley, Rolls-Royce). Current rates (from 1 July 2025): P1 = £83.77, P2 = £100.35, P3 = £106.77, P4 = £129.92, P5 = £150.79, P6 = £170.55, P7 = £198.94, P8 = £227.35, P9 = £261.48, P10 = £321.74, P11 = £451.90, P12 = £676.44, P13 = £980.84 per day (excl. VAT).

SPORTS VEHICLE RATES (SP GROUPS) — SP1 is the entry-level sports car. SP13 is the highest sports group. Current rates (from 1 July 2025): SP1 = £82.48, SP2 = £89.67, SP3 = £107.71, SP4 = £122.98, SP5 = £134.36, SP6 = £178.48, SP7 = £200.12, SP8 = £221.75, SP9 = £243.38, SP10 = £278.53, SP11 = £335.32, SP12 = £440.80, SP13 = £643.61 per day (excl. VAT).

4x4/SUV RATES (F GROUPS) — F1 is the entry-level 4x4/SUV. F9 is the highest group. Current rates (from 1 July 2025): F1 = £103.80, F2 = £103.46, F3 = £119.87, F4 = £147.06, F5 = £183.92, F6 = £206.92, F7 = £241.40, F8 = £258.65, F9 = £316.12 per day (excl. VAT).

MPV RATES (M GROUPS) — M is the basic MPV group. M6 is the highest. Current rates (from 1 July 2025): M = £61.59, M1 = £71.18, M2 = £81.17, M3 = £95.40, M4 = £112.59, M5 = £168.88, M6 = £213.92 per day (excl. VAT).

RATE UPLIFTS AND ADD-ONS — The following uplifts apply on top of the group daily rate: (1) AUTOMATICS: +£5 per day if the customer's vehicle was an automatic and the listed model is not already an automatic. Vehicles with semi-shift/paddle shift gearboxes that can operate in automatic mode also qualify. (2) ESTATES: +£5 per day if the customer's vehicle was an estate and the listed model is not already an estate. (3) EXTRAS: +£5 per day for necessary extras such as tow bars and baby seats matching the customer's vehicle. (4) DUAL CONTROL: +£12 per day (or +£7 if insurance cover not provided). (5) CONVERTIBLES: if the model listed is not already a convertible, move to the group one higher than the hardtop version (e.g. BMW M3 in SP9 → convertible in SP10). (6) DISABLED DRIVER VEHICLES: reasonable direct costs plus £10 admin charge; special adaptations to be discussed with insurer.

NON-STANDARD DRIVER INSURANCE — No charge for additional drivers unless non-standard risk. Non-standard risk includes: under 25 or over 70, held full licence less than 12 months, certain occupations (e.g. professional sportsperson, entertainment, journalist, publican), convictions resulting in unspent ban or 7+ points.
ALL RATES EXCLUDE VAT — All GTA daily settlement rates are quoted excluding VAT. VAT is added on top at the prevailing rate.

HOW TO LOOK UP A RATE — To answer a query about a GTA rate: (1) Identify the vehicle group code (e.g. S3, P5, F4, SP7). (2) Identify the date the hire commenced. (3) Find the rate period covering that date. (4) Look up the daily rate for that group in that period. (5) Apply any relevant uplifts (automatic, estate, extras, dual control, convertible). The system holds the full gta_rates.json file with all rates for all periods.

COMMERCIAL VEHICLE GROUP STRUCTURE — Within the COMMERCIAL VEHICLES category, the groups are: PV (Panel Vans, groups PV1–PV6), CV (Chassis Based Vehicles, groups CV1–CV4), CP (Pickup Trucks, groups CP1–CP3), CM (Minibuses, groups CM1–CM3), CS (Commercial 4x4 vehicles, groups CS1–CS5), and RV (Refrigerated/Chilled Vehicles, groups RV1–RV2). Where no appropriate group exists for a commercial vehicle being hired (e.g. hearse, car transporter), the CHO should try to agree a rate with the insurer before the commencement of the hire.

PANEL VAN RATES (PV GROUPS) — PV1 is the smallest panel van (e.g. Citroen Berlingo, VW Caddy, Renault Kangoo). PV6 is the largest (e.g. Ford Transit Jumbo, Mercedes Sprinter LWB High Roof). Current rates (from 1 July 2025): PV1 = £47.50, PV2 = £55.13, PV3 = £55.13, PV4 = £61.14, PV5 = £64.22, PV6 = £67.31 per day (excl. VAT).
CHASSIS BASED VEHICLE RATES (CV GROUPS) — Current rates (from 1 July 2025): CV1 = £69.92, CV2 = £70.50, CV3 = £74.12, CV4 = £105.59 per day (excl. VAT).
PICKUP TRUCK RATES (CP GROUPS) — Current rates (from 1 July 2025): CP1 = £64.64, CP2 = £73.34, CP3 = £83.50 per day (excl. VAT).
MINIBUS RATES (CM GROUPS) — Current rates (from 1 July 2025): CM1 = £84.04, CM2 = £97.25, CM3 = £110.15 per day (excl. VAT).
COMMERCIAL 4x4 RATES (CS GROUPS) — Current rates (from 1 July 2025): CS1 = £75.87, CS2 = £81.14, CS3 = £87.14, CS4 = £92.39, CS5 = £98.39 per day (excl. VAT).
REFRIGERATED/CHILLED VEHICLE RATES (RV GROUPS) — Current rates (from 1 July 2025): RV1 = £91.80, RV2 = £126.98 per day (excl. VAT).
Motorcycles & Scooters are designated by prefix B: B1 (0-50cc), B2 (51-125cc), B3 (126-500cc), B4 (501-650cc), B5 (651-1000cc), B6 (1001cc+).
Current motorcycle rates (from 1 July 2025): B1=£45.89, B2=£53.60, B3=£75.58, B4=£91.01, B5=£106.34, B6=£107.97 per day (excl. VAT).
£35 plus VAT allowed for motorcycle topboxes/panniers where the customer can demonstrate a need and regular use.

Private Hire & Taxi vehicles have group codes: T1-T4 (pre-31/03/11 registered), NT3-NT4 (post-31/03/11 registered), T5-T8 (any registration date), PT9/PT13 (prestige), T10/T12/T14 (specialist). Each group has two rates — one for newer vehicles (under 3 or 4 years old) and one for aged vehicles.
No additions allowed for taxi signage, radio hire, or taxi display equipment, but actual Council costs for replating vehicles and fitting different radio are reimbursable at cost.
articles.summary: Reference article for GTA vehicle group classifications and maximum daily settlement rates for Cars (Standard, Prestige, Sports, 4x4/SUV, MPV), Motorcycles and Scooters, Private Hire & Taxi vehicles from July 2019 to June 2026. Covers all group codes (S1-S7, P1-P13, SP1-SP13, F1-F9, M-M6, B1-B6, T1-T8, NT3-NT4, PT9/PT13), rate uplifts for automatics, estates, extras, dual control and convertibles, and guidance on how to look up the applicable rate for a given vehicle group and hire date. All rates exclude VAT. Also covers Commercial Vehicle groups: PV (Panel Vans PV1-PV6), CV (Chassis Based CV1-CV4), CP (Pickup Trucks CP1-CP3), CM (Minibuses CM1-CM3), CS (Commercial 4x4 CS1-CS5), and RV (Refrigerated/Chilled RV1-RV2).

articles.title: Conduct of Litigation: Delegation to Unauthorised Persons — CILEX v Mazur [2026] EWCA Civ 369
articles.author: Steve Evans
articles.summary: Analysis of the landmark Court of Appeal decision in CILEX v Mazur [2026] EWCA Civ 369, handed down on 31 March 2026, which overturned the High Court decision of Sheldon J that had threatened to criminalise the widespread practice of solicitors delegating litigation tasks to supervised but unauthorised staff. The Court of Appeal clarified that 'carry on the conduct of litigation' requires direction, control and responsibility — not merely performing the tasks — meaning properly supervised paralegals, legal executives and clerks can lawfully perform litigation tasks without committing a criminal offence under section 14 of the Legal Services Act 2007.
key_points: The Court of Appeal (Sir Colin Birss, Sir Geoffrey Vos MR, Andrews LJ) allowed CILEX's appeal against the decision of Sheldon J in [2025] EWHC 2341 (KB), which had held that unauthorised persons could only 'support or assist' a solicitor but could not conduct litigation under supervision.

The court held at §162 and §170 that 'conduct of litigation' refers to the tasks to be undertaken, while 'carry on' refers to direction, control and responsibility for those tasks. An unauthorised person performing delegated tasks is not 'carrying on' the conduct of litigation if the authorised individual retains responsibility.

At §187(v), the court confirmed that an unauthorised person may lawfully perform any tasks within the scope of the conduct of litigation, for and on behalf of an authorised individual such as a solicitor or appropriately authorised CILEX member, provided the authorised individual retains responsibility.

The degree of supervision required depends on the circumstances: for complex matters, prior approval and close oversight may be needed; for routine cases (e.g. debt recovery, standard PI claims), regular meetings and sampling of work may suffice (§187(vi)).

The offence under section 14 of the 2007 Act must be interpreted narrowly, consistent with the predecessor offence in section 70 of the Courts and Legal Services Act 1990 (§187(ii)). Parliament was aware of the widespread practice of delegation when enacting the 2007 Act.

The court at §192 identified activities that are unlikely to fall within 'conduct of litigation' at all: pre-litigation work, giving legal advice in connection with proceedings, conducting correspondence, gathering evidence, instructing experts and counsel, signing statements of truth, and signing documents the CPR permits legal representatives to sign.

Andrews LJ stated at §198 that the essential question is whether the unauthorised person is 'in truth acting on behalf of the authorised individual' — if so, it is the authorised individual who is conducting the litigation, not the unauthorised person.

This decision is of direct relevance to credit hire practitioners because credit hire litigation is frequently conducted by legal executives, paralegals and claims handlers working under solicitor supervision. The Sheldon J decision had created uncertainty about whether such individuals were committing criminal offences. The Court of Appeal has now confirmed that their work is lawful provided proper supervision arrangements are in place.

Key related cases considered by the court include Ndole Assets v Designer M&E Services [2018] EWCA Civ 2865 (fact and degree test for section 14 offence), Baxter v Doble [2023] EWHC 486 (KB) (scope of conduct of litigation — distinguished as concerning a litigant in person, not supervised delegation), Agassi v Robinson [2005] EWCA Civ 1507 (narrow interpretation of section 70 offence), and R v AUH [2023] EWCA Crim 6 (scope of conduct of litigation in criminal context).

Interveners included the Legal Services Board, the Association of Personal Injury Lawyers (APIL), and the Law Centres Network, reflecting the profession-wide significance of this issue. The SRA and Law Society appeared as respondents, having previously argued for a more restrictive interpretation at first instance.

Mazur v Sheldon [2025] EWHC 2341 (KB)
Ndole Assets v Designer M&E Services [2018] EWCA Civ 2865
Baxter v Doble [2023] EWHC 486 (KB)
Agassi v Robinson [2005] EWCA Civ 1507
R v AUH [2023] EWCA Crim 6
SRA v Khan [2021] EWHC 3765 (Ch)
Hollins v Russell [2003] EWCA Civ 718
