articles.title: Defendant Insurer Requests for Claimant Personal ID and Address Verification
articles.author: Steve Evans
articles.summary: Analysis of whether defendant insurers are entitled to demand personal identification documents and address verification from credit hire claimants. The answer depends critically on whether the claim is litigated (where a solicitor has conduct) or is being handled pre-litigation or under the GTA by a credit hire operator (CHO). In litigated claims, the solicitor will already have completed Know Your Client checks under the SRA Code of Conduct and the defendant has no right to demand personal ID. In pre-litigation or GTA claims handled by a CHO, the insurer may have grounds to request this evidence, but such a request should itself be treated as a warning sign.
key_points: The critical distinction is whether the claim is litigated or not. The answer to a defendant insurer's request for personal ID depends entirely on this.

In litigated claims where a solicitor has conduct, the solicitor will already have completed Know Your Client (KYC) checks in compliance with the SRA Code of Conduct. The defendant insurer has no right to demand the claimant's personal ID documents in these circumstances — the solicitor's professional obligations provide sufficient assurance of the claimant's identity.

In pre-litigation claims or claims proceeding under the GTA where a credit hire operator (CHO) is acting for the client rather than a solicitor, the third party insurer may demand evidence of identity if they suspect fraud or have other concerns about the claimant's bona fides.

However, even in non-litigated claims, a request for personal ID from the insurer should be treated as a red flag. In most cases the insurer will already have conducted their own background checks through HPI, Experian or similar credit reference agencies to verify the claimant's identity and address. They will also typically have sought clarification from the claimant's own insurer.

The suspicion, therefore, is that a request for personal ID from the defendant insurer is indicative of an underlying concern about the claim that the CHO should be cautious about. If the insurer is asking for ID despite having access to their own verification tools, there may be a reason for their concern that the CHO should investigate.

If the CHO has not carried out their own client verification checks before commencing the hire, they should do so before responding to the insurer's request. A CHO that places a vehicle on hire without verifying the client's identity exposes itself to risk if the claim later proves to be fraudulent.

In practical terms: if you are a solicitor with conduct of the claim, you can decline the request by confirming that you have completed your KYC obligations under the SRA Code of Conduct. If you are a CHO without solicitor involvement, treat the insurer's request as a prompt to review your own client checks and satisfy yourself of the claimant's bona fides before engaging further with the insurer on the point.
legislation: SRA Code of Conduct for Solicitors
SRA Code of Conduct for Firms

articles.title: Defendant Insurer Requests for Parts Purchase Invoices — Repair Cost Evidence
articles.author: Steve Evans
articles.summary: Guidance on whether a claimant must provide parts purchase invoices to a defendant insurer when a repair invoice and independent engineer's report are already available. The short answer is no — the repair invoice agreed by an independent engineer is prima facie evidence of the reasonable cost of repair, and the burden is on the defendant to prove otherwise. However, the practical response should also consider why the insurer is asking and whether cooperation might unlock settlement.
key_points: The starting point is the burden of proof: once a claimant produces a repair invoice agreed and assessed by an independent engineer, that invoice is prima facie evidence of the reasonable cost of repair. The burden shifts to the defendant to prove the amount is unreasonable (Coles v Hetherton [2013] EWCA Civ 1704; Smyth v Diamond [2010] NIQB 74 at §42). The defendant has no right to demand parts purchase invoices as a matter of accepted practice.

The defendant insurer's likely motivation in requesting parts purchase invoices is to identify trade discounts available to the repairer, with a view to arguing that the repair cost should be reduced to reflect what the insurer could have obtained through its own managed-repair network. This argument was addressed and rejected in Coles v Hetherton, which confirmed that the measure of recoverable repair cost is the reasonable cost of repair at the time and place of repair, not what the insurer would have paid under its own arrangements.

An independent engineer is instructed to provide an objective professional assessment of the reasonable retail cost of repairs at the time and geographic location where repairs were carried out. They have no financial interest in the insurer's outcome. An insurer's staff engineer, by contrast, performs a cost-control function — their role is to negotiate repair costs downward. A court would give significantly more weight to the independent engineer's assessment.

However, before refusing the request outright, the claimant or CHO should consider the practical impact. The key question is: why is the insurer asking for this information? If the insurer can be asked to explain the purpose of the request, it becomes easier to meet the challenge head-on.

If it is a trivial issue that will unlock payment of the invoice, it may be worth cooperating. For example, if the insurer has a specific query about a particular part or its pricing that can be quickly resolved, providing the information may accelerate settlement and avoid unnecessary delay or litigation costs.

However, if the insurer refuses to explain why they want the parts invoices or cannot identify a specific concern, the request is probably a delay tactic aimed at under-settling the claim. In those circumstances, the correct response is to decline the request, confirm that a repair invoice assessed by an independent engineer has been provided, and remind the insurer that the burden of proving unreasonableness rests with them.

The practical recommended response is therefore: (1) Ask the insurer to specify what concern they have about the repair costs and why the independent engineer's assessment is insufficient; (2) If they identify a specific and reasonable query, consider cooperating to resolve it; (3) If they refuse to be specific or simply assert a blanket entitlement to parts invoices, decline the request and restate the prima facie position supported by the independent engineer's report.
cases_referenced: Coles v Hetherton [2013] EWCA Civ 1704
Jones v Stroud District Council [1986] 1 WLR 1141
Smyth v Diamond [2010] NIQB 74
Matchett v Hamilton [2011] NIQB 131

articles.title: The Highway Code and Liability in Road Traffic Accidents — A Practical Guide for Credit Hire Professionals
articles.author: Steve Evans
articles.summary: A comprehensive analysis of how the Highway Code (effective 10 April 2025) can be used to establish or challenge negligence and contributory negligence in road traffic accident claims. Covers the legal distinction between mandatory rules (MUST/MUST NOT, backed by statute) and advisory rules (should/should not), the Hierarchy of Road Users principle (H1-H3), and maps the most liability-relevant Highway Code rules to common accident scenarios encountered in credit hire litigation.
key_points: The Highway Code is a useful reference for identifying whether a party's behaviour was consistent with the rules of the road. Whilst failure to follow the Code does not necessarily constitute an offence in itself, it may be used as evidence of culpability — either in full or in part — where that failure contributes to an accident.

Rules using MUST or MUST NOT are legal requirements backed by specific Acts of Parliament (Road Traffic Act 1988, Road Traffic Regulation Act 1984, Construction and Use Regulations 1986, etc.). Breach of a MUST/MUST NOT rule is a criminal offence and provides strong prima facie evidence of negligence in civil proceedings.

Rules using 'should' or 'should not' are advisory. Failure to follow advisory rules does not constitute an offence, but it can be relied upon in civil proceedings as evidence of negligence or contributory negligence. This distinction is critical when assessing the strength of a liability argument.

The Hierarchy of Road Users (Rules H1-H3), introduced in 2022 and retained in the 2025 edition, establishes that those in charge of vehicles that can cause the greatest harm bear the greatest responsibility to reduce danger to other road users. This principle is particularly relevant in cases involving pedestrians, cyclists, horse riders, and motorcyclists.

For rear-end collisions, Rule 126 (stopping distances and the 2-second rule) is critical. Stopping distances range from 12 metres at 20 mph to 96 metres at 70 mph on dry roads. These distances at least double on wet roads and can be up to 10 times greater on icy roads (Rule 230). A driver who collides with the vehicle ahead has, in most cases, failed to maintain a safe following distance.

For junction accidents, Rules 170-172 are key. Drivers must give way to pedestrians crossing at junctions (Rule 170, reinforcing H2). Drivers MUST stop at Stop signs (Rule 171) and MUST give way at Give Way signs. A driver who pulls out and causes a collision has strong evidence of negligence against them.

For overtaking incidents, Rules 162-168 apply. Drivers must leave at least 1.5 metres when overtaking cyclists at speeds up to 30 mph, and more at higher speeds. When passing horse riders, drivers should travel at under 10 mph and allow at least 2 metres of space. Rule 167 prohibits overtaking near pedestrian crossings, at double white lines, or after a No Overtaking sign.

Mobile phone use while driving is one of the most common Highway Code breaches in modern RTA claims. Rule 149 provides that drivers MUST NOT use a hand-held mobile phone or similar device while driving, including when stationary in traffic (RTA 1988 s41D, CUR reg 110). This is a strict MUST NOT rule and evidence of phone use at the time of collision is powerful evidence of negligence.

Speed limits (Rules 124-125) are MUST NOT rules. The standard limits for cars are: 30 mph in built-up areas, 60 mph on single carriageways, and 70 mph on dual carriageways and motorways. Different limits apply to goods vehicles, vehicles towing trailers, and buses. Exceeding the speed limit at the time of an accident is strong evidence of negligence and may also support a charge of dangerous or careless driving under RTA 1988 sections 2 and 3.

For pedestrian crossing accidents, Rules 191-199 are directly relevant. Drivers MUST give way to pedestrians on a zebra crossing (Rule 195) and MUST stop on red at pelican, puffin, and toucan crossings (Rule 196). Parking within the zig-zag area is also prohibited (Rule 191). These are MUST rules and breach provides strong evidence of fault.

Adverse weather significantly affects liability. Rule 227 confirms stopping distances at least double in wet weather. Rule 230 states stopping distances can be 10 times greater on ice. Rule 226 requires headlights when visibility is seriously reduced (under 100 metres). A driver who fails to adjust their driving to weather conditions and causes a collision may face arguments of negligence based on these rules.

Rule 144 prohibits dangerous driving, driving without due care and attention, and driving without reasonable consideration for other road users (RTA 1988 sects 2 and 3). This is the overarching rule for driver conduct and forms the basis of most negligence arguments in RTA claims.

Seat belt rules (Rules 99-101) are directly relevant to contributory negligence arguments. If a claimant was not wearing a seat belt at the time of the accident, the defendant may argue contributory negligence on the basis that injuries would have been less severe if a belt had been worn — see Froom v Butcher [1976] QB 286 for the standard reductions (typically 15-25% depending on the impact on injuries).

Drink and drug driving limits (Rules 95-97) are MUST NOT rules. England and Wales limits are 35 micrograms per 100ml breath, 80mg per 100ml blood, and 107mg per 100ml urine. Scotland has lower limits of 22 micrograms breath, 50mg blood, and 67mg urine. Driving over these limits is a criminal offence and, if established in a civil claim, is powerful evidence of negligence.

The door opening rule (Rule 239) advises using the 'Dutch reach' technique — opening the car door with the hand on the opposite side, which forces the driver to look over their shoulder for passing cyclists or pedestrians. A driver who opens a door into the path of a cyclist without checking may face liability under this rule, supported by the Hierarchy of Road Users principle.

For motorway incidents, key rules include: MUST NOT exceed speed limits (Rule 261), MUST NOT drive in a closed lane (Rule 258), MUST NOT use the hard shoulder except in an emergency (Rule 269), and MUST NOT reverse on any part of the motorway (Rule 263). Motorway collisions often involve higher speeds and more severe injuries, making the credit hire claim correspondingly larger.

Post-accident obligations are set out in Rules 286-287. A driver involved in a collision causing damage or injury MUST stop and exchange details (RTA 1988 sect 170). Failure to stop is a criminal offence and may also be relevant to a credit hire claim where it delays the identification of the at-fault party.
cases_referenced: Froom v Butcher [1976] QB 286
Powell v Phillips [1972] 3 All ER 864
legislation: Road Vehicles (Construction and Use) Regulations 1986 reg 110

articles.title: The Principles Governing Liability in Road Traffic Accident Cases — Chan v Peters and the Common Law Framework
articles.author: Steve Evans
articles.summary: An analysis of the 14 core principles governing liability and contributory negligence in road traffic accident cases, as comprehensively distilled by Cavanagh J in Chan v (1) Peters (2) Advantage Insurance Company Ltd [2021] EWHC 2004 (QB). This article complements Article 209 (Highway Code rules) by covering the common law framework that courts apply when assessing whether a breach of road traffic rules amounts to actionable negligence. Based on the Ropewalk Chambers analysis by Shilpa Shah.
key_points: In Chan v Peters [2021] EWHC 2004 (QB), Cavanagh J comprehensively set out the 14 core principles governing liability and contributory negligence in RTA cases. The case concerned a 17-year-old pedestrian struck by a car near his school. The judge described it as a 'back to basics' exercise, but the resulting framework is the most complete single-case restatement of the applicable principles in modern RTA litigation.

Principle 1: The defendant will be liable in negligence if she failed to attain the standard of a reasonable careful driver AND if the accident was caused as a result. Both elements — breach and causation — must be established. This is the overarching test.

Principle 2-3: The burden of proof rests with the claimant, who must establish on the balance of probabilities that the defendant was negligent. This is the standard civil burden — it does not reverse even where a Highway Code rule has been breached.

Principle 4: The standard of care is that of the reasonably careful driver, armed with common sense and experience of the way pedestrians — and particularly children — are likely to behave (Moore v Pointer [1975] RTR). This means a driver near a school should anticipate that children may behave unpredictably, but this does not impose an impossibly high standard.

Principle 5-6: If a real risk of danger emerging would have been reasonably apparent to such a driver, then reasonable precautions must be taken. However, if the danger was no more than a 'mere possibility', which would not have occurred to such a driver, there is no obligation to take extraordinary precautions (Foskett v Mistry [1984] 1 RTR 1). This distinction between 'real risk' and 'mere possibility' is critical in many RTA liability disputes.

Principle 7: The defendant is not to be judged by the standards of an ideal driver, nor with the benefit of '20/20 hindsight' (Stewart v Glaze [2009] EWHC 704 (QB)). This is one of the most frequently cited principles in RTA defence arguments. The question is what the reasonable driver would have done in the circumstances at the time, not what the driver could theoretically have done with perfect foresight.

Principle 8: Drivers must always bear in mind that a motorcar is potentially a dangerous weapon (Lunt v Khelifa [2002] EWCA Civ 801). This principle, while seemingly simple, imposes a heightened standard of vigilance on drivers and is regularly cited in pedestrian and cyclist injury cases.

Principle 9: Drivers are taken to know the principles of the Highway Code. This is the critical bridge between the Highway Code rules (covered in Article 209) and the common law. A driver cannot claim ignorance of the Highway Code as a defence, and breach of a Highway Code rule — while not automatically establishing negligence — is evidence from which negligence may be inferred.

Principles 10-11 concern the treatment of evidence at trial. Lambert v Clayton [2009] EWCA Civ 237 cautioned against turning 'guesstimates' into secure findings of fact with unwarranted precision — where there are inherent uncertainties, precise findings are dangerous and the party bearing the burden of proof may be in difficulty. Similarly, Stewart v Glaze warned that accident reconstruction expert evidence about reaction times and stopping distances should not be elevated into a 'fixed framework or formula' against which the defendant's actions are rigidly judged with mathematical precision. These principles are directly relevant to challenging overly precise BHR-style arguments about what a driver should have done.

Principle 12: The assessment of whether the defendant's driving fell below the requisite standard cannot be conducted in a vacuum — it must be done by reference to the actual circumstances of the actual collision (Sam v Atkins [2005] EWCA Civ 1452). Road conditions, weather, lighting, traffic volume and other real-world factors at the time of the accident must all be considered.

Principles 13-14 address contributory negligence. The Supreme Court in Jackson v Murray [2015] UKSC 5 held that the apportionment of responsibility is inevitably a 'somewhat rough and ready exercise' (Lord Reed at §28). An appellate court should only interfere where the trial judge was clearly wrong. Apportionment involves both the causative potency of the party's conduct and their relative blameworthiness. Additionally, the age of a claimant is not in itself a relevant factor for the determination of the extent of contributory negligence.

The practical significance for credit hire professionals is that these principles apply directly when liability is disputed. In a credit hire claim, if the defendant insurer challenges liability, these are the principles the court will apply. Understanding the difference between a 'real risk' and a 'mere possibility', knowing that the test is the reasonable driver (not the ideal driver), and recognising that apportionment is a rough exercise can all inform the approach to settlement negotiations and trial preparation.

These 14 principles should be read alongside Article 209 (Highway Code rules). Article 209 identifies what the Highway Code requires; this article explains how courts assess whether a failure to follow those requirements amounts to actionable negligence. Together they provide the complete framework for liability analysis in RTA cases.
cases_referenced: Chan v (1) Peters (2) Advantage Insurance Company Ltd [2021] EWHC 2004 (QB)
Moore v Pointer [1975] RTR
Foskett v Mistry [1984] 1 RTR 1
Stewart v Glaze [2009] EWHC 704 (QB)
Lunt v Khelifa [2002] EWCA Civ 801
Lambert v Clayton [2009] EWCA Civ 237
Sam v Atkins [2005] EWCA Civ 1452
Jackson v Murray [2015] UKSC 5

articles.title: Pay Now vs Pay Later Rates and 'From...' Pricing — Why BHR Headline Rates Are Not What They Seem
articles.author: Steve Evans
articles.summary: An analysis of the fundamental difference between 'Pay Now' and 'Pay Later' rental rates and the emerging challenge of 'From...' pricing in BHR evidence. Explains why only 'Pay Later' rates provide a genuine comparison to credit hire rates, and why the lowest advertised headline rate is frequently not the rate that would have been available to a credit hire claimant on equivalent terms.
key_points: BHR rates from conventional rental companies have historically been advertised on one of two bases: 'Pay Now' and 'Pay Later'. The distinction between these two pricing models is fundamental to the comparability of BHR evidence, yet it is frequently overlooked or deliberately obscured in defendant rate reports.

The 'Pay Now' rate is typically the cheapest headline rate advertised by a conventional rental company. It requires the hirer to prepay the full rental charge at the time of booking, restricts or eliminates the right to cancel without penalty, and — critically — requires the hirer to commit to the full anticipated hire period at the outset. This rate model assumes certainty: the hirer knows when the hire will start, when it will end, and is willing to accept the risk of losing the prepaid amount if circumstances change.

In a credit hire context, that certainty almost never exists. If there is a delay from the bodyshop ordering parts for the intended repair, the hire start date may shift — but a 'Pay Now' booking cannot easily be moved without penalty. If repairs are completed earlier than anticipated, the hirer loses the unused element of the prepaid charges because the booking was for a fixed period. Equally, if the hire needs to run longer than anticipated (a very common occurrence where repairs are delayed), the rate for any extension is unknown at the time of the original booking and may be significantly higher than the 'Pay Now' rate.

The 'Pay Later' rate has always been considered the correct basis on which BHR rates should be compared to credit hire rates. A 'Pay Later' rate requires a deposit to be paid at the start of hire based on the daily rate booked, but — and this is the key difference — it allows flexibility in terms of delay, cancellation or extension of the hire at the prevailing daily rate booked at the outset. This flexibility mirrors the position of a credit hire claimant, who does not know at the outset how long repairs will take and needs the ability to adjust the hire period without financial penalty.

The practical consequence is that where a defendant's BHR evidence cites a 'Pay Now' rate, it is citing a rate that was not genuinely available to the claimant on equivalent terms. The claimant could not have committed to a fixed hire period in advance because the repair timeline was uncertain. The claimant could not have accepted the risk of losing prepaid charges if repairs finished early. And the claimant could not have budgeted for an unknown extension rate if repairs ran over. The 'Pay Now' rate is therefore not a like-for-like comparator and should be challenged.

An additional and more recent challenge has emerged: many conventional rental companies now advertise rates on a 'From...' basis. Rather than quoting a specific daily rate, they advertise the lowest possible rate a rental might be charged at — for example, 'From £25 per day'. It is only when the booking is being confirmed that the differentiating prices are revealed, based on whether the hirer is paying now or later, committing to a fixed term or retaining flexibility, and other variables such as time of booking and availability. The 'From...' rate is a marketing tool, not a rate that any specific hirer would necessarily pay.

Those scrutinising BHR evidence should be alive to this practice. A defendant's rate report may cite a rate that is in reality a 'From...' figure — the absolute floor price, available only under the most favourable conditions (advance booking, full prepayment, fixed term, no flexibility). The rate that would actually have been available to a credit hire claimant — who needs flexibility on start date, end date and the ability to extend — may be significantly higher.

The practical recommended approach when reviewing defendant BHR evidence is to: (1) identify whether the rate cited is a 'Pay Now', 'Pay Later' or 'From...' rate; (2) if it is a 'Pay Now' or 'From...' rate, challenge it as not comparable to the credit hire rate; (3) obtain the 'Pay Later' rate from the same supplier for the same vehicle, location and dates as a direct comparator; (4) if the defendant cannot demonstrate that the rate cited was genuinely available on a 'Pay Later' basis with full flexibility, the rate evidence should be given little or no weight.

This issue intersects with several other well-established BHR challenges: rate timing (Article 40 KP5 — same-day vs advance-booking rates), the CMA's findings on volatile online pricing (Article 41 KP4), and the broader question of whether the defendant's evidence reflects a rate that was genuinely available to this claimant at the relevant time and on equivalent terms (Stevens v Equity, McBride v UK Insurance).
cases_referenced: Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management [2015] EWCA Civ 93
McBride v UK Insurance Ltd [2017] EWCA Civ 144

articles.title: Credit Repair — A Distinct Head of Loss: Principles, Recovery, and Common Quantum Disputes
articles.author: Steve Evans
articles.summary: A comprehensive explanation of credit repair as a distinct and separate head of loss from credit hire. Credit repair concerns the recovery of vehicle repair costs funded on credit; credit hire concerns the recovery of replacement vehicle hire charges. The two arise from the same accident but are governed by different principles, different leading authorities, and different quantum arguments. This article sets out the legal framework for credit repair recovery, the role of independent engineers, common quantum disputes, and the critical distinctions that practitioners must understand.
key_points: CREDIT REPAIR AND CREDIT HIRE ARE ENTIRELY DIFFERENT HEADS OF LOSS. Credit repair relates to the recovery of the cost of repairing the damaged vehicle. Credit hire relates to the recovery of the cost of hiring a replacement vehicle while the damaged vehicle is off the road. Although both typically arise from the same road traffic accident and are often presented together in a combined claim by a credit hire organisation (CHO), they are legally and factually distinct. The principles governing recovery, the leading authorities, the quantum arguments, and the evidence required are fundamentally different for each head of loss. A question about 'credit repair' is a question about repair costs — not about hire charges.

THE CREDIT REPAIR MODEL: In a typical credit repair arrangement, the CHO refers the damaged vehicle to a bodyshop (often an independent repairer rather than an insurer-approved panel repairer). The bodyshop prepares a repair estimate. An independent engineer — instructed by or on behalf of the CHO or claimant — inspects the vehicle, reviews the estimate, and agrees the reasonable cost of repair. The repairs are then carried out and the CHO pays the repair invoice on the claimant's behalf (on credit). The CHO subsequently recovers the repair costs from the at-fault insurer as part of the claimant's damages claim. The claimant's obligation to pay the repair costs (whether or not the CHO has already paid the bodyshop) is what gives rise to the loss — the claimant has incurred a liability for the cost of restoring their vehicle to its pre-accident condition.

THE LEADING AUTHORITY — COLES V HETHERTON [2013] EWCA CIV 1704: Coles v Hetherton is the primary authority on the recovery of credit repair costs. The Court of Appeal held that the measure of damage for a vehicle that has been repaired is the reasonable cost of repair at the time and place of repair. This is not what the at-fault insurer would have paid through its own managed-repair network — it is the retail market cost of the repair carried out by the repairer who actually did the work. Paragraphs 28 and 29 of the judgment are the key passages. The court confirmed that the claimant's loss crystallises at the moment of damage as the diminution in value of the vehicle, and the reasonable cost of repair is evidence of that diminution. Insurance arrangements between the parties are irrelevant to the measure of loss (res inter alios acta).

THE OBLIGATION TO PAY PRINCIPLE: The claimant's entitlement to recover repair costs does not depend on whether the claimant has personally paid the repair invoice out of their own pocket. The loss is the claimant's obligation to pay for the repairs — not the act of payment itself. This principle derives from Jones v Stroud District Council [1986] 1 WLR 1141 and was affirmed in Coles v Hetherton. The claimant who has entered into a credit repair arrangement has incurred a real liability to the CHO for the cost of repairs, and that liability is recoverable as damages from the tortfeasor. The at-fault insurer cannot argue that because the CHO paid the bodyshop, the claimant has suffered no loss.

THE ROLE OF THE INDEPENDENT ENGINEER: The independent engineer is central to credit repair recovery. Their function is to provide an objective, professional assessment of the reasonable retail cost of the repairs. They have no financial interest in the outcome, no panel relationship with the repairer, and no contractual obligation to the insurer. Once an independent engineer has assessed and agreed the repair estimate, that agreed figure is prima facie evidence of the reasonable cost of repair. The burden then shifts to the defendant to demonstrate that any specific item in the account was unreasonable. A bare assertion by the insurer's staff engineer that the costs are 'too high' — without detailed, item-by-item justification — will not displace the independent engineer's report. See Article 110 for the full analysis of staff engineer vs independent engineer disputes.

QUANTUM DISPUTES ON CREDIT REPAIR — WHAT IS TYPICALLY CHALLENGED: Quantum disputes on credit repair are about whether the repair costs claimed are reasonable — they are not about hire charges, mitigation of hire, or intervention offers. Common challenges include: (1) Labour rates — the insurer argues that the repairer's hourly rate exceeds the insurer's managed-repair network rate (this fails on Coles v Hetherton); (2) Parts costs — the insurer argues that OEM parts should not have been used when pattern parts were available (the repairer and engineer must justify parts selection based on the vehicle's age, safety, and manufacturer requirements); (3) Paint and materials — the insurer disputes the number of hours or the cost per panel; (4) ADAS calibration — the insurer argues that post-repair ADAS calibration was unnecessary (this is increasingly difficult to sustain as ADAS calibration is mandated by IIR verification rules); (5) EV-specific charges — battery quarantine, power-down/reinstatement, and EV recharge costs are challenged as novel or excessive; (6) Compliance charges — BS 10125 compliance fees and manufacturer-approval uplifts are resisted as 'administrative overheads'. See Article 93 on the ABP rate card for detailed analysis of each of these categories.

WHAT CREDIT REPAIR IS NOT: Credit repair is not about the cost of hiring a replacement vehicle (that is credit hire). It is not about whether the claimant needed a hire car (that is need for hire). It is not about the basic hire rate or BHR evidence (that is the rate at which a replacement vehicle could have been hired). It is not about intervention offers from the at-fault insurer to provide a hire vehicle (that relates to mitigation of hire charges). It is not about impecuniosity (that concerns whether the claimant could have afforded to hire at the spot rate). These are all credit hire concepts. When a question is asked about credit repair, the answer must focus on the repair costs, the repair evidence, and the principles governing the recovery of those costs.

THE INSURER'S MANAGED-REPAIR ARGUMENT: The most common strategic argument deployed by insurers against credit repair claims is that the claimant should have used the insurer's own approved repair network, where repairs would have been carried out at the insurer's contracted rates (typically significantly lower than retail market rates). Coles v Hetherton conclusively rejects this argument. The claimant is entitled to have their vehicle repaired by a repairer of their choosing at the reasonable retail cost — not at the at-fault insurer's preferred rate. The insurer's internal cost-management arrangements are irrelevant to the measure of the claimant's loss. The claimant has no relationship with the at-fault insurer's repair network and is under no obligation to use it.

CREDIT REPAIR AND CREDIT HIRE — THE COMBINED CLAIM: In practice, a CHO will typically present both credit hire charges and credit repair costs in a single claim to the at-fault insurer. This does not make them the same head of loss. The credit hire element is governed by Dimond v Lovell [2002] 1 AC 384, Clark v Ardington [2002] EWCA Civ 510, and Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management [2015] EWCA Civ 93 (among others). The credit repair element is governed by Coles v Hetherton. The quantum arguments, the evidence required, and the legal principles are different for each. When advising on or responding to a quantum dispute, it is essential to identify which head of loss is in issue and to apply the correct legal framework.
cases_referenced: Coles v Hetherton [2013] EWCA Civ 1704
Jones v Stroud District Council [1986] 1 WLR 1141
Darbishire v Warran [1963] 1 WLR 1067
Dimond v Lovell [2002] 1 AC 384
Clark v Ardington [2002] EWCA Civ 510
The London Corporation [1935] P 70
Bee v Jenson [2007] EWCA Civ 923

articles.title: BHR Supplier Profile — Wheels to Work – Silverstone (Silverstone)
articles.subcategory: Supplier Profiles — Scooter and Moped Hire
articles.source: Provider profile compiled by Steve Evans; data current at 2026-04-10
articles.author: credit-hire.ai research
articles.content:
Wheels to Work is a brand name of Spyder Motorcycles offering a limited range of scooters to residents within 30 miles of their base address or in Leicester. The rates are not a fair comparator for BHR rates for a variety of reasons: they are geographically restricted, only available for one vehicle, exclude courier and delivery use, do not come with insurance cover - a separate proposal has to be made with the hire company broker, and require the hirer to be in regular work within the catchment area and to commit to a minimum 4-month hire period.

The website was last updated on 28/03/2025 although some of the content was unchanged from an earlier date and the detail in this note were current at 10/04/2026.

articles.title: BHR Supplier Profile — Road Trip Motorcycles – Woking (Woking)
articles.source: Provider profile compiled by Steve Evans; data current at 2026-04-10
articles.author: credit-hire.ai research
articles.content:
Road Trip is a rental provider located in Woking, Surrey, 14 miles from London Heathrow, and 24 minutes from central London by train. Their business is predominantly for experienced motorcyclists looking to rent a vehicle to tour. Hired motorcycles must be collected from and returned to the company's office in Woking. A delivery and collection service is not available. Road Trip offer larger and more powerful motorcycles from Ducatti, Triumph, and BMW, and a 125cc motorcycle from Yamaha.

Hire is subject to eligibility criteria including a minimum and maximum age requirement, licence type, and provision of proof of address. Rentals are offered for relatively short minimum terms, with advance payment and a deposit required at the outset. Courier use is not permitted. Insurance terms include a substantial excess, mileage is restricted to 200 miles per day with excess mileage charged @50p per mile.

Where a claimant satisfies the eligibility requirements, is able to collect and return the vehicle in person, accepts the excess terms, and the rental period aligns with the credit hire duration, and the hirer is able to accurately predict the rental period and pay the anticipated hire charges, plus a non-waivable deposit in full, this provider may represent a valid basic hire comparator. In circumstances where personal collection is impractical, excess terms are materially different, or other eligibility requirements are not met, rates derived from this provider may not reflect a realistic alternative.

The website was last updated on 19/02/2026 although some of the content was unchanged from an earlier date and the detail in this note were current at 10/04/2026.

Their business is predominantly for experienced motorcyclists looking to rent a vehicle to tour.
Hired motorcycles must be collected from and returned to the company's office in Woking.
A delivery and collection service is not available.

Road Trip offer larger and more powerful motorcycles from Ducatti, Triumph, and BMW, and a 125cc motorcycle from Yamaha.

Hire is subject to eligibility criteria including a minimum and maximum age requirement, licence type, and provision of proof of address.

articles.title: BHR Supplier Profile — Raceways Motorcycles – London (London)
articles.source: Provider profile compiled by Steve Evans; data current at 2026-04-10
articles.author: credit-hire.ai research
articles.content:
Raceways provide rental services from their location in London SE16 2LW. They do not offer a delivery and collection service.

They do not represent a reasonable comparator for a BHR provider because they do not permit the use of their vehicles for courier or delivery services, the vehicles are provided with third party insurance cover, the minimum age to hire is 25, there are a series of restrictions based on a driver's driving history and endorsements, they do not offer delivery or collection services, the hire period has to be pre-booked and paid in full before hire starts and no refunds are permitted for early return either through hirer early termination or because of a theft or accident whilst on hire.

The website was last updated on 12/09/2025 although some of the content was unchanged from an earlier date and the detail in this note were current at 10/04/2026.

The website was last updated on 12/09/2025 although some of the content was unchanged from an earlier date and the detail in this note were current at 10/04/2026.

articles.title: BHR Supplier Profile — Lamba Motorcycles – Carshalton (Carshalton)
articles.source: Provider profile compiled by Steve Evans; data current at 2026-04-10
articles.author: credit-hire.ai research
articles.content:
Lamba Motorcycles are a Yamaha dealer based in Carshalton, Surrey. They only offer for hire a Yamaha XSR 125 model with an excess of £450 where the hirer is between 17 and 25 years of age and £250 where the hire is over 25 and under 75. They do not offer delivery or collection. Vehicles are provided with comprehensive insurance cover, subject to the excess stipulated as the deposit, but do not allow the hire of their motorcycles for any purposes other than social, domestic, and pleasure use. Business use is excluded and so rates are not relevant for demonstrating BHR when the hirer needs to use the motorcycle for courier or delivery services. No refund of prepaid hire charges will be considered if the client returns the hire vehicle ahead of the end date for which the booking was made. The client must stipulate the hire period and prepay the anticipated charges and the non-waivable deposit before the start of hire. The deposit may take up to 10 days after the end of hire to be repaid.

No Persons with more than 6 points on licence may ride the motorcycle.

Any persons convicted within the last two years of any offence with endorsement codes DD, DR, BA, UT, DG, LC or CD on their licence will not be able to hire.

Any person who has had motor insurance declined/refused or cancelled, has a restricted medical licence will require specific underwriting approval. Professional Sports persons, those working within the entertainment industry, or within the racing or gambling profession are also excluded from hire.

The website was last updated on 24/02/2026 although some of the content was unchanged from an earlier date and the detail in this note were current at 10/04/2026.

articles.title: Non-Party Cost Orders: Retrospective Applications on Historic Credit Hire Cases
articles.author: Stephen Evans
articles.summary: Following the Court of Appeal decision in Tescher v DAML; AXA v Spectra [2025] EWCA Civ 733 and the Supreme Court's refusal of permission to appeal (10 October 2025), defendant law firm Keoghs LLP is pursuing retrospective Non-Party Cost Order (NPCO) applications against Credit Hire Organisations on historic cases dating back to 2022. Keoghs contend there is a genuine basis for the delay, arguing they were waiting for binding authority. CHOs receiving such correspondence should respond promptly and instruct specialist counsel.
key_points: The Court of Appeal held on 13 June 2025 in Tescher v DAML; AXA v Spectra [2025] EWCA Civ 733 that courts are 'likely, absent some special feature' to make an NPCO against a CHO where a credit hire claim fails and a costs order is made against a QOCS-protected claimant.
The Supreme Court refused permission to appeal on 10 October 2025 (UKSC/2025/0117), confirming the Court of Appeal guidance as binding authority.
Keoghs LLP is now trawling through historic case files to identify matters where they succeeded at first instance but did not apply for an NPCO at the time because the law was not in their favour.
Keoghs are sending correspondence threatening NPCO applications on cases decided as long ago as September 2022, relying on the Tescher/Spectra outcome having retrospective value.
Keoghs argue the delay is justified because binding authority was pending — the alternative would have been a raft of first instance applications clogging up the courts.

Fraser McAndry at Keoghs argues that CHOs are aware of adverse costs orders and should not benefit from delay, and that applications are justified where a CHO has declined to enter meaningful dialogue after being put on notice.
Ben Williams KC (4 New Square), who acted for the CHOs in the Tescher/Spectra appeals, is understood to believe there is merit in running the retrospective argument on older cases.
Several CHOs and solicitors have received letters from Keoghs about older cases. Responses have been robust and only one actual application is known to have been made.
CHOs should not ignore correspondence threatening retrospective NPCO applications — take it seriously, respond promptly, and seek specialist advice early.
Recommended counsel for defending retrospective NPCO applications: Matt Waziak, Theo Barclay, or Ben Smiley at 4 New Square; Shannon Eastwood at Hundred Court Chambers, Liverpool.

Supporting authority on promptness — Gray v Going Places Leisure Travel Ltd [2005] EWCA Civ 189, at [11] and [15] per Neuberger LJ: 'in the absence of at least a good reason to the contrary, the costs of proceedings… should be dealt with by the tribunal which determines the issue which disposes of the case immediately after the judgment in disposing of the case', and 'in principle, there is no difference in this connection between a costs order against a party and a costs order against a non-party'. Relied on at [15]: late applications are permissible 'but… the court entertaining the application late will necessarily grant it if there is no good reason for the delay'. Shared by Mr Benjamin Williams KC for use in resisting Keoghs' retrospective NPCO applications. See separate case_law entry.
cases_cited: Tescher v Direct Accident Management Ltd [2025] EWCA Civ 733
AXA Insurance UK Plc v Spectra Drive Ltd [2025] EWCA Civ 733
Pereira and Others v Chambers and Others and Direct Accident Management Limited [2024]
Gray v Going Places Leisure Travel Ltd [2005] EWCA Civ 189
articles.full_text:
NON-PARTY COST ORDERS: RETROSPECTIVE APPLICATIONS ON HISTORIC CREDIT HIRE CASES

Stephen Evans
April 2026

Summary

Following the Court of Appeal decision in Tescher v DAML; AXA v Spectra [2025] EWCA Civ 733 and the Supreme Court's refusal of permission to appeal (10 October 2025), defendant law firm Keoghs LLP is now pursuing retrospective Non-Party Cost Order (NPCO) applications against Credit Hire Organisations (CHOs) on historic cases, some dating back to 2022. CHOs and their solicitors who receive correspondence or applications on older settled matters should treat this as a serious development and take prompt legal advice.

Background: The Key Cases

Pereira and Others v Direct Accident Management Ltd (20 June 2024)

HHJ Saunders at Central London County Court heard four applications by defendants for NPCOs against Direct Accident Management Limited (DAML). He concluded that it was just to make NPCOs in each case, finding DAML was the 'real party' to the litigation. Although HHJ Saunders made clear that NPCO decisions are always fact-sensitive, he acknowledged the significance of his ruling given how common credit hire arrangements are.

Tescher v DAML; AXA Insurance v Spectra Drive Ltd [2025] EWCA Civ 733 (13 June 2025)

The Court of Appeal (Coulson, Nicola Davies and Birss LJJ) heard conjoined appeals and issued binding guidance on NPCOs in credit hire cases. The key holdings were:

(1) Where a credit hire claim fails and a costs order is made against a QOCS-protected claimant, courts are 'likely, absent some special feature' to make an NPCO against the credit hire organisation.
(2) CHOs have 'effective control in practice' over the litigation arising from the financial realities of credit hire agreements, which place 'all the risk' on the claimant if they settle or discontinue against the CHO's wishes.
(3) QOCS was not designed to protect commercial non-parties such as CHOs.
(4) The court applied a structured two-stage test, finding DAML was the 'real party' to the litigation with a direct financial interest in the outcome.

In Tescher, 100% of costs were awarded against DAML. In AXA v Spectra, Spectra was ordered to pay 65%.

Supreme Court Refusal (10 October 2025)

Both DAMS and Spectra applied for permission to appeal to the Supreme Court (UKSC/2025/0117). Permission was refused, confirming the Court of Appeal's guidance as binding authority.

The Current Issue: Keoghs' Retrospective Applications

What is Happening

Keoghs LLP, a major defendant law firm, is now trawling through historic case files to identify matters where they succeeded at first instance but did not apply for an NPCO at the time — because the law was not yet settled in their favour. They are sending correspondence to CHOs and solicitors threatening NPCO applications on cases decided as long ago as 2022.

Keoghs' Argument for Delay

Keoghs contend that there is a 'genuine and logical basis' for the delay in pursuing these applications. Their reasoning is as follows:

(1) When many of these cases were decided (from September 2022 onwards), the Pereira cases were still being heard or were subject to appeal.
(2) The Tescher v DAMS and Spectra v AXA appeals were proceeding through the Court of Appeal and subsequently to the Supreme Court.
(3) They argue the alternative to waiting would have been 'a raft of first instance NPCO applications which would have clogged up the Court system and wasted time and costs.'
(4) They say the law was not settled until the Supreme Court refused permission to appeal on 10 October 2025.

Keoghs' Position on CHO Awareness

Keoghs argue that CHOs are aware of adverse costs orders and should not be permitted to benefit from, or avoid liability for costs, solely based on delay. They contend that an application will be justified where a CHO has been given the opportunity to settle the costs, especially if it has been put on notice of the application but declined to enter 'meaningful dialogue.'

Assessment and Practical Guidance

Is the Argument Strong?

This is a developing area. Ben Williams KC (4 New Square), who acted for the CHOs at the Court of Appeal in the Tescher/Spectra appeals and therefore knows the issues intimately, is understood to believe there is merit in running the retrospective argument on older cases. However, 2022 is a considerable time ago and there are strong counter-arguments around delay, prejudice, and the fact that the law was genuinely unsettled at the time these cases were decided.

What Has Happened So Far

Several CHOs and solicitors have received letters from Keoghs about older cases. Responses have been robust, and the likelihood is that CHOs will dispute the retrospective applications. As of the time of writing, only one actual application is known to have been made.

What Should CHOs Do

(1) Be alert to any correspondence from Keoghs (or other defendant firms) referencing older settled cases and threatening NPCO applications.
(2) Do not ignore such correspondence. Take it seriously and respond promptly.
(3) Consider whether the specific facts of any case make it more or less vulnerable to a retrospective NPCO application.
(4) Seek specialist legal advice early.

Recommended Counsel

If a CHO receives a retrospective NPCO application, the following counsel are recommended:

- Matt Waziak — 4 New Square
- Theo Barclay — 4 New Square
- Ben Smiley — 4 New Square
- Shannon Eastwood — Hundred Court Chambers, Liverpool

Key Dates Timeline

September 2022 onwards: Historic cases now being targeted by Keoghs.
20 June 2024: HHJ Saunders gives judgment in Pereira v DAML at Central London County Court, making NPCOs against DAML.
13 June 2025: Court of Appeal hands down judgment in Tescher v DAML; AXA v Spectra [2025] EWCA Civ 733.
10 October 2025: Supreme Court refuses permission to appeal (UKSC/2025/0117).
2026: Keoghs begin sending retrospective NPCO correspondence on historic cases.

Relevance

This article is relevant when a CHO or its solicitor receives a notice of application or correspondence from Keoghs (or any defendant firm) seeking to make a retrospective Non-Party Cost Order on an older, previously settled credit hire case. It provides context for the legal basis being relied upon and practical steps to take in response.

Article prepared: April 2026
Sources: Tescher v DAML; AXA Insurance UK Plc v Spectra Drive Ltd [2025] EWCA Civ 733; UKSC/2025/0117; Pereira and Others v Chambers and Others and Direct Accident Management Limited [2024] (Central London County Court, HHJ Saunders)
articles.source: Stephen Evans, Credit Hire Industry Commentary

articles.title: Taxi Licensing Reform 2026: Impact on Credit Hire
articles.author: Gary Herring
topics: English Devolution and Community Empowerment Bill
articles.summary: Analysis of proposed taxi licensing reforms under the English Devolution and Community Empowerment Bill and the DFT consultation closing 1 April 2026. The Bill will introduce national minimum standards for drivers, vehicles and operators, consolidate 263 licensing authorities into 70 Local Transport Authorities, close the cross-border licensing loophole, mandate enhanced DBS vetting, and restrict grandfather rights. From a credit hire perspective, the reforms are likely to increase CHO fleet costs (driving uplifted contractual and potentially ABI rates), create arguments for higher specification hire vehicles where claimants' pre-accident vehicles no longer meet new standards, raise total loss valuation disputes where written-off vehicles must be replaced with compliant vehicles, extend hire durations due to regulatory bottlenecks and stricter roadworthiness requirements, and increase fraud risk on vehicles approaching licence renewal. Royal Assent expected summer 2026 with implementation likely late 2026 or early 2027.
key_points: The English Devolution and Community Empowerment Bill will establish national minimum standards for all taxi and private hire drivers, vehicles and operators across England.

The 263 existing licensing authorities will be consolidated into approximately 70 larger Local Transport Authorities (LTAs), closing the cross-border licensing loophole exploited in areas such as Rossendale and Wolverhampton.

Enhanced DBS checks and mandatory safeguarding training will be required for all drivers. Existing drivers must meet new standards upon licence renewal, restricting grandfather rights for sub-standard vehicles.
A 2028 deadline has been suggested in some quarters for newly licensed vehicles to be zero emissions or EV, following Transport for London's 2023 mandate.
CHO fleets will face immediate cost impacts to meet new minimum standards, likely resulting in uplifted contractual credit hire rates and potential pressure for uplifted ABI rates.

Claimants driving older vehicles under grandfather rights at the time of accident will likely be provided newer, higher specification hire vehicles meeting the new requirements — with the CHO charging the rate of the compliant vehicle and arguing there was no alternative.

Total loss claims may include arguments for the cost of a new compliant vehicle rather than pre-accident value, though this is problematic given the vehicle would have needed replacing at the next licensing renewal in any event.
Insurers should be alert to potential increases in fraudulent claims for written-off vehicles approaching licence renewal date.
Hire durations may increase due to stricter roadworthiness requirements (even minor cosmetic damage may prevent continued use) and regulatory bottlenecks at LTAs causing longer waits for inspections.
The DFT consultation closed on 1 April 2026. Royal Assent is expected summer 2026, with secondary legislation setting specific standards likely late 2026 or early 2027.
articles.full_text:
TAXI LICENSING REFORM 2026: IMPACT ON CREDIT HIRE

Gary Herring, Keoghs LLP
April 2026

Despite an ever more favourable legal landscape having evolved in recent years, with a raft of key judicial decisions in insurers' favour which limit the measure of damages, there are many aspects of taxi claims which remain problematic. One of the challenges is that the current taxi licensing regime is highly localised and inconsistent, with each of the 263 local authorities operating often vastly different licensing criteria and sets of rules. Through proposals set out within a current DFT consultation, along with the English Devolution and Community Empowerment Bill, that is all about to change.

So what are the changes?

National Minimum Standards

The Bill will establish strict, national minimum standards for all drivers, vehicles, and operators. This means a clear, legal baseline for safety and accessibility will apply across every local authority in England.

Zero-Emissions Vehicles

It is not yet clear as to whether the new minimum standards will mandate that newly licensed vehicles must be EVs or zero emissions, but a 2028 deadline for this has been suggested in some quarters. This appears to be the direction of travel in any event, with Transport for London having adapted this from 2023 with others expected to follow.

Cracking Down on Out-of-Area Working

One of the most heavily targeted issues in the new 2026 proposals is cross-border or out-of-area working. This loophole previously allowed drivers to obtain a licence in a council area with cheaper fees or less stringent checks, but then operate in a different city. Under the new proposals, the 263 licensing authorities would be absorbed into 70 larger Local Transport Authorities (LTAs).

Enhanced Vetting and Mandatory Databases

With the objective of improving passenger safety, the Bill mandates that licensing authorities cannot grant a licence unless the driver meets the new national criteria, for instance enhanced DBS checks and safeguarding training.

Suspension, Revocation and Renewal

The Bill will allow for rules stipulating when a local authority must suspend or revoke a licence to ensure passenger safety. In addition, it will ensure that existing drivers must meet the new standards upon licence renewal, restricting grandfather rights for sub-standard vehicles.

When will this apply?

Assuming there are no Parliamentary delays, the Bill is expected to receive Royal Assent and be passed into law in summer 2026. However, this does not mean that the changes will come into force straight away upon enactment. The Bill merely grants the Secretary of State the power to make regulations concerning the matters covered, meaning that secondary legislation will be required which sets out the specifics in terms of the exact standards and criteria which will apply. This is the point at which the regulations will converge with the current DFT consultation, the outcome of which will no doubt heavily influence what form they eventually take.

The DFT consultation is due to close on 1st April 2026. Following on from this, it is probably reasonable to expect the national minimum standards and consolidation of licensing authorities to be rolled out some time late in 2026 or early 2027.

What is the impact on credit hire?

Whilst the closure of the licence shopping loophole — typically exploited within hotspots such as Rossendale and Wolverhampton — will no doubt be welcomed across the board, the new regime will create some potential risks from a credit hire perspective.

There will be an immediate cost impact on replenishment of CHO fleets, which will need to meet the new minimum standards. The response is likely to be uplifted contractual credit hire rates, with a potential clamour for uplifted ABI rates alongside them. In addition, it is likely that claimants who at the time of the accident are driving older vehicles under grandfather rights, will be provided far newer and higher specification credit hire vehicles which meet the new requirements, and charged the rate of the latter. In these circumstances it could be argued that there was no choice but to hire a better vehicle to continue working.

That argument is also likely to be extended to the increased cost of permanently replacing written off vehicles, with claims potentially being made for the cost of new compliant vehicles, rather than the pre-accident value of the accident damaged vehicle. This is a far more problematic argument for a claimant to make persuasively, given the non-compliant vehicle would have had to be replaced in any event at the next licensing renewal. However, insurers will also need to be alive to the prospect of an increase in fraudulent claims for written off vehicles which are approaching licence renewal date.

Durations also have the potential to be impacted. Firstly, any argument that a taxi is roadworthy and should continue to be used post-accident will become even more difficult to sustain, even in the cases of the most minor cosmetic damage.

Secondly, given the increased regulatory burden on LTAs and more stringent compliance testing to be applied, there are likely to be increasingly long waits for inspections, or other administrative bottlenecks, which create delays which are on the face of it not within the control of the claimant.

Keoghs say they will continue to monitor the progress of the DFT consultation and the route of the Bill through Parliament and will provide an update in due course.
articles.source: Gary Herring, Keoghs LLP

articles.title: BHR Location Data — Avis Car Rental UK Branches

articles.content:
Avis Car Rental operates 51 rental locations across the United Kingdom. Geographic breakdown: England (31), Scotland (14), Wales (2), Northern Ireland (3), Isle of Man (1). 29 locations are at airports. Cities with the highest concentration of branches include London (8), Edinburgh (4), Glasgow (3), Belfast (2), Lerwick (2), Manchester (2), Exeter (2), Norwich (2), Cardiff (2), Kirkwall (2). This data is relevant to BHR rebuttal analysis for car hire claims: when assessing whether a claimant could realistically have hired from Avis Car Rental, the nearest branch location, its opening hours, and its accessibility from the claimant's home address are all material factors.

articles.title: BHR Location Data — National Car Rental UK Branches

articles.author: credit-hire.ai research
articles.content:
National Car Rental operates 44 rental locations across the United Kingdom. Geographic breakdown: England (33), Scotland (8), Wales (1), Northern Ireland (2). 21 locations are at airports. articles.title: Jones v Benger — Cross-Hire Disclosure Not Required: CHO's Cost of Vehicle Supply Irrelevant to Measure of Damages
articles.source: Approved Judgment, Walsall County Court
articles.author: HHJ Gregory (Designated Civil Judge)
articles.content:
Jones v Benger [2014] (Walsall County Court, Case No 3 YQ 53408, 9 July 2014) is an approved judgment of HHJ Gregory, Designated Civil Judge, dismissing an application by the defendant insurer for third-party disclosure under CPR 31.17 of cross-hire supply agreements between the credit hire organisation (Accident Exchange) and its vehicle suppliers. There is no contrary authority; this judgment stands as the high water mark on cross-hire disclosure.

FACTS: The claimant hired a Range Rover from Accident Exchange for approximately 113 days following a non-fault accident, incurring credit hire charges of approximately £43,077. The defendant's solicitors discovered that the vehicles supplied to the claimant were not owned by Accident Exchange but leased from other hire companies (cross-hired). The defendant applied for disclosure of the contractual documentation between Accident Exchange and those supplier companies, arguing that this would reveal the true cost of vehicle provision and allow the court to assess what portion of the credit hire charge represented non-recoverable additional services.

HELD: HHJ Gregory dismissed the application, holding that:

1. Even if the cross-hire cost to Accident Exchange was less than the BHR rates evidence, this could only help the defendant if they could persuade the court that a similar rate would have been available to the claimant had he gone into the market himself (paragraph 9).

2. It is 'blindingly obvious' that a substantial financial concern such as Accident Exchange is in a far better position to negotiate hire rates, particularly for a large fleet of vehicles hired over the long term, than is an individual consumer simply engaging a high street concern (paragraph 10). The cross-hire rate is a function of the CHO's commercial buying power and volume — it is not a rate the claimant could ever have obtained.

3. The traditional approach — obtaining BHR/spot rates evidence — is the proper and established method for assessing the reasonable hire rate. This is 'far more compelling than any attempt to make an assessment of the market rate of hire through the disclosure that is sought' (paragraph 12).

4. Disclosure of the cross-hire agreements would not save costs but would add substantially to them, and was not necessary to dispose fairly of the claim (paragraph 12).

5. The application was described as 'novel' (by Mr Williams) and 'unusual' (by Ms Shah). HHJ Gregory found no merit in it and dismissed it (paragraph 13).

The case also references Abdi v Fletcher (HHJ Denyer QC, Designated Civil Judge for Bristol, 4 December 2012), where a similar approach to cross-hire disclosure had been taken at first instance.

CRITICAL PRACTICAL DISTINCTION — DISCLOSURE OBLIGATIONS IN CROSS-HIRE CASES:

There is a fundamental difference between two categories of document that a defendant insurer may demand in a cross-hire scenario, and the obligations on the claimant differ accordingly.

(A) DISCLOSABLE — The credit hire agreement between the claimant and the CHO: The claimant's claim for hire charges arises from the contract between the claimant and the CHO. The defendant insurer is entitled to see the full terms and conditions of that agreement, because it is the contractual basis of the loss claimed. This includes the daily rate charged to the claimant, the hire period, any additional charges (delivery, collection, insurance, excess protection, etc.), and the total sum claimed. The CHO's invoice to the claimant should be provided in full and unredacted.

(B) NOT DISCLOSABLE — The cross-hire supply agreement between the CHO and its vehicle supplier (e.g. VRG): Per Jones v Benger, the defendant has no entitlement to disclosure of the terms on which the CHO acquired the vehicle from its supplier, nor to the supplier's invoice to the CHO showing the wholesale rate paid. The CHO's cost of acquiring the vehicle is irrelevant to the measure of the claimant's damages. The cross-hire rate is a commercial fleet rate reflecting the CHO's buying power and volume — it is not a rate any individual claimant could have obtained in the open market. The proper comparator for testing reasonableness of hire charges is BHR/spot rates evidence, not the CHO's wholesale costs.

RECOMMENDED RESPONSE when an insurer demands cross-hire disclosure: Provide the full credit hire agreement and CHO invoice to the claimant. Decline to provide the cross-hire supply agreement or supplier invoice, citing Jones v Benger. Invite the insurer to obtain their own BHR/spot rates evidence if they wish to challenge reasonableness — that is the established method endorsed by the courts from Dimond v Lovell onwards.
key_points: The claimant MUST disclose the full credit hire agreement between the claimant and the CHO (including all terms and conditions), because this is the contractual basis of the claimed loss.

The claimant is NOT obliged to disclose the cross-hire supply agreement between the CHO and its vehicle supplier (e.g. VRG), nor the supplier's invoice to the CHO. Jones v Benger is the authority; there is no contrary authority.
The cost to the CHO of cross-hiring a vehicle from its supplier is irrelevant to the measure of the claimant's damages for loss of use.
The cross-hire rate reflects the CHO's commercial buying power and fleet volume — it is not a rate available to an individual consumer in the open market.
The proper method for assessing reasonable hire charges is traditional BHR/spot rates evidence, not reverse-engineering from the CHO's wholesale costs.
Cross-hire disclosure adds substantially to costs and is not necessary to dispose fairly of the claim.
Jones v Benger stands as the high water mark on this issue — there is no contrary authority.
If the insurer presses for cross-hire disclosure, invite them to obtain their own BHR/spot rates evidence to challenge reasonableness.
cases_referenced: Dimond v Lovell [2002] 1 AC 384
Abdi v Fletcher (HHJ Denyer QC, 4 December 2012)
articles.summary: HHJ Gregory (Designated Civil Judge, Walsall County Court) dismissed a defendant insurer's application for third-party disclosure of cross-hire agreements between the CHO (Accident Exchange) and its vehicle suppliers. There is no contrary authority; this is the high water mark on cross-hire disclosure. CRITICAL DISTINCTION: The claimant must disclose the full credit hire agreement between the claimant and the CHO (the contractual basis of the claim), but is NOT obliged to disclose the cross-hire supply agreement between the CHO and its vehicle supplier, nor the supplier's invoice to the CHO. The CHO's cost of acquiring vehicles is irrelevant to the claimant's measure of loss — the cross-hire rate reflects commercial fleet buying power, not a rate available to consumers. The proper comparator is BHR/spot rates evidence obtainable by a consumer in the open market.
