articles.title: Highway Authority Liability for Road Defects — Highways Act 1980, Sections 41 and 58: Duty to Maintain and Statutory Defence
articles.content:
Section 41(1) of the Highways Act 1980 imposes an absolute duty on the highway authority to maintain the highway. A claimant who suffers damage (whether personal injury or property damage) caused by a failure to maintain the highway can bring an action under s.41. The claimant must prove that: (a) the highway was in a dangerous condition (i.e. the defect — pothole, broken surface, etc. — was dangerous to traffic); (b) the dangerous condition caused the damage; and (c) the highway authority was the responsible maintainer.

However, section 58 of the Highways Act 1980 provides the highway authority with a statutory defence. The authority is not liable if it proves that it had taken such care as in all the circumstances was reasonably required to secure that the part of the highway to which the action relates was not dangerous for traffic. In determining whether the authority has made out the s.58 defence, the court shall have regard to: (a) the character of the highway and the traffic reasonably expected to use it; (b) the standard of maintenance appropriate for a highway of that character and used by such traffic; (c) the state of repair in which a reasonable person would have expected to find the highway; (d) whether the authority knew, or could reasonably have been expected to know, that the condition of the part of the highway was likely to cause danger to users; and (e) where the highway authority could not reasonably have been expected to repair that part of the highway before the cause of action arose, what warning notices of its condition had been displayed.

In practice, the s.58 defence turns on the authority's system of inspection and repair. The authority must demonstrate: (1) a documented inspection regime with frequencies appropriate to the road classification and traffic volume (e.g. monthly for A roads, quarterly or annually for minor roads); (2) that inspections were actually carried out at the scheduled intervals; (3) that the defect was either not present at the last inspection, or was present but at a level below the intervention threshold (e.g. less than 40mm depth for potholes under many authority policies); and (4) that where defects meeting the intervention threshold were identified, they were categorised, prioritised, and repaired within the authority's published response times.

The leading cases include: Mills v Barnsley MBC [1992] PIQR P291 (CA) — the highway must be dangerous, not merely worn or in imperfect condition; Goodes v East Sussex CC [2000] 1 WLR 1356 (HL) — s.41 duty to maintain means to keep in repair, not to prevent accumulations of ice/snow (though see s.41(1A) for later amendment re winter maintenance); Wilkinson v City of York Council [2011] EWCA Civ 207 — the s.58 defence requires evidence of a system that was actually followed, not merely a system on paper.

CRITICAL POINT FOR PRACTITIONERS: The existence of a pothole or road defect at the time of the incident does NOT automatically establish liability. Video or photographic evidence of the defect proves the condition on the day of the incident only. It does not prove the authority knew or ought to have known about the defect. The claimant's solicitor must request disclosure of: (a) the authority's inspection records for the relevant road; (b) the defect reporting log (including any reports from the public via FixMyStreet or the authority's own system); (c) the repair history for the relevant stretch; and (d) the authority's highway maintenance policy document setting out inspection frequencies and intervention levels. Without this evidence, the strength of the claim cannot be properly assessed. If the authority's records show the road was inspected shortly before the incident and the defect was not present or was below the intervention threshold, the s.58 defence is likely to succeed regardless of the severity of the defect on the day of the incident.
key_points: Section 41 Highways Act 1980 imposes an absolute duty on the highway authority to maintain the highway
Section 58 provides a statutory defence if the authority took reasonable care to keep the highway safe for traffic
The defence turns on the authority's inspection regime — frequency, execution, and response to identified defects
A pothole existing on the day of the incident does NOT automatically establish liability
Video/photographic evidence proves condition on the day only — not that the authority knew or should have known
Claimant must request inspection records, defect logs, repair history, and maintenance policy from the authority
If the authority inspected recently and the defect was absent or below intervention threshold, s.58 defence likely succeeds
Leading cases: Mills v Barnsley MBC [1992], Goodes v East Sussex CC [2000], Wilkinson v City of York [2011]

articles.title: Hertz 24/7 Retail Kiosk Locations (B&Q, IKEA, Costco) — Not Traditional Rental Branches: Implications for BHR Proximity Analysis
articles.content:
Hertz operates two distinct types of UK location: (1) traditional staffed rental branches offering the full range of cars, vans, and standard rental agreements, and (2) Hertz 24/7 self-service retail kiosks located at B&Q, IKEA, and Costco stores. Of Hertz's 368 UK locations, approximately 192 (52%) are retail kiosks rather than traditional branches.

Hertz 24/7 kiosks offer short-term van hire only — typically by the hour or by the day — aimed at retail customers who need a large vehicle to transport DIY materials, furniture, or bulk purchases home from the store. Access is via a digital code or key fob with no counter service. The service operates under separate terms from standard Hertz car rental. Crucially, these kiosks do NOT offer: standard car rental of any vehicle group; like-for-like replacement vehicles suitable for credit hire; standard Hertz rental agreements or terms and conditions; weekly or monthly hire periods typical of credit hire claims.

For BHR proximity analysis, Hertz 24/7 retail kiosk locations must be excluded when identifying the nearest Hertz branch. Including a B&Q kiosk as the 'nearest Hertz location' would be misleading because the kiosk cannot provide the type of vehicle or rental service relevant to a credit hire claim. The correct comparator is the nearest traditional staffed Hertz branch.

Kiosk locations can be identified by their branch names, which typically contain 'B&Q', 'IKEA', 'Costco', '24/7', or 'Van Rental At'. The credit-hire.ai location query system automatically excludes these kiosks from the nearest-branch results and flags them separately with an explanatory note.
key_points: 192 of Hertz's 368 UK locations (52%) are Hertz 24/7 self-service retail kiosks at B&Q, IKEA, and Costco stores
Kiosks offer hourly/daily van hire only — no standard car rental, no like-for-like replacement vehicles
Access is by digital code, not counter service — separate terms from standard Hertz rental
Kiosks must be EXCLUDED from BHR proximity analysis as they cannot provide vehicles relevant to credit hire claims
The nearest traditional staffed Hertz branch is the correct comparator for BHR purposes
Kiosk locations identifiable by branch names containing B&Q, IKEA, Costco, 24/7, or Van Rental At
credit-hire.ai automatically excludes kiosks from proximity results and flags them separately
Source: Hertz 24/7 Hourly Van Hire product page (hertz.co.uk/p/business-hire/24-7-business-van-hire)

articles.filename: Smith v Tucker - Need and Leisure Vehicles (Credit Hire).docx
articles.title: Smith v Tucker: Credit Hire Need and Leisure Vehicles — SDP Use Advisory Framework
articles.author: Credit Hire Forum / DWF (Liam Knight)
articles.summary: Case analysis and advisory framework for Smith v Tucker, in which the credit hire claim for a replacement motorbike was dismissed on the basis that the claimant had not established need. The vehicle was predominantly used for leisure/hobby purposes and the claimant had access to alternative vehicles. Includes principles for advising on SDP-use cases and a framework for distinguishing the decision where the client's vehicle was used for genuine essential transport.
key_points: Need in credit hire is a question of reasonable mitigation, not mere preference — 'He prefers to ride, but that preference cannot be enough to establish a need to hire'
Where a claimant has access to alternative vehicles capable of meeting essential transport needs, the need to hire a like-for-like replacement for a leisure vehicle is significantly undermined
Predominant use determines whether the court characterises a vehicle as a leisure vehicle — where primary use is hobby/events rather than transport, need is harder to establish
Objective mileage evidence (MOT history, commute distance vs actual daily usage) is powerful evidence for challenging stated need
The restitution principle (Lagden v O'Connor) does not guarantee like-for-like hire — it operates within the bounds of reasonableness and the duty to mitigate
Where the hire claim fails, loss of use may be awarded as a fallback but at significantly lower value (£4,000 vs £23,567.94 claimed hire)

To distinguish Smith v Tucker in SDP cases: establish genuine regular essential use, demonstrate no available alternative vehicle, frame SDP use as necessary not discretionary, challenge the leisure vehicle characterisation
A standard car insured for SDP is fundamentally different from a motorbike used for hobby events — the insurance category itself reflects everyday use
These principles extend to motor homes, caravans and other leisure vehicles (Billingham v Royal & Sun Alliance)
Appeal dismissed by HHJ Brownhill — trial judge's evaluation of evidence and application of Lagden v O'Connor confirmed as the correct legal framework
cases_cited: Lagden v O'Connor
Billingham v Royal & Sun Alliance Insurance Limited
articles.full_text: Smith v Tucker — Credit Hire — Need — Leisure Vehicles — SDP Use. Mr Steve Mark Smith v Mrs Gillian Tucker (DWF Ref 2019276-440). County Court, DJ Pain (first instance), HHJ Brownhill (appeal — permission refused). The Claimant claimed £23,567.94 for 157 days hire of a replacement motorbike from 4th Dimension. The matter exited from Stage 3 to Part 7 proceedings. The Claimant pleaded loss of use as applicable but only if the hire agreement was found to be void. Rate, enforceability, period and need were challenged. The Claimant stated pre-litigation it was 'inconvenient' for him to use his other vehicles. The matter ran to trial with financial disclosure incomplete and BHR obtained. The Defendant made a Part 36 offer of £5,000.00 which wasn't accepted. At the First Instance Hearing, enforceability was accepted but need, period and rate were disputed. Under cross examination the Claimant confirmed that he had a car and a van (despite only referring to a van in his witness evidence). It was accepted the van may not be suitable for day to day use, and the Claimant averred that the car was 'off the road' at the time of the accident. The Claimant's commute was a 26 mile round trip, but he averaged 8 miles per day on his bike. The Claimant explained that before his car had become unroadworthy he sometimes used it for commuting, and admitted he repaired the car himself in a day or so. The Claimant also admitted the main use of the bike was attending biking events on a hobby basis. The Defendant submitted there was no need to hire. The Trial Judge accepted need was not satisfied and found that the claim for hire should fail, commenting: 'The question of whether he has satisfied need relates to whether the hiring of a bike was a reasonable step in mitigating his loss. ... I do not consider that he has discharged the burden. He could have fixed his car quickly and before the need to use the van arose. The bike events do not establish a need to hire a bike. He prefers to ride, but that preference cannot be enough to establish a need to hire.' The Trial Judge did allow the Claimant to make an oral application to amend their pleadings to advance a claim for loss of use. An award of £4,000.00 was made for loss of use — resulting in the Defendant beating their Part 36 offer, and we were awarded costs. The Appeal: The Claimant appealed on three grounds: (1) The Court failed to attach sufficient weight to the principal the Claimant should be restored to a position he was in; (2) The Court failed to attach weight to the fact the Claimant used the vehicle for more than just transport, and was an avid biker; (3) The Court failed to attach weight to the need to replace a damaged vehicle with a similar vehicle. HHJ Brownhill dismissed permission to appeal, commenting: 'Ground 1 — the DJ was in my view aware of and addressed the principle of restitution. He referred to it specifically. So I disagree with Claimant's Counsel. .... The judge also considered para 24 of Lagden v O'Connor. That is the correct legal framework. I'm satisfied that ground 1 has no real prospect. Grounds 2 and 3 — I agree with Mr Ferguson that they are a challenge to discretion. DJ Pain's comment later in the hearing highlights that specifically. I do not agree that the judge failed to take into account the Claimant's explanation as to his biker lifestyle, ease of commuting, he specifically did refer to them, he was entitled to evaluate the Claimant's evidence, in particular the matters addressed in cross examination, the lack of detail as to what happened at the biker events, the mileage, the lack of reference to having a car before oral evidence and the Claimant's evidence that had he known the costs involved he would not have hired.' Related Case: Billingham v Royal & Sun Alliance Insurance Limited — DWF also acted in a related matter. The Claimant claimed injury and other losses, including hire of £44,023.20 for a panel van from OnHire for 221 days to replace a converted motor home. The Defendant challenged need. The Claimant's statement asserted he travelled 100 miles per week, yet MOT history showed only 6,000 miles over a 4 year period. When this discrepancy was raised, the Claimant's solicitors accepted the Part 36 offer (valuing hire at £4,001.80) out of time in January 2024. Source: DWF Group insight article by Liam Knight, 19 February 2024.
articles.source: DWF Group (Liam Knight)

articles.filename: Fundamental Dishonesty and Credit Hire - s57 Analysis.docx
articles.title: Fundamental Dishonesty and Credit Hire: Why s.57 Does NOT Extend to Non-Personal Injury Claims
articles.author: Credit Hire AI Knowledge Base (compiled from Diaw v ERS, Basir v Larizadeh, Hansard, and practitioner materials)
topics: s57 Criminal Justice and Courts Act 2015
articles.summary: Comprehensive analysis of whether s.57 of the Criminal Justice and Courts Act 2015 extends to dismiss non-personal injury claims (credit hire, vehicle repairs, property damage) when a claimant is found fundamentally dishonest about their personal injuries. Covers the textual analysis of s.57, the Hansard legislative history showing Parliament explicitly rejected extending s.57 to credit hire, the Diaw v ERS judgment rejecting the wider interpretation, the flaws in Basir v Larizadeh, and practical guidance for responding to the DAC Beachcroft template defence.
key_points: CORE PRINCIPLE: Section 57 of the Criminal Justice and Courts Act 2015 applies ONLY to 'claims for damages in respect of personal injury'. It does NOT give the court power to dismiss credit hire claims, vehicle repair claims, or other non-personal injury heads of damage, even where the claimant has been found fundamentally dishonest about their personal injuries.

TEXTUAL ANALYSIS (Diaw v ERS, paras 60-73): The word 'claim' is used consistently throughout s.57 to mean 'claim for damages in respect of personal injury'. The 'primary claim' in s.57(1) is defined as the 'claim for damages in respect of personal injury'. The duty to dismiss under s.57(2) applies to this primary claim. Section 57(3) extends this duty to 'any element of the primary claim' - meaning elements of the PI claim such as general damages, physiotherapy, lost earnings - NOT to separate heads of claim like property damage or credit hire.

THE WORD 'PROCEEDINGS' IN S.57(1): This word serves to make clear that s.57 applies not only to pure PI actions but also to 'mixed claims' (PI plus property damage, as in many RTA cases). A claimant cannot escape s.57 by bolting on a property damage claim. But once s.57 is engaged via the proceedings containing a PI claim, its operative provisions (dismissal) bite only on the PI claim itself.

HANSARD - LORD HUNT'S REJECTED AMENDMENT (October 2014): During the committee stage of the Criminal Justice and Courts Bill in the House of Lords, Lord Hunt of Wirral proposed an amendment to 'extend the remit of the sanction for fundamental dishonesty to cover not only injury claims but claims presented where the injury itself is used as a means by which a dishonest claim - for example, a claim for credit hire - is made.' This amendment was REJECTED. Lord Faulks QC (the minister) refused to accept it, stating it would 'unnecessarily complicate the clause, and could have unintended consequences that would not be desirable.' Lord Faulks specifically noted the risk to subrogated rights between insurers and the potential to disadvantage claimants' insurers. Lord Hunt withdrew his amendment. This is powerful evidence that Parliament considered and deliberately rejected extending s.57 to credit hire claims.

HANSARD - LORD FAULKS ON SUBROGATED RIGHTS: Lord Faulks explained that losses caught by Lord Hunt's amendment 'arise primarily in motor accident claims, and in practice payments for such losses are generally made up front by the claimant's insurer, and are then recovered by them from the defendant's insurer... This means that the amendment could affect subrogated rights between insurers, and could operate to the disadvantage of the claimant's insurer, who would find it much more difficult to recover such sums. That might in turn have the undesirable consequence of making insurers less inclined to make payments in respect of this kind of loss to genuine victims of accidents for whom, for example, the rapid replacement of a vehicle could be essential.'

HANSARD - LORD FAULKS'S PASSAGE RELIED ON BY HHJ SAUNDERS: HHJ Saunders in Basir quoted Lord Faulks as saying 'Clause 45 seeks to strengthen the law so that dismissal of the entire claim should become the norm in such cases.' However, Recorder Mark Jones in Diaw (para 88) found this passage equivocal - 'I can discern nothing from the passage quoted from Hansard that makes it clear that s.57 was to extend beyond personal injury claims' - and doubted whether the Pepper v Hart conditions for admitting Hansard were even met.

DIAW v ERS [2020] - THE LEADING COUNTER-AUTHORITY: Recorder Mark Jones conducted the most detailed judicial analysis of whether s.57 extends beyond PI claims. He explicitly held 'I think HHJ Saunders was wrong' (para 81) and rejected every plank of the Basir reasoning: the textual analysis, the perverse results argument, the Hansard reliance, the QOCS analogy, and the cited High Court cases. Crucially, HHJ Saunders in Basir did not have the benefit of argument from both sides (the claimant was unrepresented), whereas Recorder Mark Jones heard full argument from both counsel.

THE 'PERVERSE RESULTS' ARGUMENT - WHY IT FAILS: HHJ Saunders argued it would be perverse if a claimant who completely fabricated injuries kept their credit hire claim while one who merely exaggerated lost everything. Recorder Mark Jones rejected this (para 86): 'There is nothing odd about a provision designed to hit those who dishonestly exaggerate their injuries, or inflate their claims by adding on bogus claims in respect of physiotherapy or assistance in the garden... The fact that s.57 has no impact on the claimant who dishonestly invents his injuries in their entirety is neither here nor there.'

HIGH COURT CASES CITED IN BASIR - ALL DISTINGUISHABLE: The four High Court cases relied on by HHJ Saunders (Molodi v Cambridge Vibration Maintenance Service [2018] EWHC 1288, LOCOG v Sinfield [2018] EWHC 51, Stanton v Hunter [2017] LTL, Pinkus v Direct Line [2018] EWHC 1671) were all analysed by Recorder Mark Jones and found to provide no binding authority on the point. In each case, either the point was not argued, the ratio was distinguishable, or the court simply swept up small property damage claims without analysis.

QOCS PROVISIONS ARE DIFFERENT FROM S.57: The QOCS provisions (CPR 44.13-44.16) are drafted in materially different terms to s.57. CPR 44.16(2)(b) makes express provision for claims 'for the benefit of the claimant other than a claim to which this Section applies' - showing the draftsman explicitly addressed mixed claims. Section 57, by contrast, contains no such provision. Caution should be exercised before transferring jurisprudence from QOCS to s.57 (Diaw, paras 90-95, 105).

THE DAC BEACHCROFT TEMPLATE RESPONSE: Defendant insurers, particularly through DAC Beachcroft, routinely serve template responses citing Basir v Larizadeh and Blake v Advantage as authority for the proposition that credit hire claims will be dismissed if the claimant is found fundamentally dishonest. This template response is legally flawed because: (a) Basir is a county court decision with no binding authority; (b) Basir has been subject to detailed judicial criticism in Diaw v ERS; (c) Basir was decided without the benefit of argument from both sides; (d) the Hansard record shows Parliament explicitly rejected extending s.57 to credit hire; (e) the textual analysis of s.57 does not support the wider interpretation.

CORTEN v CLIFFORD - THE COMPROMISED HIGH COURT APPEAL: In Corten v Clifford, Horwich Farrelly (instructed by the defendant insurer) succeeded at first instance in having credit hire and property damage claims dismissed under s.57. Ben Williams QC was instructed for the appeal and prepared a skeleton argument comprehensively demolishing the wider interpretation. The case compromised before the appeal, with Horwich Farrelly agreeing not to pursue the same point in other cases. The skeleton argument stated: 'in the light of the legislative history, it is clear beyond argument that s.57 was only intended to lead to the demise of a claimant's personal injury claim, and not to any freestanding claims for special damages such as those arising from damage to property.'

HUMAN RIGHTS ACT ARGUMENT: An interpretation of s.57 that deprived a claimant of compensation for property damage (not just personal injury) would constitute an unjust and disproportionate interference with rights under ECHR Article 6 and Protocol 1, Article 1. It would also interfere with the rights of innocent third parties including insurers, vehicle finance providers, vehicle repairers, and credit hire companies who provide services in the legitimate expectation of recovery from the at-fault party. Under s.3(1) of the Human Rights Act 1998, the court must interpret s.57 consistently with these Convention rights.

PRACTICAL IMPACT ON CHO SOLICITORS: Smaller CHO solicitors who are unfamiliar with this area of law may be intimidated by the DAC Beachcroft template response and wrongly advise their clients to drop or settle credit hire claims when fundamental dishonesty is alleged. This is not legally justified. The proper response is to cite Diaw v ERS, the Hansard legislative history (Lord Hunt's rejected amendment), and the textual analysis of s.57 to demonstrate that credit hire claims survive a finding of fundamental dishonesty.

KEOGHS/DEFENDANT INSURER ASPIRATIONS: Gary Herring of Keoghs stated in September 2021 that one of their aspirations was 'Extending the definition of Fundamental Dishonesty beyond Personal Injury so as to include credit hire claims.' This confirms that the current law does NOT extend s.57 to credit hire - it is an aspiration, not the current legal position.

PATTERN OF INSURER BEHAVIOUR: Defendant insurers have a pattern of raising the s.57/credit hire point aggressively at county court level (where cases are often poorly defended or undefended on this point) but settling before appellate courts can provide binding authority. The Corten v Clifford compromise is an example. This prevents authoritative appellate guidance while allowing insurers to benefit from the chilling effect of the Basir decision on under-resourced claimant firms.

KEY COUNTER-ARGUMENT SUMMARY FOR PRACTITIONERS: When faced with a s.57 application seeking to dismiss credit hire claims, respond with: (1) s.57 on its plain wording applies only to 'claims for damages in respect of personal injury'; (2) Parliament explicitly considered and rejected extending s.57 to credit hire (Hansard, Lord Hunt's amendment, October 2014); (3) Lord Faulks refused the extension citing risks to subrogated rights; (4) Diaw v ERS [2020] provides detailed judicial analysis supporting this position; (5) Basir v Larizadeh is not binding, was decided without claimant representation, and has been subject to judicial criticism; (6) the Human Rights Act requires s.57 to be interpreted consistently with Convention rights.

Basir v Larizadeh [2019] (Central London County Court)
Corten v Clifford (High Court appeal - compromised)
Molodi v Cambridge Vibration Maintenance Service [2018] EWHC 1288 (QB)
LOCOG v Sinfield [2018] EWHC 51 (QB)
Stanton v Hunter [2017] LTL (Liverpool County Court)
Pinkus v Direct Line [2018] EWHC 1671 (QB)
Haider v DSM Demolition Ltd [2019] EWHC 2712 (QB)
Wagenaar v Weekend Travel Ltd [2014] EWCA Civ 1105
Pepper v Hart [1993] AC 593
Mustard v Flowers [2021] EWHC 846 (QB)
Howlett v Davies [2017] EWCA Civ 1696
Blake v Advantage Insurance Co Ltd
articles.full_text: This knowledge base entry covers the critical question of whether s.57 of the Criminal Justice and Courts Act 2015 extends to dismiss non-personal injury claims such as credit hire and vehicle repairs. The answer, based on textual analysis, legislative history, and judicial authority, is NO. Section 57 applies only to claims for damages in respect of personal injury. Credit hire claims, vehicle repair claims, and other non-PI heads of damage survive a finding of fundamental dishonesty. Key authorities: Diaw v ERS [2020] (Recorder Mark Jones - detailed analysis finding s.57 does not extend to non-PI claims), Basir v Larizadeh [2019] (HHJ Saunders - held s.57 extends to whole claim, but subsequently criticised in Diaw). Key Hansard reference: Criminal Justice and Courts Bill committee stage, House of Lords, October 2014 - Lord Hunt's amendment to extend s.57 to credit hire was rejected by Lord Faulks QC. See https://hansard.parliament.uk/Lords/2014-10-22/debates/14102295000250/CriminalJusticeAndCourtsBill
articles.source: Compiled from Diaw v ERS judgment, Basir v Larizadeh judgment, Hansard records, Corten v Clifford skeleton argument (Ben Williams QC), and practitioner webinar materials

articles.title: Bourgrine v Natoora Limited (County Court at Liverpool, HHJ Graham Wood KC, 23 February 2026)
articles.source: County Court at Liverpool (appeal from District Judge Metcalfe)
cases_referenced: Armstead v Royal and Sun Alliance Insurance Co Ltd [2025] AC 407
Giles v Thompson [1993] 3 All ER 32
Lagden v O'Connor [2004] 1 AC 1067
Bent v Highways and Utilities Construction [2012] RTR 17
Wilding v British Telecommunications PLC [2002] ICR 1079
Banco de Portugal v Waterlow [1932] AC 242
Summers v Fairclough Homes [2012] UKSC 26
key_points: BACKGROUND AND FACTS: The Claimant, a food delivery rider, was using a Honda motorcycle for social, domestic and pleasure purposes when it was struck by a forklift truck driven by the Defendant's employee in May 2020. Liability was admitted. The Claimant also owned an e-bike which he used for work (courier runs) and which had a battery life of approximately three hours before requiring a four-hour recharge. The Claimant hired replacement motorcycles on credit hire terms for 66 days. The claim comprised a personal injury claim, PAV of £1,225.80, storage charges of £1,107.60 and credit hire of £13,195.28. Impecuniosity was not pursued, so the hire claim ultimately fell to be considered on a BHR basis of c.£3,884.

FIRST INSTANCE: District Judge Metcalfe, sitting in the County Court at Liverpool on 20 September 2024, found the Claimant fundamentally dishonest in relation to the personal injury claim. In respect of the credit hire claim she concluded, at paragraph 48, that 'the Claimant has not established need on the balance of probabilities', relying in particular on (a) the availability of the e-bike, (b) the fact that the Claimant had not used the damaged motorcycle for work, and (c) the 'very significant cost of a credit hire motorbike'. The credit hire claim was accordingly dismissed in its entirety.

COURT ON APPEAL: His Honour Judge Graham Wood KC, Designated Civil Judge for Cheshire and Merseyside, sitting in the County Court at Liverpool. Counsel: Mr Benjamin Williams KC (Bond Turner) for the Appellant/Claimant; Mr Paul Higgins (Horwich Farrelly) for the Respondent/Defendant.

DECISION: Appeal allowed. The learned District Judge had misdirected herself in two respects. First, she had effectively placed a legal burden on the Claimant to prove need, when the correct position (recently clarified by the Supreme Court in Armstead v RSA [2025] AC 407 at [59]-[60]) is that the legal burden of proving a failure to mitigate lies on the defendant; the claimant carries only an evidential burden. Second, she had taken an irrelevant factor into account – the cost of the credit hire – when assessing need. Cost is a matter going to rate (and perhaps duration) and must be addressed separately under those headings: 'a compartmentalised approach' is required.

LEGAL AND EVIDENTIAL BURDENS ON NEED: At paragraph 35 the Court held: 'As the incurrence of credit hire charges goes to the issue of mitigation of loss, the starting point must be that there is no legal burden of proof on the Claimant to establish need… strictly speaking it is a defendant who has to show that a claimant has acted unreasonably. However in most cases there is little difference and the court will consider the Claimant's conduct under the general umbrella of reasonableness. Nevertheless there remains an evidential burden on a claimant which is particularly apposite in the context of the previous use of the vehicle which was damaged.' The District Judge's conclusion that the Claimant 'has not established need on the balance of probabilities' risked elevating the evidential burden to a legal one.

THE LOW THRESHOLD FOR NEED: The evidential burden on need is a low one. Applying Giles v Thompson [1993] 3 All ER 32, a claimant need not show that the vehicle was essential, or that the need arose on a daily basis. Lord Mustill's example in Giles of 'the Sunday afternoon drive' was cited: 'a claimant's evidential burden did not require him to show that a replacement motorbike was essential or that the need arose on a daily basis' (¶39).

ALTERNATIVE TRANSPORT: Where a claimant owns two DIFFERENT vehicles that serve different purposes (a motorcycle with significant range and load capacity vs an e-bike with limited range and a lengthy charging cycle), the existence of the second vehicle does not defeat need: 'Where the vehicles are different and serve different purposes, that is a conclusion which [sc. no need] is more difficult to sustain' (¶40). Contrast the hypothetical case of a claimant who owned two identical vehicles and only used one by choice.

COST IS IRRELEVANT TO NEED: The learned District Judge had erred by referring to 'the very significant cost of a credit hire motorbike' when analysing need. HHJ Wood KC held (¶41): 'there is demonstrably an error in her reference to the cost of the credit hire in the context of need. Whilst reasonableness of the cost would be at the forefront of the court's consideration on the issue of rate of hire it has no relevance to the issue of need.' Cost is a matter for rate (and, where impecuniosity is in issue, for the BHR/CHR debate) and for duration.

FUNDAMENTAL DISHONESTY AND THE WIDER EVIDENCE: A finding of fundamental dishonesty in relation to the personal injury claim does NOT automatically mean that the claimant's evidence on other heads of loss is rejected. The Defendant had relied on Summers v Fairclough Homes [2012] UKSC 26 at [52] to argue the contrary. HHJ Wood KC accepted that such a finding entitled the court to look at the other evidence more critically, but held that here the Claimant's evidence as to his pre-accident use of the Honda (including the 100-mile-per-week estimate and the social, domestic and pleasure use) had not been challenged in cross-examination and had not been rejected by the District Judge: '… in my judgment the learned District Judge did in fact find… that prior to the accident the Claimant had a motorcycle which he did not use for work, but only for social, domestic and pleasure purposes' (¶38).

DISPOSAL: The appeal was allowed on grounds 1 and 2(a). It was unnecessary to determine ground 2(b) (Wednesbury unreasonableness of the decision). HHJ Wood KC indicated that a short further hearing on period and rate would likely be required, subject to written submissions on the consequential orders.

SIGNIFICANCE FOR PRACTITIONERS: Although a decision of a Circuit Judge on a County Court appeal is not binding, the judgment carries considerable persuasive weight. It should be cited: (a) whenever a defendant argues that a claimant has 'failed to establish need'; (b) whenever a first-instance judge appears to impose a legal burden on the claimant as to need; (c) whenever the availability of a materially different alternative vehicle (including an e-bike) is said to extinguish need; and (d) whenever the cost of the credit hire is wrongly imported into the analysis of need rather than rate. The judgment is also a useful route into the Supreme Court's articulation of the burden of proof in Armstead v RSA [2025] AC 407.
articles.full_text:
BOURGRINE v NATOORA LIMITED

In the County Court at Liverpool (on appeal from District Judge Metcalfe)
Case No 080DC180 – Appeal No 88 of 2024
Before His Honour Judge Graham Wood KC
Hearing: 17 February 2026 – Judgment: 23 February 2026
Mr Benjamin Williams KC (instructed by Bond Turner Solicitors) for the Appellant/Claimant
Mr Paul Higgins (instructed by Horwich Farrelly Solicitors) for the Respondent/Defendant

HEADNOTE
An appeal from a fast-track decision of District Judge Metcalfe dismissing a credit hire claim on the ground that the Claimant, a food delivery rider whose Honda motorcycle had been struck by a Defendant's forklift, had failed to establish need. Liability was not in issue. The Claimant had been found fundamentally dishonest in relation to his personal injury claim, but impecuniosity had been conceded at trial so the hire claim was to be considered on a BHR basis. The District Judge concluded (at ¶48) that the Claimant 'has not established need on the balance of probabilities'.

HELD, allowing the appeal: (1) The legal burden of proving a failure to mitigate loss lies on the defendant (Armstead v RSA [2025] AC 407 at [59]–[60]). A claimant bears only an evidential burden as to need. The District Judge's language risked elevating that evidential burden into a legal burden. (2) The evidential burden on need is a low one (Giles v Thompson [1993] 3 All ER 32): occasional use, including social, domestic and pleasure use, will usually suffice. (3) The availability of a materially different alternative vehicle (here, an e-bike with limited range and lengthy charging time) does not, without more, defeat need where the damaged vehicle served a different purpose. (4) The cost of the credit hire is irrelevant to need: cost goes to rate and duration, which must be analysed separately under a 'compartmentalised approach'. (5) A finding of fundamental dishonesty on the PI claim does not automatically taint the claimant's evidence on other heads of loss, though the court may approach it more critically (Summers v Fairclough Homes [2012] UKSC 26).

KEY PASSAGES
¶35: 'As the incurrence of credit hire charges goes to the issue of mitigation of loss, the starting point must be that there is no legal burden of proof on the Claimant to establish need.'
¶36: the District Judge's concluding finding that the Claimant 'has not established need on the balance of probabilities' gave rise to concern about the correct allocation of burdens.
¶39: 'a claimant's evidential burden did not require him to show that a replacement motorbike was essential or that the need arose on a daily basis'.
¶40: 'Where the vehicles are different and serve different purposes, that is a conclusion which is more difficult to sustain… having accepted the premise of the previous usage, in my judgment she had no alternative but to accept that the Claimant had discharged the evidential burden in relation to previous need.'
¶41: 'there is demonstrably an error in her reference to the cost of the credit hire in the context of need. Whilst reasonableness of the cost would be at the forefront of the court's consideration on the issue of rate of hire it has no relevance to the issue of need.'
¶42: 'it is important that a compartmentalised approach is taken with the starting point addressing need only, where the rate of hire is not taken into account.'
¶43: 'this appeal is allowed on the basis of the two errors of law identified in grounds 1 and 2(a).'

articles.title: Need, Burden of Proof and Reasonableness after Bourgrine v Natoora: the Circuit Bench Confirms the Post-Armstead Position
articles.author: Steve Evans
articles.summary: A commentary on Bourgrine v Natoora Limited (County Court at Liverpool, 23 February 2026, HHJ Graham Wood KC), decided on appeal from District Judge Metcalfe and argued by Mr Benjamin Williams KC. The judgment is a persuasive post-Armstead statement of the proper allocation of the legal and evidential burdens on need in credit hire, and of the often-neglected distinction between the practical test ('has the claimant acted reasonably?') and the strict legal test ('has the defendant shown that the claimant acted unreasonably?'). It is also a clear authority for the proposition that the cost of the credit hire cannot be imported into the analysis of need.
key_points: The legal burden of proving a failure to mitigate is on the defendant, as confirmed by the Supreme Court in Armstead v Royal and Sun Alliance Insurance Co Ltd [2025] AC 407 at [59]-[60]. HHJ Wood KC's judgment transmits that principle into the everyday context of a fast-track motorcycle hire claim.

The claimant carries only an evidential burden as to need. That burden is low. HHJ Wood KC endorsed the formulation in Pattni and Bent v Highways and Utilities Construction [2012] RTR 17 at [32] and the example in Giles v Thompson [1993] 3 All ER 32 of the occasional Sunday driver.

Practitioners often slip into saying that 'the claimant must prove need on the balance of probabilities'. That is a useful shorthand but - strictly - incorrect. The strict question is whether the defendant has shown that the claimant acted unreasonably in hiring. HHJ Wood KC accepted (at ¶35) that 'in most cases there is little difference' but warned that the distinction matters when the evidential burden is being treated as a legal burden.

The distinction between 'reasonable' and 'unreasonable' was drawn sharply by the Court of Appeal in Wilding v British Telecommunications PLC [2002] ICR 1079 at [55], quoting Banco de Portugal v Waterlow [1932] AC 242: it is not enough for the wrongdoer to show that it would have been reasonable for the claimant to take alternative steps - the defendant must show that it was unreasonable of the claimant not to take them. Bourgrine confirms that this distinction applies to credit hire.

The 'cost' of the credit hire is IRRELEVANT to need. Where a first-instance judge describes a credit hire as 'very expensive' and reasons back from that to a conclusion of 'no need', an appeal will lie. Cost goes to rate (and in impecuniosity cases to the BHR/CHR question) and occasionally to duration, but it must be analysed under those separate headings. HHJ Wood KC's call for a 'compartmentalised approach' (¶42) is the practical takeaway.

Alternative transport arguments require nuance. The availability of an e-bike (with a three-hour battery life and a four-hour charging cycle) does not displace the need for a motorcycle used for different purposes. Contrast the hypothetical case of two identical vehicles and only one in use: there the absence of need may well be obvious.

A finding of fundamental dishonesty on a personal injury claim does not automatically destroy a claimant's evidence on other heads of loss. Summers v Fairclough Homes [2012] UKSC 26 at [52] entitles the court to look at the other evidence more critically, but unchallenged factual evidence on need should still be accepted where the cross-examination has not challenged it. Defence counsel who do not put need squarely in cross-examination may find themselves unable to rely on the fundamental dishonesty finding to resist the hire claim.

The judgment is persuasive rather than binding. However it is a reasoned decision of HHJ Graham Wood KC, Designated Civil Judge for Cheshire and Merseyside, argued by Mr Benjamin Williams KC, and expressly drawing on Armstead, Giles v Thompson, Lagden v O'Connor and Pattni and Bent. It therefore carries currency in first-instance trials before District and Deputy District Judges, particularly in the North West circuit but also more widely.

LE Jones (Insurance Brokers) Ltd v Portsmouth City Council [2002] EWCA Civ 1723
articles.full_text:
NEED, BURDEN OF PROOF AND REASONABLENESS AFTER BOURGRINE v NATOORA
Steve Evans of The Credit Hire Forum
April 2026

INTRODUCTION
On 23 February 2026 His Honour Judge Graham Wood KC, Designated Civil Judge for Cheshire and Merseyside, handed down judgment in Bourgrine v Natoora Limited, an appeal from District Judge Metcalfe in the Liverpool County Court. The appeal, argued for the Claimant/Appellant by Mr Benjamin Williams KC, concerned a fast-track motorcycle credit hire claim of the kind that dominates the daily list in Liverpool: a delivery rider, a Honda motorcycle struck by a Defendant's forklift, a PAV of a few thousand pounds, a modest storage claim and a credit hire claim of over £13,000. The learned District Judge had found the Claimant fundamentally dishonest on the personal injury claim and had dismissed the credit hire claim at the first hurdle on the basis that the Claimant had 'not established need on the balance of probabilities'. On appeal, HHJ Wood KC allowed the appeal on two grounds, both of which go to the heart of day-to-day credit hire practice.

This article considers five points: (1) the proper allocation of the legal and evidential burdens on need; (2) the difference between 'reasonable' and 'unreasonable' behaviour and why the distinction matters; (3) why the cost of the credit hire is irrelevant to need; (4) the treatment of alternative transport and vehicles serving different purposes; and (5) the effect of a finding of fundamental dishonesty on ancillary heads of loss.

1. LEGAL BURDEN v EVIDENTIAL BURDEN
The Supreme Court in Armstead v Royal and Sun Alliance Insurance Co Ltd [2025] AC 407 at [59]-[60] has provided a definitive statement that, once a claimant has proved that a tort has been committed and that a loss has been caused, the defendant bears the legal burden of pleading and proving the 'limiting principles' – including failure to mitigate. Bourgrine transmits that principle into the context of a typical credit hire claim. HHJ Wood KC held (¶35): 'As the incurrence of credit hire charges goes to the issue of mitigation of loss, the starting point must be that there is no legal burden of proof on the Claimant to establish need… strictly speaking it is a defendant who has to show that a claimant has acted unreasonably.' The claimant's burden on need is evidential only. It is therefore a legal error for a first-instance judge to conclude that a claimant 'has not established need on the balance of probabilities' in a way that treats the evidential burden as a legal burden.

2. REASONABLE v UNREASONABLE
HHJ Wood KC drew a clear distinction between the practical formulation ('has the claimant behaved reasonably?') and the strict legal test ('has the defendant shown that the claimant behaved unreasonably?'). The distinction flows from Lord MacMillan in Banco de Portugal v Waterlow [1932] AC 242 and the speech of Sedley LJ in Wilding v British Telecommunications PLC [2002] ICR 1079 at [55]: 'It is not enough for the wrongdoer to show that it would have been reasonable to take the steps he has proposed: he must show that it was unreasonable of the innocent party not to take them. This is a real distinction.' The practical consequence is that a defendant cannot succeed on mitigation simply by pointing to a cheaper or less inconvenient alternative. The defendant must go further and show that it was unreasonable of the claimant not to take it. Bourgrine is a useful authority to deploy whenever a defendant argues mitigation on the basis of mere availability of alternatives without engaging with the reasonableness test.

3. COST IS IRRELEVANT TO NEED
Perhaps the most practically important point in the judgment concerns the segregation of need from cost. District Judge Metcalfe had held at ¶48 that it was not reasonable for the Claimant 'to have incurred the very significant cost of a credit hire motorbike when he could and did use the e-bike as an acceptable alternative'. HHJ Wood KC allowed the appeal on this ground among others, holding (¶41): 'there is demonstrably an error in her reference to the cost of the credit hire in the context of need. Whilst reasonableness of the cost would be at the forefront of the court's consideration on the issue of rate of hire it has no relevance to the issue of need.' And, at ¶42: 'it is important that a compartmentalised approach is taken with the starting point addressing need only, where the rate of hire is not taken into account.' The Circuit Bench has thus reminded District Judges that the structure of the analysis matters: need, period, type of vehicle and rate are distinct questions and must be addressed separately. Cost considerations belong to rate (and, where impecuniosity is in issue, to the BHR/CHR debate) and occasionally to duration.

4. ALTERNATIVE TRANSPORT AND VEHICLES SERVING DIFFERENT PURPOSES
Defendants regularly argue that the availability of an alternative vehicle (a partner's car, a bicycle, public transport, an e-bike) defeats need. HHJ Wood KC addressed this squarely at ¶40: 'In some situations a court might be entitled to conclude that where a claimant owned two identical vehicles and only used one of them by choice, that there was no evidence of the need to hire. Where the vehicles are different and serve different purposes, that is a conclusion which is more difficult to sustain.' In Bourgrine the e-bike had a three-hour battery life, a four-hour charging cycle and was used for work as a courier; the Honda was used for longer journeys and for running errands. The two vehicles served different purposes. The Court of Appeal's observation in Giles v Thompson that need can be established even by the occasional Sunday afternoon drive underscores how low the threshold is.

5. FUNDAMENTAL DISHONESTY AND ANCILLARY LOSSES
The Defendant relied on Summers v Fairclough Homes [2012] UKSC 26 at [52] to argue that a finding of fundamental dishonesty on the PI claim should cause the court to reject or discount the Claimant's other evidence, including his evidence on need. HHJ Wood KC accepted that such a finding entitled the court to scrutinise the remaining evidence more critically, but held on the facts that the Claimant's evidence as to his pre-accident usage of the motorcycle (running errands, social and domestic use, up to 100 miles per week) was unchallenged in cross-examination and had not been rejected by the District Judge. Defence counsel who do not put need squarely in cross-examination may therefore find themselves unable to rely on the fundamental dishonesty finding to defeat the hire claim.

STATUS OF THE JUDGMENT
Bourgrine is a decision of a Circuit Judge sitting in a County Court appeal. It is not formally binding. But its persuasive weight is significant: it is a reasoned judgment of HHJ Graham Wood KC, argued by Mr Benjamin Williams KC, and it turns on (a) binding Supreme Court authority (Armstead), (b) House of Lords/Court of Appeal authority (Giles v Thompson, Lagden v O'Connor, Pattni and Bent) and (c) long-standing principles on mitigation (Banco de Portugal, Wilding). Claimant practitioners should deploy it where a defendant seeks to place the legal burden on the claimant, where an alternative vehicle argument is being over-deployed, or where a first-instance judge has allowed cost to infect the analysis of need. Defence practitioners should expect it to be cited and should pitch their cross-examination and pleadings accordingly.

CONCLUSION
Bourgrine v Natoora is a modest, fast-track appeal judgment. It does not break new ground so much as remind us – clearly and at Circuit Judge level – of what the Supreme Court and the House of Lords have long held. Its value lies in the clarity with which it applies these principles to the daily diet of motorcycle hire cases in the County Court. It should be on the shelf of every credit hire practitioner, claimant and defendant alike.
articles.source: The Credit Hire Forum

articles.title: Scotland — QOCS, Increased Litigation and the Defender Strategy of Blanket Fraud Allegations (MASS / Dallas McMillan, 2026)
articles.summary: The Motor Accident Solicitors Society (MASS) responds to Scottish defender agents' criticism of increased litigation volumes in Scotland, arguing that the rise is a foreseeable and legitimate consequence of QOCS providing access to justice, and that the real driver of increased litigation is defenders' blanket approach of alleging fraud rather than engaging with claims on their merits under the Compulsory Pre-Action Protocol.
articles.content:
Scottish defender agents have published articles in industry journals and The Scotsman newspaper criticising the increase in litigated cases in Scotland since the introduction of Qualified One-Way Costs Shifting (QOCS), arguing it gives pursuers an unfair advantage. MASS, through Dallas McMillan solicitors, challenges this narrative on several grounds.

First, the increase in litigation is a foreseeable consequence of QOCS — it enables individuals to access justice where previously they could not afford to litigate. Second, the Scottish courts and the majority of pursuer agents have consistently taken a robust approach to weeding out potentially fraudulent claims at the earliest stage.

The article identifies a problematic trend among defender firms: blanket fraud allegations deployed as a pre-litigation strategy, often running contrary to the Compulsory Pre-Action Protocol. Defenders are responding to intimations by claiming 'concerns' about claims simply because they were not intimated immediately after the accident — despite there being no legal deadline for intimation beyond the limitation period. These blanket denials frustrate the legitimate pre-litigation process and force cases into litigation that could otherwise be resolved earlier.

Critically, MASS argues that QOCS does not give pursuers an unfair advantage. The balance is built into the system: if a claim is found to be fraudulent, defenders can ask the court to set aside QOCS, meaning the pursuer bears the costs. The articles criticising increased litigation consistently fail to mention this safeguard.

The practical consequence of the defender approach is self-defeating: immediate presumption of fraud followed by media commentary causes more litigation, increases costs, and leads to larger bills for insurance companies — the very outcome defenders claim to oppose.
key_principles: QOCS in Scotland provides access to justice for pursuers who previously could not afford to litigate — the increase in litigated cases is a foreseeable and legitimate consequence
Scottish courts and pursuer agents have consistently taken a robust approach to identifying and removing fraudulent claims at the earliest stage of litigation
Defender firms are deploying blanket fraud allegations pre-litigation that run contrary to the Compulsory Pre-Action Protocol, frustrating the legitimate claims process

There is no legal deadline by which a claim must be intimated beyond the limitation period — defenders alleging fraud simply because a claim was not intimated immediately after the accident are acting without justification
QOCS does not give pursuers an unfair advantage: if a claim is found fraudulent, defenders can apply to have QOCS set aside and recover their costs — this is the built-in balance
The defender strategy of blanket fraud denials is self-defeating — it forces cases into litigation that could be resolved pre-litigation, increasing costs for insurance companies
When faced with unjustified fraud allegations, pursuers should refuse to accept the position, proceed to instruct medical evidence, and litigate as the Protocol permits
Reducing fraudulent claims requires cooperation between both sides, not a unilateral presumption of fraud by defenders followed by industry journal and media campaigns
articles.source: MASS (The Motor Accident Solicitors Society) / Dallas McMillan Solicitors, 'Get the Balance Right – Increased Litigation in Scotland', April 2026
articles.author: Dallas McMillan Solicitors (for MASS)

articles.title: Illegality (Ex Turpi Causa) — Uninsured Claimant's Driving Without Insurance Is No Bar to Recovery of Hire Charges (Edgar v Rayson, 2000)
articles.summary: County Court authority in which the defendant's appeal against an award of £10,400 for personal injuries, motorcycle damage and associated losses (including hire of an alternative vehicle) was dismissed notwithstanding that the claimant had been uninsured at the time of the accident. The court held that illegality was no bar to recovery because the claimant did not need to plead or rely upon the illegality to establish his claim, the direct losses were not tainted by the illegality, and the hire agreement itself was provided with insurance and therefore untainted.
articles.content:
Edgar v Rayson is a County Court decision (Altrincham County Court, Judge Tetlow, 23 October 2000) addressing whether a claimant's lack of motor insurance at the time of a road traffic accident operates as a bar to recovering damages including credit hire charges.

The defendant (R) appealed against an award of £10,400 in favour of the claimant (E) for personal injuries, motorcycle damage, and associated losses including hire of an alternative vehicle. E had not been insured at the time of the accident and there was no evidence he would have arranged insurance in the period following the accident. R contended that E should not recover in tort because illegality (driving without insurance) acted as a bar to recovery under the doctrine of ex turpi causa.

The court dismissed the appeal on three grounds. First, applying the test from Tinsley v Milligan [1994] 1 AC 340 and Revill v Newberry [1996] QB 567, the appropriate question was whether E had to plead or rely upon the illegality in order to establish his case — he did not. Second, the direct losses (personal injury and the value of the motorcycle) were not tainted by the illegality and E could recover these heads of loss. Third, E's hire of an alternative vehicle could also be recovered because the hire car itself was provided with insurance and the hire agreement was therefore not tainted by the illegality.

The court further observed that the purpose of motor insurance was to provide protection against claims brought by injured third parties. The law only required a person to have third party insurance to protect a tortfeasor from claims brought by third parties as a result of his negligence. Hence, even if E had been riding legally with third party insurance, R would still have been required to compensate him.

Note: This is a County Court decision and is not binding on other courts. The underlying doctrine of ex turpi causa has been considered at higher levels in Tinsley v Milligan (House of Lords) and Revill v Newberry (Court of Appeal), which remain the leading authorities. However, Edgar v Rayson provides a useful illustration of how the doctrine applies specifically in the credit hire context where the claimant was an uninsured road user.
key_principles: The test for whether illegality bars recovery is whether the claimant has to plead or rely upon the illegality to establish his case (Tinsley v Milligan applied) — if not, ex turpi causa does not operate as a defence

A claimant's lack of motor insurance at the time of the accident does not taint the direct losses (personal injury and vehicle damage) because those losses arise from the defendant's negligence, not from the claimant's uninsured status
Credit hire charges can be recovered by an uninsured claimant because the hire agreement itself is provided with insurance and is therefore not tainted by the claimant's original illegality

The statutory purpose of compulsory motor insurance is to protect injured third parties from the tortfeasor's negligence — it does not exist to shield the negligent defendant from liability to the uninsured victim
Even if the claimant had been legally insured, the defendant would still have been required to compensate him — the absence of insurance does not alter the defendant's liability
This is a County Court decision (not binding) but provides a practical illustration of how ex turpi causa applies in credit hire claims involving uninsured claimants
The leading authorities on illegality as a defence remain Tinsley v Milligan [1994] 1 AC 340 (HL) and Revill v Newberry [1996] QB 567 (CA) — Edgar v Rayson applies those principles at first instance
cases_referenced: Edgar v Rayson (Altrincham County Court, Judge Tetlow, 23 October 2000, unreported) [Ex rel. NDH Edwards, Barrister, 8 King Street, Manchester]
Tinsley v Milligan [1994] 1 AC 340; [1993] CLY 1839 (House of Lords)
Revill v Newberry [1996] QB 567; [1995] CLY 3660 (Court of Appeal)
articles.source: Current Law Year Book / member referral, April 2026
articles.author: NDH Edwards (ex rel.)
articles.notes: County Court decision — not binding on other courts. Included in knowledge base as a practical illustration of the application of ex turpi causa in credit hire claims. Users should rely primarily on the House of Lords and Court of Appeal authorities (Tinsley v Milligan; Revill v Newberry) when addressing illegality defences. See also Patel v Mirza [2016] UKSC 42 for the Supreme Court's more recent reformulation of the illegality doctrine.

articles.title: No Duty to Use Credit Cards or Personal Credit Facilities Before Entering Credit Hire (Impecuniosity and Mitigation)
tags: no obligation to use credit card
articles.summary: Analysis of whether a claimant has a duty to use personal credit cards or other personal credit facilities (overdraft, loan, savings) to fund spot hire rather than entering a credit hire agreement. The answer is clear: there is no such duty. The House of Lords in Lagden v O'Connor held that an impecunious claimant who cannot afford to pay spot hire rates is entitled to recover the credit hire rate. The question of impecuniosity and whether a claimant should exhaust personal credit facilities is well settled — a claimant is not required to put themselves into debt, deplete savings, or use credit cards to fund a replacement vehicle.
articles.content:
A common defendant argument in credit hire litigation is that the claimant should have used a personal credit card (or other personal credit facility such as an overdraft, loan, or savings) to hire a vehicle at the basic/spot hire rate rather than entering a credit hire agreement at the higher credit hire rate. This argument fundamentally misunderstands the law on impecuniosity and mitigation.

THE LAW ON IMPECUNIOSITY AND CREDIT HIRE

The House of Lords in Lagden v O'Connor [2004] 1 AC 1067 established that where a claimant is impecunious — meaning they cannot afford to pay the spot hire rate upfront — the claimant is entitled to recover the credit hire rate even though it is higher than the basic hire rate. The additional cost arising from the claimant's impecuniosity is recoverable because it was reasonably foreseeable that a person of modest means would need to use a credit hire company.

Critically, there is no requirement for a claimant to exhaust personal credit facilities before being treated as impecunious. A claimant does not have to:

1. Use personal credit cards to fund spot hire.
2. Take out a personal loan to fund spot hire.
3. Use an overdraft facility to fund spot hire.
4. Deplete savings to fund spot hire.
5. Borrow from family or friends.

The reason is straightforward: the duty to mitigate does not require a claimant to put themselves into debt or deplete their financial resources to reduce the tortfeasor's liability. As Lord Hope stated in Lagden v O'Connor, the wrongdoer must take the victim as he finds them, including their financial circumstances.

WHY CREDIT CARDS ARE NOT RELEVANT TO MITIGATION

The duty to mitigate requires a claimant to take reasonable steps to minimise loss. However, 'reasonable' does not mean the claimant must incur personal financial risk or obligation. Using a credit card to hire a vehicle at spot rates would mean:

- The claimant taking on personal debt (credit card liability) to reduce the defendant's exposure.
- The claimant bearing interest charges on that debt.
- The claimant risking their credit rating if unable to repay.
- The claimant depleting available credit that may be needed for essential living expenses.

None of these steps can properly be described as 'reasonable' mitigation. The law does not require a claimant to subsidise the tortfeasor's wrongdoing by taking on personal financial risk.

THE ADDITIONAL BENEFITS POINT

In Dimond v Lovell [2002] 1 AC 384, the House of Lords held that a credit hire agreement provides additional benefits beyond mere hire — principally the benefit of credit (not having to pay upfront) and the claims management/accident management services. Where the claimant is impecunious (Lagden), the credit element is recoverable. Whether or not the claimant has a credit card is irrelevant to the question of what the claimant's hire arrangement actually provided.

PRACTICAL APPLICATION

When a defendant argues that the claimant should have used a credit card:

- This is not a valid challenge to the rate claimed. The question is whether the claimant was impecunious, not whether they had unused credit facilities.
- Having a credit card does not mean the claimant is not impecunious. Impecuniosity is assessed by reference to the claimant's ability to pay the spot hire rate from their own resources without unreasonable financial hardship.
- A credit card represents borrowed money, not the claimant's own resources. Using it would create a new personal liability.
- The defendant is the wrongdoer. It is not for the claimant to go into debt to reduce the consequences of the defendant's tort.

KEY AUTHORITIES

- Lagden v O'Connor [2004] 1 AC 1067 (House of Lords): The leading authority on impecuniosity in credit hire. An impecunious claimant can recover the credit hire rate.
- Dimond v Lovell [2002] 1 AC 384 (House of Lords): Established the 'additional benefits' analysis for credit hire agreements.
- Giles v Thompson [1994] 1 AC 142 (House of Lords): The foundational credit hire authority establishing that credit hire agreements are not champertous and that need for hire must be established.
- Zurich Insurance v Sherrill (County Court): Addressed whether a claimant was required to use personal funds/credit to hire at basic rates — held that the claimant was not so required.

SUMMARY

There is no duty on a claimant to use credit cards, personal loans, overdrafts, savings, or any other personal credit facility to fund spot hire in order to avoid the higher credit hire rate. The law is clear: if a claimant is impecunious (cannot afford to pay upfront), the credit hire rate is recoverable. The existence of personal credit facilities does not prevent a finding of impecuniosity, and the duty to mitigate does not extend to requiring a claimant to incur personal debt to reduce the tortfeasor's liability.
key_principles: A claimant has NO duty to use personal credit cards to fund spot hire instead of credit hire — the duty to mitigate does not require a claimant to incur personal debt to reduce the tortfeasor's liability

Impecuniosity is assessed by reference to whether the claimant can afford the spot hire rate from their own resources — having a credit card does not mean the claimant is not impecunious (Lagden v O'Connor [2004] 1 AC 1067)
A credit card represents borrowed money, not the claimant's own resources — using it would create a new personal liability that the claimant should not be required to take on
The wrongdoer must take the victim as he finds them, including their financial circumstances (Lord Hope in Lagden v O'Connor)
There is no requirement to exhaust personal credit facilities (credit cards, overdrafts, loans, savings) before being treated as impecunious
The duty to mitigate requires reasonable steps — it is not reasonable to expect a claimant to risk their credit rating, deplete essential credit, or take on debt to reduce the defendant's exposure

Dimond v Lovell [2002] 1 AC 384 recognises that credit hire provides additional benefits (credit and claims management) — whether the claimant has a credit card does not affect the analysis of what the hire agreement provided
cases_referenced: Lagden v O'Connor [2004] 1 AC 1067 (House of Lords)
Dimond v Lovell [2002] 1 AC 384 (House of Lords)
Giles v Thompson [1994] 1 AC 142 (House of Lords)
Zurich Insurance v Sherrill (County Court)
articles.source: Credit Hire AI Knowledge Base — compiled from established authorities on impecuniosity and mitigation, April 2026
articles.author: Credit Hire AI
articles.author_role: Knowledge base — synthesised from House of Lords authorities

articles.title: No Insurance and Credit Hire — Illegality, Targeted Causation and the Appellate Authorities (Ali v HSF, Hewison v Meridian Shipping, Patel v Mirza)
articles.summary: Comprehensive analysis of how the absence of motor insurance affects a credit hire claim, drawing on the appellate authorities. Ali v HSF Logistics [2024] EWCA Civ 1479 (Court of Appeal) confirms that illegality in credit hire is addressed through 'targeted illegality' based on causation, not a blanket bar. At paragraph 19, Stuart-Smith LJ left open whether no insurance — a more serious offence than no MOT — might ground a broader illegality defence, referencing Hewison v Meridian Shipping [2002] EWCA Civ 1821 (Court of Appeal) where fraudulent concealment of epilepsy to obtain employment was held to bar recovery. Patel v Mirza [2016] UKSC 42 (Supreme Court) provides the modern framework requiring proportionality. This article synthesises these authorities for practitioners advising on credit hire claims where the claimant was uninsured.
articles.content:
THE ISSUE

Where a claimant was driving without motor insurance at the time of the accident, the defendant may argue that the credit hire claim is barred by illegality (ex turpi causa). This is a more serious issue than a missing MOT — driving without insurance is a criminal offence under s.143 Road Traffic Act 1988, carrying up to 6 penalty points, an unlimited fine, and discretionary disqualification. The question for practitioners is: does the absence of insurance defeat the credit hire claim?

THE APPELLATE FRAMEWORK

1. Patel v Mirza [2016] UKSC 42 (Supreme Court)

The leading modern authority on illegality. The Supreme Court replaced the old 'reliance test' from Tinsley v Milligan with a structured balancing exercise. The court must consider: (a) the underlying purpose of the prohibition that has been transgressed and whether that purpose will be enhanced by denial of the claim; (b) any other relevant public policy on which the denial of the claim may have an impact; and (c) whether denial of the claim would be a proportionate response to the illegality. This proportionality framework governs all illegality defences in credit hire.

2. Ali v HSF Logistics [2024] EWCA Civ 1479 (Court of Appeal)

The Court of Appeal confirmed that in credit hire cases involving regulatory illegality (such as no MOT), the preferred approach is 'targeted illegality' — a causation-based analysis rather than a blanket bar. The question is: but for the accident, for how long would the claimant's vehicle have remained in its unlawful state? Hire charges are disallowed only for that period.

CRITICALLY, at paragraph 19, Stuart-Smith LJ addressed the insurance point directly. He observed that driving without insurance presents a potentially stronger case for the illegality defence than driving without an MOT. An MOT offence is summary-only, carries no penalty points and no disqualification. By contrast, driving without insurance is a more serious criminal offence carrying endorsement and discretionary disqualification. Stuart-Smith LJ did not decide the insurance point but left it open — noting that the same targeted illegality/causation approach may apply, but that the greater seriousness of the offence might tip the proportionality balance differently.

3. Hewison v Meridian Shipping Services Pte Ltd [2002] EWCA Civ 1821 (Court of Appeal)

Referenced in the context of Ali v HSF, Hewison is a Court of Appeal authority on illegality where the claimant had fraudulently concealed his epilepsy to obtain employment as a seafarer. When he was injured at work, the court held that his claim for loss of earnings was barred by illegality because his employment was obtained through criminal deception. The illegality was directly connected to the loss claimed — the employment itself was unlawful.

Hewison is relevant to no-insurance credit hire cases because it illustrates where the Court of Appeal has found illegality sufficiently serious and causally connected to bar recovery. The analogy is that if the claimant's presence on the road was itself unlawful (because uninsured), and the loss claimed arises from being deprived of the use of that unlawfully-driven vehicle, there is a closer causal nexus between the illegality and the loss than in a simple no-MOT case.

However, Hewison is distinguishable: the fraud in Hewison went to the very foundation of the employment relationship, whereas a claimant driving without insurance may have simply forgotten to renew, or the policy may have lapsed inadvertently. The degree of moral turpitude matters under the Patel v Mirza framework.

APPLICATION TO CREDIT HIRE CLAIMS

For practitioners, the position on no-insurance credit hire claims is as follows:

(a) The claim is NOT automatically barred. Patel v Mirza requires a proportionality analysis, not a blanket rule. The court must weigh the seriousness of the illegality against the consequences of denying the claim.

(b) The targeted illegality approach from Ali v HSF will likely apply: the causation question is for how long the claimant would have remained uninsured but for the accident. If the claimant would have renewed insurance within days, only that short period is disallowed.

(c) However, no insurance is MORE serious than no MOT. Stuart-Smith LJ's obiter at paragraph 19 of Ali signals that the Court of Appeal recognises this distinction. A claimant who has been uninsured for a prolonged period with no intention to obtain insurance faces a stronger illegality argument.

(d) The claimant's state of mind matters. Was the lack of insurance deliberate, reckless, or inadvertent? Under Patel v Mirza's proportionality framework, a claimant who genuinely forgot to renew is in a very different position from one who deliberately chose not to insure.

(e) Edgar v Rayson (County Court, 2000) held that an uninsured claimant could recover hire charges because the illegality did not need to be pleaded or relied upon (applying Tinsley v Milligan). However, this is a first-instance decision pre-dating Patel v Mirza. The Patel v Mirza proportionality framework now governs, and the analysis may be more nuanced than the simple 'reliance test' applied in Edgar v Rayson.

(f) The hire agreement itself is provided with insurance by the credit hire organisation. This means the replacement vehicle is lawfully insured during the hire period — the hire arrangement is not tainted by the claimant's prior lack of insurance.

EVIDENTIAL CONSIDERATIONS

Claimant practitioners should gather evidence of:
- When the insurance lapsed and why
- Whether the claimant had a history of continuous insurance
- Whether renewal was imminent or had been arranged
- The claimant's awareness (or lack of awareness) of the lapse
- Whether the vehicle was otherwise roadworthy and taxed

Defendant practitioners should investigate:
- The duration of the uninsured period
- Whether the claimant had any intention to obtain insurance
- The claimant's driving record and any previous insurance offences
- Whether the claimant was prosecuted for the insurance offence

KEY DISTINCTION FROM EDGAR v RAYSON

Edgar v Rayson (County Court, 2000) applied the pre-Patel v Mirza 'reliance test' from Tinsley v Milligan and held that an uninsured claimant could recover. While this remains a useful illustration, practitioners should be aware that: (1) it is a County Court decision and not binding; (2) the Supreme Court in Patel v Mirza [2016] replaced the reliance test with a broader proportionality framework; and (3) the Court of Appeal in Ali v HSF [2024] has signalled at paragraph 19 that no-insurance cases may attract a stronger illegality argument than no-MOT cases. The appellate authorities should be the primary focus of any submission on this point.
key_principles: Driving without insurance is a more serious offence than driving without an MOT — Stuart-Smith LJ acknowledged this distinction at paragraph 19 of Ali v HSF Logistics [2024] EWCA Civ 1479 (Court of Appeal), leaving open whether the illegality defence applies more broadly in no-insurance cases

Patel v Mirza [2016] UKSC 42 (Supreme Court) provides the governing framework for all illegality defences: the court must balance (a) the purpose of the prohibition, (b) other relevant public policies, and (c) proportionality — there is no blanket rule barring or permitting recovery

The 'targeted illegality' approach from Ali v HSF applies: the causation question is for how long the claimant would have remained uninsured but for the accident — hire charges are disallowed only for that period, not the entire claim

Hewison v Meridian Shipping [2002] EWCA Civ 1821 (Court of Appeal) illustrates where illegality was sufficiently serious and causally connected to bar recovery — relevant by analogy where the claimant's presence on the road was itself unlawful due to lack of insurance
The claimant's state of mind is critical under the Patel v Mirza proportionality framework: inadvertent lapse is treated very differently from deliberate non-insurance

Edgar v Rayson (County Court, 2000) held an uninsured claimant could recover, but this pre-dates Patel v Mirza and is not binding — the appellate authorities (Ali, Patel v Mirza, Hewison) should be the primary focus of submissions
The credit hire agreement itself is provided with insurance, so the replacement vehicle is lawfully insured — the hire arrangement is not tainted by the claimant's prior illegality
A credit hire claim by an uninsured claimant is NOT automatically barred — proportionality under Patel v Mirza and the targeted causation approach from Ali v HSF govern the analysis
cases_referenced: Ali v HSF Logistics Polska Sp. Z O.O [2024] EWCA Civ 1479 (Court of Appeal)
Patel v Mirza [2016] UKSC 42 (Supreme Court)
Hewison v Meridian Shipping Services Pte Ltd [2002] EWCA Civ 1821 (Court of Appeal)
Edgar v Rayson (Altrincham County Court, Judge Tetlow, 23 October 2000, unreported)
Tinsley v Milligan [1994] 1 AC 340 (House of Lords)
articles.source: Credit Hire AI Knowledge Base — synthesised from Court of Appeal and Supreme Court authorities on illegality and insurance in credit hire, April 2026
articles.author: Credit Hire AI
articles.author_role: Knowledge base — synthesised from appellate authorities

articles.title: Smith v Tucker — Need Defeated for Leisure Motorcycle: Preference to Ride Is Not Need to Hire (County Court, DJ Pain; Appeal Dismissed, HHJ Brownhill)
articles.summary: County Court decision (DJ Pain; appeal dismissed by HHJ Brownhill) in which the claimant's credit hire claim for a replacement motorcycle (£23,567.94 for 157 days from 4th Dimension) was dismissed for failure to establish need. The claimant had a car and a van available. His main use of the motorcycle was attending biking events as a hobby. The judge held that preference to ride is not the same as need to hire. Loss of use of £4,000 was awarded instead. The defendant's Part 36 offer of £5,000 was not beaten. This case is sometimes cited by defendants to justify low daily hire rate offers (e.g. £25/day) but it does NOT establish any daily rate — it is a need case, not a rate case.
articles.content:
Smith v Tucker (Mr Steve Mark Smith v Mrs Gillian Tucker) is a County Court decision reported by DWF Group (defendant solicitors, ref 2019276-440, published 19 February 2024). The matter exited the MOJ portal to explore hire charges.

FACTS

The claimant claimed £23,567.94 for 157 days' hire of a replacement motorbike from 4th Dimension. He pleaded loss of use as an alternative if the hire agreement was found void. The defendant challenged rate, enforceability, period and need. Pre-litigation, the claimant stated it was 'inconvenient' for him to use his other vehicles. Financial disclosure was incomplete and BHR evidence was obtained. The defendant made a Part 36 offer of £5,000 which was not accepted.

FIRST INSTANCE (DJ Pain)

The defendant was represented by Martin Ferguson of Farrar's Building. Enforceability was accepted but need, period and rate were disputed.

Under cross-examination the claimant confirmed he had a car AND a van (having only referred to a van in his witness evidence). The van was accepted as not suitable for day-to-day use. The claimant said the car was 'off the road' at the time of the accident. His commute was 26 miles round trip but he averaged only 8 miles per day on the bike. He admitted the main use of the bike was attending biking events on a hobby basis. He also admitted he repaired the car himself in a day or so.

The trial judge found need was not satisfied, commenting: 'The question of whether he has satisfied need relates to whether the hiring of a bike was a reasonable step in mitigating his loss. I do not consider that he has discharged the burden. He could have fixed his car quickly and before the need to use the van arose. The bike events do not establish a need to hire a bike. He prefers to ride, but that preference cannot be enough to establish a need to hire.'
