payment, it clearly did not operate as a release of debts where there
was a right of recovery from third parties. This would defeat the very
purpose of the IVA, which was to maximise the recovery from such third
parties, including by pursuing legal claims.

The correct analysis is therefore that while the IVA may have operated
as an agreement between the creditors *inter se* to settle C's debts at
a discount (if necessary) once all of the assets subject to the IVA
(including the proceeds of claims) had been fully realised, it did not
constitute any sort of release of the claims themselves (and cf *Green v
Wright*, as discussed at §§41-42 above). Essentially, the body of
creditors (deemed by s 260(2) of the Act to include DAML itself) agreed
to share the proceeds of such claims so that each of its members would
receive a maximised and equitably shared return.

C submits that this is an additional reason for treating the IVA as *res
inter alios acta*. It is not an arrangement entered into with the
intention of benefitting third parties, least of all C's debtors (of
which D was one), but of benefitting C's creditors. For a wrongdoer to
appropriate the benefit of the IVA would be actively detrimental to
creditors, and defeat the whole purpose of the IVA, which is to gather
in C's assets (including rights to damages) to the fullest possible
extent and apply them to the benefit of creditors.

Looked at in this way, and with respect, the outcome before the judge is
perverse. He has allowed D, a debtor, to rely on the IVA, an arrangement
intended exclusively to benefit creditors, to escape a liability to pay
damages to C under agreements 1 and 2, which would have massively
increased the pool of funds available to the creditors as a body (on the
judge's other findings, the damages payable in respect of agreements 1
and 2 will be £78,300.96). In doing so, he has given D a windfall which
is wholly unmerited.

In *Merrett v Capitol Indemnity* \[1991\] l Lloyd's Rep 169, Steyn J
considered the position where an insurance broker made a voluntary
payment to its client, and reinsurers attempted to rely on that payment
to reduce their own liability. He recalled the famous dictum of Lord
Reid that '*the common law ought never to produce a wholly unreasonable
result*', and concluded that the arrangement with the insurance broker
fell to be disregarded: it was intended solely for the benefit of the
insured, and therefore 'must be disregarded in assessing the assured's
recoverable loss.'

With appropriate modifications, the position should be the same here.
The IVA was entered for the benefit of creditors and maximising the
recovery thereby available to them. Benefits accruing thereunder are
*res inter alios acta* and should not be brought into account so as to
diminish D's (otherwise unanswerable) liability.

**Basis (c): Policy & Extending the Categories of *Res Inter Alios*
*Acta* Benefits**

Ben further argued that even if this conclusion does not flow from
established principles (as bases (a) and (b) contend), then the class of
collateral benefits currently recognised as 'conventionally' *res inter
alios acta* should now be extended to include reductions in liability
that would otherwise accrue to a wrongdoer from the happenstance of an
IVA.

As Lord Reid states in *Parry v Cleaver* \[1970\] AC 1, 13, whether a
wrongdoer can take the benefit of some external arrangement to reduce
its liability is not subject to a universal rule and has been treated by
the common law as '*depending on justice and public policy*.' It is
because of these considerations that, for example, the proceeds of
insurance, charity and disability pensions are not generally taken into
account when assessing damages (again, see *Parry's case*).

The partial release of debts under an IVA should similarly be
disregarded. Any other conclusion undermines the whole purpose of the
IVA regime, and has the perverse effect of benefitting debtors rather
than creditors. Indeed, it would operate as an incentive for creditors
to reject an IVA and bankrupt a debtor instead. The present case offers
a good example. If C had been bankrupted after entering the second
agreement with DAML, that would have had no impact on his claim against
D at all, save that it would have vested in his trustee in bankruptcy.
The trustee would have been able to pursue the claims arising out of the
two agreements in full, albeit the proceeds would have formed part of
the general fund available to creditors. On the judge's findings,
bankruptcy would therefore have realised £78,300 more for creditors than
the IVA. This cannot be right. An IVA is an alternative to bankruptcy,
and the position under the two complementary regimes should be
analogous: *Green's case* at §32. Hence, the 'justice and public policy'
imperatives stated in *Parry v Cleaver* here too strongly favour
discounts under an IVA being treated as *res inter alios acta*.

**[Agreements Potentially Enforceable]{.underline}**

The final ground for appeal only arose if C's case, as set out above, is
misplaced. However, even if the effect of the IVA were treated as
rendering the hire agreements unenforceable, or enforceable at a
discount, the judge should still have allowed the claims, because on any
view the hire agreements potentially remained fully enforceable, and no
injustice would thereby be done to D as (i) there was no prospect of
double recovery (i.e., C being entitled to keep the proceeds of the
claim for himself) or (ii) risk of over-payment by D (whose liability
was already limited by the doctrine of mitigation, such that the only
thing it might be deprived of was a windfall from the IVA which was
adventitious and unmerited).

The impact of the IVA cannot be compared to an irremediable statutory
unenforceability, as under the now repealed provisions of the Consumer
Credit Act 1974 considered in *Dimond v Lovell*.\
\
The IVA merely operated as a moratorium on enforcement (*Green's case*
at §21), which would lapse if the IVA were breached or terminated early,
or if D became bankrupt for other reasons. None of these events was
unlikely. Indeed, the very fact that C failed to declare his debts to
DAML when entering the IVA, and thereafter entered agreement 3, could
easily have led to the IVA failing. Equally, as addressed under ground 3
above, the logical corollary of the judge's findings on the effect of
the IVA is that C's creditors are better of bankrupting him, so as to
maximise the recovery of assets from D in the RTA claim.

In these circumstances, and absent any risk of resulting injustice to D,
the judge should have held that the law permitted an undiscounted
recovery of damages in respect of C's potential liabilities under the
first two agreements.

The court should not put an injured party at risk of unquantifiable
future uncertainties in order to relieve a wrongdoer of the consequences
of its breach: cf *Freeman v Lockett* \[2006\] EWHC 102, \[2006\] PIQR
P23, §35. Just as an injured party is not expected to expose himself to
third party litigation in order to reduce the wrongdoer's liability (the
well-known principle in *Pilkington v Wood* \[1953\] Ch 770), C ought
not to be exposed to potential enforcement action by DAML should the
moratorium resulting from the IVA later fail.

As such, the fact that, at its highest, the IVA merely worked as a
contingent and therefore highly uncertain bar on DAML enforcing C's full
liability under the first two hire agreements ought to have led to its
existence being rejected as any sort of defence to C's claim.

**[Serious Procedural Irregularity: Failure to Address *Res Inter Alios Acta*]{.underline}**
============================================================================================

The judge at first instance failed to address, or even mention, the *res
alios inter acta* issue. It was, however, explicitly raised in the
Reply; C's skeleton for trial; and in closing submissions. Thereafter, C
drew the judge's attention to this on a further three occasions: upon
receipt of the draft judgment; in his written PTA application; and upon
receipt of the draft order. All these attempts proved futile.

While the judge obviously did not need to deal with every possible point
arising during the trial, he had an obligation to address the key points
raised by the parties, to determine them, and to give reasons for his
conclusion. As to the last requirement, the principles stated in
*English v Emery Reimbold & Strick* \[2002\] EWCA Civ 605, \[2002\] 1
WLR 2409 are very well-known. As that case shows, a failure to give
reasons is of itself a ground for appeal, and here the failure was
total.

[^1]: Contingent because it was subject (i) to the IVA being fully
    performed without modification or revocation, and on the amount of
    C's assets that were ultimately gathered in.

[^2]: The analysis is not impacted in any relevant way by the subsequent
    decision of the Court of Appeal in the case, \[2020\] EWCA Civ 1151,
    \[2021\] 3 All ER 571.

articles.title: Williams v Liverpool Victoria Insurance (CC Cardiff, HHJ Jarman KC, 24 Feb 2023) - IVA Challenge to Credit Hire Recovery
articles.source: County Court at Cardiff - HHJ Jarman KC; PTA granted by Nicklin J; appeal settled before hearing

Lagden v O'Connor [2004] 1 AC 1067
Dimond v Lovell [2000] UKHL 27

Thai Airways v K I Holdings [2015] EWHC 1250

Pilkington v Wood [1953] Ch 770
English v Emery Reimbold & Strick [2002] EWCA Civ 605
key_points: FACTS. Cardiff RTA. Claimant hired from DAML across three sequential credit hire agreements (24 March, 16 June and 2 September 2020), remaining in hire for 215 days. On 10 August 2020, during the second agreement, the claimant entered an IVA. DAML was not notified.

FIRST INSTANCE (HHJ Jarman KC). C held impecunious and entitled to credit rate. Reasonable period to early October 2020. First two (pre-IVA) agreements dismissed as 'unenforceable' because of the IVA (analogising Dimond v Lovell). Only the post-IVA agreement allowed (GBP 13,624.56 for 28 days).
PTA GRANTED ON IVA POINT. Impecuniosity PTA refused by Stacey J and Nicklin J; IVA point PTA granted by Nicklin J. Appeal settled. Not a binding determination.
ERROR AT FIRST INSTANCE. Misinterprets s 260(2)(b) of the Insolvency Act 1986 and Green v Wright [2017] EWCA Civ 111: the IVA is a moratorium on enforcement, not an extinction of debt.

APPEAL ARGUMENT (BWKC). Three bases: (a) collateral arrangement / res inter alios acta; (b) D is not a beneficiary of the IVA; (c) policy / Parry v Cleaver. See the Steve Evans counsel_article for full analysis.
DIMOND v LOVELL DISTINGUISHED. Dimond unenforceability arose out of the mitigating step itself. An IVA entered months after the hire agreements is a subsequent, collateral composition.

STATUS. Not binding. A first-instance County Court judgment overtaken by granted PTA and out-of-court settlement. Value: the focal case around which the IVA defence is currently fought at first instance.
articles.full_text: WILLIAMS v LIVERPOOL VICTORIA INSURANCE (County Court at Cardiff, HHJ Jarman KC, 24 February 2023). Full analysis including the skeleton arguments prepared by Mr Benjamin Williams KC for the abortive appeal is available in the accompanying counsel_article 'Impecuniosity and Individual Voluntary Arrangements (IVAs)' by Steve Evans of The Credit Hire Forum. The case is the focal first-instance authority in the emerging IVA defence to credit hire recovery and is routinely deployed by defendant insurers, notably Keoghs LLP. Its first-instance reasoning (analogising the IVA with Dimond v Lovell unenforceability) is widely regarded as wrong in law; the defence is nevertheless being run in reliance on it.

articles.title: BHR Comparator Validity Where the Hire Period Exceeds the Supplier's Maximum Rental Period (e.g. Enterprise 88-day cap)
articles.author: Steve Evans
articles.summary: When a defendant offers an Enterprise (or other mainstream) BHR rate as the comparator for a long hire, and the supplier's published terms cap the rental period at fewer days than the actual hire (Enterprise: 88 days), the defendant has not discharged the evidential burden under Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management [2015] EWCA Civ 93. The comparator must be a vehicle the claimant could ACTUALLY have hired for the period of need. A supplier whose own terms refuse the booking is not such a comparator. The rebuttal therefore proceeds in three steps: (1) produce the supplier's terms and identify the rental period cap, (2) treat the defendant's quote as evidentially incomplete beyond the cap, (3) require the defendant to point to a comparator that genuinely was available for the full period or to bring additional evidence (multiple sequential bookings with admin fees and deposits, or a higher-rate long-hire-friendly supplier such as AMT or Drivalia).
key_points: PRINCIPLE. BHR is what the claimant would have paid in the open market BUT FOR the credit hire. The comparator must be a vehicle the claimant could actually have hired for the period required to repair the tort. See Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management [2015] EWCA Civ 93 at paragraphs 38-39.

ENTERPRISE 88-DAY CAP. Enterprise Rent-A-Car UK Limited's published rental conditions cap the rental period at 88 days (rental_period.maximum_days = 88, recorded in bhr_terms_db.json on credit-hire.ai). On Enterprise's own terms it would not have continued to hire to the claimant beyond day 88. A defendant relying on Enterprise rates for a hire of more than 88 days has therefore offered evidence of a comparator that was not, on its own terms, available for the period needed.

THE EVIDENTIAL BURDEN IS ON THE DEFENDANT. McBride v UK Insurance and Stevens are clear: the defendant who alleges that the claimant could have hired more cheaply must put forward credible evidence of a vehicle the claimant could actually have hired in the relevant local market for the actual period. A supplier whose terms refuse the booking is not that.

PRACTICAL ARGUMENT (1) - Produce the Enterprise terms. The most powerful single document is Enterprise's own rental conditions extract showing the 88-day cap. Put it in cross-examination of the defendant's BHR expert. Ask the expert to confirm that on those terms the booking could not have run to (e.g.) day 117. The comparator falls there.

PRACTICAL ARGUMENT (2) - Treat the BHR quote as covering only the days within the cap. At highest, the defendant's Enterprise quote discharges the burden for days 1-88. For days 89-117 the defendant must produce a different comparator. A claimant cannot be assumed to have hired from a provider that would not have hired to him.

PRACTICAL ARGUMENT (3) - Sequential bookings carry costs the defendant has omitted. If the defendant says the claimant should have re-booked at day 89 (for example, two separate sequential 60-day Enterprise bookings would in theory cover 117 days), each booking carries its own fixed costs - admin/booking fee, security deposit, excess insurance, off-hire/on-hire gap. The Enterprise daily rate x 117 calculation is a misleading proxy for what the claimant would actually have paid.

PRACTICAL ARGUMENT (4) - The honest comparator for a long hire. Long-hire-friendly suppliers (AMT Vehicle Rental Ltd, Drivalia UK Ltd, certain Hertz UK long-stay arrangements) typically offer monthly or quarterly rolling contracts at higher daily rates than Enterprise's short-stay rates. The defendant should be required to address the long-hire market specifically. If the defendant cannot do so, the claimant's credit hire rate stands.

DOCTRINE - the comparator must be like-for-like. Pattni and Bent v Highways and Utilities Construction [2012] RTR 17 at paragraph 32 emphasises that the comparator must be equivalent to the damaged vehicle. By extension the comparator must be equivalent in the dimension of period: a vehicle that can be hired for 88 days is not equivalent to a hire of 117 days.

MITIGATION DOES NOT REQUIRE THE IMPOSSIBLE. The claimant cannot be required to mitigate by booking a hire from a supplier whose terms refuse the period needed. Mitigation requires only reasonable steps. Bourgrine v Natoora Limited (HHJ Wood KC, 23 February 2026) confirms the compartmentalised approach: cost is not relevant to need or period; period must be addressed on its own facts.

PRACTICAL CHECKLIST. Where the defendant relies on an Enterprise BHR for a long hire: (a) produce the Enterprise rental conditions excerpt with the maximum_days clause highlighted, (b) cross-reference with bhr_terms_db.json on credit-hire.ai to confirm the current cap, (c) put it to the defendant's expert in cross-examination, (d) require the defendant to identify a long-hire comparator at trial, (e) if no honest long-hire comparator is offered, the claim should be allowed at the credit hire rate (subject to impecuniosity/Lagden).

OTHER PROVIDERS. As at the date of this article, Enterprise is the only major short-stay provider in bhr_terms_db.json with an express rental period cap recorded. Other mainstream providers (Avis, Hertz, Europcar, Sixt, Budget, National, Thrifty) handle long hires through different mechanisms (monthly rolling, long-stay product, or no express cap). The claimant should still test the comparator against the actual hire period and ask: would this provider, on their actual terms and on the actual booking the claimant would have made, have hired this vehicle for the full period at the rate quoted? If not, the comparator is incomplete.

AVOID THE WRONG LENS. The wrong way to read this question is to treat it as a mitigation question about whether the claimant should have terminated his own hire at day 88. He didn't hire from Enterprise; he hired from a credit hire company. The supplier's 88-day cap is relevant only because the DEFENDANT has tendered Enterprise as the comparator. The right way to read the question is: is the defendant's BHR comparator valid?
cases_cited: Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management [2015] EWCA Civ 93
Pattni and Bent v Highways and Utilities Construction [2012] RTR 17
McBride v UK Insurance Ltd
Lagden v O'Connor [2004] 1 AC 1067
Bourgrine v Natoora Limited (HHJ Wood KC, 23 February 2026)
articles.full_text:
BHR COMPARATOR VALIDITY WHERE THE HIRE PERIOD EXCEEDS THE SUPPLIER'S MAXIMUM RENTAL PERIOD
Steve Evans, The Credit Hire Forum

THE QUESTION. The defendant produces a BHR quote from Enterprise Rent-A-Car. The claimant's hire was 117 days. Enterprise's rental conditions cap the rental period at 88 days. What argument do you run?

THE WRONG LENS. The temptation is to treat this as a mitigation question - 'should the claimant have stopped at day 88?' That misses the point. The claimant did not hire from Enterprise. He hired from a credit hire company. The supplier's 88-day cap is in play only because the defendant has tendered Enterprise as the BHR comparator.

THE RIGHT LENS - COMPARATOR VALIDITY. The right question is whether Enterprise is a valid comparator at all. Per Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management [2015] EWCA Civ 93 at paragraphs 38-39, the BHR is what the claimant would have paid in the open market BUT FOR the credit hire. To establish a BHR, the defendant must put forward evidence of a vehicle the claimant could ACTUALLY have hired for the period of need. A supplier whose own published terms refuse the booking is not such a vehicle.

THE ENTERPRISE 88-DAY CAP. Enterprise Rent-A-Car UK Limited's published rental conditions cap the rental period at 88 days. The cap is recorded in the bhr_terms_db.json on credit-hire.ai under companies.enterprise.versions[-1].rental_period.maximum_days. On those terms, Enterprise would not have continued to hire to the claimant beyond day 88.

WHAT THIS DOES TO THE DEFENDANT'S EVIDENCE. At highest, the defendant's Enterprise quote discharges the evidential burden for days 1-88. For days 89-117 the defendant has produced no comparator at all. The claimant cannot be expected to have hired from a supplier that would have refused the booking. The defendant must, if he wants to rely on a BHR for the whole period, identify a comparator that was actually available for the whole period - or accept that for days 89-117 there is no BHR challenge and the credit hire rate stands (subject to impecuniosity / Lagden v O'Connor [2004] 1 AC 1067).

SEQUENTIAL BOOKINGS DO NOT SAVE THE COMPARATOR. The defendant may argue that the claimant could have made two sequential Enterprise bookings (e.g. 60 + 57 days). That argument fails on three grounds. First, sequential bookings carry repeating fixed costs (admin/booking fee, security deposit, excess insurance per booking) which the defendant's daily-rate-multiplied-by-117 calculation does not include. Second, sequential bookings imply an off-hire/on-hire gap which leaves the claimant vehicle-less for at least a day, which the claimant cannot be required to accept where the tort has given rise to genuine need. Third, the daily-rate-multiplied-by-117 calculation is a misleading proxy for the true cost of the booking pattern the defendant is actually proposing.

THE HONEST LONG-HIRE COMPARATOR. Long-hire-friendly suppliers (AMT Vehicle Rental Ltd, Drivalia UK Ltd, certain Hertz long-stay arrangements) typically offer monthly or quarterly rolling contracts at higher daily rates than Enterprise's short-stay rates. If the defendant wants a BHR for a 117-day hire, he should put forward a quote from a supplier of that type. Failing that, the credit hire rate is the only rate in evidence and should stand.

THE COMPARATOR MUST BE LIKE-FOR-LIKE. Pattni and Bent v Highways and Utilities Construction [2012] RTR 17 at paragraph 32 stresses that the comparator must be equivalent to the damaged vehicle. The same analysis applies in the time dimension: a vehicle that can be hired for 88 days is not the equivalent of a hire of 117 days.

MITIGATION DOES NOT REQUIRE THE IMPOSSIBLE. McBride v UK Insurance, Stevens, and the line of authority running through them all proceed on the basis that the claimant must take REASONABLE steps. Booking a supplier that would have refused the booking is not such a step. The compartmentalised approach in Bourgrine v Natoora Limited (HHJ Wood KC, 23 February 2026) at paragraph 42 reinforces this: cost considerations belong to rate, not to need or period.

PRACTICAL STEPS AT TRIAL.
1. Produce the Enterprise rental conditions excerpt with the 88-day rental period clause highlighted.
2. Cross-reference with bhr_terms_db.json on credit-hire.ai (companies.enterprise.versions[-1].rental_period.maximum_days = 88).
3. Cross-examine the defendant's BHR expert: 'On Enterprise's published terms, would Enterprise have continued to hire this vehicle to the claimant beyond day 88?' If the answer is 'no', the comparator falls.
4. Require the defendant to identify a long-hire comparator. If none is produced, allow the credit hire rate (subject to any other heads of challenge such as need, impecuniosity, type or rate).
5. Distinguish this argument from any argument that the claimant should have terminated his hire at day 88. The claimant's hire was with the credit hire company, not Enterprise. The defendant cannot import a third party's contractual cap as a mitigation duty against the claimant.

OTHER PROVIDERS. At the date of this article, Enterprise is the only major short-stay UK provider in the bhr_terms_db.json on credit-hire.ai with an express rental period cap recorded. Other mainstream providers (Avis, Hertz, Europcar, Sixt, Budget, National, Thrifty) handle long hires through different mechanisms - monthly rolling contracts, long-stay products, or no express cap. The same comparator validity test applies: the claimant should test each comparator against the actual hire period. Would this provider, on their actual terms and on the actual booking the claimant would have made, have hired this vehicle for the full period at the rate quoted? If not, the comparator is incomplete and must be supplemented or replaced.

STATUS. Steve Evans of The Credit Hire Forum. Argument framework based on the cited authorities. The specific Enterprise figure (88 days) is taken from the rental conditions recorded in bhr_terms_db.json on credit-hire.ai and is correct as at the date of this article. Practitioners should verify the cap against the version of Enterprise's terms that was in force at the date of the relevant accident.
articles.source: The Credit Hire Forum

articles.title: Motorcycle, Motorbike, Scooter and E-Bike BHR: Why the Stevens Hierarchy Collapses and Insurance Differences Defeat the Comparator
articles.author: Steve Evans
articles.summary: When a motorcycle, motorbike, scooter, moped or e-bike is hired on credit hire terms and the defendant tenders a BHR comparator from a local motorcycle hire provider, the analysis is materially different from a car BHR analysis. Two structural points almost always defeat the comparator. First, the Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management [2015] EWCA Civ 93 mainstream-first hierarchy collapses: there are no mainstream national UK motorcycle hire providers — Avis, Hertz, Enterprise, Europcar, Sixt and Budget do not hire motorcycles, so Tier 1 is unavailable in every motorcycle BHR analysis. Every motorcycle BHR comparator is therefore at Tier 2, where the 'respected' requirement is the dominant test. Second, and more importantly, motorcycle hire providers almost universally offer only third-party insurance, with the hirer remaining liable for theft and for own-fault damage to the bike. Credit hire motorcycle agreements by contrast provide comprehensive cover with zero excess. The defendant's BHR comparator is not like-for-like on insurance cover and fails the equivalence test in Pattni and Bent v Highways and Utilities Construction [2012] RTR 17 — the BHR rate quoted does not represent what the claimant would have paid in the open market for a vehicle on equivalent terms.
key_points: STEVENS HIERARCHY COLLAPSES FOR MOTORCYCLES. Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management [2015] EWCA Civ 93 establishes a two-tier hierarchy: Tier 1 mainstream national supplier (Avis, Hertz, Enterprise, Europcar, Sixt, Budget); Tier 2 respected local supplier as fallback. None of the mainstream nationals hire motorcycles in the UK. Tier 1 is therefore unavailable in every motorcycle BHR analysis and the defendant must rely on Tier 2 only.

TIER 2 'RESPECTED' IS THE OPERATIVE TEST. With Tier 1 off the table, the entire weight of the inquiry falls on whether the supplier in the defendant's BHR evidence is genuinely a 'respected local supplier'. The indicia are Trustpilot ratings, Google reviews, Companies House trading history and an established physical presence. Examples in the credit-hire.ai BHR supplier database include Raceways Motorcycles, Superbike Rental Ltd, Ugo Scooters, Omega Bikes and Rent a Scoot. A supplier with a thin trading record, mediocre ratings or no physical presence does not pass the 'respected' threshold and the comparator can be discounted on that ground.

INSURANCE COVER IS NOT LIKE-FOR-LIKE — THE HEADLINE POINT. (a) Mainstream and respected local CAR hire providers offer COMPREHENSIVE insurance, normally with an excess that the hirer can waive by paying CDW. (b) Motorcycle hire companies almost universally offer only THIRD-PARTY insurance: the hirer remains personally liable for THEFT of the bike and for any DAMAGE to the bike where the hirer is at fault. Comprehensive cover is unusual and where available is materially more expensive. (c) Credit hire motorcycle agreements ALMOST ALWAYS include COMPREHENSIVE cover with ZERO excess. The claimant under credit hire therefore has the benefit of cover that no respected local motorcycle BHR provider offers on equivalent terms.

THE LIKE-FOR-LIKE TEST. Pattni and Bent v Highways and Utilities Construction [2012] RTR 17 at paragraph 32 stresses that the BHR comparator must be EQUIVALENT to the damaged vehicle. Equivalence is not limited to vehicle make and model. It extends to the terms on which the vehicle would be made available — including, materially, the insurance basis. A motorcycle hired under a comprehensive zero-excess credit hire agreement is not equivalent to a motorcycle hired under a third-party-only respected local BHR. The defendant's BHR rate is not what the claimant would have paid 'but for' the credit hire (Stevens at [38]–[39]) — at most it represents what the claimant would have paid for a materially inferior insurance basis with a materially different risk profile.

PRACTICAL CONSEQUENCE. Where the defendant tenders a motorcycle BHR rate, the rebuttal proceeds in three steps. (1) Confirm the bike type and ascertain that no mainstream national was available to the claimant — usually self-evident because no mainstream national hires motorcycles. (2) Test whether the local supplier in the defendant's evidence is genuinely 'respected' (Trustpilot, Google reviews, Companies House). If not, the comparator falls at the Tier 2 hurdle. (3) Even if respected, compare the insurance basis: third-party only, hirer liable for theft and own-fault damage. The credit hire agreement provides comprehensive zero-excess cover. The comparator fails Pattni and Bent equivalence and is not what the claimant would have paid in the open market for an equivalent vehicle.

EXPERT EVIDENCE. The defendant's BHR expert should be cross-examined on (i) what insurance the quoted local provider supplies as standard, (ii) whether the hirer is liable for theft, (iii) whether the hirer is liable for own-fault damage to the bike, (iv) whether comprehensive cover is offered as an add-on and at what cost, and (v) whether the quoted rate includes that add-on. In most cases the answers will demonstrate that the quoted rate does not provide cover equivalent to the credit hire agreement.

ADDITIONAL UPLIFTS NORMALLY OMITTED. Local motorcycle hire providers commonly require security deposits, charge per-mile excess fees, and impose age, licence and experience restrictions (e.g. minimum age 25, full UK Cat A licence held for 2+ years) that may exclude many delivery-rider claimants. Even where the comparator is otherwise valid, the defendant's BHR rate must be tested against these omitted real-world frictions before it can be said to represent 'what the claimant would have paid'.

DEFAULT POSITION. In a typical motorcycle credit hire dispute, where (a) the claimant is a delivery rider on a Honda or scooter, (b) the defendant tenders a local supplier rate, and (c) that supplier offers third-party insurance only, the defendant's BHR challenge is unlikely to succeed in full. The court should be invited to allow the credit hire rate (subject to need, period and impecuniosity / Lagden v O'Connor [2004] 1 AC 1067).
cases_cited: Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management [2015] EWCA Civ 93
Pattni and Bent v Highways and Utilities Construction [2012] RTR 17
Lagden v O'Connor [2004] 1 AC 1067
articles.full_text:
MOTORCYCLE, MOTORBIKE, SCOOTER AND E-BIKE BHR — WHY THE STEVENS HIERARCHY COLLAPSES AND INSURANCE DIFFERENCES DEFEAT THE COMPARATOR
Steve Evans, The Credit Hire Forum

INTRODUCTION. Motorcycle credit hire claims now make up a substantial part of the daily diet of credit hire litigation, particularly in the food-delivery sector where the typical claimant rides a Honda or scooter. When the defendant tenders BHR evidence in a motorcycle case, the analysis differs in two material respects from the analysis in a car case. Both points should be raised in every motorcycle BHR challenge.

1. THE STEVENS HIERARCHY COLLAPSES FOR MOTORCYCLES. In Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management [2015] EWCA Civ 93 the Court of Appeal established a two-tier hierarchy for BHR comparators. Tier 1 is a recognised mainstream national supplier — Avis, Hertz, Enterprise, Europcar, Sixt or Budget. Tier 2, available only when Tier 1 is not, is a 'respected local supplier'. None of the mainstream nationals hire motorcycles in the UK. The Tier 1 inquiry is therefore exhausted at the first step in every motorcycle BHR analysis: there is no mainstream national comparator. The defendant must rely on Tier 2 alone. The 'respected' requirement carries the entire weight of the inquiry.

Indicia of 'respected' include Trustpilot ratings, Google reviews, Companies House trading history and an established physical presence. Examples of respected local motorcycle hire providers known to the industry include Raceways Motorcycles, Superbike Rental Ltd, Ugo Scooters, Omega Bikes and Rent a Scoot — supplier profiles for these are held in the credit-hire.ai BHR supplier database. A defendant who tenders a quote from a thinly-traded operator with poor ratings has not produced evidence of a respected local supplier and the comparator falls at the Tier 2 hurdle.

2. INSURANCE COVER IS NOT LIKE-FOR-LIKE. The deeper problem with motorcycle BHR comparators is that the insurance basis is fundamentally different from credit hire. Three observations:

(a) CAR HIRE. Mainstream and respected local car hire providers offer comprehensive insurance with an excess. The hirer can waive the excess by paying an additional CDW or excess-waiver fee. The hirer is not liable for theft of the vehicle (subject to compliance with the agreement) and not liable for damage caused by the hirer's own fault beyond the excess.

(b) MOTORCYCLE HIRE. Motorcycle hire companies almost universally offer only third-party insurance. The hirer is liable for theft of the bike. The hirer is liable for damage to the bike where the hirer is at fault. Comprehensive cover is unusual; where offered, it is at materially higher cost. The hirer's exposure under a typical motorcycle BHR is therefore the full replacement value of the bike (commonly several thousand pounds for a delivery scooter, materially more for a larger motorcycle).

(c) CREDIT HIRE. Credit hire motorcycle agreements almost always provide comprehensive cover with zero excess. The claimant has no exposure for theft and no exposure for own-fault damage.

3. THE LIKE-FOR-LIKE TEST. Pattni and Bent v Highways and Utilities Construction [2012] RTR 17 at paragraph 32 stresses that the BHR comparator must be EQUIVALENT to the damaged vehicle. Equivalence is not limited to make and model. It extends to the terms on which the vehicle would be made available, including the insurance basis. A motorcycle hired under a comprehensive zero-excess credit hire agreement is not equivalent to a motorcycle hired under a third-party-only respected local BHR. The defendant's BHR rate is not what the claimant would have paid 'but for' the credit hire (Stevens at paragraphs 38-39): at most it represents what the claimant would have paid for a materially inferior insurance basis with a materially higher residual risk profile.

4. THE PRACTICAL REBUTTAL. In a motorcycle BHR challenge, advance the following points in this order. First, confirm there is no Tier 1 mainstream national that hires motorcycles — the defendant cannot point to one because none exists. Second, test whether the supplier in the defendant's evidence is genuinely 'respected' on Stevens criteria. Third, even if the supplier is respected, examine the insurance terms in their published rental conditions: are they third-party only? Is the hirer liable for theft? For own-fault damage? Is comprehensive cover offered, and at what cost? In most cases the comparator will be third-party only and the BHR rate will not include any cover equivalent to the credit hire comprehensive zero-excess package.

5. EXPERT CROSS-EXAMINATION. The defendant's BHR expert should be cross-examined on (i) the insurance the supplier provides as standard; (ii) the hirer's exposure for theft of the bike; (iii) the hirer's exposure for own-fault damage; (iv) whether comprehensive cover is available at all from the supplier; (v) the additional cost of any comprehensive cover; and (vi) whether the daily rate quoted in the BHR evidence includes any of that additional cost. The answers normally demonstrate that the quoted rate does not provide cover equivalent to the credit hire agreement.

6. ADDITIONAL FRICTIONS. Local motorcycle hire providers also tend to impose practical restrictions that a typical delivery rider could not satisfy: minimum age 25 (some 28 or 30), full UK Category A licence held for 2 or 3 years, security deposit (commonly GBP 500-1,500), and per-mile excess fees on commercial use. These features mean that even where the comparator is theoretically respected, the defendant's BHR rate is often not a rate the claimant could actually have obtained.

CONCLUSION. In a typical motorcycle credit hire dispute the defendant's BHR challenge is unlikely to succeed in full. Tier 1 is unavailable. Tier 2 is available only with a respected local supplier. Even a respected local supplier is not a like-for-like comparator on insurance cover. The credit hire rate should ordinarily stand, subject to the usual challenges on need, period of hire, type of vehicle and (where pleaded) impecuniosity per Lagden v O'Connor [2004] 1 AC 1067. The argument framework set out above should be deployed in pleadings and at trial in every motorcycle BHR challenge.
articles.source: The Credit Hire Forum

articles.title: BHR Comparators: Who Is and Is Not a Mainstream National Supplier under Stevens, and the Deceitful-Avoidance Challenge
articles.author: Steve Evans
articles.summary: A practical guide to which UK car hire suppliers actually qualify as 'mainstream national' under the Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management [2015] EWCA Civ 93 hierarchy, which do not, and how to challenge a defendant's BHR evidence that skips Tier 1 in favour of a non-mainstream operator. The mainstream nationals are FOUR companies only — Avis, Hertz, Enterprise and Europcar. Sixt is predominantly airport-based and does not have nationwide high-street coverage; Budget is part of the Avis Group but operates from materially fewer locations; Thrifty, Arnold Clark, AMT, PCH, Evision and Drivalia are regional or niche operators. Where a defendant's rate witness cites a non-mainstream supplier and one or more of the four mainstream nationals were available in the claimant's locality at the relevant date offering an equivalent vehicle, the rate witness has skipped Tier 1 — this is typically a deceitful attempt to advance a rate lower than the rate the claimant would actually have paid. Challenge it directly.
key_points: THE FOUR MAINSTREAM NATIONALS. For the purposes of the Stevens hierarchy the only true Tier 1 mainstream nationals in the UK are: AVIS, HERTZ, ENTERPRISE and EUROPCAR. Each operates a network of high-street and airport branches with broad national coverage and a wide range of vehicle groups. These are the suppliers a claimant could realistically have approached for an equivalent rental in any normal locality, and they are the suppliers the defendant must address first if the BHR evidence is to discharge its evidential burden.

WHY NOT SIXT. Sixt is a recognised brand with high-quality vehicles, but its UK footprint is predominantly airport-based with a limited handful of major-city locations. It is not a true high-street national in the way Avis, Hertz, Enterprise and Europcar are. A claimant in a non-airport locality typically has no Sixt branch to walk into. Sixt is therefore Tier 2 (respected local) at best for most BHR analyses.

WHY NOT BUDGET. Budget is part of the Avis Group but operates a materially smaller branch network than Avis itself. Where a defendant cites Budget rates while Avis was also locally available, ask why. The two operators are not interchangeable for Stevens Tier 1 purposes.

WHY NOT THRIFTY, ARNOLD CLARK, AMT. Thrifty has limited geographical coverage in the UK. Arnold Clark is heavily concentrated in Scotland and the north of England with a thin network elsewhere. AMT operates from only a few regional locations. None of them is a mainstream national. They may sometimes qualify as Tier 2 respected local suppliers if they are genuinely local to the claimant and have the indicia of respect — but never Tier 1.

WHY NOT PCH, EVISION, DRIVALIA. PCH (Performance Car Hire) is a niche operator in higher-value or specialist vehicles. Evision deals only with electric vehicles. Drivalia is a regional operator. None has the broad national coverage required for Tier 1.

ORDER OF EVIDENCE. The defendant's BHR evidence must address Tier 1 first. Only if no mainstream national was available in the claimant's locality at the relevant date — geographically absent or not offering the requisite vehicle group/category — does the analysis move to Tier 2. The defendant cannot pick a non-mainstream supplier in preference to a mainstream national merely because the non-mainstream rate is lower.

THE DECEITFUL AVOIDANCE CHALLENGE. Where a defendant's rate witness cites a non-mainstream supplier (Thrifty, Sixt, Budget, AMT, Arnold Clark, Drivalia, PCH, Evision or otherwise) and a mainstream national WAS available in the claimant's locality at the relevant date, the rate witness has skipped Tier 1. This is almost always a deliberate selection of a lower rate rather than a defensible application of the Stevens hierarchy. The challenge: invite the rate witness in cross-examination to confirm (i) which of Avis, Hertz, Enterprise and Europcar had branches in the claimant's locality at the relevant date, (ii) which of them offered the requisite vehicle group, (iii) what their daily rates were, and (iv) why those rates were not cited in the witness's report. The answers usually demonstrate that Tier 1 was available and avoided.

PRACTICAL CONSEQUENCE. If the defendant has skipped Tier 1, the BHR evidence is evidentially incomplete. At highest the rate witness can be invited to substitute the mainstream national rate for the non-mainstream rate — which is typically materially higher and usually approaches the credit hire rate. Where the rate witness refuses to provide the mainstream national rate, the defendant has not discharged the evidential burden in Stevens at paragraphs 38-39 and the claimant's credit hire rate should stand (subject to other heads of challenge).

INTERSECTION WITH THE COMPARATOR-VALIDITY POINT. Even where a mainstream national IS cited, the comparator must still pass the like-for-like test in Pattni and Bent v Highways and Utilities Construction [2012] RTR 17 — vehicle group, period of hire (see the separate article on Enterprise's 88-day cap), insurance basis, and any practical restrictions. The supplier classification analysis in this article is the FIRST step. Comparator validity tests are layered on top.

INTERSECTION WITH MOTORCYCLE BHR. None of the four mainstream nationals hires motorcycles in the UK. For motorcycle, motorbike, scooter, moped or e-bike hires the Tier 1 inquiry is exhausted at the first step and the analysis is automatically Tier 2 only. See the separate article on Motorcycle BHR for the additional insurance-equivalence point.
cases_cited: Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management [2015] EWCA Civ 93
Pattni and Bent v Highways and Utilities Construction [2012] RTR 17
articles.full_text:
BHR COMPARATORS — WHO IS AND IS NOT A MAINSTREAM NATIONAL SUPPLIER UNDER STEVENS, AND THE DECEITFUL-AVOIDANCE CHALLENGE
Steve Evans, The Credit Hire Forum

INTRODUCTION. Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management [2015] EWCA Civ 93 establishes a two-tier hierarchy for BHR comparators. Tier 1 is a mainstream national supplier; Tier 2, the fallback only where Tier 1 is unavailable, is a 'respected local supplier'. In practice, a great deal of BHR evidence ignores the ordering and goes straight to a non-mainstream supplier whose rates happen to be lower than the four real national operators. This article sets out who is and is not a mainstream national, why the distinction matters, and how the rebuttal is run.

THE FOUR MAINSTREAM NATIONALS. The mainstream nationals in the UK are AVIS, HERTZ, ENTERPRISE and EUROPCAR. They each operate networks of high-street and airport locations across the country and offer a broad range of vehicle groups. They are the suppliers a claimant could realistically approach in any normal locality. Defendants whose BHR evidence does not start with these four have not discharged the evidential burden under Stevens.

WHY NOT SIXT. Sixt is a recognised quality brand but its UK footprint is overwhelmingly airport-based, with only a handful of major-city locations. The claimant in a typical suburban or provincial locality will not have a Sixt branch to walk into. Sixt is therefore not a Tier 1 mainstream national. It may qualify as a Tier 2 respected local supplier where the claimant is locally served and Sixt is the only respected option, but never Tier 1.

WHY NOT BUDGET. Budget is part of the Avis Group but operates a materially smaller branch network than Avis itself. Where Budget is cited and Avis was also locally available, the rate witness has cherry-picked the cheaper Avis-Group brand without addressing the headline brand. Budget is not Tier 1.

WHY NOT THRIFTY, ARNOLD CLARK, AMT. Thrifty has limited UK coverage. Arnold Clark is concentrated in Scotland and the north of England with a thin network elsewhere. AMT operates from only a few regional locations and is more often a long-stay specialist than a high-street operator. None of them passes the Tier 1 nationality threshold. They may qualify as Tier 2 if they are genuinely local to the claimant and respected; never Tier 1.

WHY NOT PCH, EVISION, DRIVALIA. PCH (Performance Car Hire) is a niche specialist; Evision is electric-only and niche; Drivalia is regional. None has the breadth of network required for Tier 1 status.

THE ORDER OF EVIDENCE. The defendant's BHR evidence must address Tier 1 first. The question is, were any of Avis, Hertz, Enterprise or Europcar locally available to the claimant at the relevant date offering the requisite vehicle group? If yes, the BHR is set by reference to those four. Only if the answer is no — geographically absent or no equivalent vehicle — does Tier 2 come into play. The defendant cannot prefer a non-mainstream supplier merely because the non-mainstream rate is lower.

THE DECEITFUL-AVOIDANCE CHALLENGE. Where a defendant's rate witness cites a non-mainstream supplier and one or more mainstream nationals WAS locally available, this is typically a deliberate selection of a lower rate rather than a defensible application of Stevens. The rebuttal proceeds in five steps:

(1) Establish the locality of the claimant at the relevant date.
(2) Identify which of Avis, Hertz, Enterprise and Europcar had branches in or near that locality.
(3) Establish whether each of them offered an equivalent vehicle group.
(4) Cross-examine the rate witness on why those rates were not cited in the BHR evidence.
(5) Invite the witness to substitute the mainstream national rate; failing that, invite the court to find that the BHR evidence is evidentially incomplete and that the defendant has not discharged the Stevens evidential burden.

PRACTICAL CONSEQUENCE. The mainstream national rate is typically materially higher than the non-mainstream rate the rate witness has selected, and frequently approaches the credit hire rate. If the rate witness refuses to provide a mainstream national rate, the defendant has not put forward evidence of the rate the claimant would have paid in the open market: see Stevens at paragraphs 38-39 ('but for' the credit hire). The claimant's credit hire rate should stand, subject to other heads of challenge.

LAYERING WITH OTHER COMPARATOR TESTS. This supplier-classification analysis is the FIRST step in any BHR challenge. Even where a mainstream national IS cited, the comparator must still pass the like-for-like test in Pattni and Bent v Highways and Utilities Construction [2012] RTR 17 — vehicle group, ACRISS, period of hire (see Enterprise 88-day cap), insurance basis, and any practical restrictions. Where the case involves a motorcycle, motorbike, scooter, moped or e-bike, none of the four mainstream nationals hires those vehicles, so Tier 1 is unavailable and the analysis is automatically Tier 2 only (see the Motorcycle BHR article for the additional insurance-equivalence point).

CONCLUSION. The Stevens hierarchy is strict and the field of mainstream nationals is narrow — Avis, Hertz, Enterprise and Europcar only. Defendants who skip Tier 1 in favour of a cheaper non-mainstream supplier should be challenged directly. The rebuttal is straightforward and almost always succeeds where the mainstream rates were demonstrably available.
articles.source: The Credit Hire Forum

articles.title: Powell v Moody (1966) - Filtering Motorcyclist 80/20 Liability: How a Claimant-Side Practitioner Should Rebut Reliance on the Case
articles.source: The Credit Hire Forum (drawing on Lyons Davidson Solicitors commentary, June 2019)
tags: right turn out of side road
Smith v Chief Constable of Nottinghamshire
cases_referenced: Powell v Moody (1966) 110 SJ 215 (citation per Lyons Davidson commentary; not independently verified)
Farley v Buckley [2007] EWCA Civ 403
Pell v Moseley [2003]
Smith v Chief Constable of Nottinghamshire [2012] EWCA Civ 161
Davis v Schrogin [2006] EWCA Civ 974
Powell v Phillips [1972] 3 All ER 864
key_points: WHAT THE CASE DECIDED. The claimant motorcyclist filtered past two lines of stationary traffic on the offside of a busy London road. The defendant car driver edged forward out of a side road intending to turn right and was beckoned out by a milk tanker driver who had left a gap. The two collided. Both were found negligent. Liability apportioned 80% against the claimant motorcyclist and 20% against the defendant car driver. The judge described offside filtering past stationary vehicles as 'an operation fraught with hazard' requiring great care.

WHY DEFENDANTS LOVE IT. The 80/20 apportionment is the headline. Defendant insurers cite Powell v Moody for the broad proposition that a filtering motorcyclist hit by a vehicle emerging from a side road or making a right turn through a queue should bear at least 75-80 per cent of the liability. The Lyons Davidson commentary (June 2019) acknowledges that the case 'is still commonly used by insurers acting for defendants, even though the case is now over 50 years old'.

REBUTTAL 1 - AGE OF THE CASE. Powell v Moody is now over 60 years old (1966). Driving conditions, road layouts, traffic volumes and the legal and regulatory framework have all moved on. The case predates: (a) the modern Highway Code; (b) the codification of the Hierarchy of Road Users in the 2022 revision of the Highway Code (rules H1, H2 and H3); (c) the body of Court of Appeal authority on motorcycle apportionment that has built up since; and (d) the modern risk-assessment expectations placed on drivers of larger vehicles. A 1966 apportionment should not be applied as if it were dispositive of every modern filtering case.

REBUTTAL 2 - THE 2022 HIERARCHY OF ROAD USERS. The Highway Code revision in force from 29 January 2022 introduced rules H1 (those in charge of vehicles that can cause the greatest harm have the greatest responsibility to reduce the danger they pose to others), H2 (drivers must give priority to pedestrians waiting to cross at junctions) and H3 (drivers must not cut across cyclists, horse riders or horse drawn vehicles when turning). Although H3 is framed for cyclists, the underlying philosophy - greater duty on the driver of the larger and more dangerous vehicle - is now part of the regulatory landscape and is admissible evidence of the standard of care expected of drivers under section 38(7) Road Traffic Act 1988. Apportionments of the kind made in Powell v Moody are increasingly difficult to justify in light of that framework.

REBUTTAL 3 - FACT-SPECIFICITY. Powell v Moody turned on a particular factual matrix: a busy London road, two lines of stationary traffic, a milk tanker driver waving the defendant out, the defendant edging forward and unable to see the motorcycle. Modern filtering accidents rarely replicate that geometry. The most important sentence in the Lyons Davidson commentary is: 'These decisions underline the principle that each case must be assessed on its own particular facts.' That principle cuts BOTH ways. Defendants who rely on Powell v Moody as a global apportionment rule are arguing the opposite of what the case actually says.

REBUTTAL 4 - LATER AUTHORITY. The motorcycle apportionment field has moved on materially since 1966. Notably (a) Smith v Chief Constable of Nottinghamshire [2012] EWCA Civ 161 - 50/50 apportionment after careful review of relative fault. (b) Davis v Schrogin [2006] EWCA Civ 974 - claimant motorcyclist who collided while overtaking found NOT to have caused or contributed to the accident on the facts. (c) Powell v Phillips [1972] 3 All ER 864 - failure of a pedestrian/road user to wear conspicuous clothing not necessarily contributory negligence. The general direction of travel, particularly post-2022, is towards more nuanced apportionment that takes seriously the relative vulnerability of road users.

REBUTTAL 5 - WHAT THE CASE DOES NOT DECIDE. Powell v Moody does not decide that filtering is itself negligent. The Lyons Davidson commentary correctly notes that motorcyclists 'are entitled to carry out manoeuvres such as filtering'. The Highway Code expressly permits filtering at low speeds. The case decides only that, on those particular facts, the motorcyclist did not exercise the great care the circumstances required. The challenge is to demonstrate that the modern facts are different.

REBUTTAL 6 - THE EMERGING DRIVER'S CONTRIBUTORY ROLE. In Powell v Moody itself the defendant was found 20% liable for failing to anticipate the actions of other road users. A defendant driver who edges out from a side road through a gap in stationary traffic, especially on the prompting of a third-party driver in the queue, takes a substantial share of the risk. Modern decisions tend to scrutinise the emerging driver's conduct more rigorously. Where the defendant has been beckoned out, has limited visibility, or has not paused at the offside line of the queue to check for traffic, the apportionment against the motorcyclist should be materially less than 80%.

PRACTICAL CHECKLIST FOR THE CLAIMANT PRACTITIONER. (1) Plead the Highway Code Hierarchy of Road Users (rules H1, H2, H3) explicitly. (2) Obtain CCTV/dashcam evidence of the speed of the filtering motorcycle - low-speed filtering is materially more defensible than high-speed overtaking. (3) Examine the geometry of the side road and the gap in the queue: did the defendant have a sightline to oncoming traffic? Did they pause? Were they beckoned out? (4) Adduce expert evidence on stopping distances, perception-reaction times and the visibility of the motorcyclist (lights, hi-vis, vehicle colour). (5) Distinguish Powell v Moody on the facts: the case is fact-specific and the defendant cannot rely on it as a universal apportionment rule. (6) Cite later authorities (Smith v Chief Constable, Davis v Schrogin) and the 2022 Highway Code where they assist.

RELATED CASES IN THIS KNOWLEDGE BASE. Farley v Buckley [2007] EWCA Civ 403 (claimant motorcyclist overtaking refuse wagon, claim dismissed; not a credit hire case but commonly cited). Pell v Moseley [2003] (overtaking past stationary traffic at motocross event, 50/50). Both are referenced in the Lyons Davidson commentary. Counter-authorities include Smith v Chief Constable of Nottinghamshire [2012] EWCA Civ 161 and Davis v Schrogin [2006] EWCA Civ 974.

SOURCE NOTE AND CAUTION ON WEIGHT. The factual narrative in this entry is drawn from the Lyons Davidson commentary 'Liability and motorcycle accidents' (June 2019), which is a defendant-firm publication. The original Powell v Moody report (Solicitors Journal 1966) has not been independently consulted as part of this entry. Practitioners should obtain the original report or a primary citation before relying on the case at trial. The 80/20 apportionment is the headline; the precise reasoning of the Court of Appeal is not exhaustively reproduced in the secondary commentary.
articles.full_text:
POWELL v MOODY (1966) - FILTERING MOTORCYCLIST 80/20 LIABILITY - CLAIMANT-SIDE REBUTTAL
Steve Evans, The Credit Hire Forum (Apr 2026)

WHY THIS NOTE EXISTS. Powell v Moody is one of the oldest cases still routinely cited by defendant insurers and their solicitors in motorcycle filtering accidents. It pre-dates the modern Highway Code, the codification of vulnerable-road-user duties in the 2022 Hierarchy of Road Users (rules H1, H2 and H3) and decades of Court of Appeal apportionment authority that have moved the law on. Despite that, defendants continue to brandish the 80/20 outcome as if it were a global rule. This note sets out the case factually and gives claimant-side practitioners the rebuttal framework.

THE FACTS. The claimant was riding his motorcycle on a busy London road. He approached two lines of stationary traffic which were held up at an upcoming junction. He decided to filter past on the offside. The defendant driver had edged forward from a side road, intending to turn right across the queue, and was beckoned out by a milk tanker driver who had left a gap in the queue of traffic. The motorcycle and the car collided.

THE DECISION. Both parties were found negligent. The judge held that any road user who jumps a queue of stationary vehicles by filtering on the offside was 'undertaking an operation fraught with hazard' that had to be carried out with great care, because it was always difficult to see, 'from the offside of a queue of stationary vehicles, gaps in the queue on its nearside from which traffic might emerge'. Liability was apportioned 80% against the claimant motorcyclist and 20% against the defendant car driver.

WHAT THE CASE DOES NOT DECIDE. (a) Powell v Moody does not decide that filtering is per se negligent. The Highway Code expressly permits motorcyclists to filter at low speed. (b) It does not establish a global 80/20 rule for every motorcycle filtering accident: the apportionment was tied to the particular facts (busy London road, two lines of stationary traffic, milk tanker beckoning, defendant unable to see). (c) It does not relieve the emerging driver of the duty to take care: the defendant in Powell v Moody was himself found 20% liable.

THE LYONS DAVIDSON COMMENTARY. The June 2019 commentary by Lyons Davidson (a defendant firm) makes the helpful concession that 'these decisions underline the principle that each case must be assessed on its own particular facts' and that 'motorcyclists are entitled to carry out manoeuvres such as filtering'. Both concessions cut against any global application of the 80/20 outcome.

REBUTTAL FRAMEWORK FOR THE CLAIMANT PRACTITIONER.

(1) AGE OF THE CASE. Powell v Moody is now over 60 years old. Driving conditions, road layouts, traffic volumes and the legal and regulatory framework have all moved on. A 1966 apportionment is at best persuasive context, not a dispositive rule.

(2) THE 2022 HIERARCHY OF ROAD USERS. The Highway Code revision in force from 29 January 2022 introduced rules H1, H2 and H3. Rule H1 expressly provides that those in charge of vehicles that can cause the greatest harm have the greatest responsibility to reduce the danger they pose to others. Although H3 is drafted for cyclists, the underlying philosophy is now part of the regulatory landscape and is admissible under section 38(7) Road Traffic Act 1988 as evidence of the standard of care expected of drivers. Apportionments of the kind made in Powell v Moody are increasingly difficult to justify in modern conditions.

(3) FACT-SPECIFICITY. Modern filtering accidents rarely replicate the particular Powell v Moody geometry: two lines of stationary traffic, a milk tanker beckoning, a defendant unable to see the offside. Where the modern facts differ - traffic speeds, sightlines, the conduct of the emerging driver, the speed of the filtering motorcycle - the case is distinguishable and the apportionment should reflect the modern facts, not the 1966 outcome.

(4) LATER AUTHORITY. The motorcycle apportionment field has moved on. Smith v Chief Constable of Nottinghamshire [2012] EWCA Civ 161 (50/50). Davis v Schrogin [2006] EWCA Civ 974 (claimant motorcyclist NOT contributorily negligent on the facts). Powell v Phillips [1972] 3 All ER 864 (no automatic contributory negligence for failure to wear conspicuous clothing). The general direction is towards more nuanced apportionment that takes seriously the relative vulnerability of motorcyclists.

(5) SCRUTINY OF THE EMERGING DRIVER. In Powell v Moody the defendant was held 20% liable. Modern decisions scrutinise the emerging driver's conduct more rigorously. Where the defendant was beckoned out by a third party, had limited visibility, did not pause at the offside edge of the queue, or edged forward without checking for filtering traffic, the apportionment against the motorcyclist should be materially less than 80%.

PRACTICAL CHECKLIST. (a) Plead the Highway Code Hierarchy explicitly. (b) Obtain CCTV/dashcam evidence of the speed of the filtering motorcycle. (c) Examine the geometry: sightlines, pause, beckoning. (d) Expert evidence on stopping distances, perception-reaction times, conspicuity. (e) Distinguish Powell v Moody on facts; do not let the defendant deploy it as a global rule. (f) Cite later authority and the 2022 Highway Code where they assist.

RELATED CASES. Farley v Buckley [2007] EWCA Civ 403 (entry already in this knowledge base / case database) - claimant motorcyclist overtaking a refuse wagon, claim dismissed in entirety; described by the trial judge as 'reckless'. Pell v Moseley [2003] - overtaking at a motocross event, 50/50 apportionment. Both are referenced in the Lyons Davidson commentary alongside Powell v Moody.

SOURCE NOTE. The narrative above is drawn from the Lyons Davidson commentary 'Liability and motorcycle accidents' (June 2019), authored by Lyons Davidson Solicitors (a defendant firm). The original Powell v Moody report (Solicitors Journal 1966, 110 SJ 215 per Lyons Davidson) should be obtained and reviewed before reliance on the case at trial.

articles.author_role: Editorial team — Credit Hire Forum knowledge base
articles.summary: A worked liability case study from a claimant-side practitioner training deck. The animated scenario: a motorcyclist overtakes a line of traffic as a car pulls out of a side road into the overtaking lane. The deck steps trainees through the duty of care owed by both drivers, the Highway Code rules engaged for both the motorcyclist (rules 86, 211, 204) and the driver (rules 129, 163), the conclusion that BOTH parties have breached duties — though on the deck's analysis the blue car ultimately stops at the stop line and the motorcyclist overtaking across double white lines into a side-road conflict point bears principal responsibility. Includes guidance on the role of S.38(7) Road Traffic Act 1988 and on evidence (vehicle damage, road markings) when fact-finding the accident.
key_points: BOTH parties owe each other a duty of care under common law negligence — the threshold question in every RTA liability assessment is which driver breached that duty and whether the breach caused the accident.

Section 38(7) Road Traffic Act 1988: a failure to observe a provision of the Highway Code may be relied upon by any party in civil proceedings as tending to establish or negative liability. The Highway Code is the practitioner's starting point in every RTA file.

Highway Code rules engaged on the MOTORCYCLIST side: Rule 86 (daylight riding — make yourself visible from front, side and rear; light or brightly coloured helmet, fluorescent clothing, dipped headlights), Rule 211 (drivers must look out for motorcyclists especially when waiting alongside or coming up from behind, and at junctions), Rule 204 (Hierarchy of Road Users — those who can cause greatest harm bear greatest responsibility).

Highway Code rules engaged on the DRIVER side: Rule 129 (double white lines — must not cross or straddle when nearer line is solid except in defined exceptions); Rule 163 (overtake only when safe and legal — give cyclists, horse riders and motorcyclists at least as much room as a car).

Apportionment turns on FACT: in this scenario the deck's analysis is that the blue car does stop at the stop line and the motorcyclist's overtaking manoeuvre across double white lines into a sightline-conflicted side road is the primary cause. The fact-pattern is fact-specific — practitioners should not extract a tariff from it.

Evidence drives the assessment: vehicle damage location and severity, road markings, position of impact on the road, and any CCTV/dashcam will determine whether the case is argued at 100/0, 80/20 or 50/50.

Practitioner takeaway: at intake, plead the specific Highway Code rules engaged, capture the road geometry and visibility evidence quickly, and remember the hierarchy of road users when arguing the apportionment for a vulnerable claimant motorcyclist.
