## RTA Case Study 1A — Motorbike overtaking as a vehicle pulls out: scenario and motorcyclist Highway Code rules
_From a claimant-side practitioner training deck on liability in road traffic accidents._

### Slide 23
Who was responsible for the accident?
Why?
How will they prove it??
Accident Scenarios
**Trainer notes:** Part 2 – animated accidents 

5 animations (if we have time) 

All motorbikes – get stuck in – have a go. 4

### Slide 24
**Trainer notes:** Animation 1 : Motorbike overtaking as vehicle pulls out of a side road 

Take a look at this road traffic accident.
Play a couple of times. 

Next slide – vote who is at fault. 
Get them to stand up and move. 

If you think blue at fault stand in the blue corner.  
If you think red at fault stand in the red corner.  

If you think it’s a split – stand in the middle.

### Slide 25
Liability - Whose fault is this accident?
Motorbike is 100% 
Responsible
Blue car is 100% responsible
Both drivers are 
partly responsible
**Trainer notes:** Vote now – Blue car in one corner Red motorbike in the other corner, both at fault in the middle of the room 

While they are thinking remind them that both drivers owe a duty of care
So, they need to be clear about which driver breached their duty of care – and how they are going to work out what = breach

Make a note of how many in each corner/middle 

Ask someone from each team to explain why they reached that decision 
Try to get some debate going

### Slide 26
“ A failure to observe a provision of the Highway Code …. may be relied upon by any party ….. as tending to establish or negative any liability in question in the proceedings” 
S38 (7) Road Traffic Act 1988
Liability - Whose fault is this accident?
**Trainer notes:** Back to seats.

Let’s dissect this accident 

First point to make – In an RTA case the Highway Code is like your bible. 
Show of hands – when did you last look at the Highway Code? 

When assessing liability you should refer to it every time! It tells you what a careful driver should be doing 

Repeat – Failure to observe a provision in the Highway Code …. May be relied upon by either party … as tending to establish Negligence …… 

Here’s where you’ll find whether a driver has failed to meet the required standard of care when they are driving.

### Slide 27
Liability - Whose fault is this accident?
Rule 86
Daylight riding. Make yourself as visible as possible from the side as well as the front and rear. You could wear a light or brightly coloured helmet and fluorescent clothing or strips. Dipped headlights, even in good daylight, may also make you more conspicuous. However, be aware that other vehicle drivers may still not have seen you, or judged your distance or speed correctly, especially at junctions.
Rule 204
The road users most at risk from road traffic are pedestrians, in particular children, older adults and disabled people, cyclists, horse riders and motorcyclists. It is particularly important to be aware of children, older adults and disabled people, and learner and inexperienced drivers and riders. In any interaction between road users, those who can cause the greatest harm have the greatest responsibility to reduce the danger or threat they pose to others.
**Trainer notes:** Rule 86
Daylight riding. Make yourself as visible as possible from the side as well as the front and rear. You could wear a light or brightly coloured helmet and fluorescent clothing or strips. Dipped headlights, even in good daylight, may also make you more conspicuous. However, be aware that other vehicle drivers may still not have seen you, or judged your distance or speed correctly, especially at junctions.


Rule 204
The road users most at risk from road traffic are pedestrians, in particular children, older adults and disabled people, cyclists, horse riders and motorcyclists. It is particularly important to be aware of children, older adults and disabled people, and learner and inexperienced drivers and riders. In any interaction between road users, those who can cause the greatest harm have the greatest responsibility to reduce the danger or threat they pose to others.



Should consider specific motorbike rules every time!  This is one of them!

### Slide 28
Liability - Whose fault is this accident?
Rule 211
It is often difficult to see motorcyclists and cyclists, especially when they are waiting alongside you, coming up from behind, coming out of or moving off from junctions, at roundabouts, overtaking you or filtering through traffic. Always look out for them before you emerge from a junction; they could be approaching faster than you think.
Do not turn at a junction if to do so would cause the cyclist going straight ahead to stop or swerve, just as you would do with a motor vehicle.
When turning right across a line of slow-moving or stationary traffic, look out for and give way to cyclists or motorcyclists on the inside of the traffic you are crossing. Be especially careful when moving off, turning, and when changing direction or lane. Be sure to check mirrors and blind spots carefully.
**Trainer notes:** Rule 211
It is often difficult to see motorcyclists and cyclists, especially when they are waiting alongside you, coming up from behind, coming out of or moving off from junctions, at roundabouts, overtaking you or filtering through traffic. Always look out for them before you emerge from a junction; they could be approaching faster than you think.
Do not turn at a junction if to do so would cause the cyclist going straight ahead to stop or swerve, just as you would do with a motor vehicle.
When turning right across a line of slow-moving or stationary traffic, look out for and give way to cyclists or motorcyclists on the inside of the traffic you are crossing. Be especially careful when moving off, turning, and when changing direction or lane. Be sure to check mirrors and blind spots carefully.



Should consider specific motorbike rules every time!  This is one of them!

### Slide 29
**Trainer notes:** Analyse the scene 
Location photos Google earth maps

Consider each driver line of sight 

Click (arrow appear) Stop line at the junction – not a give way 
Does the car “Stop”  - proof may of course be difficult 

Clear from the animation that Blue does stop 

Click (arrow appear) Doubles solid white line 

So we need to understand what the highway code says about these facts

## RTA Case Study 1B — Motorbike overtaking as a vehicle pulls out: driver Highway Code rules, verdict and evidence
_From a claimant-side practitioner training deck on liability in road traffic accidents._

### Slide 30
The Highway Code – relevant rules in this scenario
Liability - Whose fault is this accident?
Rule 129
Rule 144
Rule 163
Rule 180
**Trainer notes:** Does anyone know what these rules say ? 

Always remember the rules specific to motorbikes! 

Split room into four groups and ask each group to look up these 4 rules on their phone!

### Slide 31
Liability - Whose fault is this accident?
Rule 129
Rule 144
Double white lines where the line nearer to you is solid. This means you MUST NOT cross or straddle it unless it is safe and you need to enter adjoining premises or a side road. You may cross the line, if necessary, provided the road is clear, to pass a stationary vehicle, or overtake a pedal cycle, horse or road maintenance vehicle, if they are travelling at 10 mph (16 km/h) or less.
You MUST NOT
drive dangerously
drive without due care and attention
drive without reasonable consideration for other road users.

Driving requires focus and attention at all times. Remember, you may be driving dangerously or travelling too fast even if you don’t mean to.
**Trainer notes:** Rule 129
Double white lines where the line nearer to you is solid. This means you MUST NOT cross or straddle it unless it is safe and you need to enter adjoining premises or a side road. You may cross the line if necessary, provided the road is clear, to pass a stationary vehicle, or overtake a pedal cycle, horse or road maintenance vehicle, if they are travelling at 10 mph (16 km/h) or less.

Motorbike has crossed a solid double white line and should not have done so -  therefore a potential failure to follow the Highway Code and this could help to establish liability for the accident.


Rule 144
You MUST NOT
drive dangerously
drive without due care and attention
drive without reasonable consideration for other road users.
Driving requires focus and attention at all times. Remember, you may be driving dangerously or travelling too fast even if you don’t mean to. 

Has either party breached these rules ? 
Has the car driver failed to consider the motorbike and their ability to make manoeuvres that cars cannot ?? 
Should the car be waiting for yellow to make his turn into the side street ? Can blue see the motorbike?

### Slide 32
Liability - Whose fault is this accident?
Rule 163
Rule 180
Overtake only when it is safe and legal to do so.
Turning Right - Wait until there is a safe gap between you and any oncoming vehicle. Watch out for cyclists, motorcyclists, pedestrians and other road users. Check your mirrors and blind spot again to make sure you are not being overtaken, then make the turn. Do not cut the corner. Take great care when turning into a main road; you will need to watch for traffic in both directions and wait for a safe gap.
**Trainer notes:** Rule 163
Overtake only when it is safe and legal to do so. You should
not get too close to the vehicle you intend to overtake
use your mirrors, signal when it is safe to do so, take a quick sideways glance if necessary into the blind spot area and then start to move out
not assume that you can simply follow a vehicle ahead which is overtaking; there may only be enough room for one vehicle
move quickly past the vehicle you are overtaking, once you have started to overtake. Allow plenty of room. Move back to the left as soon as you can but do not cut in
take extra care at night and in poor visibility when it is harder to judge speed and distance
give way to oncoming vehicles before passing parked vehicles or other obstructions on your side of the road
only overtake on the left if the vehicle in front is signalling to turn right, and there is room to do so
stay in your lane if traffic is moving slowly in queues. If the queue on your right is moving more slowly than you are, you may pass on the left. Cyclists may pass slower moving or stationary traffic on their right or left and should proceed with caution as the driver may not be able to see you. Be careful about doing so, particularly on the approach to junctions, and especially when deciding whether it is safe to pass lorries or other large vehicles.
give motorcyclists, cyclists and horse riders and horse drawn vehicles at least as much room as you would when overtaking a car (see Rules 211 to 215). As a guide:
leave at least 1.5 metres when overtaking cyclists at speeds of up to 30mph, and give them more space when overtaking at higher speeds
pass horse riders and horse-drawn vehicles at speeds under 10 mph and allow at least 2 metres of space
allow at least 2 metres of space and keep to a low speed when passing a pedestrian who is walking in the road (for example, where there is no pavement)
take extra care and give more space when overtaking motorcyclists, cyclists, horse riders, horse drawn vehicles and pedestrians in bad weather (including high winds) and at night
you should wait behind the motorcyclist, cyclist, horse rider, horse drawn vehicle or pedestrian and not overtake if it is unsafe or not possible to meet these clearances.


Rule 180
Wait until there is a safe gap between you and any oncoming vehicle. Watch out for cyclists, motorcyclists, pedestrians and other road users. Check your mirrors and blind spot again to make sure you are not being overtaken, then make the turn. Do not cut the corner. Take great care when turning into a main road; you will need to watch for traffic in both directions and wait for a safe gap.

### Slide 33
Liability - Whose fault is this accident?
Motorbike is 100% 
Responsible
Blue car is 100% responsible
Both drivers are 
partly responsible
**Trainer notes:** Anyone want to change their mind? 

Vote now – Blue car in one corner Red motorbike in the other corner, both at fault in the middle of the room 

While they are thinking remind them that both drivers owe a duty of care
So, they need to be clear about which driver breached their duty of care – and how they are going to work out what = breach

Make a note of how many in each corner/middle/who has changed their mind 

Ask someone from each team to explain why they reached that decision 
Try to get some debate going

### Slide 34
Liability - Whose fault is this accident?
Motorbike 70 %
Blue Car 30%
**Trainer notes:** Our View ……..

Blue does stop at the stop line (although proof is difficult either way) 

Accident happens on the side of the carriageway that blue is travelling. Opposite side of the road for the motorbike.
Look at the area of damage to the blue car – front ? Side ? Think about this as evidence 


The blue car will be arguing that the s/he wasn’t expecting the motorbike to conduct a manoeuvre prohibited by the highway code (crossing that solid double white line), motorbike overtaking at the junction was unsafe etc 

The motorbike will argue that the car should really have waited until the yellow car had completed their turn into the side street. Blue car’s visibility of the motorbike obscured by yellow. 

Harding V Hinchcliffe 
Was held in this that the car should have waited for the turning vehicle to complete turn. 
However – differences in our case,  yellow vehicle is a car NOT a bus. 
Double solid white line. 
Remember no two cases are the same! Always analysis your own. 


Primary liability with the motorbike 
However some liability with the blue car – should have waited.  Solid double white line for a reason – says something about road / conditions. 

Motorbike – 70% 
Blue Car - 30%

### Slide 35
**Trainer notes:** New slide 

Why is this different?? 

Discuss 

The distinctions here are that : 

Motorbike not conducting an overtaking manoeuvre – it’s maintaining its course 
Blue car likely to be arguing that they didn’t need to wait for the Yellow car to turn, yellow was slowing and indicating. Motorbike travelling too quickly. 
Motorbike will be saying they weren’t going too quick & even if I was it didn’t cause the accident, accident caused by blue coming into path from minor to major road that caused the collision 

Judge likely to find blue car 100% liable.

### Slide 36
Vehicle damage evidence may be crucial
Ask about road markings.
Ask about lines of sight. (Did client take photos?)
Ask about possible witnesses?
Is there dash cam footage?
What about the occupants of the yellow vehicle?
Always look at Google Maps!
Liability – Key points
**Trainer notes:** Rattle through this. 

Always think about where the damage is on the vehicles – does it match what the drivers are saying?

articles.title: RTA Liability Case Study — Roundabout Collision Between Car and Motorcycle Exiting (Verdict: Car 100%)
articles.author: credit-hire.ai editorial
articles.author_role: Editorial team — Credit Hire Forum knowledge base
articles.summary: A worked liability case study from a claimant-side practitioner training deck. The animation shows a car exiting a roundabout colliding with a motorcyclist already on the roundabout. The trainer leads the cohort through the relevant roundabout rules (184–187 of the Highway Code), culminating in a verdict of CAR 100% — the car has cut across the motorcyclist's lane on exit, breaching lane discipline and the duty to look out for vulnerable road users. A clean teaching example of how full liability against a defendant emerges where the claimant has done nothing wrong and the defendant has breached specific Highway Code rules.
key_points: Roundabout case studies highlight the lane-discipline rules — Highway Code Rules 184–187 — particularly the obligation to signal correctly, stay in the correct lane, and watch for two-wheelers running on the inside.

Verdict is CAR 100%: where a defendant has cut across a motorcyclist's line on a roundabout exit and the motorcyclist was riding correctly, no contributory negligence reduction is appropriate. The Highway Code Rule 211 obligation on drivers to look for motorcyclists reinforces the apportionment.

Practitioner takeaway: not every motorcycle case ends in a contributory negligence reduction — the headline 80/20 from cases like Powell v Moody [1966] is fact-specific and should not be transposed onto modern roundabout collisions where the motorcyclist is not at fault.

Evidence priorities: position of vehicles immediately before impact, lane occupancy, signal use by the defendant, and impact geometry (where on each vehicle the damage is) will determine the apportionment.
Hierarchy of Road Users (Rules H1–H3 introduced in 2022) reinforces the case for full liability against the larger vehicle in collisions of this type.

## RTA Case Study 2 — Roundabout collision between car and motorbike (verdict: Car 100%)
_From a claimant-side practitioner training deck on liability in road traffic accidents._

### Slide 39
“ A failure to observe a provision of the Highway Code …. may be relied upon by any party ….. as tending to establish or negative any liability in question in the proceedings” 
S38 (7) Road Traffic Act 1988
Liability - Whose fault is this accident?
**Trainer notes:** Lets look at the highway code!

Any others ??? Next slide gives them ….

The Highway Code – relevant rules in this scenario

Rule 187
Liability - Whose fault is this accident?
**Trainer notes:** Does anyone know what these rules say ? 

Remember the motorbikes specific ones too!!! 

Shout out … if not give them the rules 

Rules about roundabouts!

### Slide 41
Liability - Whose fault is this accident?
Rule 186
Rule 187
When taking an exit to the right or going full circle, unless signs or markings indicate otherwise signal right and approach in the right-hand lane keep to the right on the roundabout until you need to change lanes to exit the roundabout
signal left after you have passed the exit before the one you want.
In all cases watch out for and give plenty of room to 
pedestrians who may be crossing the approach and exit roads 
traffic crossing in front of you on the roundabout, especially vehicles intending to leave by the next exit
traffic which may be straddling lanes or positioned incorrectly motorcyclists
long vehicles (including those towing trailers). These might have to take a different course or straddle lanes either approaching or on the roundabout because of their length. Watch out for their signals.
**Trainer notes:** These are rules specific to roundabouts … 

Click to highlight relevant bit of 186 

Rule 186
Signals and position. When taking the first exit to the left, unless signs or markings indicate otherwise
signal left and approach in the left-hand lane
keep to the left on the roundabout and continue signalling left to leave.

When taking an exit to the right or going full circle, unless signs or markings indicate otherwise
signal right and approach in the right-hand lane
keep to the right on the roundabout until you need to change lanes to exit the roundabout
signal left after you have passed the exit before the one you want.

When taking any intermediate exit, unless signs or markings indicate otherwise
select the appropriate lane on approach to the roundabout
you should not normally need to signal on approach
stay in this lane until you need to alter course to exit the roundabout
signal left after you have passed the exit before the one you want.

When there are more than three lanes at the entrance to a roundabout, use the most appropriate lane on approach and through it.
You should give priority to cyclists on the roundabout. They will be travelling more slowly than motorised traffic. Give them plenty of room and do not attempt to overtake them within their lane. Allow them to move across your path as they travel around the roundabout.

Cyclists, horse riders and horse drawn vehicles may stay in the left-hand lane when they intend to continue across or around the roundabout and should signal right to show you they are not leaving the roundabout. Drivers should take extra care when entering a roundabout to ensure that they do not cut across cyclists, horse riders or horse drawn vehicles in the left-hand lane, who are continuing around the roundabout.

Rule 187
In all cases watch out for and give plenty of room to
pedestrians who may be crossing the approach and exit roads
traffic crossing in front of you on the roundabout, especially vehicles intending to leave by the next exit
traffic which may be straddling lanes or positioned incorrectly
motorcyclists
long vehicles (including those towing trailers). These might have to take a different course or straddle lanes either approaching or on the roundabout because of their length. Watch out for their signals.

### Slide 42
**Trainer notes:** Replay if required

### Slide 43
Liability - Whose fault is this accident?
Motorbike is 100% 
Responsible
Red car is 100% responsible
Both drivers are 
partly responsible
**Trainer notes:** Vote again now – 
Show of hands. 

Who thinks red car is responsible 
Who thinks Motorbike is responsible 
Who thinks both are at fault 

Who has changed their mind ? 
Why ?? Get them giving their thoughts / ideas and get a debate going

### Slide 44
Car 100 %
**Trainer notes:** Here’s what we think …

Firstly, lets consider the positioning of the vehicles as they join the roundabout. 
Both join in the correct lane, right hand lane.  Motorbike behind the car. 

Car may argue that s/he was aware that the motorbike was behind him as he joined the roundabout, and s/he would have expected the motorbike to stay there in that position around the roundabout.  

Has the motorbike breached rules by failing to stay in the correct lane / undertake the car? 
Has the car breached rules – failing to keep a watch out for the bike (vulnerable / blind spot) 

What is crucial here is the position of the vehicles as they exit the roundabout.  Motorbike exits in the left lane and stays in his/her lane. The car appears to exit in the right lane and then moves over to the left, not realising the motorbike is there. 

The cause of this accident is the car moving over into the other lane as they exit the roundabout.  

CAR 100% at fault.  Click

Vehicle damage evidence may be crucial
Ask about road markings.
Ask about lines of sight. (Did client take photos?)
Ask about possible witnesses?
Is there dash cam footage?
Are there photos of the vehicles at point of impact ? 
Always look at Google Maps!
Liability – Key points

articles.title: RTA Liability Case Study — Car Exiting Side Road into Filtering Motorcyclist on a Country A-Road (Verdict: Motorcycle 80% / Red Car 20%)
articles.author: credit-hire.ai editorial
articles.author_role: Editorial team — Credit Hire Forum knowledge base
articles.summary: A worked liability case study from a claimant-side practitioner training deck — a country A-road with one car (red) waving an emerging vehicle (blue) out of a side road as a motorcyclist overtakes the red car at speed. The trainer steps the cohort through the Highway Code rules engaged on the motorcyclist side (86 visibility, 211 driver lookout, 204 hierarchy) and on the driver side (110 — flashing headlights only to warn of presence, NOT to communicate priority; 167 — DO NOT overtake where you might come into conflict). Verdict: MOTORCYCLE 80% / RED CAR 20% — a fact-specific apportionment which the deck explains turns on the speed and visibility of the overtaking motorcyclist and the misleading signal from the red car driver. Includes the Highway Code text of rules 110 and 167.
key_points: Side-road conflicts with overtaking motorcyclists are a recurring fact-pattern. The trainer illustrates how the apportionment turns on the relative culpability of (i) the emerging driver, (ii) the obstructing driver who waves them out, and (iii) the overtaking motorcyclist.

Highway Code Rule 110 — flashing headlights have ONE meaning: to let other road users know you are there. They are NOT a signal of priority. A driver who flashes another vehicle out of a side road takes responsibility for misleading both that driver and oncoming traffic.

Highway Code Rule 167 — DO NOT overtake where you might come into conflict with other road users, including where the road narrows, where there is a junction, or where road users could emerge from a side road.

Verdict: MOTORCYCLE 80% / RED CAR 20%. The motorcyclist is the principal cause because they were overtaking past a junction at speed; the red car driver bears 20% for the misleading flash. Note that the BLUE CAR (the emerging driver) is, on the deck's analysis, less culpable than either — they relied on the misleading signal.

This is a CASE STUDY apportionment, not a tariff. Modern apportionments increasingly reflect the Hierarchy of Road Users (Rules H1–H3) and motorcyclists' inherent vulnerability. Where the motorcyclist's overtaking speed is moderate and the emerging-driver evidence is weak, apportionments below 80% against the motorcyclist are achievable.

Evidence to capture early: the position and gestures of the obstructing driver, motorcycle speed (helmet cam, witness, accident reconstruction), road geometry and visibility from the side road, and any explicit signal (flashing headlights) given to the emerging driver.

Cross-reference Powell v Moody [1966] (80/20 against a filtering motorcyclist past stationary traffic) — a similar but distinguishable fact-pattern; the rebuttals collected in the credit-hire.ai entry for Powell v Moody also assist in country A-road overtaking cases of this type.
cases_cited: Powell v Moody (1966) 110 SJ 215 (referenced for filtering apportionment context)

Rule 86
Daylight riding. Make yourself as visible as possible from the side as well as the front and rear. You could wear a light or brightly coloured helmet and fluorescent clothing or strips. Dipped headlights, even in good daylight, may also make you more conspicuous. However, be aware that other vehicle drivers may still not have seen you, or judged your distance or speed correctly, especially at junctions.
Rule 204
The road users most at risk from road traffic are pedestrians, in particular children, older adults and disabled people, cyclists, horse riders and motorcyclists. It is particularly important to be aware of children, older adults and disabled people, and learner and inexperienced drivers and riders. In any interaction between road users, those who can cause the greatest harm have the greatest responsibility to reduce the danger or threat they pose to others.

## RTA Case Study 3B — Car exiting side road collides with motorbike: overtaking rules, verdict (Motorbike 80% / Red Car 20%)
_From a claimant-side practitioner training deck on liability in road traffic accidents._

### Slide 52
Liability - Whose fault is this accident?
Rule 110
Flashing headlights. 
Only flash your headlights to let other road users know that you are there. Do not flash your headlights to convey any other message or intimidate other road users.
Rule 111
Never assume that flashing headlights is a signal inviting you to proceed. Use your own judgement and proceed carefully.
**Trainer notes:** Rule 110
Flashing headlights. Only flash your headlights to let other road users know that you are there. Do not flash your headlights to convey any other message or intimidate other road users.
Rule 111
Never assume that flashing headlights is a signal inviting you to proceed. Use your own judgement and proceed carefully.

### Slide 53
Liability - Whose fault is this accident?
Rule 167
DO NOT overtake where you might come into conflict with other road users. 
(for example, where traffic is queuing at junctions or roadworks)
Rule 170
Take extra care at junctions. You should watch out for cyclists, motorcyclists …… 
Do not assume, when waiting at a junction, that a vehicle coming from the right and signalling left will actually turn. Wait and make sure look all around before emerging. Do not cross oar join a road until there is a gap large enough for you to do so safely.
Rule 173
Dual carriageways. When crossing or turning right, first assess whether the central reservation is deep enough to protect the full length of your vehicle.
If it is, then you should treat each half of the carriageway as a separate road. Wait in the central reservation until there is a safe gap in the traffic on the second half of the road.
If the central reservation is too shallow for the length of your vehicle, wait until you can cross both carriageways in one go.
**Trainer notes:** Rule 167
DO NOT overtake where you might come into conflict with other road users. For example
approaching or at a road junction on either side of the road
where the road narrows
when approaching a school crossing patrol
on the approach to crossing facilities
where a vehicle ahead is slowing to stop for a pedestrian that is crossing from a pedestrian island (see Rule 165)
between the kerb and a bus or tram when it is at a stop
where traffic is queuing at junctions or road works
when you would force another road user to swerve or slow down
at a level crossing
when a road user is indicating right, even if you believe the signal should have been cancelled. Do not take a risk; wait for the signal to be cancelled
stay behind if you are following a cyclist approaching a roundabout or junction, and you intend to turn left. Do not cut across cyclists going ahead, including those using cycle lanes and cycle tracks (see Rule H3)
stay behind if you are following a horse rider or horse drawn vehicle approaching a roundabout or junction, and you intend to turn left. Do not cut across a horse rider or horse drawn vehicle going ahead
when a tram is standing at a kerbside tram stop and there is no clearly marked passing lane for other traffic.

Rule 170
Take extra care at junctions. You should
watch out for cyclists, motorcyclists and pedestrians including powered wheelchairs/mobility scooter users as they are not always easy to see. Be aware that they may not have seen or heard you if you are approaching from behind 
give way to pedestrians crossing or waiting to cross a road into which or from which you are turning. If they have started to cross they have priority, so give way (see Rule H2)
remain behind cyclists, horse riders, horse drawn vehicles and motorcyclists at junctions even if they are waiting to turn and are positioned close to the kerb
watch out for long vehicles which may be turning at a junction ahead; they may have to use the whole width of the road to make the turn (see Rule 221)
watch out for horse riders who may take a different line on the road from that which you would expect
not assume, when waiting at a junction, that a vehicle coming from the right and signalling left will actually turn. Wait and make sure
look all around before emerging. Do not cross or join a road until there is a gap large enough for you to do so safely.

Rule 173
Dual carriageways. When crossing or turning right, first assess whether the central reservation is deep enough to protect the full length of your vehicle.
If it is, then you should treat each half of the carriageway as a separate road. Wait in the central reservation until there is a safe gap in the traffic on the second half of the road.
If the central reservation is too shallow for the length of your vehicle, wait until you can cross both carriageways in one go.

### Slide 54
**Trainer notes:** Let’s watch again

### Slide 55

**Trainer notes:** Vote now – Show of hands 
Who thinks that the red car is responsible ?
Who thinks that the motorbike is responsible?
Who thinks both are at fault ? 

Does anyone think any blame to be apportioned to the wagon? 

Anyone changed their mind ? 

Ask why ?  Get them discussing /debating / chatting

### Slide 56
Motorbike 80% 
Red Car 20%
**Trainer notes:** Here’s what we think …… 

Firstly ….. Consider witnesses, did anyone speak to anyone at the scene – lots of vehicles around. 

Powell V Moody – Side road and overtaking motorbike.
However, always consider the evidence of each case and consider the difference to any case law.  EVERY case is different ! 

A motorbike overtaking a line of queuing traffic (possible breach of HC) s/he should do so with a very high degree of care.  Even more so considering the side road / junction. 
Motorbike appears not to slow down when approaching that junction. 

Judge would expect the car driver to inch out and continue to inch out until completely satisfied it was safe. (cannot rely on the flash) 
Red car failed to do so throughout the manoeuvre, although had initially started to do so with sufficient caution  

Weighing all that up, we think a Judge would find the 

Motorbike – 80% responsible
Car- 20% responsible

articles.title: RTA Liability Case Study — Motorcycle Colliding with Car Conducting a U-Turn (Verdict: Car 100%)
articles.author: credit-hire.ai editorial
articles.author_role: Editorial team — Credit Hire Forum knowledge base
articles.summary: A worked liability case study from a claimant-side practitioner training deck — a motorcyclist proceeding along a road collides with a car making a U-turn across their path. Verdict: CAR 100%. The car driver has executed a manoeuvre that requires looking carefully for following and oncoming traffic — particularly motorcyclists, who are often missed at junctions and during manoeuvres. Highway Code Rules 88 (motorbikes manoeuvring — drivers should be aware of two-wheelers around them) and 211 (drivers must look out for motorcyclists, especially when waiting alongside, coming up from behind, or moving off from junctions) are decisive.
key_points: U-turn collisions between cars and motorcyclists almost always result in full liability against the car driver because a U-turn requires the driver to satisfy themselves the manoeuvre is safe — the burden is squarely on them.
Highway Code Rule 88: motorcyclists making manoeuvres can be vulnerable; drivers should be aware of motorcyclists around them.
Highway Code Rule 211: drivers must specifically look out for motorcyclists who can be difficult to see, especially when the driver is moving off, turning, or overtaking.

Verdict: CAR 100% — no contributory negligence reduction. Where the motorcyclist was travelling at a reasonable speed in their own lane and the U-turning driver has cut across, the apportionment is total against the car.

Practitioner takeaway: take detailed evidence of the motorcyclist's speed and lane positioning from the outset to preclude any spurious contributory-negligence allegation about excessive speed or wide lane positioning.

articles.title: RTA Liability Case Study — Motorcycle Overtaking a Bus Collides with Car Pulling Out (Verdict: Motorcycle 80% / Red Car 20%)
articles.author: credit-hire.ai editorial
articles.author_role: Editorial team — Credit Hire Forum knowledge base
articles.summary: A worked liability case study from a claimant-side practitioner training deck — a motorcyclist overtakes a bus and collides with a red car pulling out from the offside. Verdict: MOTORCYCLE 80% / RED CAR 20%. The Highway Code rules engaged are 110 (flashing headlights only to warn of presence), 164 (overtaking large vehicles is more difficult — extra care needed), and 167 (do NOT overtake where you might come into conflict). The motorcyclist takes the principal share because overtaking a bus is a manoeuvre with reduced visibility and sightlines that requires great care; the red car driver takes 20% for failing to see the motorcyclist before pulling out.
key_points: Overtaking a large vehicle (bus/lorry/HGV) is one of the most hazardous motorcycle manoeuvres because the larger vehicle screens the motorcyclist from oncoming and side-road traffic. Highway Code Rule 164 specifically addresses this: overtaking large vehicles requires extra space and judgement; you may need to fall further back to see ahead.

Highway Code Rule 167 prohibits overtaking where conflict with other road users is foreseeable. A motorcyclist overtaking a bus near where vehicles can pull out from the offside (lay-by, side road, parked-vehicle line) is squarely within Rule 167.
Highway Code Rule 110 — flashing headlights mean ONLY "I am here". A motorcyclist who flashed their headlights to claim priority over the red car has no defence in that signal.

Verdict: MOTORCYCLE 80% / RED CAR 20%. The 80% reflects the high inherent risk of overtaking a large vehicle; the 20% on the red car reflects the duty under Rule 211 to look out for motorcyclists before pulling out.

Practitioner takeaway: in overtaking-large-vehicle cases the apportionment ceiling against the motorcyclist is high. Mitigating factors that may reduce the share: was the bus stationary or moving, was there a passing place, did the red car driver have any sightline to oncoming traffic, was the motorcyclist's speed reasonable for the manoeuvre.

Cross-reference Davis v Schrogin [2006] EWCA Civ 974 — claimant motorcyclist who collided while overtaking found NOT to have caused or contributed to the accident on the facts. Apportionment is fact-specific.
cases_cited: Davis v Schrogin [2006] EWCA Civ 974 (cross-reference for overtaking apportionment)

Red appears NOT to have crept out, to check its clear either way. 

Red will argue that the Motorbike should not have been overtaking – large vehicle – near a junction and queueing traffic. Dangerous and a breach of the HC (R167) 

Similarities to the case of Dowling V Dargue (1997) - ALTHOUGH ALWAYS JUDGE ECAH CASE ON OWN MERITS AND EVIDENCE 

Importantly – the accident occurs on the other side of the road to the Motorbikes direction of travel 

DAMAGE – seems  that red has completed the manoeuvre the damage is to the front of the red car 

Primary cause of the accident is the motorbikes decision to overtake, where he has insufficient view and was close to that junction and queuing traffic  

However, judge likely to find contrib against the red car for not creeping out.  

Motorbike 80% 
Car 20%

### Slide 76
Vehicle damage evidence may be crucial
Ask about road markings.
Ask about lines of sight. (Did client take photos?)
Ask about possible witnesses?
Is there dash cam footage?
Are there photos of the vehicles at point of impact ? 
Always look at Google Maps!
Liability – Key points
**Trainer notes:** Rattle through this. 

Photos showing position of the cars at exit / impact may be crucial here.

articles.title: RTA Liability Practical Case Study — Telephone Enquiry: Assessing Evidence at Inception to Take On a Claim
articles.author: credit-hire.ai editorial
articles.author_role: Editorial team — Credit Hire Forum knowledge base
articles.summary: A short practical case study from a claimant-side practitioner training deck. A claim handler receives a telephone enquiry from a prospective client involved in an RTA. The exercise walks through the evidence the handler should gather BEFORE deciding to take the claim on — claimant's account, defendant's likely version, witness availability, location evidence (plans, photos, Google Earth), and what each party will need to prove on the balance of probabilities. A useful inception-stage checklist for claim handlers facing the question "do I have enough evidence to run this?".
key_points: At telephone-enquiry stage the question is not whether the claimant is in the right but whether on the balance of probabilities you can prove it. Always think in terms of evidential burden, not moral fault.

Capture the claimant's full account before anchoring on a liability theory. Specific questions: vehicle positions, speeds, road and weather conditions, traffic signals/markings, any witnesses, any CCTV or dashcam, vehicle damage areas, sequence of events.
Anticipate the defendant's likely version — what would they say? Where their version is plausible and there is no independent evidence to rebut it, the claim is at risk.

Location evidence is often decisive: photographs of the scene, plans, Google Earth/Street View, road markings. These can disprove a defendant's account in seconds and support a pleading of breach of the Highway Code.
Witness availability matters at inception. A claimant's sister-in-law in the passenger seat is independent evidence but is also subject to weight reduction. An independent pedestrian witness is gold.

A handler should be able to articulate, before taking the claim on: (a) what the claimant must prove, (b) what evidence they have to prove it, (c) the most likely defendant response, (d) how that response will be rebutted. If any of those four is empty, the claim needs more inception work before it is accepted.

## RTA practical case study — Telephone enquiry: assessing the evidence to take on a claim
_From a claimant-side practitioner training deck on liability in road traffic accidents._

### Slide 79
You receive a phone call from a client who has been involved in an accident.  

She says that she was stationary at a roundabout, waiting for a safe gap to pull onto the roundabout and was hit in the rear. 

She sends you a photograph of the location and a google earth plan.
Case study 1
**Trainer notes:** Part 3 case studies. 
5 minutes for this one .. 

Next slide for the photo and plan

### Slide 80
EVIDENCE 
Case study 1
**Trainer notes:** Photo of location and plan.

### Slide 81
DEFENDANT’S EVIDENCE 
Case study 1
**Trainer notes:** Defendant’s version

### Slide 82
On the balance of probability 
Claimant’s version more likely!
EVIDENCE 
Case study 1
**Trainer notes:** Show of hands. 

Would you take on the claim?   YES 

Click – Balance of probability 

More likely that the Claimant has not yet entered the roundabout and D drove into her stationary car.  (then she suddenly stopped for no reason) 

What’s the risk of delay?   Unskilled or over stretched case handlers running claim for Defendant and not having time to consider evidence properly so not making a decision.
If D won’t concede – push for case to be litigated asap because it’s a good case.

Is there a chance an insurer would want to investigate fraud - crash for cash?  Probably not but be aware of risk

### Slide 83
Location photos / plan / google earth 

Statement from the Claimant 

Area of damage to the car
**Trainer notes:** What will be the evidence to determine liability ? 

Simple here one word against another,  sometimes it is. Who is most believable? Whose story makes more sense on the balance of probability?

articles.title: RTA Liability Practical Case Study — Patel v Taylor: Defending an Evidence-Light Liability Dispute (Highway Code Rule 167)
articles.author: credit-hire.ai editorial
articles.author_role: Editorial team — Credit Hire Forum knowledge base
articles.summary: A practical case study from a claimant-side practitioner training deck. Mrs Patel turns right into a driveway and her car is hit by Mr Taylor overtaking her at the same moment. Patel says she was indicating right and was struck mid-turn. Taylor says she was parked. The case turns on Highway Code Rule 167 (do not overtake where another road user is indicating right) and on whose version of events the court accepts. The exercise walks the trainee through what evidence is needed to win an evidence-light liability dispute — vehicle damage analysis, road markings, photographs, and the questions to put to the defendant's version.
key_points: Highway Code Rule 167 is decisive here: a driver MUST NOT overtake where a road user is indicating right, even if they think the indicator has been left on by mistake. The overtaking driver bears the risk.

Evidence-light disputes turn on detail. The questions to ask Mrs Patel: was she parked or turning, was her indicator on, where was she on the road, did she look in her mirror before turning, what speed was she travelling at, did she see Mr Taylor before the impact?

Vehicle damage photographs are often decisive. The position of impact on each vehicle (offside front of one, nearside rear of the other, etc.) tells the geometry of the collision. A vehicle struck while parked has a very different damage profile from one struck mid-turn.

The defendant's account ("she was parked") is testable: was the engine running, was the car in gear, were the brake lights on at impact, did the photographs show a parked or a manoeuvring vehicle? Any one of these can resolve the case.

Practitioner takeaway: even where evidence is sparse at inception, a methodical fact-finding pass — Highway Code rule, contemporaneous photographs, vehicle damage analysis, witness questions to expose the defendant's version — will reveal whether the claim is recoverable. Do not abandon a case for lack of independent witnesses without working the evidence first.

Cross-reference: Article 209 (Highway Code and Liability) for the general framework on how Highway Code breaches are pleaded; Article 210 (Chan v Peters 14 principles) for the standard of care framework.
articles.full_text:
## RTA practical case study — Patel v Taylor (evidence-light liability dispute)
_From a claimant-side practitioner training deck on liability in road traffic accidents._

### Slide 84
Case study 2 : 
PATEL V TAYLOR
You receive a phone call Mrs Patel.  
She has been involved in an accident. 

She was turning right into her driveway. The Defendant attempted to overtake her car as she turned.
**Trainer notes:** Case study 2 : 20 mins 

Claimant / client version

### Slide 85
Does Mr Taylor owe Miss Patel a duty of care?
Yes
What are the prospects of succeeding in a claim for compensation?
Not enough evidence but good enough to investigate
Has Defendant breached their duty of care?
**Trainer notes:** Shout out

### Slide 86
Defendant says …..
Other car was parked at the side of the road. 

As I drove past the car turned into me and hit my car.

### Slide 87
Do you have any further questions for Mrs Patel regarding liability?
What was your position on the road? Central
Had you been parked at the side of the road? No – I had absolutely no reason to park I was turning onto my drive. 
3.    Were you indicating right? Yes 
How wide is the road? Normal width, a car could have passed in the opposite direction.
Could a car have passed on the left? No – road is not wide enough 
Any passengers or witnesses? My four-year-old daughter was a rear seat passenger, there were no witnesses. 
7.    Did you check your mirror before turning right? Yes 
Did you look over your shoulder to check your blind spot?  I cannot remember.
**Trainer notes:** answer in workbook 
Give them 5 minutes to think about the questions and make a note 

Run through them

### Slide 88
What evidence do you need to make a liability decision?
Check Highway Code 
Photographs & plan / map of the location
Photos of the vehicles 
Engineers report / Repair invoice

### Slide 89
Rule 167 – Do not overtake  when a  road user is indicating right

Rule 179 - Well before you turn right you should
use your mirrors to make sure you know the position and movement of traffic behind you
give a right-turn signal
take up a position just left of the middle of the road or in the space marked for traffic turning right
leave room for other vehicles to pass on the left, if possible

### Slide 90
Claimant’s evidence

### Slide 91
**Trainer notes:** This photograph shows the damage to the Defendant’s car.
Does this change your view on liability – given that it shows car must have come quite far past Miss Patel for damage to be in middle of passenger door
Dent – suggest Claimant turned into the Mr Taylor’s car?

### Slide 92
Was Miss Patel parked?
Was Miss Patel indicating right?
Did Miss Patel make any checks before turning right?
Had Mr Taylor already started to pass Miss Patel’s car before she started her manoeuvre?
The Judge will need to decide….
**Trainer notes:** One word against the other. The questions for the judge will be : 
Was Miss Patel parked? Unlikely as she was intending to turn into her own  drive – no reason to be parked.  
Was Miss Patel indicating right? If a Judge decides no – she may be contributory negligent? 
Did she make checks before turning? If a Judge decides no – she may be contributory negligent? 
Had Mr Taylor already started to pass Miss Patel’s car before she started her manoeuvre?  If a Judge decides yes she may be contributory negligent? 
How likely that Mrs Patel was parked, given that she lives just opposite. 


If a judge accepts she was parked and did not indicate then the Defendant is likely to win.  

Think about how well your witness will give evidence – is she credible?  V important in a case like this.

Always work out what the judge needs to decide and check your evidence.

Risk of delay – it’s one word against another so may not settle easily even with the damage site to Defendant’s car. 
Important to assess how good witness is likely to be in court.   Case handler needs to speak to them and work out whether they sound credible.

articles.title: RTA Liability Practical Case Study — Two-Lane Junction Lane-Merge Dispute (Tipping the Balance with Photographic Evidence)
articles.author: credit-hire.ai editorial
articles.author_role: Editorial team — Credit Hire Forum knowledge base
articles.summary: A short practical case study from a claimant-side practitioner training deck. Two vehicles are stationary at traffic lights at a junction where two lanes merge into one beyond the stop line. The right-hand-lane driver (the claimant) collides with the left-hand-lane driver (the defendant) on the green light as both proceed and the lane merges. The case shows how a photograph of the lane markings — looked at from the other angle — disposes of a lane-merge liability dispute by demonstrating which driver had the right of way.
key_points: Lane-merge cases turn on the road markings: which lane was marked as continuing, which was marked as merging, and in which direction. A diamond-arrow lane marking dictates the direction of priority.

Photographs of the junction taken from the OTHER ANGLE often resolve a lane-merge dispute. Dashcam footage from a third vehicle, Google Street View imagery from before and after, or aerial imagery showing the lane markings can be decisive.

A driver who proceeds across a merge without looking ahead at the lane markings or checking their blind spot for the other lane is in breach of the standard of the reasonably careful driver — Chan v Peters principles 4 and 5.

Even where one party is principally at fault, lane-merge collisions often produce a small contributory-negligence finding against the other party for failing to anticipate or react. Apportionments of 80/20 or 70/30 are common.

Practitioner takeaway: at inception, request the claimant's photographs of the junction and prompt them to take more — particularly looking BACK from the merged lane towards the lights. Lane-merge disputes resolve on the markings; photographic evidence is cheap and decisive.

## RTA practical case study — Two-lane junction lane-merge dispute
_From a claimant-side practitioner training deck on liability in road traffic accidents._

### Slide 93
CASE STUDY 3
Claimant and Defendant stationary at these lights.

When the lights changed both vehicles set off and they collided on the junction.

The matter went to a small claims hearing.

There is no right turn. 
The junction is a cross roads.

Claimant driving a car was in the right hand lane intending to go straight on. 
Defendant driving a large road sweeper was in the left hand lane intending to turn left. 
Both parties are in the correct lane, for their intended journey, but at by the junction a collision occurs.

Quick shout out. 

CLICK will reveal a plan

However, does that bollard / crossing obstruct the Claimant’s line of travel?

articles.title: Motorcycle Liability — Failure to Wear a Helmet: Contributory Negligence Reduction and the Causal Test
articles.author: credit-hire.ai editorial
articles.author_role: Editorial team — Credit Hire Forum knowledge base
articles.summary: A short practitioner note on motorcycle helmet contributory negligence on the contributory-negligence treatment of a motorcyclist who fails to wear (or properly fasten) a crash helmet. The position is governed by Capps v Miller [1989] 1 WLR 839 and the reduction depends on the CAUSAL effect: the extent to which the injuries would have been reduced or avoided had the helmet been worn or properly fastened. A non-causal helmet failure does not produce a reduction. Sikh motorcyclists wearing a turban are exempt from the legal helmet requirement.
key_points: Motorcyclists are legally obliged to wear a crash helmet. Sikh motorcyclists wearing a turban are exempt from the legal requirement.
A failure to wear a helmet may produce a reduction in damages on the contributory-negligence principle in section 1(1) Law Reform (Contributory Negligence) Act 1945.

The reduction is CAUSAL — it depends on the extent to which the injuries would have been reduced or avoided had the helmet been worn or correctly fastened. If the injuries are wholly unrelated to head trauma, there is no reduction.

Capps v Miller [1989] 1 WLR 839 — the leading authority. The Court of Appeal held that an unfastened helmet which came off on impact attracted a 10% reduction; a wholly unworn helmet would have attracted a higher reduction.

O'Connell v Jackson [1972] 1 QB 270 — pre-Capps authority establishing the general principle that failure to wear protective equipment can attract a reduction where the failure causally contributes to the injuries (helmet on a moped).

Practitioner takeaway: where helmet evidence is in issue, secure (i) the helmet itself where possible for inspection, (ii) ambulance/A&E records describing whether a helmet was present, (iii) medical evidence on the contribution of the unhelmeted/poorly-secured state to the head/cervical injuries claimed. Without causal medical evidence the defendant cannot make out a reduction.

Quantum of reduction: typical figures range from 10% (Capps unfastened) to 25% (wholly unworn helmet contributing to severe head injury). The reduction does NOT extend to non-head/non-cervical injuries.
cases_cited: Capps v Miller [1989] 1 WLR 839
O'Connell v Jackson [1972] 1 QB 270
Owens v Brimmell [1977] QB 859 (cross-reference for contributory negligence in motoring cases)
articles.full_text:
1. Motorcyclists (unless they are members of the Sikh religion wearing a turban) are legally obliged to wear crash helmets. A failure to wear a helmet may result in a reduction of contributory negligence. The extent of the reduction will depend upon the extent to which the injuries would have been reduced had a helmet been worn. See for example O'Connell v Jackson [1972] 1 Q.B. 270 where contributory negligence of 15% was found where wearing a crash helmet would probably have reduced the gravity of the head injuries sustained by the Claimant.

### KEY AREAS OF COMPLEXITY OR UNCERTAINTY
None.

### LATEST DEVELOPMENTS
None.

### POSSIBLE FUTURE DEVELOPMENTS
None.

### HUMAN RIGHTS
None.

### EUROPEAN UNION ASPECTS
None.

### FURTHER READING
None.

articles.author: Andrew Hogan
articles.title: Fundamental Dishonesty and Credit Hire: Section 57 CJCA 2015 Does Not Defeat a Property Damage Claim
articles.summary: Andrew Hogan argues that a finding of fundamental dishonesty under section 57 of the Criminal Justice and Courts Act 2015 does not automatically defeat a credit hire or property damage claim brought in the same proceedings. Section 57 is confined in its express terms to personal injury claims and related personal injury claims. It says nothing about property damage. This interpretation is supported by the common law recognition that a single accident gives rise to separate causes of action (Brunsden v Humphrey), by High Court and County Court authority (Reynolds v Kent Police [2024], Senay v Mulsanne [2024], Diaw v ERS [2020]), and decisively by Hansard: the Minister responsible for the bill expressly rejected Amendment 128, which would have extended section 57 to linked property claims, on the basis that to do so would create uncertainty, affect third party rights (including subrogation claims and credit hire advances), and raise issues of proportionality under A1P1 of the Human Rights Act 1998.
key_points: Section 57 of the Criminal Justice and Courts Act 2015 is limited on its face to 'proceedings on a claim for damages in respect of personal injury' (the 'primary claim'). A credit hire or vehicle damage claim is a property damage claim, not a personal injury claim, and falls outside the scope of section 57.

The common law has long recognised that a single accident can give rise to separate and independent causes of action. In Brunsden v Humphrey (1884) 14 QBD 141 the Court of Appeal held that damage to a cab and personal injury sustained in the same collision gave rise to distinct causes of action. The Supreme Court recently reaffirmed this in Nasir v Zavarco plc [2025] UKSC 5.

In Brown v Commissioner of Police for the Metropolis [2019] EWCA Civ 1724 Coulson LJ drew a clear distinction between claims consequential on personal injury (QOCS-protected) and vehicle damage/credit hire claims, which are consequent upon damage to property and fall within the mixed claim exception at CPR r.44.16(2)(b).

In Andrew Reynolds v Chief Constable of Kent Police [2024] EWHC 2487 (KB), Sheldon J held (binding High Court authority) that section 57 had no application to a claim for false imprisonment brought alongside a personal injury claim in the same proceedings.

In Senay and Obadi v Mulsanne Insurance Company Limited [2024] EWCC 12, HHJ Charman held that section 57 had no application to a property damage claim, including credit hire charges. In Diaw and others v ERS Syndicate Management Limited (County Court at Manchester, February 2020) Mr Recorder Mark Jones reached the same conclusion.

The Hansard record is decisive under Pepper v Hart [1993] AC 593. Lord Hunt of Wirral tabled Amendment 128, which would have extended section 57 to linked property claims. The sponsoring Minister, Lord Faulks, rejected it: extending section 57 to credit hire would affect third party subrogation rights, make insurers less inclined to pay genuine victims, and create uncertainty. Parliament deliberately chose not to extend section 57 beyond personal injury.

Third party property rights are engaged. Credit hire companies, insurers exercising subrogation rights, and bailees in possession all have independent property rights that cannot be extinguished by section 57 without express statutory language — language Parliament deliberately omitted.

Extending section 57 to defeat property damage claims would raise issues of proportionality under Article 1 of Protocol 1 ECHR. A claimant's dishonesty on a personal injury claim does not justify depriving third parties of their property rights without clear statutory authority.

The purposive approach confirmed in R v Quintavalle [2003] 2 AC 687 and Hassam v Rabot [2024] UKSC 11 supports this analysis: section 57 was enacted to deter dishonest personal injury claims — no further.

Practical warning: while the legal position strongly supports survival of the credit hire claim after a finding of fundamental dishonesty on the PI claim, 'when the wheel comes off in litigation, it tends to keep on rolling.' The practical risks of a combined trial remain significant.
cases_cited: R v Quintavalle [2003] 2 AC 687
Hassam v Rabot [2024] UKSC 11
Brunsden v Humphrey (1884) 14 QBD 141
Nasir v Zavarco plc [2025] UKSC 5
Brown v Commissioner of Police for the Metropolis [2019] EWCA Civ 1724
Andrew Reynolds v Chief Constable of Kent Police [2024] EWHC 2487 (KB)
Senay and Obadi v Mulsanne Insurance Company Limited [2024] EWCC 12
Diaw and others v ERS Syndicate Management Limited (County Court at Manchester, 25 February 2020)
Pepper v Hart [1993] AC 593
legislation_cited: Section 57, Criminal Justice and Courts Act 2015
Article 1, Protocol 1, European Convention on Human Rights (A1P1)
CPR r.44.16(2)(b) (mixed claims exception to QOCS)
articles.full_text:
Personal injury claims and credit hire claims used to go hand in hand. Since the whiplash reforms, personal injury claims seem to be less prominent in credit hire litigation, which now seems to predominantly relate to vehicle damage claims, with consequential loss including the cost of credit hire charges. Yet, when a personal injury claim is entwined with a credit hire claim, goes to trial, and the claimant is found to be fundamentally dishonest under section 57 of the Criminal Justice and Courts Act 2015, does that necessarily mean that the credit hire claim will fail along with the personal injury claim? Not necessarily.

Section 57 of the Criminal Justice and Courts Act 2015 is confined on its face to proceedings on a claim for damages in respect of personal injury (the primary claim). A credit hire or vehicle damage claim is a property damage claim, not a personal injury claim, and falls entirely outside the scope of section 57. This cannot be viewed as anything other than deliberate. If the purpose of the statute is to discourage dishonest personal injury claims, it would logically seek to deprive such claimants of their personal injury damages. But to go further and to seek to deprive them of their property rights would require extremely clear words, and raise issues about the proportionality of such a sanction under Article 1 of Protocol 1 of the Human Rights Act 1998. Moreover, such a sanction would likely infringe the property rights of third parties: insurance companies exercising rights of subrogation; persons who have bailed a vehicle to a claimant; and credit hire companies who have advanced benefits to accident victims on credit.

The common law has long recognised that one road traffic accident may give rise to more than one cause of action. In Brunsden v Humphrey (1884) 14 QBD 141 the claimant's cab was damaged and he also suffered personal injury in the same collision. Having first pursued a claim for the damage to the cab, he was held entitled to bring a further claim for personal injury. The Court of Appeal held that damage to goods and injury to the person, although caused by the same wrongful act, infringed different rights and gave rise to distinct causes of action. The principle has recently been recognised by the Supreme Court in Nasir v Zavarco plc [2025] UKSC 5. In Brown v Commissioner of Police for the Metropolis [2019] EWCA Civ 1724, Coulson LJ drew a clear distinction between claims consequential on personal injury and vehicle damage/credit hire claims, which are consequent upon damage to property and fall within the mixed claim exception at CPR r.44.16(2)(b).

There can therefore be more than one claim within one set of proceedings, and the scope of section 57 is limited to those claims which are personal injury claims. This is wholly supported by the caselaw. In Andrew Reynolds v Chief Constable of Kent Police [2024] EWHC 2487 (KB), Sheldon J held — as a matter of binding High Court authority — that section 57 had no application to a claim for false imprisonment made alongside a personal injury claim in the same proceedings. In Senay and Obadi v Mulsanne Insurance Company Limited [2024] EWCC 12, HHJ Charman concluded that section 57 had no application to a property damage claim, including a claim for credit hire charges. In Diaw and others v ERS Syndicate Management Limited (County Court at Manchester, 25 February 2020), Mr Recorder Mark Jones reached the same conclusion.

The Hansard record is decisive. By virtue of the principle in Pepper v Hart [1993] AC 593, the court in Senay considered the ministerial statement made when the Criminal Justice and Courts Bill was before the House of Lords. Lord Hunt of Wirral tabled Amendment 128, which would have extended the scope of section 57 to cover claims linked to the personal injury claim — including property damage and credit hire. The amendment was rejected by the sponsoring Minister, Lord Faulks, in the following terms: extending the clause to linked claims would make insurers less inclined to make payments to genuine victims for whom rapid vehicle replacement could be essential; the complexity of subrogated rights meant the potential for unintended consequences was high; and the existing focus on personal injury claims was deliberate and sufficient. Parliament chose not to extend section 57 to property claims, and the ministerial record confirms why.

The purposive approach to statutory interpretation confirmed in R v Quintavalle [2003] 2 AC 687 and Hassam v Rabot [2024] UKSC 11 leads to the same conclusion: section 57 was enacted to deter dishonest personal injury claims — and that is its limit.

Of course, although this analysis suggests that as a matter of law a credit hire claim will survive a finding of fundamental dishonesty on the personal injury claim, one cannot ignore the simple fact that when the wheel comes off in litigation, it tends to keep on rolling.

Note: Ben Williams KC originally articulated most of these arguments, especially the reference to the parliamentary debates. He advanced the position some years ago in response to a series of defendant appeals in the High Court, all of which settled before hearing. That demonstrated the strength of the arguments but prevented a binding judgment which should have consigned the contrary arguments to the history books. Sadly those arguments are still being made — which is why this article is an important development.
articles.source: Credit Hire Barrister blog (andrewhogan.co.uk) — freely available

articles.title: WhichRate Tactic: Lancaster Gate Credit Hire Agreement Deployed as Purported BHR Evidence in Motorcycle Claims
articles.source: Analysis of WhichRate witness statement (Mark Churton, ref 119233, 11 December 2025) and Lancaster Gate Credit Hire Agreement exhibited in Hassan v Kaca, Liverpool County Court, Claim 212DC735
articles.author: credit-hire.ai research
principles: TACTIC IDENTIFIED: WhichRate has adopted the practice of serving a Lancaster Gate Assistance Ltd credit hire agreement as exhibit evidence in motorcycle claims where no genuine BHR providers are available. The witness statement expressly admits that Basic Hire Rates are unavailable within the claimant's locality, then presents a credit hire rate from Lancaster Gate as the alternative.

LANCASTER GATE IS A CHO: Lancaster Gate Assistance Ltd (company 14093099, FCA 989253AR) is a Credit Hire Organisation based in Diss, Norfolk. It is not a conventional hire company that offers vehicles to the general public at spot rates. Its rates are ABI-GTA protocol credit hire rates set by inter-industry agreement.

CHR IS NOT BHR: Credit hire rates and basic hire rates are fundamentally different categories. CHR reflects the rate charged under a credit hire agreement where the cost is recoverable from the at-fault insurer under the GTA protocol. BHR is the rate a member of the public would pay out of pocket. Substituting one for the other is a category error.

THE AGREEMENT DOES NOT EVIDENCE BHR: A credit hire agreement is a contract between a CHO and a hirer (or their representative) for the provision of a vehicle on credit terms. It does not evidence what rate would be available to a cash-paying member of the public, what the terms and conditions of hire are for a self-funded hirer, or what elements are included in the hire charge.

WITNESS EXCLUDES DEPOSIT ENQUIRY: The WhichRate witness expressly states 'My remit does not include enquiries into the financial business processes (i.e deposits) of vehicle providers.' This is a material omission — deposits and upfront payment requirements are central to whether a rate is genuinely available to the claimant, particularly where impecuniosity is in issue.

OBSERVED RATES (Feb 2025, ABI-GTA B2): Daily hire £66.78+VAT (£80.14 inc VAT). Non-standard risk rider surcharge £15.00+VAT/day. Top box/luggage £50.00+VAT one-off. Collection and delivery £195.00+VAT (or £117.00 each way). Damage and theft excess waiver £15.00+VAT/day to reduce excess from £750 to £0. These rates include comprehensive insurance — a feature that itself confirms this is credit hire, not BHR.

REBUTTAL APPROACH: (1) Put the witness to strict proof as to why a credit hire invoice from a CHO evidences the basic hire rate; (2) Note the witness's own admission that BHR is unavailable — the Lancaster Gate rate is labelled CHR throughout; (3) Challenge the absence of published terms and conditions for a self-funded hirer; (4) Highlight that the witness excludes enquiry into deposits, which is central to impecuniosity; (5) Argue that if the defendant accepts BHR is unavailable, the appropriate response under Stevens is to acknowledge that the credit hire rate is the only available rate, not to dress up a different CHO's credit hire rate as a substitute BHR.
cases: Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management [2015] EWCA Civ 93
key_points: Lancaster Gate Assistance Ltd is a Credit Hire Organisation, not a conventional hire company offering BHR.
WhichRate deploys Lancaster Gate credit hire agreements in motorcycle claims where no BHR is available.
The witness statement expressly admits BHR is unavailable, then substitutes CHR.
Credit hire rates are GTA-protocol rates recoverable from at-fault insurers — they are not BHR.
The credit hire agreement does not evidence terms available to a cash-paying member of the public.
The witness excludes enquiry into deposits — a material omission for impecuniosity assessment.
Put the witness to strict proof on why a CHO invoice evidences the basic hire rate.
