articles.summary: Independent market research by Future Market Insights covering the UK car rental industry from 2025 to 2035. Provides tier-based market share data that directly supports the mainstream national supplier classification used in BHR rebuttal work. The Top 3 (Enterprise, Hertz, Europcar) hold 50% of the market; Avis and Sixt together hold 15%; the next five (Thrifty, Alamo, Budget, Green Motion, Virtuo) hold 20%; and emerging/regional brands hold the remaining 15%. Market concentration is assessed as 'Medium' (30-60%). This data independently corroborates that only Enterprise, Hertz, Europcar and Avis are genuinely mainstream national operators with sufficient market presence to serve as Tier 1 BHR comparators under Stevens.
key_points: TOP 3 BY MARKET SHARE (50%): Enterprise, Hertz and Europcar collectively hold approximately 50% of the UK car rental market. These are the dominant mainstream national suppliers with the largest fleets, most extensive branch networks, and broadest vehicle group coverage.

REST OF TOP 5 (15%): Avis and Sixt together account for approximately 15% of the market. Avis is a mainstream national; Sixt's UK footprint is predominantly airport-based with limited high-street presence.

NEXT 5 OF TOP 10 (20%): Thrifty, Alamo, Budget, Green Motion and Virtuo collectively hold approximately 20%. Thrifty has limited UK coverage; Alamo and Dollar are Enterprise Holdings airport brands; Budget is an Avis Budget Group subsidiary with a smaller branch network than Avis; Green Motion and Virtuo are emerging/niche operators.

EMERGING AND REGIONAL (15%): Peer-to-peer, EV-only, and regional operators account for the remaining 15%. This includes Arnold Clark (regional, concentrated in Scotland/north of England), PCH (niche), Evision (electric-only), and similar operators.
MARKET CONCENTRATION: The UK car rental market is assessed as 'Medium' concentration (30-60%), described as 'Fairly Consolidated'. The Top 3 alone control half the market.

TIER CLASSIFICATION: FMI classifies suppliers into Tier 1 (Enterprise, Hertz, Europcar — 50% share), Tier 2 (Avis, Sixt — 15% share), and a combined lower tier (Thrifty, Alamo, Budget, Green Motion, Virtuo — 35% share including emerging brands).

ENTERPRISE HOLDINGS: Enterprise is the single largest operator, having expanded its EV fleet and corporate leasing. Its subsidiary brands Alamo and Dollar share fleet and locations but are positioned as airport-focused brands.

KEY BRAND STRATEGIES: Enterprise focuses on EV expansion and corporate leasing; Hertz on digital-first reservations and contactless check-ins; Europcar on flexible long-term rentals; Sixt on AI-based pricing models.

BHR RELEVANCE: This independent market data supports the proposition that only Avis, Enterprise, Europcar and Hertz have sufficient UK market share, branch coverage and fleet diversity to qualify as mainstream national Tier 1 comparators under Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management [2015] EWCA Civ 93. Defendants who cite suppliers outside the Top 5 as BHR comparators are selecting from operators holding individually small market shares with limited geographic reach.

FORECAST: The market is shifting toward digital platforms, EV integration, and subscription-based models. Online and app-based rentals dominate with 60% of bookings. Short-term and daily rentals account for 40% of the market.
cases_referenced: Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management [2015] EWCA Civ 93
articles.full_text:
UNITED KINGDOM CAR RENTAL MARKET SHARE & INDUSTRY LEADERS
Future Market Insights, Inc.
February 2025 (Page last updated 24 February 2025)
Historical Data: 2015-2023 | Base Year: 2024 | Estimated Year: 2025 | Forecast: 2026-2035
250 pages | Reviewed by Rahul Pandita

The UK car rental industry is changing as the focus shifts toward affordability, convenience, and sustainable mobility by businesses and travelers. The growth in digital platforms, contactless transactions, and integration of electric vehicles is leading the sector through a transformative shift. Companies with focus on fleet diversity, app-based reservations, and flexible rental options are the current market leaders.

The leading companies like Enterprise, Hertz, and Europcar together take up 50% of the market share with large fleets, corporate deals, and a seamless digital experience. Independent and regional car rental companies make up 30% of the market share with low-cost services and localized offerings. New entrants in electric vehicle rentals, peer-to-peer car-sharing, and AI-driven pricing models make up 20% of the market.

GLOBAL MARKET SHARE OVERVIEW BY LEADING UK-BASED AND INTERNATIONAL CAR RENTAL BRANDS

Market Segment | Industry Share (%)
Top 3 (Enterprise, Hertz, Europcar) | 50%
Rest of Top 5 (Avis, Sixt) | 15%
Next 5 of Top 10 (Thrifty, Alamo, Budget, Green Motion, Virtuo) | 20%
Emerging & Regional Brands (peer-to-peer, EV rentals) | 15%

MARKET CONCENTRATION OUTLOOK FOR 2025
Concentration Level: Medium (30-60%) — Market is Fairly Consolidated

SALES CHANNELS
Online & App-Based Car Rental Services dominate with 60%. Airport & Travel Hubs hold 25%. Corporate & Long-Term Leasing accounts for 10%. Peer-to-Peer & Subscription-Based Car Rentals make up 5%.

PRODUCT TYPE
Short-Term & Daily Rentals are at the top with a share of 40% due to tourism and urban mobility. Long-Term & Corporate Leasing is at 30% as a flexible solution for fleet management. Luxury & Premium Car Rentals occupy 20%. Electric & Sustainable Car Rental Services constitute a 10% share.

KEY BRANDS THAT SHAPED THE YEAR
Enterprise: Expanded its electric vehicle fleet and corporate leasing programs.
Hertz: Go digital-first with reservations along with contactless check-in experiences.
Europcar: Strengthen long-term rental offerings including flexible returns.
Sixt: Use AI-based pricing models to ensure lowest rental costs.
Emerging Brands: Startups like Virtuo and Green Motion introduced app-based EV rentals and subscription car leasing services.

TIER-WISE BRAND CLASSIFICATION BASED ON NETWORK SIZE, TECH INTEGRATION, AND FLEET MODERNIZATION (2025)

Tier 1: Enterprise, Hertz, Europcar — Market Share 50%
Tier 2: Avis, Sixt — Market Share 15%
Tier 1 (lower): Thrifty, Alamo, Budget, Green Motion, Virtuo — Market Share 35%

BRAND KEY FOCUS AREAS
Enterprise: Electric vehicle expansion & corporate leasing
Hertz: Digital-first reservations & contactless check-ins
Europcar: Flexible return options & long-term rentals
Sixt: AI-based pricing models & fleet optimization
Emerging Brands: App-driven EV rentals & subscription leasing

FORECAST HIGHLIGHTS
Growth in Electric Vehicle & Sustainable Car Rentals: EV adoption will drive rental fleet modernization.
Expansion of Peer-to-Peer & Subscription-Based Car Sharing: Consumers will favor flexible, app-driven rental options.
Rise in AI-Driven Pricing & Fleet Optimization: Smart algorithms will enhance affordability and availability.
Customization & Contactless Rental Experiences: Digital check-ins and keyless access will redefine convenience.
Corporate & Business Car Leasing to Rise: Long-term, flexible rental programs will support enterprise mobility.

STRATEGIC RECOMMENDATIONS
Invest in Electric Vehicle & Green Mobility Solutions.
Enhance Digital-First & Contactless Car Rentals.
Expand Peer-to-Peer & Subscription-Based Car Rental Services.
Improve AI-Powered Pricing & Demand Forecasting.
Strengthen Corporate & Long-Term Leasing Partnerships.

Source: Future Market Insights, Inc. (https://www.futuremarketinsights.com/reports/united-kingdom-car-rental-market-share-analysis)
Copyright Future Market Insights, Inc.

articles.title: Cancellation Notices and Credit Hire
articles.author: Azeem Ali
articles.summary: A comprehensive analysis of cancellation notice requirements in credit hire, covering both the Consumer Credit Act 1974 and the Consumer Contracts (Information, Cancellation and Additional Charges) Regulations 2013. Explains when cancellation notices are required, the consequences of non-compliance, and the critical distinction between distance, off-premises and on-premises contracts. Argues that while lower courts are likely to follow Dimond v Lovell and Salat v Barutis in finding unenforceable agreements where notices are missing, the legal landscape has changed since those cases because all breaches are now redeemably unenforceable under s127(1), creating scope for enforcement orders.
key_points: Two sources of cancellation notice requirements: Consumer Credit Act 1974 (for regulated agreements with antecedent negotiations signed away from business premises) and the 2013 Regulations (for distance and off-premises contracts regardless of credit)

Under the Consumer Credit Act 1974, a cancellation notice is required for 'cancellable agreements' under s67 where antecedent negotiations included oral representations in the presence of the debtor and the agreement was signed away from certain business premises

The 2013 Regulations require cancellation information for distance contracts (arranged remotely by phone, email, online) and off-premises contracts (signed at consumer's home, roadside etc.) but NOT for on-premises contracts signed at the trader's business premises

Under the CCA 1974, failure to provide a cancellation notice renders the agreement unenforceable without a court order (s65), but since the 2006 amendments, all breaches are now redeemably unenforceable and can potentially be cured via s127(1) application

Under the 2013 Regulations, failure to comply with off-premises cancellation notice requirements is a criminal offence (Regulation 19); for distance contracts it extends the cancellation period from 14 days up to 12 months (Regulation 31)

Dimond v Lovell and Salat v Barutis held that unenforceable hire agreements meant no loss and hire charges should be dismissed, but both dealt with irredeemably unenforceable agreements — the law has since changed
The author argues there is a genuine argument that where a redeemably unenforceable agreement exists and the hire company applies under s127(1), there IS a loss — but higher court guidance is needed
Most credit hire agreements arranged by telephone or online are likely to be distance contracts requiring cancellation information under the 2013 Regulations
Agreements signed at the customer's home when the vehicle is delivered are likely to be off-premises contracts
cases_cited: W v Veolia Environmental Services (UK) Plc [2011] 7 WLUK 808
Salat v Barutis [2013] EWCA Civ 1499
Dimond v Lovell
legislation_cited: Consumer Contracts (Information, Cancellation and Additional Charges) Regulations 2013, regs 5, 10, 13, 19, 30, 31
Cancellation of Contracts made in a Consumer's Home or Place of Work etc. Regulations 2008, reg 7(2)

articles.title: Copley v Lawn (Intervention Letters) and Electronic Communications
articles.author: Azeem Ali
articles.summary: An examination of whether electronic communications (emails and text messages) can serve as valid intervention letters under Copley v Lawn. Analyses two county court cases — Alexander Massie v Ageas (intervention by text) and Trevinder Thandi v AA Underwriting (intervention by email) — and re-examines the Court of Appeal's decision in Copley v Lawn in light of modern communication methods. Notes that while county courts appear to accept emails and texts as valid delivery methods, there is no higher court authority directly on point, and a text without prior communication could be viewed as a 'cold call' which Copley v Lawn condemned as 'inappropriate'.
key_points: No higher court authority currently addresses whether intervention letters sent by text or email are valid
In Alexander Massie v Ageas [2019] 10 WLUK 862, a county court found the claimant did receive an intervention letter via text (link in text message) and reduced hire charges accordingly

In Trevinder Thandi v AA Underwriting [2025] 4 WLUK 723, a Recorder found the claimant should have switched to the intervention vehicle within 7 days of receiving the email — no party argued email was an invalid delivery method
The Court of Appeal in Copley v Lawn described cold telephone calls as 'inappropriate' and said the practice should be 'discontinued forthwith' — this could extend to unsolicited text messages
A text message with an intervention letter link, particularly without any prior communication, could be considered analogous to a cold call
Email appears harder to challenge as a delivery method — it can be opened and read on a full screen, unlike a text with a link on a small mobile screen
The author suggests using multiple delivery methods (post, text, and email) for robustness, as evidenced by a recent case where the defence used all three

The ratio decidendi of Copley v Lawn was that the intervention letter did not contain the cost of hire to the defendant — the Court of Appeal was also reluctant to accept that defendants should be entitled to intervene at all
Higher court guidance on electronic delivery of intervention letters would be most desirable
cases_cited: Copley v Lawn [2009] EWCA Civ 580
Alexander Massie v Ageas Insurance Limited [2019] 10 WLUK 862
Mr Trevinder Thandi v AA Underwriting Insurance Company Limited [2025] 4 WLUK 723

articles.title: Credit Hire: Part 7 (Trial) or Part 8 (Stage 3 Hearing)?
articles.author: Azeem Ali
articles.summary: A detailed tactical analysis of whether credit hire cases should proceed as Part 7 trials or Part 8 Stage 3 hearings. Draws on the Court of Appeal decisions in Phillips v Willis and Islington LBC v Bourous/Davis v Yousaf, plus four of the author's own cases, to demonstrate that the choice of forum is often the most important tactical decision in credit hire litigation. Argues that Stage 3 hearings are almost always advantageous for claimants (no cross-examination, witness statements taken at face value, rough justice) while Part 7 trials are generally better for defendants who want to mount substantial challenges on impecuniosity, enforceability or misrepresentation.
key_points: The choice between Part 7 trial and Part 8 Stage 3 hearing is arguably the most important tactical issue in credit hire today, yet is regularly overlooked
Part 7 is a full trial with cross-examination and oral evidence; Part 8 Stage 3 is a short oral hearing (often 10-30 minutes) based on papers with no witness evidence
Phillips v Willis [2016] EWCA Civ 401: the Court of Appeal overturned a transfer from Stage 3 to Part 7, finding it disproportionate where the difference between the parties was only £462
The Court of Appeal stated that transfer from Stage 3 to Part 7 is not reserved for 'rare and exceptional' cases as the White Book had suggested — this was a misstatement
Islington LBC v Bourous: a party cannot raise at Stage 3 an issue not raised at Stage 2 — the defendant's failure to raise loss of profit at Stage 2 meant it could not be argued at Stage 3

Davis v Yousaf: at Stage 3, a witness statement asserting impecuniosity is effectively sufficient to prove it — the Court of Appeal confirmed financial disclosure (bank statements, tax returns) is not required
For claimants, Stage 3 is almost always advantageous: no cross-examination, witness statements taken at face value, claimants routinely awarded all or most of their hire claim
For defendants wanting to mount a substantial challenge, Part 7 trial is the better forum — but the decision must be made early by exiting the Protocol at the appropriate stage
Once Stage 3 proceedings commence, there is no guaranteed path to Part 7; it is usually too late and an uphill struggle to get a transfer

A defendant who has never challenged impecuniosity in the Protocol and there is no witness statement may find a hire agreement and invoice alone sufficient for a court to award the full claim at Stage 3
cases_cited: Phillips v Willis [2016] EWCA Civ 401
Islington LBC v Bourous/Davis v Yousaf [2022] EWCA Civ 1242
Lagden v O'Connor [2004] 1 AC 1067
legislation_cited: Pre-Action Protocol for Low Value Personal Injury Claims in Road Traffic Accidents from 31 July 2013, paras 6.15, 7.46, 7.76

articles.filename: credit hire or basic hire rates.pdf
articles.title: Credit Hire Rate or Basic Hire Rate? — Ellis on Credit Hire (Seventh Edition)
articles.author: Andrew Hogan
articles.author_role: Barrister, author of Ellis on Credit Hire
articles.summary: Authoritative chapter from Ellis on Credit Hire (7th ed.) explaining the legal basis for the basic hire rate (BHR) rule and why claimants are generally limited to the spot rate rather than the full credit hire rate. Critically distinguishes between betterment (the actual reason for the BHR reduction) and mitigation (which is NOT the reason). Traces the rule from Lord Hoffmann's obiter in Dimond v Lovell through its entrenchment in Sayce v TNT, Burdis v Livsey, Bee v Jenson, Copley v Lawn, Bent no1, Bent no2 and Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management. Identifies the four additional benefits in credit hire contracts that justify the spot rate deduction: (1) the credit charge itself, (2) costs of the litigation action, (3) possibly avoiding a residual liability, (4) relieving the anxiety of the claim. Explains Lord Hobhouse's alternative apportionment approach and why Lord Hoffmann's spot-rate proxy approach prevailed. Notes that courts have retreated from attempts to strip out additional benefits via disclosure of charging structures (Stevens paras 30, 36). Essential reading for understanding and constructing BHR rebuttals.
key_points: The BHR rule originates from Lord Hoffmann's obiter in Dimond v Lovell: 'in the case of a hiring from an accident hire company, the equivalent spot rate will ordinarily be the net loss after allowance has been made for the additional benefits which the accident hire company has provided.'
This was obiter dicta -- the ratio of Dimond related to CCA 1974 enforceability, not rates. Lord Saville expressly declined to decide the rates issue.

Despite being obiter, the rule is now firmly entrenched: Sayce v TNT confirmed it would not be right to disregard those parts of their Lordships' speeches in Dimond v Lovell that deal with the recovery of that part of the credit hire costs that relate to additional benefits on the grounds that they are obiter.

The REASON for the BHR restriction is BETTERMENT, not MITIGATION. Lord Hoffmann accepted Mrs Dimond acted reasonably in going to 1st Automotive -- the restriction is because she obtained additional benefits beyond mere use of the car.

Lord Hoffmann identified four additional benefits in the credit hire contract: (1) the credit charge itself (relieved of paying upfront), (2) the costs of the action (litigation conducted for her), (3) possibly avoiding a residual liability (may never have to pay), (4) relieving the anxiety of pursuing the claim.

Lord Hoffmann's measure: the spot rate is a proxy for the claimant's actual loss -- the difference between what Mrs Dimond was willing to pay 1st Automotive and what she would have paid an ordinary hire company for use of a car.

Lord Hobhouse agreed on the additional benefits but preferred a direct apportionment approach -- stripping the unrecoverable elements from the credit hire charge rather than using the spot rate as a proxy.
Lord Hobhouse noted it is unlikely that any scheme could be devised which would enable the insurance element to be recovered.

Lord Nicholls dissented: he accepted additional benefits existed but argued they should be fully recoverable as they redress the imbalance between the individual car owner and the insurance companies.
In practice, Lord Hoffmann's spot-rate approach prevailed over Lord Hobhouse's stripping approach -- it is simpler and avoids costly disclosure exercises.

Courts have retreated from disclosure/stripping: in Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management, the Court of Appeal canvassed whether CHOs could disclose their BHR alongside the credit hire rate, but ultimately noted the selection of the lowest reasonable rate was justified by absence of disclosure (paras 30, 36).

It remains open to credit hire companies to avoid BHR analysis by providing appropriate disclosure of charging structures and breaking down charges into constituent elements -- but in practice none do.
The general rule (unless the claimant is impecunious): the claimant is restricted to recovering the equivalent spot hire rate -- i.e. the basic hire rate.

Key cases tracing the rule: Dimond v Lovell [2002] 1 AC 384, Sayce v TNT [2011] EWCA Civ 1583, Burdis v Livsey [2003] QB 36, Bee v Jenson (2007) 4 All ER 791, Copley v Lawn para 3, Bent no1 [2010] EWCA Civ 292, Bent no2 [2011] EWCA Civ 1384, Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management Ltd [2015] EWCA Civ 93.
cases_cited: Dimond v Lovell [2002] 1 AC 384
Sayce v TNT [2011] EWCA Civ 1583
Burdis v Livsey [2003] QB 36
Bee v Jenson (2007) 4 All ER 791
Copley v Lawn
Bent v Highways & Utilities (Bent no1) [2010] EWCA Civ 292
Bent v Highways & Utilities (Bent no2) [2011] EWCA Civ 1384
Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management Ltd [2015] EWCA Civ 93
British Westinghouse v Underground Electric Railways [1912] AC 673
Bellingham v Dhillon [1973] QB 304
articles.full_text:
CREDIT HIRE RATE OR BASIC HIRE RATE?

It is perhaps helpful to start by stating the general rule again. In Dimond v Lovell,[1] after emerging from the thicket of consumer credit issues to address the issue of rates, Lord Hoffmann (with whom Lord Browne-Wilkinson agreed) stated that:-

"in the case of a hiring from an accident hire company, the equivalent spot rate will ordinarily be the net loss after allowance has been made for the additional benefits which the accident hire company has provided."

It should be acknowledged at the outset that this part of the speech was obiter dicta. The ratio of Dimond v Lovell related to the enforceability of the hire agreement under the Consumer Credit Act 1974. This led Lord Saville to abstain from deciding the rates issue on the basis that it:-

"does not arise for decision in the present case. This is a question of great importance and difficulty, the answer to which may well have widespread ramifications. It is accordingly a question that I would prefer to consider in a case where it does arise for decision."

Although the other four Judges did address the issue, Lord Nicholls dissented on the basis that the full credit hire rate should be recoverable and Lord Hobhouse agreed that the credit hire rate should not be recoverable, but proposed a different way of ascertaining the recoverable damages. Only Lord Browne-Wilkinson agreed entirely with Lord Hoffmann.

Nevertheless it would, in practice, be very difficult to argue that lower courts should not follow Lord Hoffmann's approach. As the Court of Appeal explained in Sayce v TNT:

"There are circumstances in which, although not technically bound by a decision of a higher court, a lower court should follow and apply that decision, even though it may disagree with it. There may be room for legitimate disagreement between judges of co-ordinate jurisdiction and in those circumstances reasoned difference of opinion may provide a useful springboard for an appeal. That is not the case, however, where a higher court has decided a question of principle, albeit obiter, for the purpose of clarifying the law for the profession at large. It would not be right, for example, for this court to disregard those parts of their Lordships' speeches in Dimond v Lovell that deal with the recovery of that part of the credit hire costs that relate to additional benefits on the grounds that they are obiter."[2]

Moreover, many subsequent cases confirm that Lord Hoffmann's dicta have become firmly entrenched in the jurisprudence. They have been repeatedly confirmed in subsequent cases. Thus in Burdis v Livsey, the Court of Appeal endeavoured to follow the House of Lords' "guidance as to the principles to be applied in arriving at the correct measure of damages for loss of use".[3]

In a similar vein in Bee v Jenson, the Court of Appeal noted that "the House held, secondly, that even if the claimant could have recovered he could have recovered no more than the spot charge and not the charges made for an agreement that entitled the claimant to more benefit than the cost of hire itself".[4]

In Copley v Lawn, the Court of Appeal "regarded it as well settled that, although a claimant can recover the cost of hiring a replacement car, he can only recover the reasonable rate of such hire; that has been held in Dimond v Lovell to be the market or spot rate".[5]

In Bent v (1) Highways and Utilities Construction Ltd (2) Allianz Insurance ("Bent no1"),[6] the Court of Appeal summarised the authorities thus "The authorities establish that in the case of pecunious (as the Judge described Mr Bent) claimants, the damages to be awarded are normally to be assessed at spot hire rates - the rate at which a broadly similar car could be had on the market."

In Bent v (1) Highways and Utilities Construction Ltd (2) Allianz Insurance no2 ("Bent no2"), the Court of Appeal repeated "If the claimant could afford to hire a replacement car in the normal way, ie. without credit terms and by paying in advance,[7] then the damages recoverable for loss of use of the damaged car will be that sum which is attributable to the basic hire rate of the replacement car".[8]

Similarly, in Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management Ltd, the Court of Appeal summarised the general principle in these terms: "If he [the claimant] could have afforded to hire a replacement vehicle in the normal way, that is to say without credit hire terms and by paying in advance, then the damages recoverable will be that sum which is attributable to the basic hire rate (or BHR) of the replacement vehicle".[9]

The result is that, although it was strictly obiter, the general rule established by Lord Hoffmann in Dimond is now deeply entrenched in the jurisprudence. Unless the issue returns to the Supreme Court (and unless the claimant is impecunious) the general rule is therefore that the claimant is restricted to recovering "the equivalent spot hire rate".

The Reason for the General Rule

Before going on to consider how the Court should determine the basic hire rate, it is necessary to explain the reason for this general rule. Understanding the reason for the rule, should guide the application of the rule to the evidence.

In Dimond, Lord Hoffmann, with whom Lord Browne Wilkinson agreed, stated:

"My Lords, I would accept the judge's finding that Mrs. Dimond acted reasonably in going to 1st Automotive and availing herself of its services . . . She cannot therefore be said not to have taken reasonable steps to mitigate her damage.

But that does not necessarily mean that she can recover the full amount charged by 1st Automotive. By virtue of her contract, she obtained not only the use of the car but additional benefits as well."

This finding is significant because by accepting that Mrs Dimond acted reasonably in going to the credit hire company, as the Court of Appeal and the trial Judge had already held below, Lord Hoffmann made it clear that the reason for not allowing the claimant to recover the credit hire rate is nothing to do with mitigation or reasonableness. Rather it is a matter of betterment.

The point is that a credit hire company typically provides a range of accident management services to a claimant, which go beyond the simple provision of a replacement vehicle. Lord Hoffmann identified the additional benefits inherent in the credit hire contract at page 401 as:

1. the credit charge itself
"She was relieved of the necessity of laying out the money to pay for the car."

2. the costs of the action
"She was relieved of the risk of having to bear the irrecoverable costs of successful litigation and the risk, small though it might be, of having to bear the expense of unsuccessful litigation". Lord Hoffmann also referred to other associated costs: "Paying commission to brokers"; "checking that the accident was not the hirer's fault."

3. possibly avoiding a residual liability
"Depending upon the view one takes of the terms of agreement, she may have been relieved of the possibility of having to pay for the car at all."

4. relieving the anxiety of the claim
"She was relieved of the trouble and anxiety of pursuing a claim against Mr. Lovell or the C.I.S."

Lord Hoffmann continued:-

"My Lords, English law does not regard the need for any of these additional services as compensatable loss. As Sir Richard Scott V.-C. said (at [1999] 3 W.L.R. 561, 580) 'damages for worry and for the nuisance caused by having to deal with the consequences of an accident are not recoverable.' If Mrs. Dimond had borrowed the hire money, paid someone else to conduct the claim on her behalf and insured herself against the risk of losing and any irrecoverable costs, her expenses would not have been recoverable. But the effect of the award of damages is that Mrs. Dimond has obtained compensation for them indirectly because they were offered as part of a package by 1st Automotive. There is in my opinion something wrong with this conclusion."

He then referred to the cases of British Westinghouse Electric and Manufacturing Co. Ltd. v. Underground Electric Railways Co. of London Ltd [1912] A.C. 673 and Bellingham v. Dhillon [1973] Q.B. 304 which emphasised that only compensatory damages were recoverable. In other words, additional benefits received whilst mitigating loss should not be taken into account in quantifying the claim. But how does this impact on the assessment of damages? Lord Hoffmann explained:-

"How does one calculate the additional benefits that Mrs. Dimond received by choosing the 1st Automotive package to mitigate the loss caused by the accident to her car? . . . I do not think that a court can ignore the fact that, one way or another, the other benefits have to be paid for . . . How does one estimate the value of these additional benefits that Mrs. Dimond obtains? It seems to me that prima facie their value is represented by the difference between what she was willing to pay 1st Automotive and what she would have been willing to pay an ordinary car hire company for the use of a car . . . I quite accept that a determination of the value of the benefits which must be brought into account will depend upon the facts of each case. But the principle to be applied is that in the British Westinghouse case [1912] A.C. 673 and this seems to me to lead to the conclusion that in the case of hiring from an accident hire company the equivalent spot rate will ordinarily be the net loss..."

Thus, the only way to strip out the additional and irrecoverable benefits from the credit hire charges is by reference to the "equivalent spot rate".

On the face of it, Lord Hobhouse adopted a similar analysis. He expressly said that he agreed with Lord Hoffmann that the judge and the majority of the Court of Appeal had approached the issue in the wrong way. He also decided that Mrs Dimond had acted reasonably and went on to emphasise the additional benefits contained in the credit hire contract. He described these at pages 406 - 407 as:

1. the credit charge itself
"It is financing the transaction until the expected recovery is made from the other party".

2. the costs of the action
"it is bearing the cost of handling the claim and effecting that recovery"; "something in respect of costs".

3. possibly avoiding a residual liability
"it is bearing a commercial (though not normally the legal) risk that there may be a failure to make that recovery".

Thus aside from one (removing the anxiety of litigation), Lord Hobhouse identified the same additional benefits as Lord Hoffmann.

However, his analysis of how the recoverable damages should be calculated differed from that of Lord Hoffmann. Lord Hobhouse favoured "the approach of making a commercial apportionment between the cost of hiring a car and the cost of the other benefits included in the scheme."

He explained that Mrs Dimond would not have been able to recover the whole cost of the credit hire charges "as the cost of mitigating the loss of use of her car". He said later, "The elements to which the uplift in the charges of the accident hire company was attributable were (and inevitably must be) elements which were not properly included in the claim for damages for loss of use."

In other words, the claim for the uplift was not recoverable in the claim for loss of use. This may have left open the possibility that it may be recoverable in other ways. Indeed, this is supported by his further statement that "As appears from what I have said, some might be recovered from the wrongdoer in another form". In practice, however, it is difficult to see how the Claimant could recover for these additional benefits via any other route. First, the Claimant would not be able to recover for the same thing twice.

Second, Lord Hobhouse stated that, "it is unlikely that any scheme could be devised which would enable the insurance element to be recovered". This appears to refer to the additional benefit which comprised the possibility of avoiding a residual liability.

The heart of the difference between Lord Hoffmann and Lord Hobhouse is this: Lord Hoffmann thought that the measure of the Claimant's recoverable loss could safely be approximated by looking to the equivalent spot hire rate while Lord Hobhouse seemed to prefer an approach based on stripping out the unrecoverable additional benefits from the credit hire charges.

As the caselaw set out above and below illustrates, it is Lord Hoffmann's approach - searching for the equivalent basic hire rate - which is most often adopted in practice. However, that does not mean that the Courts have not expressed certain reservations. From time to time, the Court of Appeal has flirted with the idea that the credit hire company could disclose details of its charging structure in order to allow the charge for credit to be stripped out in a manner reminiscent of Lord Hobhouse's approach. Most recently, in Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management Ltd the Court of Appeal canvassed "whether credit hire companies could, as a matter of course, disclose their estimate of the BHR alongside every quoted credit hire rate". It later suggested that the selection of the lowest reasonable rate was justified by the absence of evidence from the credit hire company to "elaborate upon and give disclosure relating to its charging structures".

On the whole, however, the Courts have retreated from the disclosure and costs implications of any attempt to strip out the elements of the credit rate. Nevertheless, in theory it remains open to credit hire companies to seek to avoid the analysis of basic hire rates altogether by providing appropriate disclosure of their charging structures and an analysis breaking down the credit hire charges into their constituent elements.

For completeness, in dissent in Dimond Lord Nicholls accepted that the credit hire contract incorporated additional benefits but argued that the uplift should be recoverable. He identified the same additional services as Lord Hobhouse. Lord Nicholls then went on to explain why these additional benefits ought, in his view, to be recoverable:-

"The additional services . . . redress the imbalance between the individual car owner and the insurance companies. They enable car owners to shift from themselves to the insurance companies a loss which properly belongs to the insurers but which, in practice, owners of cars often have to bear themselves. So long as the charge for the additional services is reasonable, this charge should be part of the recoverable damages."

However, Lord Nicholls' was a lone voice in dissent. The majority approach in Dimond is now firmly entrenched in the jurisprudence. His dissent is now mainly interesting from a historical perspective.

Footnotes:
[1] Dimond v Lovell, [2002] 1 AC 384.
[2] Sayce v TNT, [2011] EWCA Civ 1583 paragraph 24.
[3] Burdis v Livsey, [2003] QB 36 paragraph 136.
[4] Bee v Jenson, (2007) 4 All ER 791 para. 6.
[5] Copley v Lawn, para. 3.
[6] Bent no1, [2010] EWCA Civ 292, see para. 6 in particular.
[8] Bent no2, [2011] EWCA Civ 1384.
[9] Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management Ltd, [2015] EWCA Civ 93 para. 11.
[10] Stevens, para. 30.
[11] Stevens, para. 36.
[12] Stevens, para. 30.
articles.source: Ellis on Credit Hire (Seventh Edition), Andrew Hogan, published by Sweet & Maxwell / Thomson Reuters. Free chapter from publisher's website, no restrictions on use.

articles.title: Enterprise Mobility Brands: Enterprise is a Mainstream National Supplier; Alamo and National Are Not (Stevens v Equity)
articles.author: Credit Hire Forum
articles.author_role: Industry reference note (sources: Enterprise Mobility publications)
articles.summary: Enterprise Mobility's own publications establish that Enterprise Rent-A-Car is the flagship mainstream national brand (airport AND neighbourhood network across thousands of communities), while Alamo (a value/airport-leisure brand) and National (a premium frequent-airport-traveller brand) are airport-focused fascias that, in the UK, share the same parent, fleet and locations as Enterprise. On the Stevens v Equity Tier 1 test, Enterprise Rent-A-Car qualifies as a mainstream national supplier but Alamo and National do not — and citing them alongside Enterprise is duplicative and misleading.
key_points: Enterprise Mobility (privately held by the Taylor family of St. Louis) manages three rental brands: Enterprise Rent-A-Car, National Car Rental and Alamo — together more than 2.4 million vehicles across over 9,500 locations in more than 90 countries.

Enterprise Rent-A-Car is the FLAGSHIP brand, offering 'an expansive network of airport and neighborhood locations' and meeting customers' 'local mobility needs in thousands of communities'. This broad neighbourhood-plus-airport coverage is what makes Enterprise a mainstream national supplier under Stevens v Equity Tier 1 (rental of a wide range of models throughout the UK, accessible to the claimant locally).

Alamo is, in Enterprise's own words, 'a value-oriented brand' that 'primarily serves the rental needs of airport leisure travelers' at 'the most popular travel destinations'. It is an airport/leisure brand, not a neighbourhood/community network.

National is, in Enterprise's own words, 'a premium, internationally recognized brand serving the frequent airport traveller', built around the Emerald Aisle / Emerald Club at airports. It is an airport/business-traveller brand, not a neighbourhood/community network.

In the UK, Enterprise, National and Alamo are owned by the same parent (Enterprise Mobility/Holdings) and 'usually share the same physical fleets at airports and city branches'. National and Alamo are therefore not independent nationwide networks — they are airport/city-branch fascias over Enterprise's fleet.

Consequence for BHR: the correct mainstream-national comparator within the Enterprise group is Enterprise Rent-A-Car itself. Alamo and National do not satisfy the Stevens Tier 1 test because they lack a neighbourhood branch network accessible to a claimant and are airport/leisure-focused.

Where a defendant's rate witness cites Alamo or National (often to advance a lower airport-leisure rate), challenge it: these are not mainstream national suppliers, and citing them alongside Enterprise is duplicative and misleading because they share fleet, locations and corporate ownership with Enterprise.
Enterprise also operates a niche specialist arm, the Exotic Car Collection by Enterprise (exotic sports cars, luxury sedans and SUVs) — a specialist operation, not a mainstream national comparator.
cases_cited: Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management Ltd [2015] EWCA Civ 93
articles.full_text:
ENTERPRISE MOBILITY BRAND STRUCTURE — SUPPORT FOR THE STEVENS v EQUITY MAINSTREAM-NATIONAL ANALYSIS

The following is drawn from Enterprise Mobility's own publications (a press-release footer and the Enterprise Mobility 'Meet Our Brands' page) and is useful where a user asks whether Alamo or National are mainstream national rental suppliers.

About Enterprise Mobility (standard press-release footer):
"Enterprise Mobility is a leading provider of mobility solutions including car rental, fleet management, flexible vehicle hire, carsharing, vanpooling, truck rental, luxury rental, retail car sales and vehicle subscription... Enterprise Mobility, inclusive of its subsidiaries and franchisees, and affiliate, Enterprise Fleet Management, manage a diverse fleet of more than 2.4 million vehicles through an integrated network of over 9,500 fully staffed neighborhood and airport rental locations in more than 90 countries and territories. Privately held by the Taylor family of St. Louis, Enterprise Mobility manages the Enterprise Rent-A-Car, National Car Rental and Alamo brands."

Meet Our Brands (Enterprise Mobility):
"In the UK, Enterprise, National, and Alamo are all owned by the same parent company (Enterprise Mobility/Holdings) and usually share the same physical fleets at airports and city branches."

Alamo — "a value-oriented brand, recognized for offering everyday low car rental rates at popular travel destinations. Internationally recognized, Alamo primarily serves the rental needs of airport leisure travelers and is one of the largest car rental providers for international travelers visiting North America... Today, Alamo offers everyday low rental rates and a hassle-free customer experience at the most popular travel destinations worldwide."

Enterprise Rent-A-Car — "the flagship brand of Enterprise Mobility... Launched in 1957, Enterprise Rent-A-Car offers unparalleled convenience through an expansive network of airport and neighborhood locations to serve business and leisure travelers' daily rental needs." Enterprise "meets its customers' local mobility needs in thousands of communities across the world."

National — "a premium, internationally recognized brand serving the frequent airport traveller who wants time savings for personal and business trips. National Car Rental's Emerald Aisle is the fastest way to rent in the U.S. and Canada... National consistently ranks as one of the top brands among airport car rental customers."

Exotic Car Collection by Enterprise — a specialist operation offering exotic sports cars and luxury sedans and SUVs with personalized service; a niche specialist arm, not a mainstream national comparator.

ANALYSIS (Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management Ltd [2015] EWCA Civ 93):
Tier 1 of Stevens requires the basic hire rate to be evidenced from a recognised mainstream national supplier — one offering rental across a wide range of models throughout the UK and accessible to the claimant in their locality. On Enterprise's own descriptions: (1) Enterprise Rent-A-Car is the flagship brand with an expansive network of airport AND neighbourhood locations serving thousands of communities — it is a mainstream national supplier. (2) Alamo is a value brand that 'primarily serves the rental needs of airport leisure travelers' — airport/leisure-focused, not a neighbourhood network. (3) National is a premium brand 'serving the frequent airport traveller' via the Emerald Aisle/Emerald Club at airports — airport/business-focused, not a neighbourhood network. (4) In the UK all three share the same parent, and 'usually share the same physical fleets at airports and city branches'. Accordingly, Alamo and National are not mainstream national suppliers for BHR purposes; the correct mainstream-national comparator within the Enterprise group is Enterprise Rent-A-Car itself. Where a rate witness cites Alamo or National, the rate should be challenged as (a) not a mainstream national supplier under Stevens Tier 1, and (b) where Enterprise is also cited, duplicative and misleading, since Alamo/National share fleet, locations and ownership with Enterprise rather than constituting genuinely independent alternatives.
articles.source: Enterprise Mobility press release ('About Enterprise Mobility' footer) and Enterprise Mobility 'Meet Our Brands' / Enterprise Mobility Careers page; uploaded June 2026.
